Jiang Mi kept her head down. "In reply to Your Majesty, this subject's daughter came out to look for Mianmian."

She paused, then hugged the small cat a little closer. "This... this is Mianmian."

Mianmian, unable to sit still, wriggled and pushed against her arms, eager to jump down and explore.

Afraid that letting go would allow it to run away, Jiang Mi could only hold on tighter.

Mianmian, squirming happily, pressed its snow-white paws onto something soft. It seemed to discover a fascinating new toy, so it extended its other paw and pressed down as well, both little paws kneading away in perfect rhythm.

Jiang Mi's face instantly flushed crimson. She didn't know whether to drop the kitten or keep holding it.

What made it even more mortifying was that she could sense Xiao Huaiyan's gaze fixed directly on Mianmian.

Abandoning all decorum, she spun around in mortified fury, turning her back on him.

Jiang Mi trembled with rage, burning with shame. She had nothing to do with him now—how dare he still act so wantonly?!

Xiao Huaiyan was a little surprised.

Going by the Empress Dowager's intentions, this girl should not be reacting this way.

Xiao Huaiyan asked casually, feigning ignorance, "Miss Jiang, are you cold? You're shaking so much."

"No, not cold," Jiang Mi said through gritted teeth.

"In that case, Miss Jiang should not delay any longer. Let's go."

Jiang Mi wasn't quite sure what he meant. If he wanted to leave, then he could leave—why would she be the one delaying?

She was more than happy for him to leave as soon as possible.

Just as she was puzzling over this, Chengzhong appeared before her, holding a dark cloak in both hands, head bowed respectfully. "Miss, the night wind is strong. Please put this on. His Majesty's intention is to escort you back to the Palace of Compassion and Tranquility."

A flicker of hesitation passed through Jiang Mi's eyes. When she had crawled out of the bushes, her skirt had gotten dirty, and now Mianmian had smeared its soiled little paws across her chest, leaving unsightly marks.

Moreover, she had already lost her way and would have great difficulty returning to the Palace of Compassion and Tranquility on her own.

Though reluctant, Jiang Mi quietly draped the cloak over her shoulders and followed behind with Mianmian in her arms.

Xiao Huaiyan was already seated in the imperial palanquin, watching that slow-moving figure finally approach.

The moment Jiang Mi drew near, the palanquin set off.

Throughout the journey, Xiao Huaiyan sat in the palanquin with his eyes closed, resting, paying no attention to Jiang Mi.

Jiang Mi walked behind the palanquin and let out a quiet breath of relief. She liked this kind of distance.

......

The imperial procession arrived at the Palace of Compassion and Tranquility—followed, as it turned out, by Miss Jiang, the Third Young Miss, whom everyone had been frantically searching for.

The palace maids rushed to report this to Empress Dowager Jiang.

The stern expression on Empress Dowager Jiang's face gradually softened. She asked, "How did the two of them end up walking together?"

The palace maids had no answer.

While the Empress Dowager was still puzzling over this, Xiao Huaiyan and Jiang Mi arrived at the bedchamber one after another.

Empress Dowager Jiang noticed that Jiang Mi was wearing a man's cloak, with a stray strand of hair hanging loose at her temple. At a casual glance, nothing seemed amiss—but she had watched Tangtang lean against her and act adorably that evening, and she remembered her hair quite clearly. When Tangtang had finished dinner, that little strand had been pinned up.

The Empress Dowager's heart lurched. A man's cloak, a slightly disheveled hair—could it be that the Emperor had favored Tangtang?

But she quickly dismissed the thought.

Xiao Huaiyan was not like the late Emperor, nor like his brothers. He carried himself with noble reserve. If he truly wanted Tangtang, he would give her a proper title first.

"Tangtang, where did you go?" the Empress Dowager asked.

Jiang Mi, full of guilt, explained the whole story of going out to look for Mianmian. The Empress Dowager shook her head repeatedly as she listened.

"This little creature is something I gave you to cheer you up, and here you've gone and gotten yourself into this state over it. You disappeared without a word—do you know how worried I was? Lucky you ran into the Emperor. Otherwise, you'd have been in for a miserable time."

Jiang Mi sniffled, holding back tears. "Auntie, I know I was wrong."

Xiao Huaiyan, who had been sitting at the head of the room without saying a word, let his index finger tap the table idly. He turned to the Empress Dowager and said, "Has the Empress Dowager taken her medicine?"

The Empress Dowager had, in fact, forgotten all about it.

Qingxue, perceptive as always, spoke up immediately: "Your Majesty's medicine has been kept warm. This servant will go fetch it right away."

Jiang Mi took advantage of the moment and quietly slipped out alongside Qingxue.

Qingxue murmured, "Miss, please go change your clothes and then come back. Leave Mianmian with this servant—I'll take good care of it."

Jiang Mi asked anxiously, "Sister Qingxue, is Auntie very angry?"

Qingxue smiled and answered, "As long as the Miss is safe, Her Majesty's anger will pass, no matter how great."

A weight lifted from Jiang Mi's heart. She placed Mianmian into Qingxue's arms. "Then I'll trouble Sister Qingxue."

......

Jiang Mi changed into a brocade skirt embroidered with begonia flowers in gold thread. She pinned her hair back up and clasped in a gold-inlaid lotus hairpin that swayed with each step. When she emerged from her room, she had neatly folded the dark cloak.

Walking to the door of her aunt's bedchamber, she found Chengzhong still standing outside. He had not left yet.

Jiang Mi held out the cloak to him. "Thank you, Eunuch."

Chengzhong received it with both hands and bowed his head. "Miss is thanking the wrong person."

Jiang Mi stepped into the hall to find the Empress Dowager and Xiao Huaiyan in conversation.

"The medicine Imperial Physician Gu prescribed is excellent in every way—except that it's terribly bitter. Fortunately, Tangtang made me these osmanthus cakes, which helped cover the bitterness. If only I could eat more of them at once—this little plate is not nearly enough."

Xiao Huaiyan glanced at the plate on the Empress Dowager's table, which now held only four cakes remaining. He couldn't help but think of Imperial Physician Gu's expression earlier that day—repeatedly gulping water yet unable to recover for the longest time.

The Empress Dowager continued, "Your Majesty tasted them today as well, yes? What did you think? Were they to your liking?"

Xiao Huaiyan glanced toward Jiang Mi, who was standing in the doorway, and replied, "Memorable."

The Empress Dowager, her eyes and brow curved in delight, thought the Emperor was very fond of them. She pushed the plate a little closer toward him. "Would you like another piece?"

Jiang Mi's left eye twitched involuntarily. She knew full well that Xiao Huaiyan disliked sweets like this, and he would certainly refuse.

Xiao Huaiyan had been about to decline—but he caught sight of the anxious look on Jiang Mi's face and instead picked up a piece and took a bite.

His eyebrow arched slightly, and a faint surprise flashed across his expression.

He finished the entire piece unhurriedly, then took a sip of tea.

Then he looked toward Jiang Mi, and let out a soft laugh. "Miss Jiang truly has a clever way of doing things."

chapters.title: Seven
chapters.content:
That night, Xiao Huaiyan's words—"Miss Jiang truly has a clever way of doing things"—sent Jiang Mi spiraling into a nightmare.

Against her will, she was pulled back to her past life.

"The Empress Dowager has been gone for a year now. You cannot keep hiding away. What matters most is earning the Emperor's favor and bearing him an heir—that would honor the Empress Dowager's dying wish when she had the Emperor take you in."

"You have missed too many opportunities. You will need to find another way."

A dancing costume embroidered with golden peonies, draped in brilliant glass beads. The innermost garment, a breast wrap, barely covered what it needed to. Pearl fringe hung down to her navel, exposing a slender, snow-pale waist. The outer shawl was thin as a cicada's wing, concealing nothing.

The skirt was cut into four petal-like panels; with every movement, her long, straight legs showed and hid in turn.

She endured the shame and put it on, not daring to look at herself in the mirror.

She recalled the steps taught to her by the Western-region dancer, took a few tentative movements—and became a dazzling sight.

She waited anxiously all day, and at last the imperial presence arrived.

She had forgotten how she welcomed him in, and could not remember how she dismissed the servants.

In the wavering candlelight, she removed her outer wrap, revealing that exotic dancing costume beneath.

She stepped forward, bare feet against the floor, and began to move with the drumbeat.

She was too nervous to look at him.

In the spinning blur of her peripheral vision, the man drank cup after cup of wine, his expression unreadable.

Only the gaze that rested on her made her skin heat involuntarily.

Halfway through the dance, when she saw that he had done nothing but drink, she didn't know what to feel.

The pearls at her waist rustled with each movement. The man's darkened eyes fixed on that swaying cascade, then drained his cup once more. "Miss Jiang truly has a clever way of doing things."

Jiang Mi heard his words and felt a rush of joy in her heart.

He was praising her, wasn't he?

Incense thickened in the air. Emboldened, Jiang Mi attempted the backbend and leg lift the dancer had taught her—something she had practiced hard and long to master.

Her waist arched softly backward, her leg just beginning to rise—and in a blur she caught the flicker of a figure moving, then before she could react, an iron-clad grip seized her, flinging her over a shoulder, voice razor-sharp with a fury she had never heard from him before. "You truly have a death wish."

She was thrown onto the bed. The incense burner was kicked over by someone, sending smoke curling into the air, and Jiang Mi lay there, stunned... not understanding what had happened.

She had wanted to win his favor, to earn his affection—that was why she had done something so outrageous. In her imagination, the Emperor would first praise her, and then they would share tenderness together...

Though she had not spent much time with him, she had thought him mild-tempered and even-keeled. Even if he disliked her methods, at worst he would scold her a little—it shouldn't have been like...

Like... like a different person entirely.

Jiang Mi stared in terror at the shadow looming over her. Those long, narrow phoenix eyes were crimson—like a beast roused from long dormancy, finally unleashing the bloodlust it had kept contained.

The dancing costume, with its few scraps of fabric, was torn to pieces and thrown off the bed.

Outside, a gale rose and rain came crashing down, and her sobbing was swallowed by the sudden deluge.

After that night, Jiang Mi did not show her face again for half a month.

When she finally went to pay her respects at the Palace of Earthly Tranquility, other consorts made veiled remarks about her being spoiled and putting on airs.

The imperial edict elevating her rank never came. The concubines all mocked her behind her back.

Jiang Mi hid under her blankets and wept in secret. She was so frightened, so hurt...

*******

"It hurts... it hurts so much... sob, sob, sob..." The sleeping woman wept, murmuring through her tears.

"Miss, wake up... Miss, what's wrong?" Qiuyu's heart ached with worry. She had been dozing through her night watch when she heard faint crying, and started awake to find her young mistress weeping and whimpering in pain.

Qiuyu used a cloth to wipe away Jiang Mi's tears, her face torn with anxiety over whether to go and call for someone.

The thick lashes fluttered. Qiuyu saw that Jiang Mi seemed to be waking and called softly, "Miss, Miss..."

Jiang Mi's eyes opened in a daze, and for a moment she didn't know where she was.

"Miss, where does it hurt? What's wrong?" Qiuyu asked with concern.

"I..." Jiang Mi paused, brow furrowing slightly. "I... I think I have a stomachache."

Just as Jiang Mi was about to sit up, she felt a dull, cramping ache in her lower abdomen.

Qiuyu said, "This servant will go find Nanny Cui and ask for an imperial physician."

"Wait!" Jiang Mi called her back. She had noticed something, and said, embarrassed, "I... I think my monthly flow has come."

Qiuyu lifted the blanket to check, and sure enough, it was as the Miss said. She let out a sigh of relief.

They changed to fresh bedding, Qiuyu helped Jiang Mi drink a few sips of warm water, then eased her back down.

Qiuyu asked, still concerned, "Were you having a nightmare just now, Miss? Shall this servant light some calming incense?"

Jiang Mi shook her head. "No need. Qiuyu, go back to sleep."

Qiuyu saw she was insistent and relented. "This servant will be in the outer room. Call if you need anything."

Jiang Mi nodded obediently and urged Qiuyu to go to bed again.

Roused from nightmare, with a dull ache still pulsing in her abdomen, Jiang Mi had long since lost any desire to sleep.

She tossed and turned, turning over and over the meaning behind Xiao Huaiyan's words.

Had he actually eaten the osmanthus cakes she had sent to the Qianqing Palace? But he disliked sweets and detested pastries of all kinds—the cakes she sent would only have been passed along to the attendants below. And the attendants would never run to him saying how sweet and rich the cakes were; they would merely receive them, gratefully, as a reward.

If he hadn't eaten them, then why had he used that tone, that look, when he glanced at her? As though her every small scheme were laid bare before him.

That one ambiguous remark had led her aunt to believe the Emperor adored the osmanthus cakes, and to suggest sending him more.

He had neither agreed nor refused.

He had changed the subject, and before long, departed from the Palace of Compassion and Tranquility.

He came and went so freely, while she had been dragged back into that nightmare by a single sentence of his.

She had never wished so badly for the Emperor's Birthday to arrive quickly. Once it passed, her aunt would have no good reason to keep her in the palace any longer, and she could go home.

......

Outside the Qianqing Palace, Li Fu carried a sandalwood box painted with gilded cloud patterns in his hands, limping along toward the inner hall.

The hall was utterly still. The man on the imperial throne was bent over his desk, brushing through memorials—finishing one, then pulling out the next.

Li Fu bowed forward and offered up the box with both hands. In a low voice he said, "Your Majesty, this is the new medicine that Imperial Physician Gu has prepared. It is hoped that for Your Majesty's headaches..."

Xiao Huaiyan didn't even glance at it. He set down his brush, having just crossed out a name on the memorial, and said indifferently, "Leave it."

Li Fu set the box down with care and stepped to one side.

He stood there until dusk. Li Fu noticed his adopted son Chengzhong hovering at the palace entrance, pulling his head in and out—wanting to report something yet not daring to enter.

Thinking that the imperial kitchen must have sent the evening meal, Li Fu steeled himself to give a gentle reminder. "Your Majesty, it is time to dine."

Xiao Huaiyan set down his vermillion brush and glanced at the sky.

Li Fu, relieved that this one had finally listened, beckoned to Chengzhong. Chengzhong understood immediately and directed the palace maids to carry in the imperial dishes.

Xiao Huaiyan looked at the table full of fine delicacies and asked, "Did anyone send anything to the Qianqing Palace today?"

Li Fu replied, "The Noble Consort Dowager sent over some bird's nest soup, saying she had personally simmered it. This servant did not wish to disturb Your Majesty, and took the liberty of sending the bearer away." After all, without the Emperor's word, they would not dare accept any such gifts on his behalf.

Xiao Huaiyan casually picked up a piece of roasted venison. "Nothing else?"

Li Fu thought carefully, then answered with caution, "In reply to Your Majesty—it would seem... there was not."

No sooner had he spoken than Li Fu noticed the Emperor eat the venison and continue dining as usual. Yet somehow, Li Fu felt a vague unease.

Chengzhong, standing to the side, looked as though he wanted to say something but dared not.

Li Fu gave him a look, and he finally knelt forward. "There—there was one thing this servant forgot to report to Your Majesty. The Palace of Compassion and Tranquility has summoned a physician. It seems... Miss Jiang has taken ill."

A silence fell over the room. Then came a single quiet laugh.

Li Fu and Chengzhong even forgot to breathe.

......

Jiang Mi stayed in bed for five or six days before her monthly flow finally ended.

This time, it had been as bad as a full illness. Her aunt had specially summoned a physician to prescribe medicine for her, telling her to properly tend to her health.

No one mentioned sending osmanthus cakes to the Qianqing Palace again.

She was happy to have the relief, and spent her days curled up in the warm room playing with Mianmian.

At least she had Mianmian for company these past few days, which kept the boredom at bay.

Today the sun was bright, and Jiang Mi asked Qiuyu to carry the pot of Emei spring orchid outside to bask in the light.

The orchid had been a last-ditch effort to save it, but the plant turned out to be surprisingly resilient—it had actually revived, and showed signs of putting out new leaves.

To protect it from Mianmian's destructive little paws, Jiang Mi always had Qiuyu move it out of the room whenever the cat was around.

Feeling in relatively good spirits today, Jiang Mi spread out a sheet of painting paper and began working on her birthday gift.

What her aunt wanted was heartfelt thought—not rare and expensive things bought with money.

The gift she had prepared in her past life was not appropriate to offer this time around, so she could only scramble together a painting at the last minute.

Fortunately, her painting technique had not deteriorated. It would do as a gift.

Not brilliant, but passable.

Passable was enough.

As the Emperor's Birthday drew near, Jiang Mi took the painting to the main hall to have her aunt look it over. She had just walked in when she heard her aunt discussing with Nanny Cui the matter of relocating the Imperial Consorts to the country estate.

Empress Dowager Jiang made no effort to hide this from Jiang Mi. She pointed to a section of the list in the register she was holding. "These will be moved first, then the rest in batches. As for Noble Consort Dowager Xian, Lady Liu, Lady Zhang, and Lady Wu—they are of advanced age and have rendered merit by raising imperial children; they shall remain in the palace and be honored in comfort."

Jiang Mi thought of the grief-stricken woman she had glimpsed that night, wondering whether she was on the list—or whether, as Xiao Huaiyan had said, she had been sent to accompany the late Emperor in death.

Jiang Mi pretended to glance casually at the register in the Empress Dowager's hands, but she could only see part of it.

Empress Dowager Jiang was amused by her wanting-to-look-but-not-daring-to-look expression. She pinched Jiang Mi's cheek and placed the register in her hands. "If you want to look, then look."

Caught in the act, Jiang Mi smiled with a guilty flush.

She flipped through the register. The ages all skewed older, and none matched the woman she had seen.

Then the Empress Dowager drew out another register, much thinner, and handed it to Nanny Cui. "These shall be sent to the nunnery, to pray and copy scriptures on behalf of the late Emperor."

Jiang Mi only managed a quick glance, but she saw that these were all younger Imperial Consorts—the ages matched the woman from that night.

To spend the bloom of one's youth accompanying an ancient lamp and the Buddha's name?

"Auntie. Are they not going with the other consorts to the estate in Tongzhou?" Jiang Mi couldn't help but ask.

The Empress Dowager looked at her in surprise and explained, "They are still too young. If they go to the estate, there is risk of misconduct. It is better for them to spend some time at the nunnery first. Tangtang, why do you suddenly ask about this?"

Jiang Mi shook her head and held the painting tighter.

chapters.title: Eight
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi set the painting scroll on the table and slowly unrolled it.

She watched her aunt's expression with nervous care, unsure whether her aunt would be pleased with the painting.

The Empress Dowager stepped forward to examine it closely. "Searching for plum blossoms in the snow—the red plum blooming amid the white expanse, an admirable artistic conception. Among the four gentlemen—plum, orchid, bamboo, and chrysanthemum—the plum holds first place, and is also used to praise the virtue of a true gentleman."

Jiang Mi quietly let out a breath of relief.

"Only..." the Empress Dowager's expression became somewhat hesitant.

Jiang Mi's heart clenched tight. She gripped her handkerchief and asked, "Auntie, is something amiss?"

The Empress Dowager turned to look at Jiang Mi, her expression carrying a deeper meaning. "The painting is lovely, yet I feel it is somehow lacking. Tangtang, I had expected your gift on the Emperor's Birthday to be something rather more remarkable."

Jiang Mi lowered her head in shame. "Tangtang is clumsy and unaccomplished—I could not do better. I have disappointed Auntie."

The Empress Dowager patted her on the shoulder. "It's all right. The painting is already very fine. Perhaps I am asking for too much."

On her way back with the painting, Jiang Mi reflected on her aunt's words, her heart quietly heavy. In her past life, the gift she had prepared was truly something different. She had spent a long time on it, poured her heart and effort into it, practiced day and night—all to present it on the Emperor's Birthday.

But that gift would never appear in this lifetime.

On the twenty-ninth of the ninth month, the Emperor's Birthday arrived.

It was the first birthday the new Emperor celebrated after his accession—the whole empire rejoiced.

Though the new Emperor himself advocated for modest celebrations, no one inside or outside the court dared to be careless.

The palace maids were all dressed in festive colors, with warm smiles on their faces. The palace was adorned with hanging lanterns and decorations, and the atmosphere was lively and full of joy.

Jiang Mi woke early, dressed in a long scarlet begonia-print skirt, wearing a jade-green hairpin that swayed with each step, and diamond-shaped earrings. She quickly ate a few bites of the breakfast Qiuyu brought, then hurried to the main hall of the Palace of Compassion and Tranquility.

Today, the wives and daughters of noble families both inside and outside the palace would come to the Palace of Compassion and Tranquility to pay their respects. The Empress Dowager would hold a banquet to receive the female guests. After the Emperor had celebrated with his court officials in the front hall, he would come to pay his respects to the Empress Dowager.

That was when the gift-presenting ceremony would take place.

When Jiang Mi arrived at the flower hall, some of the Imperial Consorts and imperial clanswomen were already waiting. Jiang Mi stepped forward to pay her respects and waited with the consorts for the Empress Dowager to emerge.

The Empress Dowager entered in formal court robes, wearing the phoenix crown, dignified and stately.

She rested her left hand on Nanny Cui's arm and walked in slowly. The ladies in the flower hall rose and bowed in unison.

After the inner court ladies finished their greetings, the outer court ladies filed in and paid their respects in turn.

Jiang Mi followed the Empress Dowager around, meeting round after round of noble wives and daughters, until her head spun. She focused on greeting them properly.

At last she spotted her great-uncle's wife, Lady Chen of the Marquis Chengen's household, arriving with her second elder sister Jiang Yi—but there was no chance to speak.

The banquet was still some time away, and the Empress Dowager was busy chatting with the wives of the imperial clan and noble families. Seeing that Jiang Mi had nothing to do, she told her to go and rest first.

Jiang Mi felt a wave of relief and was about to go look for her second sister Jiang Yi, but with so many ladies in attendance, she looked around and couldn't find her—Jiang Yi had been pulled away by one of her close friends to admire the flowers.

Some unfamiliar young ladies approached to make conversation. After exchanging pleasantries with them for a while, Jiang Mi realized she was hungry and decided to quietly slip back to the warm room for a bite to eat.

Qiuyu had stayed behind in the warm room to look after Mianmian rather than following Jiang Mi to the main hall. When she saw Jiang Mi return, she looked surprised. "Miss, why are you back so soon?"

Jiang Mi: "The banquet is still a while away, and I won't be able to eat properly once it starts. Bring out the osmanthus dumplings—I'll have a little something first."

"Yes, Miss." Qiuyu put down what she was doing and went to the small kitchen.

Jiang Mi glanced over at the nest she'd made for Mianmian. The little creature was lying on its back, stomach facing the sky, basking in utter contentment.

That look of lazy bliss made Jiang Mi envious. She reached over and kneaded Mianmian's belly. Mianmian rolled over and rubbed against her hand with a soft meow.

Jiang Mi smiled and picked it up, setting it on her lap as she stroked its fluffy fur. "You really do love being pampered."

Mianmian settled down for a moment, then jumped off Jiang Mi's lap and trotted toward the door.

"Mianmian, come back!" Jiang Mi stood up and called after it.

The small white cat paused its steps at the doorway, lingering for a moment, and looked back at Jiang Mi.

Jiang Mi followed, and saw a little girl of about eleven or twelve standing outside the door. She had gathered her skirt and was carefully crouching beside Mianmian, reaching out to pet it—but Mianmian quickly dodged away.

The white kitten extended a paw, gave it a lick, and padded back to Jiang Mi's feet, curling up beside her with lazy ease.

The little girl was adorable as carved jade. Her round eyes were fixed intently on Mianmian. She looked up at Jiang Mi, startled for a moment by her face. "Fairy sister, may I play with it?"

The little girl's voice was bright and clear, with a natural endearing sweetness. The quality of her clothes and adornments made it plain at a glance that she was from a prominent official's family.

Jiang Mi couldn't help but smile. "Which family's young miss are you? What brings you all the way out here?"

The little girl's eyes swirled around in thought. "I—I am my grandmother's granddaughter. I got separated from my grandmother. Sister, may I pet the cat?"

Seeing she had no intention of giving her family name, Jiang Mi didn't press further.

Jiang Mi picked up Mianmian and beckoned the little girl over. She took the little girl's hand and gently guided it across Mianmian's back. When Mianmian didn't reject the touch, she said, "It's a bit timid—at first, it can be a little wary of strangers. Take it slowly; it's actually very gentle."

The little girl, following Jiang Mi's guidance, kept talking softly to Mianmian while carefully reaching out to touch one of its small paws.

Mianmian, settled in Jiang Mi's arms, was mildly annoyed by the little girl's pestering—but it didn't run away.

When Qiuyu came in carrying the osmanthus dumplings, this was the scene she walked in on.

She exclaimed, "Miss, who is this?"

Jiang Mi glanced at the little girl. "Consider her a small guest."

When Qiuyu set the osmanthus dumplings before Jiang Mi, the little girl caught the fragrance and swallowed, asking shyly, "Sister... may I taste some?"

Jiang Mi would not have finished the bowl on her own. She ladled out a small portion for the little girl.

The little girl smiled until her eyes curved like the crescent moon. "Sister, you are so kind."

Once they had eaten their fill, they went out with Mianmian to play for a while. Then Jiang Mi decided she had better take the little girl back to the main hall.

The little girl had just gotten acquainted with Mianmian and was very reluctant to leave, her eyes lingering on Mianmian as the cat batted at a bamboo ball.

......

Not far away, two elegantly dressed women were walking in this direction, surrounded by palace maids.

Princess Anyang stopped in her tracks and said to the person beside her, "Wait—isn't that Jiang Mi?"

Xie Mingshan looked over and nodded.

Anyang suddenly smiled. "Ashan, come with me. I'm going to go and settle a score for you!"

......

Jiang Mi was just about to tell Qiuyu to take Mianmian back, when a figure suddenly strode up and delivered a sharp kick at the small white cat crouching on the grass.

Before the little creature could even register what had happened, it let out a piercing wail.

Jiang Mi flew forward. Mianmian struggled to its feet, its fur standing on end, limping on three legs and crying out to Jiang Mi.

Jiang Mi pulled Mianmian into her arms, her heart clenching with anguish.

She glared up at the person before her. "Princess Anyang—why did you hurt Mianmian?!"

Anyang sneered, "Tsk, you even gave that beast a name! It's your little beast that was blocking the way. I haven't even said anything yet, and you're already being disrespectful to me?"

"You were the one who kicked Mianmian! The path is so wide—Mianmian was just sitting there playing with a bamboo ball. Such a tiny little thing couldn't possibly block anyone's way! You kicked it on purpose!" The little girl, who had been stunned into frozen shock, suddenly seemed to snap back to herself. She was standing closest to Anyang, and in her fury, she reached out and shoved her.

Caught off guard, Anyang stumbled back several steps.

"How dare you! Who do you think you are, to push a princess?! Someone, seize her!" Having said this, Anyang raised her hand to slap the girl.

The little girl had never faced anything like this before; she was paralyzed with fright and didn't know how to dodge.

Just as the slap was about to land, Jiang Mi stepped in front of the little girl, grabbing Anyang's wrist with all her strength.

Jiang Mi was furious beyond measure. "Princess Anyang, every noble lady here today is a guest come to celebrate the Emperor's Birthday. Moreover, we are in the Palace of Compassion and Tranquility. Even if someone is to be punished, it must go through the Empress Dowager. You cannot carry out private punishment on your own authority!"

Her words only fanned Anyang's fury higher. "Disrespecting me, defying your superior! Jiang Mi, don't think I won't strike you too!"

"Oh? So capable now, are you?"

A faintly cool male voice came from behind the crowd.

chapters.title: Nine
chapters.content:
Anyang's hand was flung violently away. She spun around, her expression faltering. "Your—Your Imperial Highness, Imperial Brother."

Jiang Mi had used all her strength to block that blow. When her arm fell back to her side, it was already trembling faintly.

She turned around. The young Emperor stood in golden dragon robes, his features exquisitely handsome, like a bright moon hanging in the heavens—every gesture radiating the authority of one born to rule.

Walking beside him was an elegant, dignified elderly woman who appeared to be in her early fifties.

At a single glance, Jiang Mi recognized her—she was the late Emperor's eldest sister, Grand Princess Zhaoyang.

Jiang Mi lowered her eyes and curtsied along with the others.

She didn't forget to tug at the terrified little girl beside her, guiding her to bow as well.

Then, unexpectedly, the little girl broke away and ran straight toward the imperial procession, crying as she ran, breathlessly calling out, "Grandmother! Grandmother!"

The little girl threw herself into the arms of the elderly woman standing beside the Emperor. She pointed a finger at Anyang and, between sobs, cried out, "Grandmother! Grandmother! You must stand up for Zhuzhu! She was going to hit me! She was going to hit me and Jiang sister... wuwuwuwu!"

With that tearful outburst, the little girl's identity was revealed.

To wail and cry so freely in the presence of the Grand Princess, she was surely a child cherished beyond measure at home.

Grand Princess Zhaoyang had, after her marriage to the old Duke of Zhen Guo, led a life of quiet seclusion. The current Duke of Zhen Guo was her eldest son. His wife had delivered their youngest daughter prematurely, and that youngest daughter had been frail from birth, spending her childhood following the Grand Princess to Jiangnan. For the Emperor's Birthday, the Grand Princess had finally brought this beloved little granddaughter back to the capital.

The Grand Princess had at first only heard some raised voices ahead of her, and had not imagined that her own granddaughter could be caught up in it.

She drew the little girl close and apologized to the Emperor at her side. "My granddaughter is unacquainted with proper conduct—I beg Your Majesty's forgiveness."

She then guided the little girl to bow.

Xue Ningzhu, tears still clinging to her face, followed her grandmother's lead and curtsied.

Xiao Huaiyan paid it little mind. He glanced briefly at Jiang Mi standing in the distance with a white cat in her arms, and said mildly, "All of you, rise."

Only now did Princess Anyang understand the identity of the little girl. Seeing the inscrutable expression on her imperial brother's face, she grew increasingly uneasy in her heart.

She had not spent much time around Grand Princess Zhaoyang—that long-removed great-aunt—but she had heard her late mother's consort say that even the late Emperor had shown that lady three parts deference.

By her imperial brother's nature, he would surely respect this elder aunt as well.

Anyang cursed herself inwardly. Had she only known, she would have waited for another day to go looking for trouble with Jiang Mi. If she had, she would not have invited this disaster upon herself.

Grand Princess Zhaoyang soothed her aggrieved granddaughter, inwardly displeased with Anyang—but as the Emperor had not yet spoken, she could not presume to overstep her bounds.

"Anyang—is what the Xue girl said true?" Xiao Huaiyan asked.

Hearing that her imperial brother's tone was still relatively mild, Anyang quietly let out a breath.

She thought: he hadn't actually landed a blow—Jiang Mi had blocked it. She could easily claim it was a moment of loose talk, an unintended lapse, and no real harm was done.

The worst that would happen was offering an apology to the Grand Princess and letting the matter pass quietly.

She raised her head, just about to reply—when she saw that the imperial brother who usually greeted people with warm and gracious ease now wore an expression of unmistakable, inexplicable distance.

Inexplicably, she felt an urge to retreat.

"I—I was only joking with them..."

Xue Ningzhu refused to accept this. She stamped her feet in indignation. "You're lying! You were the one who came charging out and kicked Jiang sister's Mianmian—and then you wanted to hit me too! If Jiang sister hadn't shielded me, I would have been struck by you!"

Anyang glared furiously at that little girl. Such impudence. If not for her imperial brother and the Grand Princess standing there, she would teach her a proper lesson...

Xie Mingshan, standing behind Princess Anyang, was debating whether to speak up in the princess's defense. But she had seen full well that Anyang had picked the fight herself—she herself had stood on the sidelines and watched. If she vouched for Anyang now and the Emperor turned his anger on her, she would be dragged into it too. And she still had a gift to present at the banquet; she couldn't afford to give the Emperor a poor first impression.

But if she stayed silent, and her aunt later found out—would she be blamed for it?

Xie Mingshan kept her head lowered, torn between two paths.

She noticed a corner of imperial gold moving in her direction, and her breath caught; she couldn't suppress a surge of excitement.

But almost immediately, that flash of gold swept straight past her and came to a stop in front of Jiang Mi.

This was the second time Xiao Huaiyan had seen the cat in her arms, and every time it was worse off than before. This time it was huddled in her embrace, trembling, one leg bent at an unnatural angle. Her slender, pale hands were moving gently over it in comfort.

Just as he had done that night, Xiao Huaiyan reached out and scratched the cat under its chin. "Poor little thing."

No one in the crowd could quite read his meaning.

As Xiao Huaiyan withdrew his hand, he brushed against the back of Jiang Mi's hand—unintentionally. Jiang Mi's arm hair stood on end instantly.

Jiang Mi quietly rubbed her fingers against Mianmian's back.

Xiao Huaiyan turned and addressed the room. "Anyang—do you know what you have done wrong?"

Anyang was taken aback. Her imperial brother hadn't even questioned Jiang Mi yet—why was he already demanding she acknowledge her wrongdoing?

She had only kicked a cat. What was there for her to admit to?

Anyang was evasive, reluctant to concede. "It was the cat that ran at me and frightened me—I only pushed it aside. Imperial Brother, I only said a few foolish things in a moment of anger..."

With every word she spoke, she felt less sure of herself under her imperial brother's still, calm gaze.

The observing Grand Princess shook her head in disappointment. She had not expected a princess of the imperial household to be raised in such a manner.

Xiao Huaiyan gave a brief smile. He had given her a chance. Since she did not wish to take it, he had no desire to waste further time.

"Princess Anyang, showing no remorse, bullying the weak, and conducting herself with arrogance and insolence. Confined to quarters for three months—return her to the Palace of Eternal Spring at once."

The moment his words fell, Li Fu directed the eunuchs and palace maids behind him to step forward and "escort" Princess Anyang back to her palace.

"Imperial Brother! Imperial Brother! I don't want three months of confinement—I know I was wrong! Imperial Brother, Imperial Brother, I won't dare again, I know I was wrong! Please take back your command! Imperial Brother!!" Anyang was struck with panic. She shoved aside the palace maids closing in around her, desperately trying to plead for mercy and beg her brother to lighten the punishment.

Li Fu stood in her path and said with cordial politeness, "Your Highness the Princess, please do not provoke His Majesty's anger further. If the Embroidered Uniform Guard must be summoned later, the situation will be even more undignified."

Catching sight of the fierce-looking Embroidered Uniform Guards behind the Emperor, Anyang dared not cause any more trouble. She could do nothing but be led away by the palace maids.

Xie Mingshan was so frightened she went pale as a sheet. She had expected the Emperor to give Anyang a verbal scolding at most and have her apologize to the Grand Princess, and then the matter would be over. She had never imagined the punishment would be this severe—to have no regard for her aunt's dignity on such a grand and festive occasion. How was Anyang going to face anyone from now on?

After Anyang was taken away, the surroundings went eerily quiet.

Xiao Huaiyan turned his jade ring idly in his hand and said to Grand Princess Zhaoyang, "Does Elder Aunt feel this punishment is too harsh?"

Grand Princess Zhaoyang deliberated and replied, "Too much temper in a young woman needs tempering. Your Majesty is wise."

In her heart, the Grand Princess knew this penalty was heavy for Anyang. What Anyang had done could have been treated as minor or serious, and she had expected the Emperor to smooth things over out of consideration for Noble Consort Dowager Xian. She had not expected such a strict ruling.

Xiao Huaiyan said, "The banquet at Taihe Hall is in progress, and I must go. Honored Aunt may go first to the Palace of Compassion and Tranquility, and I will come to pay my respects during the feast."

Grand Princess Zhaoyang nodded. "This subject respectfully sends off Your Majesty."

Jiang Mi curtsied along with the others.

After Xiao Huaiyan's entourage departed, those who remained in the spot visibly breathed a collective sigh of relief.

Xie Mingshan said her farewells to the Grand Princess and hurried off to find Noble Consort Dowager Xian.

Grand Princess Zhaoyang studied Jiang Mi with a careful, appraising eye. She could not deny that the Empress Dowager's niece was an extraordinarily beautiful young woman—her bearing graceful and measured. Truly a rare and stunning beauty.

"Today Zhuzhu was fortunate enough to receive your protection—and did not come to harm. I will not forget this kindness."

Jiang Mi replied with alarm, "I would not dare claim credit. This is what a subject's daughter ought to do."

The Grand Princess invited Jiang Mi to come to the banquet together, but Jiang Mi declined, saying she would make her own way there shortly.

The Grand Princess did not insist, and departed with Xue Ningzhu.

Once everyone had gone, Jiang Mi looked down at Mianmian in her arms—and only then did her eyes quietly fill with tears.

They fell drop by drop onto Mianmian's fur. "I'm sorry, Mianmian. You'll have to hold on a little longer. Auntie is hosting a banquet at the Palace of Compassion and Tranquility right now and has no time to spare for us, so I can't trouble her about getting a physician. In a little while, Qiuyu will take you back and give you some goat's milk—will that be all right?" Jiang Mi was overcome with guilt. If only she hadn't brought Mianmian out, perhaps it wouldn't have had to suffer this.

Mianmian seemed to sense her feelings. It let out a small, soft meow and licked her fingers.

Not far away, the person who should have left long ago stood in silence, watching.

The girl cried in the quietest, most muted way imaginable—and only allowed her tears to fall after everyone else had gone.

A teardrop trembled at the tip of her pointed chin, looking fragile enough to shatter.

The man rubbed his fingers together again, and turned to call out to the side, "Chengzhong."

Chengzhong proved himself remarkably perceptive. "This servant will go at once and take Qiuyu to find an imperial physician."

chapters.title: Ten
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi carefully lowered Mianmian into Qiuyu's arms, gave it a gentle pat on the head, and said, "Be good. Once you're better, I'll add extra dried fish for you."

"Miss, don't worry—this servant will take good care of Mianmian. Go on to the banquet now, don't be late." Qiuyu said with concern. Today was the Emperor's Birthday banquet, and so many noble wives and daughters from prominent families had come. As the Empress Dowager's own niece, everyone's eyes would be on her—if she arrived late, people would talk.

Jiang Mi knew she couldn't delay any longer. She tidied herself up and hurried off toward the main hall.

Qiuyu watched until Jiang Mi disappeared from sight, then looked back down, meaning to take Mianmian and head inside first.

She had only taken a few steps when she heard someone call out to her from behind.

Qiuyu turned and saw a round-faced little eunuch she vaguely recognized come running up.

"Qiuyu, there's a physician in the imperial medical office who's on shift today and can treat this little cat's injuries—come with me!"

Qiuyu recognized him now. Was this not Chengzhong, the eunuch who had been attending the Emperor earlier?

......

By the time Jiang Mi arrived at the banquet hall, the entire chamber was nearly full.

Fortunately, her aunt had not yet arrived, so she was not considered late.

A palace maid guided Jiang Mi to the Marquis Chengen household's seating area, where she found the Marquis Chengen's wife, Lady Chen, and Second Elder Sister Jiang Yi.

"Great-aunt's wife, Second Sister."

The Marchioness smiled and took Jiang Mi's hand. "Come, sit down. What kept you so long?"

Jiang Mi replied, "I was held up along the way."

"No matter—the meal hasn't started yet. Better late than never." The Marchioness moved to reassure her first.

Jiang Yi leaned over with a grin. "Mother really does play favorites. If I were the late one, she'd have no end of things to say! But Third Sister gets away with it—and even gets the best seats ready and waiting."

The Marchioness raised a hand and lightly swatted Jiang Yi. "What nonsense you talk!"

Jiang Mi's seat was level with the Marchioness, while Jiang Yi's was placed one row behind. Jiang Mi knew this was her aunt's arrangement—the front row was more conspicuous.

Many daughters and mothers from noble families were also seated in the front row. Jiang Mi swept her gaze across the room, and her eyes happened to meet those of a young woman seated across the hall.

The girl looked to be around fifteen. She was refined in appearance, with a scholarly air. When she realized Jiang Mi had noticed her looking, she quickly averted her gaze, her expression going slightly awkward—the kind of guileless embarrassment of a well-behaved girl suddenly caught in the act.

From the person seated beside the girl, Jiang Mi worked out her identity.

This was Grand Secretary Shen's fourth daughter, Shen Yaowei.

In her past life, that man had left the empress's seat vacant—waiting for her to come out of mourning.

At the time, she had envied this young woman from within the imperial harem. Shen Yaowei's elder sister was the betrothed the late Emperor had once chosen for the current Emperor. Though that second Miss Shen had passed away young, the Emperor still showed favor to the Shen family, and even held the position of Empress open for them.

The world said the Emperor was a man of deep affection and loyalty.

Jiang Mi couldn't help but give a silent snort. All those eyes in the world must have gone blind.

It was fortunate that she had died early enough in her past life—she didn't have to watch this Miss Shen take her place as mistress of the central palace.

She looked further around the room. Among the assembled guests she could see quite a few familiar faces from the harem of her past life. Some now sat near the front, others in the middle or toward the back—all seated beside family, all bashful and demure, stealing glances toward the door and waiting for that man to appear.

In this lifetime, she had no desire to call any of them sister again. Thinking of those unbearable days only strengthened her resolve to never enter the palace. She had to find a way to persuade her aunt, and quickly.

While Jiang Mi was lost in thought, a eunuch's voice rang out beyond the hall: "Noble Consort Dowager Xian has arrived!"

Everyone in the hall rose to pay their respects.

Noble Consort Dowager Xian wore her hair in a high cloud coiffure, dressed in a trailing crimson gown embroidered with a phoenix ascending through clouds, crowned with a double-phoenix hairpin studded with red gems. As she passed by Jiang Mi, there was a sharpness in her gaze.

Xie Mingshan, who had come with Noble Consort Dowager Xian, took a seat at the Xie Ducal household's place.

A short while later, the announcement rang out again: "The Empress Dowager and Grand Princess Zhaoyang have arrived!"

Everyone rose again and bowed deeply—not even Noble Consort Dowager Xian was exempt.

The Empress Dowager settled into the place of honor. Grand Princess Zhaoyang took the first seat on the left.

The Empress Dowager raised her hand. "All may be seated."

The ladies all returned to their seats.

"Let the feast begin!"

Musical instruments rang out, and the arranged performances took the stage one by one.

There were street performers and acrobats from among the common folk, their acts breathtaking one moment and warmly amusing the next, drawing laughter from the ladies without end.

Beautiful dancing girls also performed, their long sleeves swaying as they turned and dipped in graceful dance.

The banquet was in full swing, with song and dance everywhere to look.

Fruit wine paired with the fine dishes made it hard to resist refilling one's cup.

Young ladies who had been stiff at first gradually loosened up, leaning in to whisper to one another.

Some noble daughters had only sipped a little water, their expressions tense, waiting their turn to present their talents.

Jiang Mi picked up another grape and ate it, feeling rather at ease. Her painting was only a formality—she would roll it out, let the birthday honoree take a look, and then have it put away. After that, she could step down.

She noticed Xie Mingshan looking as though she were about to face a great ordeal, and could tell she had poured a great deal of effort into her performance—hoping, no doubt, to dazzle that man.

The musical instruments fell silent. The dancers withdrew from the stage.

"His Majesty the Emperor has arrived!"

The announcement sent a ripple of excitement through the whole banquet.

Jiang Mi hurriedly swallowed the grape still in her mouth, wiped her hands with a cloth, and rose along with the rest to bow.

She had eaten too quickly—she accidentally choked a little. She didn't dare make a sound, and held back until tears pricked her eyes.

By the time the uncomfortable sensation passed, the Emperor was already seated on the imperial throne.

Having shared a few drinks with the court officials at the Hall of Heavenly Purity, the Emperor's phoenix eyes were slightly flushed at the corners, lending his already striking face an even more breathtaking quality.

The young ladies in the room blushed.

Xiao Huaiyan was listening with a pleasant expression to the words of an imperial clan elder. The jade ring on his finger, however, turned faster and faster.

He cast an unobtrusive glance toward where Jiang Mi was sitting. He saw her quietly raise a cloth and press it to the corner of her eye.

Still not recovered? How fragile.

Xiao Huaiyan patiently endured the compliments being offered at his side, raised his cup, and drank with the company.

Noble Consort Dowager Xian chose this moment to propose the gift-presenting ceremony.

Everyone knew full well that so-called gift-presenting was simply a chance for each family's young ladies to make themselves seen before the Emperor.

Though the Empress Dowager had no fondness for Noble Consort Dowager Xian, she permitted the suggestion.

Xiao Huaiyan was indifferent to it either way. He inclined his head in approval.

Noble Consort Dowager Xian immediately signaled to Xie Mingshan with a look.

Xie Mingshan composed herself and stepped out from her seat with grace, curtsying. "This subject's daughter has little talent, yet she would like to offer Your Majesty a piece of music. May the Emperor live ten thousand years and enjoy good health and peace."

Eunuchs quickly brought in a guqin and set it in place.

Xie Mingshan settled herself gracefully before it, her slender fingers drawing across the fine strings—and the sound that arose was like a clear spring cascading down a snowy mountain, cool and pleasant to the ear.

It was invigorating as a spring breeze, and when the melody quickened, it became like sparrows at play—lively and bright, making all who heard it glad.

Even the Grand Princess, who had not been well-disposed toward Xie Mingshan, could not but concede that with this performance, she had surpassed all the other young ladies.

When the piece ended and Xie Mingshan rose, a wave of praise followed.

One of the noble wives on good terms with the Xie Ducal family called out, "Miss Xie is indeed the foremost talent in the capital—her qin artistry is every bit as renowned as its reputation!"

Many voices echoed the sentiment.

The other young ladies who had planned to play the guqin hung their heads in defeat and silently abandoned any thought of stepping forward. With skills inferior to hers, offering another guqin performance would only invite ridicule upon themselves.

What Xie Mingshan most hoped to hear was the evaluation from the man on the imperial throne.

But even after the next young lady presented ninety-nine scrolls of scripture, she still had not received any particular words of praise from him.

He wore a faint smile, and while he appeared reasonably pleased with all the gifts from the noble daughters, none of them seemed to have truly caught his eye.

Jiang Mi saw Nanny Cui walk over to her side and knew it was her turn to go forward with the painting.

She rose from her seat and took the painting scroll from the palace maid.

Jiang Mi was just about to enter the banquet hall when, for some reason, her heart gave a sudden lurch.

She stopped in her tracks, looked down at the scroll in her hands—and knew at once something was wrong.

Along what should have been a clean white border, there were dark, layered blotches.

She hastily unrolled the scroll, and found that her painting—Searching for Plum Blossoms in the Snow on a Southern River—had been blotted and ruined by a large splash of ink. It was completely unpresentable.

Nanny Cui's face went white as a sheet. The palace maid who had brought the painting up was already on the floor in a trembling heap.

The gift-presenting ceremony was moments away, and the painting had been destroyed.

There was no time to prepare anything else.

At that very moment, Grand Secretary Shen's daughter had just presented an ancient chess manual. It had been an incomplete copy, but she and her grandfather had worked together to restore what was missing.

Xiao Huaiyan had it brought before him, leafed through it, and said, "Grand Secretary Shen is thoughtful."

Shen Yaowei lowered her head and stepped back.

Noble Consort Dowager Xian noticed that Xiao Huaiyan had offered a rare word of praise—and though he had addressed Grand Secretary Shen, it was equally a compliment to this young Miss Shen.

She felt an inner restlessness rising. The Emperor had just confined her Anyang to quarters—she had hoped that Ashan's performance would make a favorable impression on the Emperor and give her an opening to plead for mercy on Anyang's behalf. But the Emperor had seemed largely unmoved by the guqin piece, treating all the young ladies' gifts with equal, measured regard.

Noble Consort Dowager Xian sipped at her wine and glanced toward the Empress Dowager. Then, with a deliberate air, she spoke up: "How strange—I don't see Miss Jiang at her seat anymore. Could she be preparing to present a gift? I wonder what kind of surprise Miss Jiang has in store for the Emperor!"

chapters.title: Eleven
chapters.content:
"We'll know soon enough—why the hurry?" The Empress Dowager's tone was unhurried, a serene smile on her lips, as though she already knew exactly how everything would unfold.

Noble Consort Dowager Xian felt as though she had swallowed her own words. She smoothed her expression, stroked the vivid crimson lacquer on her fingernails, and curled the corner of her lips. "Then I shall wait and see."

Xiao Huaiyan poured himself a cup of wine. A faint smile rested on his face, but those long, narrow phoenix eyes were as dark and fathomless as an abyss. He slid the jade ring off his thumb and began toying with it in his hand—a gesture that, unlike his usual composure, carried a subtle, uncharacteristic restlessness.

Li Fu, attending close at hand, felt his eyelid twitch. Surely His Majesty's head ailment wasn't flaring up? He quietly drew a small sandalwood case from his sleeve and edged it toward the Emperor, debating whether to urge him to take his medicine.

Xiao Huaiyan glanced at him sideways. Li Fu was so startled he hastily tucked the sandalwood case back up his sleeve.

"The Marquis Chengen household presents its gift!" The ceremonial announcement rang out again.

Xiao Huaiyan gave the jade ring a couple of idle spins and put it back on his thumb.

The Empress Dowager appeared outwardly composed, but the way she had quietly straightened in her seat betrayed her true feelings.

Jiang Mi should not have been gone so long—what had happened? She had only just sent Qingxue out to look for her when the announcement rang from outside...

Noble Consort Dowager Xian leaned forward, her eyes bright with anticipation, eager to watch the spectacle unfold.

Over a hundred pairs of eyes in the great hall turned toward Jiang Mi as she walked in.

The Empress Dowager's brow furrowed. Why did Tangtang not have the painting in her hands? Her hands were completely empty!

Noble Consort Dowager Xian could barely suppress a laugh. She remarked mockingly, "Miss Jiang comes before us empty-handed—surely she does not intend to recite a poem for His Majesty?"

At that moment, Jiang Mi was doing everything in her power to steady herself.

She curtsied toward the man on the dragon throne. "This subject's daughter offers her congratulations to His Majesty on this auspicious occasion, and wishes the Emperor a reign of ten thousand years and the empire ever-lasting prosperity."

Xiao Huaiyan raised his eyes, his tone unhurried. "Rise."

Once Jiang Mi had risen and composed herself, she turned to Noble Consort Dowager Xian and replied, "Noble Consort Dowager has guessed wrong. Like Miss Xie, this subject's daughter will offer His Majesty a piece of music."

Her words immediately set off a ripple of whispered exchanges among the guests.

With such a brilliant performance already before them, was this Jiang girl not afraid of the stark comparison—of making a fool of herself?

No one had ever heard that the Third Young Miss of the Marquis Chengen household was particularly accomplished at the qin!

"However, before I play, I will need to borrow Miss Xie's qin."

These words gave everyone pause. A qin is a personal instrument—only one's own, familiar qin could produce music in true harmony with the player. For Miss Jiang to set aside her own qin and borrow Miss Xie's instead, it was hard not to suspect that something had gone wrong with hers.

On an occasion of such great importance, a damaged qin could only have been the work of deliberate sabotage.

The dark intrigues of the imperial palace were not something to be aired openly at a time like this—whatever the reason, it would only dampen the Emperor's mood. Borrowing the qin, then, was a way of smoothing things over.

Yet everyone still wondered: even with Miss Xie's qin in hand, could Miss Jiang truly manage?

Xie Mingshan, though inwardly deeply reluctant, had no choice but to affect generosity and offer the qin.

Jiang Mi sat quietly before the instrument, her thoughts churning beneath a still surface. She had not imagined she would ever play this piece again in this lifetime.

And yet—the piece she had never been able to let him hear in her past life was, against all expectation, about to be played for him now.

Jiang Mi closed her eyes and cleared her mind of every other thought. The music, embedded in her very bones, seemed to possess a memory of its own—responding to her intent as naturally as breath.

A pair of slender, graceful hands rested upon the strings. The first notes slipped free—soft and lingering, reminiscent of a southern folk melody, winding and gentle. It was lovely, but not yet extraordinary; not quite the kind that made eyes widen with sudden wonder. Compared to what Miss Xie had played moments before, it seemed a touch subdued. Some in the audience began to exchange skeptical glances.

The Empress Dowager's brow tightened, her expression grave.

Noble Consort Dowager Xian was inwardly triumphant. At last, some satisfaction. Once word of today spread, Jiang Mi would forever be overshadowed by Ashan. To come out and embarrass herself when she couldn't even match the other's skill—how utterly presumptuous.

Xue Ningzhu was nestled against the Grand Princess, murmuring softly, "Jiang sister is so pretty, her hands on the qin look like flowers, Jiang sister's playing sounds so much lovelier..."

The Grand Princess shook her head with a quiet, affectionate laugh. Truly the words of a child.

Xiao Huaiyan's expression remained unchanged. He poured a cup of wine and drank it down.

Then, gradually, the music began to shift. The tone, which had been tender and wistful as two lovers reluctant to part, suddenly turned mournful—clinging, longing, threaded with something like hope.

The melody grew slowly bleak and urgent. Faint echoes of a war horn rose within it, and the cry of warhorses charging through the dark.

The music soared—bold, turbulent, capricious as storm clouds. Now it was harrowing; now it fell still. It conjured the brutal, desperate struggle of the battlefield, where life and death hung on the narrowest thread.

Then the qin fell quiet by degrees, as if after the fury of battle, exhausted soldiers found rest in the quiet harbor of deep night—gazing toward the far horizon with a weary, enduring hope...

The audience was swept along as though they had lived through it themselves: the parting from family and beloved ones, the long march to war, the clashing of arms in defense of hearth and home... Enemy forces driven back again and again, territory held, the nation's honor upheld. And in the moment of exhaustion, a soldier looks up at the moon hanging over the battlefield and thinks of the one who loves him and waits for him in a distant home—and drifts into a dream of warmth and peace.

In the great hall, the music wound about the rafters. Those lost in the spell of the qin held their wine cups, staring into nothing; others quietly pressed their sleeves to their eyes.

The new Emperor had built his empire from Yunzhou, and most who had followed him were military men. During the late Emperor's reign, foreign tribes had invaded more than once—and each time, it was the current Emperor who led the armies out and drove them back until they dared not strike again. Those victories had been won, yes, but who truly knew the danger and hardship they had cost?

Many of the new nobility who had come to the banquet today were the families of soldiers who had earned their titles through merit in battle. They had fathers and brothers stationed at the frontier, months and years passing without a reunion. With every conflict, they lived in dread. This music gave voice to all of it.

The last note fell. The great hall went utterly still.

The Emperor brought his palms together in a slow, deliberate applause, breaking the silence.

Grand Princess Zhaoyang followed with warm praise. "Miss Jiang is so young, and yet she can pour her very soul into the music—that is something no mere technical skill can replicate. Truly exceptional."

Empress Dowager Jiang's expression softened, and she smiled. "You flatter her—Tangtang could never deserve such praise!"

Anyone could see that however modest the Empress Dowager's words were, the pride and satisfaction in her eyes was something she could not have hidden if she tried.

Grand Princess Zhaoyang said, "If Miss Jiang does not deserve it, I dare say no one else in this hall does either. Otherwise, why would His Majesty have applauded? Your Majesty—do you not agree?"

Xiao Huaiyan smiled. "Indeed, very fine."

"Then surely a reward is in order?" Only the Grand Princess could offer such a remark so naturally—and only she was the right person to say it.

Xiao Huaiyan considered for a moment, then looked down at the young woman below. "I have a qin—'Green Ripple'—which I shall bestow upon Miss Jiang. From now on, Miss Jiang shall have no need to borrow anyone else's instrument."

Green Ripple! The ancient qin, Green Ripple!

Xie Mingshan could not believe what she had heard. The Emperor had offered not a single word of praise after she played—and now he was gifting Jiang Mi with Green Ripple?!

She refused to accept it. On what grounds? On what grounds did Jiang Mi deserve this? Jealousy and resentment surged together in her chest, and she had never hated another person so fiercely.

Noble Consort Dowager Xian had long since lost any trace of her earlier smugness. Her expression darkened to something frightening, and one of her vividly painted long fingernails snapped cleanly in half under the force of her grip.

Jiang Mi herself, having just finished playing, had not yet fully surfaced from the emotion of the music. When she heard the Emperor had bestowed the ancient qin Green Ripple upon her, she too was taken aback.

She remembered that in her past life, Consort Rou had asked for it several times, and he had refused each time.

Jiang Mi expressed her gratitude and returned to her seat.

The gift-presenting ceremony continued, but most of the audience had little attention left for it—they were still adrift in the beautiful music they had just heard.

Particularly among the wives of military officials, hushed conversations buzzed around the room: "Now that—that is music such as angels hear in heaven. How often does one encounter it here on earth? Wonderful, truly wonderful!"

"A thousand times more moving than that soft, gentle piece before it!"

"Miss Xie may have skill, but against Miss Jiang's performance, she is thoroughly outclassed. That title of 'first talent in the capital' won't hold much longer!"

"What a crushing victory. Extraordinary!"

......

The Emperor did not linger—he still had to return to the Hall of Heavenly Purity—so he departed first.

With his departure, the gift-presenting ceremony concluded quickly as well.

When the banquet finally broke up, Xue Ningzhu was reluctant to leave. She tugged at the Grand Princess's sleeve and asked, "Grandmother, I want to play with Jiang sister—can't she come home with us?"

The Grand Princess laughed despite herself. "Your Jiang sister can't leave the palace yet. When the time comes, send her an invitation and ask her to come visit you at the residence."

The Duchess of Zhen Guo stood to one side and said with mild exasperation, "Zhuzhu, your elder cousin the heir is waiting at the palace gate. If you dawdle any longer, all those snacks he bought—I'll confiscate every last one."

Xue Ningzhu instantly perked up. She seized the Grand Princess's hand and pulled her toward the palanquin. "Grandmother, quickly, quickly! I must go see Elder Cousin! Mother, don't you dare confiscate them—Elder Cousin promised he'd bring me something delicious!"

chapters.title: Twelve
chapters.content:
The banquet dispersed. Empress Dowager Jiang reclined in her chaise longue with an air of weariness, eyes closed in quiet rest.

Jiang Mi stood nearby, holding the ruined painting scroll.

Her aunt had taken one look at the painting, then ordered a thorough investigation. Every palace servant who had laid hands on it now knelt on the floor, shaking too much to make a sound.

Before long, Nanny Cui returned in great haste.

Her expression was grim. She bowed and said, "Your Majesty, the body of Yuntao has been found in the lake. There were no traces of foul play—it appeared as though she had simply slipped and fallen in."

The Empress Dowager slowly opened her eyes. "I had not expected a spy planted so close to me. Have them taken to the Office of Scrutiny—let the inspectors question them thoroughly. Comb through every single person in the Palace of Compassion and Tranquility, from top to bottom!"

"By your command."

The kneeling palace maids and eunuchs wept and cried out their innocence, but within moments they were silenced and dragged away by the attendants of the Office of Scrutiny.

The Empress Dowager said, "Go to the Palace of Eternal Spring. Deliver my verbal edict: during Princess Anyang's period of confinement, she is to copy the Women's Code and the Women's Rules one hundred times and reflect deeply on her transgressions. Noble Consort Dowager Xian, as Anyang's birth mother and the one responsible for her upbringing, has failed in her duty and shall share the same punishment—confined to quarters for three months."

Nanny Cui replied, "Yes, Your Majesty."

She departed with her retinue toward the Palace of Eternal Spring.

Jiang Mi had never seen her aunt this furious before.

The Empress Dowager beckoned to Jiang Mi.

Jiang Mi stepped forward. "Auntie."

"Are you frightened?" the Empress Dowager asked.

Jiang Mi nodded. The palace was like this—danger crouched in the shadows, ready to spring without warning. The consequences could range from grave injury to death itself. She had experienced more than enough of this in her past life.

The Empress Dowager drew her close and sat her down beside her, studying Jiang Mi's face. "Don't be afraid. Your aunt is here."

Those words made something ache in Jiang Mi's chest. But Auntie—you cannot protect Tangtang forever.

When you are gone, even with Tangtang in the palace, she couldn't shield the Jiang family. When the crime of killing the Emperor's birth mother is laid at her door, there will be no protection left.

She so desperately wanted to ask outright—in the sixteenth year of Yongshun's reign, when the Emperor's mother, Consort Jin, accompanied the late Emperor on his southern tour and passed away in Suzhou, did that have anything to do with her aunt?

She refused to believe her aunt could have harmed Consort Jin. Yet when the witnesses and evidence appeared, there had been nothing left to argue against.

How could she possibly ask now? How could she dare?

Jiang Mi's eyes swam with tears. In a trembling voice she said, "Auntie, Tangtang is not yet even in the palace, and it is already this dangerous. Today I escaped by luck—but who knows where the next trap will be waiting? Auntie, I am frightened... I—can I stop trying to enter?"

She was very nervous, very carefully testing the limits of her aunt's resolve.

The Empress Dowager was silent for a long moment, then let out a soft laugh. She took the handkerchief from Jiang Mi's hands and gently wiped away her tears. "What a childish thing to say. Only the other day you turned shy whenever the Emperor's name was mentioned—now you no longer admire him? How can you simply say you won't enter the palace and mean it?"

"Today the Emperor rewarded you alone with an ancient qin—and not just any qin, but the celebrated Green Ripple. Clearly his heart has turned to you. Once the name list is submitted and the Emperor gives his approval, you'll be able to come to the palace early and keep your aunt company. When you take your seat as Empress, the others will have no room to act up."

Jiang Mi could not conceal her disappointment—though she had expected as much.

Her aunt would not relent so easily. The Jiang family had invested tremendous effort in raising and cultivating her with the single goal of securing her a place in the imperial palace and extending the family's glory.

But things would not unfold as smoothly as her aunt imagined.

In her past life, Xiao Huaiyan had shown no intention of appointing an Empress at this point, nor had he taken any consorts.

It was the following year before he began taking consorts—and the position of Empress he had always reserved for the Shen family.

Her aunt's carefully laid plans were about to come to nothing.

By the time Jiang Mi returned to the warm room, the moon had risen. Its cold, pale light made the autumn night feel even more chill.

Qiuyu came out to meet her. "Miss, you're finally back. An eunuch from the Qianqing Palace brought a qin—he said the Emperor bestowed it. This servant dared not touch it and left it on the table."

Jiang Mi stepped past the folding screen and saw the ancient qin, faintly luminous with a greenish sheen.

Her gaze then moved to Mianmian's nest—it was empty. She turned and asked, "Where is Mianmian?"

Qiuyu replied, "This servant was about to bring Mianmian back, but little Eunuch Chengzhong from the Qianqing Palace called out to me and took me to the imperial medical office, where Imperial Physician Li bandaged Mianmian's injuries. The physician said he was worried Mianmian's internal organs might have been damaged, so he kept it there for one night of observation. We can go collect it tomorrow."

Jiang Mi frowned slightly. Chengzhong? Since when did he concern himself with such trivial matters?

Qiuyu poured Jiang Mi a cup of tea. "Miss, please don't worry—when this servant left, Mianmian was looking much more lively."

Jiang Mi accepted the tea, drank a couple of sips, felt a little better, and set the cup down.

She stood at the table and reached out to touch the strings of Green Ripple. A feeling both absurd and astonishing washed over her.

The qin that Consort Rou—so dearly beloved in her past life—had longed for with all her heart had somehow ended up in her hands.

In her past life, she would have been overjoyed.

But now, this qin meant nothing to her.

She ran a pensive hand over the intertwining vine patterns carved into the instrument—and her fingertip caught on something that felt subtly different. She pressed along the seam, and a hidden compartment slid open.

Inside the compartment lay a yellowed sheet of paper.

She picked it up, curious, and unfolded it.

In one glance, all color drained from Jiang Mi's face. Her hands began to tremble, uncontrollably, and a cold sensation crept up the length of her spine.

Qiuyu sensed something was wrong and hurried over to steady her. "Miss, what's the matter?"

Jiang Mi's knees felt as though they might give way. She could not believe she had made such a catastrophic error.

The paper in her hands bore a hastily scrawled musical score—the kind dashed off in a spare moment, incomplete, only a handful of passages.

She had seen this score before, in her past life. It had been in the imperial study, the day he had pinned her against the desk with overwhelming force—in her struggle, she had knocked over a book, and this score had slipped out from between its pages.

Afterward, he had picked the score up from the floor and told her, offhandedly, that he had composed it during the chaos of the Rebel King's uprising.

At the time she had only glimpsed it for a few moments—yet she had memorized it.

Later, when she played the qin, she would unconsciously weave those memorized passages into the pieces she composed. She had always regretted that she had never been able to present that music to him on his birthday—time and again she revised it, refined it, wanting to one day offer him the very finest version.

And the piece she had played in the great hall today was exactly that—those passages, woven in.

She had no business knowing this score in the present moment. There was absolutely no way she could have played it.

Had Xiao Huaiyan recognized it?

With his suspicious nature—could he truly believe this was mere coincidence?

He had bestowed Green Ripple upon her specifically to lead her to find the hidden compartment, and through it, the score.

Was this another warning?

Jiang Mi forced herself to stay calm. Don't be afraid. Don't panic.

Coincidences happen in the world every day. As long as she never admitted to it, no one could know she had ever seen that score.

......

"This subject has investigated—no one from the third branch of the Marquis Chengen household has ever been to Yunzhou. The Third Young Miss of the Jiang family has never left the capital, and she has rarely attended the social gatherings of other households."

The speaker appeared to be in his late twenties. His bearing was cold, his features stern, and a knife scar cut across the peak of his right brow, lending him an air of dangerous intensity. This was Pei Chi, Commander of the Embroidered Uniform Guard.

Xiao Huaiyan leafed through the case file in his hands, a small smile resting at the corner of his mouth. "Never left the capital? Can there truly be such a coincidence in this world?" His voice was mild. "She grows more and more interesting."

The curve of his lips thinned. The smile did not reach his eyes.

chapters.title: Thirteen
chapters.content:
Her body was lifted into the air—seized by arms like iron clamps—and pinned to the surface of a desk.

The woman kicked her legs and wept in a low, desperate voice, "Not here... not here, please... Consort Rou is still outside the door..."

The man paid no heed to her muffled weeping. With a sweep of his hand, he sent the memorials in his way scattering to the floor.

"What is that sound inside? Has His Majesty finished his work? Li Gonggong, this ginseng soup must be drunk while it's hot—please let this consort go in."

The voice from outside reached the ears of the woman curled upon the desk. She trembled, terrified that the carved lacquer doors would be pushed open.

A scorching hand closed around her ankle. She could not wrench free, and the man did not pause. She could only press her own hand over her mouth, desperately afraid of letting a single sound escape.

"Noble Consort Rou, His Majesty does not like to be disturbed while reviewing memorials. If Your Ladyship were to enter uninvited, this servant would be unable to bear the consequences."

There was a pause outside, as though the woman were weighing the matter. "Very well—I'll leave the soup with you. Be sure to remind His Majesty to drink it."

"Your Ladyship may rest easy. This servant respectfully sees off Noble Consort Rou."

Sunlight poured through the glazed windows, flooding the room with brightness. That body of skin, white as frost and snow, was scattered now with small crimson marks like plum blossoms scattered across snow—a sight both pitiable and startling.

The man drew the woman into his arms, a sheet of musical notation in one hand. In a tone of bored indifference, he murmured, "Such a small matter—why cry so bitterly?"

......

The next morning, Jiang Mi sat before her dressing table, studying her own reflection with a quiet sigh. She had patted a thin layer of powder over her face—her eyes were still slightly red and swollen.

Last night had been deeply unsettled. Her dreams were a tangled mess, and she had woken herself up crying again in her sleep.

At least she hadn't disturbed Qiuyu this time.

She pressed her fingertips to her faintly aching head. If she stayed in this palace any longer, she feared she truly would fall ill from fright.

Thinking of how she would need to take her leave of her aunt, and not wanting to worry her, Jiang Mi applied a soft peach-blossom makeup to conceal the redness around her eyes.

But once the makeup was finished, her already striking face became even more alluring—her almond-shaped eyes needed only the slightest curve to become like a small hook, catching at the heart.

When Jiang Mi arrived at the main hall, she found her aunt in the middle of breakfast. The Empress Dowager looked up as Jiang Mi walked in and let admiration show openly in her eyes. She gestured for Jiang Mi to sit beside her and share the meal.

"With Tangtang looking like this today, your aunt could manage an extra bowl of congee. 'Beauty pleasing enough to eat'—now I finally understand what that truly means." The Empress Dowager teased.

Jiang Mi pressed her fingers over her smile and said with deliberate playfulness, "Then Auntie had better look well now—once Tangtang goes home, you won't be able to see her whenever you like."

Jiang Mi kept her aunt company through another half-bowl of plain rice porridge.
