The Empress Dowager set down her chopsticks, satisfied at seven parts full.

Jiang Mi served her a cup of hot tea and waited at her side, quietly attentive.

The Empress Dowager said, "This time, your aunt won't keep you too long. Now that the Emperor's Birthday has passed, let us wait for word from the Emperor's side."

When her aunt agreed so readily, Jiang Mi was flooded with relief. She could finally leave the palace.

The Emperor had not yet taken any consorts—and with the Empress Dowager here, it would hardly be proper for an unmarried young woman to remain in the palace indefinitely. She had already heard that the very day the banquet ended, Xie Mingshan had left with the rest of the Xie Ducal household.

Jiang Mi had known this morning would go smoothly. The smile on her face grew brighter.

She stepped out of her aunt's bedchamber, her steps light. She needed to hurry and tell Qiuyu to collect Mianmian, and then leave the palace and go home!

She had just come down the steps when she walked straight into an approaching party.

Jiang Mi saw the man at the head of them—dressed in imperial yellow dragon robes, only a few steps away—and barely suppressed the urge to turn and flee. She stepped to the side, lowered her head, and curtsied. "Long live the Emperor."

"No need for formalities." Xiao Huaiyan replied, his tone warm.

Jiang Mi waited for him to proceed inside, but he did not move.

Jiang Mi had no choice but to stay where she was.

Xiao Huaiyan's gaze rested briefly on her face, and then he said, with a smile, "Miss Jiang—are you afraid of me?"

"This—this subject's daughter is overwhelmed with trepidation." Jiang Mi had no idea what he was after.

"Then look up." Xiao Huaiyan's voice remained as mild and approachable as ever.

Those words seemed to reach into the deepest part of Jiang Mi and stir something there. She slowly raised her head—but her eyes did not dare meet his. Her thick, dark lashes trembled faintly.

That face, vivid and striking as spring blossoms, strove as hard as it could to stay composed—yet the fear beneath the surface could not be hidden.

Xiao Huaiyan turned his jade ring slowly between his fingers, then asked, "I forgot to inquire—has Miss Jiang found the Green Ripple qin comfortable to play?"

What did he mean by that?

Was he testing whether she had found the hidden score? Or did he have some other suspicion entirely?

Jiang Mi hesitated, then said, "In reply to Your Majesty—Green Ripple is far too precious a gift for this subject's daughter to accept with any ease. I humbly ask that Your Majesty withdraw the bestowal and return this instrument to its true and rightful owner."

Xiao Huaiyan watched her lower her eyes and speak those words of refusal, and the smile at the corner of his mouth slowly deepened.

He took a step closer, catching the faint, clean scent that drifted from her. His gaze cooled slightly. He let out a quiet laugh. "Miss Jiang, when that game of feigning interest while pulling back is played too often, it loses its novelty."

With that, he did not spare her another glance—he clasped his hands behind his back and walked up the steps, disappearing into the Palace of Compassion and Tranquility.

Jiang Mi stood rooted to the spot. She was drenched in cold sweat, and when the wind blew, her soaked undergarment clung to her like ice against her skin.

Back in the warm room, she drank a full cup of hot tea before the warmth returned to her body.

A little while later, Qiuyu came back from the imperial medical office with Mianmian in her arms.

Jiang Mi saw that Mianmian's left leg had been wrapped and splinted with bandaging. She gave its little head a gentle stroke. The creature nuzzled against her hand in response and let out a soft meow.

Jiang Mi tucked it into her arms and told Qiuyu to hurry and pack their things—they were leaving the palace immediately.

Fortunately, she did not have many belongings, and some things could simply be left behind. In less than an hour, Qiuyu had everything ready. As for the Green Ripple qin—she had intended to leave it here in the warm room, but Xiao Huaiyan's words came back to her, and she resigned herself to having Qiuyu bring the instrument along as well.

By this time, the palace attendants her aunt had arranged had already brought a palanquin and were waiting. With her aunt's token, the journey out of the palace went without a hitch.

At the palace gate, a carriage from the Marquis Chengen household was waiting.

Jiang Mi settled into the carriage and lifted a corner of the curtain. Outside, the streets were full of life—crowds of people, vendors crying their wares in the busy market. She felt the grounded solidity of returning to the world of ordinary people.

She leaned against the cushions and let out a long, quiet breath.

As the carriage drew closer to the Marquis Chengen household, she felt a strange, almost shy nervousness—the kind that comes when home has been away so long it feels almost unfamiliar.

For her, it had been three years.

The carriage stopped. A smaller sedan chair carried her directly into the courtyard.

Yuanzhi Courtyard—her own small corner of the world.

After her grandmother passed away, she had moved here. It was her private sanctuary.

chapters.title: Fourteen
chapters.content:
In the main room of Tinglan Courtyard, a refined woman of around thirty was going through the household account books. Her brow creased and released in turn as her eyes moved down the pages.

A well-dressed serving woman hurried in and said in a low voice, "Madam, the Third Young Miss has returned from the palace."

Lady Su set down the account book, startled. "She's back?"

"Yes, Madam. The Third Young Miss went directly to Yuanzhi Courtyard—she'll likely come over to pay her respects shortly."

Lady Su shook her head. "Never mind. Send someone over and ask whether Yuanzhi Courtyard is lacking anything. And take those porcelain pieces from Jiangnan, the silks, and those fox-fur pelts without any markings—have them sent over for the Third Young Miss to choose from. Tell her that if she's tired, she need not come to pay her respects today."

......

Toward evening, Lady Su of Tinglan Courtyard was composing the menu for dinner. The master of the house would come to the main courtyard to dine after finishing his work at court—she needed to prepare in advance.

She had already summoned her daughter Jiang Rong and her young son Jiang Xuan.

As for Jiang Mi's side—Lady Su had not sent anyone to invite her. For one thing, Jiang Mi had just returned from the palace and would surely be tired—she would not want to make the trip. For another, Jiang Mi had never been in the habit of dining with them; she had her own small kitchen, and would likely feel more at ease there.

Lady Su had just handed the menu to a serving woman and sent her off.

Then her head maidservant Xiyue came in hurriedly. "Madam, the Third Young Miss has come."

Lady Su was startled. On previous occasions when the Third Young Miss returned from the palace, she would always come to pay her respects the following day.

Why had she come now, so unexpectedly? Had something happened?

......

As Jiang Mi walked toward Tinglan Courtyard, she ran into Jiang Rong and Jiang Xuan coming the other way.

Jiang Rong was twelve years old, her hair in the double-loop style of a young girl. The first traces of a quietly lovely appearance were already beginning to show.

Jiang Xuan was only five—the age when children can't sit still for a moment. He had been pestering Jiang Rong about catching crickets, but then he caught sight of Jiang Mi standing not far away.

Jiang Xuan stared at her blankly, looking as though he wasn't quite sure who she was.

Jiang Rong was equally surprised. Third Elder Sister had grown up with their grandmother, and was dearly favored by the Empress Dowager—she had her own separate courtyard and rarely came to this part of the house.

Jiang Rong hesitated, then took her brother's hand and walked over. "Third Elder Sister," she called.

Jiang Xuan looked up at this pretty elder sister who seemed vaguely familiar and followed Jiang Rong's lead. "Eldest Sister..."

Jiang Xuan noticed the fish-shaped pendant hanging at Jiang Mi's waist—bright and glistening, very lovely.

He couldn't help reaching out for it. Jiang Rong, sharp-eyed, quickly swatted his hand and held him back firmly.

Jiang Rong said, a little embarrassed, "Third Elder Sister, Xuan is still young and doesn't know any better."

Jiang Mi smiled. "It doesn't matter."

She unclipped the fish pendant and held it out to Jiang Xuan. "Since Xuan likes it, take it as a gift."

Jiang Xuan broke into a delighted grin, grabbed the pendant, and announced loudly, "Thank you, Eldest Sister!"

Jiang Rong stood there a little awkwardly, not quite knowing what to say.

Jiang Mi opened her palm again. A beautifully carved jade rabbit pendant rested in her hand. She held it out to Jiang Rong. "Do you like it?"

Jiang Rong's eyes lit up, but she hesitated.

"If you like it, then it's yours, Rong." Jiang Mi's voice was gentle—the kind of gentleness that drew people in without their even realizing it.

Jiang Rong accepted the pendant with both hands, pressing her lips together. "Thank you, Elder Sister."

Jiang Mi walked with the two siblings into Tinglan Courtyard.

She watched their backs as they went, and her thoughts turned to a memory from her past life.

At that time, the Jiang family had already fallen. She had been lying in bed, worn down by too much worry, too little strength. She had not gotten what she had waited for—that man had not come—but Jiang Rong had.

Jiang Rong had been sixteen by then, her figure slender, her face lovely but drawn—she had lost too much weight.

Jiang Mi had never known how Jiang Rong had managed to get into the palace.

The moment Jiang Rong saw her, she had started to cry, pulling several crumpled banknotes from her sleeve.

Jiang Mi still remembered how pale Jiang Rong's face had been. "Third Elder Sister, this is the last money the family can send in. You must rest and recover—don't worry about things at home."

Just before she left, Jiang Rong had glanced back. "Elder Sister—I... tomorrow I am to become the concubine of Pei Chi. After that, we may rarely see each other. Please take care of yourself."

Only after Jiang Rong left did Jiang Mi learn the truth—Jiang Rong had used the connections of the Embroidered Uniform Guard's Commander Pei Chi to gain entry into the palace to see her.

The Jiang family's daughter, who had once been from a proper official household, whose engagement had been broken off, had been forcibly taken into Pei Chi's household as a concubine.

Jiang Mi thought back on her distance from Lady Su, from Jiang Rong, from Jiang Xuan—through all the years before the fall. Yet in the end, when the Jiang family collapsed, Lady Su had poured in all of her own dowry money to try to manage things, and had not forgotten her—this out-of-favor consort in the palace—sending Jiang Rong to bring money to her.

A bitterness welled in Jiang Mi's heart.

Whatever came, she had to try in this life to change what she could. Even if that calamity came again, she would at least be able to shield her family from the worst of it.

......

Lady Su looked up to see the three siblings walking in together.

The Third Young Miss—who was usually politely distant with everyone—had actually taken Xuan's hand, and the boy could be heard chattering nonstop: "I want to see the cat! The cat! Why didn't you bring it with you, Elder Sister?!"

That voice of his was so loud it made Lady Su want to rap him on the head.

Yet the Third Young Miss showed not the slightest impatience. She answered in her soft, gentle way, "Mianmian is still recovering from an injury and is resting in Yuanzhi Courtyard. If you finish your calligraphy practice tomorrow, you can come visit it!"

Jiang Xuan perked up at once. "Elder Sister, don't worry—I'll definitely finish early. You have to wait for me!"

Jiang Rong stood beside them, biting her lip, a quiet envy in her gaze as she watched her brother.

Jiang Mi smiled. "Rong, if you're free, come along with Xuan. I'll make osmanthus cakes for both of you."

Lady Su watched all of this with the distinct feeling that she must still be half-asleep.

What had happened to the Third Young Miss?

Jiang Mi glanced up and caught sight of Lady Su, whose expression had gone slightly stiff. She gave her a light smile. "Paying my respects to Madam."

Lady Su immediately rose from her chair. "No need for such formalities—please, Third Young Miss, come and sit."

Lady Su's feelings toward this stepdaughter were more respectful than affectionate.

She knew that the Third Young Miss had been raised by the old matriarch and cultivated by the Empress Dowager to one day enter the palace as a noble consort.

Moreover, the Third Young Miss's birth mother came from a family of hereditary nobility. Lady Su's own family, the Sus, may have been wealthy beyond measure in Suzhou, but they were merchants at heart.

She had always been afraid of giving the Third Young Miss even the slightest slight.

Jiang Mi set down her tea cup and said with a smile, "I brought back some pastries that the Empress Dowager graciously bestowed—I thought Madam might enjoy a taste. And I'd also like to impose and have dinner here with you, if I may."

Lady Su was delighted to agree and immediately told the kitchen to add several dishes that Jiang Mi was fond of.

......

Jiang Qingxuan walked into Tinglan Courtyard in the evening light, and the moment he reached the main room, he sensed something subtly different from usual.

Inside, Lady Su was speaking, and at intervals came the soft sound of a woman's gentle laughter.

He walked in to find his eldest daughter seated upright in a carved huanghuali chair, listening with a quiet smile.

His second daughter and youngest son were leaning close to their elder sister—the picture of easy warmth.

Jiang Mi noticed Jiang Qingxuan's arrival and stood. She called out with proper decorum, "Father."

The two younger ones followed with their own greetings.

Jiang Qingxuan gave a measured nod.

He turned to Jiang Mi and asked, "Were you well looked after in the palace?"

Jiang Mi nodded. "With Auntie watching over me, everything went smoothly."

Jiang Qingxuan was not a man of many words. Having asked after Jiang Mi, he moved on to question the other two about their studies.

When Lady Su saw the master had arrived, she had the kitchen begin serving, and everyone moved to the main dining hall.

Jiang Qingxuan believed in eating without conversation. The table was quiet.

Only Jiang Xuan produced the occasional small clink or clatter.

Jiang Mi did not eat much, and feeling rather tired, she put down her chopsticks after little more than half a bowl of rice.

Jiang Rong stole several glances at her third elder sister, watching her eat only a few bites and then set her chopsticks down.

She studied Third Elder Sister's slender waist, then glanced at her own, and quietly put down her chopsticks as well.

Lady Su noticed and asked, "Is the food not to your liking?"

Jiang Mi shook her head and explained, "I've always eaten lightly in the evenings."

Before long, Jiang Qingxuan finished as well and was about to return to his study—when Jiang Mi called out to stop him.

"Father, your daughter has something to say to you."

Jiang Qingxuan paused, then nodded. "Follow me to the study."

Lady Su watched the two of them leave, and only then let out a quiet breath.

......

Jiang Qingxuan led Jiang Mi into the study, gestured casually at a chair, and said, "Sit. Your father heard that you played the qin on the Emperor's Birthday and received the Emperor's praise—and that he bestowed upon you the ancient qin Green Ripple?"

Jiang Mi replied, "Yes..."

"Well done. But going forward, do not let it go to your head—you must remain composed. Only through calm can one truly master the qin." Jiang Qingxuan seemed to worry his tone was too stern and added, "If there is anything you do not understand, you may come and ask me. Not long ago I came across a score by the great Master Fang of the previous dynasty—I'll have someone bring it to you tomorrow."

Jiang Mi smiled. "Thank you, Father." She realized, with some surprise, that her father actually had quite a bit to say. In the past, there had been a knot in her heart about him, and she had never been willing to speak much with him.

Jiang Qingxuan gave a slightly self-conscious cough. "Now, what was it you wanted to say?"

Jiang Mi let her smile fall away. She placed the painting scroll on the desk and unrolled it. "Father—your daughter had intended to present this painting on the Emperor's Birthday, but when the moment came, she discovered it had been ruined, and had no choice but to play the qin instead."

Jiang Qingxuan had not expected this.

He looked at the painting carefully. The anger in his eyes was shot through with a flash of something close to fear. He asked, "You painted plum blossoms?"

Jiang Mi nodded. "Searching for Plum Blossoms in the Snow in Jiangnan."

Jiang Qingxuan fixed his gaze on the painting, drenched in ink. He slammed his palm on the desk. "This is outrageous!"

Jiang Mi had not expected her father's fury to run even deeper than she had imagined.

She had come here partly because she had not managed to sway her aunt—she wanted to try from her father's side, to sound out where he stood on the question of her entering the palace.

In her past life, before she entered the palace, her father had asked her whether she was willing of her own accord.

Perhaps her father was the opening she needed.

Jiang Mi pressed the handkerchief to her eyes, and tears came. "Father, I am frightened... Even with Auntie's protection in the palace, I escaped this once—but who knows if I will escape the next? Everyone envies the wealth and splendor of the imperial palace, but after what happened today, your daughter is truly afraid... I—I, can I stop pursuing this?"

It was the first time Jiang Qingxuan had ever seen his eldest daughter weep before him. His brow knotted even tighter, and he was at a complete loss for how to comfort this delicate creature.

A struggle passed through his eyes. Then, in a low, heavy voice, he said, "Tangtang, don't cry. Tomorrow I will go to the palace to seek an audience with the Empress Dowager."

Jiang Mi was struck with surprise. Could it really be so effective?

She wiped her tears, sniffled, and took her leave.

......

The following morning, Jiang Qingxuan sent his calling card to the Palace of Compassion and Tranquility and entered the palace with the ruined painting to request an audience with the Empress Dowager.

The Empress Dowager received him.

Seeing that Jiang Qingxuan's expression was strained and his color was off, she dismissed those around them and asked, "What has happened?"

Jiang Qingxuan placed the painting of plum blossoms before her. "Your Majesty—does this seem like a simple act of ruining a painting?"

The Empress Dowager shifted her gaze away. "Did Tangtang come to complain to you?"

Jiang Qingxuan said, "Does Your Majesty truly not know the true intent of the person who ruined this painting?"

The Empress Dowager's hands trembled involuntarily. She forced herself to stay composed.

When the Empress Dowager said nothing, Jiang Qingxuan continued. "Searching for plum blossoms in the snow in Jiangnan—the pure and noble plum blossom, defiled by ink. Your Majesty, do not forget what happened in the fourteenth year of Yongshun's reign. Do not forget the name of that person—there is a character for 'plum' in it. And do not forget how she died. If Tangtang had not discovered that the painting had been ruined before she brought it out, and she had simply unrolled it in front of all those assembled noble families and court wives—whether or not she had been framed—the implicit suggestion that the Emperor's mother's virtue was besmirched would have been plain to see. What would the Emperor have thought?"

"Enough—say no more!" The Empress Dowager cut him off.

The veins at Jiang Qingxuan's temples stood out. "Your Majesty knows the road ahead is full of peril—then why persist in this and push Jiang Mi toward it?"

The Empress Dowager gripped her hands white-knuckled, her eyes reddening. "Because she is the daughter of the Marquis Chengen household. Because she bears the name Jiang."

chapters.title: Fifteen
chapters.content:
An autumn wind stirred, sending the leaves rustling with a dry, papery sound.

Jiang Qingxuan walked into Yuanzhi Courtyard with a qin score in his hand and a look of guilt on his face.

As soon as he stepped through the gate, he saw his eldest daughter and second daughter seated across from each other beneath the parasol tree, each holding a piece—one black, one white—absorbed in a game of weiqi. And there was his youngest son, crouched at the stone table, nibbling on a cake in one hand while teasing the small white cat lying in a bamboo basket with the other.

Jiang Mi studied the board and gently set down a white stone, then turned to Jiang Xuan with a reminder. "Xuan, don't feed Mianmian osmanthus cake—it can't eat those."

Jiang Xuan swallowed the remaining piece in his hand in one gulp. "Elder Sister, I can barely get enough for myself—I wouldn't feed it to Mianmian."

He then licked his fingers.

Jiang Rong frowned in distaste. "Xuan, how many times have I told you not to lick your fingers?"

Jiang Xuan shot to his feet and pressed himself against Jiang Mi's leg, complaining, "Elder Sister, Fourth Sister is being mean to me."

Jiang Mi reached over and pinched his soft, plump little cheek. "Fourth Sister is right. Go have Nanny Liu take you to wash your hands, or you won't get any more osmanthus cake as punishment."

Jiang Xuan kicked at a pebble on the ground, pushed out his lower lip, and turned to go—only to find Jiang Qingxuan walking toward him.

He hid his hands behind his back in a flash, straightened up, and called out properly, "Father!"

Jiang Mi set down the piece she was holding and stood. "Father—what brings you here?"

Jiang Rong also rose and called out her greeting in a small voice.

Jiang Qingxuan looked at Jiang Mi with a complicated expression and held out the score. "This is the piece I mentioned yesterday. You... take a look and see if it suits you."

Jiang Mi accepted it with both hands, her face lighting up. "Thank you, Father."

Seeing the warmth and longing in Jiang Mi's eyes, Jiang Qingxuan's guilt deepened.

He thought of how this child had lost her mother young and been raised by the old matriarch—how she and he had never grown very close.

And now she had been frightened and hurt in the palace and had come to him for help.

But he had failed her. He had not managed to persuade the Empress Dowager to abandon the plan of sending her to the palace.

Jiang Qingxuan said, "Don't stand on ceremony. If there is anything you need, just send word to me directly."

He paused, then added, "I have other matters to attend to. You all carry on."

"Father, please wait." Jiang Mi called after him.

She picked up a black lacquered food box from the stone table and brought it over. "Father, these are osmanthus cakes I made myself—I saved some especially for you to try."

Jiang Qingxuan's lips moved. He seemed to want to say something, and then gave up.

He took the food box, made a small sound of acknowledgment, and left.

Jiang Mi watched his retreating figure and smiled.

She knew that even if her father had gone to see her aunt, he would not easily change her aunt's mind.

But it was a good beginning. At least her father was willing to try.

Given enough time—as long as her father's sense of guilt toward her deepened—perhaps he would one day agree to a certain plan of hers.

A marriage arrangement made through proper parental consent could be set in motion quietly, announced only once it was settled. Even if her aunt found out afterward, she would have no grounds to force her into the palace.

In this life, she and Xiao Huaiyan could be strangers entirely.

She would no longer be tormented by nightmares. No longer live in constant dread.

......

Jiang Rong and Jiang Xuan stayed in Yuanzhi Courtyard until noon, each carrying a food box Jiang Mi had packed for them, and went home happy.

After lunch, Jiang Mi lay down on the daybed for a brief rest.

She woke up quickly, though.

She couldn't sleep—there was too much to think through.

She was sixteen now. Girls her age were mostly either already engaged or being looked over for a match.

But she had been chosen so early for the palace that no one had ever thought to look for a suitable husband for her.

Whether she entered the palace or not, she could not escape the matter of marriage.

If she was going to have to marry regardless, she might as well choose someone who would treat her well.

Her plan, in her heart, was to spend this window of time looking for a suitable match. Then she would find a way to earn her father's sympathy and have him agree to a private betrothal.

By the time Xiao Huaiyan began taking consorts next year, she would be nearly married—and even if her aunt still wanted to push her into the palace, it would be impossible.

The most important question, then, was who.

First, the family's character had to be upright. Someone close to the throne, or from a family with real power—capable, a few years down the road, of protecting those who had become commoners from being trampled on.

If the disaster could not be avoided, she could only prepare for the very worst.

The title stripped away. The official post revoked. The marquisate raided and confiscated. The family reduced to common folk.

When the tree falls, the monkeys scatter. Anyone would feel free to step on the Jiang family.

So the man she married could not be from too lowly a background. He had to be someone of real ability, valued within his own clan, someone whose word carried weight.

Someone who could, in a moment that truly mattered, offer some measure of protection.

Second, his nature had to be good—considerate, ideally gentle, possessed of compassion. A man of genuine integrity.

If his household was uncomplicated, so much the better.

Jiang Mi turned over on the daybed, lightly chewing her fingertip with a slight deflation of spirit.

Any man who truly met all those conditions was surely one that every noble family's daughter was scrambling to marry—where would there be any room left for her?

Such an ideal match—who wouldn't want one?

Jiang Mi knew very little about the world beyond her walls. She had rarely accompanied the Marchioness or the ladies to social gatherings.

She had no clear picture of which eligible young men among the capital's noble families were currently unmarried.

Worry, worry—wherever was she going to find such a perfectly suited person?

Jiang Mi tossed and turned on the daybed.

......

Two days later, the Marchioness of Chengen sent some fresh crabs over to Yuanzhi Courtyard, along with a maidservant bearing a question: tomorrow she would be taking Jiang Yi to Qianshuang Temple to offer incense, and would Jiang Mi like to come along?

Jiang Mi had been wondering how to get out and gather some news—and her great-aunt's wife had delivered the perfect opportunity, right on cue.

She sent the maidservant back with her answer: she too had been wanting to go to the temple to pray, and she hoped it wasn't too much of an imposition.

Sitting quietly, she thought it through—was this not the time when the Marchioness was looking over a potential match for Second Sister Jiang Yi?

By including her in the trip, the occasion would not seem too pointed.

If Second Sister and the young man happened to suit each other, wonderful for everyone.

If not, the two sisters would simply have gone to the temple together to pray and offer incense.

And it gave Jiang Mi a perfect opportunity to gently probe Jiang Yi and the Marchioness about which eligible young men in the capital might, even loosely, fit the qualities she was looking for.

In her past life, at this very time, she had still been locked away in her room weeping in secret, too ashamed to step outside after what had happened to her in the palace. The Marchioness would never have thought to invite her.

Jiang Mi told Xiaru to go to Tinglan Courtyard and let Madam know that tomorrow she would be going to Qianshuang Temple with the Marchioness and Second Sister.

......

The Marchioness of Chengen, Lady Chen, was helping Jiang Yi choose a hairpin for the next day.

She had just picked up a tourmaline beaded hairpin when Jiang Yi shook her head. "Mother, that one is a little too matronly."

Lady Chen put it down and picked up a jade seven-treasure hairpin. "This one looks a little livelier."

Jiang Yi was still not satisfied.

She couldn't help complaining, "Mother, why is it that I'm only being matched with the second son of the Deputy Minister of Revenue, while Jiang Mi gets to go into the palace and wait upon the Emperor? Auntie is so unfair. I'm the legitimate daughter of the Marquis Chengen household, aren't I?" After all, it was her father who held the title—so why did Auntie favor Jiang Mi so much?

Lady Chen shot her a look and dropped her voice. "Is that something you're supposed to say? When the Empress Dowager sent people to instruct you two, who was it that started crying and complaining about pain and giving up first? Look at Jiang Mi—did she ever let up, no matter how bitter cold the winter or sweltering the summer? And you—of qin, chess, calligraphy, and painting, which can you match her in? And as for learning to dance—they only pressed your legs a little, bent your waist a little, and you were crying for your mother and father and screaming that you refused to practice anymore."

Jiang Yi, thoroughly scolded, hunched her shoulders like a quail and pouted. "That immodest dance—what proper girl from a decent family would even bother learning it?"

Lady Chen threw up her hands. "Go say that in front of the Empress Dowager, then. Do you think serving the Emperor is a good thing? With that unguarded mouth of yours, every emotion written plain on your face—even if they let you into the palace, how long do you think you'd last among those sharp-minded people?"

"Keep on making a fuss like this and I'll write to the Deputy Minister's wife and tell her you've caught a cold and the whole thing is off."

Jiang Yi saw that Lady Chen was about to sweep out and quickly grabbed her sleeve. "I—I won't say anything more. Mother, the date is already set—we can't just change it at the last moment. Besides, isn't Jiang Mi coming along to burn incense too? All that back-and-forth would be too much trouble!"

Lady Chen, seeing her be stubborn even in surrender, said coldly, "Are you choosing a hairpin or not? If not, I'll have them all put away."

Jiang Yi pressed her lips together and pointed at one at random.

......

The next morning, Lady Su prepared a spacious, comfortable carriage for Jiang Mi, furnished inside with a soft white rug and plush cushions, with a tea set and a chess board all laid out.

The carriage set off at an easy pace toward Qianshuang Temple.

Jiang Mi leafed through a casual book, while Qiuyu poured her a cup of hot, fragrant tea.

The fresh scent of the tea drifted through the carriage.

Warm sunlight slanted in through the carriage window. Jiang Mi closed her eyes. If only there were none of these worries and complications, she thought, what a pleasant life this would be.

When Jiang Mi had just drifted into a pleasant doze, the carriage came to a stop.

Qiuyu called softly, "Miss, we've arrived at Qianshuang Temple."

Jiang Mi rubbed her eyes. She was still half in a dream—drowsily, prettily blurred at the edges.

Qiuyu helped Jiang Mi down from the carriage.

Jiang Mi was dressed in a brocade skirt embroidered with golden lotuses, her figure graceful and delicate. As she walked, she moved with an unhurried elegance. Her skin was white as snow, and her cheeks—flushed from having just woken—were tinged with the faintest, most natural rose. A single glance was enough to make the heart quicken.

Jiang Yi walked over and couldn't help asking, "Tangtang, which perfumery do you get your rouge from?"

Jiang Mi touched her cheek, a little puzzled. "Second Sister, I'm not wearing any rouge."

Jiang Yi looked at her hair next—only a simple jade-and-pearl hairpin.

She let out a quiet grumble. Truly, comparing oneself to others was a recipe for despair.

The Marchioness looked at the two sisters and said with an amused smile, "We should go to the main hall—don't miss the right moment for the incense."

The first stick of incense had to be offered at the proper time. To miss it would be inauspicious.

The group followed a young monk to the temple's main hall. As they entered, they found several noble ladies and young women already there ahead of them—and among them, a young, handsome man in a blue robe.

The Marchioness's brow tightened briefly, then smoothed again.

It was not the Deputy Minister of Revenue's wife and her son—it was relatives of Grand Secretary Shen's household.

The two families were not well acquainted. They exchanged polite greetings and a few pleasantries, then each went about their own business.

Standing among the Shen family guests, Shen Yaowei immediately spotted Jiang Mi—whose beauty was matchless.

She leaned toward the young man beside her and said in a quiet voice, "Third Brother, that is the Miss Jiang who astonished everyone with her playing at the Emperor's Birthday banquet. And the Green Ripple you've been dreaming about—it's in that Miss Jiang's hands now!"

The young man frowned slightly and said with a gentle rebuke, "Awei, don't be rude."

chapters.title: Sixteen
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi had naturally noticed Shen Yaowei as well.

She truly envied her — the position of empress had been kept in reserve for Shen Yaowei, and she even had a brilliant elder brother who had swept all three imperial examinations in succession.

The young man in the pale blue robe who had just departed alongside Shen Yaowei must be Shen Qianxiu, who would claim the top scholar title in next spring's imperial examinations.

She had heard his name in her past life, yet had never laid eyes on him.

He did not resemble Shen Yaowei very much.

Jiang Mi knelt before the solemn, benevolent visage of the Buddha alongside her great-aunt.

With her palms pressed together, she prayed with sincere devotion, beseeching the Buddha's blessing: that in this life, her heart's wishes might be granted. Her first wish — to steer clear of that baleful star. Her second wish — that the Jiang family would remain safe and sound. Her third wish — to find herself a worthy and agreeable husband.

After completing her three proper bows, the girl beside her, Jiang Yi, let out a stifled laugh. "Tangtang, you bowed so earnestly — are you praying for a good marriage too?"

Jiang Mi lowered her head and smiled softly. "So it's Elder Sister Yi who was praying for a good marriage? Have you set your sights on someone?"

Jiang Yi twisted her handkerchief, her cheeks flushing crimson, and reached out to pinch Jiang Mi.

The Marchioness of Cheng'en stepped between them. "You little minx — you try to tease Tangtang and end up giving yourself away instead. And you still have the nerve to go after her..."

She took Jiang Mi's hand and led her toward the entrance of the main hall. "Tangtang, pay her no mind. She's just a little flustered right now..."

"To tell you the truth, your great-aunt brought your Elder Sister Yi here today to look over a young man. I've arranged to meet with the Shilang's wife in the meditation courtyard to the south — I'll take your sister over there for a look shortly. The scenery here at Qianshuang Temple is quite lovely. Feel free to wander at your leisure; there's a wishing tree to the east that they say is very efficacious."

Though the Marchioness had invited Jiang Mi along, she was reluctant to bring her to the courtyard. With Jiang Mi's beauty, if she were to stand there, the Shilang's son would not know which young woman to look at.

Jiang Mi understood her great-aunt's meaning. "I've long heard of Qianshuang Temple's wishing tree — Tangtang will go take a look there first, then."

The Marchioness nodded with a relieved smile. "Once I'm finished over there, I'll send the little novice monk to find you. Then we can all go to the vegetarian hall for a meal together — the meatless dishes here are really quite good."

……

After parting ways with the Marchioness, Jiang Mi put on her veiled hat and strolled through the temple grounds with her maid, Qiuyu. Qianshuang Temple had no shortage of worshippers — scholars praying for success in the examinations, unmarried young men and women praying for good marriages, and ailing patients seeking medicinal lots.

It was said that one of Qianshuang Temple's senior monks had been a renowned physician before taking his vows, and the medicinal lots drawn here were said to be highly effective. The Medicinal King Hall had the longest queue of all, and since Jiang Mi had no desire to push through the crowds, she asked a novice monk for directions to the wishing tree and made her way there instead.

……

In a secluded meditation courtyard of Qianshuang Temple, guarded by a tight cordon of security, an elderly monk with a kind and gentle countenance carefully drew a long needle from the head of a man dressed in fine, resplendent robes.

"Your Majesty's headaches have been recurring frequently of late," the monk said as he removed another needle from a nearby acupoint. "Is there some trigger behind them?"

Xiao Huaiyan closed his eyes. "No."

The monk retrieved a black medicinal pill from a box of purple sandalwood, sniffed it, then touched a small amount to the tip of his tongue. "There is nothing wrong with Imperial Physician Gu's prescription either. Your Majesty, aside from the headaches, are there any other symptoms?"

Xiao Huaiyan did not answer, merely rotating the jade thumb ring on his finger.

Seeing the gesture, the monk's eyes filled with quiet concern. "Since Your Majesty has already managed to suppress that vicious energy and malevolent impulse, it would be best to keep the jade ring on as much as possible."

The jade thumb ring was far more than a mere ornament to Xiao Huaiyan — it was a form of restraint, a check against the murderous, restless impulse that lurked within him, ever eager to be unleashed.

The monk sighed and produced a string of prayer beads, about to offer them to the lord of the realm.

Xiao Huaiyan smiled. "We have no use for prayer beads. This jade ring is Our prayer beads."

The monk could only relent. "Once Your Majesty's old ailment has fully healed, that malevolent urge will naturally fade on its own."

Xiao Huaiyan gave an unconcerned smile. "Venerable Master, can hallucinations occur when a headache worsens?"

"Your Majesty has been experiencing hallucinations?" The monk's expression grew grave.

"We seem to hear the sound of bells," Xiao Huaiyan said — and a woman's faint, muffled weeping.

"The sound of bells? Was Your Majesty awake at the time, or asleep?"

"Somewhere between sleeping and waking. Whether hallucination or dream, it is difficult to say."

"This humble monk has never encountered such a symptom before... With Your Majesty's existing condition, this ought not to be occurring." The monk worried that if the emperor's old ailment worsened, it would affect his temperament.

Xiao Huaiyan, however, remained unperturbed. He understood perfectly well that while slaughter could cow people into submission and instill fear, the consequences were far more troublesome than they were worth. Cloaking himself in the garb of a virtuous ruler was far simpler.

The world adored a gracious and magnanimous sovereign. But he alone knew what he truly was.

Whether to restrain himself or not was a matter that only he could decide.

Xiao Huaiyan turned the words around to reassure the monk instead. "Do not concern yourself too greatly. Perhaps it will pass on its own before long."

……

When Jiang Mi caught sight of the wishing tree, she was struck speechless.

The towering ancient tree, a hundred years old if it was a day, was draped from branch to branch in red cloth strips. As the autumn wind swept through, the strips swayed and fluttered — sacred and breathtakingly beautiful.

It was as though these strips, each one a vessel of someone's deepest wishes, would ride the wind all the way to the realm of the gods, so that every deity in the heavens might hear them.

Jiang Mi turned to Qiuyu. "Go ask one of the novice monks for a red cloth strip and some ink and brush."

"Yes, Miss."

It was not long before Qiuyu returned with the items in hand.

Jiang Mi removed her veiled hat and sat at the stone table beneath the wishing tree. Lifting the brush, she wrote out her wish.

Once the ink had dried, she took the red cloth strip and tried to hang it on the tree — only to find herself at a loss. Every branch within reach had already been filled to capacity, not a single gap remaining.

And the higher branches were too far up; even jumping, she could not reach them.

"Miss," said Qiuyu, "shall I ask the novice monks if they have a ladder? If so, I'll go fetch one."

Jiang Mi nodded.

While waiting for Qiuyu to return, she walked a slow circle around the wishing tree, craning her neck upward in search of a suitable spot.

Finding no one nearby, she rose on her tiptoes and gave two experimental hops — sure enough, it was out of reach.

She tossed the red cloth strip upward, but it never made it to the height she needed and drifted back down lightly.

Jiang Mi bent to pick the strip up, when a voice sounded from behind her: "That is not how you hang a cloth strip."

She turned around to see a young man with clear, refined features bowing to her. "Forgive my boldness for startling you, Miss. If you would not mind, I can show you how to hang it."

The young man kept his eyes lowered and did not look directly at her, standing at a respectful distance.

Jiang Mi recognized him at once — was this not Shen Yaowei's brother, Shen Qianxiu, the same man she had noticed in the main hall earlier?

In the past, she would have declined without hesitation.

But this time, Jiang Mi hesitated for just a moment before clutching the cloth strip and saying shyly, "I would be grateful for the young master's guidance."

With her permission granted, Shen Qianxiu stepped forward a few paces. He crouched and picked up a smooth river stone from the ground, then offered it to her. "Tie one end of the cloth strip around this stone, then throw it up into the tree. It will catch on the branches that way."

Jiang Mi marveled that the solution was so simple — she couldn't believe she hadn't thought of it herself.

She accepted the stone, tied the strip to it, and, following his advice, flung it upward toward the wishing tree.

The red strip settled steadily onto a branch, while the stone tumbled down and rolled away across the ground.

Jiang Mi succeeded on her very first try. Delighted, she turned back to Shen Qianxiu with a smile. "Thank you so much, young master."

Shen Qianxiu froze for an instant. When she smiled like that, the most brilliant crimson maple grove of autumn could not compare to the radiance of her expression.

His heart lurched and he hastily averted his eyes. "N-no need to thank me, Miss."

……

Not far away, on a hillside overlooking the temple grounds, there was a viewing pavilion.

A man robed in black brocade stood there at his leisure, looking down from the vantage point at the pair by the wishing tree — a young man and a woman, a perfect match like jade and gem.

A cool, thin smile touched his lips, and his tone was one of idle amusement. "Quite the display of mutual admiration."

The attendant Chengzhong, standing nearby, caught the sight of his master's ice-cold gaze and lowered his head as far as it would go. He was deeply regretting having followed his master this direction.

He simply could not understand. Was Miss Jiang not destined to enter the palace? How was it that she had turned around and grown so familiar with another man?

……

When Qiuyu returned, Shen Qianxiu did not linger. He gave a bow to Jiang Mi and took his leave.

Jiang Mi watched his retreating figure, lost in thought.

"Miss, who was that?" Qiuyu asked. "Did he give you any trouble?"

Jiang Mi shook her head with a gentle smile. "A warm-hearted young gentleman."

Just as Jiang Mi was walking away from the wishing tree, she came across the same novice monk from before, who greeted her with a look of relief. "Benefactress, I've finally found you. An honored guest sent this humble monk to look for you."

Jiang Mi assumed her great-aunt must have finished her business and was calling for her to come share the vegetarian meal.

She nodded and followed the novice monk.

The monk led her in the direction of the meditation courtyard. Along the way, the number of worshippers thinned out, and the surroundings grew increasingly quiet.

At last, before a large vermilion gate, the novice monk stopped. "Benefactress, we have arrived. This humble monk will take his leave."

The monk departed swiftly, vanishing around a corner in no time at all.

Jiang Mi pushed open the gate. Inside the courtyard stood a tall ginkgo tree, its leaves all turned to gold, breathtakingly beautiful.

Yet the surroundings were utterly still — no maids or serving women came forward to receive her.

After only a few steps, an inexplicable impulse to retreat came over Jiang Mi without warning.

When she turned around, she found that the gate had been shut behind her at some point without her noticing.

Qiuyu, who should have been right at her back, had also vanished without a trace.

Jiang Mi's heart leaped into her throat, gripped by a mounting dread.

A shadowy, dark-green figure appeared soundlessly and bowed with deference. "Miss Jiang, the master is inside waiting for you."

Jiang Mi looked over — it was Chengzhong!

If Chengzhong was here, then the master he referred to must be...

Seeing her standing motionless, Chengzhong urged her gently. "Miss Jiang, please go in quickly."

Unhurried footsteps sounded from within, drawing gradually nearer.

The man inside was seated before a chess board, holding a black piece between his fingers, undecided where to place it. He lifted his head — and saw Jiang Mi walking through the door.

Her face was drained of all color, her eyes brimming with fear, her slender figure swaying as though she might collapse at any moment. She forced herself to appear calm, as she always did, and greeted him in that soft, gentle voice of hers.

Xiao Huaiyan placed his black piece on the board. "Miss Jiang, are you not disappointed to find it is Us waiting for you here?"

Jiang Mi did not understand his meaning. "This subject daughter would not dare. I had no idea Your Majesty would be at this place."

Xiao Huaiyan said, "Come here. Keep Us company for a game."

Jiang Mi had not expected Xiao Huaiyan to ask her to play chess.

Her eyes flickered. Hesitating, she said, "If it please Your Majesty, this subject daughter came here with her great-aunt to pay respects to the Buddha. Having been away so long, I fear she will be worried. It is truly not convenient for me to play chess with Your Majesty at this moment. I beg Your Majesty's forgiveness."

Jiang Mi had declined politely.

After speaking, she waited anxiously for his reply.

But half a day passed without a sound.

She tilted her head up just slightly — the man had simply placed another piece on the board as though she hadn't spoken at all.

As long as Xiao Huaiyan said nothing, Jiang Mi could not leave.

After deliberating at length, she could only step forward and say, "This subject daughter's chess skills are poor — I am afraid I will not play well."

Only then did Xiao Huaiyan respond, unhurried as ever. "It matters not. Sit."

Jiang Mi sat as though on a bed of needles, tormented by every passing moment, wanting only for it to be over so she could be free.

chapters.title: Seventeen
chapters.content:
The room lay in peaceful silence, broken only by the soft click of chess pieces being laid on the board.

The faint, elusive scent of dragon-ambergris incense drifted through the air. Jiang Mi, who was already wound tight, felt as if each moment stretched on for an eternity.

She could never have imagined encountering Xiao Huaiyan at Qianshuang Temple.

She had just finished wishing at the tree — praying specifically to be rid of this man — and here he was before the prayer was even cold. It was uncanny.

What in the world had brought him to this temple?

She thought back to the glimpse she had caught of Shen Yaowei. Could it be that he had come to see her?

In her past life, he had been deeply devoted to Shen Yaowei. It was not entirely out of the question.

Jiang Mi stared at the haphazard, disorderly pattern of pieces on the board, her mind elsewhere entirely, longing only for the game to end.

In her past life, she had played chess with Xiao Huaiyan more times than she could count. He favored luring the opponent deep into a trap before closing in for the kill — letting his prey think there was still a sliver of hope, a last breath of survival, and then striking with a final, ruthless encirclement. Merciless. Absolute.

She had never enjoyed playing chess with him back then either. Every time she lost, there was a penalty.

The memory surfaced, and her hand trembled slightly. She pressed her lips together, willing her lashes down.

Xiao Huaiyan watched the person across from him. Her slender, fair fingers held a piece, wavering back and forth, unable to decide. Each time that delicate hand moved, it carried with it a subtle, lingering fragrance that crept, without sound or warning, into his space.

Jiang Mi frowned at the board. The white piece in her hand appeared to hold the advantage — if she retreated, there remained a single thread of survival. But if she followed Xiao Huaiyan's lead, she would lose everything.

She had no interest whatsoever in winning or losing. She only wanted it to end quickly so he would let her go.

Jiang Mi raised her hand to place the white piece down.

At that moment, Xiao Huaiyan spoke. "Is Miss Jiang certain?"

Of course she was certain. The moment she placed this piece, she would lose. And then she could leave!

"Once placed, there is no taking it back. This subject daughter is certain."

Xiao Huaiyan let out a soft, quiet laugh.

At the sound of that laugh, Jiang Mi's heart seized with dread.

When Xiao Huaiyan set down his black piece, the game unfolded precisely as Jiang Mi had foreseen. She let out a quiet breath of relief.

"Your Majesty's chess skills are formidable," she said. "This subject daughter falls far short."

Xiao Huaiyan laughed again, lightly. "We have often heard the Empress Dowager speak highly of your chess."

Jiang Mi looked suitably abashed. "My aunt is far too generous. This subject daughter's skills are simply lacking."

Xiao Huaiyan made no comment one way or the other. "Is that so?"

Jiang Mi replied, flustered, "This subject daughter has embarrassed herself before Your Majesty. When I return home, I shall make certain to study and improve."

She thought to herself: the game was finished, she had lost, she had offered her compliments — surely he would let her leave now?

"There is no hurry now. It is already noon." Xiao Huaiyan said. "Miss Jiang, why not remain and dine with Us?"

Jiang Mi was taken aback. They expected her to dine with them as well?

Could she even eat, sitting across from Xiao Huaiyan?

Before she had a chance to respond, the waiting attendants filed in one after another, arranging the vegetarian dishes on the nearby round table of yellow pear-blossom wood.

At a glance, there were no fewer than twenty dishes, filling the table to capacity. The food was beautifully presented, the colors rich and inviting, and the aroma made one genuinely hungry.

Jiang Mi was, in truth, a little hungry by now. But dining alongside Xiao Huaiyan — she would have willingly gone hungry instead.

Xiao Huaiyan rose and took his seat at the head of the table.

Reluctant as she was, Jiang Mi had no choice but to follow.

When she reached the table, she hesitated. She was not yet one of Xiao Huaiyan's concubines — it would be improper for her to sit and share the meal with him as an equal. The correct thing to do was surely to stand at his side, serve him food first, and only after he had eaten his fill would she be permitted to sit and eat.

Xiao Huaiyan's fondness for making things difficult for people truly hadn't changed one bit.

Xiao Huaiyan had just finished rinsing his hands in the water Chengzhong had poured for him, and was drying them with a cloth.

Then he saw Jiang Mi, standing at his side, pick up the silver chopsticks and place a slice of what appeared to be meat into the dish before him.

The attendant Chengzhong glanced at Miss Jiang in quiet astonishment. His Majesty disliked having others serve him food. Did Miss Jiang not know?

But seeing that His Majesty said nothing and simply took a bite of the bamboo shoot that had been shaped to look like meat, Chengzhong pressed his lips together and stood perfectly still, doing his best impression of a piece of furniture.

Jiang Mi swallowed quietly. That bamboo shoot was thick and plump, drenched in sauce — it must be wonderfully savory.

She watched Xiao Huaiyan finish it off in two or three bites. The flavor clearly wasn't doing much for him.

Jiang Mi lifted her sleeve and placed a segment of lotus root in his dish next. It was stuffed with something bright red — probably sweet and crisp.

She glanced at Xiao Huaiyan. He ate this one at a slightly slower pace, but finished it just as calmly.

Jiang Mi understood: he did not like this one either.

Over the course of the meal, she selected various dishes and gradually worked out from memory — drawing on everything she had learned of Xiao Huaiyan's tastes in her past life — which ones he genuinely enjoyed and which ones he merely tolerated.

Watching him eat with evident pleasure made something in Jiang Mi's chest feel suddenly uncomfortable.

She spotted a dish that resembled braised pork belly, but had apparently been made from radish.

Her eyes lit up, and she switched to a silver spoon to ladle some into Xiao Huaiyan's bowl.

His expression did not change. He tasted it.

She knew perfectly well that radish was the one thing he disliked most. But he hated to let others guess his preferences, so even when something didn't suit him, he would always take at least one bite.

The fragrance of the vegetarian dishes grew richer and more enticing. The more she served him, the more keenly her own hunger made itself known.

Xiao Huaiyan ate slowly, and she had no idea how long she would need to keep serving him.

She spotted another dish containing a spice she knew he disliked and transferred some to his bowl. Several dishes in a row were selected from ones she knew he couldn't stand, interspersed with a handful he could just barely tolerate.

Even for someone as composed as Xiao Huaiyan — who never allowed emotion to cross his face — he eventually set down his chopsticks and said, "Miss Jiang, please sit and eat."

Jiang Mi rubbed her aching hand. At least she could rest now.

Yet even with dishes that were perfect in color, aroma, and flavor laid before her, having Xiao Huaiyan seated right beside her dampened Jiang Mi's mood by half.

Famished as she was, she didn't rush to reach for the main dishes. She had had her eye on that emerald broth since the meal began.

It appeared to have been made from taro and wild mountain herbs — the creamy white broth floated delicate fragments of deep green leaf, and it looked quite lovely.

She ladled a bowl, blew gently on the steam, and when it had cooled a little, drank it in small, careful sips.

Just as she had expected, the broth was fragrant, silky, and refreshing. A few sips and her stomach felt warm and settled.

Only after that did she finally pick up her chopsticks.

After Jiang Mi had taken a few bites, the silent Xiao Huaiyan spoke. "We notice that vegetarian braised pork knuckle looks quite good — We shall give it to Miss Jiang as a gift."

Jiang Mi's hand stilled. The vegetarian braised pork knuckle had been made from yam, but it contained mushrooms, and she could not stand the taste of mushrooms.

But Xiao Huaiyan had declared it a gift, and the attendant Chengzhong was already setting the dish in front of her.

Jiang Mi: "..."

**

By the time the meal was over, Jiang Mi was utterly exhausted.

She had only reached for a few dishes before he discerned her preferences and, out of what could only have been deliberate mischief, presented her with that mushroom-filled vegetarian pork knuckle.

She was convinced that Xiao Huaiyan had been born to torment her.

Cradling a cup of warm tea, she worked hard to suppress the lingering taste of mushrooms.

An unfamiliar attendant entered, carrying a lacquered wooden box.

Xiao Huaiyan glanced at Jiang Mi. "Will Miss Jiang not stay and keep Us company for another round?"

"This subject daughter would not dare to further embarrass herself."

Xiao Huaiyan smiled. "Then do not keep your family waiting any longer, Miss Jiang."

Jiang Mi felt as though she had been granted a reprieve. He was finally letting her go. Inwardly she fumed — if he hadn't ordered someone to lure her here in the first place, she would have found her great-aunt long ago, and been able to enjoy these vegetarian dishes with a perfectly pleasant mood.

Instead she had been made to sit here and swallow her grievances.

Coming out of the room, Jiang Mi saw Qiuyu.

Once they were well out of earshot, Qiuyu pressed a hand to her chest. "That gave me such a fright, Miss. You don't know — the moment we entered that courtyard, a man dressed all in black covered my mouth and whisked me away without a single sound. I was so worried something had happened to you, I nearly went out of my mind. It was only when Chengzhong came to explain that I knew His Majesty was here too."

"Qiuyu," said Jiang Mi, "what happened today is not to be spoken of to anyone."

Qiuyu nodded emphatically. "You can count on me, Miss. I won't breathe a word."

……

The two of them had not walked far when the novice monk who had shown them the way reappeared, and they were soon escorted without incident to the meditation courtyard where the Marchioness was waiting.

The moment Jiang Mi stepped inside, she found Jiang Yi sitting with her head bowed, squeezing a fresh persimmon in her hands.

The Marchioness stood with her back turned, scolding Jiang Yi. "What is wrong with the second young master? He is handsome, presentable, dignified in bearing — what on earth is there to be dissatisfied about?"

Jiang Yi pouted. "Mother, are your eyes failing you? He's so dark — like a piece of charcoal! What's handsome about that? I refuse. I won't agree."

The Marchioness was so furious she could have taken a feather duster to her. "You refuse! Do you know how many young women would jump at the chance?"

"He's just been darkened from training with the army — it's not permanent! Besides, a man can't be judged on looks alone. I can see from his manner that he's upright and decent. Your own half-sisters are all craning their necks to watch and waiting in line! What you're throwing away is a prize!"

Jiang Yi hurled the persimmon across the room. "Then let them have him! I'm not marrying him, and that's final!"

The persimmon rolled all the way to Jiang Mi's feet. Jiang Yi happened to look up and spot her.

"Tangtang! Where have you been? What kind of scenery was so wonderful that you missed the midday meal for it?"

The Marchioness turned around. She hadn't finished fuming, but the sight of Jiang Mi softened her expression. "Tangtang, have you eaten? Should I have the novice monks bring you another portion?"

Jiang Mi shook her head. "Great-aunt, I had a meal in another vegetarian hall. I was so taken with the autumn maple leaves on the hillside that I lost track of time. Please forgive me."

"No matter — we only just finished ourselves." The Marchioness settled back into her seat and drained the cup of tea in her hand, working to smooth the irritation in her chest.

"Mother, since Tangtang is back, shouldn't we be heading home?" Jiang Yi had no desire to linger; she was worried she might run into that 'piece of charcoal' again.

The Marchioness saw right through her and shot her a sharp look.

In her past life, Jiang Mi had not accompanied her great-aunt to Qianshuang Temple for the matchmaking, and so she hadn't known that Jiang Yi would take such a strong dislike to the young official's son.

And yet, in that past life, Jiang Yi had ended up marrying him in the end.

She had heard that after the wedding, Jiang Yi and her husband had quarreled frequently and the marriage had been a rocky one. But when the Jiang family fell from grace, the young official had shielded Jiang Yi. Though he'd had no power to intervene in the Jiang family's affairs, he had quietly sent money to help them in secret.

The Marchioness's judgment had not been wrong at all.

But it was not Jiang Mi's place to speak now. Since in her past life Jiang Yi had eventually married him, their fate was already entwined — let it unfold as it would.

Worn down by Jiang Yi's pestering, the Marchioness relented and agreed to head home.

Jiang Mi fell in behind the Marchioness as they walked out. The whole party had just passed through the main gate of Qianshuang Temple when a sudden tremor went through the air.

The deep, heavy sound of something crashing to the ground.

Jiang Mi stopped in her tracks and turned instinctively to look back.

The Marchioness was equally startled. What had just happened?

Several worshippers hurried past them, buzzing with talk.

"Who would have thought — that hundred-year-old wishing tree has actually collapsed."

"Terrifying. Thank heavens no one was standing near it just then, or there would have been casualties."

"Out of nowhere — why did it fall like that? Is this some kind of ill omen?"

"Don't talk nonsense. I heard one of the monks say the tree had been rotting from the inside for some time, and with the winds this past several days, and no proper maintenance done, it simply gave way."

The Marchioness drew Jiang Mi close. "Thank heaven you are safe. The guilt would have been more than I could bear otherwise."

A vague sense of unease settled in Jiang Mi's chest.

When she had been there hanging her cloth strip, the wishing tree had been in full, vibrant health — branches thick with leaves, brimming with life. It had shown not the slightest sign of decay.

And besides — these past few days' winds strong enough to topple a towering, century-old tree?

……

After the Marchioness and Jiang Yi had boarded their carriage, Jiang Mi made her way to her own.

She had taken Qiuyu's hand and was about to step up when a sharp, thin voice called from behind her. "Miss Jiang, please wait."

Jiang Mi paused and turned around — and there was Chengzhong, carrying a tray and walking toward her.

He smiled pleasantly. "Miss Jiang, this is a gift from the master."

On the tray lay a book and a set of chess pieces.

Jiang Mi had no idea what to make of it.

"Perhaps the miss might take them home and study at her leisure," Chengzhong said.

Reluctant as she was to accept, she had no choice — she took the items with both hands and offered her thanks.

Once inside the carriage, she idly flipped through the book. It was a chess manual.

She truly could not fathom what Xiao Huaiyan was thinking. He had given her a half-dead spring orchid and told her to tend it well. He had bestowed upon her the temperamental famous zither, Lüqi, and somehow she had managed to play it beautifully. Now he had given her a chess manual and chess pieces — what game was he playing this time?

Jiang Mi tossed the book aside in vexation.

chapters.title: Eighteen
chapters.content:
"Your Majesty, it appears that the Shen and Jiang families were at Qianshuang Temple today by sheer coincidence, with no prior connection between them. The Shen family came to light an offering lamp for the soul of their late daughter born of the first wife. As for the Marchioness of Cheng'en, her purpose was to arrange a marriage with the family of the Ministry of Revenue's Director, and the two families had agreed to meet there for an introduction." Having finished his report, Chengzhong stood with head bowed.

In the fading glow of the evening sun, the man's figure stood half-veiled in light and shadow. He extended one finger and lifted the lid of the lacquered box. Inside, resting silently, was a red cloth strip inscribed with a blessing.

He took it out. Written on the strip in delicate, ornate clerical script were the words: "Peace and prosperity — may I find a worthy husband."

The man let out a quiet, dismissive laugh. "We are no worthy husband."

He tossed the cloth strip carelessly back into the box.

"Send Pei Chi in."

Pei Chi had been waiting in the cold wind outside for some time. Though he had changed into his flying-fish-patterned official robes, nothing could conceal the scent of blood that seemed to emanate from the very marrow of his bones. A murderous aura hung heavily between his brows, and the scar at his brow's peak made his expression all the more menacing.

"The remnant forces of the Qi Prince's faction had been lying low in the Jiangnan region. This time, however, they dispatched a company of assassins to the capital. One of them made contact with a hidden operative inside the palace, but when this minister followed the trail to apprehend them, they found themselves cornered and crushed the poison sacs hidden in their mouths. Only one was left alive. From him, we learned that their mission was to retrieve a certain object — though they themselves did not know precisely what it was. This minister is at fault: the surviving captive could not withstand the interrogation and, catching this minister off guard, bit off his own tongue and died. He took his own life."

Pei Chi could not comprehend what could possibly be worth the risk of exposure that the Qi Prince's remnants were willing to take in sending people all the way to the capital as backup.

Xiao Huaiyan's narrow, phoenix-shaped eyes were utterly still, betraying nothing. He said mildly, "Is there any movement from the Duke of Zhenguo's household?"

"For the moment, all is calm," Pei Chi replied. "The Duke of Zhenguo's eldest son is still confined to his bed. However, the Grand Princess will be hosting a flower banquet in ten days' time — ostensibly as entertainment, but in truth to select a bride for the heir."

A smile curved Xiao Huaiyan's lips. "Interesting."

……

Seated inside her carriage, Jiang Mi kept feeling as though the scent of dragon-ambergris incense clung to her and would not dissipate.

It must have rubbed off on her during the time she had spent in Xiao Huaiyan's presence.

That scent was just like the man himself — impossible to shake off, impossible to feel at ease around.

When they arrived back at Yuanzhi Courtyard, Jiang Mi's voice was urgent. "Qiuyu, have Xiaro light the black aloeswood incense. And tell the kitchen to send hot water — I want a bath."

Qiuyu could hear the urgency in her mistress's tone and did not dare delay.

But then she noticed the book and chess set still left in the carriage and asked, "Miss, where shall I put the book and chess pieces?"

Jiang Mi paused. "Lock them away with the zither," she said.

She had her own chess board at home and had no wish to use anything Xiao Huaiyan had given her. Out of sight, out of mind.

Jiang Mi soaked in a long, comfortable bath and washed her hair while she was at it. Now she reclined on the beauty couch, letting Chunhui help dry her hair with fragrant smoke.

Breathing in the clean, understated scent of black aloeswood, she finally felt at peace.

Seeing that Jiang Mi looked on the verge of drifting off, Chunhui murmured softly, "Miss, you haven't had dinner yet."

Jiang Mi shook her head drowsily. "I don't want anything — I don't want anything. Let me just rest my eyes for a moment."

She was genuinely exhausted.

……

In a water pavilion during the height of early summer, a man spoke in a low voice. "Since you lost the bet, take off your shoes."

The palace attendants serving nearby had all quietly retreated.

The young woman bit her lip and shook her head, backing away with a look of helpless dismay.

Behind the carved railing lay a pond of lotus flowers. The night breeze carried their fragrance, cool and clean, but it brought no comfort to the beauty whose eyes shimmered with unshed tears.

"Your... Your Majesty..."

"Do not make Us say it twice."

She looked around with tear-filled eyes at the empty pavilion and the open expanse of lake — there was no one left but herself and the man.

The gauze curtains billowed in the wind off the water. As though resigning herself to fate, she slowly drew her small, delicate feet out from beneath her skirt.

The embroidered slippers were exquisite — fine needlework embellished with clusters of pearls, beautiful and refined.

First the slippers fell, and then the silk stockings.

Her jade-pale feet curled inward as though trying to hide. Before she could even react, a sudden cold encircled her ankle — a golden and jade anklet bell had been clasped around it.

Her ankle was gripped with force. The clear, sweet sound of the bell rang out across the lakeside for half the night.

……

Jiang Mi, sleeping peacefully, furrowed her brow and kicked her right leg forward, as though struggling to shake off something that held her bound.

The movement startled Xiaro, who had been drying her hair with the incense smoke. She rushed forward to steady her. "Miss, careful — don't fall!"

Jiang Mi opened her eyes, momentarily disoriented. She breathed for a moment to calm herself, and then understood — she had been dreaming again.

Qiuyu poured a cup of calming floral tea and brought it over. "Miss, were you having a nightmare again?"

Jiang Mi took the cup, inhaled its fragrance, and drank a slow sip.

"If only we'd gotten a protective amulet at Qianshuang Temple before leaving," Qiuyu said, "and put it under your pillow — you'd be able to sleep soundly then."

Jiang Mi shook her head again and again. "Not one from Qianshuang Temple. Those aren't effective."

"Why do you say that, Miss? Hasn't Qianshuang Temple always had a reputation for being efficacious?" Xiaro chimed in. "Though back in my hometown village, we had a folk remedy for nightmares — you put a pair of scissors under your pillow. After that, the bad dreams stop."

Jiang Mi looked at her skeptically. "Really?"

Xiaro looked a little embarrassed. "It's just something my family used, Miss. It worked for me, though I don't know if it would help you."

Jiang Mi thought about it. Scissors were a sharp implement — perhaps they could ward off something malevolent.

No harm in trying.

"Go and find a pair of scissors, then," she said.

Xiaro went off to look for some.

Whether it was the scissors that truly did the trick, she couldn't say, but Jiang Mi slept the rest of the night without dreaming of her past life again.

The next morning she had a small bowl of congee, intending to head out to the bookshop afterwards, when Chunhui appeared in the doorway. "Miss, the Second Young Miss has come to see you."

Jiang Yi? What was she doing here?

Jiang Yi swept in with her skirts gathered in her hands, breezing up to Jiang Mi like a gust of wind and seizing her hand with breathless urgency. "Tangtang, come with me. We're going out."

Jiang Mi was nearly pulled off her feet. "Where — where are we going?"

Jiang Yi looked left and right, seeming hesitant.

Jiang Mi sent Qiuyu and the others outside.

"All right, Elder Sister Yi. You can speak now."

Jiang Yi squirmed, looking a little awkward. "It's just... just that I need to go find that piece of charcoal from yesterday."

"You want to meet with Young Master Zhang in private?" Jiang Mi was genuinely taken aback.

"Ugh, stop saying 'meet in private.' I just want to speak plainly with him. Make things clear."

She twisted her handkerchief. "My mother doesn't seem ready to give up, and I'm worried she might actually agree. So I thought — if I go to Young Master Zhang and make it clear from my end, then as long as he tells his family he's not interested either, even if Mother is still keen, there'll be nothing to it. The match falls through on its own."

Jiang Mi had not expected this plan. She didn't approve. "Elder Sister Yi, I don't think this is a good idea. If Great-aunt found out, she would never forgive you."

"Tangtang, the Empress Dowager is managing your marriage for you — you don't need to worry about these things. But I don't have it so easy. Mother pushes me to go on these introductions, I find someone I dislike, and then she lectures me on top of it. I just want to know if he's interested too. If he isn't either, then we both walk away happy — why should we be tied to each other in misery? Tangtang, will you help or not? If you won't, I'll find another way to get out myself!"

Jiang Mi could see there was no talking her out of it. And if she went alone, at least Jiang Mi could keep an eye on things.

She had no choice but to agree.

……

Jiang Yi had told the Marchioness they were going out to look at cosmetics together, and got permission without any trouble.

In the carriage, Jiang Mi ventured to ask, "Elder Sister Yi, if you don't like Young Master Zhang, is it because you already have someone in mind?"

Jiang Yi gave a little laugh. "Someone in mind? Not yet, really. Though I've paid attention to the eligible young men in the capital."

Slightly embarrassed but emboldened by Jiang Mi's curious expression — so unusual on someone who was otherwise better at everything — Jiang Yi opened the floodgates: "Among girls of good family, aside from those hoping to enter the palace and serve the Emperor, many are in love with Shen Qianxiu, the third son of Grand Secretary Shen's household. The heir of the Duke of Zhenguo, Xue Jinglin, would also be a splendid match, but his health is poor and he rarely appears in public these days. Then there's the third son of the Zhenguo household, Xue Jinglan, and the young general of the Loyal and Brave Marquis household, Gu Chang. Beyond them, there are all the young lords of the various marquis and earl families, or those waiting for the spring examination results. These are all the men women are eager to marry! But do you know who no one wants to marry — who everyone is actually afraid of?"

Jiang Mi: "Who?"

Jiang Yi lowered her voice. "The Commander of the Embroidered Guard, Pei Chi."

She let out a small breath of relief, then added, "Though none of this is really your concern — you just have to wait until it's time to enter the palace."

Jiang Mi only smiled and said nothing.

When they arrived, Jiang Mi and Jiang Yi put on their veiled hats. A handful of maids followed behind them as they entered the restaurant.

Jiang Yi had learned that Young Master Zhang regularly came to this restaurant for his meals, so the plan was to wait here and catch him.

All the private rooms were taken, leaving only some open seats on the second floor.

Jiang Yi chose a spot by the window and fixed her eyes on the street below and on anyone climbing the stairs.

Jiang Mi, meanwhile, was far more interested in the menu.

After listening to the server recite a long list of dishes, she picked out a few and placed the order.

"Sister Yi, is there anything else you'd like?"

Jiang Yi waved a hand. "No, no, you decide."

Jiang Mi seldom had the chance to go out, and even more rarely visited a proper restaurant. She felt a thread of curiosity and excitement.

When all the dishes arrived, Jiang Yi found the veiled hat too cumbersome and took it off.

Seeing this, Jiang Mi followed her example.

After only a few bites, Jiang Yi reached for her water cup several times in quick succession. "Tangtang, why did you order so many spicy dishes?"

Jiang Mi herself was beginning to sweat a little, and felt slightly sheepish. She had only been paying attention to how nice the names sounded.

Jiang Yi called the server back and added two more dishes.

As the two of them ate, a table of men diagonally across the room kept glancing their way.

Among them, a slightly stout young man in lake-blue brocade hiccuped, swayed to his feet with his wine cup in hand, and began weaving his way toward Jiang Mi's table. The other men — flushed red from drinking — followed along behind him.

"This sour-braised goose and duck is excellent — Tangtang, you must try some." Jiang Yi pointed to a dish on her left.

Jiang Mi was about to reach for her chopsticks when she noticed the group heading their way.

Qiuyu and Jiang Yi's maid Caixing stepped forward, blocking their path. "What do you think you're doing?"

The unsteady young man raised his wine cup and leered. "Who's your family, little ladies? I've come over to offer a toast and get acquainted — what do you say?"

One of the men behind him called out, "This here is the heir of the Duke of Xie! For him to take notice of you ladies is your good fortune — why not accept the toast?"

Jiang Yi had a sharp temper; she was furious enough to curse them aloud. But there were too many of them, and she worried they'd get the worse of it. She was just about to announce their family name when Jiang Mi pressed a hand on her arm.

Jiang Mi frowned and drew Jiang Yi back a step or two. In a place like this, it was not wise for a woman to reveal her identity. If word got out, the consequences would fall entirely on her — being harassed at a restaurant might barely matter to the man involved, a mere passing anecdote, but for a young lady of good family, any rumor of impropriety would be deeply damaging to her reputation.

Jiang Mi spoke up. "So the title of heir to the Duke of Xie can just be claimed by anyone these days, can it?"

This caught them all off guard.

"I have heard that Miss Xie Mingshan of the Duke of Xie's household is learned, virtuous, and graceful. How could she have a brother like this — a useless drunkard? This must be an impostor. Qiuyu, send someone to report this to the authorities!"

The drunken men finally grasped that this young woman was insulting them in the most roundabout of ways.

Emboldened by wine, one of them shoved the maid aside. "Little miss, it seems you'd rather take the harder way."

The maid was on the verge of being knocked to the ground when a door to a nearby private room swung open and a young man stepped out. "Stop!"

Three more men in scholarly robes followed close behind him, all of them wearing expressions of righteous indignation.

Jiang Mi looked up. The young man in Confucian robes — was that not Shen Qianxiu, whom she had met at Qianshuang Temple?

chapters.title: Nineteen
chapters.content:
"Who do you think you are? Mind your own business!"

Fortified by drink and confident in their numbers, the rowdy group paid little mind to what appeared to be a handful of bookish scholars.

Shen Qianxiu stood straight and tall as a stem of green bamboo, stepping between the two sides. His voice was cold. "Under Great Wei's laws, harassing a civilian woman carries penalties ranging from flogging to exile or even execution. I trust that no one here wishes to test that law upon themselves."
