One of the scholars behind him nodded vigorously. "Indeed. Accosting a woman in broad daylight — where is your sense of decency?"

Xie Xupeng's head was already swimming with wine. He squinted at the man speaking to him, vaguely recognizing the face but unable to place it in his muddled state. But he had just been publicly rebuffed by a beauty, and his wounded pride had turned into blazing fury. He snarled, "Ha! The law! Let me show you all what the law really is! Break it! Break everything! Let's see who dares to stick their nose in this after that!"

With a crash, one of his attendants stepped forward and upended the table, sending bowls and dishes shattering across the floor.

Jiang Yi let out a frightened cry, her face pale with alarm.

Jiang Mi's face had drained of color too. She had not expected this man to be so brazen. She tightened her grip on Jiang Yi's hand, trying to calm her.

Shen Qianxiu's expression darkened. "Xie heir, you will regret this!"

Xie Xupeng, looking at the beauty's frightened, helpless expression, felt an even more intolerable itch beneath his skin. A surge of hot-blooded recklessness rose through him. "Regret? Ridiculous! I am going to be the future imperial brother-in-law! And not only am I going to rough people up today — I'm taking the little beauty home with me too. Come on, men — grab her!"

The mob of drunken wastrels began shoving and jostling against the scholars and maids who stood in their way.

Shen Qianxiu stepped in front of Jiang Mi, catching the arm of the man who had lunged forward. Drunk and unsteady, the man was easily overpowered — Shen Qianxiu twisted his arm and sent him sprawling to the ground.

But for every one that fell, others surged forward to take their place.

Shen Qianxiu was a man of letters, yet he planted himself firmly between Jiang Mi and the crowd, keeping her shielded at his back.

Just as the brawl threatened to spiral completely out of control, a sharp, orderly rush of footsteps sounded from below — firm, measured, and uniform.

A moment later, a man in flying-fish-patterned robes with an embroidered spring saber at his hip came striding up the stairs, followed by a file of fierce-eyed Embroidered Guards.

The man's gaze swept the room like a hawk's, sharp and cold, and when it landed on a figure who had no business being there, a flicker of surprise crossed his face.

"Embroidered Guard business. Who is causing this disturbance?"

At those words, two columns of Embroidered Guards stepped forward from behind him, surrounding the troublemakers, and drew their embroidered spring sabers with a cold, gleaming hiss.

The group of drunken young wastrels who had just been shouting for blood went rigid with a shock that sobered them halfway up in an instant.

How had the Embroidered Guard ended up here?

Someone immediately tried to grovel. "Lord Pei, this is all a misunderstanding — a misunderstanding! We were just having a little drink. We'd never get in the way of the Embroidered Guard's business!"

Pei Chi gave a cold laugh. "Is that so? And yet it seems to this official that someone in this room just claimed to be the future imperial brother-in-law. His Majesty has not yet named an Empress — so exactly who has the audacity to go around calling themselves his in-law?"

The question killed every voice in the room.

Xie Xupeng's legs went weak. Cold sweat poured down his back.

"Why so quiet all of a sudden?" Pei Chi asked again.

Since Pei Chi had appeared, Jiang Mi's expression had grown complicated.

Pei Chi was Xiao Huaiyan's most trusted lieutenant. In her past life, she had barely crossed paths with him — only catching a glimpse of him on the occasional visit to see Xiao Huaiyan. She had never understood why, in that life, Pei Chi had forced her younger sister, Jiang Rong, to become his concubine. He was nearly a full twelve years older than Jiang Rong at least.

"Lord Pei."

Into the silence, a soft voice spoke.

Pei Chi raised an eyebrow and turned toward the speaker.

Jiang Mi continued: "Might I ask, Lord Pei — does the Embroidered Guard handle cases of harassment, attempted abduction of a civilian woman, and the beating of students from the Imperial Academy?"

"Oh?" Pei Chi looked again at Xie Xupeng. "There is such a matter as well?"

Shen Qianxiu stepped forward. "There is, and that is the truth of it."

Pei Chi recognized the third son of Grand Secretary Shen. He looked once more at the Empress Dowager's niece — the Jiang family's young miss.

"If what the young miss says is accurate, then the Embroidered Guard will most certainly handle it."

"Take them all in. Interrogate them properly — and while you're at it, we might just manage to smoke out that man who called himself the future imperial brother-in-law."

At Pei Chi's command, the Embroidered Guards moved forward to make arrests.

Those who had been shouting for blood just moments ago now dropped to their knees begging for mercy.

Everyone knew that being taken in by the Embroidered Guard meant, at best, losing a layer of skin — and at worst, one's life.

"Pei Chi, you can't arrest me! My father is the Duke of Xie — my aunt is the Noble Consort Xian! How dare you?"

"Lord Pei, have mercy! I was drunk — I lost my head for a moment!"

"Lord Pei... Lord Pei, please spare us!"

After the Embroidered Guard took the men away, Pei Chi paused as he passed Jiang Mi, said nothing, then turned silently and descended the stairs.

Those who remained felt as though they had survived a disaster, and every last one of them exhaled in relief.

Jiang Mi had not truly expected Pei Chi to act on it. She had invoked the Embroidered Guard only as a way to intimidate Xie Xupeng — she had not imagined Pei Chi would actually order an arrest.

Seeing that Jiang Mi had fallen quiet and said nothing, Shen Qianxiu assumed she had been badly frightened, and began to speak. "Miss Jiang, actually..."

Thump thump thump — another group of people came pounding up the stairs.

This time it was officers from the capital prefecture.

The officer was breathless. "Young Master Shen, where are the criminals?"

Shen Qianxiu said evenly, "You've arrived too late, officer. They've already been taken by the Embroidered Guard."

The officer went pale. "What? The Embroidered — the Embroidered Guard?"

Since when did the Embroidered Guard take an interest in something like this?

This was not their jurisdiction at all.

……

"Miss Jiang, to prevent anything else from happening on the road, allow me to see you and your sister home," Shen Qianxiu offered.

Still shaken, Jiang Yi agreed immediately and was the first to climb into the carriage, leaning on her maid.

Jiang Mi looked up at Shen Qianxiu. "Thank you for your help today, Young Master Shen."

Shen Qianxiu shook his head. "I did very little."

Jiang Mi smiled. "You need not be modest, Young Master Shen. Had you not come out to intervene first, my sister and I would certainly have suffered for it."

Shen Qianxiu hesitated before speaking again. "Miss Jiang, it was not actually necessary to involve the Embroidered Guard." He had already sent someone to the prefectural office — once the officers arrived, they could have handled it. As for the Xie family's unruly behavior, there would be people to file a formal complaint in court. Although the Embroidered Guard had, unexplainably, chosen to step in today, the Guard's fearsome reputation was not something that reflected well on Miss Jiang.

"The lapse in judgment was mine," Jiang Mi admitted, turning it over in her mind. If she had known Young Master Shen had already sent for the authorities, she would not have said what she did. At the time, Xie Xupeng's men were clashing with Young Master Shen and the scholars, and though they had held them off for the moment, they were wearing thin — and it was the scholars who would end up bearing the brunt of it. That was why she had taken the risk.

Shen Qianxiu saw the shadow that crossed her face and worried he had spoken too harshly. He wanted to offer some comfort.

But the more he thought about it, the more he feared he might overstep, so he simply said, "Please board the carriage, Miss Jiang."

Once Jiang Mi was inside, Shen Qianxiu swung up onto his horse and rode ahead of the carriage.

Jiang Yi pushed aside the curtain for a look, then turned to Jiang Mi. "Shen the Third truly lives up to his reputation — a gentleman of refinement, warm and gracious as jade. No wonder so many women are smitten."

Jiang Mi found herself thinking of the way he had stood before her, shielding her with his own body. She lowered her gaze.

……

In the Imperial Study, Li Fu noticed that His Majesty was still going through the memorials, and stepped forward to replace his tea with a fresh, steaming cup.

Xiao Huaiyan opened a memorial from the Ministry of Rites, urging once again that the matter of selecting an Empress and consorts be advanced — attached was a newly drafted list of candidates.

He picked up his vermilion brush, and his gaze paused for a moment on two particular characters. Then he set the memorial aside.

He looked up, and found Li Fu wearing an expression of reluctant, uncertain duty.

Li Fu bowed. "Your Majesty, the Duke of Xie requests an audience. He has been waiting outside the palace for quite some time."

Xiao Huaiyan said, without much interest, "What does he want?"

"The Duke of Xie wishes to lodge a complaint against Lord Pei Chi, Commander of the Embroidered Guard, accusing him of wrongful arrest — of taking his son into custody without cause."

Li Fu, upon learning of the Duke of Xie's request for an audience, had already sent someone to make discreet inquiries.

As he learned the details, it turned out the matter was tangled up with that young miss from the Jiang household.

Without waiting to be asked, Li Fu recounted the full story from beginning to end.

Li Fu could not read His Majesty's mood; he only saw those dark, unfathomable eyes, deep as still water — enough to send a chill through one's heart.

"Send the Duke of Xie in," said Xiao Huaiyan. "And find Pei Chi."

chapters.title: Twenty
chapters.content:
"Your Majesty, you must uphold justice for this old servant! My worthless son had merely drunk a little too much and said a few foolish things, and Lord Pei had him arrested for it. Lord Pei has gone beyond his authority — this is an abuse of power, and a dereliction of duty!"

The Duke of Xie was a portly man. Kneeling on the ground and speaking with such agitation, he was breathless, his forehead damp with a sheen of perspiration.

He had assumed that by making such an elaborate show of deference — prostrating himself fully and giving every courtesy — a man of his rank and seniority would certainly be offered a seat. But after he finished speaking, the figure on the imperial throne showed not the slightest reaction.

The Duke of Xie, his belly full of barely suppressed fury, began to feel his heart quietly sinking.

Xiao Huaiyan set down his teacup and spoke at an unhurried, measured pace. "Is this the truth of it?"

Seeing the Emperor finally speak, the Duke of Xie could not contain his agitation. "Every word this minister says is true! This minister begs Your Majesty to do right by him! Even if my worthless boy had said something to offend Lord Pei in the heat of the moment, that is no reason to arrest him and throw him in the Embroidered Guard's prison! Lord Pei acted out of personal spite and jailed an innocent man — there is no fairness in this. I have no idea in what way we ever offended Lord Pei, that he would use his position for this kind of revenge."

The moment the Duke of Xie had been told that his eldest legitimate son had been arrested by Pei Chi, he had thought he was hearing wrong.

Upon pressing for details, he learned it was over no more than a matter of harassing some women, a scuffle with a few scholars — a trivial business.

The Embroidered Guard — those black-hearted, stone-blooded men — once in their hands, the very best outcome was losing a layer of skin.

Pei Chi had always done exactly as he pleased; now he had come for the Duke of Xie's household. Between fury and anxiety, the Duke had resolved to request an imperial audience and bring charges against the man.

Li Fu came in from outside and murmured quietly, "Your Majesty, Lord Pei has arrived."

"Summon him."

When the Duke of Xie heard that Pei Chi had arrived, he could no longer keep the anger from his face.

Pei Chi entered the hall, took in the sight of the Duke of Xie kneeling on the floor, and walked past him without a word. He pulled aside the hem of his robe and knelt on one knee before the imperial throne. "This minister pays his respects to Your Majesty."

"Rise, both of you."

The Duke of Xie and Pei Chi spoke their thanks in unison and stood.

The Duke of Xie had been on his knees for quite some time and rose with some difficulty.

Xiao Huaiyan spoke in a warm tone. "Bring a chair for the Duke of Xie."

Li Fu directed the attendants to carry over a chair.

The Duke of Xie was immediately overcome with gratitude, reassured that the Emperor still regarded him with favor.

Once the Duke was settled, he heard the Emperor address Pei Chi.

"Pei Chi — did you arrest the Duke of Xie's son?"

"Yes, Your Majesty. This minister was just about to report this matter to you."

"Speak."

"This minister was following a lead in the investigation of a treasonous faction when the trail took him to the Wangxian Restaurant. Upon reaching the ground floor, I heard someone loudly proclaiming himself to be the future imperial brother-in-law. I went upstairs to look into it. What I found was a man claiming to be the heir of the Duke of Xie — smashing tables, attacking people, and attempting to abduct a civilian woman. The third son of Grand Secretary Shen's household and several students from the Imperial Academy were in the midst of trying to stop him, and were being beaten as well. Since Your Majesty has not yet named an Empress, and here was a man going about calling himself the future imperial brother-in-law, I brought him in for a thorough interrogation — on the chance that we might have missed a suspicious element."

With every word Pei Chi spoke, the Duke of Xie's expression shifted — from composed ease to the ashen gray of someone utterly devastated. He was terrified to his very core.

How could he keep sitting still? He lurched to his feet. "That — that can't be right. My son would never say something so outrageous."

Pei Chi smiled pleasantly. "Your Grace, surely you have not only heard the servants' account without also making further inquiries? Half the entire Wangxian Restaurant heard it. Shall I have people brought in to confront him with the evidence?"

Seeing how certain Pei Chi was, the Duke recalled how his heir's attendants always told him only what he wanted to hear. This time, they must have hidden the most damning parts, not daring to repeat a word that smacked of treason.

The Duke of Xie collapsed to his knees with a thud, his voice trembling with fear. "This minister has failed in his duty as a father. I beg Your Majesty's forgiveness. My wretched son must have been drunk and led astray by bad company, or he would never have behaved so shamefully and spoken such words."

Xiao Huaiyan let out a low, quiet laugh. "The future imperial brother-in-law?"

"Duke Xie — does the Xie family harbor such ambitions?"

The Duke of Xie bowed his head even lower, his voice shaking. "This minister would not dare. This minister harbors no such presumptuous desires."

Xiao Huaiyan's fingers tapped lightly on the table. "We believe you, Your Grace. In that case, the Xie family's daughter's name need not be submitted any further."

The Duke of Xie's face went ashen as death, yet he had no choice but to nod. "Everything is at Your Majesty's discretion."

Xiao Huaiyan gave a slow sigh. "Your Grace, in the future, do not be so quick to listen only to one side of things — listen more broadly, consider all perspectives. If a man cannot cultivate himself, how can he govern his household, let alone serve the realm? If there is no improvement, the position of Minister of Revenue ought to be yielded to a more worthy candidate."

The Duke of Xie touched his forehead to the floor, his heart bleeding, his voice hoarse. "This minister will heed Your Majesty's sacred instructions."

"Li Fu," Xiao Huaiyan said, "accompany the Duke of Xie to the Embroidered Guard's prison and bring the Xie heir out. Since he has proven to be neither rebel nor traitor, let him go to the Capital Prefectural Office and receive his punishment according to the law."

The Duke of Xie bowed deeply to the imperial throne once more. "This minister is grateful for Your Majesty's magnanimous grace."

"Understood." Li Fu received the order and stepped to the Duke of Xie's side to help him up. He had the distinct sense that the Duke had aged ten years in a single afternoon.

Once Li Fu and the Duke of Xie had left, only Xiao Huaiyan and Pei Chi remained in the great hall.

The smile faded from Xiao Huaiyan's lips, his gaze growing dark and still. "Pei Chi. Do you know your offense?"

Pei Chi knelt. "This minister knows his offense."

Xiao Huaiyan regarded him for a moment. "Then go outside and take thirty lashes."

"This minister thanks Your Majesty."

……

Chengzhong stood outside the hall and watched as Commander Pei knelt at the foot of the steps to receive the lash.

He was genuinely baffled. Clearly the Duke of Xie's household was in the wrong — so why had His Majesty also punished Commander Pei?

But Pei Chi himself understood. His actions today had exceeded the bounds of his authority. He had attempted to second-guess the Emperor's intentions on his own. Thirty lashes were no more than he deserved.

……

When the Duke of Xie collected Xie Xupeng from the Embroidered Guard's prison, Xie Xupeng was a hollow shell of a man, frightened half out of his wits. Then he was taken to the Capital Prefectural Office, where he was flogged until his flesh split and bled, leaving him barely clinging to his last breath.

The Duke of Xie no longer had the heart to concern himself with whether this son lived or died. He sat in the main hall in a daze, deaf to the weeping of his wife and daughters.

Gone. Just like that.

The Xie family's last hope — gone, just like that?

His talented and beautiful legitimate daughter — forget becoming Empress; she would never have the chance to enter the palace at all now.

And it was her own brother who had brought this ruin upon them.

Glancing at the son who was still moaning in agony, there was a moment — just one — when the Duke wished he were dead.

After all, he still had other sons. He could always name a new heir.

His daughter could have entered the palace and brought glory to the entire family.

But now it was all dust and ashes.

Xie Mingshan looked at her brother — barely alive, suffering terribly — and was filled with grief. She looked up seeking comfort from her father, only to meet his gaze, and the expression in his eyes was so terrible that she did not dare utter a single word.

What on earth had happened? Why had the entire household been turned upside down in the space of a single night?

……

Jiang Mi and Jiang Yi had returned home and, naturally, did not dare conceal what had happened.

So the wrath of their elders descended upon them.

After Jiang Yi haltingly told the whole story, the Marchioness was so furious she nearly fainted. She summoned the Marquis of Cheng'en, Jiang Qingde, along with Jiang Qingxuan and Lady Su.

The Marquis, beside himself with rage, was on the verge of calling for the family rod to formally punish Jiang Yi. Only Jiang Mi and Jiang Qingxuan pleading on her behalf spared her from that — instead, she was sent to kneel before the ancestral shrine as punishment.

Jiang Mi, though not subjected to kneeling, was confined to Yuanzhi Courtyard under house arrest.

Jiang Qingxuan personally escorted Jiang Mi back and spoke in a measured, grave tone. "Today you were fortunate — it ended without real harm. But if something had truly gone wrong, the consequences would have been beyond recovery. Tangtang, you are destined to enter the palace. You cannot afford even a single misstep."

For reasons she could not quite name, a swell of grievance rose in Jiang Mi's chest.

Because she was destined to enter the palace, she was treated with special care. Her very existence revolved around entering the palace — around pleasing the Emperor for the sake of the Jiang family.

If she were to say she had no wish to enter the palace, people would only think her ungrateful and ignorant of her own good fortune.

On an impulse, Jiang Mi said, "Father — if your daughter had someone she cared for, would you still send her into the palace?"

Jiang Qingxuan stared at her, visibly shaken. "You... you..."

"Your daughter spoke foolishly. Father need not take it to heart." Jiang Mi offered him a small bow and walked inside.

……

Under house arrest in Yuanzhi Courtyard, Jiang Mi spent her days playing with Miangmiang, the little cat, and had Jiang Rong and Jiang Xuan visiting regularly, so her days passed in quiet leisure.

The maids mentioned that Elder Sister Yi had knelt through the entire night in the ancestral shrine and had caught a chill. The Marquis eventually took pity on her and let her return to her room to recover under house arrest. That chapter was finally closed.

Jiang Mi had worked through all the books she had with her and was bored. She had originally intended to visit the bookshop, but with the house arrest, she could only ask Qiuyu to go and see what might be found.

Just as she was about to call for Qiuyu, Xiaro came hurrying in with a bright, delighted expression. "Miss, the Empress Dowager has sent someone to bring you persimmon grapes from the Western Regions. The eunuch is asking to see you — he says the Empress Dowager has a message to pass along."

Jiang Mi sat up from the couch, a little surprised.

Her aunt usually just had things dropped off and left — why had she sent along a message this time?

"Show him in."

Xiaro led the man inside. The moment Jiang Mi looked at him more closely, something felt wrong. As he raised his head and smiled at her in greeting, she felt her breath catch.

"This servant pays his respects to Miss Jiang."

Jiang Mi's expression shifted, and she frowned. "You shouldn't have. Chengzhong — why are you here?"

Chengzhong offered a careful, placating smile. "The master sent this servant with a message."

Jiang Mi found this stranger than ever. What in the world did she have to do with his master? What message could there possibly be to convey?

She swallowed her questions and turned to Xiaro. "You may go."

"Yes, Miss."

Once Xiaro had left, Jiang Mi said, "You may speak now, Chengzhong."

Chengzhong privately thought Miss Jiang was quite a difficult person to please. Anyone else in her position would have been overwhelmed with excitement and flustered with shy delight by now.

Though — he was almost certain he had just seen a flash of something on Miss Jiang's face.

Panic, of all things.

But surely this was joyous news. What was there to panic about?

Chengzhong said with a smile, "Miss Jiang, the master wishes to see you. The carriage is waiting right outside."

He thought to himself — surely Miss Jiang would be pleased now? This kind of favor had never been shown to anyone before.

But a long moment passed with no response from her. Chengzhong looked up.

He was even more perplexed. Why had Miss Jiang's face gone a shade paler than before?

Jiang Mi clenched the handkerchief in her hands and forced a smile. "Chengzhong, I am afraid today will not be possible. I am still under house arrest and cannot leave freely."

"You need not worry, Miss," Chengzhong replied. "This servant has arranged everything — no one will notice. And if you are concerned, this servant could go and inform the Marquis of Cheng'en directly, only..." — only the master would be displeased.

Jiang Mi could not let Chengzhong go to her great-uncle. If he found out, he would only escort her there with the utmost deference.

Jiang Mi let out a slow breath of resignation. "No need. Lead the way."

chapters.title: Twenty-one
chapters.content:
From Yuanzhi Courtyard all the way to the western side gate, they did not encounter a single servant or maid. The journey was smooth and unimpeded. At the gate, a carriage had been waiting for some time.

The young eunuch standing guard caught sight of Chengzhong escorting that delicate guest out. He immediately leaped down from the carriage and hurried forward, bending low so the honored guest could use his back as a step to board.

Jiang Mi stepped back, about to tell him to stand up, when Chengzhong's voice snapped out. "Useless thing — can't you read the room? Go fetch the mounting stool at once, so Miss Jiang may board properly."

The young eunuch sprang up nimbly and set the prepared stool in place, bowing as he said, "Please, Miss."

Chengzhong stood to one side and held the curtain open with his arm, taking care not to brush against Miss Jiang's hair or her ornaments.

Jiang Mi stepped inside. Every detail her eyes landed on was exquisite.

The interior of the carriage was spacious and comfortable. On the small side table sat hot tea and pastries. But Jiang Mi was in no state of mind to appreciate any of it. She curled herself into a corner, filled with anxious dread, not knowing where this carriage was taking her.

Since the encounter at Qianshuang Temple, Jiang Mi had been utterly unable to fathom why he would send for her — why he would want to see her at all.

Xiao Huaiyan's thoughts were a mystery she could not begin to unravel.

……

At first, the carriage wound through busy streets, and the noise of the crowds outside helped to drive away some of the unease in her heart. Gradually, the voices faded. The surrounding quiet deepened.

The carriage slowed. Jiang Mi's heart lifted with dread once more.

At last, it stopped. Chengzhong's voice came from outside. "Miss Jiang, we have arrived."

Jiang Mi closed her eyes for a moment, tightened her grip on the handkerchief in her hands, and lifted the curtain.

This was not the palace. It was a private residence.

Jiang Mi followed Chengzhong inside. The garden had been laid out in the style of Jiangnan — graceful and intricate, filled everywhere with rare and extraordinary flowers and plants, as though autumn's desolation had no claim over them. One could only wonder how many skilled craftsmen had poured their hearts into cultivating such a place.

At a decorative rockery, Chengzhong stopped and said quietly, "Miss Jiang, His Majesty is waiting for you in the pavilion above. This servant cannot accompany you further."

He withdrew.

Jiang Mi could only smile bitterly to herself. Xiao Huaiyan's fondness for making things needlessly mysterious truly hadn't changed one bit.

She made her way up the stone steps in silence, and before long the view opened up before her.

In the pavilion ahead sat a young man dressed in robes the color of moonlight. He had just set down his teacup and, hearing a sound nearby, turned his head in her direction.

His bearing was elegant and refined — the very picture of a graceful, accomplished gentleman.

Even as she feared him, Jiang Mi could not help but concede that Xiao Huaiyan was, in outward appearance, remarkably fine.

She looked away quietly and dipped into a curtsy. "This subject daughter pays her respects to Your Majesty. Long may Your Majesty reign."

Xiao Huaiyan gave a soft laugh. "The Marchioness of Cheng'en's household does keep good manners. Since We are traveling incognito, there is no need for such formality. Come here."

Those three words sent a prickling sensation across Jiang Mi's scalp, yet she had no choice but to obey. Every step felt heavy.

The closer she drew, the more clearly she caught the scent of dragon-ambergris incense from his person — a fragrance that staked its claim without a word, announcing that she had entered his territory.

Seeing her stiffness, Xiao Huaiyan said mildly, "Sit."

There were no palace attendants in the pavilion. On the white jade round table sat a gently steaming teapot and several fine porcelain cups. And in the center of the table — impossible to ignore — was a chess board, with an unfinished game left upon it.

Jiang Mi sat down across from Xiao Huaiyan. The white pieces were on her side.

Xiao Huaiyan picked up one of the porcelain cups with easy grace, lifted the steaming teapot, and poured Jiang Mi a cup — the motion as fluid and natural as flowing water.

Jiang Mi was so alarmed she rose to her feet at once. "This subject daughter — this subject daughter could not dare —"

She could not accept tea poured by Xiao Huaiyan himself. The moment he showed her any form of kindness, every instinct she had told her that nothing good would follow.

Xiao Huaiyan said, "There is no need for nerves. We invited you here to play chess — at the very least, We should offer you a cup of tea."

Chess.

He had gone to all this trouble simply to have her play chess with him?

Jiang Mi relaxed by the smallest degree. For the moment, she had no real connection with him and was not yet one of his consorts. Such a reason was, at least, believable.

Though she remembered that in her past life, he hadn't particularly cared for chess. When he had made her play with him back then, it had only ever been a way to torment her.

Xiao Huaiyan noticed that the expression across from him had just barely eased before furrowing again. He couldn't tell what she was thinking.

He turned his teacup in his fingers and said, "If Miss Jiang does not mind, shall we pick up from this existing game?"

Jiang Mi nodded and took up a white piece.

She had made up her mind: she would follow Xiao Huaiyan's lead for a time, then lose as quickly as possible.

Just as she had done at Qianshuang Temple.

Before long, the outcome was clear. Jiang Mi put up not the slightest resistance and lost with swift efficiency.

This time, however, Xiao Huaiyan showed a certain patience. When one game ended, he began the next.

Jiang Mi naturally obliged.

Three games in a row — and Jiang Mi lost every one.

The look of shame on her face grew more pronounced. She said quietly, "Your Majesty's skill is formidable. This subject daughter concedes completely."

Xiao Huaiyan turned a black piece idly between his fingers and gave a light laugh. "Somehow We have the impression that Miss Jiang possesses an uncanny foreknowledge — as though she knows which moves lead to a dead end before she even arrives at them."

Jiang Mi rose and offered an apology. "This subject daughter's chess is truly nothing worth showing. I have spoiled Your Majesty's pleasure."

"The last time at Qianshuang Temple, Miss Jiang said she would go home and practice diligently to improve her game. How is it she has made no progress at all?"

Jiang Mi was still weighing how to answer when she heard Xiao Huaiyan ask, "Did you read the book We gave you?"

She froze.

The book he gave her? Could he mean the chess manual she had locked away without a second glance?

She couldn't possibly tell him the truth. She answered with unease, "This subject daughter is truly slow-witted. Even having read it, I could not bring all the lessons to bear."

Xiao Huaiyan responded with a noncommittal smile, his tone laden with meaning. "Is that so?"

Jiang Mi said, "This subject daughter's chess is poor. If Your Majesty seeks a worthy opponent, there are surely those with far greater skill to be found."

"Oh? It seems Miss Jiang already has someone in mind to recommend." Xiao Huaiyan's smile did not change.

But drawing on the experience of a past life spent in his company, Jiang Mi caught a faint thread of danger beneath his words.

She pressed her lips together and said no more. The color had drained from her face, and her expression was one of barely concealed distress.

Xiao Huaiyan's gaze darkened. For some reason, the image of her turning back to smile under the wishing tree rose unbidden in his mind.

He realized then that Jiang Mi had never once smiled in his presence. What she showed him was only fear and unease.

He could not help but ask, "What exactly is it that you fear about Us? Even when you lose at chess — do you think We would punish you for it?"

The question struck Jiang Mi like a blow from another life. She heard it in a different voice, teasing and cruel: "Lose the game and take your punishment — isn't that right, Noble Consort Jiang?"

Past life and present collapsed into one.

Panic seized her. She wanted to flee at once. Her arm swung out — and sent the cup of tea Xiao Huaiyan had poured for her skidding off the table and shattering on the ground, the liquid splashing onto her clothes.

But that sharp, sudden sound was what pulled her back from the edge.

chapters.title: Twenty-two
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi stumbled into a flustered bow. "This subject daughter has been improper before Your Majesty — please forgive her."

She was trembling, so unsteady she looked as though a breath of wind would topple her.

Xiao Huaiyan looked at her like this, and something in him that had been sharpened went dull.

He raised a hand. "Enough. You may go."

Jiang Mi thought she had misheard. Xiao Huaiyan was letting her go — this easily?

She recovered herself quickly, schooling the flicker of relief from her eyes. "This subject daughter thanks Your Majesty for his grace."

The surroundings were utterly still.

The man in the seat above said nothing in return.

Her heart in her throat, Jiang Mi did not dare linger. She kept her head low and withdrew step by careful step, never once lifting her gaze to look at Xiao Huaiyan.

The smile on Xiao Huaiyan's lips had faded. His narrow phoenix eyes were dark and cold.

He sat in silence and watched Jiang Mi's retreating figure. As she descended the stone steps one by one, the hem of her skirt lifted just enough to reveal the embroidered slippers beneath.

In an instant, it was as though he heard a bell ringing.

The sound was clear and lingering.

Her slender figure disappeared from view.

The sound of the bell did not.

Xiao Huaiyan pressed a hand to his temple. The sound grated at him, fraying his composure.

A surge of violent feeling rose from somewhere deep, unbidden. He swept a hand across the jade table. The chess manual flew off and struck the ground with a crack, sending black and white pieces scattering in every direction.

……

Still not quite herself, Jiang Mi made her way down from the pavilion. Chengzhong was waiting for her at the rockery.

He stepped forward with a smile. "This servant will escort Miss Jiang back to the residence."

Jiang Mi nodded. "Thank you for the trouble, Chengzhong."

Settled back in the carriage, she wrapped both hands around a cup of hot tea and drank. Only slowly did the rigid cold that had settled into her body begin to thaw.

Her behavior today had very likely angered Xiao Huaiyan.

But she could not control her fear. She only wanted to be as far from him as possible.

She could only hope that after this, Xiao Huaiyan would put the idea of summoning her for chess out of his mind entirely.

It would make this frightening ordeal worth something, at least.

The carriage rolled out of the quiet streets and back into the busy thoroughfares.

Jiang Mi's mind settled a little. She noticed that the afternoon was still young — it seemed a shame to come all this way and not stop by the bookshop.

She lifted the curtain and called out, "Chengzhong, I should like to visit Zhiyuan Bookshop on the way. Would that be convenient?"

Chengzhong agreed readily and directed the driver to pull up before the shop.

"Take your time, Miss," he said. "This servant will wait out here."

Jiang Mi put on her veiled hat, stepped down from the carriage, and walked inside alone.

At this hour, the bookshop was not crowded. A handful of customers moved quietly through the shelves, each browsing for what they needed. Some were academy students searching for works by renowned masters; others were young ladies in search of the newest popular tales.

Jiang Mi picked out several of the latest stories herself, but what she most wanted to find was a travel account written by someone who went by the name Songxun.

She had once read his account of Suzhou — a record of everything he had seen and heard there: the local customs and way of life, the food, the ancient landmarks and famous mountains. It was witty and full of fresh insight, the kind of writing that made a reader ache to go there themselves.

She had never left the capital. Places she had only heard of filled her with curiosity — she wanted so much to see them with her own eyes.

Jiang Mi searched the shelves carefully. She could not find it.

She flagged down a shop assistant and asked, "Why is there nothing by Songxun?"

The young man heard such a pleasant voice and blinked, momentarily dazed. Even with her hat on, it was clear this was a beauty.

He rallied his enthusiasm. "Master Songxun's books sell very well — a new shipment came in just a few days ago and was nearly all gone at once. Let me go check the back storeroom for you."

"Thank you." Jiang Mi said.

The assistant went off with energy to spare.

Jiang Mi stood before the shelves and picked up a poetry collection to flip through.

Then a book with a blue cover appeared before her, held out by someone's hand. "Is this the one?"

The voice was faintly familiar. Jiang Mi looked up — and found Shen Qianxiu.

"Young Master Shen?" she said in surprise. "What brings you here!"

Shen Qianxiu gave her a gentle smile. "I was browsing for books myself, and just now I heard Miss Jiang asking the shop assistant. I recognized her voice, and so I came over — I hope I am not intruding."

Jiang Mi removed her veiled hat and smiled at him. "How could that be intruding?"

She took the book from him. "This is exactly what I was looking for. Another word of thanks is owed to you, Young Master Shen."

"It was nothing at all. Think nothing of it." He noticed how happy she looked holding the travel account and asked, "Does Miss Jiang enjoy this sort of reading?"

Jiang Mi nodded. "Though it's a pity I cannot do as the author did — travel ten thousand li, roam the mountains and rivers, experience the different customs of different places in person — still, the author brings all of it to life on the page, and reading it feels almost as though I have been there myself."

Shen Qianxiu was quiet for a moment before he said, "Life is long, Miss Jiang. How can you be certain you won't go one day?"

Jiang Mi was caught off guard. Then she laughed — a real laugh, her eyes bright as stars. "You're right. I'll take that as a good omen, Young Master Shen."

Shen Qianxiu lost himself for an instant. He forced his gaze away to look elsewhere.

Jiang Mi, entirely unaware, was already leafing quickly through the pages of the book in her hands.

"Is Miss Jiang here alone?" he asked. "No maid with her?"

The hand turning the page paused. She thought of Chengzhong and the others waiting outside, and something in her mood dimmed.

She shook her head. "They're waiting outside. I should be heading back, Young Master Shen."

Jiang Mi replaced her hat and waved the shop assistant over to have her selections wrapped up.

Shen Qianxiu restrained the impulse to offer to pay for her books.

When she had collected her parcel, he bowed to her. "Until we meet again, Miss Jiang."

Jiang Mi smiled and dipped into a return curtsy.

……

After Jiang Mi left, Shen Qianxiu picked out a few books at random and departed as well.

He had not walked far when a maid rushed up to call after him. "Third Young Master, Third Young Master! The Fourth Miss is waiting in a private room at the teahouse across the way — she sent this servant to bring you over."

Shen Qianxiu's brow furrowed. Was that not one of Shen Yaowei's maids? What was she doing out here?

"Lead the way," he said.

He pushed open the door. Shen Yaowei rose to her feet with a small complaint. "Third Brother, you've kept me searching for ages."

He set his books on the table. "What did you need me for? Did you get Mother's permission before leaving the house?"

"Of course Mother gave her permission. Third Brother, things are different now — we don't have to be quite so careful anymore. I went to Zhenbao Pavilion today to look at some jewelry and got a little tired, so I stopped in here for tea, and thought I'd wait for you at the same time."

Shen Yaowei poured him a cup. "Third Brother, you must be tired. Try this tea — it's not as fine as what we have at home, but it's passable."

Shen Qianxiu picked up the cup and drank. "Awei, what did you want to talk to me about?"

Shen Yaowei sat down beside him, fidgeting a little. "Third Brother — have you been playing hero recently?"

Shen Qianxiu's expression cooled. He looked at her.

She flinched at the sight of that cold look — she had always been afraid of her Third Brother's displeasure. She hurried to explain. "Third Brother, even though you had your attendants keep quiet, word leaked from somewhere else. I heard in secret that you helped the Marchioness of Cheng'en's Miss Jiang at the Wangxian Restaurant."

Shen Yaowei saw that he said nothing, and couldn't hold back the question burning in her chest. "Third Brother — do you think that Miss Jiang is beautiful?"

She knew her Third Brother well. He was not the sort to involve himself in other people's affairs without reason. If he had come across a brute harassing a woman, he would not simply do nothing, but he would only send an attendant to fetch the authorities — he would not come forward himself.

Besides, the Shen family had no connection with the Marchioness of Cheng'en's household. If that Miss Jiang had come to grief, it would have been, if anything, a small advantage for the Shen family. And yet.

Shen Qianxiu said in a low voice, "Awei, you should not be asking that."

Shen Yaowei pressed on. "Then I'll speak my own mind. Third Brother, that Miss Jiang is more beautiful than I am — more beautiful than almost anyone. I still remember the way she looked playing the zither at the Emperor's birthday banquet. Her beauty is threatening. Though His Majesty may prefer a composed and gentle sort of grace, Miss Jiang's brilliance is the kind that makes it impossible to look away. And she is not only beautiful — she is the Empress Dowager's niece, she plays the zither magnificently, and His Majesty himself bestowed the famous zither Lüqi upon her. She has already won his favor before any of the others. If she enters the palace, will there be any place left for me at His Majesty's side?"

Shen Yaowei let out a soft sigh and lowered her voice. "Third Brother, I have no wish to spend decades crushed beneath a favored consort the way the Empress Dowager was."

"Silence!" Shen Qianxiu's voice came out sharp.

"His Majesty is a man of principle and virtue, upright and impartial. He is not a man ruled by beauty. If you conduct yourself well and fulfill your duties faithfully, His Majesty will treat you with respect and generosity!"

Shen Yaowei shrank back at his anger.

But she had gone to such lengths to find a moment alone with her Third Brother, and she was reluctant to let it go so easily.

"Third Brother, I know His Majesty will treat me kindly because of our elder sister. But if Miss Jiang were to find a suitable husband and marry early — if she could not enter the palace — then she would pose no threat to me at all, would she?"

"Third Brother, would you be willing to help me with this?"

Shen Qianxiu was silent for a long time. The look he turned on Shen Yaowei was one of unfamiliarity.

He had not realized his sister had become like this.

What shamed him still more, though, was that he did not refuse her at once.

chapters.title: Twenty-three
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi returned to Yuanzhi Courtyard. The compound looked just as it always did. Aside from Qiuyu and Xiaro, who knew she had slipped out, no one else had noticed her absence.

Qiuyu came forward to take the books from her arms, her eyes full of anxious concern — the look of someone desperate to ask but not quite daring.

Jiang Mi said quietly, "Qiuyu, Xiaro — act as though I never went out at all. Do not mention this to anyone."

Both girls agreed at once.

Jiang Mi was a little tired. She drank a few sips of the honey water Xiaro handed her and sank softly against the daybed to rest. Miangmiang, now fully recovered, leaped up without ceremony and settled herself into the crook of Jiang Mi's arm, as naturally as if she'd done it a thousand times before.

A faint smile crossed Jiang Mi's face. She absently stroked Miangmiang's fluffy fur, and by degrees the knot in her chest began to loosen.

"Did anyone come while I was out?" she asked.

Qiuyu replied, "The Fourth Miss came by around the Hour of the Goat. I told her you were resting, and she left without a fuss."

Jiang Mi nodded. Ah — that would be Ah-Rong. She wondered what Ah-Rong had wanted.

She was drifting toward sleep when she suddenly sat up and called out, "Qiuyu! Qiuyu!"

Qiuyu had been just outside the door with Xiaro, discussing what to send to the kitchen for dinner, when she heard her mistress calling. She rushed in at once. "Miss, what's wrong? Was it another nightmare?" They had all been worrying — their mistress hadn't been sleeping well of late.

"Qiuyu, go and get me the chess manual from Qianshuang Temple. The one we brought back that day."

Though Qiuyu couldn't imagine why Miss Jiang suddenly needed the thing, she could see the urgency in her face and went off at a brisk pace to fetch it.

She was back quickly, producing the manual from where it had been stored alongside the zither Lüqi, and handed it over.

Jiang Mi turned the pages with growing attention — and with every page, a coldness settled deeper in her chest.

She had been lying here with her eyes closed, replaying the events of the day, and it was Xiao Huaiyan's expression during the chess games that kept surfacing — that faint peculiarity in his manner when she had said that one thing. She hadn't paid it much mind at the time.

Now she understood.

When Chengzhong had put this chess manual into her hands at Qianshuang Temple, she had flipped through it for barely a moment and then never touched it again.

Only now did she see: this manual was for absolute beginners — the kind of entry-level material a child the age of little Xuan might study. There was nothing in it that warranted speaking of "bringing lessons to bear."

Xiao Huaiyan had done it deliberately.

All the strength seemed to drain from her body at once, replaced by a cold, crawling dread.

Qiuyu had the small kitchen prepare several of Jiang Mi's favorite dishes, but Jiang Mi had no appetite. She managed two bites before setting her chopsticks down.

She picked up the travel account she had just bought and opened to Songxun's chapter on Sichuan. But the words felt like they were floating on the surface — she couldn't take a single one in. Her mind refused to settle.

If she didn't find something to occupy herself with, she feared she'd sink into panic entirely. So she kept telling herself, over and over: even if she had lied to Xiao Huaiyan and he had seen through it, he had already let her go. It was over. She was frightening herself for nothing.

Then she heard voices outside. Someone was coming.

Xiaro appeared in the doorway. "Miss, the Fourth Miss is here."

Jiang Mi sat up. Ah-Rong again?

She set the book aside and, scooping up Miangmiang, rounded the folding screen — and there was Jiang Rong, just stepping into the room.

She was dressed in a pale lotus-pink pleated skirt, and she was carrying a lacquered black box gilded with lotus blossoms. She gave Jiang Mi a shy, tentative smile. "Elder Sister, I brought you some blood swallow's nest."

Seeing the surprise on Jiang Mi's face, she scrambled to explain. "It — it's because Qiuyu mentioned that Elder Sister has been sleeping poorly and needs to rest in the afternoons. I thought — I still had some blood swallow's nest, and so I thought I'd bring it over. I..." Her voice trailed off and she looked down. "I know Elder Sister's household doesn't lack for these things..."

Jiang Rong fell quiet — and then her hands suddenly felt light. The lacquered box had been taken from her grasp, and something warm and soft was pressed into her arms instead.

She nearly dropped it. Scrambling to hold on, she found herself clutching Miangmiang, who regarded her with mild curiosity.

She looked up to find her beautiful, composed elder sister beaming at her, eyes warm and bright. "Lack for it? How could I? What I'm short of is exactly this — Ah-Rong's thoughtfulness."

Jiang Mi passed the lacquered box to Qiuyu. "It's still early — go have the small kitchen simmer two portions for me and Ah-Rong to drink together."

Qiuyu took it with both hands and turned to Jiang Rong with a smile. "Fourth Miss, it's lucky you brought this. Our Miss barely touched her dinner this evening. The swallow's nest simmered up will be just the thing before sleep — good for settling the spirit and helping her rest."

Jiang Rong flushed.

Miangmiang shifted in her arms, only now registering that she'd changed hands. She let out a puzzled little mew.

Jiang Mi patted the top of Miangmiang's head. The cat had a mild temperament; she nestled in without complaint.

"Ah-Rong, was it something specific you wanted when you came earlier?"

Jiang Rong said shyly, "I'd been practicing my calligraphy and wanted to show you." Her mother had told her that Elder Sister preferred quiet, and she shouldn't always go and disturb her. But she liked being with Elder Sister — Elder Sister was different now, not like before, when she had barely paid attention to her and little Xuan. Now whenever Elder Sister spoke to them, she was so warm and gentle. Just sitting in Elder Sister's courtyard, doing nothing at all, felt comfortable. So she had worked at her calligraphy and wanted to come. Elder Sister was confined to the courtyard anyway — having someone to talk with had to be better.

Jiang Mi could feel the careful, cautious steps Jiang Rong was taking toward her — eager to grow closer, yet held back by some gentle timidity.

"Did you bring it with you?" she asked.

Jiang Rong shook her head. She said in a small, tense voice, "Elder Sister, if it wouldn't put you out, I could come back tomorrow instead."

"No need for all that," Jiang Mi said with a smile. "Come with me, Ah-Rong."

She led Jiang Rong to the writing desk, where paper and ink were already laid out.

Jiang Rong sat down and took up the brush, and Jiang Mi stood at her side, offering a word of guidance here and there.

Xiaro added two more candles to the room to brighten the light for the two sisters.

By the time Qiuyu came in with the simmered swallow's nest, Jiang Mi had just found an old calligraphy copybook to send home with Jiang Rong.

Jiang Rong sat happily with her elder sister and drank down a portion of swallow's nest, and felt that the taste of it was something she had never known before.

……

In the Qianqing Palace, the palace attendants went about their duties in near silence, not daring even to breathe too loudly.

Only Li Fu had the nerve to approach the Emperor and change his tea while he worked through the pile of memorials.

Having done so, Li Fu came back out.

He pulled Chengzhong aside and asked in a low voice, "You went out with His Majesty today — what exactly happened?"

Since His Majesty had returned to the palace, he had immediately summoned the head imperial physician, Physician Gu. But Gu had come out after only a short time, drenched in cold sweat — clearly he had been reprimanded.

At dinner, His Majesty had found fault with everything the imperial kitchen produced and had the kitchen staff punished.

All of Qianqing Palace knew His Majesty was in a foul mood. The attendants who didn't usually serve at his side were getting their first real taste of his wrath today.

Chengzhong looked completely at a loss. "Godfather, His Majesty only played chess with Miss Jiang for a short while and then sent her home. I honestly don't know what went wrong."

Li Fu frowned. "Peculiar indeed."

He took one look at Chengzhong's clueless expression and felt his irritation spike. "Be careful how you serve him. Watch yourself."

Chengzhong pulled his head in. "Yes, Godfather."

……

Xiao Huaiyan retrieved the memorial from the Ministry of Rites — the list of candidates for Empress and consort selection — that he had set aside.

He looked over the names, gave a quiet laugh, and picked up his vermilion brush with eyes gone cold as ice.

It was past midnight by the time Xiao Huaiyan finally lay down on the imperial bed.

The moment he closed his eyes, the clear, bright sound of a bell began to ring, slow and unhurried.

In his mind's eye, the hem of a skirt lifted on a stone step — and there, beneath it, a small embroidered slipper. Each step down was like a flash of red beneath the lotus leaves — a glimpse of a carp, and then gone.

Suddenly the scene shifted. A pair of pale, bare feet wearing golden and jade anklet bells turned in a slow, spinning dance, so white they dazzled the eye.

The ringing in his ears and the ringing of the bells on those feet fell into perfect time with each other.

Strangely, it no longer irritated him. Something else stirred in its place.

The world tilted. A muffled cry — and then those small, delicate feet dropped into his burning palms.

A surge of violent feeling rose like a tide, enough to crush what he held to pieces.

Faint struggling. A soft, stifled sob. He lifted her onto the stone table, tore through the layers of silk in his way — but a veil of mist hung between them, and he could not make out the face of the person beneath him.

Xiao Huaiyan opened his eyes.

The sky had not yet broken into dawn, but it was the hour he rose each day.

Li Fu entered with the attendants to help him dress for morning court.

Xiao Huaiyan put on his court robes in silence. When Li Fu gathered up the discarded clothing, he noticed something on the trousers — a stain --

Xiao Huaiyan said, without expression, "Burn all of it."

Li Fu felt a chill run down his neck and answered with fearful obedience.

……

The Empress Dowager Jiang had finished her morning meal and, seeing that the sun had come out today, went for a walk in the imperial garden.

The craftsmen had planted new flowers, and they bloomed brilliantly in the season. She lingered a long while before several stems of jade-white Yaotai Yufeng orchid in particular.

Matron Cui came hurrying over with news. "Your Ladyship, His Majesty rejected the Ministry of Rites' latest candidate list again at morning court. He said the rebel remnants have not yet been fully eliminated, and that Yuzhou has just suffered severe flooding — he has no heart for matters of the inner palace, and that the selection of Empress and consorts should be postponed further."

"Rejected again?" The Empress Dowager's brow creased deeply. What on earth was the Emperor thinking?

Matron Cui nodded. "It is unclear whether the Ministry of Rites' list once again failed to meet His Majesty's satisfaction."

The Empress Dowager no longer had any interest in the flowers. She boarded her imperial palanquin and returned to Cining Palace.

In her heart, she thought it unlikely to be a problem with the list. Besides daughters of the great aristocratic houses, the list also included daughters of officials newly elevated by the Emperor himself — it ought to cover all the right bases.

chapters.title: Twenty-four
chapters.content:
Whether it was the bowl of blood swallow's nest before bed that had done its work, Jiang Mi slept soundly for the first time in a while.

When Qiuyu brought the morning meal in from the kitchen, she saw that her mistress had a bit of color in her cheeks, and said cheerfully, "Seeing how much you enjoyed last night's swallow's nest, I took the liberty of having another portion simmered this morning as well. Drink it while it's hot, Miss."

Well-rested and with her appetite restored, Jiang Mi not only finished the whole portion of swallow's nest but also ate a piece of chestnut cake.

She was just about to tidy up the calligraphy Jiang Rong had left on her writing desk the night before when Chunhui came in from outside. "Miss, the Marchioness of Cheng'en is here."

Great-aunt? What brought her at this hour?

Jiang Mi set down the papers in her hand and went out.

In the main room, the Marchioness sat drinking tea. When she saw Jiang Mi come in, she set her cup down and waved her over. "Tangtang, come quickly."

As Jiang Mi drew near, the Marchioness took her hand and sighed. "It's all because of that Jiang Yi, dragging you into trouble and getting you confined to the courtyard too. You've been put through an ordeal."

Jiang Mi smiled. "It's not as though I go out much anyway. Being in Yuanzhi Courtyard makes no real difference."

The Marchioness, seeing that she truly bore no grievance, felt relieved.

She came to the point. "Tangtang, the Zhenguo Duke's household has sent an invitation to the ladies of Cheng'en Marquis's household for a flower banquet. The date is set for the sixteenth."

She smiled as she continued. "In truth, our household and the Zhenguo Duke's have very little to do with each other ordinarily. It is entirely because of you, Tangtang, that we were included this time."

From her sleeve, the Marchioness produced a folded letter. "This is from the Fifth Miss of the Zhenguo Duke's household — it's addressed to you."

Jiang Mi thought of the girl she had briefly met at the palace and opened the letter. Sure enough, it was from that bright-eyed young girl, Xue Ningzhu.

"In truth, this flower banquet has another purpose. The Grand Princess has invited a great many noble families — it is really a gathering to find a suitable match for the heir of the Zhenguo Duke's household."

The Marchioness saw Jiang Mi's look of surprise and explained further. "The Grand Princess and the Duchess of Zhenguo have their eyes on only a few families already, and ours has not yet caught their attention. Besides, Jiang Yi's temperament is not well suited to a household like that, and you are destined for the palace. Ah-Rong is still a child at heart, so the three of you can think of it as simply going to enjoy the occasion."

Jiang Mi nodded. She had rarely attended this sort of gathering before. It would be a good opportunity to quietly observe which eligible young men in the capital might make a suitable match for her.

The Marchioness added, "I hear the Grand Princess had rare and precious varieties of flowers and plants brought in from Jiangnan and Sichuan. Quite the treat for the eyes as well."

As for the Marchioness herself, she wanted to move among the other noble families and, in particular, to sound out the wife of the Ministry of Revenue's Vice-Minister — she thought the woman might be attending, and she wanted to probe the situation a little further.

The Marchioness had said what she came to say. She picked up her teacup for a final sip and was about to rise and leave.

Then a maid came running in, breathless and flustered. "My Lady, please come quickly — Dong'er has run home in tears, saying that the First Young Mistress was knocked down and lost the baby — deliberately, by the concubine — and they're over there protecting the concubine. She's begging you to come and stand up for the First Young Mistress!"

The Marchioness's face went white in an instant. The teacup slipped from her fingers and shattered on the floor, tea and broken porcelain flying everywhere.

Jiang Mi's chest clenched. The First Young Mistress — wasn't that her eldest cousin, Jiang Wan, who had married into the Duke of Ying's household?

The Marchioness's face was iron-gray with shock, her eyes bright with fear and urgency. She had no time even to say goodbye to Jiang Mi before she rushed out to find Dong'er.

Jiang Mi's expression grew grave. Jiang Wan was four years her senior. Back when their grandmother was still alive, she had married the heir of the Duke of Ying's household, Xie Xi — a prestigious match by any measure.

After the wedding, Jiang Wan went two years without conceiving. The Duchess of Ying then installed women in the heir's rooms, and by the third year had elevated one of them to the rank of honored concubine — a woman who had family ties to the Duchess's own maternal relations.

That honored concubine quickly fell pregnant. Fearing she might feel neglected, the Duchess of Ying decided she need not observe the usual courtesies before the first wife. The Marchioness had been furious on her daughter's behalf and had confronted the Duchess, who had promised in so many words that once the concubine's child was born, she would not be permitted to overstep Jiang Wan's position.

But the moment the Duchess returned home, she had Jiang Wan come before her to pay obeisance, lecturing her that she must take good care of the heir's concubine, that she must be generous and magnanimous, and that she must not behave like a jealous wife.

Jiang Wan had concealed all of this from her mother. It was only when a trusted nursemaid smuggled word out to the Marchioness that the family learned of the hardships Jiang Wan endured.

Now, at last, Jiang Wan had managed to conceive — only to have the pregnancy ended by that concubine's doing.

Jiang Mi pressed her lips together, straining to recall everything she had known about this time in her past life. Back then, she had kept entirely to herself, hiding in her rooms, and no one had come to trouble her. Several days had passed before the news reached her.

In that past life, the Marchioness had taken people to the Duke of Ying's household to demand answers. Just as she and the Duchess were in the thick of their argument over what to do with the concubine, Jiang Mi's eldest male cousin, Jiang Hong, had appeared — dragging the heir of the Duke of Ying, whom he had beaten half to death.

That turned the entire situation on its head.

The Duchess of Ying had been in the wrong to begin with, and had been on the verge of making a concession — promising to send the concubine away to a country estate once her child was born. But the moment she saw the heir beaten black and blue, all her reasonableness flew out the window and she turned on the Jiang family instead.

In the end, it was the Empress Dowager who sent Matron Cui to reprimand the Duke of Ying's household, forcing them to yield.

But Jiang Wan's life afterward could only be imagined. Before Jiang Mi entered the palace, she never managed to see her cousin again. After the Empress Dowager passed and the Jiang family fell, she heard through others that the heir of the Duke of Ying had divorced Jiang Wan on the grounds of childlessness and jealousy, cutting all ties with the Jiang family. And Jiang Hong had, for no clear reason, had both his legs broken.

The Jiang family's already desperate circumstances had grown even worse.

Jiang Mi came to her feet.

She called Qiuyu, Xiaro, and Chunhui all in at once.

"Qiuyu — go quickly and find my father. Tell him the Marchioness is going to the Duke of Ying's household to stand up for Cousin Wan, and tell him to send someone to intercept Cousin Hong. Do not let him do something rash."

"Xiaro — take a group of the stronger servants and station yourselves near the Duke of Ying's gate. If Cousin Hong turns up, block him and tell him to go home. Tell him it was I who said it: if he acts on impulse, he will be the ruin of Cousin Wan. If he still won't listen, tie him up and bring him back by force if you have to."

"Chunhui — you do the same. Take people and cover the road from the academy. Same as Xiaro: no matter what, bring Cousin Hong back."

All three agreed and left at a run.

Jiang Mi paced back and forth. As for the palace — she would need to submit a request to see the Empress Dowager first, and could only go in once permission was granted.

Should she ask the Empress Dowager to do as she had in the past life — send someone to reprimand the Duke of Ying's household, have the concubine taken to a country estate to wait out her pregnancy, and then be sent away permanently once the child arrived?

Or should she go and see Cousin Wan first, and hear what she herself wanted?

Jiang Mi hesitated, caught between the two.

In the middle of her uncertainty, a small maid appeared in the doorway. "Miss, there is someone here from the palace with a delivery for you."

Jiang Mi's heart leaped with hope. Could it be the Empress Dowager, sending word already?

"Invite them in at once."

She thought: if it was Matron Cui, the Matron carried enough authority to go directly to the Duke of Ying's household on the Empress Dowager's behalf.

But as the small maid led the visitor into the courtyard and the figure drew clearer and clearer, Jiang Mi felt as though the air had been knocked out of her lungs, as though she had seen a ghost.

Chengzhong beamed and clasped his hands in greeting. "Greetings to Miss Jiang. I trust you are well."

Jiang Mi took an involuntary step backward. "Chengzhong," she said, her voice gone flat and cold, "why are you here again?"

Chengzhong did his best to make his smile look harmless. "The master has a summons, Miss Jiang."

He had his own private misgivings about this errand. If His Majesty truly had feelings for Miss Jiang, wouldn't the Empress Dowager have simply had her brought into the palace long ago? They could have seen each other every day. As things stood, Miss Jiang was still an unmarried young lady of good family — it was only natural that she felt ill at ease. But still — wasn't her reaction perhaps a little too strong?

Jiang Mi's mind was still preoccupied with everything else: had Cousin Hong been intercepted yet? How were the Marchioness and Cousin Wan faring at the Duke of Ying's household? She had no room in her head for Xiao Huaiyan.

But unwilling as she was, she had no way to refuse.

Jiang Mi felt a hollow weariness settle over her.

The same carriage waiting by the western side gate. The same journey through the busy streets. The same quiet, deep lane at the end of it.

As before, she followed Chengzhong into the private residence. Through the covered walkway, past the festooned gate, into the same secluded and elegant courtyard.

Chengzhong stopped at the door. "Go in, Miss," he said quietly.

Jiang Mi pressed her palm to the carved rosewood door. She drew a deep breath, pressed gently — and the door swung open.

The man was reclining sideways on the daybed beneath the window. High-bridged nose, thin lips, sharp brows, phoenix eyes. The light filtering through the latticed window fell across him like snow on a distant mountain — remote and untouchable.

He was reading a book.

Jiang Mi hesitated a moment, then stepped forward and offered her greeting according to proper form.

Xiao Huaiyan set down the book. He said evenly, "Rise."

If yesterday she had been strung tight with anxiety, today her feelings were a different tangle entirely — too many things pressing in at once, her mind a muddle.

She rose and saw the chess board already set up on the table before her.

Surely he didn't mean to play chess again.

Xiao Huaiyan stood, walked to the table, and sat. He gestured across from him. "Sit."

Jiang Mi had no choice but to take the seat opposite.

He glanced at her, picked up a black piece, and said, "Miss Jiang, your color is better today than yesterday. Do you have the spirit for another game?"

Jiang Mi pressed her lips together and answered with care. "This subject daughter is slow-witted — I am afraid of failing Your Majesty's interest again."

Xiao Huaiyan gave a light laugh. "It is of no consequence."

She truly could not fathom what was going through Xiao Huaiyan's mind. And right now, she could not afford to give the game any real attention.

Her thoughts were a tangled mess. She could only fall back on what she remembered of his style from her past life, and try to work out his moves from habit alone.

She had to do the same as before — lose quickly, displease him, and be dismissed as soon as possible.

And so it began, just as she had planned. She walked each carefully misjudged step.

Hoping only to be gone as soon as she could.

When the game ended in another loss, Jiang Mi expected that she would only need to offer her usual apology to be let go as before.

But before she could say a word, she heard Xiao Huaiyan give a quiet laugh — a sound of genuine appreciation. "Miss Jiang, what a fine game you play."

She went perfectly still.

Had she not lost?

"Miss Jiang — do you know? Losing once is no great feat. The difficult thing is losing every time — and doing so in exactly the right way. As though you have already read my next move, seen through my strategy in advance, and walked with perfect precision straight into certain defeat. Could that not be called fine chess?"

His tone was the same as it had always been — light, unhurried, warm as a gentle breeze.

But the words, when they reached her ears, were cold enough to cut bone.

Jiang Mi could not stop herself from shuddering. The piece in her hand fell from her fingers and dropped to the floor.

A dead silence filled the room.

She could not suppress the fear welling up inside her.

Her small, careful deceptions had been seen through by Xiao Huaiyan.

She knew his chess style. She had known his next move before he made it.

Xiao Huaiyan's suspicion ran deep. Her behavior was tantamount to condemning herself.

He studied her — the woman trembling across from him — watching every small reaction.

Then, slowly, he spoke. "Has Miss Jiang nothing to say in explanation?"

A mist rose in Jiang Mi's eyes. Tears began to fall, silent.

From the moment he had mentioned that chess manual, she had been making one wrong move after another. What could she possibly say?

Xiao Huaiyan's expression was unreadable. He reached out and took hold of her chin, forcing her face up toward him.

"Just playing chess with Us — and you find it this distressing?"

The skin beneath his fingers was fine-grained and soft. A slight increase in pressure and the flesh reddened.

Something in him urged him to press harder.

The violent darkness stirring at the base of his heart began to stir again.

That face — brilliant as peach blossoms — showed the faintest trace of pain now, and her tears had wet his fingertips.

Then, abruptly, Xiao Huaiyan released her.

A cold laugh. He turned and walked away.

chapters.title: Twenty-five
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi's tears fell like a string of broken beads, impossible to stop.

She knew perfectly well that Xiao Huaiyan had already gone, and still she could not make them stop. 

All the fear and dread that had been pressed down inside her during these recent days seemed to have found a place to pour out at last, rushing up all at once.

In the midst of crying, she suddenly began to hiccup. Jiang Mi covered her mouth with her hand, utterly miserable.

Outside the room, the man who had left had not moved a single step since she first started crying.

Chengzhong stood at a careful distance, head respectfully bowed, thinking to himself that it was no wonder his godfather had warned him again and again never to attempt to guess at His Majesty's intentions.

The Emperor's heart was fathomless as the sea — there was no use even trying.

If one were to say His Majesty was fond of Miss Jiang, then why had he let a beautiful woman cry her heart out in there while he stood here utterly unmoved?

If one were to say he was not fond of her, then why had he sent for her again and again? There were any number of people in the capital who played chess — among officials and noble ladies alike — and yet he only ever called for Miss Jiang.

Truly impossible to understand.

Chengzhong decided to keep being a piece of furniture.

From inside the room, he could make out the sounds of Miss Jiang crying so hard she was hiccuping. Poor thing.

Xiao Huaiyan pressed a hand against his own temple and said coldly, "Take her home."

Then he turned and left.

Chengzhong received the command and hurried into the room, poured a cup of tea, and offered it over. "Miss Jiang, drink a little water — it might help."

Jiang Mi had just wiped her eyes with her handkerchief. She lifted her reddened gaze, opened her mouth to speak — and hiccuped again.

Chengzhong set the cup on the table in front of her and stepped back a few paces. In a gently consoling tone he said, "Miss Jiang, what is the point of all this suffering?" Crying like that, and that man hadn't even looked back.

Jiang Mi thought his words slightly strange. Plagued by the hiccups, she drank a few sips of tea to settle them.

"Chengzhong — am I free to go now?" she asked. Her voice came out hoarse.

Chengzhong nodded. "This servant will escort you home."

Whether it was because she had cried so thoroughly, Jiang Mi wasn't sure, but her heart felt considerably calmer now.

Leaving the private residence, she noticed the sky had deepened. She worried a little that, having been away so long with Qiuyu and the others dispatched on errands, someone else in the household might have noticed she was missing from Yuanzhi Courtyard.

If that reached her mother or great-aunt, it would take no small amount of explaining.

Thankfully, the journey back was smooth and untroubled.

She had barely stepped through the courtyard gate when she ran straight into Qiuyu, who had been about to come looking for her.

"Miss — where did you go?" Qiuyu said in a fluster. "I came back and didn't see you anywhere. I was worried sick."

"I'm fine," Jiang Mi said, brushing past the question. "What happened on your end? Did you find my father?"

Qiuyu said, "You needn't worry, Miss. I found the master without any trouble, and he immediately went to find Cousin Hong himself. I followed along the whole way. When the master caught up to him, Cousin Hong had just heard the news about the First Young Mistress losing the baby and was about to go find the heir and give him a proper beating. Fortunately the master stopped him in time and physically dragged him back home."

"So Father and Cousin Hong are both back at the residence now?"

Qiuyu nodded. "Yes. The master took Cousin Hong to find the Marquis, and they should still be at the main branch's rooms."

Jiang Mi let out a quiet breath of relief.

They had made it in time.

Not long after, Chunhui returned as well. She had taken people to intercept Cousin Hong on the road from the academy, but had missed him — it was one of his pages who told her, once she crossed paths with him, that the master had already brought Cousin Hong home.

That left only Xiaro, who had been stationed near the Duke of Ying's gate, still to return.

Qiuyu and Chunhui went off to arrange dinner with the small kitchen.

In the falling dark, Jiang Mi sat alone on a stone bench in the courtyard and let the cool evening breeze wash over her.

She stared without focus into the unlit depths beyond the lantern light.

Something warm and soft pressed against her leg, back and forth.

She looked down. Miangmiang had crept over without a sound to demand some attention.

Jiang Mi scooped her up and smoothed down her fur. "Are you hungry? Shall I find you some little strips of dried meat?"

Miangmiang rubbed her face against Jiang Mi's hand a couple of times and then extended a tiny pink tongue to lick her own paw.

Jiang Mi smiled, and carried the little cat back inside.

She took a piece of dried meat from the clay jar, tore it into thin strips, and held them out for Miangmiang one by one. The cat ate with unhurried daintiness, and Jiang Mi found a quiet pleasure in watching her.

She had just finished feeding the first strip and was reaching for the second when Xiaro came rushing in, breathless. "Miss — m-miss — the Marchioness has brought the First Young Mistress home!"

"What?" Jiang Mi straightened at once. "Tell me everything properly."

Xiaro caught her breath and continued. "I kept watch near the Duke of Ying's gate for a long time but Cousin Hong never came. What did happen was that the Marchioness's personal attendant, Matron Liu, came out of the Duke of Ying's household and recognized me. Seeing that I'd brought several of the older serving women with me, she called us all inside. It turned out the First Young Mistress had said she wanted to come home to the Marquis's household. The Duchess of Ying tried to stop her at first, saying that a woman who had just miscarried shouldn't be moved. But the Marchioness saw that the First Young Mistress wanted to leave, and she didn't care whether the Duchess was pleased or not — she simply took her."

"And the concubine?" Jiang Mi pressed.

"By the time I arrived, Concubine Bai was no longer in the courtyard. I heard from the First Young Mistress's maid Dong'er that the woman had knelt outside weeping, saying she hadn't meant any harm — that a maid had bumped into her first, and it was only then that she accidentally collided with the First Young Mistress."

Xiaro's voice sharpened with indignation. "That Concubine Bai said she was nearly at full term — there was no way she would deliberately knock into the First Young Mistress. The Marchioness heard this and was furious, and demanded the Duchess send Concubine Bai to a country estate immediately, to wait there until she delivered. For a moment it seemed the Duchess was about to agree — but then Concubine Bai suddenly let out a scream, and blood appeared beneath her skirts. The Duchess was afraid the baby would be harmed and had her rushed back inside and a physician summoned. After that, she went to the Marchioness and pleaded for leniency, saying that Concubine Bai's foetal position was unstable and that sending her to a country estate now might harm the child. She asked the Marchioness to allow some more time, and promised Concubine Bai would be sent away after she delivered. The Marchioness didn't agree at first, but the First Young Mistress said she wanted to come home — so the Duchess, even though she was displeased, had to let the Marchioness take her."

Xiaro took a sip of water and continued. "By the time I got there, Matron Liu had the serving women help carry the First Young Mistress to the carriage, wrapped up very thoroughly so she didn't catch any wind."

"Was the heir present at any point?" Jiang Mi asked.

Xiaro shook her head. "From start to finish, the heir of the Duke of Ying never appeared. He kept himself entirely out of it."

Jiang Mi thought: Xie Xi had removed himself from the situation entirely from beginning to end, keeping his own hands spotlessly clean. Truly a man without a shred of feeling or loyalty.

"Xiaro, you've worked hard today. Go eat something and rest."

She called Qiuyu over and pressed some coins into her hand. "One portion for the three of you, and give the rest to the serving women and attendants who went out with you."

Qiuyu accepted the money happily. "Thank you, Miss."

……

Jiang Mi ate very little at dinner, her thoughts elsewhere.

What had unfolded today was already different from her past life.

In that life, Cousin Wan had watched Cousin Hong beat the heir of the Duke of Ying, and with that, the Marquis of Cheng'en's household had gone in a heartbeat from the wronged party to the party in the wrong. She hadn't dared ask to come home.
