This time was different. Without Cousin Hong's interference, the Marchioness could press the Duke of Ying's household from a position of clear moral authority and demand accountability. The Duke of Ying's household was the one at fault, and they had no choice but to yield.

She only hadn't anticipated that what Cousin Wan would ask for was to come home to the Marquis's household.

Still — at least the Marchioness had loved her daughter enough to bring her back.

What Cousin Wan would choose to do from here on was something she didn't yet know.

Qiuyu came in, saw Jiang Mi sitting there in a daze, and said, "Miss, the master is here."

Jiang Mi turned to find her father already at the doorway.

She went to him and dipped a curtsy. "Father. What brings you here?"

Jiang Qingxuan settled into the pear-blossom wood chair by the window and looked at his daughter with an expression that was warm and gentle. "Tangtang, you did well today. Sending Qiuyu to find me so I could stop Jiang Hong — if you hadn't, he would have made real trouble."

Jiang Qingxuan saw that Jiang Mi had pursed her lips and said nothing, so he added, "Come, sit down."

"Your great-uncle has had Jiang Hong confined for now, to cool down before they speak. And he has lifted your house arrest — if you have time, you're welcome to go visit your cousin on the main branch's side."

Jiang Mi looked up. "Even if Father hadn't said so, I would have gone to see her."

Jiang Qingxuan nodded. "Good. I recall that when your grandmother was still alive, your cousin stayed in the same courtyard as you for a spell. The two of you were always close."

Jiang Mi turned this over quietly in her heart. She had been ten years old then, and her cousin already fourteen. Even sharing a courtyard, they had not had that much to do with each other, really.

At that time, her days had been packed with lessons. Besides the tutor her grandmother had engaged to teach her zither, chess, calligraphy, and painting, she had also been made to learn exotic dances from distant lands. She had cried through the pain, but there had been no one she could go to for comfort, no one indulgent enough to let her cry and fuss freely.

Her grandmother had been loving but strict. She would not allow laziness and expected only the best.

Her elder cousin had often watched her with eyes full of pity.

She hadn't understood it then. She had asked her grandmother once: why doesn't Elder Sister have to practice dancing?

Her grandmother had said: Elder Sister will marry into a great family and manage a household — she does not need to learn these things. But you are destined for the palace, so you must study and work hard.

Their destinies were different. Naturally, what each of them was taught was different as well.

Jiang Qingxuan, seeing how quiet she had become, asked gently, "Has something frightened you?"

Jiang Mi blinked hard, and the rims of her eyes went red.

She said quietly, "Father — Cousin Wan's household is this chaotic already. If I enter the palace, where the inner court holds three thousand beauties — what will become of me?"

Jiang Qingxuan was struck silent. He sat for a moment and then said, "His Majesty is a man of principle and sound judgment — one cannot compare him to the heir of the Duke of Ying's household. You... you mustn't let your thoughts run away with you."

Faced with his daughter's beseeching eyes, Jiang Qingxuan looked faintly stricken.

He knew — anyone who truly cared for their daughter would not willingly send her into the dye vat that was the imperial harem.

Guilt made it hard for him to stay seated. He said a few more words of reassurance and left.

Jiang Mi dabbed at dry eyes — she'd cried so much today that she had nothing left. She had hoped to stoke her father's guilt through the story of her cousin, to plant some seed of doubt. Whether it would take root at all, she couldn't say.

……

The following morning, Jiang Mi had barely risen when she heard some commotion outside the courtyard.

She had Qiuyu go and find out: it turned out the Marchioness had sent over bolts of silk, jewelry, and some fresh fruit.

Jiang Mi instructed Qiuyu to go to the storeroom and select some nourishing tonics and ingredients.

Then she changed into a simple, clean outfit, had Qiuyu bring everything along, and set off for the main branch's rooms.

The Marchioness had not slept well all night for worry over Jiang Wan and looked haggard with anxiety. She managed a faint smile when she saw Jiang Mi come in. "You've come to see Wan'er, Tangtang?"

Jiang Mi nodded. "Is Elder Sister awake?"

The Marchioness replied, "I just came from her rooms. She's awake and managed to drink a little broth, but she has no energy. Go and look in on her — you can help me cheer her up a bit. Tell her not to dwell on things too much. This child just wasn't meant to be; there will be others."

After saying this, the Marchioness let out a long sigh.

Jiang Wan was staying in Mingxiu Courtyard, her old room from before her marriage.

Jiang Mi had barely entered when Jiang Wan's devoted maid, Dong'er, came forward to meet her with a bow. "Third Miss, you've come."

Jiang Mi had Qiuyu hand over the tonics. "Go through these and find whatever is best for Elder Sister to recover on. Do what you can to get her eating a little."

Dong'er agreed, her eyes red, and led Jiang Mi to the main room. "Please go in, Third Miss."

The room was sealed tight — not a crack of air. The thick, heavy scent of medicinal herbs hung everywhere.

Jiang Mi stepped around the folding screen, and there was the woman lying in the bed.

She was terribly thin. Her complexion was sallow and her lips bloodless, her eyes dull and unfocused. Jiang Mi could scarcely believe that this was the same Jiang Wan she remembered — composed, graceful, dignified in every way.

"Cousin Wan." Her voice came out very soft.

Jiang Wan turned her head slowly. She looked at Jiang Mi for a long moment, as though she had to recognize her first. Then: "It's Tangtang."

chapters.title: Twenty-six
chapters.content:
In Jiang Wan's eyes, the little girl she remembered — with those delicate, pretty features — had grown into a beauty of rare and otherworldly loveliness. Skin like snow, a face like a flower, a slender waist that swayed like willow branches; anyone who looked at her would find it hard to look away.

"Tangtang, come closer," Jiang Wan called softly.

Jiang Mi went and sat on the embroidered stool at the bedside, taking the hand Jiang Wan held out to her.

The sight of those thin, worn hands made her heart ache.

Before Jiang Wan had married, her hands had been fine and pale and soft, her fingers tapered like green onion tips.

Jiang Mi looked at her haggard cousin and said with quiet sorrow, "Cousin Wan, you have to take care of yourself."

Jiang Wan gave a faint, hollow laugh. "What is there to take care of? It makes no difference now. I'll likely never be able to have children again."

She had worked so hard to conceive, and the pregnancy had gone badly from the start — she couldn't eat, couldn't sleep, and everything made her nauseous; she had grown terribly thin in less than a month. She had told herself that if she could just hold on until the three-month mark, once the baby had settled, things would improve. But then Concubine Bai had collided with her, and the child was gone just like that.

Jiang Mi's chest tightened. "Cousin Wan, don't think like that. Once your health is restored, you'll have children. There will be more."

Jiang Wan said nothing.

The night before, the Empress Dowager had sent a physician over. The physician had shaken his head and said that conceiving again would be difficult.

Though he hadn't declared it hopeless outright, Jiang Wan knew in her heart there was none.

For a while neither of them spoke.

Jiang Mi understood that nothing she said would be of any use.

After some time, Dong'er carried in the prepared medicine.

Jiang Mi took the bowl and blew on each spoonful to cool it before feeding it to Jiang Wan.

Jiang Wan, watching Jiang Mi's careful concentration, cooperated and drank nearly half the bowl. When Jiang Mi moved to offer more, she pressed a hand against it. "Tangtang, I can't manage any more — any more and I'll be sick."

Dong'er brought over the prepared preserved fruits to take away the bitterness in her mouth.

Jiang Wan picked up a dried plum and chewed it slowly.

Jiang Mi said, "You're afraid the medicine is bitter, so I'll bake some pastries and bring them over tomorrow to help it go down. How does that sound?"

Jiang Wan looked at her and smiled. "Tangtang has learned to bake pastries now? You're always so thoughtful. Jiang Yi came by this morning too — she threw herself against the side of my bed and cried so hard she gave me a headache."

Jiang Mi helped tuck the covers more snugly around Jiang Wan. "If you're tired, get some sleep. I'll come see you again tomorrow."

Jiang Wan nodded.

Jiang Mi had just risen when she heard the door open. A figure came around the folding screen — it was the Marchioness.

Jiang Wan looked surprised. "Mother? Didn't you come this morning already?"

The Marchioness's expression was difficult to read. She hesitated. "Wan'er, Xie Xi is here. He wants to see you."

Jiang Wan went quiet.

The Marchioness, not bothering to speak around Jiang Mi, relayed what she had just learned. "Last night, the Empress Dowager sent Matron Cui and a physician to the Duke of Ying's household. They administered a medicine to induce labor to Concubine Bai. She was in agony all night, and this morning she gave birth to a boy. The moment he was born, she was sent away to the country estate. The maid who had done the shoving was beaten to death. Wan'er — do you want to see Xie Xi?"

Jiang Mi also looked toward Jiang Wan. In her past life, Concubine Bai had been kept at the Duke of Ying's household until she delivered at full term, after which she was sent to the estate. The child had been taken in by the Duchess and raised at her knee, and after the Empress Dowager died and the Jiang family fell from power, they had brought Bai back.

What would her elder cousin choose this time?

After a moment, Jiang Wan said, "Mother, ask him to come in."

Jiang Mi followed the Marchioness out of the room and took her leave, heading back to Yuanzhi Courtyard.

The Marchioness nodded, and had a maid bring the Duke of Ying's heir, Xie Xi, to Mingxiu Courtyard.

Xie Xi was a tall man with a decent face, dressed in a deep blue robe with a hidden pattern. He saw the Marchioness standing at the door and gave her a respectful bow. "Mother-in-law."

The Marchioness bore him a deep grievance, but for Wan'er's sake she kept her composure. "Wan'er is inside. She has endured a great deal. I hope the heir will treat her more gently going forward."

Xie Xi said, "The child Wan'er lost was mine as well. I am heartbroken too. Please set your mind at ease, Mother-in-law — I will make it up to Wan'er from here on."

The Marchioness shook her head and stepped aside.

Xie Xi walked in. The thick smell of medicine made him wrinkle his nose.

He went to the bedside and looked down at the wan, exhausted woman. "Wan'er, I've come to take you home."

Jiang Wan slowly opened her eyes. She looked at him as though she had heard something faintly absurd. "Take me home?"

He disliked the sharp edge in her voice but kept his patience. "The Empress Dowager has had the maid executed and had Bai delivered early and sent to the country estate. Wan'er, you should be able to let this anger go now."

Jiang Wan looked at him with a stranger's eyes. Listening to his words, she could hear that he was still grieving for Concubine Bai. She touched her own abdomen and said bitterly, "Xie Xi, the child I was carrying was yours too. Did you spare a single thought for it?"

Xie Xi frowned. "Wan'er, I know you're in pain, and I am too. But it truly was only an accident."

His words extinguished the last faint ember of hope in Jiang Wan's heart.

Jiang Wan let out a low, soft laugh. As the laughter continued, tears began to fall. "Xie Xi, go. I won't be going back to the Duke of Ying's household."

Xie Xi clenched both fists. "Wan'er, don't do something you'll regret."

When he came out of the room, his expression was dark. He gave the Marchioness a brief bow and left without another word.

The Marchioness hurried inside. At the sight of Jiang Wan, tear tracks still fresh on her face, she was alarmed. She pulled out her handkerchief and wiped them away. "Wan'er, you mustn't cry — this is like your lying-in period. Crying too much will damage your health."

She asked urgently, "What did that Xie Xi say to you?"

Jiang Wan stared blankly at the canopy above. She murmured, "He said he came to take me home..."

"And everything between the lines was asking me to let it all go and smooth things over." Another quiet, hollow laugh. "And still grieving for the concubine they've shipped off to the estate. And yet he says he's come to take me home. How funny. How utterly absurd."

The Marchioness looked at her daughter's dazed, hollow expression and ached with sorrow.

She drew Jiang Wan into her arms. "Wan'er, if you hadn't married into the Duke of Ying's household — if you'd married into a smaller, ordinary family — I would have had a bowl of medicine forced down that woman's throat and sold her off to the farthest place we could find. But as it is, even with your aunt as the Empress Dowager, we had to bite down our anger and let her keep her child and simply send her away. That baby is the Duke of Ying's first grandson. If we had forced the pregnancy to end, the Duke might have swallowed his resentment out of deference to the Empress Dowager — but he would have held it in his heart, and how would you have lived in that household afterward? And that Concubine Bai still has family ties to the Duchess — who knows what torments she'd have devised for you then."

"If the Emperor were your aunt's own son, we could have dealt with it however we pleased and the Duke of Ying's household wouldn't have dared to breathe a word. But the Emperor is not. The Jiang family's position is neither high nor low, and it is truly..."

The Marchioness's voice broke, and she could say no more. She could only hold Jiang Wan and weep silently.

Jiang Wan stirred. She turned her head and said in a low, hollow voice, "I don't care how they handle Concubine Bai. Keep her or send her away — it makes no difference to me now. Mother, I don't want to go back to the Duke of Ying's household. I don't want to have anything to do with them ever again."

The Marchioness's whole body went rigid. "Wan'er, you — you can't mean you want a formal separation?"

……

Outside the imperial study, Li Fu, wearing a pleasant smile, saw off the last of the officials who had just concluded their business with the Emperor.

He carried in a cup of hot tea to find His Majesty already reaching for the next memorial.

He quietly replaced the cold cup and stepped back, hesitating for a moment before gathering his nerve to speak. "Your Majesty, the Empress Dowager summoned a physician last night."

Xiao Huaiyan did not look up. "Is that so?"

He wrote a few characters with the vermilion brush, then added, "Feeling unwell again?"

Li Fu, relieved to have an opening, replied, "Her Ladyship the Empress Dowager is in good health. The physician was sent to look in on the young lady from the Marquis of Cheng'en's household — the Empress Dowager's maternal family — who married into the Duke of Ying's household. She suffered a miscarriage after being knocked into by a concubine. The Empress Dowager sent the physician to attend to her."

Li Fu finished speaking and waited. When the Emperor said nothing, he glanced up cautiously.

His Majesty was still bent over the memorials. Li Fu did not dare say another word.

Xiao Huaiyan set down the last stroke and put aside the brush, rotating his wrist. He asked, "When did this happen?"

"Around the Hour of the Goat yesterday, Your Majesty."

Xiao Huaiyan's fingers tapped on the desk. That was precisely the hour he had been in the private residence outside the palace.

He turned the jade thumb ring slowly and said, "Since it is the Empress Dowager's maternal family who has been wronged, send some gifts over. Li Fu, go yourself."

Li Fu said warmly, "Your Majesty's filial devotion is a model to all. This servant will see to it at once."

……

The Marchioness came out of Jiang Wan's room and made her way to the Marquis's study.

The Marquis had returned only recently. Seeing his wife come in, he asked, "How is Wan'er? I heard Xie Xi paid a visit. What is the Duke of Ying's household's position?"

The Marchioness's face was pinched with difficulty. She knew what Wan'er wanted, and the Marquis would certainly not agree. But thinking of her daughter's wretched appearance, she steeled herself and said, "The Duke of Ying's household has kept the child and sent Concubine Bai away. Xie Xi came to take Wan'er back, and she refused."

The Marquis thought for a moment. "It hasn't even been a day and they're already at the door trying to take her back. Xie Xi mismanaged his household and allowed it to fall into disorder — Wan'er is right not to go back just yet."

Seeing that the door was open, the Marchioness let it all out at once. "Wan'er wants to never go back. She wants a formal separation."

The moment the words were out, the Marquis slapped his palm on the table and shot to his feet. "Nonsense! She cannot simply decide to separate because she wishes it!"

The Marchioness covered her face and wept. "My lord, Wan'er has been suffering! Look at the state of her now — does she bear any resemblance to that lively girl she was before she married? It wouldn't have mattered that the heir had concubines and bed-warmers, as long as he gave Wan'er the respect owed to a first wife — she would have managed the household for him in return. But from what Dong'er has told me, the heir dotes shamelessly on Concubine Bai and amuses himself with his bed-warmers, and whenever Wan'er tries to say a word, she is rebuked. Even the favored bed-warmers have dared to give her a cold look to her face. And the Duchess — she keeps a firm hand on all the household management and won't let Wan'er touch a thing, yet demands that Wan'er attend on her daily. Wan'er has been through agony."

The Marchioness grew more and more agitated. "My lord! The Empress Dowager sent a physician, and the physician said Wan'er will struggle to conceive in the future. Do you understand what that means? A first wife who cannot produce children — how is she supposed to hold her position in the Duke of Ying's household? Are you prepared to watch Wan'er be compelled to formally adopt the concubine's son as her own? And that child's birth mother is still living — the boy will never truly regard Wan'er as his mother. And if the boy ever grows up and learns the truth, who knows what resentment will fester. If Wan'er stays in that household, it will be the end of her."

The Marquis said nothing for a long while. He paced with a deeply furrowed brow.

The Marchioness's heart swung between hope and despair. She knew that for their eldest daughter to formally separate from the Duke of Ying's heir was no small matter — it required not only the Marquis's blessing but the Empress Dowager's approval as well.

"Marriages between great families are largely alliances," the Marquis said at last. "It was the Empress Dowager's intention, and it was a step up for Wan'er, that she should marry into the Duke of Ying's household. Now that the Empress Dowager has sent someone to deal with the concubine while still leaving room for the relationship to continue, she clearly does not wish to sever the bond between the two families entirely."

The Marchioness knew all of this perfectly well, and that was precisely what made her heart ache. She had hoped the Marquis would go and plead their case before the Empress Dowager.

The Marquis sighed and said, "Are you aware that the Emperor has once again rejected the Ministry of Rites' petition to select an Empress and consorts?"

The Marchioness looked up in alarm. "His Majesty — he still doesn't want Tangtang?"

The Marquis frowned. "A woman's thinking. It's not a matter of wanting or not wanting — one more woman in the inner palace, one less woman, makes no difference to the Emperor. The continued rejections mean he is dissatisfied with the proposals themselves, or dissatisfied with the Jiang family. His Majesty must already have his own candidate in mind."

"Then will Tangtang still enter the palace?"

The Marquis said gravely, "Of course she will. The Empress Dowager will find a way to get Tangtang into the palace no matter what. The Jiang family must have a woman close to the Emperor's side."

"My lord — do you mean that whether Wan'er can separate depends not only on the Empress Dowager's consent but on the Emperor's attitude as well?"

"If the Emperor is willing to grant the Jiang family more face," the Marquis said, "Wan'er's life in the Duke of Ying's household will not be so difficult." What he left unspoken was this: if the Emperor turned his back on the Duke of Ying's household, then and only then might the Empress Dowager be persuaded.

The Marchioness's heart sank. The Marquis's meaning was clear — he had no intention of supporting the separation. Was Wan'er truly to spend the rest of her life suffering in that household?

She was about to leave the study when the Marquis called after her. "Oh — and that Minister of Revenue's family you looked into for Jiang Yi's match, go and make contact again. It seems that official may be in line for a promotion."

The Marchioness nodded.

Just then the steward came running with a look of pure delight. "My lord! My lady! The palace has sent gifts down! Li Fu himself has come with the Emperor's gifts!"

The Marquis and Marchioness both froze.

The Emperor had sent gifts? And had Li Fu come in person?

The Marquis hastily straightened his clothing, and together they made their way to the main hall to receive the imperial favor with proper thanks.

Not only the masters of the main branch but those of the second branch as well were required to go and offer their gratitude.

……

Back in Yuanzhi Courtyard, Jiang Mi was watching Jiang Rong and Jiang Xuan practice their calligraphy when Lady Su came hurrying over to tell her that Li Fu had arrived with the Emperor's gifts.

She was to come to the main branch's reception hall at once to pay her respects.

Even little Jiang Rong and Jiang Xuan had to go.

Jiang Mi had no choice but to go along.

When she arrived at the main hall, she saw that every member of the Marchioness's household, large and small, had assembled — everyone except Cousin Wan, who could not rise from her bed. Even the confined Jiang Yi was there.

Jiang Mi kept her gaze lowered. This really was quite the imperial spectacle.

She listened as Li Fu read out the oral decree — its substance amounted to no more than praise of Xiao Huaiyan's filial piety and a gesture of comfort toward the Marquis of Cheng'en's household.

Looking at the gifts — mostly rare and precious medicinal tonics — she understood they were all for Cousin Wan. An imperial physician had even been left behind specifically to oversee Cousin Wan's recovery.

Between the Duke of Ying's household and the Marquis of Cheng'en's household, the Emperor had inclined toward the wronged party.

Jiang Mi stood among the others and offered her curtsy of thanks along with everyone else.

Li Fu noticed that while smiles of delight had spread across the faces of everyone in the household, the Jiang family's Third Miss kept her head bowed the entire time.

He had heard from Chengzhong that His Majesty had frightened her to tears yesterday — cried herself absolutely wretched, the poor thing. Perhaps she still hadn't recovered.

Li Fu didn't linger and headed back to the palace.

As he reached the gate of Qianqing Palace, he came face to face with the Embroidered Guard Commander Pei Chi stepping out of the hall — that severe, icy expression of his was enough to set anyone's nerves on edge. Li Fu called out, "Lord Pei."

Pei Chi paused beside him, those dark eyes shifting over with a ghost of a smile. "Li Fu, back from your errand."

Li Fu laughed politely. "Indeed — and I must go and report back to His Majesty, so I can't keep you company any longer."

Qianqing Palace blazed with light inside.

Li Fu stood within the hall and said softly, "The eldest young mistress of the Marchioness of Cheng'en's household is still bedridden and unable to rise to give her thanks. As for the Third Miss of the Jiang family — this servant spotted her in the crowd. She seems to have lost a little weight..."

Xiao Huaiyan's expression hovered between amusement and something cooler. "Did We ask you for that?"

Li Fu dropped to his knees with a thud. "This servant spoke out of turn."

……

The following morning, Jiang Mi was in the courtyard making chestnut cakes. She steamed the fresh chestnuts through, blended in a little flower-petal syrup, and shaped them into small pastries — soft, fragrant, and gently sweet.

She set a small portion aside for Jiang Rong and Jiang Xuan, who would be coming to practice their calligraphy after noon.

She packed the cakes into a food box and prepared to head to Mingxiu Courtyard.

Then Chunhui came running back, all smiles. "Miss, miss! Something's been happening over at Mingxiu Courtyard! The Duchess of Ying came with the heir to apologize and ask the First Young Mistress to come home — and the Marchioness sent them away!"

"What happened?" Jiang Mi asked.

"I don't know all the details," Chunhui said, "only that early this morning the Duchess and the heir came with medicine and tonics to call on the First Young Mistress — and that in the middle of all the talking they said they'd come to bring her back, and she refused. Things got a bit tense, and in the end the Marchi

chapters.title: Twenty-seven
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi came out from behind the folding screen, her cheeks faintly warm. Though she hadn't meant to, she had overheard the whole of her great-aunt and cousin's conversation.

The Marchioness came forward warmly, pulling Jiang Mi close and taking the food box from her hands. "Tangtang, you brought something!"

Jiang Mi smiled. "I told Cousin Wan yesterday I'd make some pastries and bring them over."

Jiang Wan nodded, her smile gentle. "I've been thinking about Tangtang's pastries ever since — I haven't even taken my medicine yet."

Jiang Mi looked at Jiang Wan. She was still drawn and pale, but there was clearly more light in her eyes than there had been yesterday.

The Marchioness opened the food box to find a collection of delicately shaped flower pastries inside. She exclaimed in admiration, "I had no idea Tangtang had such a skill."

Jiang Mi thought to herself that she hadn't always — she had developed it in her past life, teaching herself painstakingly in the hope of learning someone's tastes and pleasing him. That effort, at least, had not gone entirely to waste.

"Please try one, Cousin Wan, Great-aunt — I hope they suit your palate."

The Marchioness set a plate of the pastries before Jiang Wan, who picked one up. "They're so beautiful — almost too pretty to eat."

Even as she said it, she took a bite. Her brows lifted slightly. "Chestnut cake? And a hint of osmanthus? Soft and fragrant, sweet but not cloying — these are the finest chestnut cakes I've ever tasted."

They were small and dainty, nearly one bite each. Jiang Wan finished one in two mouthfuls and reached for a second.

The Marchioness, seeing Jiang Wan so thoroughly won over, tried one for herself and praised it without reservation.

All the compliments made Jiang Mi feel a little shy. She thought of how in her past life she had put her heart into cooking for that person and been met with nothing but complaints, and felt a small measure of comfort — at least the skill remained, and she could make things for herself and share them with the people she cared about. There was some use in that, after all.

The Marchioness, sensing that the two cousins had things to say to each other, excused herself.

Jiang Wan finished her medicine, then ate two more chestnut cakes.

She leaned back against the soft pillow and looked at the lovely young woman before her, asking gently, "Tangtang — you heard everything my mother and I said just now, didn't you?"

Jiang Mi gripped the handkerchief in her hands and nodded.

Jiang Wan's smile turned bittersweet. "And what do you think?"

The Duke of Ying's household had already made concessions, and Jiang Wan's insistence on separation was bound to be misunderstood by the world. It would also cast a shadow over the reputations of the other young women in the family. She wondered what Jiang Mi would say.

Jiang Mi lifted her head and said, slowly and clearly, "If Cousin Wan truly wants the separation — then Tangtang congratulates you on your new beginning."

Jiang Wan stared at her, mouth half open, momentarily struck speechless.

She had imagined many possible responses — words of comfort, words of persuasion — but she had never imagined that this cousin, four years her junior, would congratulate her on a new beginning.

Her father had refused. Even her mother had wavered. None of them understood that, to her, remaining trapped in the Duke of Ying's household was a life without light — that separation was the only way to breathe again.

Jiang Wan's eyes filled with tears at once. Her throat went tight. She breathed slowly, deeply, until she had pressed the surging emotion back down.

Jiang Mi rose and poured her a cup of water, helping her drink a few sips.

When Jiang Wan had steadied herself, she said, "Tangtang, I'll take your blessing. And may you get everything you wish for as well."

She knew that Jiang Mi had long been drawn to the new Emperor, and that entering the palace was not only the Empress Dowager's hope for her, but something Jiang Mi herself genuinely wanted — to be at the Emperor's side.

She could only wish her cousin's fortune would be better than her own.

The thought brought to mind something from long ago. "Tangtang," she asked, "do you still have that butterfly kite of yours?"

Jiang Mi, who had just been setting the teacup back on the table, faltered. Her hand trembled slightly, and she barely kept hold of it.

She turned around, her tone studiously calm. "What kite, Cousin Wan? I can barely remember now."

Jiang Wan found this strange. She remembered that Tangtang had come back from the palace clutching that butterfly kite as though it were the most precious thing in the world — as though she wanted to take it to bed with her. She had teased Tangtang about borrowing it for a day, and the little girl had clung to it with both arms, refusing to let go.

She had said it was something a prince had made for her especially, and that she was almost too fond of it to even fly it herself.

That prince was now the Emperor.

Tangtang had treasured it so dearly then. Why did she now claim not to remember?

Jiang Wan could see that Jiang Mi was unwilling to speak further on the subject, so she let it go. They talked of other things, until the medicine began to make Jiang Wan drowsy, and only then did Jiang Mi take her leave.

On the way back to Yuanzhi Courtyard, Jiang Mi's thoughts drifted back to when she was ten years old.

She had been staying at the palace as a guest of the Empress Dowager. Palace maids and eunuchs had accompanied her to fly a kite in the imperial garden — but the Princess Anle, daughter of a favored consort, had spotted her.

Princess Anle had an imperious, vicious temperament, and Jiang Mi usually did everything she could to avoid her. But this time she had the misfortune of running straight into her. The Princess had told her attendants to snatch the kite down, dash it at Jiang Mi's feet, and grind it underfoot.

When Jiang Mi started to cry, the Princess had warned her: if she dared to run to her aunt and tell on her, she would have her consort mother go to the Emperor and complain, and Jiang Mi would never be allowed to visit the palace again.

Terrified that Princess Anle would make good on the threat — and afraid of causing trouble for the Empress Dowager — Jiang Mi had not made a sound. The palace maids and eunuchs who had been with her were all afraid of Princess Anle and her mother, and pretended nothing had happened.

After Princess Anle and her party left, Jiang Mi had crouched down and gathered up the trampled kite and cried her heart out.

In the middle of her tear-streaked, snot-nosed weeping, a gentle voice had come from somewhere above her. "Now who does this little girl belong to, crying with a face like a little tabby cat?"

She had looked up through blurry tears to see an extraordinarily handsome older boy smiling down at her. He had ruffled her hair. "Since the kite is broken, we'll just make you a new one."

She had sniffled and stared at him in disbelief.

He had told an attendant to fetch some materials.

She heard the attendant call him "Your Highness."

When the things were brought, he painted a butterfly onto the kite himself, wings spread in mid-flight — far more beautiful than the dragonfly kite that had been ruined. She watched him finish assembling it. Then he held it out toward her and said, "Give me a smile, and this new kite is yours."

She no longer remembered exactly how she had smiled, only that she had taken the kite, and heard that warm, unhurried voice say: "What a sweet smile for a little girl."

Afterward she ran to the Empress Dowager with the kite and asked who the prince was and why she had never seen him before. The Empress Dowager had been surprised she had met him, and explained that the prince had been stationed at the frontier and rarely came back to the capital.

Jiang Mi had tucked the butterfly kite away with the utmost care, waiting for a chance to meet that gentle older boy again.

As the little girl slowly grew into a young woman, she never saw him again — but she kept that warmth he had left her tucked away in her heart.

The next time she met him, he was already the master of all under heaven.

When the Empress Dowager told her she was to enter his inner palace, no one knew how happy she had been.

……

Without noticing, she had walked all the way back to Yuanzhi Courtyard. Fallen leaves lay scattered across the ground again. Jiang Mi did not disturb anyone. She went alone up to the attic.

From a place she still remembered, she found a wooden box.

She opened it. Inside lay a butterfly kite, its colors faded with time.

Though it had aged, it had been kept with care.

Jiang Mi took the kite out and sat holding it, her thoughts drifting to her past life.

It had been in the peach blossom grove at the detached palace. Xiao Huaiyan had taken her on horseback together, riding at a leisurely pace into the depths of the grove. Laughter floated in from the distance; she had pressed herself against him, trembling with nerves, terrified that the consorts and ladies out enjoying the blossoms would wander their way.

But his arm was locked around her waist, and she had no way to move. She could only clutch at his arm helplessly, tilting her head back to look at the sky.

A kite drifted past overhead, set free by someone, and she had fallen into watching it.

But his fingers had caught her chin and turned her face back. "Pay attention..."

His thumb, pressing against her lips, had found a stray petal of peach blossom there and slowly rubbed it to pieces. Then he had bent down over her.

By the time she had caught her breath, the kite that had been floating in the sky was gone.

She hadn't understood why she had asked him what she asked, in that moment. "Your Majesty — do you still remember giving a kite to a little girl, once?"

His voice had been warm as always, just barely touched with hoarseness. "A kite? We don't remember."

……

In the dim attic, Jiang Mi sat on the floor against the wooden box.

Drops of tears fell onto the old kite, spreading in dark circles.

She held the kite in her lap. A look of struggle crossed her face. She closed her eyes and pulled hard — and the brittle paper tore apart with ease.

Dust rose around her. She stared at the scattered pieces on the floor, and suddenly began to cry out loud.

……

Time moved quickly. Before she knew it, the day of the Zhenguo Duke's flower banquet had arrived.

Jiang Mi dressed in a long gown of pale yellow brocade, a jade and kingfisher hairpin trembling in her hair, and took Qiuyu's hand to board the Marquis of Cheng'en's carriage.

Inside the carriage, Jiang Yi and Jiang Rong were already waiting. Jiang Yi was dressed charmingly and prettily; Jiang Rong looked quietly lovely.

Jiang Rong scooted over to make room, hoping her Third Elder Sister would sit beside her — but Jiang Yi's hand darted out first, catching hold of Jiang Mi and pulling her to her own side. "Tangtang, you're finally here. Ah-Rong is a little sealed pot — I've been talking at her for half a day without getting more than a few words back. I was nearly suffocated."

Jiang Mi gave Jiang Yi an amused look. "You're teasing Ah-Rong again, Elder Sister Yi!"

Jiang Yi laughed. "You've gotten rather protective of that little one."

"We're all sisters. There's nothing to be protective about."

Jiang Rong's shy smile surfaced, and she murmured, "That's right — Third Elder Sister has always been kind to me."

Jiang Yi clicked her tongue. "Little sealed pot — now you suddenly find your voice."

Jiang Rong had been confined to the courtyard for so long that now, finally out with her sisters and able to talk freely, once she started she found she couldn't stop. The words kept coming.

"If you ask me, the first thing to look for in a match is whether the household has some frail, delicate distant cousin drifting about — the kind who swoops and nearly faints the moment a breeze comes by. That's exactly how it went wrong for my elder sister. That Concubine Bai was Xie Xi's cousin. I visited the Duke of Ying's household once and caught sight of her. She would deliberately stand out in the corridor, and the moment Xie Xi appeared she would sway on her feet and pretend to nearly faint. Xie Xi assumed it was my elder sister who had mistreated Bai, and he carried that woman off right in front of my elder sister's face. Disgusting creature — and an unmarried girl like me was standing right there!"

Jiang Mi knew Jiang Yi harbored deep grievances against the Duke of Ying's household and that the Marchioness had been keeping her from speaking of it — so it only came out when she was with the two of them. Listening to her, Jiang Mi thought of how in her past life, the Concubine Rouyi had also been Xiao Huaiyan's cousin. That particular bond between cousins did seem to carry a special weight, again and again.

She said, "Then you'd better have Great-aunt pay attention, Elder Sister Yi — you should find out whether your 'piece of charcoal' has any distant female cousins living under his roof."

Jiang Yi's face went pink. "What nonsense — there's nothing to any of that!"

She turned to drag Jiang Rong into it. "Ah-Rong might be young, but twelve is twelve. Your mother has probably already started looking quietly. Ah-Rong, you'd better remember what your elder sister told you."

Jiang Mi covered Jiang Rong's ears. "Elder Sister Yi, don't go corrupting Ah-Rong."

The three sisters dissolved into cheerful chaos — and the Zhenguo Duke's household came into view.

chapters.title: Twenty-eight
chapters.content:
The Marquis of Cheng'en's party alighted from their carriage and boarded the soft-curtained sedan chairs the Zhenguo Duke's household had arranged.

The route was a procession of dazzling flowers, painted towers, ornate eaves catching the light — a feast for the eyes.

The chairs set down before the festooned inner gate, where maids and servants clustered to welcome the guests and escort them to Rong'an Hall to pay their respects to the Grand Princess Zhaoyang.

The Marchioness walked in front; Jiang Mi and her two cousins followed quietly behind.

Rong'an Hall was already lively — nearly half of the capital's noble households had sent representatives.

The moment Jiang Mi stepped inside, several familiar faces caught her eye.

The Duchess of the Duke of Xie was seated with her daughter Xie Mingshan at her side. The wife of Grand Secretary Shen had Shen Yaowei to her left, Shen Yaowei deep in conversation with an unfamiliar girl beside her. The Duchess of the Duke of Ying was there too, with her daughter Xie Yanyue.

Several wives and daughters of Ministers and Vice-Ministers were also present, though the military households were represented more sparingly.

Whatever their true reasons for coming — genuine flower appreciation or something else altogether — the Zhenguo Duke's household held a very particular place in the hearts of the capital's noble families.

Jiang Mi followed the Marchioness in offering a curtsy to the Grand Princess Zhaoyang.

No sooner had she settled into place than a figure came rushing straight toward her, linking arms with her enthusiastically. "Jiang Elder Sister, I've been waiting and waiting for you!"

The speaker was Xue Ningzhu, the Grand Princess's most beloved granddaughter.

The Grand Princess smiled broadly. "Since the palace gathering, this child has done nothing but talk about Miss Jiang. Now she'll have to do her duty as a proper hostess."

Xue Ningzhu quickly chimed in, "Of course! Later I'm going to take Jiang Elder Sister on a proper tour of the gardens."

She settled in right beside Jiang Mi as the Jiang party found their seats, planting herself there without any further deliberation.

"How is Miangmiang?" Xue Ningzhu asked.

Jiang Mi nodded with a smile. "All healed up — eating well and bounding about."

Xue Ningzhu's face lit up. "That's wonderful! Can I come visit her at the Marquis's household some time?"

"Of course you can."

Xue Ningzhu leaned closer and said in Jiang Mi's ear, "Oh, and my brother has a cat too. I'll take you to see it later."

Jiang Mi was a little surprised by Xue Ningzhu's easy familiarity. She hesitated. "Only if it's no trouble..."

"None at all!" Xue Ningzhu said at once.

Jiang Mi's lashes fluttered. She smiled and nodded.

In truth, aside from accepting the invitation, she had another purpose in coming to the Zhenguo Duke's household today — she wanted to understand what kind of man the heir that all the noble young ladies supposedly longed to marry actually was.

Jiang Rong noticed that Xue Ningzhu kept up a constant stream of chatter with her elder sister, and a flicker of something wistful passed over her brow. If only she could talk that easily. Would Elder Sister think she was dull?

Jiang Yi, meanwhile, ate her pastries and sipped her flower tea with great contentment.

Leisurely, she raised her teacup and cast a deliberate glance at Xie Yanyue of the Duke of Xie's household.

Xie Yanyue had never been fond of her brother's wife to begin with — and now that wife was refusing to come back despite both her mother and brother showing up to apologize, and she had been furious about it for days. Having that woman's sister now shoot her a pointed look across the room when she could do absolutely nothing about it — her face went a shade of green.

The ladies and young women seated in the flower hall all wore pleasant expressions as they observed the Zhenguo Duke's treasured granddaughter and the Marquis of Cheng'en's miss conversing with obvious warmth.

Inside, every one of them was quietly turning over the same question: when had the Zhenguo Duke's household and the Marquis of Cheng'en's become so close?

Xie Mingshan had her head bowed, twisting the handkerchief in her hands as though she wanted to tear it apart.

The Duchess of the Duke of Xie reached over and pressed her hand down gently.

Xie Mingshan's eyes reddened and she said under her breath, "Mother."

The Duchess murmured, "Ashan — your aunt has always told you: have patience. Nothing is decided until it's over."

Xie Mingshan knew all of this. And yet she could not suppress her bitterness. She had been so close to becoming Empress — so close to entering the palace and becoming his — and all of it had been ruined because of Jiang Mi alone.

The reason she was even at this flower banquet was that her mother hoped that since the palace was no longer possible, perhaps a marriage tie with the Zhenguo Duke's household might do.

But she had not expected the Jiang family to turn up at this gathering as well.

Shen Yaowei watched the quiet undercurrents running through the room and pressed her lips together in a faint smile. She murmured to the person beside her, "Xian Cousin — that Miss Jiang over there, talking with Ningzhu, is the one my Third Brother played the hero for. Would you say she's beautiful?"

He Xiuxian's expression stiffened slightly, and she answered with some effort, "She is, indeed."

……

Once all the guests had arrived, the Grand Princess Zhaoyang rose and led the assembled ladies out to Qin Garden to view the flowers.

The Duchess of the Zhenguo Duke's household supported the Grand Princess's arm as they walked at the head of the procession; the other guests followed behind.

Everyone had expected the heir of the Zhenguo Duke's household to come and pay his respects to the Grand Princess, but he did not appear, and there was a small ripple of disappointment. They supposed he would at least make an appearance at the flower banquet in Lake Heart Pavilion later.

The moment they entered Qin Garden, a wave of mingled fragrance washed over them. Inside these walls there were no seasons — no trace of autumn's melancholy, only a brilliant, riotous profusion of color, as if all the flowers of spring were competing for attention at once.

There were many varieties none of them had seen before, strange and remarkable. There were even specimens that existed only in books and paintings, rendering the visitors motionless in wonder.

Everyone agreed: only a household with the Zhenguo Duke's influence could have gathered such a collection in one place.

Jiang Mi stood before a ten-scholar camellia, studying the patterns on its petals. Jiang Yi had already run off with Jiang Rong to another part of the garden.

Xue Ningzhu's eyes darted around, then she tugged at Jiang Mi's hand. "Jiang Elder Sister, shall I take you to see the cat? It's white too, like Miangmiang — but with a few markings. Very pretty."

Jiang Mi was quiet for a moment. Then she gave a slow nod. "All right."

She reminded Jiang Yi to keep an eye on Jiang Rong, then followed Xue Ningzhu away from the group.

Even if anyone noticed, no one would think much of it — only that Xue Ningzhu was taking her guest to see another part of the garden.

Xue Ningzhu led Jiang Mi along a twisting route through dense, green trees that sloped down toward the lakeshore. It grew quieter as they walked.

"Is it this way?" Jiang Mi asked.

Xue Ningzhu nodded. "My brother keeps the white cat in a courtyard set apart from everyone, but I've snuck in to visit it a few times. It'll be fine, Jiang Elder Sister."

She pointed to a pavilion built over the center of the lake. "That's Lake Heart Pavilion — after we've seen the cat, we'll head straight there for the banquet. Grandmother said every dish at the banquet will have flowers in it somehow. We should hurry so we're not late."

Jiang Mi listened, growing more puzzled.

Why was the cat kept somewhere so removed from everyone? Why did Xue Ningzhu have to sneak in to see it? What manner of man was this heir of the Zhenguo Duke's household?

They passed through a rockery, and Xue Ningzhu suddenly stopped, brow furrowed, pressing a hand to her stomach. "Jiang Elder Sister, wait here for a moment. I need to use the privy. I'll be right back."

Jiang Mi nodded. "Go quickly — and mind you don't trip."

After Xue Ningzhu left, the surroundings felt even more hushed. Only the occasional call of a bird broke the silence.

The spot where Jiang Mi stood was close to the lake. The water was a clear, mirror-bright green, its surface stirred only when the breeze came through.

She was looking out at the still water when a five-colored ball went rolling and splashing into the lake. A moment later, a child of about three came running after it.

The ball, which had been drifting near the bank, began floating farther out as the child moved — and the child, intent on it, stepped forward into the water as though he meant to wade in and grab it.

Jiang Mi's heart lurched into her throat. She ran over in a few quick strides and scooped the child up before he could go any further.

She carried him back from the bank. He was a beautiful little boy, chubby-cheeked and richly dressed. She asked, "Whose child are you? Why is there no maid looking after you? Do you know how dangerous that water is!"

The child writhed in her arms, reaching toward the water. "Ball — ball ball!"

He flailed his arms and nearly struck Jiang Mi in the face.

Just as she was struggling to keep hold of him, a maid came rushing over in a panic. "Young Master, how did you end up here?"

Jiang Mi set the child down. The maid caught hold of his hand and tried to lead him away.

But the child yanked free and charged back toward Jiang Mi — and shoved her with both hands as hard as he could.

Jiang Mi looked at that furious little face, and something flickered through her mind, gone before she could catch it.

In that moment of disorientation, the push sent her stumbling backward several steps.

"Miss, careful!"

A man's voice rang out behind her, and something pressed against the small of her back, giving her just enough support to catch her balance.

Jiang Mi turned around. A young man in deep green brocade robes, face as fine as carved jade, withdrew his hand — a jade flute in his grip — and gave a bow. "I acted without thinking just now. Forgive the impropriety."

Jiang Mi returned his bow in silence, by way of thanks.

When she looked back toward the child, she could only see him being carried away by the maid.

The young man also watched the child go, his expression thoughtful. "With so many guests in the household today, that child must belong to one of the visiting families. Fortunately, Miss was there to intervene."

This was a secluded spot. With only her and this young man present, it was not a good place to linger.

Jiang Mi gave a small nod and made to leave.

From somewhere ahead, Xue Ningzhu's voice called out, "Jiang Elder Sister!"

The young man smiled. "I was too forward." With that, he tucked the jade flute under his arm and walked away in the opposite direction, his dark green figure quickly swallowed up by the trees.

Xue Ningzhu came stumbling back, skirts in hand and slightly breathless. "Jiang Elder Sister, how did you end up over here? I've been looking everywhere."

"The scenery here caught my eye," Jiang Mi said, deciding to say nothing about what had just happened.

Xue Ningzhu glanced around, looking unimpressed — she had probably grown up looking at it and was tired of it. She linked her arm through Jiang Mi's with easy familiarity. "Come on, Jiang Elder Sister. I promise the white cat is far more interesting than any of this."

Jiang Mi let herself be led along, though her attention was now only half in it.

They passed through a stand of bamboo and came to a small courtyard.

Standing at the courtyard gate, Jiang Mi thought she heard something low and rumbling from within.

Xue Ningzhu visibly slowed her steps. With one hand pulling Jiang Mi along and the other pushing open the gate, she eased it open.

When she saw what was in the courtyard, Jiang Mi went absolutely still. Her pupils contracted sharply.

"Jiang Elder Sister, look — isn't it so much bigger than Miangmiang!"

Inside the great iron cage, there was no cat. There was a white tiger.

The white tiger had been dozing in the cage, but the moment it heard someone approach, it let out a low, rolling growl. Its golden eyes snapped open. It coiled itself slowly — then launched itself at the iron bars with a thunderous crash, throwing open its enormous jaws and roaring at the intruders.

The cage shuddered and clanged, rattling as though it might fly apart.

The sudden shock left Xue Ningzhu frozen where she stood, legs trembling.

Jiang Mi had gone weak all over, but she forced herself to stay calm. She seized Xue Ningzhu's hand. "Move! Now!"

They had only taken a few steps when another crash rang out. Xue Ningzhu was so terrified her legs gave out entirely, and she crumpled toward the ground.

Jiang Mi caught her just in time. Xue Ningzhu let out a sob of fright.

At that moment, rapid footsteps approached from ahead.

Jiang Mi looked up. The young man in the dark green robes from earlier was striding toward them, attendants close behind.

He looked alarmed. "Ningzhu — what are you doing here?"

Then he too heard the white tiger's roar and the crashing of the cage from inside the courtyard. He raised a hand. His attendants ran past him toward the courtyard.

Xue Ningzhu, still leaning on Jiang Mi, threw herself toward the young man. "Elder Brother Heir, I was so frightened! Why wasn't the big cat behaving today? I specially brought Jiang Elder Sister to see it, and it scared us both half to death."

Hearing Xue Ningzhu's form of address, Jiang Mi's heart jumped. This young man was the heir of the Zhenguo Duke's household?

Xue Jinglin pressed a fist to his lips and gave a few low coughs, then shook his head. "I have told you many times not to come to this place — you simply will not listen. And now you've frightened a guest. Ningzhu, this is inexcusable."

He coughed again.

Xue Ningzhu, duly chastened, did not dare answer back.

Xue Jinglin released Xue Ningzhu and bowed to Jiang Mi. "My sister was reckless. I hope Miss has not been too badly frightened."

Jiang Mi saw how pale he looked, a sickly quality in his complexion. The rumors about his health were evidently true.

She said quietly, "A creature like that — the heir should have someone keeping a closer watch. One would hate to see a disaster."

Xue Jinglin seemed to want to explain, then thought better of it and simply smiled. "The miss is quite right. The banquet in Lake Heart Pavilion is about to begin — allow me to escort you and Ningzhu over."

……

Lake Heart Pavilion had been built above a jade-green lake, water all around it, the views magnificent from every side.

Inside the pavilion most of the guests had already arrived. The Grand Princess, in the middle of a conversation, looked up as Jiang Mi and Xue Ningzhu walked in — and behind them, her eldest legitimate grandson, Xue Jinglin.

The Grand Princess smiled. "How convenient — everyone I was waiting for has arrived at once."

The moment she spoke, nearly every eye in the room turned to look at the three of them.

Under that shifting weight of glances, Jiang Mi felt the back of her scalp prickle. She and Xue Ningzhu curtsied together and made their way to their seats.

The heir of the Zhenguo Duke's household paid his respects to the Grand Princess with perfect composure, was drawn into a few exchanges with several of the ladies, then excused himself and departed.

Jiang Mi let out a quiet breath of relief. Good — everyone would assume it was a chance encounter, and nothing further would be made of it.

Xue Ningzhu sat next to Jiang Mi as before. Seeing the girl's subdued expression, Jiang Mi patted her head gently. "Don't sneak off to visit the big cat anymore. If you're missing Miangmiang, come to the Marquis of Cheng'en's household and play with her instead."

Xue Ningzhu's lips pushed out, and she held onto Jiang Mi's sleeve. "Jiang Elder Sister, it's all my fault..."

Jiang Mi smiled. "It was quite an experience, really — I'd never seen such a magnificent white tiger before. All right, all right — don't start crying."

The flower banquet began. A procession of delicate dishes were carried out by the maids, one after another.

Every dish incorporated flowers somehow — the colors, aromas, and flavors were all exquisite.

For the ladies, the wines had been prepared as flower-infused fruit wines.

Jiang Mi liked the sweet-tart taste of the fruit wine and had a few cups more than she intended.

Perhaps it was the wine that gradually smoothed away the lingering fright from the white tiger. Leaning against the railing afterward, she let the lake breeze wash over her, and it was actually quite pleasant.

Xue Ningzhu had been called over by the Grand Princess. Jiang Rong sat quietly at Jiang Mi's side. Jiang Yi had gone off to mingle with the other young ladies.

Out on the lake, many flower lanterns had been set adrift — all shapes and sizes, floating and circling Lake Heart Pavilion. The lanterns could be fished out with a bamboo pole; those who succeeded found small slips of paper inside with auspicious phrases or lines of poetry written on them.

One young woman drew a lantern with the words "the beauty outshines the flowers" written on it. The Grand Princess presented her with a rare variety of flower as her reward.

It was a game that had clearly been arranged specially for the young ladies' amusement — catch a lantern, earn a prize based on what the paper inside said.

The lanterns drifted with the water, clustering at the east end of the lake one moment and floating over to the west the next.

Where Jiang Mi was resting, it had grown progressively more crowded with others joining in the fun, the noise and laughter giving her a dull headache.

She rose and moved toward the viewing bridge for some space. The lake wind was stronger there, carrying a chill.

Then, without warning, the cluster of young women who had been fishing for lanterns let out frightened cries and came running toward her. "Snake! There's a snake!"

"Run! A snake!"

"Help!"

The pavilion erupted into chaos.

Young ladies, maids, and serving women all crashed together in a panicked surge. Jiang Mi reached for Jiang Rong's hand but was shoved aside by the crowd, her grip finding nothing — she and Jiang Rong were pushed further and further apart.

More and more bodies pressed toward her. Her head was spinning in waves. Suddenly something slammed hard into her back, and she lost all control of her balance, pitching backward and plunging off the bridge into the lake.

There was a great splash, and the water swallowed her completely.

The shouts from the bank grew distant, then faded to nothing.

The cold water engulfed Jiang Mi. Her clothing soaked through and grew heavier by the second.

She struggled in the water. The initial shock and terror slowly gave way as she forced herself to go calm. She shed the obstructing outer shawl, regulated her breathing, and fell back on the swimming technique she had been taught in her past life.

Bit by bit, she found her rhythm and began to make progress through the water.

Through the murk, Jiang Mi saw a figure swimming toward her. She assumed it was a servant woman who had dived in to save her and swam in that direction — but when the figure drew close, she reached out and grabbed hold of Jiang Mi's ankle, dragging her down toward the deep.

Jiang Mi kicked hard and repeatedly. The woman seemed not to have expected she could swim and was caught off guard long enough for Jiang Mi to slip free.

Jiang Mi poured every remaining ounce of strength into putting distance between herself and that figure.

But she dimly made out another shape approaching through the water. This one did not move like a servant woman — this was a man.

Several thoughts flashed through her mind at once. This fall into the water had been deliberate — aimed at her specifically.

Whether they wanted her dead or ruined, she could not let them succeed.

Jiang Mi swam with everything she had. She wasn't sure which direction she was headed; she only knew she had to get away from those shapes in the water.

She did not know how long she swam. She only knew that she was nearly spent.

She had to get out of the water now.

She chose a spot thick with tall grass and undergrowth that could serve as cover and hauled herself up onto the bank.

Hidden among the grass, she gasped for breath.

She had no idea how far this was from Lake Heart Pavilion. There was no one in sight.

Jiang Mi did not dare make a sound. She had no idea what to do next.

Her soaked clothing went ice cold the moment the wind touched it.

She wrapped her arms tightly around herself, curled up small.

She couldn't help it — a sneeze escaped her.

She wasn't sure whether she was starting to run a fever, but she felt a warmth kindling somewhere inside her, and an odd itching sensation, faint but persistent, that made her pluck at her own clothes without meaning to.

……
"What sort of flowers could tempt a man who's seen everything? Why would he bother with a common flower banquet?" A clear, easy voice drifted from somewhere in the distance.

"The Zhenguo Duke's household does make for interesting spectacle."

"If the Grand Princess knew you were here, one wonders what her expression would be." There was a hint of laughter in the second voice.

Jiang Mi clenched her teeth, shivering, alternating between hot and cold. When she pressed her hand to her own forehead it was burning hotter than before.

She could faintly make out voices — men's voices.

She pressed herself further into the tall grass. She could not be found, not like this — soaked through, in disarray, without even her embroidered slippers, which had been lost in the lake. She tucked her bare feet under the hem of her skirt.

Silently, she prayed: let them go, let them pass.

But she lost control of it — another sneeze.

"Who's there?" The easy voice went cold and sharp.

Footsteps, moving toward her.

"Miaow..."

A faint cat's cry sounded from somewhere deep in the grass.

The footsteps did not stop.

As they grew closer and closer, Jiang Mi's fear became absolute. She had no strength left to run. Despair flooded through her.

……

"Jingyan — stand down."

A cool, quiet voice halted Xue Jingyan.

Xue Jingyan stepped back, watching as his master himself walked toward the patch of grass and undergrowth that blocked the view.

He found it a little strange.

Jiang Mi's awareness was beginning to blur at the edges. She thought she had heard the footsteps turning away.

She waited until the outside seemed quiet again and thought, perhaps they had been fooled and had left.

She was just beginning to exhale with relief when the branches above her were lifted aside, and a tall figure stood over her.

The man's narrow phoenix eyes were dark as ink. He looked down at the woman curled in the grass. That brilliant, delicate face — it was impossible to tell whether the wetness on her cheeks was water or tears. Her eyes were thick with terror. Her clothes were soaked through and plastered against her, the curves of her figure left nothing to the imagination.

"What have we here — an actual cat. A drowned cat."

Jiang Mi, looking up against the light, had just barely made out his face when a heavy cloak came sweeping down and covered her completely, blotting out everything.

Then her body felt light, and she was lifted into someone's arms.

Swallowed in darkness, Jiang Mi was completely bewildered. Who had she just seen?

Xiao Huaiyan?

But why would Xiao Huaiyan be at the Zhenguo Duke's household?

She caught the scent of dragon-ambergris incense on the cloak, wrapping around her like a sealed room.

Xue Jingyan, standing at the ready, watched as his master walked out — apparently carrying something.

He blinked. That was indeed quite a large cat.

His master never stopped telling tall tales.

Xiao Huaiyan glanced at Xue Jingyan and said, "Find a room."

Xue Jingyan caught a glimpse of fabric peeking from beneath the hem of the cloak — fabric of the kind a woman would wear.

He understood at once where he needed to find a room.

He led his master to his sister's courtyard, to settle in the person he was carrying.

……

The man's warmth seeped through the soaked clothing and seemed to press into her.

Jiang Mi felt hotter. The itching sensation crept through her like a colony of ants, gnawing at her.

Especially where his arms held her, pressed against her — his palms and forearms burned against her, and she could barely stand it.

Her feet dangled in the air, swinging slightly with each step Xiao Huaiyan took, occasionally grazing against his leg without her noticing.

Jiang Mi was so focused on fighting down the heat and the torment inside her body that she was barely aware of any of it.

Xiao Huaiyan's phoenix eyes narrowed slightly. He could hear the heaviness in her breathing.

Under the cloak, the hand at his side reached out and closed around those swinging feet.

His roughened palm touched bare skin — and he paused. No embroidered shoes.

The foot was small and delicate. Ice cold to the touch.

Cold meeting heat. A collision.

That chill, like a small fish, tried to dart away — but was held firmly.

He gave a single light tap of warning. "Don't squirm."

chapters.title: Twenty-nine
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi froze and did not dare move again.

But she was burning and itching all over; she clutched the front of her own clothes in a death grip, trembling uncontrollably.

The sole of her foot was pressed against that scorching palm. The itching sensation became more unbearable than ever.

Xiao Huaiyan felt the person in his arms shaking badly. The hand holding her foot stroked it, almost without thinking.

The sensation jolted Jiang Mi. She convulsed — and a faint, involuntary sound escaped her lips.

She bit down hard on her lips, furious and humiliated, and tried to kick free. But her strength was laughably feeble — it was barely a protest.

A low laugh came from above.

Xue Jingyan walked ahead as though he had heard nothing, and led Xiao Huaiyan into a courtyard.

He opened one of the rooms and did not follow them inside. Once his master had carried her in, he shut the door behind them.

Xue Ninghua came running up and asked breathlessly, "Third Elder Brother — who was just brought inside?"

"Don't ask," Xue Jingyan said. "Pretend you know nothing."

Xue Ninghua nodded.

He asked, "Why weren't you at the flower banquet?"

"Didn't feel like joining in," she said, uninterested.

Xue Jingyan considered. "Go prepare a set of clean clothing. It may be needed shortly."

He added, "New clothing, if possible — unworn."

Xue Ninghua gave him a puzzled look but agreed without argument.

……

Xiao Huaiyan set the person down on the bed. The figure bundled inside the cloak curled up and shrank toward the wall.

Xiao Huaiyan's expression was dark and unreadable as he looked at the trembling form hunched over the brocade covers.

Jiang Mi's cheeks were flushed an unnatural red. Her apricot eyes were filmed over with tears.

Her wet hair was plastered against her face. The neckline of her dress had been pulled loose. She shook and shivered, her skin like poured cream.

Her bare feet lay without covering against the brocade surface, glowing faintly like jade in the light.

A soft, sweet, intoxicating picture — seemingly ripe for the taking.

Xiao Huaiyan turned the jade thumb ring on his finger without thinking, his gaze deep and still.

He leaned over and pulled the hand Jiang Mi had pressed to her own mouth away. Her slender white fingers bore deep tooth marks, and blood was already seeping from the wounds.

He reached up and locked her wrist in place above her head, and though she twisted and struggled, he did not loosen his grip by a fraction.

"Miss Jiang," he said, unhurried and low, "why would you harm yourself like this?"

Jiang Mi felt herself barely holding on. Pain was the only thing giving her brief moments of clarity, but even that faint hurt was no longer enough to suppress the burning, itching tide rising through her.

Xiao Huaiyan's voice reached her from somewhere far away. She gasped for breath, a dizzy surge of outrage rising in her — she did not believe for a second that Xiao Huaiyan could not see what state she was in. He was asking because he knew, and he was toying with her.

Her wrist was seized and pressed up above her head. Her body was forced to tilt toward him.

Jiang Mi was nearly at the breaking point. She breathed in the dragon-ambergris incense, the heat radiating from him. She was terrified she would not be able to stop herself — that she would reach up and throw her arms around his neck, pull him toward her, pull him--

Jiang Mi wrenched her eyes open with all the will she had left. She raised her unrestrained right hand and found his arm.

She arched toward him, turned her head, and sank her teeth savagely into his arm.

Xiao Huaiyan raised an eyebrow, mildly surprised.

"We would advise Miss Jiang to save her strength," he said.

Blood bloomed on Jiang Mi's tongue. But her jaw could no longer hold.

Xiao Huaiyan released her wrist. He caught her chin between his fingers, pressed his thumb to her lips — still touched with blood — and looked into eyes full of terror and desperate resistance.

Jiang Mi could no longer hear what he was saying. She only knew that the look in Xiao Huaiyan's eyes was exactly the same as it had been in her past life.

Xiao Huaiyan saw something wrong in her expression, and leaned closer to hear. She was murmuring over and over, broken and desperate: "No... no... Your Majesty, I beg you — please, please let me go..."

Something flickered briefly through his eyes.

His thumb pressed and kneaded against those soft lips. His voice dropped beside her ear. "If you don't want this, swallow it."

Jiang Mi felt her lips being worked until they ached. Then, abruptly, something small slid into her mouth.

Faintly bitter. It dissolved almost at once, slipping down her throat with her swallow.

She swallowed again. The bitterness spread slowly through her.

Xiao Huaiyan let go. He looked at her for a moment, then turned and walked out.

Jiang Mi pressed her face against the edge of the bed, coughing, staring red-eyed in the direction he had gone.

……

Xue Jingyan, keeping watch in the courtyard, looked up with surprise when the man emerged from the room. He stepped forward. "Your Majesty, what are your orders?"

Xiao Huaiyan pressed a hand briefly to his temple. "Have someone go in and help her change."

Xue Jingyan blinked, then recovered quickly. "Yes, Your Majesty."

He had seen the situation in there and assumed His Majesty would simply take the girl, here and now. He had not expected His Majesty to come back out this fast.

"Would Your Majesty care to rest in the side room?" Xue Jingyan offered.

A faint smile touched Xiao Huaiyan's face. "No. We want to see what's going on."

At that tone of voice, a chill ran straight down Xue Jingyan's spine. He could only hope the Grand Princess in Rong'an Hall would be able to bear the surprise of whatever was coming.

Li Fu and Pei Chi had been waiting outside for some time. The moment Xiao Huaiyan emerged, they bowed in unison.

Xue Jingyan saw His Majesty off first. He had to see to the guest in the courtyard before he could follow along to watch the fun.

He called his sister back over. "Ninghua — the guest in the east wing room, go yourself to deliver the clothing. Once she's settled, bring her to Rong'an Hall. When you get there, say it like this..."

Xue Ninghua listened to her brother's instructions, her expression shifting more than once before she agreed with all due seriousness.

……

Jiang Mi was huddled inside the bedclothes. She peeled off the soaked outer robe and set it to one side.

She was still debating whether to remove the inner garment when the door opened.

She scrambled back under the covers in a panic.

A bright, clear woman's voice came from the doorway. "Don't be frightened, Miss. I'm here to bring you clothing."

Xue Ninghua stepped inside.

She saw the face peeking out from between the brocade covers — snow-pale skin, flower-soft features — and beside the bed the heap of soaked and ruined clothing. She gave the frightened young woman a kind smile. "Miss, I'll have the maids bring some hot water so you can bathe first, and then you can change into something clean and dry. These are all new, never worn before — you needn't worry."

She set the clothes on the embroidered stool and stepped back out.

Jiang Mi slowly let out a breath.

When the door opened again, someone was pouring hot water into the bathing tub behind the screen.

The same clear voice reached her. "Miss, the water is ready. You may come out and bathe."

As the woman was about to pull the door shut, Jiang Mi said in a small voice, "Thank you, Miss."

Xue Ninghua paused, then smiled. "Not at all."

In the corridor, she told one of the maids quietly, "Go prepare a bowl of medicine for chasing away the cold."

……

Jiang Mi lay in the warm water, feeling as though none of it was quite real.

If not for the dull, lingering pain in her fingers, she would have thought she was dreaming.

No nightmare she had ever had matched the danger of today.

The smallest misjudgment, the smallest hesitation, and she would have been dead.

She did not know who had pushed her. She did not know when she had been drugged. She did not know who had been sent to kill her under the water.

She thought: when had she managed to earn this depth of hatred?

Was the drug meant to make her disgrace herself publicly in Lake Heart Pavilion? Or was there a man waiting to be staged as the one who destroyed her reputation? And had the push been meant to kill her outright, with the assassins in the water standing by in case she somehow survived?

Her head throbbed in waves.

If she had not encountered Xiao Huaiyan — what would have happened?

Would a servant woman have eventually found and rescued her? Or would she have met the people sent to end her life?

Cold sweat broke out across her skin at the thought.

Since swallowing the pill Xiao Huaiyan had given her, the unbearable burning and itching had been steadily receding.

Jiang Mi rose from the water, her expression complicated. She dried herself carefully.

When she looked down at her waist, there were two reddened marks on the skin. She closed her eyes, then reached for her inner garment.

Sitting on the edge of the bed putting on her silk stockings, the memory of that scorching touch on the sole of her foot surfaced — she could have gone back into the water to wash it away a second time.

She pressed her lips together and finished pulling on the stockings, then laced up the new embroidered slippers.

The door opened again. Xue Ninghua came in carrying a bowl of medicine, and at the sight of Jiang Mi fully dressed, her eyes brightened. She set the medicine on the table and said at once, "Miss, don't move yet — your hair is still wet. Let me press it dry with a cloth, or you'll have a headache later."

Jiang Mi accepted her kindness with gratitude and introduced herself. "How should I address you, Miss?"

Xue Ninghua thought of her brother's instructions and said, "I am Xue Ninghua, Fourth Miss of the main branch of the Zhenguo Duke's household. And your name, Miss?"

Jiang Mi opened her mouth, then hesitated. Given the situation she was in, she worried that saying her name might create trouble for someone else.

​Seeing her hesitation, Xue Ninghua smiled. "It was I who was walking by the lakeshore and rescued you, so you needn't worry."

Jiang Mi understood at once what she meant.

"Jiang Mi, of the Marquis of Cheng'en's household."

Xue Ninghua's eyes lit up with recognition. "So you're Miss Jiang — I've heard so much about you."

At Jiang Mi's puzzled look, Xue Ninghua explained, "The Empress Dowager's niece receiving the Emperor's gift of the zither Lüqi at the Emperor's birthday banquet — that story has spread to every corner of the capital's noble households."

As she wrung Jiang Mi's hair dry, she encouraged her to drink the warming medicine.

Jiang Mi cradled the bowl with both hands. "Thank you, Miss Xue."

Xue Ninghua smiled warmly. "Miss Jiang is too polite."

……

Rong'an Hall had grown very quiet. The only sounds that broke the silence were occasional muffled sobs.

The Grand Princess Zhaoyang's expression was grave and anxious. She looked up sharply as a serving woman came in. "Well? Have they found her?"

The woman dropped to her knees in apology. "Ships have been sent in all four directions to search. The serving women who know how to swim have been combing the water, and guards are searching all along the lakeshore. There is still no trace of Miss Jiang."

At those words, every expression in the room changed.

It had been over an hour. If she still hadn't been found, that could only mean one thing.

The Grand Princess's prayer beads were in danger of slipping from her grip entirely. She demanded in a voice strung tight with anxiety, "Where is the heir? Send someone to find the heir at once! What progress has he made?"

The Marchioness of Cheng'en had gone the color of ash. Her whole body was trembling.

Jiang Mi had left the house with her, perfectly safe. Now she had fallen into the water and there was not a word of her. And now they were being told she still couldn't be found.

Dark spots crowded the Marchioness's vision. Her knees buckled and she began to topple backward.

"Mother!"

"My Lady! My Lady!"

Jiang Yi and the maids caught the Marchioness before she could collapse to the floor.

The Grand Princess shot to her feet and called for the physician.

The physician who had been standing by in Rong'an Hall treated the Marchioness with acupuncture and brought her back to herself.

Jiang Rong, kneeling at the Marchioness's side, wept without stopping, murmuring over and over, "It's my fault. It's all my fault. I couldn't hold onto Elder Sister. I didn't hold on."

The Marchioness drew the child into her arms and looked up at the Grand Princess, her voice raw. "I must beg the Grand Princess to give us an explanation. I want to go immediately to the palace and inform the Empress Dowager!"

chapters.title: Thirty
chapters.content:
The Grand Princess tightened her grip on her prayer beads and let out a slow sigh. "Marchioness, I understand your urgency. Do you think I am not equally beside myself? Even if the Empress Dowager were to send someone now, they could not reach us in time. At this moment every member of the Zhenguo Duke's household is searching for Miss Jiang with everything we have. Whatever it takes, the Zhenguo Duke's household will give the Marquis of Cheng'en's household a full accounting — and I will go personally to Cining Palace to kneel before the Empress Dowager and confess our fault."

Several of the other ladies murmured in agreement. "That's right, Marchioness — at the time everything was in chaos. Several snakes appeared out of nowhere and terrified everyone. All of these ladies were frightened, but they haven't left — they are all waiting here for news of Miss Jiang. She was startled and lost her footing and fell into the water — none of us wanted this to happen, and we all trust that the Zhenguo Duke's household will investigate who could have been so wicked as to release snakes inside Lake Heart Pavilion. Such a deed was truly monstrous."

"Indeed — the Grand Princess has sent every available person out to search for Miss Jiang. Marchioness, please try to calm yourself for the moment and wait a little longer. Even if the Empress Dowager sends people now, it will take time for them to arrive."
