Wait? After more than an hour, how could anyone remain calm? The Marchioness felt each word scrape against something raw inside her. Though she had sent word to the household the moment the incident happened, she had no way of knowing whether the message had reached the palace yet.

Then the girl being held in the Marchioness's arms suddenly lifted her head and said, "That's not right. Third Elder Sister didn't fall in on her own. She was pushed."

Someone shook their head. "Poor child must have been badly frightened — she's saying things that don't make sense. Why would anyone push Miss Jiang? Even if she were pushed as you say — did you see who did it? There is already enough confusion here without adding more."

Jiang Rong clutched the Marchioness's hand, weeping as she said, "Great-aunt, you have to believe me. I didn't see who pushed her — but Third Elder Sister definitely didn't fall in by herself."

When it happened, Jiang Rong had been knocked down by the surging crowd. She had heard Third Elder Sister call out to her, and when she looked up from the ground, she saw the moment Third Elder Sister toppled into the lake.

The Marchioness heard Jiang Rong's tearful insistence and was sick with grief.

She could only blame herself for not staying close to them.

Jiang Yi's tears would not stop. She was consumed with regret for having run off to join the others, leaving Tangtang and Ah-Rong on their own.

The Jiang family's anguish weighed on the Grand Princess too. This had happened in her household, and the Zhenguo Duke's household could not escape responsibility.

She was about to say a few words of comfort when her personal attendant, Matron Zhang, came scrambling in, breathless and frantic.

The Grand Princess's heart sank. Had something been found?

Matron Zhang dropped to her knees. "Your Highness! Your Highness! His Majesty — His Majesty has arrived! The Third Young Master has accompanied the imperial party!"

The Grand Princess rose to her feet in disbelief.

Rong'an Hall, which had been deathly quiet, suddenly stirred beneath the surface.

Some of the young women who had been frightened into tears quickly dried their eyes. Some smoothed their clothing. Others sat up straight, a light of barely concealed delight entering their eyes.

Only the Jiang family continued to wear expressions of pure distress.

The Grand Princess stepped down from her seat and had just reached the doorway of Rong'an Hall when she saw him — the ruler of Great Wei, Xiao Huaiyan, dressed in a plain dark court robe. Behind him followed the Head Imperial Eunuch Li Fu, Embroidered Guard Commander Pei Chi, and the Third Young Master of the Zhenguo Duke's household, Xue Jingyan, who also held the position of Junior Magistrate of the Court of Judicial Review.

The Grand Princess offered her formal greeting.

Xiao Huaiyan stepped forward and raised her up with a gentle hand. "Honorable Aunt, please rise."

The Grand Princess asked, deeply flattered and flustered, "Your Majesty came to the Zhenguo Duke's household without having Li Fu give us advance notice so we might prepare a proper welcome?"

Xiao Huaiyan smiled. "We heard that Honorable Aunt has gathered quite a collection of rare plants and flowers here, including a green plum We have long wished to see. Since We happened to be free, We thought to come for a look. There was no need to make a great occasion of it."

Talking as they went, he walked with the Grand Princess into Rong'an Hall.

The ladies and young women inside dropped into their curtsies. "Long live Your Majesty."

"Rise, all of you."

Xiao Huaiyan took the seat of honor and swept a glance over the Jiang family, who were discreetly wiping tears. He turned to the Grand Princess, seated below him. "Why is everyone still gathered in Rong'an Hall? Has there been no flower viewing?"

The Grand Princess's expression grew strained, but she could not conceal it. "The Third Miss of the Jiang household lost her footing and fell into the lake. She has not yet been found."

The expression on the Emperor's face, which had been warm and pleasant, turned grave. "Honorable Aunt, she is the most beloved young woman of Our mother the Empress Dowager. Since the Zhenguo Duke's household's people have yet to find her, let Lord Pei take the Embroidered Guard to assist — whatever happens, she must come to no harm."

The Grand Princess and the others all understood, in their hearts, that after more than an hour with no trace of her, the outlook was not good. But the Emperor's suggestion could not be refused. She looked toward Pei Chi with a complicated expression. "Then I must trouble Lord Pei."

Pei Chi bowed and took his leave.

In every spot where he passed, the assembled young ladies shrank back a few steps, unnerved by the aura of menace that surrounded him.

Just as Pei Chi was about to step out of Rong'an Hall, a small, wavering voice spoke, thick with tears: "My lord... please, I beg you, you must find my Third Elder Sister."

Pei Chi stopped. He turned and looked back.

A little girl, her face drenched with tears, was being firmly covered at the mouth by the Marchioness of Cheng'en.

Pei Chi said nothing. He turned back and walked away.

Rong'an Hall settled once more into quiet.

But it was a surface quiet only. Quite a number of the young ladies were stealing glances at His Majesty on the seat of honor.

Those who had not been able to attend the Emperor's birthday banquet were seeing the new Emperor for the first time, and found that not only was he of exceptional appearance — this sovereign was also, unexpectedly, quite approachable.

Thinking of how their fathers praised this ruler, more than a few felt their hearts quicken.

Shen Yaowei sat with composed and elegant posture, taking a few discreet glances upward. The man in the seat above seemed to be tapping the table with his fingers, not really looking at anything in particular. But even those brief glances made her heart flutter. After her coming-of-age ceremony in the new year, he would fulfill his promise to the Shen family and make her Empress.

Thinking that she would soon be so close to him — that she would possess the world's finest man — warmth crept slowly into her face.

What pleased her most was that when the Emperor heard that Jiang Mi had fallen into the water, there had been no urgency in his expression. He had only sent Pei Chi to look because Jiang Mi was the Empress Dowager's niece. Perhaps his prior attention to Jiang Mi had merely been out of deference to the Empress Dowager.

Xie Mingshan was also watching. And the more she watched, the deeper the hatred in her heart became. She had already lost her chance to enter the palace. In the years ahead, countless women would stand at his side, and even catching another glimpse of him would become an unthinkable luxury.

……

Xiao Huaiyan was deeply bored. He thought to himself — how long could it take to simply change clothes?

He turned to the Grand Princess beside him and asked, "Why is Jinglin not here?"

The Grand Princess replied, "Jinglin took people out onto the jade lake to search, and there are also the poisonous creatures to be cleared away — we don't know yet whether there might be more venomous snakes hidden elsewhere in the grounds."

Xiao Huaiyan frowned with apparent concern. "Something like that? Releasing venomous creatures inside the Zhenguo Duke's household? Let Lord Pei look into that as well while he is here."

The Grand Princess's expression flickered; she seemed about to say something, then stopped. "Lord Pei is a busy man — I would not want to trouble him further."

"It is no trouble," Xiao Huaiyan said.

The Grand Princess thanked him and agreed.

He asked again, "How is Jinglin's health?"

The Grand Princess sighed. "Still recovering — though somewhat better than a while back."

Xiao Huaiyan nodded. "Jinglin should make a full recovery soon. We are waiting for him to enter government service and come help Us."

The Grand Princess was deeply moved. "Your Majesty's grace is something I will never forget as long as I live."

As the two of them spoke, a serving woman came tumbling into the hall, nearly falling over herself. "Your Highness! Your Highness! Miss Jiang — Miss Jiang has been found!"

Every woman in Rong'an Hall jolted to attention.

Found?

Did they mean they had found the body?

The serving woman was gasping for breath, her face a picture of urgency, the words not yet fully out of her mouth — and then two figures appeared in the doorway of Rong'an Hall.

Xue Ninghua steadied Jiang Mi as they stepped through together.

A clatter rang out — someone had knocked over their teacup.

Jiang Mi was dressed in the pale yellow brocade gown. Her face was drained of color and she looked terribly weak, but she was standing there, alive, in front of everyone.

The Marchioness cried out in overjoyed relief, "Tangtang!"

Jiang Rong released the Marchioness's hand and ran straight to Jiang Mi, throwing her arms around her and bursting into tears. "Elder Sister, Elder Sister — it's really you!"

Jiang Yi supported the Marchioness as they came forward, her eyes bright with tears. "Tangtang, you're all right. That's all that matters."

The Marchioness gathered Jiang Mi into her arms. She felt the warmth of her, solid and real, and the heart that had been suspended in terror finally came to rest.

Jiang Mi patted her great-aunt gently and murmured, "Great-aunt, I'm still here. I'm all right."

The Marchioness released her at last, repeating "good, good" several times in a row.

Jiang Mi looked up and, together with Xue Ninghua, offered her curtsy to the Emperor and the Grand Princess.

Xiao Huaiyan's lips held a slight curve. "Rise. Honorable Aunt has been anxious to ask."

The Grand Princess said, "Miss Jiang, what happened? How is it that you are with Ninghua?"

"Your Highness, it was the Fourth Miss Xue who saved me."

The Grand Princess turned to look at Xue Ninghua.

Xue Ninghua lowered her head. "Grandmother, I was out taking a walk by the jade lake and I saw Miss Jiang floating in the water. I had the serving women pull her out. By then she had already lost consciousness; I had to wait until she was changed into dry clothes and had taken some medicine before she came around. I am sorry for the delay in letting anyone know — please forgive me, Grandmother."

Those in Rong'an Hall who knew the Zhenguo Duke's household well recognized that this Fourth Miss must be from the main branch — the Grand Princess did not usually have much to do with the main branch, and the two sides of the family saw relatively little of each other even within the same residence.

Miss Jiang had some real fortune about her — she hadn't drowned, hadn't been fished out by one of the search party, but had been found by the Zhenguo Duke's Fourth Miss instead. Both her life and her reputation had been preserved.

Though looking at how pale and weak she appeared, she had evidently been through a great ordeal. For a young woman of a noble household to stay afloat in the water long enough to be found was no small thing.

The Grand Princess paused before speaking. "Ninghua, today we are very fortunate that you were there to save Miss Jiang. However, from the moment Miss Jiang fell into the water, there have been so many people searching — you should have sent someone to let us know at once."

Xue Ninghua looked suitably ashamed. "You are right, Grandmother. I did not think it through. I am sorry."

Jiang Mi stepped forward and raised her head toward the man in the seat of honor. Their eyes met for only an instant before she dropped her gaze.

"This subject daughter has something to report privately to His Majesty and the Grand Princess."

Xiao Huaiyan shifted his posture slightly. "What is it? Miss Jiang may speak freely."

Jiang Mi pressed her lips together for a moment.

"This subject daughter did not lose her footing and fall. I was pushed."

chapters.title: Thirty-one
chapters.content:
The moment Jiang Mi's words landed, a murmur of whispers rippled through Rong'an Hall.

"Could Miss Jiang be mistaken? Everything was in such chaos. All the young ladies present come from good families — who among them would deliberately push someone?"

"Quite right — everyone was scrambling away from the snakes at the time. Perhaps it was simply a collision? 'Deliberately' is rather a strong word."

The Marchioness of Cheng'en's expression went stony, her brow drawn tight. The two ladies speaking were the sort who fancied themselves peacemakers in any dispute. In ordinary matters that was one thing, but Jiang Mi had been pushed into water — and if not for her exceptional fortune, this would have been murder.

The Marchioness said in clipped, controlled tones, "Human hearts are difficult to read. Who is to say no one present harbored malicious intent? And since these ladies didn't witness it themselves, how can they be so certain my niece was not pushed deliberately?"

A sharp-faced woman in the corner said, "Without evidence, if Miss Jiang were to simply name someone at random, would that not be a terrible injustice to an innocent person?"

Not far off, a round-faced military officer's wife gave a short laugh. "Listen to that. Who would risk their own life to wrongfully accuse someone? Unless, of course, someone has a guilty conscience."

The sharp-faced woman bristled. "Lady Liu, how dare you make such an insinuation!"

Just as the argument threatened to erupt, the Grand Princess Zhaoyang struck the table with a resounding slap. "Enough! Have you no regard for His Majesty or for this Princess?"

The quarreling ladies dropped at once into hurried curtsies of apology.

The Grand Princess frowned and waved them off. She turned to the Emperor. "Your Majesty — what do you think should be done?"

Xiao Huaiyan said, "Since Miss Jiang mentioned she wished to speak privately, it seems she has more to say. Except for Lord Xue Jingyan, have everyone else withdraw."

The Grand Princess glanced over at Xue Jingyan. He now held the position of Junior Magistrate of the Court of Judicial Review — it was entirely appropriate for him to remain.

Before long, Rong'an Hall held only Xiao Huaiyan, the Grand Princess, Xue Jingyan, and Jiang Mi.

Xiao Huaiyan's gaze settled on Jiang Mi. "You may speak now."

Jiang Mi turned the moment over in her mind. She had been caught completely off guard, and the push had been forceful — the contact had lasted only an instant, but one impression had stayed with her clearly.

She raised her head. "This subject daughter did not see the face of the person who pushed me. But the person who pushed me was wearing rings."

The Grand Princess looked puzzled. "And how would you know that?"

Jiang Mi paused, recalling the sensation against her back. A trace of discomfort crossed her face. She lowered her eyes and answered quietly, "Your Highness, this subject daughter is... rather sensitive to touch. The push was forceful, and at that proximity I was able to tell whether the person was wearing rings."

"If — if my guess is correct — the person was wearing rings on the index and little finger of their left hand. I do not know whether this clue is enough to find who pushed me."

By the time she finished, embarrassment had crept into her cheeks.

From the man in the seat above came a low, quiet laugh. Jiang Mi felt her face grow warmer still and did not dare raise her eyes.

Xiao Huaiyan set down his teacup. "Lord Xue, this matter is in your hands."

Xue Jingyan bowed. "This official receives the order."

After Xue Jingyan departed, the Grand Princess said, "Miss Jiang, your constitution is still weakened — please, sit."

Jiang Mi curtsied. "Thank you, Your Highness."

She found a chair and sat with composed propriety, her gaze fixed on the floor, not once looking toward the man at the head of the room.

Rong'an Hall fell into quiet once more.

The Grand Princess was privately ill at ease. Whoever Xue Jingyan turned up — given that all the young ladies who had attended the flower banquet came from distinguished families — the charge of attempting to harm the Empress Dowager's niece, even though it had not succeeded, would bring ruin upon an entire household. It was a thorny situation any way she looked at it.

What had been meant to be such a pleasant flower banquet — and now this.

Xiao Huaiyan, meanwhile, observed Jiang Mi at his leisure. She sat perfectly upright with her embroidered slippers tucked beneath the hem of her skirt, eyes downcast, her handkerchief twisting and turning in her fingers. The pale jade eardrops at her ears swayed faintly.

She seemed to have been avoiding him from the moment she walked in.

Xiao Huaiyan closed his eyes. In the darkness, a luminous, pale radiance and a vivid, intoxicating beauty surfaced in his mind's eye. At the same time that soft, breathy voice echoed in his ears: "This subject daughter is... rather sensitive to touch."

That quivering, unsteady form — was it sensitivity, or something else entirely?

Xiao Huaiyan opened his eyes, his gaze shadowed. He said to Li Fu, "Go pour Miss Jiang a cup of hot tea. If the Empress Dowager learns what Miss Jiang endured today, she will be beside herself with worry."

The words jolted the Grand Princess into alertness. "It is I who have been remiss in my hospitality — Miss Jiang suffered under this roof, and once the truth is fully established, I will personally go to the palace and beg the Empress Dowager's pardon."

She called in a maid, who replaced Jiang Mi's tea with fresh hot tea and brought over some pastries.

Jiang Mi had no appetite whatsoever; she only wanted to know whether Lord Xue had found the culprit yet. But she had no choice but to rise and offer her thanks.

In doing so her eyes lifted — and collided directly with Xiao Huaiyan's dark, fathomless phoenix eyes. Flustered and annoyed, Jiang Mi quickly looked away. And that pair of phoenix eyes broke into warmth.

Not long after, Xue Jingyan returned and bowed. He went straight to the point. "Your Majesty, Grandmother. Following Miss Jiang's description, of the ladies present in the pavilion, several wore rings. Three wore rings on the left hand. Only one wore rings on both the index and little finger. That is Miss Xie Mingshan of the Duke of Xie's household."

The Grand Princess was visibly shaken. "How could it be her?"

Xie Mingshan had always been known for her poise and refinement, and had the reputation of being the most accomplished young woman in the capital. She had even been considering Xie Mingshan as a possible candidate for Jinglin's future wife — how had she come to do something so vicious and so foolish?

Jiang Mi was not entirely surprised, only that she had not expected Xie Mingshan to act with her own hand. The Xie family bore her ill will — but had that resentment truly festered to the point of wanting her dead?

Xue Jingyan continued. "This official first told the assembled ladies and young women that Miss Jiang, in the moment of falling, had torn away a piece of fabric from the person who pushed her. Upon hearing this, several individuals inconspicuously moved to check their own sleeves and hems. I then announced that maids and serving women would need to compare fabric samples with each person, and asked them to go to separate rooms. Once the ring-wearing individual was identified, her personal maids were detained and questioned one by one, and one of them broke under interrogation. According to her testimony, Miss Xie Mingshan acted on a sudden impulse."

The Grand Princess was furious and deeply disappointed. That the daughter of the illustrious Duke of Xie's household should be capable of such wickedness — the reputation of every young woman in that family line was now destroyed. No noble household would enter into marriage with a family that raised daughters like this.

She looked to the Emperor. "Your Majesty, how do you see fit to handle this?"

Xiao Huaiyan considered briefly. "Let the young Miss Xie accompany Lord Xue to the Court of Judicial Review first, and then have the Duke of Xie come and retrieve her."

The Grand Princess was somewhat taken aback. Would His Majesty truly be so unsparing toward the Duke of Xie's household? A daughter of that family walking through the doors of the Court of Judicial Review — how would their women ever show their faces in public again?

The Duke of Xie was the maternal family of the late Emperor's Virtuous Consort. Had His Majesty not always treated the Virtuous Consort with particular respect?

As for Xie Mingshan herself — once she was brought home, there would be no easy life awaiting her there.

The Grand Princess turned to Jiang Mi. "Does Miss Jiang have any objection?"

Jiang Mi shook her head. "I defer entirely to His Majesty and to the Grand Princess."

There were still doubts pressing on her mind — who had put the drug in her drink, who had been sent to drown her — none of which she could raise here and now.

To have the person who pushed her found at all was already the best she could have hoped for.

Xiao Huaiyan rose. "The hour grows late — We should return to the palace. Lord Pei Chi will assist Honorable Aunt with the remaining matters. Honorable Aunt need not worry."

Both the Grand Princess and Jiang Mi rose. "We respectfully see off Your Majesty."

……

The flower banquet's guests could not wait to be gone, all of them desperate to extract themselves from any further entanglement.

The banquet had brought snakes, near-death, and now scandal — it was an afternoon no one would easily forget.

Jiang Mi and the Marchioness's party were just about to board their carriage when an imperial procession appeared ahead of them — palace attendants and court ladies escorting a carriage, which drew up and halted before them.

Matron Cui stepped down from the carriage and greeted Jiang Mi and the Marchioness with a bow. "My Lady, the Empress Dowager has sent this servant to escort the Third Miss to the palace."

Jiang Mi was mildly surprised. Her aunt wanted her in the palace now? She thought of what she had not been able to say inside the Zhenguo Duke's household, and decided that going to see her aunt was the right thing to do.

She released Jiang Rong's hand, said a few reassuring words to her, and then followed Matron Cui into the carriage.

……

In the inner chamber of Rong'an Hall, the Grand Princess Zhaoyang lay exhausted against the daybed cushions, her head aching with a deep, persistent pain that the medicine had not touched.

The attendant at her side saw someone come in and quickly woke the Grand Princess. "Your Highness, the heir is here."

The Grand Princess opened her eyes and slowly sat up. Xue Jinglin came forward to support her arm. "Grandmother, slowly."

The Grand Princess gripped his arm. "What did you find? Anything? Has Pei Chi left?"

Xue Jinglin said, "Grandmother, please set your mind at ease. All the venomous creatures have been located and removed. Besides the snakes, a number of scorpions concealed among the plants and flowers were found as well. Pei Chi has already departed."

"Where did all those creatures come from? Who dared to cause such havoc in my Zhenguo Duke's household!"

Xue Jinglin said, "Grandmother, calm yourself. The creatures were introduced by a purchasing steward who harbored grievances over the distribution of bonuses. He hid the venomous animals among the garden flora to deliberately ruin the flower banquet. I have dealt with him and his accomplices."

The Grand Princess found it difficult to believe. "All of this over bonus money? There was no one else pulling strings?"

Xue Jinglin nodded. "Grandmother, don't read too much into it. Today's events were a convergence of several unfortunate coincidences. That is all."

The Grand Princess was silent for a time. "Jinglin, when will your illness be well? His Majesty asked after you today."

Xue Jinglin gave a helpless smile. "Grandmother, it is more than kind of His Majesty to—"

The Grand Princess cut him off. "Xue Jingyan is already Junior Magistrate of the Court of Judicial Review. Jinglin, it is time for you to be well!"

He had barely opened his mouth to respond when a rush of iron-tasting heat surged up his throat. He pressed a handkerchief quickly to his lips and began to cough. The white cloth was soon blotted through with red.

The Grand Princess was alarmed. She made him sit down at once, blaming herself. "It's my fault for pressing you — take your medicine, quickly!"

After a bout of labored breathing, Xue Jinglin gradually steadied. He wiped the blood from the corner of his mouth, withdrew a small jade vial from inside his robe, tipped out several black pills, and swallowed them in one go.

The Grand Princess looked at his ashen face with wretched sorrow. She sighed. "I was too impatient."

Xue Jinglin smiled faintly. "Don't blame yourself, Grandmother. It's all my fault."

The Grand Princess sighed again.

Xue Jinglin asked, "Where is Ningzhu? I haven't seen her."

"That child has been weeping and carrying on ever since she heard the Jiang girl fell in the water. We had the servants give her a calming draught and she's sleeping it off."

Xue Jinglin said, "She does seem very fond of that Miss Jiang."

He paused. "Grandmother, all of Ningzhu's maids should be replaced. Today she slipped away from them and took Miss Jiang alone to see the white tiger in the side courtyard. The shock alone should have taught her a lesson. Besides, she's growing older now — she needs someone to teach her proper conduct."

The Grand Princess hesitated. "But Ningzhu's temperament has always been a little behind other children her age..."

"Which is precisely why she needs to be guided more carefully — if nothing else, to give her the outward form, so that she does not stand out as unusual in public."

The Grand Princess nodded in agreement.

Then, unable to help herself, she asked, "Jinglin — at today's banquet, was there any young woman who caught your eye?"

Xue Jinglin was quiet for a long moment. Then he looked up. "I thought the Jiang family's young miss seemed rather gentle in temperament."

"The Jiang family's miss?" The Grand Princess's tone changed entirely. "No — that won't do. The Zhenguo Duke's household is too modest a setting. The Jiang family would never look our way."

Xue Jinglin only smiled.

chapters.title: Thirty-two
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi had not expected to find herself back inside the palace so soon.

She took Matron Cui's hand and stepped down from the carriage.

Cining Palace was just beginning to light its lanterns for the evening, a warm glow spreading through the dusk. Qingxue was already waiting at the gate.

The moment she saw Jiang Mi coming, she hurried forward to meet her.

"Third Miss, thank heaven you are safe. When the Empress Dowager heard you had fallen into the water, she was beside herself with worry — her old ailment flared up again."

Jiang Mi was alarmed at once. "Take me to her quickly."

She stepped into the inner chamber to find the Empress Dowager Jiang seated on a yellow pear-wood chair, eyes closed, her fingers moving continuously over the prayer beads in her hands.

Jiang Mi's nose stung. She called out softly, "Aunt."

The Empress Dowager opened her eyes. She looked at Jiang Mi for a long, still moment, then rose and drew her tightly into her arms. "Good child. As long as you are safe."

She took Jiang Mi's hands. "Why are your hands so cold? Is this outfit not warm enough?"

Jiang Mi shook her head. "Aunt, I'm not cold. I must have been out in the night air for a little while — I'll warm up soon."

The Empress Dowager still had Qingxue go and fetch a cape for Jiang Mi to drape around her shoulders.

Jiang Mi accepted the kindness and said, "Aunt, Qingxue told me your old ailment returned. Is the pain in your chest still troubling you?"

"I'm fine — it's an old complaint. Now, Tangtang, tell me what happened. First I received word that you had fallen into the water — I immediately sent someone to find the Emperor, but he was not in the palace, so I had Matron Cui and the others go to the Zhenguo Duke's household. Then a second message arrived saying you had been rescued, and that the person who pushed you was Xie Mingshan of the Duke of Xie's household." The Empress Dowager spoke as she guided Jiang Mi to sit beside her.

Jiang Mi surmised that someone had sent word to the palace ahead of her on the road over.

She gave a full account of how she had ended up in the water at the flower banquet. Watching her aunt's expression grow progressively darker, she hesitated over whether to tell her about the person who had grabbed her legs underwater.

The Empress Dowager noticed her pause. "Tangtang, if there is more to say, say it. You needn't keep anything from your aunt."

Jiang Mi steadied herself. "Aunt, there is something I did not disclose at the Zhenguo Duke's household. When I first fell into the water, a servant woman swam toward me — I assumed she was coming to help, but she seized my leg and began dragging me downward, intent on drowning me. While she was doing so, I caught a vague glimpse of a man's figure also swimming in my direction. Fortunately I managed to kick her off and escape."

The Empress Dowager's expression hardened, a sharp glint entering her eyes. She clenched her fist and struck the table as she stood. "One scheme layered upon another — truly vicious!"

Alarmed by the shift in her aunt's color and fearing a return of the heart pain, Jiang Mi took hold of her hand. "Aunt, please don't work yourself up. Think of your health."

The Empress Dowager turned, reached out, and gently touched Jiang Mi's brow and cheek. She said with conviction, "Tangtang, don't be afraid. Your aunt will see justice done for you."

Jiang Mi's heart finally settled a little.

Even so, she said nothing about the drug. She was afraid that if her aunt pressed her further, she would let slip something that brought Xiao Huaiyan into it.

If her aunt learned that Xiao Huaiyan had been the one to rescue her — that he had seen her in that state, that he had touched her — the Empress Dowager would march straight to Xiao Huaiyan and demand a title for Jiang Mi before the night was out.

The mere thought of it made her press her lips together and say not a word.

The Empress Dowager looked at Jiang Mi's pale face with tender compassion. "Tangtang, you've been through enough for one day. Let Qingxue take you to the warm side chamber to rest. We'll talk more tomorrow."

Jiang Mi nodded and followed Qingxue out.

The Empress Dowager remained seated. She set the prayer beads on the table and let out a long sigh.

Matron Cui stepped forward with a bowl of herbal medicine. "Your Ladyship, what are you sighing about? Our Third Miss has great fortune — she escaped harm and came through safely. And who knew she could swim? Goodness knows when she learned."

The Empress Dowager did not seem to hear her. Her thoughts were still turning over what Tangtang had said.

The web of marriage alliances between the great families was a labyrinth — the Zhenguo Duke's household had no reason to wade into this particular mire, and could not possibly have arranged for a death inside their own flower banquet.

So whose hand had reached out today?

……

White mist curled and the sound of running water filled the air. Warm water poured from the mouths of golden auspicious beasts cast at the four corners of the bath, gathering in the pool below.

Xiao Huaiyan leaned against the white jade edge of the pool with his eyes closed, letting his mind drift.

In that hazy space between waking and sleep, he seemed to hear the bells again. He found himself standing behind a woman — her skin white as snow, a slender neck, and at her waist a fine-wrapped knot of ribbons. One easy pull and the small bodice would fall away.

He let go, and she thrashed in a panic at the water's surface. He reached out and caught her back. "What are you afraid of? With Us here, you won't choke."

He steadied her waist and corrected the position of her arms, then let go and let her try again.

At first she was frightened and kept clutching him, unwilling to let go. Gradually she found her ease in the water, and that expanse of pale, smooth skin rippled dazzlingly in the light.

Like a cunning little fish. The gold and jade ankle bells swayed lightly with each kick.

Xiao Huaiyan reached out and pulled her toward him. He tugged the ribbon draped at her white neck loose.

Before long, a small bodice embroidered with peonies in gold thread was floating on the surface of the rippling water.

When he caught her chin and moved to turn her face toward him, the water surged up and splashed into his eyes.

By the time Xiao Huaiyan opened them again, the bells were gone. The woman in his arms had vanished.

The bath was empty. Only him.

Xiao Huaiyan pressed his fingers against his temple. He had done it again — he had been dreaming.

There still seemed to be warmth lingering on his hands. That slender waist felt somehow familiar.

Xiao Huaiyan rose from the bath. The waiting attendants brought his robes and helped him dress.

Still carrying the warmth of the water, he stepped out — and Li Fu came forward at once, presenting tea with solicitous attention. "Your Majesty, this servant hears that Miss Jiang at Cining Palace seems to have come down with a fever."

Xiao Huaiyan gave him a sidelong glance. "And We are a physician?"

Li Fu fell silent.

He couldn't understand it. His Majesty had been in a fine mood just moments ago. Why had it soured again after the bath?

Xiao Huaiyan drew out a memorial, looked at it briefly, then set it aside.

He leaned over the desk and took up a brush, writing without raising his head. "Send Gu Hairong over."

Li Fu blinked before he caught up. He quickly acknowledged the order — though privately he thought that with imperial physicians assigned to night duty, pulling the Head Physician Gu out of bed at this hour was going to be quite the task.

……

Jiang Mi had come to the palace in a rush and had not brought Qiuyu with her, so she was attended in the side chamber by the Empress Dowager's assigned palace maids, Cainan and Caidong.

She bathed again and then went to bed.

But she had not been asleep long before the fever came on.

Cainan discovered it when she came in to replenish the water.

Badly frightened, the two maids went running to find Matron Cui.

Matron Cui brought a physician, who prescribed medicine and had it prepared. Remarkably, Head Physician Gu also arrived shortly after.

Gu examined Jiang Mi again, made adjustments to the earlier prescription, and sent the palace maids to brew the revised medicine.

"Nothing serious. Fright, prolonged exposure to cold water, and sheer physical exhaustion combined to produce the high fever. Once she takes the medicine and breaks a sweat, she will recover."

Matron Cui thanked him profusely and saw him to the palace gate herself.

Jiang Mi could not feel how hot she was from the inside — only that even buried beneath two layers of bedding, she was freezing cold.

She was both chilled and aching in every joint. Cainan helped her sit up, and she drank the medicine.

Jiang Mi looked at Matron Cui and Cainan, still keeping watch at her bedside. "Matron Cui, all of you should go and rest. I'll be fine — once I break a sweat it'll pass."

Matron Cui was reluctant to leave at first, but after Jiang Mi pressed a second time, she agreed.

Jiang Mi huddled feverishly inside the bedclothes. At first it felt as though the cold had seeped into her very bones; she curled tightly around herself.

She felt light, as if she might float away.

Her thoughts were a jumble — her past life's memories, memories of childhood, everything that had happened in this life flickering through her mind in no order, like lantern slides spinning fast.

Jiang Mi began to lose track of when she was.

In a muddled haze, she opened her eyes.

In the dim lamplight, someone was standing at her bedside.

Her gaze met a pair of long, narrow phoenix eyes.

Jiang Mi stared blankly, her mind still not quite clear.

And then, for no reason she could name, a wave of grievance welled up inside her. Her eyes filled with tears. Her mouth crumpled. She cried out to the figure before her — a small, childlike sound: "It hurts..."

Xiao Huaiyan did not move from where he stood, his eyes dark as ink, watching in still silence as the person before him became, all at once, unexpectedly young.

One person weeping quietly in the dark.

One person watching in silence.

After a long pause, Xiao Huaiyan spoke. "Where does it hurt?"

Jiang Mi gave a soft, hiccupping sob.

Xiao Huaiyan, seeing her muddled by fever, reached out to feel her forehead.

Slap. The hand had not even touched her brow before Jiang Mi batted it away.

She pulled the covers up and burrowed back, glaring at him with puffed-up displeasure. "Not allowed."

Having declared this, she sank into the bedding, cried out, and was asleep again before the tears were dry.

……

The following morning, as soon as the Emperor came down from court, Pei Chi was there with a report.

In the imperial study, Pei Chi said, "Your Majesty, this official has found nothing unusual within the Zhenguo Duke's compound. However, a white tiger was discovered dead in the side courtyard — not long dead. The heir explained that the tiger's ferocity had frightened his sister and a guest, so he had it put down."

"As for Miss Jiang's fall into the water, there are still a few points that require clarification."

After hearing Pei Chi's full report, Xiao Huaiyan gave a few brief instructions and then brought Pei Chi along to Cining Palace.

The Empress Dowager was delighted to see Xiao Huaiyan come.

He sat and said in an even tone, "We know the Empress Dowager will want to hear what happened at the Zhenguo Duke's household yesterday. We had Lord Pei Chi look into it — let him give you the account directly."

For the Empress Dowager, this was exactly what she had been waiting for.

"Lord Pei, what has been discovered?"

Pei Chi bowed to the Empress Dowager. "Your Ladyship, the Zhenguo Duke's heir has dealt with the purchasing steward and his co-conspirators. Inside the compound, two servant women and one maid also died unexpectedly — all three drowned. The official explanation is that their legs cramped while searching the water for Miss Jiang, and they were unable to stay afloat. The coroner confirmed death by drowning."

The Empress Dowager's voice sharpened. "Then that severs the trail of evidence entirely?"

Xiao Huaiyan asked, "What trail of evidence?"

The Empress Dowager did not conceal it. "Tangtang told me that after she fell into the water, a servant woman swam toward her and tried to drag her by the legs to drown her."

Xiao Huaiyan expressed surprise. "Is that so?"

"It is true — and We ask Your Majesty to investigate thoroughly."

Pei Chi paused, then continued. "Your Ladyship, the trail has not gone entirely cold. One of the drowned servant women — before the incident, her son had run up gambling debts and was about to have his hand cut off by creditors. Within just a few days, the debt was repaid in full. This official traced it back: the woman had come into an unexpected sum of money. That money was given to her by a member of the Duke of Xie's household — specifically, one of the ladies-in-waiting brought along from the Xie family."

"The Duke of Xie's household?" The Empress Dowager's brow furrowed deeply.

Xie Mingshan had already pushed Tangtang into the water — was it so hard to believe she had also arranged someone to wait below?

chapters.title: Thirty-three
chapters.content:
Yet the Empress Dowager could not stop thinking about what Jiang Mi had told her — that when she was in the water, she had seen the silhouette of a man swimming toward her as well.

When a noble young woman fell into water, it was customary for servant women who knew how to swim to go in and retrieve her — men were generally kept away. Even if circumstances demanded that a guard dive in to save her, carrying an unmarried young woman out of the water in his arms would compromise her reputation, even if it could not be said — as in the old tales — that whoever saved her must marry her.

So if they had already arranged for a servant woman to drown her below the surface, why also send a man swimming toward her? Was it not contradictory to want her dead and ruined at the same time?

After careful deliberation, the Empress Dowager said, "Your Majesty, the Duke of Xie's household bribed a servant woman inside the Zhenguo Duke's compound. With that woman now dead, evidence has vanished. But beyond that, Tangtang told me that while she was underwater, she also caught a vague glimpse of a man's figure swimming toward her."

Something flickered in the depths of Xiao Huaiyan's eyes. He asked, "Why did she not say so at the time?"

The Empress Dowager sighed. "Tangtang is cautious by nature — she feared that saying so would put the other party on guard. And she was so badly frightened by it all, barely in her right mind by the time she saw me, that only then did she remember these details."

Xiao Huaiyan found himself thinking of the moment he had fished her out — that pitiful, trembling wretch of a creature, and the way she had bitten down on his arm in a blind panic. She had indeed looked very much like a frightened small animal.

The memory brought a faint sensation to his fingertips. He absently stroked them together.

"Going by what the Empress Dowager has described," he said, "there are indeed multiple suspicious points. Mother need not worry — We will have Pei Chi pursue this until it is fully resolved."

Only then did the Empress Dowager give a satisfied nod. But her thoughts turned back to Jiang Mi, still recovering in the side chamber, and the worry returned to her face.

"Tangtang suffered terribly this time. She ran a high fever last night — it broke, thankfully, but her constitution is still weak. I don't know how long she'll need to recover."

Xiao Huaiyan seemed to consider this. He said graciously, "If We recall correctly, at the Empress Dowager's birthday celebration, We drank a little too much and withdrew to the Moon-Viewing Pavilion — it was Miss Jiang who brought Us a sobering draught. Now that she is unwell, We should go and look in on her."

A smile finally appeared on the Empress Dowager's face. "If Your Majesty is willing to visit her, that is her great good fortune."

She had no reason whatsoever to stand in the way of this. She immediately told Qingxue with barely concealed delight to lead His Majesty to the warm side chamber where Jiang Mi was staying.

Stepping out of the inner hall, Qingxue saw that His Majesty was still speaking quietly with Lord Pei Chi. She maintained a respectful distance and waited at the far end of the walkway, careful not to hear a word she wasn't meant to.

"That one tumble into the water drew out quite a few hands at once. The Xie girl's push muddied the water nicely for everyone else — the drowned servant women, the man swimming below, the drug in the wine... truly a chain of linked schemes."

Xiao Huaiyan gave a quiet laugh. "Who would have thought she'd managed to make so many enemies."

Pei Chi, for his part, had felt his heart in his throat over this Jiang girl's predicament. One wrong step and she would have been dead or ruined. That she had crossed paths with the Emperor today — her luck was, in fact, extraordinary.

Pei Chi said, "When this official arrived at Lake Heart Pavilion, the banquet tables had not yet been cleared. Miss Jiang's wine cup and tea bowl were both examined by Head Physician Gu. The drug found in the wine cup is known as Spring Slumber — a stimulant compound. There are only two places in the capital where it can be acquired. This official has already sent people to make inquiries, and expects word shortly."

Xiao Huaiyan nodded. "Have men posted to watch everyone who entered the water to search that day. Whoever incited this moved too hastily. Short of silencing them the way they silenced that servant woman, they will leave traces eventually."

Pei Chi acknowledged the order and withdrew.

Qingxue saw the lord depart and stepped forward again to guide His Majesty.

……

"Matron Cui, I really have no appetite."

Jiang Mi was half-reclined against the daybed cushions, a book propped open in her hand.

Matron Cui stood nearby with a bowl of soft, sweetly fragrant rice congee, gently coaxing her. "Head Physician Gu said she must eat a little no matter what. This congee was made with imperial-grade emerald rice from the small kitchen — simmered on low heat all night. Please, just a few spoonfuls."

Seeing Matron Cui's persistence, Jiang Mi let Cainan help her sit up, managed two mouthfuls, and then set it aside.

When she reached for the book again, Matron Cui's hand darted in and swept it away before she could touch it.

"Miss, when you're sick you're supposed to rest. You've had that book open half the day — it strains the eyes and the mind both. Lie down and wait until you're well."

Jiang Mi let out a small sigh.

Since the illness, everyone from her aunt down to the maids had been treating her as though she were made of spun glass — barely stopping short of setting her on a shrine.

She had no choice but to close her eyes and rest.

The fever last night had brought strange, turbulent dreams all the way through. By the time it broke and the sweat came out, those incoherent visions had dissolved and she could no longer remember them.

Matron Cui was just about to help her lie back when, out of the corner of her eye, she caught the flash of an imperial yellow robe walking in.

She started, then immediately bowed low. "Long live Your Majesty."

Jiang Mi's thick lashes fluttered. She kept her eyes shut.

She heard footsteps approaching. Beside her, a warm, unhurried voice said, "Asleep? It seems We have come at a poor time."

Jiang Mi's heart was beating fast. She fixed all her hopes on pretending to be asleep until he went away.

Then Matron Cui said from the side, "Your Majesty, the young miss is only resting lightly — she should... she should wake very soon." She spoke the words while simultaneously giving Jiang Mi a discreet nudge.

Knowing she couldn't keep the pretense up, Jiang Mi slowly allowed herself to stir as if waking.

She saw the man standing at the bedside and moved to rise, but Xiao Huaiyan raised a hand to stop her.

"You are unwell. No need for ceremony."

His gaze swept calmly over the kneeling maids and attendants filling the room. "All of you, rise."

Xiao Huaiyan settled himself into the chair beside the daybed. Matron Cui hastily had tea brought.

The room fell into a hush.

Matron Cui led the palace maids out.

Xiao Huaiyan took in her appearance — a plain half-robe, her long dark hair uncoiled and spilling from one shoulder, exposing a length of white neck. A pale face. A pointed chin. Delicate and quietly lovely.

It brought to mind how last night she had stared up at him with red-rimmed eyes and cried out that she was in pain.

"We heard Miss Jiang ran a high fever last night. Are you feeling better?"

Jiang Mi glanced up slightly — and caught the dark, unfathomable gleam of his eyes. She quickly dropped her gaze back to the embroidered pattern on the coverlet. "This subject daughter thanks Your Majesty for his concern. I am much improved."

Xiao Huaiyan noticed that her fingers were moving absently over the brocade. On one of those slender white fingers, the place where she had bitten herself was beginning to scab — a dark brownish mark against pale skin, rather unsightly.

"Miss Jiang has no need to be tense. We came because We heard from the Empress Dowager that you were unwell, and wished to check on you. There are some leads in what happened to Miss Jiang at the Zhenguo Duke's household — though We also have a question We'd like to ask."

Jiang Mi looked up. "What does Your Majesty wish to ask?"

Xiao Huaiyan fixed those dewy, uncertain eyes with a steady gaze. He smiled. "When did Miss Jiang learn to swim?"

Young women of good family could study the Four Books and Five Classics, could master zither, chess, calligraphy, and painting — but swimming was rare.

He thought of that absurd dream, and could not have explained why it had brought him here, to ask her this.

Jiang Mi felt a jolt of anxiety.

Why was he asking this?

Her thoughts scattered into confusion. She couldn't swim — not in this life. In her past life, the Consort Liu had dragged her along into a pool with her, and though she had been pulled out quickly, Xiao Huaiyan had laughed at her. That very night he had held her and dropped her into the bath, teaching her — if it could be called teaching — though she suspected the process amused him far more than it instructed her.

The memory of learning to swim in that past life made her complexion go even paler. She said faintly, "When I was young and playing carelessly, I once fell into water by accident. After that, a servant woman taught me how to stay afloat."

Xiao Huaiyan nodded without visible reaction. "Then Miss Jiang is quite fortunate. Though you do seem to have a particular quarrel with water — best to avoid it going forward."

Her answer was entirely plausible, yet for some reason Xiao Huaiyan felt a vague, formless irritation. He turned the jade thumb ring slowly, then rose from the chair. His gaze drifted to the pot of Emei spring orchid on the window ledge.

Lush green leaves, full of life. Someone had clearly tended it with care.

"A well-kept orchid."

Jiang Mi followed his eyes to the pot. It was the half-dead plant he had once given her as a gift.

Xiao Huaiyan stood at the window and regarded the orchid for a long while before he spoke again.

"Does Miss Jiang have nothing to say to Us?"

Every muscle in Jiang Mi's body pulled taut. Did she need to say something?

Her mind went completely blank.

What did Xiao Huaiyan want?

The room was so quiet it was unnerving.

Jiang Mi made a few attempts to speak. Her throat was too dry to form the words.

Xiao Huaiyan turned and looked at her sitting there, visibly at a loss, and gave a short laugh. "We rescued a drowned cat. That cat not only showed no gratitude — she turned around and bit Us. Even a cat understands gratitude. Surely Miss Jiang does not fall short of a cat?"

Jiang Mi went still.

He had saved her. Under the lightless, smothering cloak, he had gripped her bare feet. On the bed, he had pinned her wrists and pressed his thumb against her lips. Those liberties he had taken — every one of them — were things she had spent all her energy trying to erase from her mind.

However much she might wish to flee from it all, the fact that he had saved her was something she could not undo.

Jiang Mi pushed back the coverlet, stepped onto the carpet in just her silk stockings, and dropped into a deep bow toward Xiao Huaiyan. "This subject daughter is grateful to Your Majesty for saving her life. This subject daughter is full of gratitude and reverence — upon returning home, she will erect a tablet in Your Majesty's honor and make offerings to it every day."

From the moment she threw back the covers, a faint, delicate scent drifted up. Xiao Huaiyan's gaze fell on her slender figure — that waist, just barely enough to circle with one hand, impossibly close to the waist of the woman in his dreams.

He gave a quiet snort. "We have not died yet. Miss Jiang need not make offerings quite so devotedly."

Jiang Mi bit her lip, flustered. "This subject daughter spoke carelessly — please pardon her, Your Majesty."

She was silently trying to work out his intentions. A debt of life — how was she supposed to repay it?

He was the master of all under heaven. Gold and silk meant nothing to him.

But she could not bring herself to say that she would do anything he asked in repayment. She simply could not.

And perhaps his old suspicious nature had reasserted itself — was he testing her? Did he think she was using this as an excuse to attach herself to him and push her way into his inner palace?

Xiao Huaiyan looked at that wan, stricken face for a long moment. Something in him went flat. He gave a brief, quiet laugh. "Forget it."

And he turned and walked away.

After Xiao Huaiyan left, Jiang Mi practically collapsed against the daybed.

Matron Cui came back in wearing an expression of restrained excitement, only to find the young miss sitting on the floor, and rushed over to help her up. "Miss, what's happened to you?"

She pressed a hand to Jiang Mi's forehead and immediately exclaimed, "Goodness, how have you broken out in a cold sweat again?"

Jiang Mi leaned against Matron Cui in silence, her nerves completely frayed.

chapters.title: Thirty-four
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi changed into fresh clothing and lay back down.

Every time she closed her eyes, the echo of Xiao Huaiyan's cold laugh seemed to linger just at the edge of her hearing.

She knew his nature. When he was willing to keep up the pleasant facade, he could be the picture of a warm and gentlemanly ruler, easy to speak with. When he decided the facade was no longer worth his effort, there was no force in the world that could defy him.

Jiang Mi felt utterly drained. Even lifting her arm took effort.

"Matron Cui," she called softly. "I'd like something to eat."

Matron Cui was genuinely pleased. She quickly brought congee with rice, chicken broth, and a selection of light, appetite-rousing small dishes.

Jiang Mi looked at the spread and suppressed a frown. She took a few sips of congee and could not manage more.

But if she didn't eat, how could she recover quickly? And if she didn't recover quickly, how could she get home?

She forced herself through half a bowl of congee and a full bowl of broth, pushing past her body's reluctance.

When she truly could not go on, she had Matron Cui clear it away.

Not long afterward, Jiang Mi pressed a hand to her mouth and hurried out of bed. She retched into the chamber pot until her stomach was emptied — everything she had just eaten, brought back up.

She was left dizzy and wretched.

Matron Cui was frightened. She sent Caidong to inform the Empress Dowager and dispatched another maid to summon the physician.

When Jiang Mi had settled, Matron Cui helped her rinse her mouth with fresh water and eased her back into bed.

"Miss, what is the matter?" Matron Cui asked, deeply worried.

Jiang Mi shook her head in silence. "Now that it's out," she said weakly, "I actually feel much better."

Head Physician Gu arrived quickly and performed his examination, a cloth over her wrist as he took her pulse.

After a moment he said, "How has someone so young come to carry such a heavy burden of worry? Try not to think so much — open your heart and let it breathe. That will do more good than any medicine. That said, the purging was not entirely a bad thing — how do you feel now, Miss? Somewhat lighter?"

Jiang Mi nodded.

"I'll write out another prescription to clear away the lingering chill. The stomach is still delicate — don't eat too much or too quickly. It needs to be coaxed along gently."

She understood — she had been impatient. "Thank you, Physician Gu."

He gathered his case and was moving toward the door when he paused and turned back. "If the young miss finds herself restless, put on something warm and take a slow walk in the courtyard. Don't let the worry gather into something worse."

Jiang Mi acknowledged the advice quietly.

Cainan soon brought the freshly brewed medicine. Jiang Mi drank it while it was hot and was asleep again before she knew it.

She dreamed.

A flat-bottomed boat gliding through a sea of lotus leaves and blossoms. Jiang Mi leaned over the side and plucked a seedpod, shelled the fresh lotus seeds into her palm, and put one in her mouth — clean and sweet, the tender heart still without its bitterness.

Just as she was reaching for another, hidden on the far side of a thick cluster of leaves, there were other people.

"Sister Zhang, the other day I saw that Noble Consort Jiang come out of the imperial study with red eyes. She clearly had been scolded. It's just like her to have no sense of propriety, always fluttering around His Majesty with that coy manner of hers. And pretending to be delicate like Consort Rouyi — every morning after her nights in the Emperor's chambers, she's practically wilting, as if the lightest wind might blow her over. I haven't been called in yet myself, but I know His Majesty is a gentle person — how could he make anyone uncomfortable? She does it to show off, obviously."

The other voice laughed. "You don't know the half of it. When she first got to serve in his chambers, she didn't just displease His Majesty — she lost the promotion in rank she was supposed to receive."

"Oh, Sister Zhang, tell me everything!"

The two women murmured together and then broke into peals of laughter. "With that sort of behavior, no wonder His Majesty doesn't favor her..."

One of them must have spotted Jiang Mi first. Both fell into a hurried curtsy.

The boats had drifted close together. Consort Liu, trembling with apprehension, clutched at Jiang Mi's sleeve. "Noble Consort Jiang, this concubine knows she was wrong — please, Noble Consort, do not report this to Consort Duan."

In her fright, Consort Liu had seized Jiang Mi's sleeve, and the wide, loose fabric had slid back to reveal her bare arm — across which several darkened, intimate marks were plainly visible. Both Consort Liu and Consort Zhang went momentarily silent.

Jiang Mi, humiliated and furious, wrenched her arm away. "How insolent!"

Consort Liu, it seemed, had only just gathered herself — but then lost her footing, lurched forward, and grabbed Jiang Mi's arm to steady herself. Together they toppled off the boat and into the water.

Jiang Mi choked on several mouthfuls of water before she was pulled out.

She was taken back to her quarters.

She had barely changed out of her wet clothing, her hair not yet wrung dry, when Xiao Huaiyan arrived.

Without a word, he gathered her into his arms and, draping a cloak around her, took her to the bath.

The warm water swallowed her. She was frightened — she could only cling to his shoulders and refuse to let go.

But Xiao Huaiyan pulled her hands loose and let her flounder, saying in an offhand tone that she shouldn't panic; he wouldn't let her choke.

He said it. And yet at the same time his hands were pulling her clothing off piece by piece, until only a small bodice embroidered with gold peonies remained. She wrapped her arms around herself in shame, but Xiao Huaiyan pressed up behind her and guided her arms with his, moving them through the water.

As she had known he would, he eventually pulled the tie of the bodice loose. In the water she could barely draw breath.

His casual disregard for her — that was why the other consorts mocked her too. She had felt like nothing but a plaything for him to amuse himself with and discard.

……

Jiang Mi opened her eyes. Someone was sitting at the bedside.

"You're finally awake. Tangtang, what were you dreaming about? Your aunt watched you looking like you were about to cry in your sleep." The Empress Dowager reached over with a cloth and gently dabbed the perspiration from Jiang Mi's brow.

Jiang Mi sniffled. Her eyes went red.

The Empress Dowager laughed softly. "Oh dear — you really are going to cry in front of your aunt?"

Jiang Mi pressed down the burning in her eyes. "Aunt — what brings you over?"

"You're suffering like this — how could your aunt not worry?"

The Empress Dowager reached in and felt Jiang Mi's back. "Good — you're sweating. Sit up and change into something dry, then have a little plain rice congee."

With the palace maids' help, Jiang Mi changed her clothing.

Her energy was still low, but her body felt much looser than before.

She did not go back to bed — she sat at the table to eat.

The Empress Dowager sat beside her, watching. "You've lost weight this illness, Tangtang. Your face looks smaller."

Jiang Mi touched her own cheeks. She could not feel the difference.

"Don't worry yourself too much," the Empress Dowager said. "Your Majesty will get to the bottom of what happened at the lake. Just focus on getting well."

Jiang Mi sipped her congee without answering.

The Empress Dowager continued, "While you were sleeping, the Virtuous Consort removed her hairpins and dressed in plain robes, and sent a written plea of guilt to me through a palace maid. She wrote that the Xie family had failed in raising their daughter and committed a grave error, and that Xie Mingshan has already been sent to the family's temple to seek penance by the light of incense and oil lamps. Her words were earnest and humble, asking for my forgiveness. She also said that once the Princess Anyang is married off, she will ask to be moved to a detached palace herself, and will not remain in the inner court."

"The Virtuous Consort has been navigating self-interest ever since the late Emperor's time — knowing when to bow and when to bend. Now that she's under house arrest and the Xie family is beset by trouble on all sides, this display of submission is meant for the Emperor's eyes."

Jiang Mi set down her spoon. "Xie Mingshan has been sent to the family temple?"

The Empress Dowager gave a cold smile. "If she wasn't, who would dare marry any of the eligible daughters from the Xie household?"

Jiang Mi looked down and stirred the congee slowly. "Aunt," she said, "if I had never entered the palace, if I had never played the zither at the Emperor's birthday banquet and received that gift — do you think none of this would have happened?"

The Empress Dowager looked at her. "Has Tangtang been frightened? The inner palace has always been a place of scheming and rivalry. That is unavoidable. But so long as you win the heart of one man and earn his protection, no plot or intrigue will be able to touch you."

Jiang Mi raised her eyes. Sorrow lay in them. "But Aunt, I don't want that. I'm afraid."

The Empress Dowager looked away. She rose. "Tangtang — as a daughter of a great family, you have enjoyed all the privileges that come with that position, and so you must also bear its responsibilities. Don't dwell on this too much. In your aunt's view, His Majesty has been treating you well."

The Empress Dowager thought then of the late Emperor — his all-consuming devotion to the Honored Consort, how she had borne him one child after another while he sheltered and doted on all of them, never letting the Honored Consort suffer a moment of discomfort. That favoritism had fed their ambitions, and in the end they had turned on one another for the throne.

She was the one who had survived. And yet in the dead of night, she sometimes wondered what it would have felt like to be the one adored.

Her Tangtang's beauty surpassed the Honored Consort's. She could play the zither, wield the brush, command the chessboard. And she had learned that bewitching dance as well.

Whoever sat on the throne, her Tangtang might not be Empress — but to be the favored consort was more than within reach.

……

Matron Cui accompanied the Empress Dowager back to the inner chamber, and watched as the Empress Dowager tipped two pills from a white jade vial into her palm.

She knew at once that the heart pains had returned. She quickly poured water and brought it over.

After the Empress Dowager swallowed the pills, Matron Cui said gently, "Your Ladyship, the Third Miss is still young — a little more time, and she'll come around."

"She was so overjoyed when His Majesty first came to power," the Empress Dowager said. "And when I told her she was to enter the palace, her cheeks went pink and she could barely contain her happiness. But lately I keep getting the feeling that Tangtang is afraid of the Emperor."

Matron Cui said, "She is still young and inexperienced, and all of this has frightened her. Once she settles, she'll find her footing again."

The Empress Dowager let out a long sigh. "Looking at her like that, I feel for her. If that incident from back then had never happened, perhaps she could have had her wish."

Matron Cui's eyes flickered. She said softly, "Your Ladyship, no one could have seen that coming. Please don't blame yourself."

……

Jiang Mi took more medicine before bed and slept deeply. When she woke the next day, she felt as though the illness had largely passed.

She could not help but admire Head Physician Gu's skill.

She ate a light breakfast and, remembering his advice, changed into a fresh outfit and went out for a walk.

She thought she would go pay her respects to her aunt, then stroll through Cining Palace.

As she entered, she came face to face with the Grand Princess Zhaoyang and the heir of the Zhenguo Duke's household, just coming out from inside.

Before Jiang Mi could bow, the Grand Princess stopped her and took hold of her hand. "Miss Jiang, are you feeling better?"

Jiang Mi nodded. "Thank you for your concern, Your Highness."

The Grand Princess said with evident remorse, "It was all because of our household that you suffered. At least the true culprit has been found, and the others involved have all been dealt with. I hope, Miss Jiang, that you will not let this distance you from me."

Xue Jinglin noticed that the Jiang young miss was at a loss for how to respond to his grandmother's effusive guilt, so he stepped forward. "Grandmother, Ningzhu asked me to deliver something to Miss Jiang. Miss Jiang — could I have a brief word?"

The Grand Princess glanced at him and released Jiang Mi's hand.

"Go along, then." The Grand Princess took her leave.

Jiang Mi, puzzled, followed the heir a few steps to the side. "What did Ningzhu want the heir to deliver?"

Xue Jinglin withdrew a small, neat box from his sleeve and opened it toward her. "This is a beaded bracelet Ningzhu strung herself. She said it is her apology to you for the fright she caused — the oversight was our household's, and she hopes you will forgive us."

Jiang Mi said, "The heir is too kind. Such an unexpected turn of events — no one could have foreseen it."

Xue Jinglin gave a faint smile. "Then please accept the bracelet from Ningzhu. The little one wanted to come and see you herself — but today Grandmother and I came to offer our apologies to the Empress Dowager, and it wasn't appropriate to bring her along. It took quite a lot of coaxing before she agreed to let it go."

Jiang Mi hesitated, then reached into the box and took out the bracelet.

It was a wrist bracelet — pearls and gemstones and tourmaline of different sizes strung together. The combination had a kind of wild, jeweled beauty that was also, somehow, endearingly childlike.

Xue Jinglin gave a light cough and said with a helpless look, "If you don't accept it, that little one will flood the house with tears. I truly cannot manage her."

Jiang Mi pressed her lips together to hide a smile. "Thank you, heir. Please pass my thanks to Ningzhu — once I'm home from the palace, I'll have her come and visit."

Xue Jinglin placed the open box into her hands, bowed, and turned to leave.

But at some distance away, a small group had stopped and stood waiting — and for how long, it was impossible to say.

Li Fu's forehead was slick with cold sweat. When he saw the Jiang girl not only accept the gift but smile at the man, his right eye began to twitch.

He kept his head down and did not dare look at the Emperor's face.

In that instant, a quiet laugh broke the silence. "Quite skilled at working every room, isn't she."

chapters.title: Thirty-five
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi accepted the bracelet the heir of the Zhenguo Duke's household had delivered on Ningzhu's behalf.

It was the first time she had ever received something strung by a little girl's own hands, and she found it charming.

She slipped the bracelet onto her pale wrist, raised her hand to the light, and turned it slowly. The pearls and gemstones caught the morning sun and scattered in small bright flashes.

Jiang Mi smiled faintly. Such a pretty, sparkling thing.

She went inside to pay her respects to the Empress Dowager, who looked well.

"Aunt." Jiang Mi called out softly.

The Empress Dowager smiled. "Why did you come over? Is your constitution better?"

"Much better. Head Physician Gu told me not to just lie in bed — I should walk around a little when I can."

The Empress Dowager nodded. "Quite right. The sun is good today, not too cold."

Jiang Mi asked, "Aunt, I just ran into the Grand Princess and the Zhenguo Duke's heir outside. Has there been any resolution on that end?"

The Empress Dowager's smile faded slightly. "The results there match what Pei Chi reported. With those people dead, even a link back to the Duke of Xie's household means nothing without surviving witnesses. However, at morning court today, someone submitted a memorial against the Duke of Xie — a matter involving his official duties that had gone wrong. His Majesty spoke in an unusually sharp tone about it. The Duke's position as Minister of Revenue may not be secure for much longer."

Jiang Mi had never paid close attention to court affairs. She recalled that in her past life, the Duke of Xie's household had still been standing when she entered the palace.

Perhaps this life truly was beginning to diverge.

The Empress Dowager remarked, "The Grand Princess and the heir coming to offer their apologies to me was something I've never seen before — the Grand Princess bowing her head to anyone. That flower banquet was intended to find a match for Jinglin, but instead ended up like this. Poor child — if only he weren't ill, he'd be long overdue for a wife."

Jiang Mi let her curiosity lead her. "Aunt — Lord Xue Jingyan seems to be a few years older than the heir. Why didn't the Grand Princess arrange his marriage first?"

The Empress Dowager paused, then decided Jiang Mi could know. "This actually has to do with an old matter in the Zhenguo Duke's household. It hasn't been spoken of in years, and few people are aware of it. Everyone assumes the Grand Princess favors the second branch, which is why the title and heir's position went to the second branch's son. But the truth is that the main branch is not the Grand Princess's own family. They are children left behind by the late Duke of Zhenguo's first wife. The Grand Princess has kept a certain distance from them, and understandably so. In name, the Grand Princess is Xue Jingyan's grandmother, but in practice she does not feel it is her place to arrange his marriage. She reasons that if a match goes well, all is fine — but if it turns out unhappily, the blame will fall on her. So she has simply left it entirely alone."

She added, "The young woman who saved you is the fourth miss of the main branch. I've already had gifts sent over. When she finds a suitable family to marry into, I'll issue an imperial marriage decree for her — give her some distinction, and let her go to her new home in proper style."

Jiang Mi's lashes trembled. She lowered her head and turned the bracelet on her wrist. "Thank you, Aunt."

……

In the Qianqing Palace, Li Fu watched His Majesty work through the morning as usual — reading memorials, annotating them, calling in ministers to discuss state affairs.

It was only at dinnertime that he finally dismissed the last group of officials.

Li Fu had braced for the worst, warning everyone below stairs to keep their wits about them.

But to his surprise, during the meal His Majesty actually praised one of the dishes — a stuffed braised mushroom — and even rewarded the imperial cook.

Li Fu had rather expected that after seeing the Jiang girl accept a gift from the Zhenguo Duke's heir, and exchange what looked like perfectly comfortable pleasantries with him, His Majesty would have done something about it.

After all, Chengzhong had once told him that His Majesty had visited Qianshuang Temple and somehow ended up having the hundred-year blessing tree cut down.

And yet — nothing. Perfectly calm.

Li Fu tended his duties all the way through seeing His Majesty to bed without incident, and only then permitted himself a slow exhale of relief.

He shook his head quietly. It was better not to try and guess at the imperial will.

Perhaps Miss Jiang had stirred some passing interest in His Majesty — but when His Majesty chose to let something go, he could do so in the blink of an eye.

Li Fu lowered the bed curtains, brought the attendants out with him, and left.

Xiao Huaiyan closed his eyes.

As though he had known it was coming, he began to dream again.

In the dream he saw that woman again.

This time she was curled on the surface of a writing desk, trembling, shy and flustered.

The desk in his imperial study — the one usually covered with intelligence reports, documents, and memorials from the court.

At this moment, all those papers had been swept to the floor.

He leaned over her and took hold of her arms. His lips pressed against white skin — only a moment's pause — and a red mark bloomed.

"Truly fragile."

Fingers intertwined with hers, palm to palm. On the inner side of her white wrist was a very small red mole.

He lowered his head.

That small, slight body gave a shiver.

He laughed silently.

He lifted her onto himself.

Bright afternoon light poured through the hall, illuminating everything. Inside the imperial study, in the full light of day, all propriety left aside.

Those long, narrow eyes looked perfectly composed — and burned with absolute, exquisite pleasure.

……

Xiao Huaiyan opened his eyes again. He tugged at his collar.

His mouth was inexplicably dry.

"Li Fu. What is the hour?"

Li Fu jolted awake. Dawn was still far off — why had His Majesty woken?

He hurried into the inner chamber and bowed. "Your Majesty, it is just past the second quarter of the Hour of the Tiger."

Xiao Huaiyan pressed his fingers to the bridge of his nose. "Bring me tea."

Li Fu rushed out and returned with the cup.

Xiao Huaiyan drank several sips. The restless heat refused to subside.

He frowned. He pushed back the covers and got up, changed into an outer robe, and went straight to the training grounds.

……

Li Fu kept watch outside the imperial study, bored senseless.

His Majesty had extraordinary energy today.

He had gone to the training grounds and put a dozen sparring partners flat on their backs, then emerged looking refreshed and vigorous for morning court. After court, he had summoned a group of ministers to the study, and they were still at it.

Just then, the Director of the Imperial Music Bureau, Zhong, sidled up wearing a wide, ingratiating smile. "Chief Eunuch Li — any word yet on what we discussed before?"

Li Fu turned away as though he hadn't heard.

Director Zhong followed him, slipping a banknote into his hand. "Chief Eunuch Li, the Music Bureau has composed several very fine pieces and dances — we're only waiting for His Majesty to enjoy them."

Li Fu said coldly, "I know exactly what you're scheming. I've already told you — His Majesty has no interest in this sort of thing. Don't come back."

Director Zhong slipped in another banknote. "Chief Eunuch Li, His Majesty had only just ascended the throne before — too busy to think of us at the Music Bureau. But now that things have settled, who's to say His Majesty might not find some leisure time and take an interest? Chief Eunuch Li, the whole Bureau would be in your debt."

Li Fu had been about to dismiss him out of hand for good. But something made him hesitate — he found himself thinking of the singed sleeping robe he had been ordered to burn not long ago.

He thought of His Majesty's recent behavior. He began to weigh the idea.

Director Zhong saw that Li Fu had not refused him outright as he normally did, and sensed an opening. He leaned in conspiratorially. "Chief Eunuch Li, the young woman selected for the new piece — beautiful and talented both, truly exceptional. One performance and the Emperor will surely be delighted."

Li Fu looked up. The doors of the imperial study had opened. Ministers were filing out.

He flapped a hand at Director Zhong. "Go back for now. Wait for word from me."

chapters.title: Thirty-six
chapters.content:
Li Fu saw His Majesty set down the vermilion brush and stepped forward attentively with a cup of tea.

Xiao Huaiyan took a sip and, seeing Li Fu wearing the expression of a man with something caught in his throat, said flatly, "What is it?"

Li Fu arranged his face into an ingratiating smile and ventured, "Your Majesty, the Imperial Music Bureau has passed word that they have composed several new dances and would like to present them for Your Majesty's enjoyment. Would Your Majesty be in the mood to watch?"

Xiao Huaiyan raised an eyebrow, his expression hovering somewhere between amusement and cool detachment. "How much did they pay you?"

Li Fu was so frightened he dropped into an apology at once. "This servant would never dare — this servant only thought that with Your Majesty so busy of late, perhaps a little music and dance might help him relax..."

Li Fu thought to himself: disaster. He'd misread the moment again, complimenting the horse by patting its leg.

Xiao Huaiyan set down the teacup. "Have them wait in Wangyun Pavilion. We will go when We have finished with the memorials."

The moment the words were out, Li Fu stood frozen, convinced he had heard wrong.

His Majesty had actually agreed?

Surely the sun was rising in the west today.

Li Fu drifted out of the imperial study in a daze.

Chengzhong sidled up with concern. "Godfather, what's the matter?"

Li Fu shook his head. "Go to the Music Bureau and tell Director Zhong that His Majesty will proceed to Wangyun Pavilion to view the performance once he is finished with the memorials."

Chengzhong was equally astonished, but did not dare delay and went off at once to deliver the news.

The Music Bureau erupted into jubilant excitement.

Director Zhong walked over to a tall, strikingly beautiful young woman and smiled. "Miss Lu — was that money not well spent after all?"

The young woman dipped into a curtsy. "Director Zhong's generosity is something Lingyun will never forget." She slipped two more banknotes into his hand as she said it.

Director Zhong patted them with satisfaction. "May you achieve all you wish for and rise to great heights. And if you do, mind you don't forget this humble Director of yours."

Lu Lingyun said modestly, "The Director flatters me. I am only a lowly dancer — I'll be counting on the Director to look out for me going forward."

Director Zhong was thoroughly pleased.

……

Word that the Emperor was proceeding to Wangyun Pavilion to watch a performance spread through the palace almost immediately.

In Changchun Palace, the Virtuous Consort had just snipped a stem of flowers when the news reached her. She smiled. "Now that is something. I imagine certain people will not be sleeping tonight."

In Cining Palace, the Empress Dowager Jiang was copying sutras. She paused briefly. "That is not without its uses. It is better than His Majesty remaining buried in state affairs all the time. Even if some performer catches his fancy, the most she would receive is the rank of Cairen — it changes nothing of consequence."

Since his accession, the Emperor had given his full attention to governing, repeatedly deferring the selection of an Empress and consorts until the following year, leaving the inner palace all but empty. If he was now allowing the Music Bureau to perform for him, that too was a kind of signal.

Jiang Mi had just returned from a walk in the imperial garden, carrying a small armful of flowers she had gathered. She stepped into Cining Palace.

Matron Cui came to meet her. "Third Miss, are you tired?"
