Jiang Mi smiled. "Not too bad. I did work up a bit of a sweat though — I'll bathe in a little while. Let me bring the flowers to my aunt and arrange them in a vase first."

She walked into the hall as she spoke, only to find the Empress Dowager absent. "Matron Cui, where is my aunt?"

"Her Ladyship has gone to the prayer hall. She likely won't be out for some time."

Jiang Mi nodded. She knew her aunt kept regular hours of Buddhist observance.

She arranged the fresh flowers in a vase, adjusted them until she was satisfied, then told Matron Cui, "I'll head back for now. I'll come keep my aunt company at dinner."

Matron Cui saw her to the door. "Do take care, Third Miss."

Jiang Mi returned to the side chamber and had Cainan draw hot water. The walk through the imperial garden had left her feeling sticky, and she wanted to bathe.

After her bath and a change of clothes, she felt considerably fresher.

Cainan set out the midday meal. Jiang Mi managed half a bowl of rice and a bowl of soup, and noticed with some relief that the smell of meat no longer made her queasy.

That was a marked improvement over the past two days. After eating she took a short turn about the room to aid digestion, drank her medicine, and lay down to sleep.

……

By the time Xiao Huaiyan finished the memorials, the hour had moved into late afternoon. He stood, rolling his wrists slowly.

Li Fu stepped forward. "Your Majesty, the performers from the Music Bureau are waiting in Wangyun Pavilion."

Xiao Huaiyan seemed to remember this only now. He made a sound of acknowledgment and walked out of the hall.

Inside Wangyun Pavilion, everyone had been waiting with craned necks, and the imperial party's arrival was met with barely suppressed excitement. For many of the performers, this was the first time they had laid eyes on the Emperor himself. They dropped to their knees in unison to receive him, and all any of them could see was that sweep of apricot-yellow dragon robes passing by — not one of them dared raise their eyes to the imperial face.

Xiao Huaiyan sat and said, "Begin."

Three claps rang out. The drums began.

A great drum, so large it required several men to carry it, was borne into the hall. Atop it stood a woman of lithe, graceful figure, dressed in scarlet robes, her feet bare against the drumskin.

She danced to the drumbeat, her long water sleeves spinning out like wings, her face a flash of vivid beauty. Her slender waist moved like a current of water, and the great sleeves billowed in wave after wave — breathtaking to behold.

Li Fu and the others were quite captivated. The Music Bureau had genuinely outdone itself this time.

Li Fu stole a glance at His Majesty.

The Emperor held his wine cup, his expression placid — but his gaze had gone distant.

In Li Fu's years of experience with him, that particular look meant one thing: His Majesty was somewhere else entirely.

Such a brilliant performance, and His Majesty was drifting?

The drums and music thundered on. The crimson-clad dancer spun to the beat, pouring every last shred of her skill into capturing the Emperor's favor.

Xiao Huaiyan took another sip of wine, and in that moment he seemed to sink into the half-lit world of a dream — where there was also a woman dancing barefoot, her feet white as snow, her hips swaying with a teasing, intoxicating allure. He could not make out her face, yet she effortlessly stirred the dark current of desire that lay coiled at the bottom of him.

And especially those feet, adorned with the tinkling ankle bells — delicate and precise, trembling faintly in his grip.

Xiao Huaiyan closed his eyes and opened them again. The drums were still thundering. The woman dancing atop the great drum had an excellent face and flawless form; just now she lifted her sleeve, baring a pale, slender arm.

Xiao Huaiyan's gaze remained perfectly still. Not a ripple.

For some reason his mind had flashed back to the dim room at the Zhenguo Duke's household, where he had locked the trembling woman's hands above her head and forced her wrist up — and there, on the inside of that pale wrist, it seemed...

In a sudden flash of clarity, Xiao Huaiyan rose to his feet.

The piece had just ended. Lu Lingyun, prostrated atop the drum, was trembling with nervous excitement.

In the instant she pressed her forehead to the drumskin, she caught the movement of the young Emperor. She held her breath — and heard that warm, unhurried voice say, "Award them."

Lu Lingyun went still, forgetting to join the others in calling out her thanks.

Just two words?

He wasn't going to ask her name?

She looked up toward the raised seat — only to find the Emperor already gone.

……

Jiang Mi woke from her nap with cheeks flushed pink, as though someone had dusted them with rouge.

She sat before the dressing table while Cainan combed her hair behind her.

Cainan said, "Miss looks so well right now — even the finest rouge couldn't replicate that sunrise glow."

Jiang Mi pressed her lips together in a smile. "I slept too long — it's nearly dinnertime and I've only just gotten up. Just put it up simply, I need to get to my aunt's before it's too late."

She changed into a lotus-pink brocade gown, and the trace of illness that had lingered over her these past days was nowhere to be seen.

Walking to Cining Palace, Jiang Mi thought that since her health was mostly restored, she ought to start broaching the subject of leaving the palace with her aunt.

Passing through a covered corridor, she caught two young palace maids speaking with their backs to her.

"I heard the Music Bureau's performance left the Emperor greatly pleased — he gave them a generous reward."

"Indeed. I wonder whether the lead dancer might be the first to receive the imperial favor..."

Cainan gave two timely coughs. The maids startled, turned, saw Jiang Mi, and bowed their heads in a low curtsy.

Jiang Mi said nothing and waved them off.

Cainan murmured reassuringly, "Miss, pay them no mind. They were just picking up fragments and running away with them."

Jiang Mi smiled faintly. "I'm all right."

How could she mind?

She only heard the words "Music Bureau" and found her thoughts drifting to that leading dancer, Lady Lu, from her past life. She had watched Lady Lu perform at various ceremonies and festivals. The dancing had genuinely been excellent, the costumes original and striking.

It was only that after every performance, Xiao Huaiyan would always have the costume sent to her — altered — and make her put on the ankle bells and dance for him alone.

A gust of cool autumn wind saved her, washing the heat from her face.

Jiang Mi walked into the Empress Dowager's inner chamber. Her aunt immediately praised the flowers she had left behind.

"Tangtang's flowers have brightened up the whole room." The Empress Dowager drew Jiang Mi forward and studied her. "That outfit suits you. Your color is much better. It does your aunt's heart good just to look at you."

Jiang Mi ducked her head, a little flustered by all the praise. "Aunt..."

The Empress Dowager smiled and asked, "Are you hungry? What would you like? Your aunt will have the imperial kitchen send it over."

"Whatever Aunt is having is fine with me."

The Empress Dowager tapped her lightly on the nose.

Then from outside came the announcement: "His Majesty the Emperor arrives!"

The Empress Dowager looked surprised.

The smile on Jiang Mi's lips froze in place.

Xiao Huaiyan stepped into the hall, shrugged off his cloak and tossed it to Li Fu, and said to the Empress Dowager, "Has Mother taken dinner yet?"

The Empress Dowager rose with a smile. "Your Majesty has come at just the right moment — I was about to have the meal served."

"Then We'll dine here with Mother."

The Empress Dowager was pleasantly surprised, if a little overwhelmed, and quickly told Matron Cui to have the imperial kitchen add more dishes.

Once Xiao Huaiyan was seated, a palace maid came forward with tea.

Jiang Mi had no choice but to come forward and curtsy. "Long live Your Majesty."

Xiao Huaiyan gave a nod. "Rise. Miss Jiang, is your health improving?"

Jiang Mi kept her gaze lowered. "This subject daughter thanks Your Majesty for his concern. I am much better."

Xiao Huaiyan said no more. His posture was more relaxed than usual — there was a certain unhurried ease about him tonight.

Both the Empress Dowager and Jiang Mi caught a faint drift of wine.

The Empress Dowager said, "Your Majesty has had quite a bit to drink. Let me have someone prepare a sobering draught."

Xiao Huaiyan pressed his fingers briefly to his brow. "Thank you, Mother."

The Empress Dowager, noticing he seemed slightly unwell, asked, "Your Majesty, are you uncomfortable? Should we call for a physician?"

Xiao Huaiyan gave a brief laugh. "Only a mild headache. A little rest will fix it."

The Empress Dowager glanced toward Jiang Mi and said, "Whenever my own head troubles me, Tangtang massages the pressure points for me and it eases considerably. I wonder, if Your Majesty wouldn't mind..."

Xiao Huaiyan's gaze moved to Jiang Mi, a faint smile on his face. "Miss Jiang has this skill as well?"

The Empress Dowager said, "Tangtang is always thinking of others — she was afraid of my headaches and made a special effort to learn the acupressure points."

Xiao Huaiyan looked at Jiang Mi, who had not raised her eyes once, and smiled. "Then We'd be grateful for Miss Jiang's help."

Jiang Mi stood rooted to the spot. The Empress Dowager gave her a discreet push. "Tangtang, go on."

Jiang Mi forced herself to move, step by difficult step, until she stood at Xiao Huaiyan's side. She murmured, "This subject daughter begs your pardon."

She raised her hands. Her sleeve slid back, exposing a half-length of pale wrist. Cool fingertips pressed against the pressure points at his temples.

The pressure of a young woman's hands was soft and almost weightless against Xiao Huaiyan. He closed his eyes, drawing in the faint, delicate scent that drifted from her.

The featherlight pressure, while barely perceptible, had a strange, drowsy quality — as though it might lull one to sleep.

When Xiao Huaiyan opened his eyes again, he caught sight of the inside of her left wrist — and nestled there, half-hidden, a very small red mole.

He smiled to himself, as though he had already known it would be there.

chapters.title: Thirty-seven
chapters.content:
The memory that had flickered through him during the dance in Wangyun Pavilion now returned with quiet certainty: when he had placed the soaking Jiang Mi on the bed at the Zhenguo Duke's household and she had been gnawing at her own fingers to keep herself lucid, he had taken that bloodied hand and lifted it — and on the inside of her wrist had been a small red mole.

He had simply not registered it at the time.

Now he looked at the pale wrist close before him. That small red mole.

Exactly where the woman in his dreams bore hers. The very same slender, white wrist.

Xiao Huaiyan's gaze darkened. If it were not for the regular pulse checks the imperial physicians performed on him — and the needling treatments from the high monk — he might have started to wonder whether the Jiang girl had used some bewitching gu poison from the southern lands.

Why did he keep having those dreams?

Was the faceless woman in his dreams Jiang Mi?

But why would it be her?

The intermingled scent of dragon-ambergris incense and wine began to drift toward Jiang Mi in faint, insidious tendrils.

She kept her gaze down and worked to steady her nerves, trying to convince herself she was simply massaging her aunt's temples, that the person beneath her hands was her aunt and no one else.

She had been at it for a while now and her hands were beginning to ache — and still there was no response from him. Perhaps he had fallen asleep. If he had, could she stop?

She snuck a glance upward — only to discover that her aunt and Matron Cui had silently disappeared at some point.

Only she and Xiao Huaiyan remained in the room.

Jiang Mi's heart gave a violent lurch, and something pressed uncomfortably against her chest.

She slowed her hands and let them come to a gradual stop, just about to withdraw them — when a scorching hand closed firmly around her wrist.

Xiao Huaiyan's voice came, slightly roughened. "Why have you stopped?"

Jiang Mi bit the inside of her lip and drew a careful breath. "Your Majesty, dinner in the main hall should be ready by now."

Xiao Huaiyan felt the wrist in his grip — fine-boned and soft, so delicate it seemed it might crumble if he squeezed. On that pale, slender wrist swayed the string of gem beads, catching faint light with each movement.

His thumb settled over the small red mole and traced it once, twice. The wrist gave the faintest tremor. His eyes went deep and still. In the dreams it had been at this same spot, the same slender, pale wrist — and he had left mark after mark upon it.

Jiang Mi's heart was hammering. Was Xiao Huaiyan drunk?

He glanced down, those cool, star-dark eyes resting on the half-healed bite wound on her index finger. Abruptly he said, "Does it still hurt?"

It took her a moment to understand. She had tried several times to pull her hand back and failed entirely. Only then did she realize what he was asking.

"It — it doesn't hurt anymore." Jiang Mi answered in a small voice.

The moment the words left her, the grip that had held her like iron tongs released.

Jiang Mi snatched her hand back and thrust it deep inside the wide shelter of her sleeve.

Xiao Huaiyan turned to look at her. She sat with downcast eyes that conveyed apparent calm, but the tight press of her lips betrayed a thread of tension and fear beneath.

He leaned his head against his hand. "We were somewhat intoxicated — did We frighten you?"

Jiang Mi couldn't help stepping back two paces. Her lips parted slightly, but no words came.

Xiao Huaiyan's tone was unhurried and measured. "We are a little puzzled, Miss Jiang. Why are you so afraid of Us? We recall crossing paths with Miss Jiang several times before, and on every occasion she met Us with a smile. Did We do something to offend you?"

Jiang Mi clenched her perspiring hands tightly. She told herself, firmly, to stay calm.

She gave him a curtsy. "This subject daughter is ashamed. In the past, it was she who did not know propriety and who imposed upon Your Majesty."

Xiao Huaiyan heard these words and gave a quiet laugh. He barely remembered the specifics — only a vague impression of the Empress Dowager's niece stealing shy, sidelong glances at him.

Even if he looked down on the Jiang family's maneuverings, he could not deny that at this moment the Jiang girl in front of him had piqued a genuine desire to understand her better.

His fingers tapped the table in an arrhythmic pattern.

In the quiet room, Jiang Mi felt each tap like a small knife.

She was a little afraid of Xiao Huaiyan when he had been drinking — particularly in her past life. Even now, catching that faint trace of wine and remembering how he had seized her wrist just moments ago, an inexplicable unease crept through her.

She did not know why he had asked her those things. She only feared making another wrong move, speaking another wrong word.

So she stood quietly to one side and said nothing.

After a pause, Xiao Huaiyan spoke. "Would Miss Jiang like to know who drugged her at the Zhenguo Duke's household flower banquet?"

Jiang Mi went very still. He had found out?

She had concealed the drugging from even her aunt, certain it would be a matter without resolution.

She had not expected him to raise it.

She looked toward him, pressed her lips together, and said, "This subject daughter would like to know."

Xiao Huaiyan rose, a significant look in his eye. "Then this time, Miss Jiang — have you thought about what gift to offer in return?"

……

Xiao Huaiyan and Jiang Mi stepped out of the room one after the other. She followed a few paces behind, her mind still turning over his question.

What gift did he want?

She had not been able to answer him then, and he had not pressed the matter further.

Would he still tell her who the culprit was?

Jiang Mi found Xiao Huaiyan more and more difficult to read.

He was walking ahead of her when he suddenly stopped. Jiang Mi, her thoughts elsewhere, nearly walked straight into his back — the shock made every hair on her body stand on end.

Xiao Huaiyan turned around. Seeing the thoroughly startled person behind him looking like a frightened rabbit, he could not quite suppress his amusement. "We forgot something. Hold out your hand."

Jiang Mi had no idea what he intended, but there was no refusing. She raised her arm, palm open, small and soft and pale.

Xiao Huaiyan raised an eyebrow. Quite obedient, for once.

A small jade-colored vial dropped into Jiang Mi's palm.

She heard his voice: "Use it. Don't let it scar."

By the time she looked up again, he was already walking ahead.

The vial was smooth and warm. Jiang Mi's expression was complicated. She frowned — and then tucked it away.

……

The Empress Dowager had already had the banquet laid. Seeing the two of them come in together, she smiled. "Just in time — the dishes have just been brought out."

Xiao Huaiyan smiled. "Mother has gone to trouble."

After Xiao Huaiyan and the Empress Dowager had taken their seats, the Empress Dowager indicated for Jiang Mi to sit beside her.

Jiang Mi breathed a quiet sigh of relief. At least her aunt had not placed her on Xiao Huaiyan's side of the table.

The Empress Dowager asked, "Is Your Majesty's headache a little better? Shall we have the sobering draught brought first?"

Xiao Huaiyan said, "Thanks to Miss Jiang, it has improved a great deal."

The Empress Dowager could not help but smile. "That is good. Have a bowl of soup first to warm the stomach."

As it happened, the wild ginseng and black chicken soup had been placed within Jiang Mi's reach.

She gathered from her aunt's glance that she was meant to serve him a bowl.

Jiang Mi inwardly sighed and was just rising to do so when Li Fu stepped forward with quick little steps, smiling pleasantly. "There is no need to trouble Miss Jiang — a small thing like this, this servant is happy to do."

Li Fu ladled the soup and placed it before Xiao Huaiyan, who picked up his spoon and drank.

The Empress Dowager kept her expression neutral and asked, "How is it?"

Xiao Huaiyan nodded. "Very good."

For Jiang Mi, the meal passed in relative calm. Both Xiao Huaiyan and her aunt kept to the convention of not conversing over food, exchanging only a brief word or two during the meal.

She spent the whole time wishing it would end so Xiao Huaiyan could return to Qianqing Palace and she could breathe again.

After the meal, the palace maids came in with hot tea.

Xiao Huaiyan set down his cup. "Li Fu, bring the item."

Li Fu presented a small antique wooden box with both hands.

The moment Jiang Mi saw the beast-totem carved into the lid, her pupils contracted sharply. In her past life, the ankle bells Xiao Huaiyan had given her had been stored in a box just like this.

She forced herself to look away and steadied her breathing.

It won't be that. Whatever else he might do, he would not produce such a thing in front of her aunt.

Xiao Huaiyan said to the Empress Dowager, "This object was crafted by the hands of a venerable monk and has absorbed years of incense from the temple. Since Miss Jiang suffered a terrible fright in the water, We wish to present it to her to ward off evil and settle her nerves."

The Empress Dowager was genuinely surprised and pleased. "Tangtang, thank His Majesty at once."

Jiang Mi stepped forward and accepted the box with both hands, bowing. "This subject daughter thanks Your Majesty."

Xiao Huaiyan heard that soft, slightly wavering voice and said, "Open it. Let's see whether you like it."

The Empress Dowager was curious as well.

With both their gazes on her, Jiang Mi held her breath and opened the lid.

At the sight of what lay inside, she slowly exhaled.

"A blessing bangle!" the Empress Dowager exclaimed. "Tangtang, put it on. You've been sleeping poorly these past few days, haven't you? A bangle that has been steeped in temple incense has a calming effect. His Majesty is truly thoughtful."

Jiang Mi currently wore Ningzhu's beaded bracelet on her left wrist — she had always worn bracelets and bangles on the left hand.

To put on the new bangle, she would have to take off Ningzhu's bracelet first.

The bangle slipped on and fit as though it had been made for her wrist — the pale jade caught the warmth of the light and glowed softly against her skin.

The Empress Dowager offered several words of admiration.

But Jiang Mi felt a strange, formless unease she could not quite name.

After Xiao Huaiyan left, Jiang Mi quickly retreated to the side chamber.

She sat at the dressing table and worked the bangle off her wrist. Then she put Ningzhu's bracelet back in its box.

She took out the small jade vial Xiao Huaiyan had given her, uncapped it, and sniffed. The scent of the medicine was oddly familiar.

In her past life, Xiao Huaiyan had given her a medicine just like this before.

She placed both the vial and the bangle together in the box.

She didn't know whether it was Xiao Huaiyan's strange words and actions, but she felt deeply unsettled.

She wanted very badly to leave the palace.

She had tried to raise it gently with her aunt earlier, and her aunt had immediately redirected the conversation, saying there was no hurry, that she should rest a few more days.

Tonight's visit by Xiao Huaiyan would only reinforce her aunt's belief that he held Jiang Mi in some special regard — and she worried her aunt would now find reasons to keep her in the palace longer, perhaps even push her toward more contact with him.

The thought of repeating her past life's path made her feel cold.

……

Xiao Huaiyan's party had barely returned to Qianqing Palace when Pei Chi, who had been waiting some time, stepped forward with a bow.

Pei Chi reported, "Your Majesty, every corner of the Zhenguo Duke's household — including the side courtyard where Miss Jiang was staying — has been thoroughly searched. Nothing suspicious was found. In the side courtyard, however, there was a white tiger found recently deceased. The heir explained that the tiger's ferocity had frightened his sister and a guest, and so he had it put down."

"Regarding Miss Jiang's fall into the lake, there remain several points of irregularity."

Xiao Huaiyan also brought up, "In the twentieth year of Emperor Yongshun's reign, the Empress Dowager wished to have the eldest legitimate daughter of the Marquis of Cheng'en's household, Jiang Wan, become the principal wife of the Prince of Qi. The Virtuous Consort opposed this, and the Jiang family instead arranged a marriage with the Duke of Ying's household. The Empress Dowager then turned her efforts toward raising and grooming the third daughter of the Marquis of Cheng'en, with plans to send her into the inner palace once the Prince of Qi took the throne. Besides her studies in the arts, the Third Miss of the Jiang family also studied dance under the famous dancer Yue Yao, renowned throughout the Jiangnan region."

Li Fu, listening to Pei Chi's account, felt a chill crawl down his back.

So it meant that no matter who had ended up on the throne, the Jiang family's Third Miss would have been sent into the palace either way.

And the Jiang family had at one point actually been considering throwing their support behind the Prince of Qi. That was truly...

Li Fu did not dare glance at the Emperor's face.

Pei Chi produced a case file. "Your Majesty, this official has investigated every purchase of 'Spring Slumber' made within the past three months, and has identified a connection to the Zhenguo Duke's household flower banquet."

chapters.title: Thirty-Eight
chapters.content:
After Xiao Huaiyan fell asleep, just as he had come to expect, the dreams found him again.

This time he lay half-reclining on a daybed, pleasantly unsteady with wine, eyes closed in rest.

The door opened. Light, soft footsteps moved toward him.

"Your Majesty..." A sweet, breathy voice, calling his name softly.

A faint, delicate fragrance drifted over. A figure was kneeling before the bed, leaning close, voice barely above a murmur: "Is Your Majesty's headache troubling you again? Let this concubine press it for you."

Xiao Huaiyan did not speak, but removed the hand he had draped over his brow.

The woman's fragrance wrapped around him. Cool fingertips found the pressure points, the pressure soft as cotton — not the slightest bit of improvement since the last time.

After a while she seemed to tire and stopped.

Xiao Huaiyan reached up and circled her waist, fingers pressing into that slender curve, drawing a small, involuntary tremor from the flesh there.

She settled herself back and resumed.

After a little more time had passed, he heard her say, with evident uncertainty, "Your Majesty... I have heard the peach blossoms at the detached palace are beginning to bloom. This concubine would also like to go..."

Xiao Huaiyan said blandly, "Were you not feeling unwell?"

He felt the slender waist beneath his hand go momentarily rigid.

The voice dropped to a near-whisper. "My... my cycle has already finished. Could you add this concubine's name to the list?"

Xiao Huaiyan took her wrist and drew her forward, slowly opening his eyes.

His gaze fell first on the pale wrist — and on the inside of it, a small red mole. He raised his eyes further.

That face, vivid as a peach blossom in full bloom, was red with embarrassment.

No mist, no gauze. Perfectly clear.

It was Jiang Mi.

Only this was a shyness he had never seen in her before.

Xiao Huaiyan listened to what she had said, and a smile spread slowly across his lips. He replied with languid ambiguity, "Is that so?"

A flash of alarm crossed her eyes — as though he had seen through a previous lie, and now, desperate to be added to the list for the detached palace, she had no choice but to push through.

She bit her lip with the air of someone making a difficult decision, then took his hand and looked up at him with those wide, liquid eyes. "If Your Majesty doesn't believe this concubine, then... then perhaps you should verify it yourself..."

Xiao Huaiyan's wine-dark eyes were as fathomless as ink. He gave a low laugh.

The woman's entire face blazed crimson as she looked away.

He cupped that delicate chin. "Noble Consort Jiang, you really are..." perpetually courting your own undoing.

Her startled cry was swallowed at once. The shadows of branches swayed beyond the window. Robes lay scattered across the floor.

When the storm had passed, Xiao Huaiyan rose lazily, draped a robe loosely around himself, poured a cup of water, and helped the dazed woman drink a few sips — then pressed a kiss to those cherry-red lips.

She had already sunk into exhausted sleep. Xiao Huaiyan propped himself up at ease, a certain unhurried elegance about him, and leafed through the list of names invited to the detached palace. Jiang Mi's name was already there, written in long before.

……

Xiao Huaiyan woke from the dream, his expression still.

Last night's dream had been more outrageous than anything that had come before.

His resting chambers were impenetrable — how could anyone slip through his guard so soundlessly?

Were Li Fu and the Embroidered Guard dead?

Xiao Huaiyan pressed his palm against his brow and rubbed. Then he thought of how he had addressed Jiang Mi in the dream. Noble Consort Jiang?

So in the dream she had become one of his consorts? But why only the rank of Noble Consort?

He closed his eyes again. That helpless, boneless delicacy — the shyness, the vulnerability — was the absolute opposite of the Jiang Mi who turned and bolted the moment she saw him.

An anxious Li Fu kept peering into the inner bedchamber. Morning court was nearly upon them and His Majesty had still not risen — yet without being summoned, he couldn't very well walk in uninvited. His Majesty's temper in the mornings was not something to trifle with.

Just as he was debating what to do, he finally heard His Majesty call.

Li Fu exhaled and led the attendants in, carrying the court robes.

……

That morning, Jiang Mi woke very early.

She had spent the whole night tangled in muddled, disjointed dreams. At one point Xue Ningzhu was clinging to her in tears, crying, "Jiang Elder Sister, why have you stopped wearing the bracelet I gave you? Don't you like me anymore?" At another point her aunt was lecturing her: "Tangtang, why aren't you wearing the Emperor's blessing bangle? That is the imperial favor! You must wear it every single day!" And then there was Xiao Huaiyan with that not-quite-smile of his, asking, "Miss Jiang, you keep spinning lie after lie — are you sure you can keep all of them straight?"

Jiang Mi splashed cool water on her face and tried to clear her head.

She put both the bracelet and the bangle away. She wasn't going to wear either of them.

She sat distractedly at the dressing table, applying cream, when she caught sight of the bite scar on her finger. She stared at it for a moment, her mind going quiet.

Through careful concealment, even her aunt hadn't noticed this wound.

When Xiao Huaiyan had asked her whether it still hurt — she truly hadn't expected him to remember.

The memory of how he had rescued her at the Zhenguo Duke's household was something she had always avoided letting herself think through. She kept edging away from it.

In those moments when the drug had clouded her senses and she was half out of her mind with shame and terror, she had genuinely been afraid of him — even knowing he was the one who had saved her.

Yet she also understood that if Xiao Huaiyan had not happened to pass by that day — if it had not been for the Xue siblings — then even if she had kept her life, her reputation would have been destroyed. With the drug still working in her, if she had been brought out in front of people and lost control, she could not imagine what foul things would have been said about her.

The assassination attempt in the water and the drugging had been arranged to leave her no way out.

Of course she wanted to know who had put that drug in her drink. Besides the Xie family, who else wanted to harm her?

But it also meant she owed Xiao Huaiyan yet another debt.

Thinking of his words and actions last night, Jiang Mi felt a deep, complicated unease and pushed open the lattice window. The drizzle had finally stopped. The leaves and flowers held their drops of water. The sky wasn't quite so heavy, and where the clouds still covered the morning light, a sliver of dawn was beginning to push through.

Jiang Mi wrapped her cape around her and stepped outside.

The early morning air was clean and bracing — exactly what her head needed.

She had been carrying Head Physician Gu's advice with her all week: don't let worry pile up and fester into illness. She had been taking it seriously, making herself come out for a walk instead of shutting herself indoors.

She looked at the ginkgo leaves that had been blown down by the wind and rain, scattered across the ground, and felt a quiet melancholy.

In her past life, her body had been worn down by the weight she carried and by the disasters that befell the Jiang family. She had slipped away in an ordinary cold — never waiting for Xiao Huaiyan to return, never getting to say all the things that had accumulated inside her.

So many things were different from her past life now. Whatever came, it couldn't be worse than what had already been.

The sight of Xiao Huaiyan still made her heart race and her nerves fray. The fear of him seemed to have seeped into her bones.

Though she didn't want to admit it, his repeated probing suggested he had already sensed something was off. Yet "rebirth" and "returning from the dead" were such staggering impossibilities — he would never in his wildest thoughts land on the truth.

His current tests were nothing more than attempts to understand what she was after.

She had to be especially careful and steady.

Jiang Mi sighed inwardly again. When would she be able to face Xiao Huaiyan with genuine calm?

A ginkgo leaf still pearled with rain came spinning down. Jiang Mi held out her hand and it settled into her palm.

Behind her, a hurried voice called out. "Miss Jiang — Miss Jiang, please wait!"

She turned. It was Chengzhong.

Chengzhong came forward with a bow. "Miss Jiang, His Majesty requests your presence."

She could not refuse him outside the palace. She could not refuse him inside it either.

The ginkgo leaf fell silently from her hand. She kept her gaze down. "Please lead the way, Chief Eunuch."

She followed Chengzhong and found herself on the path toward the imperial study.

She stopped. "Chief Eunuch Cheng, this is not a place I should be going."

Chengzhong smiled pleasantly. "Miss Jiang, when His Majesty himself has summoned you, there is no place in this palace you cannot go."

Jiang Mi had a particular reluctance about the imperial study. In her past life, Xiao Huaiyan had done as he pleased there with no regard for propriety.

Li Fu, keeping watch outside the study doors, came to meet them when he saw the pair approaching. He smiled. "Miss Jiang, His Majesty is still in there finishing up with the ministers. Please come to the side room and rest a moment."

The side room was not unfamiliar to Jiang Mi.

In her past life she had slipped in quietly and waited here as well.

She picked up her tea and took a sip. She had the quiet, half-formed sense that this summons had something to do with who had drugged her.

Waiting was its own kind of slow torture, wearing down her composure.

At first she managed to stay calm. Gradually, a restless unease crept in.

The side room was so quiet the servants might not have been breathing.

After roughly the length of time it took to burn one stick of incense, Li Fu appeared. "Miss Jiang, if you would follow this servant."

The doors of the imperial study were pushed open.

Jiang Mi took in the arrangement of the room — nearly identical to how she remembered it from her past life.

This was meant to be a solemn, weighty place. And yet...

She pushed that thought away and gave a bow toward the man seated above. "Long live Your Majesty."

"Rise."

When she looked up, she found there was someone else in the room: Embroidered Guard Commander Pei Chi.

Jiang Mi gave him a small curtsy. Pei Chi stepped aside to avoid it.

Xiao Huaiyan, seeing that exceptionally proper posture, could not help calling to mind the Jiang Mi of his dream — hair half-undone, lying across this very desk...

He said, unhurried, "Miss Jiang need not stand on ceremony. Be seated."

Jiang Mi thanked him and sat.

Once she was settled, Xiao Huaiyan said, "Miss Jiang, didn't you want to know who else was behind what happened? Pei Chi — tell her what you found."

Pei Chi acknowledged the order and turned to face Jiang Mi.

"Miss Jiang, the drug administered to you that day is known as Spring Slumber. After tracing every purchase made of that substance, we determined that a maidservant from the Duke of Ying's household bought it just over a month ago. Further questioning revealed she was in the personal service of the heir's concubine, Bai — and this was not her first purchase. The maid who switched Miss Jiang's wine cup at the flower banquet was in the service of the Duke of Ying's fourth daughter, Xie Yanyue. Under interrogation she confessed that Xie Yanyue had visited Concubine Bai at the country estate several days earlier, at which time Bai passed the drug to Xie Yanyue. According to the maid's testimony, Bai's reasoning was this: if Miss Jiang were humiliated at the flower banquet and her reputation ruined, she would be unfit to enter the palace, and the shame would taint the entire Jiang family — giving Bai and her child a chance to return to the Duke of Ying's household. The Duchess of Ying appears to have been aware of this scheme and permitted it to proceed."

Jiang Mi's hands gripped each other tightly. She had never once suspected the Duke of Ying's household.

The Duke of Ying's household — and they still shared a family connection with the Marquis of Cheng'en's household through Cousin Wan's marriage. And they had been willing to go this far.

She thought back to the flower banquet. The Duchess of Ying had made a particular point of seeking out the Marchioness and speaking at length, even offering a cup of wine in apology. And Xie Yanyue had come to Jiang Mi herself, saying the Duke of Ying's household felt terrible about the wrong done to Cousin Wan, and asking Jiang Mi to help persuade her to come home sooner.

She had brushed off Xie Yanyue's overtures without much engagement.

Had that been when her cup was switched?

If she had not fallen into the lake — if the drug had taken full effect while she was still at the banquet and she had begun to shed her clothing in a stupor — what would the other ladies of the Jiang family have done? And if there had been further arrangements waiting — a man placed in position to destroy her reputation entirely — then what?

Xiao Huaiyan dismissed Pei Chi with a wave. Pei Chi bowed and withdrew.

Xiao Huaiyan looked at Jiang Mi's completely colorless face. "Miss Jiang — what do you wish to do?"

chapters.title: Thirty-nine
chapters.content:
What did she wish to do?

Jiang Mi heard the way Xiao Huaiyan had phrased the question — was he giving her a choice?

The fact that she had been drugged was not something that could be made public. Even now that she knew who was responsible, justice could only be sought quietly, behind closed doors. And any reckoning with the Duke of Ying's household — any real price they were made to pay — could not happen without going through Xiao Huaiyan.

Jiang Mi looked up at him and asked with some uncertainty, "Your Majesty will help this subject daughter?"

Xiao Huaiyan had half-expected this delicate young woman to crumble into frightened tears upon hearing all of it.

Yet though she was pale and red-eyed, she was, remarkably, still composed.

He asked, "What would you like Us to do?"

Jiang Mi rose from her seat and bowed to Xiao Huaiyan. "This subject daughter wants the people who did this to receive the punishment they deserve. And she wants her eldest cousin, Jiang Wan, to be able to formally separate from the Duke of Ying's heir, Xie Xi."

The second request was unexpected.

Xiao Huaiyan said, "The matter of severing the marriage tie between the Marquis of Cheng'en's household and the Duke of Ying's household — has Miss Jiang discussed this with the Empress Dowager?"

Jiang Mi did not answer. Xiao Huaiyan understood perfectly.

Jiang Mi's gaze dropped. "Your Majesty, may this subject daughter make one more request?"

Xiao Huaiyan said with patience, "Go ahead."

A flicker of hesitation crossed her eyes — but she said it. "This subject daughter does not want her aunt or her family to know she was drugged."

After a long silence, Xiao Huaiyan looked at her steadily. "Miss Jiang, do you not feel wronged?"

Jiang Mi went quiet. She tried to find the words, but they would not come.

Xiao Huaiyan turned the jade thumb ring on his finger. "Very well. We will see it done."

……

That night, Embroidered Guard Commander Pei Chi led his men to surround the Duke of Ying's household and forced their way inside.

Xie Yanyue was pacing her room in restless anxiety. When a maid came in, she asked immediately, "Have you found Qiaolan?"

The maid shook her head, her expression frightened. "Fourth Miss, the Duke requests you come at once."

Xie Yanyue frowned. What would her father want at this hour?

"Did he say anything?" she asked.

The maid shook her head quickly, not daring to say another word.

Xie Yanyue walked out, distracted, not noticing that the maid who had delivered the message was pale as ash and shaking.

As she made her way through the compound, she saw almost no servants about. Even at the entrance to her parents' main quarters, there was no maid to greet her at the door.

The moment she stepped into the main hall, her eyes went wide. The room was full of people kneeling on the floor.

Her father the Duke stood with a grim expression. Her mother's face was ashen. Her elder brother, the heir, stared blankly at nothing.

And seated to one side was a man in an embroidered flying-fish uniform. The aura around him was murderous. He looked up as Xie Yanyue walked in and pulled his lips into a thin smile. "My lord Duke, now we're all here."

Xie Yanyue froze, caught between advance and retreat.

The Duke snapped, "You wretched girl — get over here!"

She could only make her way forward on legs that didn't want to hold her.

As she drew closer, she saw that among those kneeling on the floor was not only Qiaolan — who she had been searching for — but also Cousin Bai, bound and brought here.

Bai's mouth had been stuffed with cloth. Tears streamed down her face.

Abruptly, a packet of powder was tossed down in front of Xie Yanyue. Pei Chi said with cool dispassion, "We'd like Miss Xie to identify this for us."

Xie Yanyue felt as though every drop of strength had been drained from her. She collapsed to the floor, recoiling from the powder as from a snake. "I — I don't know what that is."

Pei Chi gave a cold laugh. "No matter. Perhaps a stay in the imperial prison will help Miss Xie's memory."

Xie Yanyue crawled toward the Duchess, weeping. "Mother, help me — please, help me!"

The Duchess drew her sobbing daughter into her arms. "Lord Pei, why press so hard?" she said, her voice edged with resentment.

Pei Chi did not look at her. He addressed the Duke instead. "My lord Duke, this matter has already been handled with more consideration for the Duke of Ying's household than strictly necessary. Please do not make a mockery of the Emperor's grace."

The Duke's face twitched. He closed his eyes. "Someone come. Put the fourth daughter in a carriage and send her to Qingzhou. She is never to return to the capital."

Xie Yanyue could not believe what she was hearing. She cried out in disbelief. "Father!"

The Duchess clutched her daughter tight. "My lord — Yanyue is my only daughter. Send her to the country estate if you must — she'll repent, she'll change. I beg you, please don't send Yanyue to Qingzhou!"

A daughter of a Duke's household, exiled to Qingzhou for life, never permitted to return to the capital — that was being cast out by the family entirely. Before long the Duke would likely announce to the world that Yanyue had died of a sudden illness. And then there would be no more Fourth Miss Xie Yanyue in this world — stripped of her title, with no good life waiting for her in Qingzhou.

The Duke closed his eyes against the pain. "My lady, your constitution is poor. Going forward, the household management will be handled by my second brother's wife. Return to your rooms and rest."

The Duchess went cold all over. She had no illness whatsoever — he was putting her under house arrest, and dressing it up.

"No — my lord, I am not ill..." Her face had gone white with dread. If she were confined to her rooms, the concubines who had always been kept at bay would have free rein in the household.

The Duke steeled himself and shouted at the servants standing nearby, "What are you waiting for? Take the Fourth Miss and the Duchess away!"

Just as the servants moved to separate Xie Yanyue and the Duchess, Pei Chi said lightly, "There's no need to rush. Perhaps the Duchess and Miss Xie would prefer to remain and witness the proceedings before they leave. It would be awkward for me otherwise."

Those words landed like a stone in still water. Everyone in the hall went rigid.

Concubine Bai, bound on the floor, began struggling frantically. She turned her tear-streaked face toward Xie Xi and let out a muffled, desperate cry for help.

Xie Xi's heart contracted with pain. He had taken two steps toward her when an Embroidered Guard stepped in front of him, blade drawn.

Pei Chi rose from his seat at an unhurried pace. "Officers — drag the condemned out and beat them to death."

The people on their knees wailed and pleaded — but it did not stop the Embroidered Guard from coming forward to drag them away.

Xie Xi was helpless. He watched Bai be hauled away by the guards.

Even with their mouths stopped, the sound of the plank falling again and again — heavy, merciless blows — reached them through the walls in a series of wretched, keening cries.

Xie Xi's jaw was clenched so tight his teeth ground. "Pei Chi, you will come to no good end."

Pei Chi gave a short laugh. "Among everyone in this court who wishes me a bad death, My Lord Heir is certainly not the only one."

He watched the condemned go still one by one, then looked back at the Duke of Ying's heir, who had not dared take a single step outside the hall. He said pleasantly, "My Lord Heir, do remember to deliver the formal separation document to the Marquis of Cheng'en's household. Don't forget."

Then he turned to the Duke. "My lord Duke — I trust you know which things should be spoken of and which should not."

Having said his piece, Pei Chi led the Embroidered Guard out of the Duke of Ying's household.

Xie Yanyue had fainted dead away when the punishment began. The Duchess lay crumpled on the floor, blank-faced and trembling, as though she had been transported straight to the underworld.

Xie Xi could not bring himself to look in the direction where Bai had been dragged. He did not want to imagine what had become of her.

The Duke swept a glance over the Duchess's form on the floor and said, his voice stripped of warmth, "How many times did I tell you both not to act rashly. You refused to listen. Is this the outcome you wanted?"

In that moment, the Duke looked as though he had aged several years all at once.

……

Deep in the night, the moon was swallowed by clouds. Layer upon layer of black cloud split with lightning, and the rumble of thunder crashed so close it seemed to burst right beside the ear.

Jiang Mi, a light sleeper at the best of times, was jolted awake.

She burrowed deeper into her covers and pressed her hands over her ears.

She didn't know how much time passed before that round of thunder finally moved on, and then the patter of rain came — a heavy, rushing autumn downpour.

Sleep was gone. She draped her cape over her shoulders, slipped on her embroidered shoes, pushed open the lattice window, and looked out into the solid blackness, listening to the rain on the leaves, her mind drifting to places she couldn't quite name.

……

Xiao Huaiyan was dreaming and knew even within the dream that the thunder had sounded. The warm, soft figure curled in the crook of his arm burrowed deeper against him. He tightened his arm around her, and felt hands reach around his waist in return. He gave a low, quiet laugh and absently patted her back.

Another crack of thunder split the air. Xiao Huaiyan, still half in the dream, reached to soothe the person in his arms — his arm closed around nothing but empty air.

He opened his eyes, brow furrowed.

The thunder had passed. The rain came down.

Sleep would not return. He pushed back the covers, got up, pushed open the window, and stood watching the autumn rain fall in silence.

……

One autumn rain, one fresh chill. Jiang Mi put on an extra layer when she woke, propped herself against the daybed cushions, and read.

The rain outside had not let up, so she did not go for her walk.

When she had read enough, she closed her eyes to rest them.

Cainan came in softly. "Miss, the Marchioness of Cheng'en has come to the palace. The Empress Dowager would like you to come over."

After a pause, Jiang Mi said, "I understand."

On the way there, she thought: could this be about the Duke of Ying's household?

So soon?

She had barely stepped into the inner chamber when she heard her great-aunt's voice. "Your Ladyship the Empress Dowager, I am at a loss to understand what the Duke of Ying's household intends. Early this morning the Duke himself came with the heir and delivered a formal separation document. The Duke also said that Concubine Bai has been dealt with, that the household management has been handed over to the second branch's wife, and that the Duchess is unwell. And yet if all of that is so, why would they present Wan'er with a separation document? I could not decide what to do on my own, so I came straight to Your Ladyship."

The Marchioness had just finished speaking when she heard footsteps and turned to see Jiang Mi come in.

Jiang Mi greeted the Empress Dowager and then curtsied to the Marchioness.

The Marchioness took her hand, her eyes soft with concern. "Tangtang, you've grown so thin."

The Empress Dowager said, "The child had shock on top of fright on top of a high fever — of course she's thinner. Come, Tangtang, sit down."

The Marchioness remembered that pleasantries would have to wait — the serious matter had to be settled first.

Jiang Wan had been longing for this separation document with everything she had. But without the Empress Dowager's word, the Marchioness did not dare let Wan'er sign it.

Both the Marchioness and the Empress Dowager spoke openly in front of Jiang Mi.

The Empress Dowager asked, "You're saying that last night the Embroidered Guard Commander Pei Chi visited the Duke of Ying's household?"

The Marchioness nodded. "This is what my lord husband heard. We don't know exactly what happened, but the Embroidered Guard apparently surrounded the entire compound and forced their way in."

The Empress Dowager thought: what reason would Pei Chi have to march openly into the Duke of Ying's household?

She asked, "Were you able to find out what the matter was?"

The Marchioness shook her head.

The marriage alliance with the Duke of Ying's household had not been easily won — which was why even knowing Jiang Wan was suffering, it had been so hard to let go of it. But now the Duke himself had come in person with the heir and the separation document. Whether she wanted it broken or not, it was broken.

The Empress Dowager sighed. "Since the Duke came forward himself, let it be done. See to Ah'Wan — comfort her, tell her not to dwell on it, and focus on her health."

The Marchioness kept the relief from her face and acknowledged this quietly.

At this point Jiang Mi spoke. "Aunt, I'd like to go home and see Cousin Wan."

The Empress Dowager paused for a moment. "Very well. It will do you two sisters good to talk."

chapters.title: Forty
chapters.content:
The Marchioness personally saw Jiang Mi as far as Yuanzhi Courtyard.

"Tangtang, rest first," she said. "I'll go now and relay the Empress Dowager's word to your uncle so Wan'er can sign the document."

Jiang Mi nodded. "Cousin Wan's matter comes first, Great-aunt. Please go. I'll visit her tomorrow."

The Marchioness patted her hand and left.

Qiuyu and the others came rushing forward with tears in their eyes. "Miss, you're finally back."

Jiang Mi smiled. Yes, she was back. She could breathe again.

At her legs, Miangmiang had been circling and circling — and now, getting no attention, let out an insistent meow.

Jiang Mi bent down and scooped her up, ruffling the thick, fluffy fur. "Miangmiang, I was only gone a few days. Have you gotten fatter?"

Miangmiang gave one mew, and her long tail swept across Jiang Mi's arm.

She had barely settled back into her room when Lady Su arrived with Jiang Rong and Jiang Xuan.

Lady Su sent over a number of tonics and supplements and told Jiang Mi to take proper care of herself.

Jiang Rong clutched Jiang Mi's hand with red-rimmed eyes and would not let go.

Jiang Xuan, not quite understanding any of it, ran around the room chasing Miangmiang.

Lady Su watched this with a mild headache, then said to Jiang Mi, "Third Miss, as they say, great danger survived brings great fortune after. You are a person of good fortune. But a woman's body after a fall into water needs careful tending — it should not be taken lightly. I have found a female physician who specializes in women's health and recovery. If you are willing, I'll have her come and see you tomorrow."

Jiang Mi was a little surprised. Lady Su had always ensured she lacked for nothing in the way of food, clothing, and daily comforts — gave her the very best of everything. But in other matters she had always kept a careful, respectful distance.

Jiang Mi said, "You've gone to a lot of trouble, ma'am."

Lady Su was visibly pleased that Jiang Mi had accepted. "Think nothing of it, Third Miss."

Jiang Mi smiled. "Please, call me Tangtang."

This left Lady Su feeling unexpectedly moved. She knew that the nickname was something only those who were close to Jiang Mi were permitted to use. She had always kept a polite distance from this Third Miss — treating her with deference but keeping her arm's length.

But over these past weeks, she had watched her own two children grow closer and closer to this elder sister. And the Third Miss had been nothing but patient and kind with them.

Jiang Rong, who was naturally reserved and quiet, had been smiling more since she and the Third Miss began spending time together.

Though they were half-sisters, not full, they were sisters all the same. Lady Su had not expected her introverted daughter to lean on this elder sister so readily.

What had happened at the Zhenguo Duke's household had filled Lady Su with sympathy for Jiang Mi. Those undeserved calamities had all come from the struggle for position in the inner palace — and she hadn't even entered the palace yet. Once she was inside, there was no telling how much harder it would get.

Even with the Empress Dowager watching over her, a person couldn't be guarded forever.

Jiang Mi had lost her mother while she was still young. There were things — the private matters that concerned women, the ways to care for herself — that no one had ever taught her. Whatever Lady Su could do to help, she would.

She thought of her own Ah-Rong, and felt that she hoped her daughter would marry into a simple, uncomplicated household. Not looking for prestige — only for a peaceful and steady life.

Lady Su drew Jiang Rong away. "Ah-Rong, let your elder sister rest."

Jiang Mi squeezed Jiang Rong's hand. "Don't worry, Ah-Rong. I'm fine now."

Jiang Rong reluctantly rose, and said in a small voice, "I'll come find you later, Elder Sister."

Jiang Mi nodded.

Jiang Xuan had given up chasing Miangmiang by now and came running to Jiang Mi. "Elder Sister, when you're all better, let Miangmiang play with me. She only listens to you."

He was still young enough to know only that Elder Sister had been sick and that their aunt had taken her to the palace to recover.

Jiang Mi ruffled his hair and said with a smile, "All right."

After Lady Su's household left, Jiang Mi sprawled lazily on the daybed.

Qiuyu stood beside her. "Miss, while you were in the palace, several households sent things over. The Zhenguo Duke's household sent the most — they said it was an apology to you. The Marchioness didn't feel right refusing, so everything was brought to the courtyard. I've made a full record."

Jiang Mi glanced through the list and paused on one name.

Grand Secretary Shen's household — Shen Yaowei?

The Shen family had sent the usual assortment of gifts, but tucked among them were also a few books.

"Bring me the books the Shen family sent."

Qiuyu went to fetch them at once.

Jiang Mi looked at the familiar covers. They were books by Master Songxun.

She picked one up and examined it — and found that one of them was a handwritten manuscript.

She turned the first few pages, and a small painting fell out.

It depicted a tree hung all over with red prayer ribbons.

It was Shen Qianxiu's work.

Jiang Mi stared at the manuscript in her hands, with its elegant, distinctive brushwork. Was this Shen Qianxiu's own handwriting?

The text contained his observations and impressions from his time in Luoyang. The manuscript appeared to be unpublished.

Could Shen Qianxiu actually be Master Songxun?

She looked at the painting in her hand for a long time, lost in thought.

Shen Qianxiu had sent these things under Shen Yaowei's name. What did he mean by it?

Shen Qianxiu — the man who had placed first in all three imperial examinations, the future top scholar. His younger sister had already been internally designated as Empress by Xiao Huaiyan, and he himself was held in high regard by the Emperor. His future was all but limitless.

When she had been at Qianshuang Temple, she had deliberately allowed Shen Qianxiu to come to her aid — she had been thinking something then, some small calculation.

But at the Zhenguo Duke's flower banquet, when she had felt the gaze of the young woman sitting beside Shen Yaowei — she had cooled on that thought somewhat.

Now, though, she was struggling under the relentless pressure of Xiao Huaiyan's scrutiny. If Shen Qianxiu truly had feelings for her, did that give her another option?

Jiang Mi sank back into her old tangle of worries. Even if Shen Qianxiu had feelings for her, her aunt would never allow it. It would likely outright anger her aunt. The only approach that had any chance would be going through her father — getting her father to agree, and quietly arranging a betrothal.

But would her father agree? Would the Shen family agree? A betrothal arranged in secret — would the Shen family really go about something this important so quietly?

She shook her head.

Jiang Mi, she told herself sternly. A man sends you a few books and you're grasping at straws already?

If only someone in a position of power could issue a marriage decree directly.

The only people who could do that were her aunt and Xiao Huaiyan.

She was clearly feverish to even be thinking like this.

Her aunt would never issue a decree like that. And as for Xiao Huaiyan — unless he chose on his own initiative to make such a promise, she had better stop dreaming.

She should think, instead, about what kind of gift she owed Xiao Huaiyan.

By evening, her mood had not improved.

Every time she thought back on Xiao Huaiyan's behavior in the palace, the unease returned.

She had thought that being home, in her own courtyard, she might sleep better. But with that weight still pressing on her chest, real peace was impossible.

Jiang Mi took down her hair, removed her earrings, and moved toward the bed.

Qiuyu came in. "Miss, the Fourth Miss is here."

Jiang Mi looked surprised. "This late — why has Ah-Rong come? Bring her in quickly."

A moment later Jiang Rong came in, wrapped in a pale yellow cape, looking a little uncertain.

She said to Jiang Mi, her cheeks going faintly pink, "Elder Sister — can I sleep here with you?"

Jiang Mi's eyes widened slightly. She had not expected this request.

She laughed softly and beckoned Jiang Rong closer, undoing the clasp of her cape for her. "Come on then, before you catch cold."

Jiang Rong was overjoyed. She slipped off her outer robe and embroidered shoes and climbed onto the bed.

Sharing a bed with a sister was something Jiang Mi had almost no experience with. In her past life the only time had been with Consort Duan — the elder sister figure who had been the one truly kind person the inner palace had offered her. Consort Duan had died of a sudden illness even before Jiang Mi herself had, which made her loss all the harder.

Jiang Mi lay down. Jiang Rong pressed close against her, small hand looping around Jiang Mi's arm. She murmured softly, "Elder Sister, you smell nice. It's lovely."

Jiang Mi smiled and touched her face — and felt the warmth of a tear against her fingers.

She wrapped an arm around Jiang Rong and said gently, "Ah-Rong, I'm all right. Don't blame yourself."

Jiang Rong sniffled, her voice too thick to speak.

She had been having nightmares every night — dreaming of the moment Elder Sister was pushed into the water, herself completely powerless. Every time she woke from it she wondered: if she hadn't been knocked down, if she had gotten up faster, if she had grabbed her a second sooner--

Waiting for news of Elder Sister in Rong'an Hall had been agony.

Even that frightening official — the one who looked like he had walked out of a nightmare — she had decided in her heart was a good person, simply because he was going to find Elder Sister.

Only holding onto her sister now made the world feel solid again.

Jiang Mi felt a quiet, bittersweet ache. She held Jiang Rong close, and they warmed each other.

On a night when her heart was full of worry and no easy answers, having Jiang Rong beside her — she found her way into sleep.

……

The next morning, Jiang Mi woke before Jiang Rong.

Jiang Rong slept very neatly, curled small and still. And for the first time in many nights, Jiang Mi found that she had slept well.

At the first stir from Jiang Mi, Jiang Rong blinked awake and rubbed her eyes.

She looked at Jiang Mi with a slightly bashful smile. "Good morning, Elder Sister."

Jiang Mi called her up, and together they washed, dressed, and had breakfast.

After the meal, Jiang Rong had to go to her lessons, so she took her leave.

Jiang Mi spent a little while playing with Miangmiang, then headed to the main branch to visit Jiang Wan.

This time, Dong'er came out to meet her with a bright, untroubled face. "Third Miss, you're here! My mistress has been asking after you all morning."

Jiang Mi pressed her lips together in a smile. Things were different now that the separation had gone through — even the way Dong'er referred to Jiang Wan had changed.

She walked inside. The room still carried the faint smell of medicine, and Jiang Wan was still resting in bed.

But the light in her was utterly transformed. Her face held color, her eyes were bright and clear and resolute.

The moment she saw Jiang Mi, Jiang Wan smiled before she spoke.

Jiang Mi said, "Congratulations on your new beginning, Cousin Wan. You got what you wished for after all."

Jiang Wan took her hand. "Tangtang, I never dared believe it would happen so quickly. It felt like a dream."

"Cousin Wan's dream has come true. Are you happy?"

Jiang Wan nodded over and over. "Nothing could make me happier. Before, I had no choice. From now on, I make my own choices."

Jiang Mi watched her with a quiet envy that she barely let herself feel. How wonderful — at least one of them had escaped the cage.

Jiang Wan pulled Jiang Mi down to sit on the edge of the bed beside her. Her smile faded as she studied her carefully. "Tangtang — I've heard about everything that happened at the Zhenguo Duke's household. Do you... do you still want to enter the palace?"

Jiang Mi lowered her eyes. "Cousin Wan, if I said I was frightened — would there be any choice left for me?"

Jiang Wan felt a wave of sadness. For women of the Jiang family, there had never been a question of what they wanted.

Jiang Wan had escaped through sheer chance. For Jiang Mi...

For a moment neither of them spoke.

Then Dong'er came in to announce, "Third Miss, the heir of the Zhenguo Duke's household and Miss Xue Ningzhu have arrived. They are in the Marchioness's courtyard just now. The Marchioness asks if you would come over."

chapters.title: Forty-one
chapters.content:
The moment Jiang Mi stepped into the main hall, Xue Ningzhu wanted to rush toward her, but Xue Jinglin's hand pressed down on Xue Ningzhu's shoulder, keeping her seated properly in her chair.

Jiang Mi smiled at Xue Ningzhu, then paid her respects to the Marquess of Cheng'en's wife, and greeted the young lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor.

The Marquess's wife said, "Tangtan, the young lord and Miss Xue have come especially to see you. Miss Xue in particular has been waiting most anxiously."

The Marquess's wife looked at Jiang Mi with a face full of warmth. As she spoke, the little Xue girl shook off the young lord's hand and came trotting over to Jiang Mi.

Xue Ningzhu cautiously tugged at Jiang Mi's sleeve, looked up, and blinked. "Sister Jiang, you're not upset with me, are you?"

Jiang Mi laughed. "What are you saying, child? Why would I be upset with you?"

Only then did Xue Ningzhu relax. She took Jiang Mi's hand. "Sister Jiang, take me to your courtyard. I have so much to tell you, and I want to play with Mianmian too."

"Of course." Jiang Mi had known Xue Ningzhu was coming and had already planned to bring her over. This little girl had long been asking to visit.

Xue Jinglin looked helpless, and apologized to Jiang Mi. "Ningzhu is too willful. I have caused Miss Jiang trouble."

Jiang Mi said, "Young Lord, you speak too seriously. Ningzhu is innocent and lively — she is a delight."

Xue Ningzhu said to Xue Jinglin, "Young Lord Brother, see? I told you Sister Jiang wouldn't mind."

The Marquess's wife, seeing that the Xue girl was eager to follow Jiang Mi, said tactfully, "Tangtan, take Miss Xue to Yuanzhi Courtyard."

Jiang Mi nodded in assent. "Great Aunt, then Ningzhu and I shall take our leave first."

Xue Jinglin bowed to the Marquess's wife. "My Lady, I will see Ningzhu off and come to collect her at dusk."

The Marquess's wife smiled. "Please, Young Lord, as you wish."

Watching the figures depart, the Marquess's wife could not help but think that the young lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor had the bearing of an orchid-jade tree — modest and courteous. Even if his health was somewhat frail, he would make a fine husband for someone.

One could only wonder which young lady would be so fortunate.

Xue Jinglin followed Jiang Mi and Xue Ningzhu as far as the floral gate, and then stopped.

He bent down to instruct Xue Ningzhu, "Mind what Sister Jiang says. Do not cause trouble."

Xue Ningzhu smiled up at him. "Young Lord Brother, if you don't trust me, why not come along and stay in Sister Jiang's courtyard to keep an eye on me yourself?"

Xue Jinglin reached out and pinched Xue Ningzhu's cheek. "Nonsense! When we get home, I'll have Grandmother give you a proper scolding."

Xue Ningzhu was not the least bit afraid — she giggled.

Xue Jinglin felt his head ache. He gave a low cough, then bowed to Jiang Mi. "My little sister is unruly. I ask Miss Jiang for her patience."

Seeing how well the siblings got along, Jiang Mi smiled. "Young Lord, rest assured. When you come to collect Ningzhu, I will return her to you exactly as she is."

Xue Jinglin heard the teasing in Jiang Mi's words. He suppressed the urge to cough and broke into a warm smile. "Then I shall certainly keep that appointment."

Xue Jinglin was naturally handsome with a remarkable bearing, and when he smiled, it was like ice and snow melting — carrying a warmth with it.

......

Jiang Mi brought Xue Ningzhu back to Yuanzhi Courtyard. The moment Xue Ningzhu spotted Mianmian dozing on the rug, she ran straight over.

Jiang Mi noticed she was only crouching nearby and watching, wanting to touch but holding herself back.

Jiang Mi instructed the maid Qiuyu, "Have the small kitchen prepare some cakes and snacks."

Qiuyu nodded and went to carry out the order.

Jiang Mi walked over to Xue Ningzhu and asked, "What is it? Didn't you say you wanted to see Mianmian?"

Xue Ningzhu said, "Sister Jiang, I was waiting for you to come. We haven't seen Mianmian for so long — what if I scare it by touching it? And I wonder if it still remembers me."

Jiang Mi thought that Xue Ningzhu, being twelve years old, was far more childlike than A'rong. It was endearing.

Jiang Mi fetched two strips of dried meat. She handed one to Xue Ningzhu. "Feed this to Mianmian, and it'll warm right up to you. Mianmian is just like you — easy to win over."

Xue Ningzhu laughed happily.

She muttered to herself, "I am not that easy to win over."

After Xue Ningzhu had fed one strip of dried meat, Mianmian was already willing to be held in her arms.

The little creature was perfectly agreeable the moment there was food involved.

Holding Mianmian, Xue Ningzhu sidled up to Jiang Mi and peered over. "Sister Jiang, are you tracing a pattern for embroidery?"

Jiang Mi nodded. She continued sketching the lines and asked, "Is it pretty?"

Xue Ningzhu looked at the vivid peony and praised, "It's beautiful. Sister Jiang's hands are so skilled."

Jiang Mi smiled gently. "Do you like it? Shall I embroider it onto a handkerchief for you?"

Xue Ningzhu's eyes went wide with delight. "Really? You're drawing this for me?"

"Of course." When Jiang Mi had received Xue Ningzhu's pearl bracelet, she had been thinking of a gift in return. She would first embroider a handkerchief herself as a token of friendship, and then prepare a more precious gift for Xue Ningzhu's birthday.

Xue Ningzhu suddenly found Mianmian far less interesting, and had a maid bring over an embroidery stool so she could sit beside Jiang Mi and watch her trace the pattern.

After noon, having eaten lunch, Xue Ningzhu — who was in the habit of taking an afternoon nap — lay down. Jiang Mi tucked the covers around her and returned to the writing desk to trace another embroidery pattern.

Since she was already making a handkerchief for Xue Ningzhu, she would embroider one for A'rong as well.

By the time Xue Ningzhu woke, Jiang Rong had come over with Jiang Xuan.

Jiang Rong, as usual, stayed beside Jiang Mi to practice calligraphy.

Xue Ningzhu had watched Jiang Rong writing for a short while and tried her hand at it too, but she could not sit still for long and ended up playing with little Xuan.

The two of them took Mianmian out to play in the courtyard.

Seeing this, Jiang Mi's misgivings deepened.

As dusk approached, the time came for Young Lord Xue to collect Ningzhu, and Jiang Mi went to see her off.

Xue Ningzhu was reluctant to leave. "Sister Jiang, come home with me."

Jiang Mi stroked her head. "Next time Ningzhu can come visit again."

Xue Ningzhu pouted as if on the verge of tears and refused to get in the carriage.

Xue Jinglin walked over and told the maid standing behind Xue Ningzhu, "Help the young lady into the carriage."

Xue Ningzhu refused to budge. She clung to Jiang Mi's hand and implored Xue Jinglin, "Young Lord Brother, please let Sister Jiang come back with me."

Xue Jinglin said sternly, "Ningzhu, behave yourself. Don't make things difficult for Miss Jiang."

Seeing the displeasure on her brother's face, Xue Ningzhu reluctantly let go.

Even after boarding the carriage, Xue Ningzhu could not help lifting the curtain to call out to Jiang Mi, "Sister Jiang, I'll come again next time!"

Jiang Mi smiled and said she would be welcome.

Xue Jinglin furrowed his brows slightly. He hesitated, then addressed Jiang Mi. "Miss Jiang, might I have a word with you in private?"

Jiang Mi assumed it concerned Xue Ningzhu and did not refuse.

Xue Jinglin said apologetically, "I have made Miss Jiang laugh at us."

Jiang Mi shook her head. "Young Lord, are you here to speak about Ningzhu?"

Xue Jinglin sighed. "I expect you have already noticed something. Ningzhu was not well from the time she was born and was taken to Jiangnan by our grandmother to recover. Later, we discovered that Ningzhu's mind seemed to have remained at the level of a young child. She has never had many friends, and fearing that others might notice, we never allowed her to become close with other young ladies. So the fact that she gets along so well with Miss Jiang has surprised all of us greatly. If Ningzhu has been in any way improper, I offer my apologies on her behalf."

Xue Jinglin bowed. Jiang Mi stepped to the side to deflect the gesture.

"Young Lord, if Ningzhu and I are well-suited to each other, it is simply fate. She has been nothing but proper. If it is convenient for you, please bring her to visit again."

Xue Jinglin looked at her with a distant gaze, then gave a quiet smile. "Very well. We will certainly impose on you again."

......

Jiang Mi passed two peaceful days, and felt the gloom of recent events lifting somewhat. Her spirits lightened a little.

The autumn sun was warm on her skin. Jiang Mi was embroidering a handkerchief, and the room was very quiet.

Footsteps sounded from outside. Thinking it was Qiuyu, Jiang Mi looked up.

In that instant, a sharp pain pricked her fingertip, and a bead of blood rolled out, staining the handkerchief.

Chengzhong wore a smile on his face and said respectfully, "Miss Jiang, His Majesty requests your presence."

Jiang Mi had thought that by leaving the palace, she might be able to hide for a while.

She had not expected that what was meant to come would come regardless.

Jiang Mi slowly rose, set down what was in her hands, and wordlessly followed Chengzhong out.

As before, no one noticed as she went. She boarded the carriage and sat quietly, listening to the sounds outside.

Perhaps because she had been through so many frights of this kind, she was somewhat calmer than the last time.

Though she hoped the journey would be long, the carriage inevitably stopped before the gate of that private residence.

Chengzhong stole a few glances at Miss Jiang. He found it rather strange — in the past, Miss Jiang would ask a question or two along the way, but this time, she had not uttered a single word.

Chengzhong led Jiang Mi into a room. "Miss Jiang, please wait here a moment. His Majesty will arrive shortly."

Jiang Mi's heart grew more anxious still.

Waiting would erode what little courage she had.

Jiang Mi looked around the room, and her expression gradually shifted to one of wonder. The walls were hung all around with paintings — a night banquet scene, landscapes, beauties — and on closer inspection, every one of them was an original work by a great master of previous generations.

Were these paintings simply... hanging here?

Even a genuine piece by Master Yunyin of the previous dynasty was among them.

Jiang Mi had once copied a reproduction of Master Yunyin's work, but had never seen an original.

She was so absorbed in looking that she did not notice someone walking toward her.

Xiao Huaiyan looked at Jiang Mi's profile and spoke. "So captivating?"

Jiang Mi's scalp tingled. Half her body seemed to go numb.

Xiao Huaiyan was standing no more than two or three paces away.

Jiang Mi stepped back and curtsied. "Long live Your Majesty."

Xiao Huaiyan laughed softly. "Rise."

Seeing Jiang Mi standing with her eyes downcast, Xiao Huaiyan noted that the brightness in her eyes as she looked at the paintings had been entirely concealed.

Xiao Huaiyan said, "Is Miss Jiang uncomfortable now that I am here?"

"This subject would not dare," Jiang Mi replied.

Xiao Huaiyan said, "We think Miss Jiang dares quite a bit — slipping out of the palace without a sound. Has she forgotten something?"

Jiang Mi thought to herself: it really was this. There was no avoiding it.

She said quietly, "This subject has not forgotten. It is only that I had not yet thought of a fitting gift of thanks for Your Majesty, and was too ashamed to face you."

Xiao Huaiyan's gaze swept over her. "If the young lady truly cannot think of one, We can suggest something."

Jiang Mi's heart lurched. Could Xiao Huaiyan really mean that so generously?

She heard Xiao Huaiyan pause, and then say in an unhurried tone, "Miss Jiang could perform a dance as her gift of thanks."

Jiang Mi was left utterly dumbfounded.

A dance?

Xiao Huaiyan wanted her to dance?

Jiang Mi panicked. She bit her lip. "Your Majesty, this subject... does not know how to dance."

Her aunt had once told her: her ability to dance was something to be revealed only after entering the palace, as a means of gaining the Emperor's favor. Before entering the palace, no one outside of those who knew was to know that she could dance.

chapters.title: Forty-two
chapters.content:
"You don't know how to dance?" A glint of amusement crossed Xiao Huaiyan's eyes.

Jiang Mi would not admit, under any circumstances, that she was skilled in dance.

"Your Majesty must be mistaken. This subject does not know how to dance." Jiang Mi's heart was pounding. She could not understand why Xiao Huaiyan would bring up dancing.

Why would he think she could dance?

Xiao Huaiyan said with a note of regret, "Is that so? The Empress Dowager has spoken to me at length in praise of you. Perhaps I did indeed misremember."

Though those were the words from his mouth, his gaze moved slowly and deliberately over Jiang Mi, inch by inch.

In that dream, she had worn golden jade bells at her ankles, swaying her snow-white waist with languid, alluring eyes — her skin like snow, wrapped in only a few scant layers of silk.

Her voice soft and sweet as honey.

Jiang Mi waited for a long while, but Xiao Huaiyan said nothing more. The room was so quiet that even the sound of the wind could be heard.

Jiang Mi felt a shadow fall over her. She looked up, and met those deep, unfathomable phoenix eyes directly.

Without realizing it, the two of them had drawn close enough to feel each other's breath.

Jiang Mi held her breath, resisting the encroaching scent of dragon saliva incense.

Seeing Xiao Huaiyan raise his hand toward her face, she could endure it no longer. She pushed forward with both hands, stepped back several paces, and called out sharply, "Your Majesty."

It was meant as a warning, but her voice trembled — coming out soft and weak.

Xiao Huaiyan frowned, and in an instant, came back to his senses.

He had just been bewitched — confused between the dream and reality.

The same word — "Your Majesty."

In the dream, it was sweet and clinging.

Here and now, though still soft, it carried fear.

The same face, yet utterly different in disposition.

Xiao Huaiyan composed himself and walked to the writing desk to sit down. As if nothing had happened, he said calmly, "Would Miss Jiang be so kind as to retrieve a painting from the third shelf of the bookcase."

With Xiao Huaiyan at a greater distance, Jiang Mi's breathing finally eased.

She glanced at the bookcase — it was rather high. She would have to stand on her toes to reach.

She grumbled inwardly: he really does know how to make things difficult.

But as long as he did not bring up the dancing again, fetching a painting was nothing.

Jiang Mi stood on the tips of her toes, stretched out her arm, and reached up toward the shelf.

Xiao Huaiyan watched in silence as she rose onto her toes. In the dream, the young woman had worn bells at her bare feet, pushing off on the tips of her toes, her whole body spinning like a flying bell, while her legs remained within the span of a single palm's width.

Now, Jiang Mi appeared to be searching for the scroll with some effort, yet her raised toes did not waver in the slightest. That kind of steadiness was not possible without years of training.

This was hardly what one would expect from someone who claimed not to know how to dance.

After a lengthy search, Jiang Mi finally found a long scroll. She struggled to bring it down.

She held the scroll in both hands and walked to the writing desk. "Your Majesty, the painting you requested."

Xiao Huaiyan said, "Open it."

Jiang Mi untied the binding and spread the painting slowly across the desk.

Before them unfurled a peony painting in full, radiant bloom.

A subtle stillness crossed Jiang Mi's expression. This was the last painting Master Yunqing had left before his death — the Peony Beauty.

There was no beauty in the painting. Only peonies.

Yet Master Yunqing had said that this painting depicted the most beautiful woman in the world.

Strangely enough, those who had looked at this peony painting long enough claimed they truly could see a woman within it.

Some said Master Yunqing had painted a beloved female companion; others said it was his young cousin who had died in her youth; still others said it was his devoted wife.
