Each had their own interpretation. The painting had taken on an almost legendary quality, and different people saw different beauties within the peony.

After Master Yunqing's death, the painting had vanished.

No one knew how it had ended up in Xiao Huaiyan's possession.

In her previous life, she had once borrowed this painting from Xiao Huaiyan to copy. But thinking of what he had done to her, a flash of shame and fury crossed her eyes. Xiao Huaiyan was dissolute and had humiliated her.

Now here she was again, admiring this painting in Xiao Huaiyan's company, and every part of her felt ill at ease.

Xiao Huaiyan's hand rested lightly on the painting. Without looking up, he gazed at the petals and asked, "What does Miss Jiang think of this painting?"

"A work by Master Yunqing. The artistry is breathtaking." Jiang Mi replied in measured, conventional terms.

Xiao Huaiyan gave a soft laugh. "And can Miss Jiang see the beauty within it?"

Jiang Mi glanced at the painting, then lowered her eyes. "This subject is dull-witted. I still only see a peony."

Xiao Huaiyan looked up at her expression, which showed nothing unusual, and could not help but think of the dream the day before.

In the dream, he had barely stepped into the Hengwu Palace when she invited him to look at a painting she had done — a copy of Master Yunqing's Peony Beauty.

"Your Majesty, do you think I have captured even a fraction of its spirit?"

Her voice had carried a hint of nervousness, yet her face had shone with expectation.

He had said deliberately at the time, "It still lacks a certain allure."

Seeing her puzzled expression, he had beckoned her over and pulled her onto his lap.

He had taken the brush whose ink was not yet dry, loosened her collar, and set the brush to that trembling, snow-white skin.

With every stroke of the brush, that body could not help but shiver, her waist twisting as she tried to pull away.

One hand painting, the other pressing down on that slender waist. When he was finished, that exquisite face was flushed crimson, apricot eyes brimming with a mist of tears, the painted peony peeking from beneath her collar — far more vivid and intoxicating than any painting.

The most beautiful color in the world was right before his eyes.

He had carried her to the mirror, his voice low. "Lady Jiang, look — what do you think of this peony? Do you see the beauty now?"

The woman in his arms caught sight of herself in the mirror — and rather than being struck with wonder, her barely-held-back expression crumbled entirely, and she burst into tears.

Tsk.

Delicate and prone to weeping — quite something.

......

Xiao Huaiyan looked up again at the young woman standing before him — excessively restrained, adhering to propriety in every move.

She had shown no look of wonder at the painting. In the dream, she had treated it like a treasure, speaking all day of seeing a beauty within the peony.

Xiao Huaiyan sometimes wondered: were the fragments he dreamed a product of his own imagination, or a premonition of the future?

It was all far too improbable, and yet he wanted to get to the bottom of it.

Xiao Huaiyan tapped his fingers on the desktop and looked at Jiang Mi's profile. "If Miss Jiang neither knows how to dance nor how to appreciate paintings, then how does she intend to offer her thanks?"

On the surface Jiang Mi appeared calm, but inside she was in turmoil. She had no ideas whatsoever.

She could only bow and beg pardon. "I ask Your Majesty to give this subject a little more time. I will certainly prepare a proper gift of thanks."

Xiao Huaiyan neither agreed nor refused.

Looking at her anxious expression, he said mildly, "Rise."

Xiao Huaiyan then asked, "Can Miss Jiang ride a horse?"

Jiang Mi moved her lips slightly. She turned the answer over in her mind several times before replying, "This subject is ashamed to say she has not yet learned."

At this moment, she truly could not ride. Even if one were to ask her aunt, her aunt knew full well that she could not.

Xiao Huaiyan gave a soft laugh. "In that case, Chengzhong will come to fetch you in two days."

Xiao Huaiyan stood up and looked around at the paintings filling the room. "If Miss Jiang wishes to continue looking at the paintings, please take her time. We have matters to attend to and must return to the palace."

Jiang Mi was startled. She watched Xiao Huaiyan depart.

But — what did it mean, that he was sending someone to collect her in two days?

Jiang Mi had completely lost any desire to look at the paintings. She stepped out of the room and spotted Chengzhong waiting nearby, and immediately asked him to take her home.

......

Jiang Mi grew increasingly wary of Xiao Huaiyan's motives. Why had he asked if she could dance? Why had he sent her to fetch Master Yunqing's painting? Was it really such a coincidence?

Jiang Mi spent two unsettled days, and then Chengzhong appeared again.

This time Chengzhong carried a tray, on which lay a set of clothing.

Chengzhong said, "Miss, this riding outfit will make things easier for you."

Jiang Mi looked it over. It was ordinary riding attire. After a moment's hesitation, she put it on.

Jiang Mi came out wearing a cloak, and followed Chengzhong into the carriage.

This time they did not go to the private residence but to the imperial garden.

Jiang Mi stepped out of the carriage and was led to a wide open expanse.

She saw several horses being held by attendants, grazing. Xiao Huaiyan had not yet arrived, so she let her mind go blank and stood there daydreaming.

She thought to herself: if he had brought her to the imperial garden's riding grounds, surely he wasn't going to have someone teach her to ride?

At that moment, Chengzhong led a horse over. "Miss Jiang, this small mare has a gentle temperament. Would you like to try getting on?"

So it really was for riding lessons.

Seeing that she had not spoken, Chengzhong pointed to the attendant beside him. "Miss Jiang, do not be afraid. This is a horse trainer of many years. He will not let you fall."

Jiang Mi nodded, and with the trainer's guidance and Chengzhong's arm for support, she successfully mounted the horse.

The chestnut mare was indeed as gentle as Chengzhong had said.

The trainer held the reins and led Jiang Mi around the grounds.

Jiang Mi was content to drift leisurely, gazing at the surrounding scenery from horseback.

Suddenly, a thundering of hooves approached from a distance, like the shaking of the earth.

She felt the little mare grow restless beneath her and patted it to calm it.

In just a few moments, a tall black horse came charging into view like a gust of wind and lightning. Her instinct was to grab the reins to steady herself — but when she caught sight of the black horse's rider from the corner of her eye, she deliberately fumbled with the reins and swayed with the nervous little mare.

A firm riding crop pressed against Jiang Mi's waist just in time. Xiao Huaiyan looked down at her from above and said with a smile, "Miss Jiang, at this rate, it would take half a year to learn to ride."

He had barely finished speaking before he took the reins from the trainer. "Miss Jiang, if you don't mind, We will teach you to ride."

His tone made it perfectly clear that this was not a question. He simply pulled her little horse along and set off behind his black stallion.

His mount was nearly twice the size of her small mare, and it snorted and huffed at the poor creature, making it tremble with fright.

Truly, a horse takes after its master.

Zidian had always been this spirited — it was entirely Xiao Huaiyan's doing.

In her previous life, Zidian would allow only Xiao Huaiyan to ride it. Later, when Xiao Huaiyan insisted on taking her along, Zidian threw a fit, and Xiao Huaiyan held her in his arms while he tamed it again.

After that, though Zidian never liked her, it at least would not deliberately throw her off when she rode it.

Zidian had a fiery temper, and when Xiao Huaiyan was not around, she would never dare go near it.

Xiao Huaiyan released the reins of the horse beneath Jiang Mi and, riding crop in hand, directed her movements.

Whenever the crop touched her waist, she pressed her lips together and endured the discomfort.

At least it was better than Xiao Huaiyan teaching her hands-on.

This time he really was teaching her — even correcting her posture.

Jiang Mi could not fathom why he had suddenly taken it into his head to teach her to ride.

chapters.title: Forty-three
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi's mind was elsewhere, and she did not react quickly enough when the little mare beneath her picked up speed. Her body tilted, and by the time she came back to her senses, she had already lost more than half her balance.

At that very moment, a long riding crop whooshed through the air and coiled around Jiang Mi's waist. Xiao Huaiyan said in a measured tone, "Miss Jiang had best pay attention."

Jiang Mi hastily used the leverage to grab the reins and steady herself. She said quietly, "Thank you, Your Majesty."

Xiao Huaiyan retracted the crop, his gaze drifting briefly over that slender waist.

The imperial wardrobe's craftsmanship was indeed fine. This riding outfit was made of newly presented satin and, worn by her, made her waist look especially delicate — a single hand's span could have encircled it. He looked away and said evenly, "Miss Jiang, following what was just taught, try urging the horse into a trot for one lap."

At those words, Jiang Mi's delicate fingers tightened. She gripped the reins and pressed gently with her feet against the stirrups, urging the little mare into a run.

At first, she held back, conscious of Xiao Huaiyan watching from beside her, so she only dared ease the horse forward cautiously. But as time went on and Xiao Huaiyan said nothing, she gradually lost herself in the feeling — light and free — and let out a soft whoop of encouragement.

The horse took the cue and broke into a full gallop.

Blue sky stretched to the horizon, and a gentle wind carried warmth.

Jiang Mi drew in a deep breath. The air around her seemed to grow lighter.

In a fleeting moment of happiness, she felt as though she were a bird — free and joyful.

Jiang Mi savored the feeling, and on an impulse, urged the horse to go faster, and faster still.

Xiao Huaiyan watched the red figure gallop into the distance, his gaze growing thoughtful. She was moving that fast on her first lesson?

He urged Zidian forward, following at a slow and steady pace.

Jiang Mi was still reveling in that rare sense of ease when her face suddenly went taut with pain.

Of all things — her foot had cramped.

The piercing ache numbed her foot instantly, causing it to spasm, and yet the horse had not slowed.

Jiang Mi was swaying dangerously, on the verge of tumbling from the saddle.

A dark shadow flashed by. A large hand swept out and caught the person falling out of control, pulling her into its arms.

Xiao Huaiyan looked down and saw the pain etched on her face, her lips gone pale, and her leg trembling.

He immediately reined Zidian to a stop.

Xiao Huaiyan dismounted with her in his arms, set her on the ground, and asked, "What is it? Is it a cramp?"

Jiang Mi was drenched in cold sweat. She endured the pain and nodded.

Without another word, Xiao Huaiyan reached down and lifted the hem of Jiang Mi's skirt.

Jiang Mi recoiled in alarm and fright.

Xiao Huaiyan's hand closed around her ankle. He said in a low voice, "Here?"

Jiang Mi pressed her lips together and tried to pull her leg free. She could not move.

Another wave of pain shot through her leg. Her apricot eyes filled with a shimmer of tears.

Xiao Huaiyan lifted her foot, about to remove her embroidered shoe.

Burning with embarrassment and urgency, Jiang Mi was nearly in tears. "Your— Your Majesty, could you not call for a medical attendant?"

"A leg cramp must be treated immediately, or it will worsen," Xiao Huaiyan said, watching her flush scarlet with pain and still insisting on resistance.

"I beg of you, Your Majesty." Jiang Mi held firm. She would rather bear the pain than bare her feet in the open before Xiao Huaiyan.

That one plea made Xiao Huaiyan's thumb press hard against the jade ring on his finger. His gaze settled heavily on Jiang Mi's face.

He eased some of the force from his hold — he did not release the hand gripping her ankle, but smoothed her skirt back over her leg, and said calmly, "We will not look. Tell me where it hurts."

Another cramp of pain made Jiang Mi shake violently. Xiao Huaiyan went directly beneath the skirt hem, removed her embroidered shoe, and pressed through her silk stocking to massage the pressure points.

The clash of two kinds of pain made Jiang Mi nearly cry out.

Tears streamed down Jiang Mi's face.

Gradually, the searing pain began to subside.

Only then did her mind come free enough to register the heat of the hand at her foot.

Though he was massaging the pressure points through her silk stocking, the humiliation of it was still enough to make her burn with shame.

Jiang Mi said quietly, "Your Majesty, the cramp has passed."

Only then did Xiao Huaiyan let go. He helped her put her shoe back on and stood.

Jiang Mi was still sitting on the ground. She looked up. Against the backlight, she could not clearly make out Xiao Huaiyan's face.

A black riding crop was extended toward Jiang Mi. "Miss Jiang, now that the pain has passed, try standing up and taking a few steps. It will help."

A flicker of hesitation crossed Jiang Mi's face before she raised her hand, gripped the crop, and used it to pull herself to her feet.

She curtsied to Xiao Huaiyan. "Thank you, Your Majesty."

Xiao Huaiyan said, "Miss Jiang, since you are only just beginning to ride, do not rush. Your horsemanship still needs considerable practice."

Jiang Mi kept her eyes down. "Your Majesty is right."

As they spoke, Chengzhong had already arrived with attendants bearing a sedan chair.

Xiao Huaiyan gave his instructions. "Chengzhong, take Miss Jiang to rest and have a medical attendant come examine her."

With that, he mounted his horse. Holding the reins, he turned to Jiang Mi. "Ah, yes — Miss Jiang, do you know archery?"

Jiang Mi pressed her lips together, then gave a small nod. "This subject knows a little."

Xiao Huaiyan laughed softly. "Very well then. At the imperial autumn hunt in half a month's time, Miss Jiang may have a chance to perform well — perhaps enough to repay the debt of thanks she owes."

With that, he cracked the long whip and rode off.

Jiang Mi stood there, stunned. The autumn hunt? Perform well?

Did that mean he expected her to attend the autumn hunt?

He had first taught her to ride, then asked if she could shoot — could it be that he wanted her to hunt something herself?

At imperial hunts, it was indeed customary for noble men and women to present their kills to the ruler. But even the most modest offering had to be a fox without a single colored hair to be fit to present to the Emperor.

Xiao Huaiyan really did know how to demand the impossible.

Chengzhong stepped forward with a smile. "Miss Jiang, please board the sedan chair. The medical attendant has already been sent for and is only waiting for you."

Jiang Mi suppressed her swirling thoughts and slowly made her way to the sedan chair.

Chengzhong had the sedan chair stop at the Qingshao Pavilion in the imperial garden, then extended his arm for the young Jiang lady to lean on as she descended.

As Jiang Mi walked, she found that the left leg that had cramped already felt much better.

A medical attendant, appearing to be around ten years old with a pleasant face, was waiting at the door. She bowed and paid her respects when she saw Jiang Mi approaching.

Jiang Mi paused and looked at her with curiosity. Wasn't this the medical attendant Wei she had so often summoned in her previous life?

She was here at the palace this early?

Jiang Mi said, "Rise. Thank you for coming to examine me. I cramped in my leg while riding just now."

The medical attendant followed Jiang Mi inside and closed the door.

Chengzhong rubbed his nose and stood guard outside like a dutiful sentry.

The medical attendant helped Jiang Mi remove her embroidered shoe and stocking, revealing a pair of small, fair jade-like feet — though several spots had gone red, as if they had been firmly kneaded.

A trace of discomfort crossed Jiang Mi's face.

The attendant pressed several points and asked Jiang Mi how they felt.

Jiang Mi only felt a slight soreness but no more pain.

The attendant rolled up the trouser leg, revealing a calf smooth as cream — with several red lines along the meridians where they had been worked through.

The medical attendant said, "It is fortunate that someone treated this for you beforehand. If left too long when a cramp is severe, the leg can seize up entirely, and that would be far more difficult to deal with."

The attendant poured some medicinal oil into her palms, rubbed it warm, and spent a while massaging several pressure points along the meridians. "Miss, you must not have ridden for a long time and started up again suddenly, which caused the cramp. In the future, before riding, it would be better to loosen up your muscles and joints first."

Jiang Mi smiled and agreed.

The medical attendant handed over a small porcelain bottle. "There is nothing seriously wrong with you, Miss. Apply this medicine when you get home and massage it in — it should feel more comfortable."

Jiang Mi felt that Attendant Wei was exactly as considerate and meticulous as she had been in her previous life.

She said, "Thank you, Attendant. What is your name?"

The medical attendant looked up and gave Jiang Mi a gentle smile. "Wei Yin."

Jiang Mi nodded. Just as she had thought.

......

Jiang Mi opened the door, and Chengzhong came forward with concern. "Miss, are you feeling better?"

Jiang Mi said, "Attendant Wei's medical skill is excellent. I am much better now."

Chengzhong glanced at the medical attendant. "This servant will naturally put in a good word with Chief Imperial Physician Gu at the Imperial Medical Academy. Miss Jiang, would you like to take another stroll through the imperial garden, or shall this servant escort you home?"

Jiang Mi did not wish to remain a moment longer. "I am tired. Please see me home, Eunuch."

Chengzhong readily agreed.

By the time Jiang Mi returned to Yuanzhi Courtyard, she felt completely exhausted.

She summoned Qiuyu and asked, "Is there anything that happened while I was away?"

Qiuyu said, "A page from the Master's side delivered another piece of sheet music for you, saying once again that it's a rare manuscript from some great master."

After Jiang Mi had returned from the palace, her father had come to see her. As she had done on previous occasions, she had tearfully told her father how frightened she was of entering the palace. He had left with a look of guilty shame. Was he trying to make it up to her in whatever way he could, since he felt powerless to change things?

She had hoped to use her father's guilt to have him quietly arrange a betrothal for her.

But it seemed that approach still had its difficulties. She would need to find the right moment to speak with her father again.

Qiuyu added, "Oh yes, Miss, the medical attendant that Madam found for you has also arrived. I told her you were asleep and to come again tomorrow."

Jiang Mi nodded. "Qiuyu, have someone bring hot water. I want to bathe."

Jiang Mi went into the bathroom, took off her cloak, and removed the riding outfit that had bound her all day.

The moment she sank into the water, she felt every limb and bone in her body relax. Even her worries could be temporarily forgotten.

Jiang Mi felt herself growing drowsy. Afraid she might fall asleep in the water and catch a chill, she climbed out while she still had a thread of clarity.

She changed into clean sleeping clothes, and when she pulled on her silk stockings and caught sight of the fingerprints on her ankle, she immediately thought of Xiao Huaiyan kneading her foot beneath the hem of her skirt. She knew he had been helping her, and yet her cheeks puffed up with indignation all the same.

Qiuyu came in to help Jiang Mi wring out her hair and said, "Miss, as you instructed, the room has been scented with your favorite dark aloeswood incense, and rose dew has been added as well."

Jiang Mi was satisfied. When she couldn't sleep well, these two things would help her rest more easily.

After learning today that Xiao Huaiyan intended to bring her to the autumn hunt, Jiang Mi had worried she might not sleep soundly, so she had asked Qiuyu to combine the two scents.

This was a method Attendant Wei had taught her in her previous life.

The following morning, Jiang Mi woke up feeling sore all over.

She got up still heavy with sleep.

These were all the aftereffects of yesterday's riding — she would likely have to practice her horsemanship for the remaining half of the month.

After breakfast, the medical attendant Lin whom Lady Su had found for Jiang Mi arrived.

Jiang Mi was somewhat surprised when she saw her. She had expected a young woman, but Attendant Lin looked to be in her early thirties, with a matron's hairstyle — her face pleasant and refined, with a kind and agreeable air.

Attendant Lin took Jiang Mi's pulse and said, "Miss has a somewhat deficient constitution with a cold tendency. Some medicinal treatment to regulate this would help — otherwise your monthly cycle will be troublesome. That said, you should walk around more often in daily life, and not keep yourself shut indoors. It will help open up the mind."

Jiang Mi heard the tactful hint in her words — not to let herself become weighed down by worry.

It was remarkably similar to what Chief Imperial Physician Gu had said.

Jiang Mi said, "Please write me a prescription, Attendant Lin."

Attendant Lin then said, "Miss, was incense burned in your room last night?"

Jiang Mi nodded.

Attendant Lin frowned and leaned in to sniff carefully. "Dark aloeswood and rose dew — Miss, please do not use these two together again."

chapters.title: Forty-four
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi suppressed the unease rising in her heart. She asked, "Is there something wrong with using dark aloeswood and rose dew together?"

Attendant Lin moved among the inner quarters of great households and knew something of the hidden matters found in secluded estates. Every word she spoke had to be chosen with great care.

She thought for a moment, then said, "Both dark aloeswood and rose dew have calming, sleep-inducing properties. However, when used together, they can easily create a dependency. Some women, once they have used them for a long time, become prone to excessive rumination and grow absorbed in their dreams. In severe cases, hallucinations can even occur."

Worried that her words might frighten the young woman across from her, Attendant Lin added, "Miss need not be overly alarmed. Very few people know that these two cannot be used together. Some begin using them and, finding them effective, have no idea of the long-term consequences. This woman first learned of it in Yangzhou, where a former employer had used them in this combination. Later, due to certain troubles, that lady became consumed with grief and swallowed gold to end her life. The room at the time was full of this scent, and when I asked the serving maid, I learned that the mistress had developed an extreme dependence on it — she couldn't sleep without burning it at night, and she would sometimes tell her maids that she saw a figure standing at the foot of her bed. She wandered in a daze from morning to night."

"Since Miss has only just started using it, there should be no serious harm done. As long as you don't continue using both together for an extended period, all will be well." Attendant Lin said in a gentle, reassuring tone.

Jiang Mi took out a small porcelain bottle and offered it to Attendant Lin. "Would you please examine this medicine for me?"

Attendant Lin received it with both hands, poured out a small amount from the bottle, and examined it carefully. She looked up at Jiang Mi with a smile. "It is a topical ointment for promoting blood circulation and dispersing bruises. Quite safe to use."

Jiang Mi thanked Attendant Lin and put the porcelain bottle away, then asked, "Does Attendant Lin have a good understanding of blending fragrances?"

Attendant Lin smiled. "This woman only knows the basics. However, my junior fellow-disciple has a particular gift for it — though he is in Jiangnan."

Jiang Mi nodded. "I was only asking out of curiosity. If I have any questions about fragrance blending in the future, I will consult you again."

Attendant Lin said, "This woman would not presume to be consulted, but Miss is always welcome to come and ask whatever you wish."

Having written out a regulating prescription for Jiang Mi, Attendant Lin took her leave.

Jiang Mi asked Qiuyu to open all the windows and let the remaining traces of incense air out.

She stared at the incense burner for a long while, unable to collect herself.

In her previous life, Chief Imperial Physician Gu had been her physician, and Attendant Wei would only come to check on her when the Chief Physician was unavailable. She remembered clearly: not long after the Jiang family's downfall, when she could not sleep for night after night, Attendant Wei had seen that burning dark aloeswood alone wasn't helping, and had mentioned in passing that adding rose dew would improve the effect.

She had tried it, and indeed it was better than dark aloeswood alone.

After that, she had used it regularly.

If Attendant Wei had said those words deliberately — then who was she working for?

The thought left her feeling as though thick fog lay over the road ahead.

Xia Ruo came in and saw her mistress apparently lost in thought. She said softly, "Miss, the Marchioness is asking for you."

Jiang Mi slowly turned her head. Her great-aunt was asking for her?

Jiang Mi stood, and Qiuyu draped a cloak over her shoulders as she made her way to the main residence.

The moment Jiang Mi stepped into the front hall, she heard her great-aunt's laughter and looked up to find Second Sister Jiang Yi there as well.

The Marchioness of Cheng'en smiled brightly when she saw Jiang Mi. "Tangtan, come here quickly. I have good news for you."

She said with a face full of joy, "The list of those accompanying the Emperor on the autumn hunt includes our Cheng'en Marquessate. Your uncle and eldest cousin will both attend, and Tangtan, you and Yi'er will go too. It's only a shame that Rong'er is a little too young, otherwise she could have come along for the fun as well."

To Jiang Mi, this was hardly good news — though she had already known since yesterday.

The Marchioness pointed to a tray covered with a red cloth sitting before them. "Tangtan, these are riding outfits bestowed by the Empress Dowager for you and Yi'er. She worried you might not have time to prepare on your end, so she took care of everything. Go see if you like them."

Jiang Mi said to Jiang Yi, "Elder Sister Yi, come take a look together."

Jiang Yi was quite enthusiastic, though she knew perfectly well that Tangtan would be given first pick. She was not in a hurry.

When the red cloth was lifted, there lay several outfits in different colors, each tailored to the two girls' different figures — no selection needed.

As the two girls looked over the riding outfits, the Marchioness continued, "The Empress Dowager also says that in the coming days you two sisters should go to the imperial garden to practice riding and archery. There will be quite a few noble young men and women at the autumn hunt — you don't want to be sitting there watching others have all the fun when a competition comes up, not knowing how to do anything at all."

Jiang Yi said, "Mother, I understand, I understand. Tomorrow I'll go to the imperial garden with Tangtan. When we get to the hunting grounds, I'll catch a fire-red fox and bring it back as a fur coat for you."

The Marchioness clicked her tongue. "All talk and no substance. Aren't you afraid Tangtan will laugh at you."

Jiang Mi looked at the several riding outfits that were hers and let out a long, silent sigh.

......

First thing the following morning, Jiang Yi dragged Jiang Mi off to the imperial garden.

Along the way, Jiang Yi chattered without pause — which noble families she'd heard would be accompanying the Emperor, which military households, which young men and women of rank would be going.

Jiang Mi passed her a teacup. "Elder Sister Yi, do rest for a moment."

Jiang Yi said, "Tangtan, aren't you even the least bit curious? They say all the female relatives of the civil and military officials joining the imperial procession this time are candidates from whom the Emperor may select imperial consorts. After the new year, they are to be brought into the palace."

Jiang Mi lowered her eyes, blew on the floating tea leaves, and said, "We'll know when we get there."

Jiang Yi said in surprise, "Tangtan, you're really so composed these days. If it were before, I can tell you, you'd have been crying your eyes out."

Jiang Mi took a sip of tea. In her previous life, when Xiao Huaiyan went on the autumn hunt, the Jiang family had not been on the accompanying list. She had hidden at home and wept in secret for days on end — and yet none of it had changed anything.

Jiang Mi teased Jiang Yi, "Elder Sister Yi is paying such close attention — could it be that your 'dark-complexioned young lord' is also on the list?"

Jiang Yi was caught off guard, swallowed her tea wrong, and began coughing.

By the time the two had donned their riding outfits and stepped down from the carriage, they walked toward the riding grounds not far away.

They found that someone was already there practicing.

A closer look revealed it was the young lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor — and Xue Ningzhu.

The imperial garden was open to the imperial family and their close relatives. It was perfectly normal for the young lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor to bring his sister, given that his grandmother was the Grand Princess.

Xue Ningzhu, sitting on horseback, spotted Jiang Mi and waved enthusiastically. "Sister Jiang!"

Jiang Mi and Jiang Yi paid their respects to the young lord Xue Jinglin.

Xue Ningzhu could not wait to say, "Sister Jiang, are you here to practice riding too?"

Jiang Mi nodded.

Xue Ningzhu said, "Wonderful! Then I don't have to suffer under Young Lord Brother's drills alone anymore. Sister Jiang, my brother can ride and shoot — why don't we have him teach all of us!"

Young Lord Xue looked helpless. "I have let Miss Jiang see something embarrassing."

Jiang Mi said, "We two sisters have not practiced riding or archery in a long time. We would be grateful for any guidance from the Young Lord."

Jiang Yi nodded along. She knew the treasured daughter of the Duke Zhen State's manor had become close with Tangtan, and if they could receive guidance from Young Lord Xue, the visit would truly not be wasted.

Xue Jinglin noticed that Jiang Mi, dressed in a light blue riding outfit, mounted the horse with a certain agility. He smiled and said, "Miss Jiang is too modest."

The group rode several laps around the grounds, and the young ladies were all somewhat tired — Jiang Yi in particular needed to freshen up, and had a palace maid lead the way for her.

After Jiang Yi left, Xue Ningzhu said, "Young Lord Brother, let us rest a while. Shoot an arrow for us to watch — then when we've recovered, we'll learn from you."

Xue Jinglin frowned. "If you're tired, we can leave it for another day."

Xue Ningzhu persisted, and Xue Jinglin had no choice but to agree.

Jiang Mi was genuinely curious about this Young Lord's archery. He was frail in health, and yet it seemed he had not neglected it.

Xue Jinglin had the target set up, then took up his bow and arrow, aimed at the center, and released. The arrow struck dead center.

Xue Ningzhu clapped with delight. "Sister Jiang, isn't my brother impressive!"

Jiang Mi nodded. "The Young Lord has opened our eyes greatly......"

Before she could finish, the sound of galloping hooves rang out, and at the same time, an arrow cut through the air with tremendous force, flying straight through the Young Lord's arrow already lodged in the bullseye.

Everyone turned in astonishment to look. The Emperor, dressed in black, rode his horse forward at a brisk canter, a retinue of palace attendants and eunuchs following behind.

Jiang Mi paid her respects along with the others. "Long live Your Majesty."

Xiao Huaiyan reined in his horse and swept his gaze over those present. "Young Lord Xue's archery is as sharp as ever. Remarkable, truly — with such a frail constitution, the Young Lord has not let his skill in archery slip at all."

Xue Jinglin cupped his hands. "This subject's meager tricks are an embarrassment before Your Majesty."

Xiao Huaiyan smiled. "The Young Lord need not be modest. We look forward to the Young Lord's performance at the autumn hunt."

Xue Jinglin bowed his head. "This subject will give everything he has and will not disappoint Your Majesty."

Xiao Huaiyan said with approval, "Good."

Xue Jinglin said, "This subject has had his young sister out for some time now and was just about to take our leave. We will not disturb Your Majesty's pleasant outing."

Xiao Huaiyan gave a noncommittal sound.

Xue Jinglin withdrew, taking Xue Ningzhu with him.

In no time at all, only Jiang Mi remained.

Jiang Mi kept her head lowered, her heart pounding wildly. She steeled herself, curtsied, and said, "This subject would not dare disturb Your Majesty's pleasant outing either. I shall take my leave."

Xiao Huaiyan said nothing. He rode in a slow circle around Jiang Mi. Riding crop in hand, he said, "We personally taught Miss Jiang to ride. If Miss Jiang wishes to learn archery as well, We are not against teaching that too. Does Miss Jiang intend to slip away without a word and simply switch instructors?"

Jiang Mi went rigid. "This subject... would not dare."

Xiao Huaiyan laughed softly and asked, "Is the young lady's leg feeling better?"

Jiang Mi said, "It is fine now."

Xiao Huaiyan straightened in the saddle, his tone even. "Since Miss Jiang wishes to practice archery, let her shoot and we shall see."

With those words, an attendant stepped forward to present a bow and arrows to Jiang Mi.

She had no choice but to accept. She nocked an arrow, drew back the bowstring, and released. The arrow barely grazed the edge of the target.

An attendant presented another arrow.

Jiang Mi loosed three arrows in succession. Two went wide; only one barely caught the edge of the target.

Xiao Huaiyan had not spoken throughout. Jiang Mi could only continue.

When she had nocked the bow again, a riding crop came down lightly but deliberately on her elbow. "Raise it higher."

The crop pressed down on her shoulder, then traced slowly along her spine down to her waist, and came to rest.

Xiao Huaiyan said calmly, "Stop tensing up. Loosen your hand."

Jiang Mi's mind went blank. Her hand slipped open, and the arrow shot out with a whistle — still not quite at the center, but considerably more on target than before.

Xiao Huaiyan retracted the riding crop and said, "It seems Miss Jiang is not without aptitude."

Jiang Mi wanted nothing more than to turn and walk away.

In her previous life and in this one, Xiao Huaiyan's method of teaching archery was never quite honorable.

Xiao Huaiyan saw Jiang Mi lower the bow, and asked, "Miss Jiang is done practicing?"

chapters.title: Forty-five
chapters.content:
"This subject rode for too long earlier, and her arms are already giving out. Having just shot several arrows as well, she has no strength left. I am afraid I cannot satisfy Your Majesty." Jiang Mi's voice came out weak and soft, as though she had already spent every last bit of her energy.

A moment passed. Xiao Huaiyan retracted his gaze and said, "So be it."

With that, he gave a light flick of the riding crop in his hand. Beneath him, Zidian moved like wind and cloud, and in a moment, only a dark shadow remained.

Watching the great retinue follow him away, Jiang Mi quietly let out a breath of relief.

Jiang Yi came walking over from the distance. "Tangtan, was that the Emperor who just stopped here? What was he doing?"

Jiang Yi's face lit up. "Could it be that he came especially to see you?"

Jiang Mi paused in her step and smiled. "What are you thinking, Elder Sister Yi? The Emperor only passed through."

Jiang Yi's face fell immediately, and she let the matter drop. "I never expected riding to be so exhausting. I don't have the energy for archery practice at all. Tangtan, since Young Lord Xue and his sister have already left, shall we head back too?"

Jiang Mi was naturally in agreement.

Half a month slipped by in the blink of an eye. The site for the autumn hunt was set at the Fenghe Hunting Grounds.

Jiang Mi and Jiang Yi rode in the carriage for an entire day, arriving near the Fenghe Hunting Grounds at dusk, where they were taken to the temporary palace.

The moment they stepped off the carriage, palace servants came forward to escort them to their lodgings.

The matron leading the way knew that the Jiang women were nieces of the Empress Dowager, and was most attentive and deferential.

As Jiang Mi passed through the corridor, a group of people came walking toward them from ahead.

It was Shen Yawei, daughter of Grand Secretary Shen's household.

As the two groups crossed paths, Jiang Mi gave a slight nod in acknowledgment, expecting to simply pass each other by.

But Shen Yawei stopped, calling out, "Miss Jiang, what a coincidence."

Jiang Mi could only respond in kind. "Miss Shen, indeed."

Shen Yawei smiled and said, "Since we parted at the Duke Zhen State's manor flower banquet, I have been wondering how you were recovering, Miss Jiang."

Jiang Mi said, "Thank you for your concern, Miss Shen. I have made a full recovery."

Shen Yawei said, "That is truly a relief. I do hope we can enjoy the hunt together, Miss Jiang. But it is growing late, and I will not keep you any longer."

The moment Shen Yawei left, the matron who had been guiding Jiang Mi leaned in. "Miss Jiang, so you and Miss Shen are acquainted. The two finest courtyard suites in this temporary palace are yours and Miss Shen's — East Pavilion Garden, where you are staying, has a hot spring, which is perfect for this season. It will do wonders for the fatigue of the journey. Please follow this old servant."

Jiang Mi was still thinking about Shen Yawei's words when Jiang Yi, having heard mention of a hot spring, brightened at once and asked the matron, "You said East Pavilion Garden and South Xiang Hall are the finest suites — East Pavilion Garden has the hot spring. What does South Xiang Hall have?"

The matron smiled and said, "South Xiang Hall is most renowned for its bamboo. The scenery is lovely, though it is better suited for a summer visit."

"That makes sense. Bamboo scenery is beautiful, but at this time of year, it cannot compare to the appeal of a hot spring." Jiang Yi was already eager to take a bath.

Upon arriving at East Pavilion Garden, Jiang Mi and Jiang Yi first went to the hot spring pool.

Jiang Mi soaked in the warm water, drowsy with comfort.

Jiang Yi splashed water at Jiang Mi. Jiang Mi said with resignation, "Elder Sister Yi, please stop."

Jiang Yi played for a while and then settled down. She asked, "Tangtan, are you well acquainted with that Shen girl?"

Jiang Mi shook her head. "Not really."

Jiang Yi gave a small laugh. "But you would surely know her brother well enough. That young Lord Shen must be here too. The last time it was thanks to him......"

Jiang Yi had been about to tease her further, but the words died on her lips.

Because if Tangtan were not destined to enter the palace and serve the Emperor, she and young Lord Shen would actually have been rather well matched.

Jiang Mi stepped out of the water, and Qiuyu, waiting nearby, quickly handed over a towel to help dry her off.

Jiang Mi said to Jiang Yi, "Elder Sister Yi, don't soak too long. Come out now."

Jiang Yi did as she was told, and the two changed their clothes together.

They ate a simple dinner and went to bed early.

The following morning, Jiang Mi learned that on the first day at the temporary palace, no one was going to the hunting grounds — they would only set off for the grounds the day after.

A palace maid attending Jiang Mi at East Pavilion Garden said, "Miss, if you're bored, you might take a walk nearby and look at the maple leaves. The maples to the south have turned red — it is truly beautiful."

Jiang Mi was somewhat interested, so she called for Jiang Yi to come along.

When they arrived, the maple leaves blazed like fire and rosy clouds — ardent as flames, a brilliant splash of color in the desolate autumn landscape.

Jiang Mi picked up a maple leaf and turned it over in her hand. A breeze passed through, and with it came voices from somewhere nearby.

Jiang Yi pulled Jiang Mi along. "There seem to be people making a commotion over that way — shall we go have a look?"

Within the temporary palace were mostly the noble sons and daughters who had come along on this occasion.

From the sound of the voices, it seemed women were in the majority.

Jiang Yi had a nature that loved a good spectacle, and she tugged Jiang Mi along in that direction.

......

Around the Wanxiang Pavilion there was quite a stir.

Some gathered in twos and threes beneath the maples to chat; others sat in the pavilion playing the zither and exchanging verses; and still more were playing a game of pitch-pot in the open space outside the pavilion.

A girl in a green skirt called out loudly, "Miss Xue, since you made the bet with Princess Shuyi, you have to honor it. Don't try to back out!"

Those around her joined in: "That's right! Just now Princess Shuyi put her jade bracelet up as the wager. Now Miss Xue must put up her mutton-fat jade five-bat scepter hairpin. Whoever wins it is the new owner."

"But... but this was a birthday gift from my grandmother!"

Someone laughed and said, "Just now Princess Shuyi's jade bracelet was also given to her by Prince Zhaonan — equally precious. If Miss Xue didn't want to gamble, she shouldn't have agreed to the wager in the first place. To back out now — isn't that just being a sore loser?"

Someone else tried to smooth things over: "Miss Xue is still young. Princess Shuyi is generous — please don't make such a fuss over it."

The girl they called Princess Shuyi stood at the center of the crowd, dressed in a rosy brocade skirt with wisteria-colored satin. She looked to be around fifteen, and with a sidelong glance at the girl who had just spoken, she said, "Who are you? What rank is your family? Is it your place to get involved in the bet between Miss Xue and me?"

The young lady went pale at the words and quietly slipped back into the crowd, wanting nothing more to do with the matter. Others who had considered speaking up also fell silent. Princess Shuyi's father, Prince Zhaonan, was greatly favored by the Emperor — none of them wanted to make an enemy of someone so close to the throne.

Princess Shuyi said, "Miss Xue, it is time for the contest. Bring out the wager!"

The little girl had no choice but to remove the scepter hairpin from her head and hand it to a palace maid, who placed it on the stand — ready to be taken by whoever won.

As Jiang Mi made her way over, the crowd gathered around the pitch-pot competition erupted in mockery.

"Miss Xue, even if you throw every last arrow you have left, you'll still lose! Princess Shuyi has already hit nine in a row, and you've thrown nine arrows but only managed to clip the rim of the pot once. Even if you get the tenth one in, you'll still be behind!"

Princess Shuyi smiled and said, "No rush. This princess will wait for Miss Xue to take her final throw."

Through the gaps in the crowd, Jiang Mi could see a small girl standing alone, clutching an arrow with red-rimmed eyes.

She stepped forward and called out, "Ningzhu! What are you doing here?"

Xue Ningzhu looked up and saw Jiang Mi. With a sob, she burst into tears. "Sister Jiang! They're trying to take my hairpin. Waaah......"

Jiang Mi's brow furrowed. She helped Xue Ningzhu wipe her tears. "Ningzhu, don't cry. Tell Sister Jiang what happened."

Xue Ningzhu said, "Sister Jiang, they want my five-bat scepter hairpin as the wager — whoever wins gets to take it. But this hairpin was a birthday gift from my grandmother. I tried to offer something else, and Princess Shuyi wouldn't allow it."

Jiang Mi looked toward the tall girl who stood at the center of the crowd, pampered and surrounded by admirers, her bearing imperious.

Princess Shuyi Zhao Jiashu — daughter of Prince Zhaonan.

She had some recollection of this young princess. In her previous life, this one had also gone to considerable lengths in her pursuit of a place in Xiao Huaiyan's inner court, though for reasons unknown, she had never made it onto the final list and had eventually returned to the fief with Prince Zhaonan.

So she had come on this hunt too, in her previous life.

Princess Shuyi raised her chin and asked, "And who are you? Another one here to meddle?"

Jiang Mi said calmly, "Jiang Mi, of the Cheng'en Marquessate."

Princess Shuyi's expression shifted slightly. She looked Jiang Mi up and down, eyes narrowing. So this pale-skinned woman, beautiful as a spirit, was the Empress Dowager's niece, Jiang Mi?

"Oh~ so it is Miss Jiang. Even if the Empress Dowager is backing you, you can't let Miss Xue go back on her word. She agreed fair and square — I used the jade bracelet my father gave me as the wager, and she used her scepter hairpin. Both are equally precious. Why should it be acceptable for her to renege now? Or are you saying that the daughters of civil official families not only fall short of ours in pitch-pot — they also can't keep their word?"

Princess Shuyi's cutting words left several daughters of civil official families looking furious.

And if Jiang Mi were to forcibly intervene on behalf of the Xue girl to get the hairpin back, it would also give others something to talk about.

Some of the quieter onlookers had already seen through it all — the bad blood between the Prince Zhaonan household and the Duke Zhen State's manor was real. Normally, with the Grand Princess of the Duke Zhen State's manor, people would at least show some deference, and even if the Xue girl had made some mistake, no one would press the matter. Yet Princess Shuyi was pressing relentlessly.

Xue Ningzhu tearfully explained to Jiang Mi, "It wasn't like that, Sister Jiang — I wasn't trying to go back on my word."

Jiang Mi patted Xue Ningzhu's hand to calm her. "It's all right."

She turned to Princess Shuyi and said, "The Princess just said that whoever wins the pitch-pot contest takes the wager — is that correct?"

Princess Shuyi said, "Naturally!"

Jiang Mi said, "Then bring me an arrow. I will compete as well."

Princess Shuyi was taken aback. She looked at Jiang Mi's slender frame — a delicate girl who looked as though a gust of wind might topple her — and this girl was actually going to compete?

Well then, she would oblige her!

"Very well! Someone, bring Miss Jiang her arrows!"

And with that, even the young ladies who had been chatting beneath the maples and composing verses in the pavilion were drawn over to watch the spectacle.

The Jiang girl was going to compete in pitch-pot against Princess Shuyi.

Princess Shuyi had just hit nine in a row — Miss Jiang would need to hit all ten perfectly to win the wager.

A palace maid stepped forward with the arrows. Jiang Mi took one. Out of habit, she rubbed the tip with her thumb, stood at the marked position, and fixed her gaze on the distance between herself and the pitch-pot.

And at that very moment, from far away came the ceremonial announcement from a eunuch's voice: "The Imperial Procession arrives."

chapters.title: Forty six
chapters.content:
The young ladies around Wanxiang Pavilion all fell quiet. Those inside the pavilion stepped out; those who had been seated rose to their feet. Everyone turned to look in the direction from which the announcement had come.

Seeing that no one cared about the pitch-pot contest anymore, Jiang Mi had no choice but to set down the arrow in her hand.

At the front of the approaching group, Xiao Huaiyan was dressed in a white dragon robe and crowned with a jade cap — refined and elegant, radiating an innate imperial grandeur.

Following behind him were mostly sons of noble houses. A quick glance took in Pei Chi, commander of the Embroidered Guard; Gu Chang, the second son of the Marquessate of Zhongyong; the young lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor Xue Jinglin, alongside the third son Xue Jingyuan; Shen Qianxiu, the third son of Grand Secretary Shen's household... around ten or so young gentlemen in all, drawn over, it seemed, by word of the beautiful maple trees.

As Xiao Huaiyan drew near, a chorus of melodious voices rang out as the young ladies curtsied and paid their respects.

Xiao Huaiyan waved away the formalities. His gaze swept over the gathering, and he said with an amused smile, "It seems We have interrupted everyone's amusements."

Princess Shuyi flushed as she stole a few glances at the handsome young Emperor. Finding him warm and approachable, she sensed an opportunity and spoke up. "Your Majesty has arrived at just the right moment. We were about to hold a pitch-pot competition — would Your Majesty honor us by bearing witness?"

Xiao Huaiyan smiled. "Since it is a competition, what is the wager?"

His gaze drifted to the jade hairpin displayed on the stand behind the pot.

A flicker of irritation crossed Princess Shuyi's face, but she had no choice but to reply, "Miss Xue of the Duke Zhen State's manor has generously offered the wager."

Xue Ningzhu pressed her lips together, about to speak — but Jiang Mi took hold of her hand, signaling her to stay quiet.

Xue Jinglin furrowed his brow. He too had spotted the five-bat scepter hairpin. Was that not Ningzhu's favorite? How could she bear to put it up as a wager? With her skill at pitch-pot, she was practically handing it away to someone else.

Everyone waited for the Emperor's response.

Xiao Huaiyan smiled. "In that case, We shall add a wager of Our own."

He unclasped a warm jade piece he wore on his person and tossed it to Li Fu. "For the fun of it."

Li Fu reverently carried the warm jade over and placed it on the stand alongside the scepter hairpin.

The young ladies around all fixed their eyes on the jade piece, their hearts stirring.

Quite a few more girls were suddenly eager to join in.

Even Shen Yawei, who had not planned to take part, now had second thoughts.

......

Li Fu, seeing that the Emperor was in the mood to watch the spectacle, invited him to be seated in the pavilion.

The noble young gentlemen were equally enthusiastic.

Pitch-pot was a common pastime — they all played it themselves — but watching the young ladies of noble families play was a rare treat. Outside of one's own sisters at home, there were few occasions to see it.

Jiang Mi frowned, inwardly fuming at Xiao Huaiyan's meddling. With him involved, winning had become far more difficult.

Among the noble families who had come along for this hunt, there were quite a few daughters of military households. Pitch-pot would be nothing to them.

And now he had added a jade piece as a prize — of course the girls would compete over it.

She watched several more young ladies walk over. Wonderful — even the daughters of civil official households were joining in now.

Xue Ningzhu tugged at Jiang Mi's hand, calling out "Sister Jiang" in a pitiful, imploring tone.

Even if Jiang Mi wanted to back out now, there was no backing out.

She had said she would help Ningzhu win back the scepter hairpin, and she would do it — even if it now came with an unwanted prize attached.

Princess Shuyi, seeing the larger crowd, felt a flicker of unease, but she announced to everyone, "Since His Majesty has added to the wager, perhaps we could raise the difficulty a little as well? If the pot is set farther back, it will better test true skill."

Some were displeased, but no one voiced an objection.

Under the circumstances, none of them would want to admit they lacked the skill.

The bronze gong rang, and Princess Shuyi stepped up first. She held her breath and steadied herself. She knew the Emperor had spent years at the border — her father had told her he admired dignified, understated women, and also valued those with skill in riding and archery. She had practiced at home regularly. Pitch-pot was well within her abilities. She had asked for the increased distance precisely because, for the others, it would be harder — for her, it was just right.

Arrow after arrow flew true, drawing rounds of admiring applause.

On the very last throw, however, a gust of wind caused a slight deviation — the arrow bounced off the rim of the pot.

Nine out of ten was still an impressive score.

The young Emperor seated in the pavilion smiled and offered a word of praise.

A string of other young ladies tried next. Some managed five or six; others hit only one or two and retreated to the side in embarrassment.

After a while it was no longer about winning or losing, but about being seen in the Emperor's presence.

Surprisingly, the young lady from Grand Secretary Shen's household hit eight — a result that startled more than a few people.

When Miss Shen stepped up to throw, some watched her with barely concealed scrutiny, and others stole glances at the Emperor's expression.

After all, this young lady's elder sister had once been betrothed to the Emperor — the Shen family had a special connection to him.

After Miss Shen finished her throws, the Emperor was seen chatting easily with the Shen family's third son, laughing and at ease — clearly quite pleased.

When the Empress Dowager's niece, the Jiang girl, stepped forward, the eyes of the noble young gentlemen shifted subtly.

This Miss Jiang had a celestial quality to her beauty — skin like cream, a slender and delicate waist, and a figure at once graceful and alluring. Though her posture was perfectly proper, there was an inexplicable, unspoken enchantment about her. The small gesture of her thumb rubbing the tip of the arrow was enough to make more than a few of the young men go glassy-eyed.

Several of the young lords thought to themselves, with a certain unspoken understanding, that even if this Miss Jiang were to miss every single throw, she would still be first in their hearts.

No wonder the Empress Dowager had kept her tucked away. There was a reason for it.

Princess Shuyi watched Jiang Mi with cool composure. She had just been challenging her, and now they would see what she was actually capable of. With that delicate, bewitching figure, she could only earn the pity of those shallow young lordlings — what else could she possibly do?

Jiang Mi was already irritated by Xiao Huaiyan's interference, and she channeled it directly — she raised her hand and threw. The arrow landed squarely in the center of the pot.

She took another arrow, instinctively rubbing the tip with her thumb, and threw again. Another hit.

As she hit seven in a row, the looks directed at her slowly began to change.

Even Xiao Huaiyan, who had been sitting in a relaxed sprawl, imperceptibly straightened a little.

Then Jiang Mi raised her hand and landed yet another arrow.

A few of the young lords could not help but call out in admiration.

Gu Chang, standing beside Xue Jingyuan, nudged him. "Hey, Xue Third — don't you think the way that Miss Jiang holds the arrow looks a little familiar?"

Xue Jingyuan said nothing.

Gu Chang watched with shining eyes, unable to help himself. "Didn't expect Miss Jiang to be this good at pitch-pot. Makes me wonder how her archery is."

Shen Qianxiu's gaze had not left Jiang Mi since the beginning. Since the time they had parted ways at the study, seeing her again now, she was every bit as radiant as he remembered.

Xue Jinglin pressed a fist to his lips, suppressing the urge to cough, and watched the young lady at the pitch-pot quietly, a trace of admiration in his eyes.

Jiang Mi picked up the ninth arrow. She paused. Her arm was starting to ache a little. She knew the last two were the hardest, but she had to stay steady.

Jiang Mi rubbed the tip of the arrow again with her thumb, fixed her eyes on the mouth of the pot, and threw.

A solid thud — dead center again.

The cheers grew louder, and even the noble young ladies clapped in appreciation.

Princess Shuyi's expression darkened. Jiang Mi had tied with her.

Li Fu, standing beside the Emperor, noticed that His Majesty had shifted his posture and was unconsciously turning the jade ring on his finger. His expression remained as usual, yet his eyes were far more attentive than before.

Li Fu thought — and perhaps he was imagining things — that the habit of rubbing the tip of the arrow as Miss Jiang did was remarkably similar to the Emperor's own habit.

Whether intentional or not, he couldn't say. If Miss Jiang had deliberately studied and practiced the Emperor's manner and posture, she had truly put in considerable effort, and it spoke to a deeply hidden devotion. A sentiment she kept very well concealed indeed.

Still, the Emperor disliked being watched or imitated — by that measure, Miss Jiang's mimicry was technically a transgression against his sensibilities.

And yet, looking at the Emperor's expression... he did not seem displeased.

For the last arrow, everyone's eyes turned to Jiang Mi. As if blessed by divine aid, she raised her hand and threw — another hit.

A clear bronze gong rang out.

"Miss Jiang of the Cheng'en Marquessate wins."

Jiang Yi and Xue Ningzhu rushed joyfully at Jiang Mi, embracing her in their excitement.

Xue Ningzhu was overjoyed. "Sister Jiang, Sister Jiang, I knew you would win!"

Jiang Yi beamed from ear to ear. "My goodness, Tangtan, you've really opened my eyes today. You never let on — I had no idea you were this good at pitch-pot!"

Jiang Mi had broken into a cold sweat. Her nerves had been wound so tight that her hands trembled faintly now that it was over, her strength thoroughly spent.

At this moment, Xiao Huaiyan and the others filed out of the pavilion. He stopped in front of Jiang Mi. "We watched the entertainment to its end — it was truly splendid. Miss Jiang put her heart into it."

Jiang Mi curtsied, keeping her head lowered. "This subject is unworthy of the praise."

Li Fu personally carried both prizes over to Jiang Mi and offered his congratulations. "Congratulations to Miss Jiang on taking first place. The prizes are now yours."

Jiang Mi accepted the jade piece and the hairpin, then thanked the Emperor. "This subject is grateful for Your Majesty's gracious gift."

Xiao Huaiyan's eyes were dark as ink. He smiled, said nothing more, and departed with his retinue.

Among those who remained, some offered Jiang Mi their congratulations, while others slipped away in silence.

Shen Yawei stepped forward with a smile. "Congratulations, Miss Jiang — first it was the guqin Lüqi bestowed by His Majesty, and now his personal jade piece. Miss Jiang's skill at pitch-pot is remarkable. I wonder if there might be a chance for her to offer us some pointers."

Jiang Mi said, "I would not presume to give pointers. I was simply a little lucky today."

Shen Yawei said, "Miss Jiang is far too modest. I am sure fortune will continue to favor you at the hunt tomorrow."

Princess Shuyi let out a cold snort. "Is that so? Then this princess shall wait and see just what fortune Miss Jiang encounters."

With that, she shot a venomous glare at Jiang Mi and swept away with her maidservants.

Shen Yawei covered her mouth in surprise and shook her head with a sigh. "Princess Shuyi's temper really is something. Miss Jiang, do not let her frighten you."

Jiang Mi watched Princess Shuyi's retreating figure with a slight frown, and Shen Yawei's words left her with a feeling she could not quite name.

Shen Yawei added, "Miss Jiang, you must be tired as well. There will be a banquet tonight hosted by the Emperor — everyone is expected to attend. Do go back and rest first. I must go change my clothes as well."

Jiang Mi smiled. "Thank you for letting me know, Miss Shen."

The two exchanged a parting curtsy.

After Shen Yawei left, Jiang Mi pinned the five-bat scepter hairpin back into Xue Ningzhu's hair. "Ningzhu, since this is a precious thing, you must not use it as a wager again. Even if others goad you into it, you simply need to refuse. When it is something you do not wish to do, no one can truly force you."

Xue Ningzhu wiped her eyes and hugged Jiang Mi tightly. "Sister Jiang, you are so good to me — just as good as Young Lord Brother. If only you could be my sister-in-law."

chapters.title: Forty seven
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi's thick lashes swept down to conceal the flicker that crossed her eyes. She reached out and pinched Xue Ningzhu's cheek. "Don't say such things."

Xue Ningzhu tilted her head up with a pout. "Sister Jiang, won't you be my sister-in-law?"

Jiang Yi stood nearby watching in amusement, and deliberately teased Xue Ningzhu. "If Miss Xue wants Tangtan for a sister-in-law, then have your brother come and propose! That way Tangtan could stay in the Duke Zhen State's manor and keep you company every day."

Jiang Mi gave Jiang Yi a light smack. "Elder Sister Yi, you too? Don't encourage her."

Jiang Yi covered her mouth and laughed, because she knew it was impossible — which was precisely why she had said it as a joke.

Jiang Mi walked Xue Ningzhu back to her courtyard, gave the attending maids instructions to take good care of her, and then took her leave.

Xue Ningzhu returned to her room in a sulk.

When the young lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor, Xue Jinglin, came to look in on her and found her in low spirits, he set the pastries he had brought on the table and asked, "Who has upset Ningzhu?"

Xue Ningzhu stood up and declared indignantly, "You have, Young Lord Brother!"

Xue Jinglin laughed despite himself. "How have I upset you? I haven't even asked you yet how the scepter hairpin ended up as a wager."

Xue Ningzhu said, "Young Lord Brother, I want Sister Jiang to be my sister-in-law. Will you go to her family and propose?" She had clearly taken Jiang Yi's joke at face value.

Xue Jinglin's smile remained unchanged, but something in his eyes turned cool. "Who put these ideas in your head?"

Xue Ningzhu shook her head. "No one. Sister Jiang is so wonderful — I want her to be my sister-in-law."

Xue Jinglin sat down. "Ningzhu, your Sister Jiang is destined to enter the palace. She cannot be your sister-in-law."

Xue Ningzhu did not understand. "But Sister Jiang hasn't entered the palace yet. Why can't she? Brother, couldn't you just go and propose sooner?"

Xue Jinglin smiled without answering.

He slid the pastries toward Xue Ningzhu. "Have a little something first. The evening banquet won't be starting for a while."

Xue Ningzhu picked up a crystal cake and took a bite, then wrinkled her nose. "Not as good as the ones at Sister Jiang's."

Xue Jinglin said, "Is everything better at your Sister Jiang's?"

Xue Ningzhu nodded vigorously. "Yes, everything is better! Don't you think so too, Young Lord Brother?"

Xue Jinglin watched his sister eating her pastries and listened to her recount everything that had happened at the Wanxiang Pavilion. His eyes drifted to the five-bat scepter hairpin in her hair, and he found himself thinking of Jiang Mi's bearing during the pitch-pot contest, her posture and movements. He let his mind wander.

Of course he thought so.

......

The evening banquet at the Fengchi temporary palace was far less formal than those in the capital. All the guests who had accompanied the hunt gathered together in the great hall.

The female guests and male guests were seated at separate tables.

Jiang Mi followed the palace servants to the Cheng'en Marquessate's table and sat beside Jiang Yi.

Their seats were positioned quite far forward — by rights, the Cheng'en Marquessate's table should not have been this close to the front.

The entire hall of guests waited as the ruler arrived fashionably late.

Xiao Huaiyan, dressed in an ink-black dragon robe, carried a sharpness that his usual mild manner concealed.

High-bridged nose, thin lips, peerless beauty — his long, narrow phoenix eyes curved with a smile, sending hearts fluttering throughout the hall of noble young ladies.

Songs and dances began; strings and pipes filled the air. The banquet was in full swing.

Prince Zhaonan took the occasion of presenting a toast to the Emperor to say, "For this hunt, this subject has prepared a new dance piece for Your Majesty. This subject humbly hopes Your Majesty will be pleased to watch."

Xiao Huaiyan smiled. "Very well, We grant it."

Low drumbeats began to sound, answered by horn calls, as if a battlefield had come alive in the hall.

A line of young women dressed in red, each wielding a pair of swords, took to the air in a sweeping, fluid dance. The twin blades moved in their hands like serpents, like soaring dragons.

When the face of the dancer became clear, everyone could not help but admire Prince Zhaonan's audacity.

It was Princess Shuyi.

Performing a twin-sword dance before the Emperor — truly an unconventional gambit.

Yet the dance had captured every gaze. Accustomed to soft and flowing performances, the crowd found this sword dance especially rousing on the eve of a hunt.

Jiang Mi watched for a while. Princess Shuyi's sword dance was original and striking — Xiao Huaiyan was surely watching with great pleasure.

It had been the same in her previous life. Everyone had been scheming and striving to please Xiao Huaiyan, desperate to win his favor.

Her own exotic dance, Princess Shuyi's sword dance — all of it had been for that same purpose.

How exhausting, Jiang Mi thought. All that scheming and effort, just to earn one fleeting glance of the ruler's compassion.

She did not want to live in that constant anxiety again. She would not pour out her heart only to have it treated as worthless. She did not want to spend her days trying to read another person's mind, or to be utterly helpless when catastrophe loomed.

Jiang Mi found this kind of banquet utterly dull. She would have much preferred soaking in the hot spring.

She had no idea when it would end, and feared that after Princess Shuyi's performance, others would come forward to offer their own entertainments.

She lowered her head and took a small sip of the spiced wine.

Up on the imperial seat, although Xiao Huaiyan wore a faint smile, his fingers were tapping the tabletop one by one. His eyes seemed to be watching the sword dance, yet seemed also to be fixed on some point elsewhere in the banquet hall.

Xiao Huaiyan's gaze rested on one particular person. He watched her — her expression drifting, staring blankly at the sword dance for a moment before turning to drink alone, as though quietly nursing some sorrow.

Xiao Huaiyan glanced over at Li Fu.

Li Fu's heart gave a small jolt. What riddle was His Majesty posing now?

For some reason Li Fu could not explain — like a conditioned reflex — he snapped his gaze over to the Jiang girl.

He saw that while everyone else was watching the sword dance with great enthusiasm, Miss Jiang had her head lowered, holding her cup in lonely contemplation and taking a quiet sip.

Good heavens. Was Miss Jiang jealous and unhappy?

Li Fu suddenly understood the meaning of the Emperor's glance. He wanted Miss Jiang to stop drinking?

He quietly slipped away and called Chengzhong over.

He told Chengzhong, "Go and replace Miss Jiang's wine with a jug that won't go to her head."

Chengzhong looked puzzled. "Godfather, the ladies' side is already being served fruit wine. It should not be very strong."

Li Fu tapped him on the forehead. "Then bring a fresh jug of grape juice — not a single drop of wine added."

Chengzhong said, "Understood. Godfather can rest easy."

......

Just as Jiang Mi had predicted, the moment Princess Shuyi's dance ended, Xiao Huaiyan bestowed a reward.

Then, almost immediately, someone rose to play the guqin. Jiang Mi looked up — it was Shen Yawei.

The clear, bright notes of the instrument swept across the hall. The piece was the March of the Prince of Lanling.

Jiang Mi smiled to herself. That Miss Shen had a clever mind too. Though, if played on a pipa instead, it would have suited the piece even better.

She found herself, rather unexpectedly, envying Xiao Huaiyan — he had no shortage of pleasures for the eye and ear.

Just as she reached to refill her fruit wine, she found a new jug placed in front of her and her previous one taken away.

Jiang Mi looked up at the one who had made the exchange. Why was it Chengzhong?

Chengzhong bowed and said, "Miss Jiang, this fresh jug has been specially prepared for you. Please try it."

Jiang Mi found it odd, but said nothing.

Before Chengzhong left, he could not help saying, "Miss Jiang, please do not overthink things."

Jiang Mi frowned. What was she overthinking?

She poured herself a cup from the new jug and took a small sip. The moment it entered her mouth, her expression shifted slightly. She swallowed it slowly.

Had Chengzhong brought the wrong thing? Why had she been given a jug of grape juice?

Jiang Mi turned it over in her mind for a moment, then decided she would rather not touch the grape juice at all.

With nothing better to do, she reached into the fruit tray, took out a mandarin orange, peeled it, carefully picked away the pith, and placed a bright orange segment into her mouth.

Sweet and clean, with just the faintest edge of tartness. A satisfying flavor.

She was still eating when Jiang Yi leaned over and whispered, "Tangtan, is that Miss Shen not taking a page from your book? This piece she's playing sounds remarkably similar to what you performed at the Emperor's birthday banquet. All the military men seem to be singing its praises." She felt it was a slight against Tangtan.

Jiang Mi tucked a segment of orange into Jiang Yi's mouth. "Elder Sister Yi, never mind all that. Tell me — is this orange sweet?"

Jiang Yi bit into it and nodded enthusiastically. "Sweet!"

Jiang Mi handed her the already-peeled one and picked up a fresh orange.

Just as she was peeling it, Jiang Yi tugged at her sleeve. "Tangtan, look — the young man three rows diagonally across from us, the one with the darker complexion — doesn't he look a little like the 'dark charcoal' I saw at the Qianshuang Temple?"

This piqued Jiang Mi's interest. She teased, "Your dark charcoal young lord?"

Jiang Yi pinched her. "Tangtan, do you think I should take advantage of the hunt tomorrow and go find him privately?"

Jiang Mi smiled. "And what would you say to him? That you don't want to marry him? Elder Sister Yi, why don't you observe him during the hunt first? If he turns out to be a fine match, you shouldn't let the chance pass."

As she chatted with Jiang Yi, Jiang Mi glanced over toward the tables where the male guests were seated, searching in the direction Jiang Yi had described for the young lord Zhang.

She did not find him — but she did find Shen Qianxiu.

Shen Qianxiu had just finished speaking with someone and turned around, catching Jiang Mi's gaze directly. He paused for a moment, then gave her a small nod.

Jiang Mi startled, and quickly looked away.

......

Over at the imperial seat, Li Fu noticed that the Emperor was turning a mandarin orange over in his hand — and could not quite tell whether he intended to eat it or not.

Li Fu said quietly, "Your Majesty, shall this servant have a palace maid peel the orange for you?"

Xiao Huaiyan gave Li Fu a flat look.

Li Fu promptly fell silent again.

Very well — he would stop guessing at the Emperor's mind.

A short while later, he saw the Emperor toss the orange back into the fruit tray. His expression appeared calm, but for reasons Li Fu could not explain, he felt the Emperor's eyes had turned very dark and heavy.

Li Fu followed the direction of the Emperor's gaze — and oh dear. Miss Jiang, what was she doing peering over at the male guests' side of the hall?

What was there to look at over there? Just a bunch of noble young lords.

If she was going to look at anyone, she ought to be looking up here.

......

When Shen Yawei finished her piece and the waves of praise washed over her, her heart was full of emotion. She had played well, and she understood the savagery of battle and the danger the Emperor had faced at Yunzhou. She had hoped this piece might resonate with him.

She also wondered what reward he might give her.

Shen Yawei waited quietly for a moment — but heard nothing.

She looked up toward the dais. The Emperor seemed to have just turned his gaze back from somewhere, and spoke in his usual tone. "Very well. Give a reward."

Shen Yawei was stunned. Just those three words?

No different at all from what he had said to Princess Shuyi?

Why had Jiang Mi been given the guqin Lüqi, when she was no less accomplished? What made the difference?

Shen Yawei swallowed the sourness in her heart, gave her thanks, and returned to her seat.

She glanced over at Jiang Mi, who was peeling a mandarin orange with great unhurried ease. That air of breezy indifference made Shen Yawei's hands clench tight in her lap.

Shen Yawei looked over at Princess Shuyi as well — she seemed quite pleased with her own reward.

The banquet slowly drew toward its end. Everyone was full of anticipation for the next day's hunt.

The various factions among them harbored a quiet rivalry, each hoping to distinguish themselves in front of the Emperor.

When the Emperor departed, everyone rose to bow and see him off.

Having eaten her fill of mandarin oranges at the banquet, Jiang Mi returned to East Pavilion Garden with Jiang Yi, ready to get a good night's sleep in preparation for the hunt the following day.

Before she fell asleep, she thought of the gift of thanks Xiao Huaiyan had mentioned, and felt a headache coming on. She still had no idea what she could hunt to serve as a fitting offering.

chapters.title: Forty eight
chapters.content:
The Fengchi Hunting Grounds were some distance from the temporary palace. Though they would return to the palace to rest at the end of the day, there would still be time spent resting at camp in between.

The maids of East Pavilion Garden helped the two Jiang ladies pack what they would need.

Early the next morning, Jiang Mi changed into her lotus-pink riding outfit, draped her apricot cloak over her shoulders, and set out from East Pavilion Garden with Jiang Yi, heading toward the waiting carriages that would take them to the hunting grounds.

A bright voice rang out from not far away. "Sister Jiang, wait for me!"

Jiang Mi turned to see Xue Ningzhu and the young lord Xue Jinglin walking toward them.

Jiang Mi stopped.

Xue Ningzhu rushed to Jiang Mi's side and linked arms with her. "Sister Jiang, I want to go to the hunting grounds with you."

Xue Jingyuan looked at Jiang Mi with an apologetic expression. "Miss Jiang, might I have a word with you in private?"

Jiang Mi considered for a moment, then nodded, and stepped with him to the shade of a nearby ginkgo tree. "What did the Young Lord wish to say?"

Xue Jinglin first cupped his hands in a bow. "Yesterday you helped my sister. I offer my thanks again on her behalf."

Jiang Mi understood. She had expected the young lord to ask Xue Ningzhu about the pitch-pot wager. "Young Lord speaks too seriously. It was nothing at all."

Xue Jinglin looked at the young woman standing before him — composed and serene, her tone light with a hint of a smile — as though it really had been nothing.

If he had not witnessed with his own eyes how tense and resolute she had been during that pitch-pot contest, he might actually have believed it was nothing.

He had seen too many people who inflated their contributions or used a favor as leverage. That Miss Jiang could be so unaffected was genuinely rare.

Xue Jinglin said, "My sister has made a fuss about wanting to join Miss Jiang for the hunt. I fear I may be unable to keep an eye on her, and must ask Miss Jiang to look after her for me."

Jiang Mi nodded. "Of course. Then Ningzhu shall come with me. Young Lord need not worry."

Xue Jinglin saw that she had not hesitated in the slightest and had agreed without reservation.

It was clear she genuinely cared for Ningzhu.

His gaze toward her softened. "Miss Jiang has helped my sister time and again without complaint. I am deeply grateful. Should Miss Jiang ever face any difficulty in the future, I will do everything in my power to help."

Jiang Mi was taken aback that Young Lord Xue would say such a thing.

It was no light promise.

Xue Jinglin saw her mouth open slightly in surprise, a look of astonishment on that beautiful face — an expression of such unguarded charm that he could not quite put it into words.

Xue Jinglin smiled, cupped his hands in a brief bow, and departed without waiting for her reply.

......

Jiang Mi and her companions arrived at their destination. Stepping out of the carriage, she could see the encampment already established.

A great many people had arrived ahead of them, all waiting for the Emperor's procession.

The imperial relatives and noble families were brimming with fighting spirit, eager to show their mettle.

Among them, the old aristocratic houses, the newly risen families, the military households, and the civil officials were all quietly sizing one another up. The young lords had already divided themselves into groups and settled on their individual targets.

Jiang Mi learned that the area designated for the female guests was a stretch of woodland to the south, stocked with small, tame, and harmless game — meant to give the ladies something to participate in, with safety as the priority.

If one wished to hunt mid-sized game, one would need to head east.

To the northwest was territory strictly off-limits to female guests — the game there was large, and even the men who ventured in required armed escorts to guard against unexpected dangers.

Jiang Mi was quietly calculating. She wondered whether the southern woodland would have anything suitable to present as a gift of thanks.

If not, she would likely have no choice but to head east.

From ahead came the thundering of approaching hooves. A party of riders came sweeping toward them, led by Xiao Huaiyan astride Zidian — his face fine-featured as carved jade, his bearing that of a dragon in flight, commanding and magnificent.

He held the reins in one hand and brought the lightning-fast black stallion to a halt, looking down with imperial detachment at the bowing mass of officials stretching out before him.

"Today at the Fengchi Hunt, each person shall rely on their own abilities. Whoever hunts the most and the finest game shall be richly rewarded by Us."

The moment Xiao Huaiyan finished speaking, the military men responded with a roar of approval. The officials and young lords escorting the Emperor into the forest mounted their horses, waiting only for the signal.

Xiao Huaiyan's lips curved in a faint smile. He withdrew his gaze and urged Zidian forward into the dense woodland.

At his movement, the officials and nobles all fell in behind him and entered the forest together.

Dust rose, momentum swelling.

Once the imperial procession departed, the female guests gradually mounted their horses and followed.
