Jiang Mi's group was about to head toward the eastern woods when someone called to her from behind.

"Tangtan, A'Yi, wait a moment."

Jiang Mi turned. It was her cousin Jiang Hong — and following behind him were Shen Qianxiu and Shen Yawei.

Jiang Mi looked surprised. "Elder Cousin, what is this?"

Jiang Hong said, "Tangtan, Father told me to come check on you and A'Yi first, and then go meet up with him after you've settled in a bit."

He introduced the people beside him. "This is Young Lord Shen and Miss Shen of Grand Secretary Shen's household. Since we happen to be going the same way, the four of us can ride together."

Shen Qianxiu cupped his hands in greeting. Shen Yawei gave Jiang Mi a nod and a smile. "Miss Jiang, it seems we truly are fated to keep crossing paths. My third brother and your cousin are close friends, as it turns out."

Jiang Mi found it puzzling — when had her cousin and Shen Qianxiu become acquainted?

Their group, all told, had turned out to be quite large.

There were advantages to numbers — and disadvantages.

They wandered through the woodland for quite some time without sighting a single piece of game.

Jiang Mi gripped her bow and arrows, thinking that if they kept meandering like this, they would come back empty-handed by nightfall.

Shen Qianxiu could see that Jiang Mi was growing anxious. He turned to Jiang Hong, who had been talking without pause, and said, "Brother Jiang, this stretch of forest looks safe enough. If we spread out a little, it will be easier to find game. If anything happens, a shout should carry far enough for the others to hear."

Jiang Hong agreed readily. He reminded Jiang Mi and Jiang Yi to be careful, and rode ahead to scout.

Jiang Mi urged her horse to the left to investigate — she thought she had glimpsed a gray shadow moving in the grass just now. Something like a rabbit.

But after searching through the undergrowth, she found nothing.

Shen Yawei watched as the group slowly dispersed, leaving Jiang Mi with only a young maid at a short distance behind her.

She steered her own horse idly through the woods, and noticed a plain strip of cloth hanging from a tree branch. She looked around.

Then she called out, "Third Brother, Miss Jiang — I think I see a fox over here! Come quickly!"

Jiang Mi was not far from Shen Yawei, and at the call, she steered toward her.

She turned back to instruct Xue Ningzhu, "Ningzhu, don't rush — come along with Elder Sister Yi."

Xue Ningzhu nodded obediently. She knew Sister Jiang wanted to catch game, and she would not hold her back.

Foxes were the cleverest of creatures and fast movers — if she arrived too late, it would be gone.

When Jiang Mi reached the spot, she saw a flash of red darting through the undergrowth.

A fire fox.

Jiang Mi spurred her horse in pursuit, drawing an arrow from her quiver and nocking it to the bowstring. She loosed it at the brilliant red shape.

The arrow missed. The fox dodged away.

Jiang Mi adjusted her posture and drew again, aiming at the fire fox's left hind leg. She released.

It missed again. The arrow fell wide.

She pressed the fox hard, refusing to let it go. Urgency was creeping in. The wind rushed past her ears, and within it, she thought she heard a faint, warm voice, measured and unhurried...

"The more you want it, the less you can rush. Watch the quarry scrambling away in panic, and simply steady yourself — fix your eyes on your target, and let the arrow go as naturally as you would in practice."

Jiang Mi squeezed her eyes shut and gave a sharp shake of her head, trying to drive that voice out.

She nocked the arrow again and loosed three in quick succession.

The fire fox was driven into a corner with nowhere to run.

She drew once more and sent the arrow straight into its left hind leg.

The fire fox tumbled to the base of a tree and lay still, unable to move.

Jiang Mi swung off her horse. She could hear hoofbeats approaching from behind — likely Shen Qianxiu and the others.

She walked toward the fire fox, wanting to check its condition.

Jiang Mi had barely crouched down when, without warning, a gleaming arrow came shooting toward her.

By the time she registered it and turned her head, the arrow was already there — no time to dodge.

In the blink of an eye, a horse came flying into view. A figure in dark teal lunged in front of Jiang Mi, and the arrow grazed his arm. Blood quickly seeped through the fabric.

The man pressed his hand over his arm and sank to one knee. "Miss Jiang, are you hurt?"

Jiang Mi stared at the man before her, then at the arrow that had fallen to the ground, then at the wound on his arm. "Young Lord Xue, your hand..."

Xue Jinglin gave her a small smile. "It is nothing — only a surface wound."

Jiang Mi looked at his ashen face, and knowing that his health had never been strong, knew that this arrow, coming with such force, was not as minor as he made it sound.

A moment later, Jiang Hong and Shen Qianxiu and the others came riding in from different directions.

"Tangtan!"

"Miss Jiang!"

At the same moment, another group of riders burst into the clearing.

"Oh my, so my little fox was hiding here! Tch — Jiang Mi, you've stolen quite a prize. I've been chasing this fox for half the day!"

Jiang Mi looked up to see Princess Shuyi wearing an expression of exaggerated disappointment, her eyes cutting over with clear provocation.

Jiang Mi picked up the broken arrow from the ground. "Princess Shuyi — was this shot by you?"

Princess Shuyi said, "Miss Jiang, these things happen during a hunt — an arrow going astray is unavoidable. That fox was mine; I had my eye on it from the start. Somehow you got here before me. My companion shot without seeing you — that is entirely the fault of these dense trees blocking the sightlines."

Beside Princess Shuyi, a young woman stood pale with fright, her hands trembling where they held the bow. She did not dare contradict the Princess.

Jiang Mi contained her fury. Princess Shuyi had even found herself a scapegoat.

Shen Qianxiu was also furious. The deliberateness of it was plain to see. He said, "Princess Shuyi, is Prince Zhaonan aware of what you have done here? Everyone present today is a court official or a family member of one. Any harm done to them would be an extremely serious matter. More to the point, Miss Jiang is the niece of the Empress Dowager, and the present Emperor is renowned for his filial devotion. If it were known that you raised your hand against a member of the Empress Dowager's family, how would you explain yourself before the Emperor? And how would your father appear before the Empress Dowager to beg forgiveness? As matters stand, Young Lord Xue has taken an arrow meant for Miss Jiang — does Princess Shuyi intend to return to the capital at once and make her apologies to the Grand Princess?"

Princess Shuyi's face went rigid. Being called out so bluntly to her face was humiliating, and as she thought through the consequences, a creeping dread began to set in.

She had acted in a moment of heat, egged on by someone else, and had come up with this way to teach Jiang Mi a lesson.

But no matter how much she looked down on Jiang Mi, the woman was still the Empress Dowager's niece.

Princess Shuyi dismounted and approached Xue Jinglin, reaching out as if to examine his wound.

"Young Lord Xue, this was my fault. I failed to control my companion, and you were hurt because of it. I will go find a physician at once."

The hand she extended was moved aside by Xue Jinglin. He lowered his eyes, gave a low cough, and said, "The Princess should conduct herself with more propriety. And the person to whom the Princess owes an apology is not me."

chapters.title: Forty nine
chapters.content:
Forced by the situation, Princess Shuyi gave Jiang Mi a grudging, insincere apology — and without waiting for a response, she mounted her horse and rode away with her companions.

Xue Jinglin watched the direction of her departure with a slight frown, then quickly withdrew his gaze.

Jiang Hong stepped forward, visibly anxious, and asked Jiang Mi, "Tangtan, are you all right? Princess Shuyi has gone too far. When we get back, we will absolutely demand an explanation from Prince Zhaonan." He was beside himself with worry, terrified that Jiang Mi might have so much as a scratch. If Tangtan were hurt, neither his father nor their aunt would forgive him.

Jiang Mi shook her head. "I am fine. Fortunately, Young Lord Xue arrived in time and took the arrow for me."

Jiang Hong turned to Xue Jinglin and expressed his sincere gratitude.

Jiang Mi looked at Xue Jinglin with concern. "Young Lord Xue, your wound..."

Shen Qianxiu, who had been silent until now, stepped forward and held out a small bottle of medicinal powder to Xue Jinglin. "The Young Lord's wound is still bleeding. Use this powder to treat it for now, and have a physician come to dress it properly once we return to camp."

Xue Jinglin accepted the medicine and said to Shen Qianxiu, "Thank you, Young Lord Shen. But it really is only a minor wound — the bleeding will stop quickly. We are far from camp here, and going back now would mean coming away empty-handed today. Why not stay and look around the woods a little longer? There may yet be something to find."

Xue Jinglin tore the cloth around the wound open. There was a long gash — not terribly deep, but it looked raw and bloodied.

Jiang Mi frowned. "Young Lord, are you certain you should not return to camp and let a physician look at it?"

Xue Jinglin applied the medicinal powder from Shen Qianxiu's bottle. "I will be fine."

As Jiang Mi passed by Xue Jinglin, she said quietly, "Young Lord, please do not push yourself. It is more important to let your injury heal."

Xue Jinglin's expression shifted slightly. He looked up and gave Jiang Mi a small, warm smile. "Thank you for your concern, Miss Jiang."

Just then, Jiang Yi arrived with Xue Ningzhu. When Xue Ningzhu saw her brother's wound, she burst into frightened tears.

Xue Jinglin moved to comfort her.

Jiang Mi asked curiously, "Young Lord, were you not accompanying the Emperor's group in the other direction? How did you come to be here?"

Xue Jinglin paused, then said, "I had gone into the forest intending to hunt a deer, but I saw someone fall from their horse while chasing a roe deer. It made me think of Ningzhu — I was worried she might act rashly, so I came back this way to check on her."

Xue Ningzhu, fearing her brother might forbid her from staying with Sister Jiang, quickly said, "Young Lord Brother, I have been very good. I listened to everything Sister Jiang said."

......

Princess Shuyi had not gone far. She watched from a distance as Jiang Mi's group seemed to be discussing where to go next.

She glared in their direction with deep resentment. All she had wanted was to give Jiang Mi a little lesson, and she had ended up making a fool of herself.

She could not let go of her anger.

The companion beside Princess Shuyi tried to coax her. "Princess, shall we head back? Or maybe look for game in another part of the forest?"

Princess Shuyi ignored her. She watched as Jiang Mi and the sickly young lord seemed to be moving toward a particular path. She had been along that way before.

Something came to her. She mounted her horse and rode in that same direction.

......

Having already caught a fire fox, Jiang Mi felt less desperate.

She looked into the undergrowth. A gray-red shape darted through the grass. Drawing closer, she saw it was a pheasant.

This time she did not rush. She slowly drew an arrow and nocked it. Just as she was about to release — from not far away came a loud, heavy thud.

Immediately after, she heard Jiang Yi, Ningzhu, and Shen Yawei all cry out in alarm.

"Miss Jiang, run!"

"Tangtan, run!"

Jiang Mi turned. Not far away, an enormous wasp nest had fallen to the ground. A dark, seething cloud of wasps came swarming toward her.

The color drained from her face. Her whole body went cold.

These wasps were venomous. A mass of them like this could disfigure at best — and kill at worst.

She ripped off her cloak and wrapped it around every inch of exposed skin just in time. Beneath her, the mare had been stung and panicked, surging forward at a terrifying speed — no longer the docile creature from before — as if trying to throw her from the saddle.

She could not see what lay ahead. All she could do was grip the reins with everything she had and not be thrown.

She had no idea how long she rode. Gradually, the horse slowed.

The buzzing had stopped. It seemed she was safe.

Jiang Mi lifted the cloak from over her head. The silence around her was absolute.

She had long since lost any sense of direction or location.

The Fengchi Hunting Grounds were vast — dense forest, dense undergrowth. Beneath her, the horse felt restless and uneasy.

She heard a rustling in the grass.

A gray wolf slowly emerged, its fangs bared, and launched itself at her.

In that instant, her horse bolted. Her mind went completely blank as she clung to the reins.

How could there be a wolf in the hunting grounds?

Even when large game was released, nothing with a predatory nature would ever be set loose here.

Jiang Mi's blood ran cold. She trembled all over.

She didn't dare look behind her. She felt as though even the air rushing past smelled of blood.

But that blood — it was not from the wolf chasing her from behind. It was coming from ahead.

The trees and grass lining the path were marked with streaks of blood, and from somewhere came the faint sounds of screaming.

Her horse crashed through the dense undergrowth into a clearing, and what she saw made her feel as though she had stumbled into hell itself.

Severed limbs and remains — some belonging to wolves, some to men.

Blood soaked the air.

Jiang Mi's stomach lurched, wave after wave. She nearly retched.

She no longer had the strength to control the horse. She was swaying in the saddle, on the verge of falling.

At that moment, a black stallion came flying through the air like lightning. A pair of powerful arms swept out and caught Jiang Mi, pulling her into their hold. A pair of deep phoenix eyes looked down at her. "How did you come to be here?"

Jiang Mi stared at that face — familiar yet so far away — and shook violently, unable to speak a word. She pressed her hand over her mouth, desperate not to be sick.

Xiao Huaiyan lowered her from the horse, patted her on the back. "If you need to be sick, be sick. Don't hold it in."

Jiang Mi could barely stand without Xiao Huaiyan's arms to hold her up. She was sick until her whole body went limp.

The air she was breathing was still thick with the smell of blood, and the sounds of battle had not yet ceased.

The wolf that had been chasing her had by now been pierced by a dozen arrows.

And a number of men in black were locked in combat with the Embroidered Guard.

Xiao Huaiyan had been ambushed?

Jiang Mi looked up at him. He appeared completely unruffled, a faint smile at the corner of his lips. "Is Miss Jiang feeling better?"

Jiang Mi's hair was disheveled, her appearance utterly wretched.

She let go of the arm she had been holding, and said, "Thank you, Your Majesty, for saving me."

Jiang Mi had barely steadied herself when her foot slipped on something, and she lurched forward — stumbling straight into Xiao Huaiyan.

At that same moment, a hidden arrow shot toward Xiao Huaiyan.

He had just caught the warm, soft figure when his expression suddenly changed. An arrow was aimed directly at Jiang Mi's back.

In the very last instant, Xiao Huaiyan reached out and intercepted it — but not before the arrowhead had already sunk an inch deep.

chapters.title: Fifty
chapters.content:
The pain made Jiang Mi's vision go dark, and she nearly lost consciousness.
In that moment, only one thought occupied her mind: Xiao Huaiyan and she were cursed to bring each other nothing but misfortune — every encounter with him ended in disaster.
Xiao Huaiyan's eyes went cold as frost. He snapped the shaft of the arrow off, gathered the person in his arms, and swung back onto his horse.
With one hand holding the reins, he looked toward the ongoing fight in the distance and called out, "Pei Chi — bring the net down! We want them alive."
Pei Chi had just run a black-clad assassin through. He pulled his blade back, warm blood spattering his face, and dropped to one knee in the direction of Xiao Huaiyan's voice. "This subject obeys the imperial command."
The assassins, already at their last strength, were no match for the Embroidered Guard closing in around them. The remaining men in black covered their leader as they fled deeper into the forest.
......
Jiang Mi felt her consciousness growing dimmer and dimmer. The pain from the arrow wound in her back came in waves, making her dizzy.
She even found herself thinking: why couldn't she simply fall unconscious and be spared this torment?
She had no choice but to lean against Xiao Huaiyan's chest. His warmth and the distinctive scent of dragon saliva incense that clung to him wrapped around her.
Even with his great cloak shielding her from the wind, she still felt cold. Her body trembled beyond her control.
She wanted desperately to draw more warmth toward her, but she did not even have the strength to move.
Xiao Huaiyan felt the trembling in his arms and looked down, meeting those dazed apricot eyes. Jiang Mi's small face had gone completely bloodless, her lips pale as paper.
"Jiang Mi..." the two words rested briefly on his lips before he spoke, low and quiet. "Hold on a little longer. We are almost there."
Jiang Mi could not make out what Xiao Huaiyan was saying. The wind roared past her ears, and her eyelids felt impossibly heavy, and exhausted, and so very weary...
Xiao Huaiyan saw her eyes slowly closing. The arm around her tightened. He reined Zidian to an abrupt halt.
He pressed his fingers beneath her nose. Her breath was faint.
His thin lips pressed together. He closed his eyes for a brief moment, then urged Zidian forward again, racing in the direction of the temporary palace.
Zidian moved like the wind itself. The attendants who followed were left far behind, unable to keep pace.
Zidian cleared the shadowed forest in one great leap and broke onto the open road. Li Fu and Chengzhong and the others stared after him in stunned silence. They had assumed the Emperor would go to the camp first — no one had anticipated he would go directly to the palace.
They had no choice but to continue the chase.
Back at the Fengchi Hunting Grounds, the atmosphere had turned tense and fearful.
Wolves had appeared in the grounds, injuring a number of people.
Word had also spread that a mass of black-clad assassins had seized the chaos to attempt the Emperor's life. The Embroidered Guard and imperial soldiers were combing through the mountain inch by inch, ensuring the safe departure of the noble families while leaving no suspicious person unaccounted for.
Those who had remained near the camp were shaken but unharmed. Others, less fortunate, had been gravely wounded — some had not survived at all.
The Jiang family lingered and refused to leave, still searching for their young lady who had gone missing in the forest.
Some of those who had come back alive from the western forest said they had seen a girl from the Jiang family struck by a stray arrow. Others said the Jiang girl had taken an arrow meant for the Emperor and had rendered a service in protecting him.
Word was that the Jiang girl had been taken away for medical treatment.
More than a few people were puzzled — how had a girl from the Jiang family ended up in that dangerous place?
......
The palace servants and ladies-in-waiting remaining at the Fengchi Temporary Palace could not have imagined in their wildest dreams that the Emperor would ride on horseback directly to the sleeping quarters — and that when he dismounted, he appeared to be holding someone wrapped in a great cloak.
Xiao Huaiyan carried the person straight into the inner chamber and said in a cold, commanding voice to the attendant, "Go and bring Gu Tanghai here."
The attendant did not dare delay and rushed out at once to summon the imperial physician.
Xiao Huaiyan laid Jiang Mi down on the bed. Mindful of her wound, he positioned her face-down.
He removed her embroidered shoes and drew the quilts over her.
Seeing that she was still trembling, he turned to the palace maids. "Bring some braziers in and heat the room."
The maids set down the hot water they had just brought in and immediately went to carry out his instructions.
Xiao Huaiyan pressed his hand to Jiang Mi's forehead — she was still damp with cold sweat. He picked up a warm cloth and wiped her face.
His expression was unreadable as he looked at the frail woman before him.
This was the delicate body that had crashed into him and shielded him from a hidden arrow.
It was beyond belief — and at the same time, somehow absurd.
The cloth in his hand passed over her forehead and then gently over that disheveled, pitiful little face. Whatever she had been through, she looked thoroughly battered. Along the line of her slender neck and downward, the cold sweat had dampened the strands of hair clinging to her skin.
Xiao Huaiyan's gaze settled on the tightly fastened collar — and then his hand stilled.
At that moment, a pair of cool hands came to rest on the back of his hand. Several pale, slender fingers tried feebly to grip his, while her lips moved soundlessly, as if mouthing the word: cold.
Xiao Huaiyan enclosed those hands in his palms. Soft and small — truly fragile.
Footsteps sounded from beyond the screen. Chief Imperial Physician Gu rushed in with the medical attendant.
Xiao Huaiyan rose from the bedside. His eyes, cold and bright as winter stars, composed themselves. He let the kneeling physician and attendant come forward.
Chief Physician Gu first examined the color of the blood seeping from around the wound. It had not turned black — meaning the arrowhead was likely not poisoned. A measure of the tension in his chest released.
He pressed his fingers to her pulse through a cloth and read it, frowning involuntarily. Miss Jiang had not yet fully recovered her constitution, and now this arrow wound on top of it would make her recovery harder than for most people.
Though her life was not in danger, this injury would likely require a very long time to heal fully.
Chief Physician Gu withdrew his hand, stood, and reported to the Emperor. "Your Majesty, Miss Jiang's blood and vital energy are severely depleted. She will need ginseng broth to stabilize her before the arrow can be removed. This subject will go at once to prepare the medicine."
Xiao Huaiyan nodded.
Before long, Chief Physician Gu returned in person with the prepared ginseng broth and medicine for the arrow wound.
By this time the room had been heated, and the warmth was stifling — even the Chief Physician felt the waves of heat pressing against him.
The medical attendant and palace maids tended to Jiang Mi together, and between them managed to get half a bowl of the broth into her.
Xiao Huaiyan watched the medicine dribble down into her collar and frowned.
Chief Physician Gu judged that they had done enough for now, gave his instructions to the medical attendant, and withdrew.
The attendant stepped forward and carefully cut away the blood-soaked fabric around the wound, revealing the half-length broken shaft of the arrow.
She gently cleared a path around the injury and began to remove Jiang Mi's outer robe with a light, careful touch.
She noticed that the Emperor had not moved aside, nor had he raised any objection.
The medical attendant was inwardly shaken, and thought to herself: this Miss Jiang's fortune lies ahead. The fact that His Majesty showed no concern for propriety — there would likely be a new imperial consort before long. She understood perfectly: everything she had witnessed in this chamber, once she stepped outside that door, was to be wiped from her memory entirely. Not a single word about the Emperor's affairs was to pass her lips.
She steeled herself and continued, removing Jiang Mi's inner robe as well, leaving only a pale blue dudou, its fine strings wrapped around the nape of the neck and around the waist.
The skin of her back, white as frost, made the arrow wound look all the more stark and harrowing.
The medical attendant drew a quiet, sharp breath. She cleaned away the blood from around the wound and was about to reach in to draw out the arrow.
"Wait." Xiao Huaiyan stopped her.
His gaze, deep and dark, made the attendant take an involuntary step back.
Xiao Huaiyan sat on the edge of the bed and gathered the person lying there into his hold, letting her lie face-down across his lap. He told the medical attendant, "Have the medicine ready."
Then he fixed his gaze on the broken shaft for a long moment, raised his hand, and pulled it free in one clean motion.
In that instant, the person lying across his legs shuddered violently, and a broken whimper of pain escaped her.
The attendant felt the Emperor's motion had been too fast to follow. She did not dare delay — she applied the medicine to the wound at once.
She dressed the wound as quickly as she could, then retreated from the bedchamber.
Jiang Mi shook without stopping from the pain. She could not hold it in and began to cry, murmuring incoherently, "It hurts... it hurts so much..."
Xiao Huaiyan's voice came out low and rough. He let out a breath that was barely perceptible. "It will not hurt like this anymore. Not after this."
Jiang Mi could hear nothing. The pain shooting from her back was all-consuming — and she was utterly unaware that she lay half-undressed against Xiao Huaiyan's chest.
The medical attendant returned, carrying the second medicine Chief Physician Gu had prepared. "Your Majesty, this medicine will calm Miss Jiang's spirit and help her sleep, and will ease some of the pain she is in now."
Xiao Huaiyan took the bowl and dismissed the attendant.
He shifted Jiang Mi slightly so that she rested sideways against him, then spooned up a measure of the black medicine and held it to her lips.
But Jiang Mi was far less cooperative than she had been with the ginseng broth. Most of the medicine slid down her neck and into the hollow of her collarbone.
Xiao Huaiyan's phoenix eyes had turned as black as lacquer. He pressed his fingers to his brow and pushed down the inconvenient heat rising in him.
Jiang Mi was still crying softly, her voice delicate and almost impossibly small, and she was instinctively pressing herself closer against him.
The vein at Xiao Huaiyan's temple throbbed.
What he held was not a gentle, fragrant warmth — it was a burning coal.
He could not set her down, could not move freely, and had to restrain his every motion for fear of jarring her wound.
By the time three bowls of medicine had been coaxed into her through repeated attempts, Xiao Huaiyan's own robes were stained with the medicine she had spilled. He transferred Jiang Mi back to the bed and called the palace maids in to wipe her down and change her into clean sleeping clothes.
Xiao Huaiyan watched in weary silence. He reflected that even fighting the enemy army through an entire day and night in Yunzhou had never left him this exhausted.
Fortunately, the medicine took effect. Jiang Mi had stopped crying in pain and, by all appearances, had fallen asleep.
The palace maids tidied up and, not daring to linger, retreated quietly.
The bedchamber fell into stillness.
Xiao Huaiyan listened to the steady sound of her breathing and lowered his eyes, turning the jade ring on his finger without thinking.
Li Fu, who had been waiting outside the chamber for some time, watched Chief Physician Gu depart, and then saw the maids carrying medicine also withdraw. The room beyond was utterly quiet.
He said softly, "Your Majesty, Commander Pei has returned."
Xiao Huaiyan stepped out of the inner chamber. Li Fu bowed his head and continued, "All the officials and noble family members who had gone to the hunting grounds have returned to the temporary palace and have been settled in. Those who are injured have been sent physicians. The court officials and imperial relatives have been anxious about Your Majesty's safety and have been requesting an audience — they are currently waiting in the front hall."
Xiao Huaiyan gave a soft laugh. "They most likely want to know whether We were injured. There is no harm in seeing them."
He said to Li Fu, "You stay here and keep watch. Have her usual maidservant brought in to attend her."
Li Fu bowed. "As Your Majesty commands."
Xiao Huaiyan went to the front hall, set the officials' minds at ease, and patiently sent the court officials and imperial relatives off to rest. Then he summoned Pei Chi.

chapters.title: Fifty-one
chapters.content:
Pei Chi's flying-fish uniform was stained with a good deal of blood, with no time to change before presenting himself before the Emperor in the front hall.
Pei Chi reported, "Your Majesty, the captured assassins have been taken to the prison. Following leads extracted from them, we apprehended their contacts hiding in a farmhouse near the Fengchi Hunting Grounds. Among those people, we found Wang Luo — once a counselor to Prince Qi."
"Wang Luo?" Xiao Huaiyan turned a wolf-hair brush idly in his fingers and looked up. "This man once schemed on behalf of Prince Qi and Consort De and was behind many of their schemes. I assumed he would stay hidden in Jiangnan and never show his face again. I did not expect him to slip back into the capital."
"Has anything been extracted from him?"
Pei Chi's expression showed embarrassment. "This subject has failed Your Majesty. Nothing of use has been gotten from him yet."
Xiao Huaiyan smiled. "No matter."
He tapped the desk and asked, "Has it been established why Jiang Mi appeared in that place?"
Pei Chi said, "According to the Jiang family, Miss Jiang was attacked by a swarm of wasps which spooked her horse, and she ended up there by accident."
"Wasps?" Xiao Huaiyan raised an eyebrow. That was unexpected.
Pei Chi said, "This subject sent people to investigate. The wasp nest was shot down from the tree with an arrow. Of those in the vicinity at the time, besides the Jiang, Xue, and Shen parties, there was only Princess Shuyi of Prince Zhaonan's household."
"As it happens, Princess Shuyi was herself attacked by wolves — her left leg was bitten, and according to the imperial physicians, the injury is severe. Her lower leg may be permanently damaged." Pei Chi recalled being present when the Princess was carried in, her skirts soaked with blood, her cries of pain unceasing.
Xiao Huaiyan said, "What a pity. Since Prince Zhaonan's beloved daughter has been injured, have the physicians attend to her carefully and ensure she recovers well. Tell Prince Zhaonan that it was the remnant faction of Prince Qi that led the assassins and the wolves into the hunting grounds. He'll have somewhere to direct his anger."
Pei Chi bowed. "Yes, Your Majesty."
Xiao Huaiyan considered for a moment, then said, "As for Wang Luo — I think I would like to see this man myself."
A thought seemed to come to him, and he said in a low voice, "Bring him here."
Before long, a middle-aged man covered in blood and filth was brought in under Embroidered Guard escort.
The man's face bore the scars of an old burn, giving him a fearsome appearance.
Xiao Huaiyan said, "Wang Luo?" This was nothing like the elegant and distinguished Wang Luo he remembered.
The man addressed as Wang Luo raised his head with difficulty. When he saw the person seated above, his voice came out hoarse. "So it is the Sixth Prince."
"Insolence!" Pei Chi swung the hilt of his blade at Wang Luo's legs. Wang Luo collapsed to his knees with a heavy thud.
Xiao Huaiyan smiled without concern. "The grass over Prince Qi's grave is nearly as tall as your knees by now. I am rather curious — what did he leave behind that still keeps men like you toiling in his service?"
Wang Luo pressed his face to the floor and gave a low, rattling laugh. Blood continued to seep from his mouth. "If you had not been so relentless in hunting us down, we would not have been driven to hiding in fear for our lives, staking everything on one last desperate throw."
Wang Luo saw that Xiao Huaiyan's expression remained entirely unchanged, entirely unmoved.
He continued, "Sixth Prince — in your vendetta to avenge your mother, you cast a wide net indeed. Are you certain you have killed every last person who had a hand in it? Ha. Imperial Noble Consort, Consort De, Consort Li — Prince Jin, Prince Qi, Prince Rui — every last soul connected to that affair met their end at your hands. And the Empress Dowager sitting high in her palace today — are her hands truly clean? Ha ha ha ha. As I recall, during the southern tour, more than a few people heard the sounds coming from Consort Jin's chambers at night. And as I remember, the Sixth Prince was also present at the time..."
Wang Luo did not finish his sentence. A bamboo wolf-hair writing brush flew directly through his throat. He convulsed for a moment and went still.
Pei Chi knelt to beg forgiveness.
Xiao Huaiyan pressed his fingers to his aching temple, lowered his eyes, and composed the emotion in them. "He was deliberately trying to provoke me — which means there is something he was concealing. Search every one of their hiding places to the last inch. Someone inside the hunting grounds today was working in coordination with him."
Pei Chi said, "Yes, Your Majesty."
Xiao Huaiyan stood. As he passed the body, he said, "Drag it out. Feed it to the dogs."
......
The night at the Fengchi Temporary Palace was cold and silent.
The officials and family members who had returned from the hunting grounds were all shaken and unable to sleep.
Shen Yawei sat in her room, uneasy, the hot tea in her hand long since gone cold. When the maid she had been waiting for finally returned, she asked, "Well?"
The maid shook her head. "Miss, this servant went to East Pavilion Garden and asked around. Miss Jiang is not there recovering from her injury."
Shen Yawei asked, "What about the Emperor's quarters?"
The maid looked uncomfortable. "This servant could not get anywhere near them. That part of the palace was all Embroidered Guard — they drove away anyone who came close."
Shen Yawei frowned and waved her away.
A short while later, the maid came back in. "Miss, Third Young Master is here."
Shen Yawei was somewhat surprised. At this hour, her third brother would not normally come to see her.
She went to the front room and found Shen Qianxiu.
"Third Brother, what brings you here?"
Shen Qianxiu dismissed all the maids and held out a strip of gray cloth. "A'Wei, do you recognize this?"
Shen Yawei's heart sank. She shook her head quickly. "Third Brother, I don't know what you mean."
"Today, you led Miss Jiang to chase the fire fox and she nearly came to harm from Princess Shuyi's arrow. Then came the wasp attack that spooked her horse and caused her to go missing — none of that had anything to do with you?"
Shen Yawei's face darkened. She said, aggrieved, "Third Brother, you have truly hurt me. I know you have feelings for Jiang Mi and you worry about her, but you cannot lay these things at my door. I was with all of you the entire time — how could any of it have been my doing?"
Shen Qianxiu looked at her steadily, then said in a grave tone, "A'Wei, it would be best if none of this has anything to do with you. Otherwise I will report it to Father and invoke family law."
Shen Yawei's eyes reddened and she began to cry. "Third Brother, do not wrong me. How could I do such a thing? I was even planning to help bring you and Jiang Mi together — why would I let Princess Shuyi hurt her?"
Shen Qianxiu watched Shen Yawei cry in genuine distress and frowned slightly. Had he been wrong after all?
He sighed. "A'Wei, stop crying. And do not go doing anything foolish again. Just now you actually had a maid go out to find out the Emperor's whereabouts — you have no idea what a serious transgression that is. The promise and honor that Elder Sister bought with her life — do not throw it away with your own hands."
Shen Yawei bit her lip and nodded. "Third Brother, I know I was wrong. I heard the Emperor had been attacked and I was beside myself with worry — I lost my head for a moment. I will never dare do it again."
After Shen Qianxiu's suspicions were put to rest, he gave his sister a few more reminders and took his leave.
Shen Yawei watched Shen Qianxiu's retreating back and dried her tears.
How remarkable Jiang Mi was. The young lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor took an arrow for her; her third brother was so devoted to Jiang Mi that he came to suspect his own sister. And now, through some twist of fate involving the assassins, she had ended up tangled with the Emperor as well — injured in his presence, and people were even saying she had taken an arrow for him and saved his life.
What utter nonsense.
Why hadn't the wolves that appeared out of nowhere simply bitten Jiang Mi along with everyone else?
......
Xiao Huaiyan returned to the bedchamber.
He submerged both hands in a basin of clear water — the joints of his fingers distinct, the hands long and elegant.
He washed those already-clean hands several times in an absent-minded way, then picked up the cloth beside him, wiped them, and tossed it back casually.
Li Fu dismissed the attendant who had been holding the basin.
He said, "Your Majesty, the maid Qiuyu is inside attending to Miss Jiang."
Xiao Huaiyan walked around the screen in silence and went in.
Qiuyu was in the middle of wiping Jiang Mi's face with a damp cloth. Hearing footsteps, she turned — and the sight of who it was made her knees nearly give way. She performed a deep bow. "Long live Your Majesty."
Xiao Huaiyan glanced at the person on the bed. "Has she woken?"
Qiuyu shook her head. "Miss occasionally opens her eyes but has not regained full awareness. She has a mild fever now. The medical attendant has gone to prepare medicine."
Xiao Huaiyan sat at the bedside and looked at the person lying face-down in sleep.
He reached out and touched her forehead. It was warm.
The cool touch made Jiang Mi instinctively try to pull away, but any movement pulled at the wound, and she frowned with discomfort.
Jiang Mi lay on her side, sleeping very restlessly. In her feverish state she would occasionally open her eyes in a daze, only to close them again quickly — it was plain she had not truly come to.
That cool hand did not withdraw. After a little while, Jiang Mi's lashes trembled, and those apricot eyes opened, brimming with tears and fixing a bleary stare on the person before her.
"Awake?" Xiao Huaiyan asked.
Qiuyu, standing to one side and wishing she could sink into the floor, stole a glance at her young mistress. She was not truly awake — she was still in a daze. In this state, she would not fully recognize people.
Qiuyu wanted to say something on her mistress's behalf, but did not dare speak out of turn.
At that moment the medical attendant came in carrying the medicine. She too was mildly startled to find the Emperor had returned.
When she saw the Emperor extend his hand, she brought the medicine over.
Before the attendant left, she caught Qiuyu's eye and gestured for her to come out as well. Once they were outside, the attendant saw Qiuyu's furrowed brow and said, "What is the matter? Is it not a good thing for the Emperor to personally look after your mistress?"
Qiuyu kept her head down and said nothing. She knew that for anyone else this would be a wonderful thing — but having watched everything her mistress had been through these past days, she had a feeling her mistress would not see it that way. And she could neither explain it nor be there at her side.
She had no idea what might happen to her mistress in that confused state.
Xiao Huaiyan spooned up a measure of medicine and held it to Jiang Mi's mouth. Jiang Mi opened her lips obediently and swallowed it.
He brought the next spoonful, but she would not open her mouth this time. She murmured softly, "Bitter. Tangtan wants pastries."
Xiao Huaiyan noted the slightly childlike quality in her voice.
The way she was just now reminded him of the night after she had fallen into the water and stayed in the warm room at Cining Palace — how she had shied away from his hand and burrowed deeper into the blankets.
Xiao Huaiyan instructed a palace maid to bring in some pastries. He picked one up and held it to her lips.
Jiang Mi took a bite. Sweetness spread through her mouth and her brow slowly relaxed.
She wanted more, but the hand holding the pastry moved it away.
A spoonful of medicine came to her mouth instead. "Drink this first, then eat."
Jiang Mi was terribly aggrieved.
She wanted to knock the medicine away, but was being held and could not move. She was so frustrated she wanted to stamp her foot.
One bare foot had just poked out from under the quilt — but even that small movement pulled at the wound. The pain made tears stream from her eyes.
Xiao Huaiyan's gaze slid briefly over those fair feet, then, seeing the person in his arms crying again, he held the pastry out to her once more.
Jiang Mi sniffled and took a large bite.
Xiao Huaiyan's fingers brushed lightly over her powdered lips. "That good?"
Jiang Mi put out the tip of her tongue and licked the corner of her mouth, accidentally grazing Xiao Huaiyan's fingertip in the process. She gave a small, artless smile. "Sweet."
Xiao Huaiyan's eyes turned very dark. There was not a trace of a smile on his face.
Jiang Mi seemed to sense something was wrong. On instinct she tried to draw back — but the wound still ached, and she felt her waist being gripped with painful force. She whimpered and began to cry.

chapters.title: Fifty two
chapters.content:
Xiao Huaiyan held the person in his arms so she could not move. The low sound of her weeping made his head ache all the more.
The bowl of medicine was knocked to the floor, and the bitter smell of it spread through the room.
He tilted Jiang Mi's face up toward him. Her apricot eyes were full of tears, her lips parted slightly as she drew breath, carrying a trace of frightened timidity — she was tense, trying to pull back.
Something turbulent moved through Xiao Huaiyan's phoenix eyes. The vicious edge in him surfaced. He gripped her chin and kissed her fiercely.
Tender softness, a sweet fragrance, so yielding he felt he could swallow her whole.
Jiang Mi could not breathe. Even her tears stopped in fright.
She struggled in a panic. The wound on her back flared with pain at the movement, and she bit down on the lips that were tormenting her.
Xiao Huaiyan pressed harder against those soft lips, even when the pain hit him. He did not let go immediately.
In the tangle, he allowed a gap for Jiang Mi to catch her breath.
The person in his arms had both hands pressed against his chest, eyes squeezed shut, breath ragged, letting out small, choked sounds...
Xiao Huaiyan raised his head. His eyes gradually cleared.
His own breathing had gone unsteady. He touched his lips with one finger — a small wound stung faintly.
Xiao Huaiyan checked Jiang Mi's injury at once. Fortunately there was no fresh bleeding.
He laid the person in his arms back on the bed, letting her rest face-down, and bent to brush away the teardrops clinging to her closed lashes.
Xiao Huaiyan drew the quilt back over Jiang Mi properly and told the medical attendant to prepare the medicine again.
This time he did not feed it to her himself. He sat to one side and watched quietly as the attendant and Qiuyu coaxed her into drinking.
This time, Jiang Mi drank obediently and without fuss.
Xiao Huaiyan held a piece of pastry to Jiang Mi's lips, but she would not open her mouth this time. Eyes still closed, she turned her head away and drifted back into sleep.
Xiao Huaiyan stood at the bedside in silence for a moment. He took the pastry in his hand and ate it himself, chewing slowly. He could not help but frown.
Nothing but sweet — bland and unremarkable. What was there to enjoy?
The quiet of the bedchamber settled around him. The person on the bed breathed more steadily. Xiao Huaiyan reached out and touched her forehead — the fever had broken. He brushed the strands of hair away from her face and murmured, "Jiang Mi. This gift of thanks — I accept it."
Xiao Huaiyan walked out of the bedchamber. The night wind caught his robes and sent them snapping. He walked alone into the boundless darkness.
Qiuyu stayed to keep watch through the night, and in her half-waking, half-sleeping state, she thought she heard her mistress softly crying.
Jiang Mi felt as though she had fallen into a nightmare.
In the dream, Xiao Huaiyan kissed her with a dark and cold ferocity, as though he wanted to devour her alive.
Her whole body ached — especially her back. The suffocating breathlessness of their tangled breathing left her gasping for air.
She did not know where she found the strength, but she bit down without thinking, and that gave her back her breath.
Jiang Mi's sleep was not peaceful...
The following morning, Jiang Mi opened her eyes. She was lying face-down. Half her body had gone numb from the position, and as she looked around, she could not, for a moment, remember where she was.
She started to shift onto her back, but the pain from her wound came into sharp, clear focus, and she drew a sharp breath.
Jiang Mi closed her eyes and waited, then opened them again. Nothing had changed.
At that moment Qiuyu came in carrying a basin of warm water to change her mistress's cloth compress. When she saw Jiang Mi awake, she said with glad surprise, "Miss, you've finally woken up."
Qiuyu quickly set down the basin, poured some warm water, and spooned it to Jiang Mi's lips. "Miss, drink some water first. The medical attendant said if you woke up, you should moisten your lips and throat before anything else, and not rush to speak."
Jiang Mi's mouth tasted of bitterness. The warm water sweetened with honey helped a little.
Her head felt heavy and dull.
"Where am I?" Jiang Mi's voice was weak, almost without energy.
Qiuyu said, "Miss, you were wounded by an arrow. The Emperor brought you back to the bedchambers of the temporary palace. This is the Mingxuan Hall."
Jiang Mi's vision swam darkly. The memory was returning — just before she lost consciousness, she had slipped on loose stones underfoot and stumbled toward Xiao Huaiyan, only for an arrow to find her back.
Jiang Mi sighed inwardly. Why was her luck so dreadful?
Qiuyu continued, "Miss, do not worry. You saved the Emperor's life — no one would dare gossip about you staying in the Mingxuan Hall."
Jiang Mi's expression turned strange. Uncertain, she said, "I... I saved the Emperor's life?"
Qiuyu nodded. "I heard it from Eunuch Chengzhong: when an assassin was attacking the Emperor from behind, it was you who rushed forward without a thought for your own safety and shielded His Majesty with your body." At that, Qiuyu looked at Jiang Mi with a tender, pitying expression. "Miss, you have suffered."
The words made sense to Jiang Mi, yet the meaning inside them struck her as profoundly strange.
She had tripped and fallen — and stumbled into credit for saving the Emperor's life?
Qiuyu, noticing that her mistress seemed to be in a daze, said, "Miss, just wait a moment — I will go call Attendant Chen to examine your wound." She thought her mistress must be hungry as well and needed to have the warm broth from the kitchen brought over.
Jiang Mi stared blankly ahead, straining to recall what had happened after she was hurt. Only a few hazy fragments surfaced in her mind.
That kiss in the nightmare — the one that had nearly stopped her breath — was it a dream, or had it truly happened?
Jiang Mi stared unfocused at a fixed point, her heart uneasy.
The medical attendant came in with Qiuyu. Jiang Mi looked at her — an unfamiliar face.
The attendant took her pulse, unfastened her inner robe and changed the wound dressing, then bound it again. She offered reassurance: "Miss, rest quietly. This wound will heal before long."
Jiang Mi thanked her softly.
Qiuyu helped her sit up for a simple wash, then fed her some plain rice porridge.
Once she had gathered a little strength, Jiang Mi said, "Qiuyu, I want to go back to East Pavilion Garden to recover." She could not sleep soundly in Xiao Huaiyan's bedchamber.
Qiuyu looked alarmed. "Miss, your wound cannot be disturbed. You need complete bed rest. If the wound tears open again, you will go through all that suffering for nothing. Besides — when the Emperor left the temporary palace, he specifically instructed that you were to stay here to recover."
"The Emperor is not in the palace?" Jiang Mi asked.
Qiuyu nodded. "Miss, you were unconscious the entire time. Now you are the only one remaining here to recover. The Emperor left first thing this morning, and all the attending officials and their family members went with him. Without the Emperor's permission, outside ministers cannot stay in the temporary palace for long. Second Miss wanted to remain with you, but the Marquess took her away. Princess Shuyi was bitten by a wolf and injured her leg — the physicians attended to her on the journey, but she could not stay either."
All those words tired Jiang Mi a little to follow.
But she caught the key points.
Xiao Huaiyan was gone from the palace. That eased her mind somewhat.
Elder Sister Yi and Ningzhu had gone home with their families. She envied them — she wanted to go home too.
Princess Shuyi had been bitten by a wolf? When had that happened?
Seeing the confusion in her mistress's eyes, Qiuyu repeated what she had overheard. "Miss, Eunuch Chengzhong told me that the wolves were deliberately driven there by someone. They captured the wolf king's pup and used the pup's blood to lure the wolf pack toward where the Emperor was hunting. Princess Shuyi was unlucky — she had not gone into that area, yet a wolf appeared from who knows where and bit her. Word is her lower leg may never be the same."
Jiang Mi was stunned. So much had happened in a single night?
After her horse had bolted she too had encountered wolves — and if she had not stumbled in her panic straight into that blood-soaked hunting ground, she might have fared far worse than Princess Shuyi.
Stringing all these events together left Jiang Mi feeling frightened and exhausted.
Seeing that her mistress seemed ready to sleep again, Qiuyu pulled the quilt up around her and quietly withdrew.
When Jiang Mi woke again, lamps had been lit.
In the room she found Mama Cui — the trusted attendant who served by her aunt's side.
Mama Cui had come on the Empress Dowager's orders expressly to look after Jiang Mi.

chapters.title: Fifty three
chapters.content:
Mama Cui had just seen Chief Imperial Physician Gu out after he finished taking the pulse, and when she returned she found Qiuyu feeding Jiang Mi her medicine.
She said with fond relief, "Third Miss, the Emperor left Chief Physician Gu behind at the palace to attend to your wound. That alone shows how greatly he regards you."
"Now that the noble families in the capital all know you saved the Emperor's life, the Empress Dowager says that once your wound has healed enough for you to travel back by carriage, she will have you received directly into the palace to recover there."
Jiang Mi found the medicine entering her mouth both bitter and numbing. She said nothing, and took the slice of yun pian pastry Qiuyu handed her, chewing it slowly.
Mama Cui looked at Jiang Mi's pale, thin face with a pang of tenderness. The Third Miss had suffered so many hardships, time after time — but her bitter days were nearly at an end.
She lowered her voice in reassurance. "Miss, when the time comes, you will get what your heart desires. The Empress Dowager will certainly help you rise to the position of Empress."
Far from comforting Jiang Mi, Mama Cui's words made her heart sink lower, and she felt she could not breathe.
Jiang Mi found the yun pian pastry she had just eaten suddenly cloying and set down the half-eaten piece. "Mama, I am tired," she said, without energy.
Mama Cui immediately said, "Then this old servant will withdraw and leave you to rest, Miss. Please sleep well."
After Mama Cui and Qiuyu left, the bedchamber fell utterly silent.
Jiang Mi lay on the bed, a blank look on her face, unable to work out how the hunt had ended like this.
From Mama Cui's words she could read her aunt's mind clearly enough: her aunt believed her entry into the palace was now virtually certain, and intended to use this so-called act of saving the Emperor's life to press Xiao Huaiyan into making her Empress.
Trying to use a favor as leverage — that was simply touching his most sensitive nerve.
Why could her aunt still not see it? Whatever affection Xiao Huaiyan chose to acknowledge was affection. Whatever he did not choose to acknowledge, it was nothing at all.
And she had not saved him to begin with — she had simply tripped and fallen.
The time she spent recovering at the temporary palace was the only breathing room she had. Once she returned to the capital and was received into the palace, she would have no means of doing anything.
But what could she do, here in the temporary palace?
Jiang Mi's thoughts were in turmoil. She felt as though she were crossing a fog-shrouded bridge, every step taken with great difficulty and precarious luck — one wrong move and there would be no coming back.
......
Inside the imperial study, Li Fu attended with careful, quiet steps. After presenting the Emperor with tea, he withdrew.
Since returning to the palace, the Emperor had dealt out punishment to all the officials who had been negligent during the hunt.
He then bestowed gifts and consolation on the noble families who had been injured.
And in the evening he summoned Commander Pei, Lord Xue, and Lord Gu one after another to discuss matters of state.
Xiao Huaiyan finished reading the dossier Pei Chi had presented and gave a cold laugh.
Xue Jingyuan stepped forward. "Your Majesty, since certain old families have revealed themselves during this hunt, this subject would like to follow that thread to Jiangnan. Prince Qi's maternal family rose from Jiangnan. Even after the clan was executed, the web of connections between the great families there is intricate beyond reckoning. For the remnant faction to have hidden so deep for so long, they must have the protection of those entrenched powers. This subject is willing to shoulder this burden for Your Majesty."
Pei Chi said, his expression cold and stern, "Your Majesty was too merciful at the time — you gave those people a path to live, and it planted a seed of trouble. It would have been simpler to have cut them all down cleanly then."
Xue Jingyuan thought privately that when the Emperor was still far away at the border, his foothold in the court had not been as deep as Prince Qi's and the others'. Apart from the Shen family lending quiet support, most of the great old families had been hedging their bets. The Shen family's help had also been limited. It was the Rebellion of the Three Princes that finally gave the Emperor his opening to eliminate them one by one and claim the throne. The resistance among the old ministers had not been great — and that was due not only to the Emperor's military strength, but also to his reputation for mercy and magnanimity.
Although it had reduced the resistance and brought the court's old families and ministers into submission, there was no guarantee that some did not still harbor treacherous intent.
In his view, the Emperor's choice at the time not to purge the old ministers wholesale — taking a policy of conciliation instead — had yielded more benefit than harm.
Given a few more years, the old blood could be thoroughly cleared and replaced.
This would serve the stability of the realm.
Xue Jingyuan said, "Commander Pei, the Emperor has his own judgment. Do not be hasty."
As for Xiao Huaiyan himself, the impulse to kill stirred in his depths. He raised the hand wearing the jade ring and pressed his fingers to his brow. "Then as Jingyuan says — go to Jiangnan and investigate the full picture."
Xue Jingyuan cupped his hands in a bow. "Yes, Your Majesty."
Xiao Huaiyan asked, "Is there any movement from the Duke Zhen State's manor?"
Xue Jingyuan shook his head. "On the day of the hunt, the young lord was struck by Princess Shuyi's misdirected arrow and appears to be in recovery again."
Xiao Huaiyan said with a small laugh, "Since the young lord has been injured, send an imperial physician to check on him as well. If his condition worsens, I fear my royal aunt will blame me."
As Pei Chi, Xue Jingyuan, and Gu Chang were withdrawing, Xiao Huaiyan called Gu Chang back. "Summon Gu Xuan back from Yunzhou."
Gu Chang showed surprise. Why was the Emperor suddenly recalling his elder sister?
He did not dare ask, and could only agree.
Li Fu, who had been waiting outside, entered the imperial study after the gentlemen had departed.
He handed over a message he had received. "Your Majesty, this is a report on the patient's pulse sent by Chief Physician Gu."
Chief Physician Gu was still at the temporary palace, so whose report this was went without saying.
Xiao Huaiyan opened the letter and read what was written. He said nothing.
Li Fu could not read the Emperor's thoughts and did not dare say more.
......
Jiang Mi lay in bed recovering for two days. With the wound on her back, lying for long stretches was uncomfortable no matter what position she tried — and since the wound had not healed, she could not change positions, and simply had to endure.
Fortunately, elders and sisters from home had all sent letters and small gifts.
Reading the letters from Elder Sister Wan, Elder Sister Yi, and A'Rong lifted her low spirits a little.
Qiuyu came in carrying something and said with a smile, "Miss, guess whose letter this is?"
Jiang Mi looked at the rosewood box in her hand. "Ningzhu's?"
Qiuyu said, "Oh my, Miss, you didn't even let this servant have a moment of suspense before you guessed it."
Jiang Mi pressed her lips into a smile, knowing Qiuyu was trying to cheer her up.
Qiuyu opened the box. On top was a letter, and beneath it lay a small, exquisite porcelain figurine.
Qiuyu cried out in delight, "Miss, look — this porcelain doll looks just like you! Like they were made from the same mold. Even the eyes and brows are this delicate. I wonder which master craftsman made it."
Jiang Mi looked at the porcelain doll in Qiuyu's hands. It was a little girl on a swing, her skirt fluttering in the wind, her face lit up with a freely joyful smile.
The features of the swinging girl bore a striking resemblance to her own — but the smile on the doll was not quite like hers. She very rarely smiled like that.
That uninhibited smile stirred a faint pang of envy in her.
Jiang Mi found herself wondering: how long had it been since she last played on a swing?
She asked Qiuyu to bring her Xue Ningzhu's letter. The handwriting was just as she expected — graceful yet carrying a youthful, childlike quality.
"Sister Jiang, I hope your wound is feeling better. I wanted to stay and keep you company, but Young Lord Brother wouldn't let me — he said you need quiet rest and I shouldn't disturb you. Yesterday I pestered Young Lord Brother to make a clay figurine for me. I asked him to make one that looked like me, then begged him to make one that looked like Sister Jiang too. He made yours so pretty, but he made mine all chubby. Though Brother did promise he would make a new one for me next time. I'm sending you your doll along with this letter. Sister Jiang, when you're better, let's go on the swings together! Young Lord Brother can push me so high — it's so much fun. Sister Jiang, once you're a bit better, can I come to the temporary palace to visit you?"
After reading the letter, Jiang Mi asked Qiuyu to bring the doll over. She ran her fingers lightly over the billowing little skirt. When she was better, she was going to have a proper go on the swings.
She found herself wondering whether Young Lord Xue's wound had healed at all.
She had not yet had a proper chance to thank him before everything else had happened.
......
In the evening, Jiang Mi drank her medicine as usual and drifted off to sleep.
She had no idea how long she slept, but she woke feeling parched and thirsty.
Still heavy with sleep, she murmured drowsily, "Qiuyu, I want some water."
Shortly after, a pair of hands supported her and held a warm cup to her lips.
Jiang Mi drank slowly without opening her eyes. Then, mid-swallow, she became aware of a scent near her — not Qiuyu's clean, fresh fragrance, but dragon saliva incense.
The person who wore this particular scent should not have been anywhere near the temporary palace.
She continued swallowing the water. But her whole body was taut, and she could feel that the hands supporting her were not Qiuyu's usual hands.
Jiang Mi slowly let go. She still did not open her eyes — as a drowsy person would — and said nothing, as if she had slipped back into sleep.
Jiang Mi lay face-down again. She did not dare move a muscle, forcing herself to sleep.
Do not open your eyes. Do not wake up.
A cool hand touched Jiang Mi's forehead briefly, then withdrew.
Xiao Huaiyan gazed at the person on the bed for a moment, set the teacup back on the table, and sat down.
The bedchamber was utterly still.
Xiao Huaiyan heard the breathing — which had been irregular just now — gradually settle back to calm. His fingers tapped lightly on the tabletop.
He noticed the pastries on the table, left out for her to eat after her medicine. They were different from what he had seen last time, though he had the vague sense he had encountered them somewhere before.
He picked one up and ate it. This time he finished the whole piece.
Xiao Huaiyan rose and walked toward the outer hall.
Li Fu fell into step beside him. "Your Majesty, it is the hour of zi."
If they rode hard, they could just make it back in time for morning court.
Li Fu had not imagined the Emperor would, after finishing the affairs of state, make an abrupt trip to the temporary palace.
Yet from the sounds inside, that Jiang girl had not even woken up.
Xiao Huaiyan glanced at the maid huddled to one side. "Who made the pastries on the table?"
Qiuyu was suddenly addressed and nearly jumped out of her skin with fright. "Your— Your Majesty," she said in a trembling voice, "those are from a recipe Miss gave this servant. This servant took it to the kitchen to have them make it." She added, "Miss found the yun pian cakes a little cloying, so she recited her own recipe from memory and asked this servant to give it to the kitchen to adjust."
Li Fu saw that the Emperor had said nothing and simply departed.
He hesitated a moment, then turned to Qiuyu. "It seems Miss Jiang's recipe has something special to it. Qiuyu, recite it to me — this old servant will have to study it."
Qiuyu did not dare hold anything back and handed over the recipe.
Li Fu took it with a smile. "Qiuyu, you must remember — some things should be said, and some things must not. That is how one remains in a master's favor for many years."
Qiuyu could only nod.

chapters.title: Fifty four
chapters.content:
Li Fu walked briskly back to the Qianqing Palace, a food box in hand containing pastries the imperial kitchen had made following Miss Jiang's recipe.
He had personally overseen the imperial chefs, and the moment the pastries came out of the oven, he had packed them and hurried them over.
He had not expected that such small, humble confections could hold so much artistry — even the imperial chefs had been full of admiring praise over the recipe.
Li Fu entered the hall to find the Emperor still going through the memorials.
After returning from the temporary palace last night, the Emperor had gone directly to morning court and had not rested for a single moment since.
Li Fu set the food box carefully to one side of the desk. "Your Majesty, please rest a while. These pastries were all made from Miss Jiang's recipe. Do try one."
Xiao Huaiyan's vermilion brush did not stop moving. He glanced up at Li Fu briefly.
Li Fu did not dare say more. He refilled the tea and quietly withdrew.
Xiao Huaiyan finished reviewing the memorial in hand and set the brush down.
He looked at the dish of neat, small confections, and picked one up.
He took a bite. Light, soft, fragrant, not cloying.
Much the same taste as what he had eaten at the temporary palace.
Xiao Huaiyan pressed his fingers to the bridge of his nose and closed his eyes to rest.
He thought he could hear the light, clear sound of bells again.
The sound came from far away and then drew near, until it was just beside him.
"Your Majesty, your concubine has made new pastries. Would you like to try one?" A soft, sweet voice, carrying a faint, barely perceptible nervousness.
Xiao Huaiyan looked over to find Jiang Mi dressed in imperial consort's robes, carrying a small dish of pastries, holding them out to him.
"Your Majesty, I have changed the recipe again — these are different from last time. Won't you have a taste?" As she spoke, she hesitated for a moment, then picked one up and held it toward him.
Xiao Huaiyan's dark eyes fixed on her without speaking.
He watched as Jiang Mi swallowed nervously, the small, timid smile at her lips wavering, her outstretched hand slowly beginning to pull back.
Xiao Huaiyan, watching the disappointment gradually surface on her face, reached out and caught that pale white wrist.
He saw her look up in surprise, and gave a soft laugh — and with her hand still held, bit into the pastry she was holding.
He heard her ask with eager anticipation, "Your Majesty — how is it this time?"
Xiao Huaiyan looked at the smile on her face and said without expression, "Adequate."
He had always concealed his preferences from others. Something he liked to the extent of seven parts, he might reveal only two or three. That vagueness of manner made his mind impossible to read.
He had never been particularly fond of sweets. But this time the pastries she had made had something distinctive about them.
He noticed that Jiang Mi, having heard that one word "adequate," was already very happy.
"Your concubine will adjust it again next time, and make something Your Majesty truly likes."
Xiao Huaiyan made no comment. His appetite for food and drink was not something he placed great weight on. But Jiang Mi enjoyed going to the effort of these things — all manner of pastries, all manner of dishes — probing to learn his tastes.
This time, by giving her a clear answer, he had offered her a kind of response.
He watched Jiang Mi set the dish on the table and stand fidgeting with the handkerchief in her hands. After hesitating for a moment, she gathered her courage. "Your Majesty, your concubine's eldest cousin has committed..."
Before Jiang Mi could finish, Xiao Huaiyan's expression cooled. He cut her off. "Lady Jiang, the inner court may not interfere in affairs of state. Was that not what the Empress Dowager taught you?"
Those apricot eyes instantly reddened. Her courage seemed to collapse all at once — she looked as though she had been frightened.
Xiao Huaiyan frowned. She could not be spoken to like this — if the Jiang family's affairs could be resolved with a few words of pleading from her, she would only grow bolder, her desires multiplying once she had tasted success.
He had expected Jiang Mi to leave in tears — but instead, a struggle flickered through those mist-filled eyes. Her foot shifted, and the bells at her ankle rang softly. Beneath the visible fear on her face, something desperate and final rose in her, and she sat herself down in his lap, lifting her hands to grip his shoulders. Her legs dangled freely in the air, swaying gently, and on her pale white ankle, the gold-and-jade bells trembled...
Her voice carried a slight tremble. "Your— Your Majesty, your concubine knows she has done wrong..."
Xiao Huaiyan raised a hand to encircle that slender, yielding waist, smiled, and lifted the leg that wore the bells — not even sure when her embroidered shoe had come off, leaving her foot bare, its color white as jade, small and delicate.
Ordinarily she was quite reluctant to wear those bells — yet today, for the sake of interceding for someone, she had made preparations well in advance. She certainly knew how to please him.
The soft sounds that escaped her were muddled and breathless. What had been muffled, suppressed weeping suddenly broke into open, unrestrained crying.
Xiao Huaiyan's eyes turned deep and dark. He wrapped both arms around the trembling, shivering person in his lap and carried her toward the inner chambers.
......
Xiao Huaiyan opened his eyes. The sky beyond the window was overcast. The great hall was very still.
On the desk: a pile of accumulated memorials, and a dish of exquisite pastries.
Xiao Huaiyan pressed his fingers to his temples. There was no sound of bells, no sound of soft weeping. The dream had come suddenly and ended quickly.
He reached out and took another pastry from the dish, chewing it slowly.
It was very similar to the flavor in the dream.
But not quite the same.
Yet both were to his taste.
Had Jiang Mi already begun probing his preferences this early?
For this kind of gesture — trying to please him — he found, for the first time, that he was not particularly displeased.
......
Fengchi Temporary Palace.
Jiang Mi's days continued as before: medicine, rest, healing. Mama Cui kept close watch over the small kitchen and had dishes prepared in endless variety for her.
Jiang Mi had just finished her medicine when Qiuyu handed over the pastry she would always have one piece of afterward.
Jiang Mi's gaze paused on the plate. As she recalled it, there had been five pieces left yesterday, hadn't there?
"Qiuyu, did anyone come in last night?" Jiang Mi asked.
Qiuyu kept her head down and replied quietly, "No one, Miss."
Jiang Mi said, puzzled, "Is that so? Were you keeping watch all night?"
Qiuyu nodded. "This servant also helped Miss drink some water. Miss was half-asleep — you probably don't remember."
Jiang Mi found it strange. Could she have been so confused in sleep?
But that scent of dragon saliva incense had been so unmistakably distinct — how could she have mistaken it?
Jiang Mi picked up a pastry and watched Qiuyu's hand, holding the dish, give a slight tremble.
She said casually, "Qiuyu, last night you put a bit too much acacia honey in the water — my throat has felt a little uncomfortable since waking. Next time make it a little less sweet — I don't like it too sweet."
Qiuyu nodded again and again. "Yes, Miss. This servant will go adjust it now."
Jiang Mi watched Qiuyu's retreating back, her expression complicated.
Qiuyu was afraid. She had lied, and she had been warned.
There had been no honey at all in last night's water.
It had not been a dream, nor an illusion. Xiao Huaiyan had truly come.
Jiang Mi closed her eyes, drained of energy. What on earth was Xiao Huaiyan trying to do?
The heaviness already weighing on her heart deepened further.
His behavior showed a blatant disregard for propriety — and for her reputation.
If word of it got out, she dreaded what people would say.
By then, whether she wanted to enter the palace or not, she would have no choice.
Jiang Mi watched the nightfall arrive and felt her anxiety rising.
At dinner, Mama Cui noticed that Jiang Mi had barely eaten half a bowl of broth and had no appetite for anything further. She coaxed and cajoled her at length without success.
Worried that something was physically wrong, Mama Cui sent for Chief Physician Gu.
Chief Physician Gu took her pulse through the cloth.
After a long moment, he slowly withdrew his hand.
Mama Cui asked anxiously, "Chief Physician Gu, what is the matter?"
Chief Physician Gu could only see a blurred silhouette through the bed curtain. He said, "Miss Jiang, your condition requires quiet rest — do not let your thoughts run away with you. Do you still remember what I said to you that time at the Cining Palace?"
Jiang Mi pressed her lips together. "I remember... thank you, Chief Physician Gu." She understood what he meant — do not brood, do not let worries pile up, keep the mind open. Do not let anxiety turn into illness.
She had thought things were beginning to improve, and then she found herself back in yet another desperate situation.
She did not even know how to reason herself into calm. She could see the path of her previous life drawing close again, and she was panic-stricken and without a single plan.
Chief Physician Gu shook his head. "Since Miss already understands, let me write you a few more prescriptions."
He let out a quiet sigh and stepped out.
As Jiang Mi watched Attendant Ye preparing to leave as well, she called out to her. "Attendant Ye, I have been sleeping rather shallowly — I keep waking in the middle of the night. Could you add something to the medicine to calm the spirit and help me sleep? I would like to get a proper rest."
Attendant Ye looked a little uncertain. This was not something she could decide on her own — all of Miss Jiang's medicines had to be reviewed by Chief Physician Gu and could not be altered at will. "Miss Jiang, do not worry. I will go and inform Chief Physician Gu."
That was all Jiang Mi could do.
Chief Physician Gu, waiting outside the hall and hearing what Attendant Ye relayed, shook his head. "Miss Jiang's condition cannot bear a higher dosage. The medicine she is already taking contains ingredients to calm the spirit and aid sleep. Her body is still weak from the injury — the dosage must not be increased. However, when you bring her the medicine, you may tell her that the amount has been adjusted. It will at least give her some comfort of mind."
Attendant Ye nodded. "Yes, Chief Physician Gu."
......
Jiang Mi drank her medicine before sleep and waited for the medicine to take effect.
Before long, drowsiness came, and she slipped gradually into sleep.
That night, she did not dream.
For two nights running, nothing happened.
The worry she had been holding gradually eased a little.
After all, the temporary palace was far from the capital — Xiao Huaiyan could not possibly keep making the journey.
Perhaps the affairs of court kept him so busy that he had already forgotten. That would be best.
With her anxiety loosening, her appetite returned somewhat.
That evening, after drinking her medicine, she went to bed early.
Once Jiang Mi was asleep, Qiuyu snuffed several of the candles in the bedchamber. The softer light would help her mistress sleep.
Qiuyu had barely turned around when she saw a figure step from behind the screen.
When she made out who it was, her knees went weak and she sank to the floor. She was about to speak and offer her greeting — but a gesture from him stopped her.
Qiuyu could only withdraw in silence.
Xiao Huaiyan tossed the riding crop in his hand casually onto the table.
He sat at the table and looked at the sleeping figure on the bed.
The room was perhaps a little warm. The person under the quilt had quietly eased both legs out from beneath it.
The trouser legs had ridden up, baring slender ankles and small, fair feet.
Xiao Huaiyan poured himself a cup of the water on the table and drank.
He frowned. Why did this water taste of honey?
He set the cup down and walked to the bedside.
He took hold of the slender legs and moved to tuck them back under the quilt.
His roughened fingers could not help lingering for a moment at the ankle, tracing lightly. The touch was perhaps uncomfortable to whoever was sleeping — she gave a few small kicks.
Xiao Huaiyan looked at the tightly closed eyes of the person on the bed. In that brief instant, he had heard the previously steady breathing falter.
He released his hand.
He looked at Jiang Mi's sleeping face — whether because she had been resting these past few days and had regained a little color, or simply because the warmth of the room had flushed her cheeks, both cheeks had taken on a rose tint.
Xiao Huaiyan pulled the quilt back.
His cool hand rested on her shoulder. Through the fabric he felt the person beneath him seem to give a faint tremor.
Xiao Huaiyan's thin lips curved slightly. He reached out to loosen Jiang Mi's collar, and half a shoulder came into view.
The moon-white dudou beneath was half-revealed.
He was just about to slip the garment from her when the person beneath him finally lost control and tried to pull away.
The movement pulled at the wound on her back.
Jiang Mi let out a muffled sound.
She gripped her collar tightly, and her eyes flew open in alarm.
Xiao Huaiyan looked at her calmly and said, "Miss Jiang — done pretending to sleep?"

chapters.title: Fifty five
chapters.content:
Xiao Huaiyan sat at the bedside dressed entirely in black. In the dim lamplight, the shadows playing across his face made his features look all the more deep and striking. His long, narrow phoenix eyes were composed and unreadable. His voice, at least, sounded relatively mild.
Jiang Mi had been living in fear and anxiety, barely eating or sleeping well. She was already worn through with a deep exhaustion.
She lowered her eyes. Her long lashes trembled faintly. "Your Majesty, you should not be here."
She had just woken from sleep, and Jiang Mi's naturally sweet voice came out slightly husky with it, its soft warmth somehow carrying a tingling quality.
Xiao Huaiyan was mildly caught off guard. A faint smile touched his face. "Oh? This is Our bedchamber. If We should not be here, then perhaps Miss Jiang would tell Us where We ought to go instead?"
Jiang Mi bit her lower lip. She had thought that waking up and making herself known might make him hold back — but here he was, turning the question around on her when he was clearly the one in the wrong.
So he had given up any pretense of behaving like a gentleman.
But now that the words were out, retreating would do her no good.
"Your Majesty is right — this is Your Majesty's bedchamber. It is this subject who should not be here. Please allow this subject to go to East Pavilion Garden to recover." Even as she spoke, she tried to endure the pain and pull her loosened collar closed — and pulled at the wound again.
Xiao Huaiyan frowned and pressed his hand against Jiang Mi's shoulder. "Stop moving like that or the wound will tear open."
"Miss Jiang rendered a service in protecting the Emperor — We would not be so stingy with a single bedchamber toward the person who saved Our life. Lie back down and rest easy."
The warmth of Xiao Huaiyan's palm came through the thin fabric. Jiang Mi tried to pull away — but could not.
Xiao Huaiyan watched her face flush red. She blinked once, and the tears came spilling out.
"What are you crying for." He had not truly intended to undress her — he had only seen her pretending to sleep and been deliberately trying to frighten her a little.
He watched Jiang Mi cry in small, soft sounds.
"Jiang Mi."
At the sudden sound of Xiao Huaiyan calling her by name, Jiang Mi startled.
Xiao Huaiyan straightened her rumpled collar for her.
His thumb brushed away the tears on her face. He asked, "You fear me so much — is it that you are afraid I will take you, or afraid that I will not want you?"
These words made Jiang Mi's heart seize with fright.
What madness was Xiao Huaiyan saying?
Xiao Huaiyan saw that his words had apparently frightened her — her wide-open apricot eyes were round and quite endearing.
He said by way of reassurance, "I will not conceal it from you. You shielded me from that arrow. Your wound was treated under my watch. It was I who pulled the arrow from your back. I have already seen your wound.
He paused. "Jiang Mi — is there still anything left to avoid?"
These words, one by one, demolished what remained of Jiang Mi's defenses.
