The wound was on her back. To treat it, her clothing had to come off.
Xiao Huaiyan had already seen what he wanted to see of her. And now, having decided she was his possession, he was behaving without any restraint?
The room's warmth could not help her — she felt as though she were standing in frozen tundra.
Jiang Mi's tears fell beyond her control. Her lips trembled. She tried to speak, but her throat felt as though something had blocked it, and no sound would come.
Xiao Huaiyan watched Jiang Mi cry in silence, and said, "The Empress Dowager has again raised to me the matter of you entering the palace. Recover your wound well. When the new year has passed, I will issue the decree. Stop crying." In his dreams, Jiang Mi was his consort — his Lady Jiang.
Now that he thought about it, a consort's rank was rather too low for her.
Xiao Huaiyan touched Jiang Mi's face. The warm tears flowing onto his hand gave him, for no reason he could name, a pang of something.
This unfamiliar emotion made Xiao Huaiyan uneasy. He withdrew his hand, his expression growing unnatural. "After some time has passed, I will draft several titles for you to choose from."
Jiang Mi felt as though she had heard his words — yet also as though she had not understood them at all.
Her eyes took on a look of quiet grief. She grabbed hold of Xiao Huaiyan's arm and shook her head at him.
She did not want to enter the palace. She did not want to walk the same path as her last life.
"Your Majesty..." Jiang Mi's voice was trembling and hoarse, as though she had seized on a lifeline. "This subject... this subject would not dare to aspire to such a thing... I beg Your Majesty to rescind this command."
Xiao Huaiyan studied her, then placed her hand back and pulled the quilt over her, bending down to say quietly, "Miss Jiang, do not be too greedy. Rest well. I will come to see you again."
He knew the Jiang family wanted him to give her the position of Empress. The Empress Dowager Jiang had hinted at it several times, using the act of shielding him from the arrow as her leverage.
Jiang Mi herself had most likely been instilled with this idea by the Jiang family — and was playing at reluctance in hopes of advancing her position.
Xiao Huaiyan picked up the riding crop from the table and walked out.
Jiang Mi's crying turned abruptly into coughing. Qiuyu, hearing the sounds, rushed in.
Qiuyu fed her some warm honey water. Jiang Mi swallowed it — and vomited it back up almost immediately.
This frightened Qiuyu badly. She hurried to call for Attendant Ye.
Attendant Ye examined Jiang Mi, puzzled at why her emotions were in such turmoil.
She pressed several pressure points to help calm her, and then advised her, "Miss Jiang, whatever has happened, you must take care of yourself. The arrow wound in your back has not yet healed — you cannot add the illness of a troubled heart on top of it. No matter how great the trouble, there is always a way through."
How Jiang Mi wished that what had just happened was only a nightmare.
She had never expected Xiao Huaiyan to lay it out so plainly.
This time it was he who wanted her to enter the palace — he who was preparing to bestow a title on her.
When Xiao Huaiyan spoke those last words, his gaze had gone cold. He had almost certainly taken her refusal as a ploy — playing hard to get.
In her previous life, Xiao Huaiyan had not taken in any concubines until after the spring imperial examinations the following year.
Yet now, from the words he had spoken — he wanted her in the palace after the new year?
The new year was only two months away.
What could she possibly do now?
She had no time left.
Jiang Mi stared blankly ahead. All her careful plans struck her as laughable now.
She had put in so much thought, and none of it had come to anything.
Shen Qianxiu, though he sent her books, though he had helped her out at the hunt — he had never said anything directly, and she had no way of knowing whether he could even persuade Grand Secretary Shen to come forward and propose a match for her.
Young Lord Xue came from the Duke Zhen State's manor. She had met him several times because of Ningzhu. He was gentle and even-tempered, and because she had helped Ningzhu, he had made her a promise.
But could that promise truly help her?
Jiang Mi looked at the porcelain doll sitting on the display shelf. Ningzhu had said it was made by the young lord's own hands.
The little doll was smiling so freely and happily. Was that how she appeared in the young lord's eyes?
Her thoughts were in complete disarray, but a vague idea was forming.
Whether it would work or not — perhaps she should arrange to see Young Lord Xue.
Qiuyu fed Jiang Mi the medicine Attendant Ye had prepared, and the medicine's effect pulled her into sleep.
The following day, Qiuyu came in carrying another letter.
"Miss, Miss Xue of the Duke Zhen State's manor has written to you."
Jiang Mi, who had been resting with her eyes closed, opened them. "Bring it over quickly."
Jiang Mi read through it rapidly. In the letter, Ningzhu said she was terribly bored at home and wanted to know how Jiang Mi was doing. She had pleaded with the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang, and with the Grand Princess's token in hand, she was coming to the temporary palace to visit.
And Young Lord Xue would escort her all the way there.

chapters.title: Fifty six
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi looked at the date on the letter. If Ningzhu had set out that morning as she said, she would arrive at the temporary palace by evening.
She stared up at the canopy of the bed, thoughts drifting through her mind one after another.
Mama Cui came in carrying a bowl of broth she had been simmering. These past few days she had watched Third Miss grow more and more listless — always drowsy, as though she did not want to wake up. She had privately gone to Chief Physician Gu to ask for additional medicine, but he had only said that a sickness of the heart could only be cured by medicine of the heart, and that no amount of physical remedies would help.
She could not quite understand why Third Miss was so weighed down with worry. Had she not earned credit for saving the Emperor's life? Was that not a good thing?
Mama Cui said gently, "Miss, do have some red date and black chicken broth. It has been simmering on a low flame all night — it is wonderfully rich."
Jiang Mi was about to say she had no appetite, but she knew that if she let herself be dragged under by this despondency any longer, her body would only get worse. Since Xiao Huaiyan's departure she had felt, for one brief moment, utterly exhausted — exhausted enough that she wanted nothing more than to sink into sleep and not wake up. To stop having to face entering the palace and all the calamity that came with it.
But Heaven had given her a second chance at life. She was not willing to throw it away. Even if her struggling might come to nothing, it was still better than doing nothing at all.
And if it failed — it could not possibly be worse than her previous life.
Jiang Mi gave a small nod. "Help me sit up."
Mama Cui and Qiuyu both brightened at once. As long as their young miss was willing to eat, that was a good sign.
Jiang Mi drank a few spoonfuls of broth, gathered herself, and said, "Mama Cui, Miss Xue from the Duke Zhen State's manor will be coming to the palace. Please arrange for her to stay in a courtyard close to mine."
Mama Cui nodded. "Do not worry, Miss. This old servant will see to it. Miss Xue coming to keep you company and chat for a while will do you good — it will lift your spirits a little."
Jiang Mi drank a small bowl of broth, rested for a while, and fell asleep again.
When she woke, the lamps had been lit in the room.
A rustling sound came from nearby. Jiang Mi turned to look, and saw a young girl sitting at the table, eating the pastries on the dish. She brushed the crumbs from her hands, reached for a cup of water, turned her head — and met Jiang Mi's eyes.
Xue Ningzhu tried to speak at once, but the pastry still in her mouth was not yet swallowed, and she choked into a coughing fit.
Jiang Mi said with fond exasperation, "Ningzhu, drink some water first before you talk — you nearly choked."
Xue Ningzhu picked up the teacup and gulped a mouthful, then thumped her chest to get her breath back. She finished off the rest of the water in the cup, and finally felt better.
She hurried to the bedside and took Jiang Mi's hand. "Sister Jiang, you finally woke up. I've been waiting to eat dinner together with you."
Jiang Mi shook her head. "Ningzhu, you have been traveling all day. Don't wait for me — I don't have much of an appetite anyway."
Xue Ningzhu reached out and touched Jiang Mi's face. "Sister Jiang, you've gotten thinner. Mama Cui told me I should keep encouraging you to eat more. Eat with me — watching me eat might give you an appetite too."
Jiang Mi could not refuse Xue Ningzhu's coaxing. Mama Cui had the evening meal brought in.
Ningzhu paid no mind to the old saying about eating in silence. She talked between bites without stopping.
Watching her eat so cheerfully, Jiang Mi felt her own mood loosen a little.
She managed some bird's nest congee, then could eat no more.
Not long after, Attendant Ye came in carrying the medicine she had prepared.
Xue Ningzhu frowned at the large, pitch-black bowl of liquid. The smell of bitterness reached her from several paces away.
Yet Sister Jiang drank it all down without so much as a change of expression.
Even watching made Ningzhu shudder.
How terribly bitter it must be!
Jiang Mi popped a preserved plum into her mouth and smiled at Xue Ningzhu's scrunched-up face. "Ningzhu, come sit closer."
Xue Ningzhu came and settled at the bedside with a piece of hawthorn cake in her hand. She offered it to Jiang Mi, but Jiang Mi told her to eat it herself, and Ningzhu obliged without ceremony, taking a bite.
Jiang Mi said, "Ningzhu, was it Young Lord Xue who escorted you here?"
Xue Ningzhu nodded eagerly. "Yes, yes! Young Lord Brother couldn't come in to visit you himself, but he asked me to convey his regards. After you went missing in the forest that day, he searched for you the whole time. He didn't leave until the soldiers sealed off the mountain and news of you finally came."
Jiang Mi was quiet for a moment, watching Xue Ningzhu finish the cake in her hand.
Xue Ningzhu did not stay too long in the bedchamber. She kept Jiang Mi company for a while, and when she saw her growing drowsy again, she went back to her own rooms.
Xue Ningzhu had barely stepped into her courtyard — Qufeng Garden — when she heard the flowing sound of a xiao flute.
She gathered her skirts and ran toward it, and found her Young Lord Brother leaning against a tree, playing.
She listened without a word until the last note faded, then looked at Xue Jinglin with shining eyes. "Young Lord Brother, you play so beautifully."
Xue Jinglin drew in the flute and smiled. "Tired of being over there already? I thought you might stay the night."
Xue Ningzhu shook her head. "Sister Jiang is still unwell — she doesn't look well at all. I can't keep interrupting her."
Xue Jinglin's gaze drifted toward the darkened palace buildings in the distance. "Is her wound not healing?" After so many days, it ought to be improving.
"Sister Jiang's arrow wound does seem to be getting better, but her body still looks very weak." Xue Ningzhu's voice dropped. "Young Lord Brother, I think Sister Jiang is very unhappy."
Xue Jinglin looked at her. "Why do you say that?"
"I'm not sure how to explain it. I just feel like Sister Jiang isn't happy. Young Lord Brother, can you think of some way to cheer her up?"
......
In her sleep, Jiang Mi half-heard the sound of the flute, but it faded away before long.
The next morning she woke earlier than usual, and thought she could hear Ningzhu and Qiuyu talking.
Before long, Xue Ningzhu came in with Qiuyu, her expression conspiratorial. "Sister Jiang, I have a surprise for you. I promise you will love it!"
Then she ran back out again.
Qiuyu shook her head in helpless fondness and came to help Jiang Mi wash and dress, then brought in the morning meal.
Jiang Mi took a sip of warm honey water. "What is Ningzhu planning?"
Qiuyu said, "Miss Xue seems to have arranged for some musicians to play for you. Miss, if you find it too noisy, this servant can go and persuade Miss Xue to put it off?"
Jiang Mi smiled. "It's fine. I've been lying here so many days I'm beginning to feel stifled. A little music would do me good."
Qiuyu wrapped a cape around Jiang Mi's shoulders and helped her sit up carefully, keeping the wound undisturbed, and settle sideways against the chaise.
The screen in front of her was moved aside, and a dark-colored curtain was hung in its place. The performer would work from behind.
Xue Ningzhu sat beside Jiang Mi and clapped her hands. "Begin."
The string music started, and the dark curtain lit up. A shadow appeared — pulled by invisible strings — darting from the darkness toward the light. Quickly it became clear: a little white fox, running through a forest, running hard, until it collided headlong into a scholar who had been reading beneath a tree, and tumbled head over heels.
"Squeak, squeak, squeak..." The white fox rolled around on the ground.
Xue Ningzhu clapped her hand over her mouth, trying not to laugh.
Jiang Mi understood now: Ningzhu had arranged a shadow puppet show for her.
The performer's skill was masterful. Every voice — the scholar, the fox — was imitated with uncanny accuracy.
Jiang Mi had expected the story to follow the usual pattern from the story books: the white fox, rescued by the scholar, would transform into a beautiful maiden and come to repay his kindness. But the performer's shadow play took a different turn entirely.
The white fox was indeed rescued by the scholar, who kept it at home, safe from its pursuers, feeding it well.
And the fox did indeed want to repay him. It crept out in the middle of the night to catch chickens — and dropped them at the scholar's door.
The next day, the neighbors came to demand that the scholar pay for their stolen chickens.
The scholar scolded the white fox. The fox rolled around on the ground begging forgiveness — which made the scholar's heart soften, and made both Xue Ningzhu and Jiang Mi laugh.
This little fox never took human form. Its attempts to repay its debt of gratitude were one comedy of errors after another, leaving the scholar exasperated yet unable to bring himself to scold it seriously.
At the end, the fox had discharged its debt, bid farewell to the scholar, and returned to the forest — a free and carefree fox once more.
The music fell silent. The musicians withdrew. Only the shadow puppet performer remained, guiding the little fox's silhouette until it disappeared among the trees.
Xue Ningzhu was not satisfied with the ending. "Why didn't it stay with the scholar forever? Even after he married, he would have been good to the fox!"
A clear, light voice said, "Because it prefers to be free."
Jiang Mi heard that voice and her expression shifted. From behind the curtain stepped a figure, holding the white fox puppet in his hand, his clean-featured face carrying a faint smile.
"Young Lord Xue!" Jiang Mi had not expected that the performer who had just brought the shadow play to life was none other than Young Lord Xue himself.
Xue Jinglin gave a polite bow. "Miss Jiang, I have taken a liberty. Please forgive the intrusion."
Xue Ningzhu said with a grin, "Sister Jiang, is this not a wonderful surprise?"
For Jiang Mi, it truly was.
She had been wanting to see Young Lord Xue.
Xue Ningzhu glanced back and forth between Xue Jinglin and Jiang Mi. Then she stood up. "I'll just go and check if lunch is ready." And she slipped out.
Now there were only Jiang Mi and Xue Jinglin in the room.
Xue Jinglin looked at her — the apricot-colored cape made her face appear all the paler, and the whole of her seemed fragile, the faint worry between her brows stirring an involuntary pang of tenderness.
Jiang Mi knew it was improper, and felt a little awkward. But she also knew that if this moment passed, she might not have another chance.
"Miss Jiang, is your wound healing well?"
"Young Lord Xue, is your wound healing well?"
They both spoke at once.
Jiang Mi and Xue Jinglin were both startled, and then Xue Jinglin laughed. "The little cut on my hand healed long ago. It is Miss Jiang's arrow wound that was serious — you must take great care of it."
Jiang Mi said, "Thank you for your concern, Young Lord."
Xue Jinglin said, "Taking it upon myself today to perform this shadow play for you was truly presumptuous. Ningzhu said you seemed to be in low spirits and asked me to think of some way to cheer you up. I thought at first it was just that child making excuses to get me involved — but watching you, I can see you do have something weighing on your mind. A shadow play can only bring a moment's laughter, not the relief you need. Would you be willing to tell me what it is? Perhaps I might be of some help."
Seeing Jiang Mi's silence, he said, "Miss Jiang, do not forget — I still owe you a promise."
Jiang Mi gripped the handkerchief in her hands and looked up at him. "Can the Young Lord truly help with anything?"
Xue Jinglin said, "If you do not tell me, how will you know whether I can or not?"
Jiang Mi drew a slow, deep breath. Her voice, tight with nerves, came out a little dry. "What if I said I do not wish to enter the palace? Could the Young Lord help with that too?"
Xue Jinglin's expression showed genuine surprise, and his gaze on her grew more serious.
"Why would Miss Jiang feel that way? Forgive my bluntness — I have heard from my grandmother that the Empress Dowager intends to have you enter the palace. Now that you have earned credit for saving the Emperor's life, securing a position in His Majesty's inner court would be well within reach. How have you come to feel otherwise?"
Jiang Mi could not help feeling a little deflated. She had known this question would give him pause, might even make him pull back. It seemed Young Lord Xue might be unable to help after all.
But Xue Jinglin had been watching her face, and said, "If you don't tell me, then even if I have a way, I cannot use it."
Jiang Mi saw that his manner was sincere and composed — he did not seem frightened off.
She dropped her gaze and looked at the small fox puppet in his hand. She said quietly, "Perhaps I have something in common with that little fox."
A light moved through Xue Jinglin's eyes. He said in a low voice, "If Miss Jiang truly does not want to enter the palace, I do have one idea — though I am not sure whether you would be willing."
Jiang Mi said, "Please speak, Young Lord."
Xue Jinglin said, "Miss Jiang — would you be willing to form a match with the Duke Zhen State's manor?"
Jiang Mi's expression was complicated. She had not expected Xue Jinglin to bring it up himself.
She knew in her heart that if she did not want to enter the palace, the only option was to be married first — and the family she married into would need to be powerful enough to protect both her and her own family.
The Duke Zhen State's manor and the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang — founding meritorious subjects and also blood relatives of the imperial family. Without doubt, this was the best option available to her right now.
Xue Jinglin, seeing Jiang Mi's long silence, said, "I have been too forward with you, Miss Jiang. If you feel it is not right, I — I..."
Jiang Mi asked with difficulty, "Is the Young Lord not afraid that I would hold him back from the match he deserves?"
Xue Jinglin laughed with evident relief. "I have long had feelings for you, Miss Jiang. There is nothing to hold back."

chapters.title: Fifty seven
chapters.content:
When Xue Jinglin left, he gave Jiang Mi the little white fox puppet as a parting gift.
Jiang Mi picked up the strings and moved it. The white fox's paws came to life, waving about with charming animation.
Looking at the little fox, she could not help but think of what the Young Lord had just said.
He had feelings for her?
So he was willing to use the prospect of marriage to help her avoid entering the palace.
But Jiang Mi asked herself honestly: could she give him anything in return for those feelings?
She had helped Ningzhu, but that had been a small thing. In her view, the arrow Young Lord Xue had taken for her in the hunting grounds had long since cancelled out any debt between them.
Young Lord Xue had a clear and upright character, came from the Duke Zhen State's manor — even with his health troubles, he was considered an enviable match by all the noble young ladies of the capital.
Perhaps he had seen the hesitation in her eyes. He said that if she was unwilling, once everything had been settled, he could agree to let her go — divorce her and restore her freedom.
In that moment, Jiang Mi felt a flush of shame.
Even given that Xue Jinglin was the one who had offered the promise, she was the one seeking his help — and what could she give him in return?
Jiang Mi was deeply grateful for his understanding. Grateful for his thoughtfulness.
She felt admiration for him, and gratitude — but her heart, in its current state, had nothing left with which to answer his feelings.
Jiang Mi pressed her hand against her own chest. The heart inside was beating, warm and alive — but could it ever again give itself wholeheartedly to another person?
Just then the door was pushed open and Xue Ningzhu came bouncing in, half-running to Jiang Mi's side. "Sister Jiang, why did Young Lord Brother leave the palace so quickly?"
"The Young Lord still has matters to attend to in the capital. He will come back in a few days to take you home. Is it not good for you to keep Sister Jiang company here at the palace a little longer?"
Xue Ningzhu smiled brightly. "Of course it is! I was hoping I could stay even more days!"
Before he left, Young Lord Xue had explained that he had only come to escort Ningzhu and should have returned to the capital immediately after. With her recovering here alone, it was not proper for him as a man to linger. But now that he knew what weighed on her heart, he was even more eager to return to the capital and report the matter to the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang. If the Grand Princess agreed, the matter would be more than half settled.
Jiang Mi's mind was already turning over the idea of writing another letter to her father, to gauge his feelings. If it was possible to arrange a quiet match with the Duke Zhen State's manor without alerting her aunt, that would be best of all.
If her father still refused, she would have to prepare for the worst.
......
In the imperial study, Xiao Huaiyan tossed the memorial in his hand aside.
Another impeachment of the Jiang family.
Over the past two days, the Censorate had submitted two memorials in succession — one accusing the Marquess of Cheng'en, Jiang Qingde, of negligence in the handling of his duties, and one accusing the Ministry of Works official Jiang Qingxuan of bribery and the abuse of his position. Both were matters that could be treated as major or minor, serious or light.
Both memorials had been placed directly before him rather than submitted through the court in open session. That particular choice of method was quietly interesting.
At the front end of the hunt, a woman from the Jiang family had been permitted to recover at the temporary palace on account of her service in protecting the Emperor. And now, not long after, memorials impeaching the Jiang family had quietly begun to appear.
According to the contents, Jiang Qingde's negligence had not risen to the level of demotion — at most, a fine and a reprimand, enough to embarrass him but not truly harm him.
As for Jiang Qingxuan, an old classmate had come to him in a scrape, and he had arranged for someone to intercede and used money to smooth the matter over. The aggrieved party had been paid off at the time and considered it settled. Now that same aggrieved party had come forward again at the Shuntian Prefecture claiming that he had been pressured and had only accepted the payment to keep the peace — and had dragged Jiang Qingxuan back into the complaint along with it.
None of this would touch the roots of the Jiang family. It would merely damage their reputation.
Xiao Huaiyan gave a short laugh. How interesting. Someone had grown impatient and was probing his intentions.
He had not yet established his inner court, nor given any sign of whom he intended to bring in.
Some people, unwilling to be caught unprepared, were simply taking precautions — forestalling the possibility of the Jiang family using the act of saving the Emperor's life to secure a high rank for their daughter.
Xiao Huaiyan said to Li Fu, "Go find Pei Chi."
Li Fu assented and withdrew.
Xiao Huaiyan rubbed his brow. His mind flashed to the dream — to Jiang Mi pleading for her cousin's sake. He did not know what had occurred in the dream, but the look of frightened timidity in her apricot eyes when he had rebuked her left him inexplicably irritated.
Xiao Huaiyan closed his eyes for a moment, then opened them and picked up the pulse report that had been set to one side.
Chief Physician Gu had been recording every prescription and dosage used for Jiang Mi, along with notes on the progress of the arrow wound.
The wound was healing. She was still weak and depleted. There was excessive melancholy and brooding.
Xiao Huaiyan's gaze stopped on those last few characters. His brow drew together, and he felt a sudden aversion to them.
The feeling came without warning. He stilled himself and pushed the discomfort down.
When Li Fu and Pei Chi walked into the imperial study, Li Fu saw the Emperor with his head resting in one hand, pressing at his temple as though in pain. His heart clenched — could the Emperor's headaches be worsening?
He hurried forward and held out a small wooden box. "Your Majesty, is the head ailment troubling you? Would you like to take a pill first?"
Xiao Huaiyan turned and looked at the dark pill inside the box. He waved it away.
The discomfort just now had not been caused by the headache.
Xiao Huaiyan passed the memorial he had set aside to Li Fu, and had him hand it to Pei Chi.
"Commander Pei, have a look."
Pei Chi opened it and finished reading quickly.
Xiao Huaiyan said, "Look into it. And find out who is behind this Censor Liu."
Pei Chi bowed. "By your command."
He hesitated a moment, then said, "Your Majesty — Miss Xue of the Duke Zhen State's manor was escorted to the temporary palace by the young lord to visit the patient. However, the young lord departed the palace again very shortly afterward, leaving Miss Xue there alone."
Xiao Huaiyan was quiet for a moment. "After Xue Jinglin returned to the capital from the temporary palace, did he go anywhere?"
Pei Chi said, "He went to Qinhe Studio, bought some pastries, and went home. No other unusual activity."
Xiao Huaiyan said nothing further and dismissed Pei Chi.
......
The Duke Zhen State's Manor.
The Grand Princess of Zhaoyang, making the most of a rare spell of sunshine, was in the courtyard pruning her favorite camellia bushes.
She had just clipped away a weak branch when she saw Xue Jinglin walking over. He took the garden shears from her hand. "Grandmother, let me help."
The Grand Princess of Zhaoyang shook her head and pulled her hand away, as though on purpose to be contrary.
"You — always indulging Ningzhu in her whims. She wanted to go to the temporary palace, and you went and escorted her yourself. You two — you and your sister are getting far too close to the Marquess of Cheng'en's household."
Xue Jinglin said, "Grandmother, is it not a good thing that Ningzhu finally has someone who looks out for her and cares for her? You yourself have spoken well of that young lady from the Jiang family."
The Grand Princess straightened up and looked at him. "Jinglin, have you still not put that notion of yours to rest?"
Xue Jinglin smiled. "As expected, Grandmother knows me best."
He swept back his robe and knelt on the ground. "Grandmother, I wish to ask for the hand of the third young lady of the Jiang family. I beg you to agree!"
The Grand Princess was startled by the gesture.
She had thought Xue Jinglin had only mentioned it in passing before. She had not expected him to have been carrying it in his heart all this time — willing to take an arrow for that young woman in the hunting grounds, and now kneeling before her for the sake of making her his wife.
The Grand Princess's expression turned stern. "Jinglin, do you know what you are doing? Do you think the third young lady of the Jiang family is one you can simply ask to marry? Don't tell me you don't know that the Jiang family intends to send her into the palace to become an imperial consort."
Xue Jinglin said, "I am well aware. I know it all. But I thought — if I do not try, how will I know that it cannot succeed? Grandmother, you know Ningzhu's situation. Her nature means she needs a sister-in-law who truly likes her and cherishes her — someone who will let her live out her days in peace and happiness. That young lady from the Jiang family is rare in that Ningzhu genuinely takes to her, and she herself has a naturally kind and gentle character. I am drawn to her as well. Grandmother, I have had too many regrets in my life — can you not let me close up this one? Besides, it is only the Jiang family who wishes to send Miss Jiang into the palace. She herself has not necessarily agreed to it."
The Grand Princess's hand trembled slightly as she raised it. "Have you and that Jiang girl developed any improper attachment?"
Xue Jinglin shook his head. "Grandmother, Miss Jiang is entirely blameless. It is I who have feelings for her."
The Grand Princess sighed. Jinglin had pressed the matter to this point — he was forcing her hand, leaving her no choice but to agree.
The Grand Princess bent and helped Xue Jinglin to his feet. "If you truly want to marry her, we must proceed carefully. The Empress Dowager Jiang will not be a small obstacle. Back when the Empress Dowager tried time and again to have the Emperor take Jiang Mi into the palace and the Emperor showed no interest, it was clear enough that the Jiang girl did not mean so very much to him. But now that Jiang Mi has earned credit for saving his life, who knows what moves the Empress Dowager will make."
Xue Jinglin said, "With Grandmother here, I believe I will get what I hope for."
The Grand Princess said, "I am only agreeing to make an attempt. Whether it succeeds is a matter for Heaven to decide."
......
Fengchi Temporary Palace.
With Xue Ningzhu to keep her company, Jiang Mi's spirits improved considerably. The two of them ate dinner together, talked for a while, and when Jiang Mi noticed Ningzhu stifling yawns, she had Qiuyu walk her back to her courtyard to rest.
Jiang Mi leaned against the chaise with her eyes closed, gathering herself, waiting for Attendant Ye to bring her bedtime medicine.
A figure came around the screen and walked toward her.
Jiang Mi heard the footsteps and, thinking it was Attendant Ye, opened her eyes — and saw Xiao Huaiyan.
Xiao Huaiyan unclasped his black outer robe and tossed it casually over the pear-wood chair, then sat down across from Jiang Mi. "Why are you alone in the bedchamber?"
Jiang Mi had thought that after the cold and stiff manner in which he had left last time, he would not return.
Seeing his expression calm and his tone easy, she could not read his intentions at all.
She answered quietly, "Qiuyu has gone to walk Miss Xue back. Attendant Ye is still preparing my medicine."
A silence fell between them.
Xiao Huaiyan noticed the ring of white fur trim on the collar of what she was wearing today. The softness of it against her face gave her a look of gentle docility.
He saw that she seemed at a loss — as though she had not decided how to face him.
He found himself wondering: had his words last time been too harsh? Had he frightened her?
Xiao Huaiyan glanced around the room, mildly restless, and noticed that the curio shelf now held a porcelain doll that had not been there before. The doll bore a passing resemblance to Jiang Mi — three parts similar, perhaps — but the craftsmanship was too deliberate, too mechanical. The form was there; the spirit was not.
"When did that arrive?"
Already on edge, guessing at Xiao Huaiyan's purpose, Jiang Mi looked where he indicated and saw the porcelain doll Young Lord Xue had sent.
She held her breath and answered lightly, "Ningzhu gave it to me."
Xiao Huaiyan moved his gaze to her slight frame. He found himself idly wondering: if she were to sit on a swing, would that featherlight body of hers have enough strength to hold on to the ropes?
At that moment Attendant Ye came in carrying the medicine.
She had seen Li Gonggong at the hall entrance and understood that the Emperor was here.
She paid her respects and handed the medicine to Jiang Mi, then withdrew.
Xiao Huaiyan remembered that Jiang Mi had been rather particular about taking her medicine before — feeding it to her one spoonful at a time had taken a great deal of patience.
Now she took up the bowl herself, blew on it gently, and drank it down sip by sip.
Watching how matter-of-fact she was about it — as natural as eating or drinking — no longer complaining that it was bitter, no longer murmuring in a daze that Tangtan wanted pastries.
In front of him, she was quiet and well-behaved.
For no reason he could account for, the four words he had read in the pulse report drifted back into his mind. Excessive melancholy and brooding. She was so young — how could she have let worry accumulate into illness?
Xiao Huaiyan turned the jade ring on his thumb. "Jiang Mi — at the New Year's banquet, I will grant you one wish."
Jiang Mi could barely believe what she was hearing.
She did not know where the courage came from, but she asked, "Your Majesty, is that truly so?"
Xiao Huaiyan saw the bewilderment in her face and said with a slight smile, "You earned credit for saving the Emperor's life. This is your due reward."
"Would Your Majesty agree to anything?" Jiang Mi swallowed. The bitter aftertaste of the medicine had gone numb in her mouth.
Xiao Huaiyan smiled but said nothing. The Jiang family would make good use of this opportunity, no doubt.
As long as it was within reason, he would give her a dignified way forward.

chapters.title: Fifty eight
chapters.content:
Chapter 58
As winter deepened, the weather grew steadily colder.
The Fengchi Temporary Palace, being close to the mountains and forest, was colder even than the capital.
During this period, having Xue Ningzhu for company had considerably improved Jiang Mi's spirits.
Her wound was gradually healing too, and she was able to get out of bed and walk slowly about.
Over their meals together, she had heard Ningzhu mention the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang several times.
She could tell that Ningzhu was missing home, missing her grandmother.
Beautiful as the temporary palace's scenery was, it was still, at its heart, too desolate and still.
"Ningzhu, write to the Young Lord and ask him to come and take you back to the capital," Jiang Mi said, placing a chopstickful of osmanthus-braised fish onto her plate.
Ningzhu looked up, mid-bite. "But if I go, won't Sister Jiang be all alone?"
Jiang Mi smiled. "My wound should be nearly healed. Once Chief Physician Gu examines me and confirms that riding in a carriage would not set it back, I'll be returning to the capital as well. Besides, Qiuyu and the others are here to keep me company. You've been away so many days already — the Grand Princess must be missing you. You should go back and spend time with her."
Xue Ningzhu hesitated, then nodded.
She linked her arm through Jiang Mi's, reluctant. "If only Sister Jiang could come home with me."
Said without thinking, heard with feeling.
Jiang Mi thought to herself: if all went well, she would indeed one day be going home with Ningzhu.
The way things had unfolded was beyond even what she had anticipated.
Once Xue Ningzhu's letter was sent, Xue Jinglin arrived at the palace first thing the following morning with his escort.
Jiang Mi, leaning on Qiuyu's arm, walked slowly out to see Xue Ningzhu to the carriage.
She also caught sight of the Young Lord, whom she had not seen for some days.
Xue Jinglin smiled at her with a small nod.
From the meaning in that smile, Jiang Mi thought she understood: he had persuaded the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang.
Seeing that Jiang Mi had understood at once, his smile deepened.
Xue Ningzhu ran toward him. "Young Lord Brother, I missed you so much!"
Xue Jinglin said a few soothing words to Ningzhu, then turned to Jiang Mi. "Thank you for taking the trouble to look after Ningzhu."
Jiang Mi lowered her head. "It was Ningzhu who looked after me."
Xue Ningzhu thoroughly agreed with this and said so at once: "I was the one looking after Sister Jiang!" She took a scroll from Qiuyu's hands and held it out to Xue Jinglin. "This is a painting of the Fengchi autumn maples that Sister Jiang taught me to make. Brother, have a look — is it good? I want to give it to Grandmother when I get back."
Xue Jinglin listened to Ningzhu speak, but his eyes were on Jiang Mi. He saw her smile and give a small nod.
He took the scroll from Ningzhu's hands. "Very well. Let me hold onto it for now and take a proper look later."
Xue Ningzhu took her maid's hand and climbed into the carriage, then leaned out to wave to Jiang Mi with lingering reluctance.
Xue Jinglin swung into the saddle and cupped his hands toward Jiang Mi. "Until we meet again, Miss Jiang."
When he returned, Xue Jinglin unrolled the scroll. Something fell out from inside it — a letter.
He unfolded the paper and read it, and his expression first showed surprise, then the corner of his mouth slowly curved upward.
He sat at his desk for a long while, then burned both the letter and its envelope with a flint.
......
With Xue Ningzhu gone, the vast Fengchi Palace grew more empty and cold than ever.
Jiang Mi waited for Chief Physician Gu to come and examine her. He told her she needed a few more days of rest to consolidate her recovery — if the carriage journey jostled the wound open, the damage would not be worth it.
She had hoped to be back at the Marquess's manor before the winter solstice.
It seemed she would have to remain at the temporary palace and wait out the holiday here.
The days slipped by, and the winter solstice arrived.
The under-floor heating in the bedchamber kept it warm, and Jiang Mi had been nestled in bed these past days without much activity.
Mama Cui came in with a bright smile to announce that both the Empress Dowager and the Emperor had sent gifts, and the manor had also sent over a great quantity of food.
But Jiang Mi could not quite summon the spirit for it. If she were at home, the whole family would be gathered together for the winter solstice meal — lively and full.
For no particular reason, she found herself thinking she would like to make dumplings.
She had Qiuyu prepare the filling according to her instructions, and sat up to fold them herself.
By the time the meal came, Jiang Mi looked at the cooked dumplings and found she had lost her appetite again.
She drank her medicine that night and went to bed early.
When Xiao Huaiyan came through the darkness and saw the cold blackness of the Fengchi Temporary Palace, his expression chilled.
He glanced at Li Fu and walked straight toward the bedchamber.
Li Fu felt a cold prickling at the back of his neck — unjust, truly unjust. He had no idea why the temporary palace was so bleak and dark on the winter solstice. Could the servants he had left here be deliberately neglecting the young lady? But every one of the eunuchs and palace maids he had kept here was sharp-minded — none of them would be so foolish.
He immediately summoned the head eunuch and dressed him down at once, ordering him to get the lanterns hung and light up every inch of the Fengchi Palace.
The head eunuch protested his innocence, explaining that Miss Jiang had had no appetite and had gone to sleep early, so he had extinguished some of the lamps.
Li Fu wiped the cold sweat from his brow and sent the man off to work.
Xiao Huaiyan entered the bedchamber and, unsurprisingly, found Jiang Mi asleep.
Qiuyu had been keeping watch at the bedside and nearly jumped out of her skin at the sudden appearance of a figure. Recognizing who it was, she hastened to pay her respects.
Xiao Huaiyan asked, "Why has she gone to sleep this early?"
Qiuyu replied, trembling with nerves, "Your Majesty, this servant is not certain either. Perhaps making the dumplings earlier tired her, and she went to rest sooner."
Xiao Huaiyan raised an eyebrow. "She made dumplings?"
Qiuyu nodded, not daring to hold anything back. "Yes, Your Majesty. Miss made the dumplings, but she did not eat any herself."
Xiao Huaiyan glanced at the figure on the bed and said, "Go and cook some of the dumplings she made and bring them here."
Qiuyu blinked, but did not dare say a word, and went to do as she was told. Fortunately she had set aside the dumplings her mistress had made, thinking she might want them tomorrow. It had not occurred to her that the Emperor would ask for them first.
Xiao Huaiyan looked at Jiang Mi, lying on her side with her back toward him, curled into the quilt. According to the daily pulse reports from Chief Physician Gu, the arrow wound had scabbed over and healed, but her body was still weak and depleted, and the wound still needed careful tending.
Xiao Huaiyan picked up a fox-fur cape that was draped over the chaise. He lifted the quilt, wrapped the person curled inside it in the fur, and picked her up in his arms.
He weighed the bundle in his arms for a moment. All this recovery, and she was still this light.
Jiang Mi was a light sleeper, and had been drifting half-awake at the sound of voices. She slipped back toward sleep when they faded — but before long she felt herself rocking, as though she were in a small boat tossed in a storm, a wave nearly tipping her into the water. She reached out on instinct to grab hold of something, and her hands found something solid. She gripped it and did not let go.
She tried to look up, but first she heard a low laugh. "Awake?"
The sound of that voice jolted her fully awake from the last of her drowsiness.
She opened her eyes and, once they adjusted to the brightness, saw that what she had grabbed in the dark was Xiao Huaiyan's hand.
Jiang Mi yanked her hand back at once.
She tried to lean away and realized she was sitting in Xiao Huaiyan's arms at the table.
On the table before them sat two steaming plates of dumplings.
She struggled to get down, but his arms were like iron clamps — she could not budge him. Xiao Huaiyan gave her a light pat. "Stop fussing. Keep me company and eat some dumplings."
Jiang Mi had the distinct feeling she had not yet truly woken up. Was she still dreaming?
Xiao Huaiyan set several dumplings in a small bowl, put a pair of chopsticks in with them, and pressed it into Jiang Mi's hands. Then he helped himself calmly, dipped a dumpling in the vinegar, and began to eat.
He found the taste suited him well and ate several in a row.
But Jiang Mi had not moved.
Xiao Huaiyan looked at her bowed head. "What — do you want me to feed you?"
Jiang Mi's fingers tightened on the bowl until her knuckles went white. She bit her lip and slowly picked up a dumpling and put it in her mouth.
One bite and she knew: these were the ones she had made.
Xiao Huaiyan had nearly finished his plate. Jiang Mi's bowl still looked mostly full.
She had probably eaten only two or three, and then said she could not manage any more.
"Truly full?"
Xiao Huaiyan's hand slipped inside the fur cape and moved toward her abdomen, as though to check whether her words held up.
Jiang Mi grabbed his hand in a panic. "Your Majesty, my digestion is weak — I cannot eat too much."
Xiao Huaiyan, seeing the flustered indignation on her face, did not press her.
She was not yet the pliable, compliant creature she was in the dream. She was still a sheltered girl from a good family, with the modesty that came with it.
Xiao Huaiyan took the bowl from her hands and, perfectly composed, ate the remaining dumplings for her.
He put Jiang Mi back on the bed. "Chief Physician Gu says your wound will be more or less healed in a few more days. When the time comes, I will send someone to escort you back to the capital."
Worried that Xiao Huaiyan meant to have her brought directly to the palace, Jiang Mi looked up at him in anxiety. "Your Majesty — I want to go home. To the Marquess's manor."
Xiao Huaiyan heard the fear in her voice and understood. He said with a smile, "Of course. To the manor."
The winter solstice was past. The new year was not far, and with it the New Year's banquet. There was no need to be in such a rush.
......
In the biting cold, Jiang Mi stepped out of the bedchamber wrapped up well. In front of the main hall she saw a magnificent carriage waiting.
Beside the carriage, two escorts stood in formation.
A young general in armor dismounted and bowed to the approaching Jiang Mi. "Miss Jiang, we are under His Majesty's orders to escort you back to the capital."
The young general personally held aside the carriage curtain. "Miss Jiang, please."
Mama Cui and Qiuyu, seeing this young general's forward manner, moved to step in front of Jiang Mi — but they saw Jiang Mi fix her gaze steadily on the young general's face. Without a word of complaint, she stepped onto the mounting block and into the carriage. As the curtain fell, she said something quietly to the young general.
The handsome young general stood there, stunned.
Qiuyu and Mama Cui climbed in after, letting the curtain fall between them and that presumptuous general.
Mama Cui muttered under her breath that she would report this to the Empress Dowager once they returned.
But Jiang Mi let out a soft laugh.
Neither Qiuyu nor Mama Cui could understand why their young miss was smiling.
Outside the carriage, the young general watched the carriage begin to move, still looking puzzled.
A moment later, a young man walked over and shoved the general. "Elder Sister, I told you not to come in this getup. Of course you frightened Miss Jiang looking like that. Now we're in trouble — if Miss Jiang goes crying to the Empress Dowager, the Emperor is going to punish me for mishandling things again."
The young general shoved the bothersome younger sibling away and said, "Gu Chang, do you know what she just said to me?"
Gu Chang made a resigned face. "She tore into you for being a scoundrel?"
Gu Xuan shook her head, her tone carrying a note of disbelief. "She said 'thank you, Miss.' How did she know I was a woman?"
Gu Xuan grabbed Gu Chang and stared at him hard. "Do I look like a woman to you?"
Gu Chang said helplessly, "Elder Sister, stop making a scene. She must have heard the words wrong — you must have cursed at her and she misheard it as a thanks."
Gu Chang swung into the saddle and called to Gu Xuan, "Elder Sister, don't hold up the departure."
Gu Xuan rode to catch up without hurrying. She and Gu Chang went to the front to lead the way — the two siblings had come to the Fengchi Temporary Palace for the express purpose of escorting the third young lady of the Jiang family back to the capital without a hair out of place.
Along the road, Gu Xuan could not help saying to Gu Chang, "You know, that Miss Jiang looks so delicate and frail, like a breeze could knock her over — so how is she so good at pitch-pot? Word is she never misses. I really want to try competing with her. But then again I'm afraid of scaring her off. Hey — Gu Chang, do you know how to get on a young lady's good side?"
Gu Chang walked straight into a mouthful of cold wind and choked. "Elder Sister, I have personally witnessed Miss Jiang's pitch-pot. She really is quite something. But her wound just healed — do not go bothering her. I really cannot understand why the Emperor suddenly called you back from the border."
Gu Xuan gave her brother a contemptuous look. "Just as I thought — a useless lump who doesn't know the first thing about charming a young lady. As for why the Emperor called me back — obviously he wants me to go into the palace and be a consort!"
That last sentence nearly knocked Gu Chang off his horse. He looked at his elder sister — currently with a wild grass stem hanging from her mouth, slouching along like she owned the world — and tried to imagine her as an imperial consort.
Gu Xuan spat out the grass stem and gazed at the sky. "Don't give me that look. The Emperor said so himself — do well for two or three years, then I get to 'die of illness' and go back to Yunzhou and live as I please. Do a bad job and, heaven help me, the life is simply not worth living."
Gu Xuan lamented this for a moment, then turned to Gu Chang. "You mind the road. I'm going to go find a pretty young lady to cheer me up."
When the party stopped to rest, Gu Xuan pulled off her helmet and let her dark hair fall free. She twisted it up loosely and headed toward the carriage.
Jiang Mi was half-reclining on the soft blankets inside, turning something over in her mind. She had not expected to encounter Elder Sister Duan Fei at this particular moment.
Looking at the way she had deliberately teased her just now, Jiang Mi had not been able to resist teasing her back a little.
In her previous life at the palace, Elder Sister Duan Fei had always been scrupulously fair and impartial in her governance of the inner court — yet she had shown partiality to Jiang Mi, and the two of them had grown close. But their closeness had displeased Xiao Huaiyan. One night she had stayed chatting with Elder Sister Duan Fei until late and simply slept over in her bedchamber — only for Xiao Huaiyan to arrive at Yikun Palace in the middle of the night and drag her away by force. She had been too embarrassed to show her face before Elder Sister Duan Fei for quite some time afterward.
At least that would not happen in this life.
Only — Elder Sister Duan Fei was such a fine person, and yet she had gone before even Jiang Mi had. Truly, fate was cruel.
From outside came a bright, cheerful voice: "Miss Jiang, might I come in and beg a cup of hot tea?"
The moment she heard it, Jiang Mi knew it was Gu Xuan. She had Qiuyu invite her in.
Gu Xuan sprang lightly up into the carriage. She held out a small handful of wildflowers she had picked. "A humble little gift. Please do not think too little of it."
Jiang Mi accepted the flowers and offered a cup of tea in return. "In the middle of winter, a bunch of wildflowers is rarer by far than a cup of hot tea. Thank you for the gift."
Gu Xuan found that the more she looked at the young lady from the Jiang family, the more she liked her. Soft-natured, sweet-voiced, pleasant to talk to.
When the tea was done she was still reluctant to leave, but they were soon to set off again, and she could not keep cramming herself into someone else's carriage.
Gu Xuan rode up alongside Gu Chang. "Does Miss Jiang have any older brothers?"
Gu Chang found this baffling. "She has a younger brother of about five. Are you thinking of taking him home as a godson, Elder Sister?"
Gu Xuan turned it over in her mind. Miss Jiang was so beautiful — surely her brothers couldn't be too bad either? She was doing the Emperor a service and earning merit. Surely there ought to be some benefit in it for her.
What a pity.
Gu Chang added, "Though she does have two paternal cousins — one in the capital, one in the provinces with his father. My advice would be to abandon whatever idea you have, Elder Sister. Those Jiang cousins are not worth it. I have met the eldest — somewhat dull-witted."
Gu Xuan laughed, swishing her riding crop through the air. "A bit dull is fine. As long as they're good-looking. It's men like the Emperor — who seem to have ten hidden schemes running at once — that are the dangerous kind. Next time, take me to meet him?"
Gu Chang decided to pretend he hadn't heard a word.
......
Jiang Mi made it safely back to the Marquess of Cheng'en's manor. The unexpected gift of the journey had been the chance to get to know Gu Xuan of the Loyal and Brave Marquess's household.
At their parting, Gu Xuan warmly invited her to go out together next time, and Jiang Mi agreed with pleasure.
Back at the manor, her aunt had not sent anyone to take her into the palace — she heard from her elder aunt-by-marriage that the Empress Dowager had caught a chill, and not wanting to pass it to Jiang Mi, who had only just recovered from her wound, had chosen not to call for her.
The Marchioness of Cheng'en told Jiang Mi to rest well and restore her health, and to go into the palace to visit the Empress Dowager at the New Year's banquet.
The moment Jiang Mi returned to Yuanzhi Courtyard, Mianmian came bounding over to her.
Jiang Rong and Jiang Xuan had been waiting for her. Jiang Wan and Jiang Yi arrived a little behind them.
The small courtyard filled with noise and laughter.
But all the sisters knew Jiang Mi must not be overtired, and none of them stayed long.
Lying on her bed that night, Jiang Mi thought: how wonderful it would be if things could stay like this.
The very next morning, the first visitor to Yuanzhi Courtyard was Jiang Qingxuan.
When Jiang Mi saw her father, she immediately thought of the letter she had sent to him from the temporary palace.
She had never received a reply during her recovery there. That surely meant he had not been persuaded. She had already prepared herself for a scolding.
Jiang Qingxuan looked at his daughter — home with a body full of wounds, and still carrying the credit of saving the Emperor. He remembered how when Tangtan was little and fell and hurt herself, she would cry for a long while. This arrow wound had been dangerous and serious, and he could only imagine how much suffering she had endured. And she had spent all that time recovering alone in the temporary palace, with who knew what difficulties.
Jiang Qingxuan took out the letter she had written him. "Tangtan, is this truly what your heart wants?"
Seeing that her father did not look as though he was about to lose his temper, hope rose in Jiang Mi's chest. She bowed to him. "Father, I do not want to enter the palace. I beg you to grant me this."
Jiang Qingxuan helped her up and let out a long sigh. She had almost lost her life at the birthday banquet, at the Duke Zhen State's manor, and at the hunting grounds — she had already endured more than enough...
"Tangtan — if the day ever comes when the Marquess of Cheng'en's manor can no longer protect you, your father's wish is that you live well, regardless."
Jiang Mi's eyes filled with a glimmer of tears. Did this mean her father agreed?
Jiang Qingxuan patted her shoulder. "Your aunt will not give her consent easily. Tangtan, I will try to persuade her again. Do not be anxious. I will think of something."
Jiang Mi covered her mouth, afraid her sob would come out audibly.
She had thought her father would scold her. Had thought he would tell her to abandon this idea.
But he had agreed.
The great stone that had been pressing on her heart for so long gave way.
Jiang Qingxuan, at a loss in the face of his daughter's voiceless weeping, patted her awkwardly — he had never known how to offer comfort. "Tangtan, your aunt can be stubborn on certain matters, but she has her own hardships and reasons you don't know. Do not blame her for them."
If things truly came to that — it was not something a young girl like Tangtan could do anything to prevent.
The Empress Dowager's fixation ran too deep.
......
The days at home passed in comfort and flew by. Before she knew it, the year was nearly at its end.
New Year's Eve was almost upon them. The women of the Marquess of Cheng'en's household would be entering the palace for the New Year's banquet.
Everyone else was happily choosing their dresses and jewelry for the occasion.
Jiang Mi alone was thinking: this night was the one — her clean break from the past life.

chapters.title: Fifty nine
chapters.content:
On New Year's Eve, the palace hosted a great imperial banquet, and civil and military officials attended with their families.
The women of the Marquess of Cheng'en's household were led by palace attendants to Cining Palace first, to pay their respects to the Empress Dowager.
Jiang Mi watched Cining Palace draw closer and closer, and her nerves quietly began to rise.
Jiang Yi nudged her. "Tangtan — I was just speaking to you and you've drifted off again."
Jiang Mi turned to her. "Forgive me, Elder Sister Yi. I was just looking at the plum blossoms over there. What were you saying? Say it again?"
Jiang Yi craned her neck to look where Jiang Mi pointed. A branch of red plum had stretched out over the top of the wall, blooming in full, brilliant red.
She leaned close to Jiang Mi's ear. "Tangtan, I've heard that Gu Xuan, the eldest daughter of the Loyal and Brave Marquess, has also come back from Yunzhou. People are saying the Emperor already has her in mind. No one knows what she looks like or what kind of temperament she has."
Jiang Mi smiled. "Why is Elder Sister Yi keeping track of all this?"
Jiang Yi said, "I'm worried about you, of course! Gu Xuan has been in Yunzhou all these years — she and the Emperor go back a long way. Who knows if she'll be the first to enter the palace!"
Jiang Yi's concern made her voice rise a little, and the Marchioness of Cheng'en, walking ahead, turned around and shot her a sharp look.
Jiang Yi's neck retracted. She shrank.
Jiang Mi tried not to laugh. She knew Elder Sister Yi was worrying about whether her fellow consorts would be easy to get along with, after she entered the palace.
In her previous life, Elder Sister Gu had indeed been the first to enter the palace. She had been conferred the title of Consort Duan the moment she arrived, and had governed the inner court.
Before long they arrived at Cining Palace. The Empress Dowager's senior palace maid, Qingxue, came to receive them, all smiles. "Marchioness, Second Miss, Third Miss — the Empress Dowager has been waiting for you for some time."
She looked at Jiang Mi a moment longer. "Third Miss, you are looking well. Your color has quite improved. The Empress Dowager will be so pleased to see Third Miss."
Jiang Mi smiled at Qingxue. After her return to the capital, her aunt had sent Qingxue to call on her.
The party followed Qingxue into the inner chamber. The room was faintly fragrant with sandalwood. Empress Dowager Jiang sat on the chaise, smiling.
After everyone had paid their respects, the Empress Dowager bid them all be seated.
She said to Jiang Mi, "Tangtan, come here and let your aunt have a proper look at you."
Jiang Mi stepped forward as bidden. She noticed that in the time since she had last seen her aunt, more silver had crept into her hair, and the lines on her face had deepened. Her eyes carried traces of weariness.
"Aunt." Jiang Mi called softly.
The Empress Dowager took Jiang Mi's hands and looked her over carefully, her expression tender with concern. "You've gotten thinner. Does the wound still hurt?"
Jiang Mi shook her head. "Much better now." As long as she didn't make large movements or touch the spot, she could no longer feel the pain.
The Empress Dowager had her sit beside her. "You're always saying that. An arrow wound doesn't truly heal in a short time — you must continue to tend it carefully. Don't leave any lasting trouble behind."
Jiang Mi nodded obediently. The tenderness in her aunt's manner made the ache in her own heart all the worse.
The Empress Dowager had summoned the Marquess of Cheng'en's household before the banquet for no other reason than to see Jiang Mi. This child had suffered one misfortune after another, and it had put a distance between them. She had thought about how Tangtan's father had come to the palace three times trying to persuade her to let Tangtan go — to let the child live in peace, without the demands of the palace. But if the family fell from prominence, where was the peaceful life to come from? Thankfully, Tangtan had taken an arrow for the Emperor and now held credit for saving his life. She had one more piece of leverage in hand. Once she was in the palace, her footing would be firmer.
The Empress Dowager exchanged a few words with the Marchioness, and when it was nearly time for the banquet to begin, she rose and took Jiang Mi's arm to walk with her toward the banquet hall.
By now the hall was nearly full — the wives and daughters of the hundred officials, the women of the royal and noble clans. Even the Virtuous Dowager Consort and Princess An'yang, who had been confined to their quarters for many days without appearing, had arrived early.
The Grand Princess of Zhaoyang was also among the guests.
The Marchioness of Cheng'en led Jiang Yi ahead and was shown to their seats by a palace maid.
The Empress Dowager walked into the hall with Jiang Mi at her side. At the threshold, she said quietly to Jiang Mi, "Tangtan, do not be afraid. Your aunt will stand by you."
Jiang Mi felt a bittersweet ache in her chest. She was bound to disappoint her aunt today.
"Her Ladyship the Empress Dowager arrives!"
The eunuch's announcement brought all the conversations in the hall to a halt as the gathered women turned to look toward the entrance.
They saw a young woman of snow-white skin and flower-like features walk in slowly on the Empress Dowager's arm.
More than a few understood at once: the Empress Dowager was openly showing her support for her niece.
Some of the more eager observers glanced toward Gu Xuan — the eldest daughter of the Loyal and Brave Marquess who had recently returned from Yunzhou. She was holding a wine cup, a smile at the corner of her lips, watching the Jiang girl with obvious curiosity.
This Gu girl's father was one of the Emperor's most trusted confidants, and she herself was a frontrunner to enter the inner court.
The banquet tonight was sure to be lively.
After seeing her aunt to the head seat, Jiang Mi paid her respects to the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang and the Virtuous Dowager Consort in turn.
The Grand Princess smiled with great warmth and gave Jiang Mi a kind nod.
The Virtuous Dowager Consort was somewhat more restrained than she had been at the birthday banquet. She said with a smile, "Miss Jiang, please do not stand on ceremony — I can see how slight your frame is and it quite pains me. Do sit down."
A flicker of displeasure crossed the Empress Dowager's brow. The Virtuous Dowager Consort's comment about Tangtan's slight frame was a deliberate insinuation that she might not be well-suited to bearing children.
If the Virtuous Dowager Consort had not once shown the Emperor kindness, and if her daughter were not still unmarried, she would have long since found a pretext to pack the Virtuous Dowager Consort off to the temporary palace.
Jiang Mi returned to the Marquess of Cheng'en's party and had barely sat down when she saw that the seat directly across from her was occupied by Gu Xuan. Gu Xuan raised her cup toward her.
Jiang Mi gave a small, quiet shake of her head — she was not yet able to drink wine. She lifted the teacup beside her in return as a wordless toast.
The warm liquid entered her mouth and soothed her fluttering heart.
At the New Year's banquet, felicitations and good wishes flowed without end, and the songs and dances arranged by the Office of Music were a pleasure to behold.
When one performance ended, the announcement rang out from beyond the hall: "His Majesty the Emperor arrives!"
The dancers and musicians withdrew. The ladies all rose to their feet.
Xiao Huaiyan entered in dragon robes and jade crown, his bearing majestic and splendid — like jade, like a ceremonial scepter.
After he was seated, the ladies performed their obeisance.
Xiao Huaiyan raised his hand. "All may be seated."
He had drunk a few cups more than usual with his officials at the Jiaotai Hall, and his narrow phoenix eyes carried a faint warmth. As he spoke, his manner was as mild as a spring breeze.
The ladies offered their thanks and sat. Xiao Huaiyan's gaze moved to Jiang Mi as she rose, and found her color better than when she had been at the temporary palace.
Still too thin, though. Had the Jiang family not been looking after her properly?
The senior royal relatives were making conversation with Xiao Huaiyan. He listened with half his attention and offered a word or two in response.
Someone — it was hard to say who started it — remarked, "His Majesty is the true Son of Heaven, watched over by Heaven itself. No danger fails to turn to fortune for him."
"Exactly — at the hunt, in that most perilous moment, when the danger was at its height, someone came forward and shielded His Majesty. Under His Majesty's divine protection, our great Wei prospers year after year, and the realm is at peace."
An elderly princess said, "I hear that it was the niece of Empress Dowager Jiang? Such courage and devotion in such a delicate young girl — truly admirable!"
The Empress Dowager said modestly with a smile, "That little girl of mine didn't think half so clearly — it was pure instinct that made her throw herself forward. His Majesty's sacred person is what matters above everything."
The court ladies seated nearby understood what they were hearing. In all likelihood the Empress Dowager had deliberately arranged for someone to bring up this topic — a way of paving the road for her niece's entry into the inner court.
The Emperor's expression remained unchanged, giving no apparent sign of displeasure at the Empress Dowager trying to win recognition for her niece.
The Grand Princess of Zhaoyang, who had been silent, said, "Miss Jiang has earned such great credit — has Your Majesty bestowed any reward on her?"
The Grand Princess's words mildly surprised the Empress Dowager. She had been hesitating over whether to ask directly for a reward for Tangtan — and then the Grand Princess had said it for her.
Every lady in the hall held her breath and waited for the Emperor's answer.
Everyone knew what the Jiang family wanted. They wanted to use Jiang Mi's act of saving the Emperor's life as the key to her entry into the inner court.
Xiao Huaiyan turned the jade ring on his finger. He looked toward Jiang Mi. A flicker of amusement moved through his phoenix eyes, and he said in a mild tone, "Miss Jiang — is there something you wish to receive?"
With those words, every gaze in the hall turned toward Jiang Mi.
Gu Xuan was surprised that the Emperor would say something like this. He was always impeccable — measured and precise in his dispensation of rewards. Why would he hand the initiative to the other party? She propped her chin on her hand, genuinely curious about what the Jiang girl was going to ask for.
Shen Yawei held herself very still, her fingernails digging into her palm until it should have hurt but didn't. She was frightened — frightened that Jiang Mi would ask to be brought into the inner court. Her father had told her not to worry, that the Emperor would never make a Jiang girl his Empress, but she could not put her mind at rest. Even without the position of Empress, a high consort's rank would be threat enough.
The Virtuous Dowager Consort raised a teacup to her lips as though without a care, using it to conceal her resentment. Her own niece was spending her days in a family temple in the company of cold incense and ancient Buddhas, living a life worse than death. And yet the Empress Dowager's niece had the luck to earn this opportunity — to use her act of saving the Emperor as a lever to pry open a promise.
The Empress Dowager was so moved she could hardly contain herself. She had waited so long for this day.
Xiao Huaiyan looked at Jiang Mi calmly. He was waiting for her answer too. He had already drafted the titles; he was only waiting for her to choose one. Once she entered the palace, he would have Chief Physician Gu continue attending to her.
Jiang Mi knew she had one chance at this. She could not let herself flinch.
She rose from her seat, walked out to the open floor, and slowly bowed before the monarch on the throne. Her face was flushed a faint, shy red, and she spoke in a small voice: "This subject wishes — wishes to ask Your Majesty for a bestowal of marriage."
The words fell. All around her, people looked at one another, unable to quite believe what they had heard.
What was the Jiang girl saying?
A marriage bestowal?
Should she not be saying she wished to serve at His Majesty's side — so that he could confer a rank upon her? How had it become a marriage bestowal?
And the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang — His Majesty's own royal aunt — had involved herself in this. When had this Third Miss Jiang come to have anything to do with the young lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor?
Some faces fell grave. Others quietly exhaled in relief. Others broke into smiles, leaning forward to watch how events would unfold.
The Empress Dowager was struck dumb by Jiang Mi's words, one hand pressed to her heart, her mouth not yet open.
Before she could speak, the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang, who sat below her, rose from her seat. She was the elder generation, and she bowed with due ceremony toward the Emperor. "How could we let a young girl come forward to ask for this? These words should have come from me. Since Miss Jiang has spoken openly, allow this old woman to be shameless and ask His Majesty to bestow marriage between this humble family's worthless grandson Xue Jinglin and the third young lady of the Jiang family. I beg Your Majesty to bring this good thing to fruition!"
In the great hall, an uproar arose — whispers and murmurs broke out everywhere.
Li Fu, standing behind Xiao Huaiyan, stared open-mouthed. Had the Jiang girl lost her mind? Had the Grand Princess lost hers?
Li Fu's heart thundered with dread and he absolutely did not dare look at the Emperor's expression.
Xiao Huaiyan did not, as he usually would, immediately go to help the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang to her feet. He left her bowing in her elderly posture.
He turned the jade ring between his fingers, as though toying with it. His eyes dropped. A low, soft laugh escaped him, carrying a chill as cold as frost. "Does the Empress Dowager consent?"

chapters.title: Sixty
chapters.content:
The Empress Dowager was both furious and heartbroken. She had not expected Tangtan to take a moment like this — in front of everyone, with no thought for the family's dignity — to ask the Emperor for a marriage bestowal.
They had waited so long and so painstakingly for this chance to bring her into the palace. And she had thrown it away with her own hands.
Even if she blocked the marriage bestowal now and tried again to send her into the Emperor's inner court, the Emperor would surely have reservations, and would not agree so readily.
What Tangtan had done was to ruin her own future with her own hands.
The Empress Dowager stared at Jiang Mi — saw the pleading in her eyes — then hardened her heart and looked away. She gripped the prayer beads in her hand and said coldly, "Grand Princess, children are thoughtless. She has drunk a little too much and is speaking nonsense. Please do not involve yourself in this."
Then, in an entirely different tone, she rebuked, "Tangtan, withdraw at once!"
Jiang Mi clenched her teeth. Cold sweat had already soaked through her clothing, but she could not retreat. If she did, everything would be lost.
The Grand Princess of Zhaoyang held her posture steadily. "Empress Dowager, the fault does not lie with Miss Jiang. I set my eyes on her at a flower-viewing gathering some time ago, and afterwards I privately mentioned this to the second master of the Jiang family. Both houses have already indicated a willingness to form a match. Marriage arrangements have always gone by the word of a matchmaker and the authority of parents. I had originally thought to seek His Majesty's marriage bestowal simply as a crowning blessing. I beg the Empress Dowager to grant this union."
The Empress Dowager's face went a livid grey. Her chest tightened so badly she nearly fainted. That wretch Jiang Qingxuan had gone behind her back to arrange a match with the Duke Zhen State's manor!
The Virtuous Dowager Consort, watching the Empress Dowager's plans collapse entirely, could not have felt better. She said in a voice of sweet concern, "Empress Dowager — this is wonderful news! Young Lord Xue and Miss Jiang make a fine pair, a lovely match. I say you should agree..."
As she spoke, the Virtuous Dowager Consort shot a glance at the Emperor. The moment her eyes touched those dark, fathomless pupils, the smile on her lips began to freeze. Her heart lurched. She looked away hastily and grabbed the teacup on the table with both hands, using it to mask the sudden shock of cold fear she had just felt.
The Empress Dowager said through gritted teeth, "Grand Princess, this is a matter of the Jiang family's household. I would ask the Princess not to meddle."
Before the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang could reply, Xiao Huaiyan, who had been silent until now, spoke. "Since it is a matter of the Jiang family's household, the Empress Dowager may decide. I will return to the Jiaotai Hall."
The Empress Dowager was grateful for the Emperor's gesture in that moment. Once he left, she could have people take Tangtan away.
Jiang Mi felt a surge of desperation. She could not let Xiao Huaiyan leave. Without a definitive ruling from him, she could not let him go. Once her aunt had control of the situation, this opportunity would never come again.
Jiang Mi threw everything into it. In a trembling voice she called out, "Your Majesty — please stay!"
Xiao Huaiyan, already half-risen from his seat, stopped. He looked at her with an expression that was unreadable.
Jiang Mi raised her eyes to the sovereign seated high above her. Her heart was terrified — but one word at a time she said, "Your Majesty once said that for this subject's act of saving the Emperor, a wish would be granted. A sovereign's word is as inviolable as gold. This subject — this subject begs Your Majesty to fulfill this wish!"
Li Fu desperately wanted to rush down and clap his hand over the Jiang girl's mouth. Miss Jiang — please, no more.
The Empress Dowager cried sharply, "Jiang Mi!"
Jiang Mi sank to her knees in a full bow. "I beg Your Majesty to grant this!"
The great hall fell absolutely still.
Every breath became very soft, very quiet.
This Jiang girl had defied the Empress Dowager to the end.
No one had expected the Emperor to have made such a promise.
Some felt a pang of pity for this Third Miss Jiang — using a promise from the Emperor for a marriage bestowal seemed a terrible waste.
Gu Xuan felt a jolt in her heart. The Emperor had actually given out a promise that would work against himself? She felt suddenly a little anxious for this Miss Jiang — the promise was there, yes, but if it aligned with the Emperor's wishes, well and good; if it did not...
Xiao Huaiyan's jade ring was cracking. His head was splitting with pain. Those black phoenix eyes looked at the fragile young woman kneeling on the floor of the great hall, and he stretched out a smile. "Miss Jiang — is there no regret?"
Jiang Mi closed her eyes. No one could see the tears tracking down the corners of her face. "This subject's mind is made up. There is no regret."
Xiao Huaiyan was quiet for a moment. He gripped his hand into a fist until his knuckles showed. Fury turned itself into a cold, flat laugh. "Very well. I grant it."
At the same instant, the jade ring shattered. Fragments scattered and fell.
In that moment, Jiang Mi did not know whether it was truly what she heard, or her own imagination. She looked up — and only caught a glimpse of Xiao Huaiyan's retreating back.
She looked toward the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang in bewilderment. The Grand Princess looked back at her with a reassuring smile.
Her aunt was staring ahead like someone who had not yet come back to herself.
Jiang Mi's heart eased. It was done.
Xiao Huaiyan had agreed to the marriage bestowal. Her aunt had not been able to stop it.
She would not have to enter the palace.
Joy and unease swept over her together. Every muscle in her body had been rigid with tension; now that she released it, she felt as though all the strength had been drawn out of her.
Palace maids came forward to help her back to her seat.
The Marchioness of Cheng'en and Jiang Yi had not yet recovered. When Jiang Mi had stood up and asked the Emperor for a marriage bestowal, the entire Marquess of Cheng'en's party had been struck dumb.
The Marchioness of Cheng'en had words on the tip of her tongue and swallowed them back. How had she, as the elder aunt by marriage, not heard a single word about the second branch arranging a match with the Duke Zhen State's manor?
Jiang Yi saw that Jiang Mi's face had gone white as paper and pressed a cup of hot tea into her hands.
She whispered, "Tangtan, are you all right?"
Jiang Mi gave a small nod. She was drained of all energy, could not summon her strength. She needed a moment to recover.
Perhaps the emotional toll had been too great. She felt cold.
There was some commotion at the head of the hall. The Empress Dowager was seen to leave on Qingxue's arm, the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang announcing to the hall that the Empress Dowager had an old ailment that had flared up and she was going to rest in a nearby side hall. She bid everyone continue to enjoy themselves.
Some of the royal relatives who had gathered their wits offered congratulations to the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang: "Our felicitations to the Princess, for the fulfillment of a long-cherished wish."
The Virtuous Dowager Consort covered a smile behind her hand. "With a match blessed by the Emperor himself, the Grand Princess may look forward to holding great-grandchildren. Congratulations, Princess."
The Grand Princess smiled in return, but her mind was turning over the Emperor's manner just now, and she felt a thread of worry.
The Virtuous Dowager Consort poured herself another cup of wine. Since the confinement and the Xie family's downfall, she had not felt this lighthearted in a long time. Her niece could not serve the Emperor; neither, then, could the Empress Dowager's niece harbor any such hope.
She had barely drained the cup when she noticed Princess An'yang sitting in sullen displeasure, repeatedly shooting glares across the hall at Jiang Mi.
The Virtuous Dowager Consort frowned. What was this girl in a mood about now?
......
Jiang Mi could not settle her heart for a long while. She had a vague feeling she might be dreaming.
She looked at the empty seat at the head of the hall, knowing that her aunt must have left in a rage on her account.
Her aunt's fury and questioning were something she could not escape. She had known it from the beginning.
Jiang Mi told herself to be calm, and steeled herself to face her aunt's anger.
After the space of one cup of tea, a palace maid came to Jiang Mi's side and bowed. "Miss Jiang, the Empress Dowager asks you to come."
Jiang Mi recognized the palace maid — she had seen her at Cining Palace.
There was no avoiding this.
Jiang Mi pulled her peony-patterned cape around her and followed the maid out.
The maid led her to a side hall. She lowered her voice. "The Empress Dowager has had a recurrence of her old ailment. She has taken to rest in this nearby hall for now. Please go in, Miss."
Jiang Mi felt a deep pang of guilt. If it had not been absolutely necessary, she would not have wanted to give her aunt such a shock.
She stepped inside. The candles burned less brightly than usual. In the main room she saw no one.
Jiang Mi looked toward the inner chamber screened off behind the partition. Her aunt must be lying down in there.
