She walked in that direction, came around the screen, and looked toward the pear-wood bed. Her aunt was not there. But at the lattice window stood a figure.
Jiang Mi's pupils contracted sharply. Her feet moved back. She spun around and tried to run.
But the great hall doors had been pulled shut.
No matter how Jiang Mi pushed at those carved doors, they would not move.
She watched the figure from behind the screen slowly approach. Jiang Mi's heart hammered.
"Your — Your Majesty also came to see my aunt? Why is my aunt not here?" Jiang Mi's voice was unsteady.
Xiao Huaiyan looked at the small creature that had fled to a dead end, and said without hurry, "Miss Jiang is so perceptive — why not hazard a guess as to why it is I you find here, rather than the Empress Dowager?"
Jiang Mi had been holding onto a sliver of hope — telling herself that Xiao Huaiyan had merely come to visit her aunt, and perhaps her aunt had simply left before him.
She had been avoiding, all along, the thought she did not dare follow to its conclusion: whether Xiao Huaiyan, having agreed to the marriage bestowal, would be angry enough to act. She knew him well enough to know that in his pride, having his promise used against him to reach a purpose he had not intended — he would feel only contempt for her, not even willing to look at her again.
It seemed she had underestimated his fury.
Xiao Huaiyan gripped her chin and forced her face upward.
"Miss Jiang seems so frightened — is it that you have done something you know was wrong?"
Jiang Mi shook her head without a sound. Even though Xiao Huaiyan's face wore a smile as warm and mild as spring sunlight, it made her feel as though she had been dropped into a frozen wilderness.
Xiao Huaiyan's fingers gave a light pull, and the cape around Jiang Mi's shoulders fell to the floor.
Beneath it a pale-yellow Hangzhou silk jacket and skirt, a moon-white sash cinching her waist to its slenderest.
Xiao Huaiyan released the hold on her chin, let his fingers trail down along her long, graceful neck, and came to rest at the collar. He flicked idly at the pearl button there, and it came undone with ease.
Jiang Mi's bones and flesh went cold with terror. She saw in Xiao Huaiyan's eyes something that was desire.
She pressed down hard on his hand. "Your Majesty — you have already bestowed a marriage on me. You cannot — you cannot do this."
Xiao Huaiyan's head was throbbing in waves. Listening to her words, he felt himself growing more and more unmoored.
He could not?
He was the master of all under Heaven. What could he not do?
In the dream, she had worn sheer gauze and danced for him, turning and swaying to seduce him, her soft voice welcoming him. Even at the temporary palace she had been far more pliant than this.
Xiao Huaiyan gave a low laugh. "Jiang Mi — if I were to take you here, do you think the marriage I just bestowed upon you would still stand?"
Jiang Mi could not believe what she was hearing. The Xiao Huaiyan before her had abandoned all appearance of the gentleman's manner he usually kept up, and the worst of his nature was showing through.
She said in terror, "No — no — Your Majesty, your word is inviolable. You have already agreed. You — you cannot go back on it!"
Her only hope. The hope she had fought so hard to win — the hope of escaping the palace, escaping Xiao Huaiyan — she could not let it be crushed here.
Xiao Huaiyan watched the fear in her face, and the darkness in his eyes deepened. He swept her up off her feet.
Jiang Mi struggled and wept. "No — no! Your Majesty, I beg you — let me go! I said long ago that I have no aspirations, that I do not wish to enter the palace!"
Xiao Huaiyan paid no heed. Her weeping only sharpened the pain in his head.
He pressed down on her flailing body. "I gave you a chance."
Jiang Mi's heart fell into despair. If Xiao Huaiyan violated her here, her situation would be worse than her previous life. Only moments ago she had used the credit of saving the Emperor to ask for a marriage bestowal to the Young Lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor — and then she had gone and climbed into the Emperor's bed. No one would care whether it had been her will. They would only talk about how the young miss of the Marquess of Cheng'en's manor was faithless — betrothed to the Young Lord on one side, unwilling to let go of the Emperor on the other.
No matter how much the Young Lord wished to help her, he could not possibly marry a woman the Emperor had already touched. And entering the palace this way would only earn her contempt — she would be worse off than in her last life.
Jiang Mi was shaking all over. Just moments ago she had been rejoicing at the success of the marriage bestowal. Now she felt as though she had been thrown into hell.
She could not break free of Xiao Huaiyan's grip. Her embroidered shoe fell off in the struggle. Her hair came loose. The fastenings of her jacket were undone. Xiao Huaiyan set her on the bed and looked at her with that unsettling expression, lifting her chin and bending toward her.
Jiang Mi held her breath. She could not escape the kiss. Tears streamed down her face and her body shook. Even the arrow wound on her back was in pain.
The overwhelming scent of dragon saliva incense was nearly drowning her. She could not breathe. She tried to bite him — but he dodged her.
Xiao Huaiyan released the hand interlaced with hers, and hooked his fingers into the moon-white sash, about to pull it free — when a flash of cold light caught his eye, and on instinct he threw up his hand.
He did not know when she had gotten hold of a gold hairpin. But the pin was not aimed at Xiao Huaiyan. It was aimed at her own fragile throat.
Without reservation, with all the strength she had left, she turned it on herself.
Xiao Huaiyan blocked it in time. The hairpin drew a deep gash across his hand. Blood welled freely.
Something shifted in his composed eyes. He demanded in fury, "Why did you do that?"
The gold pin was wrenched away. Jiang Mi collapsed onto the bed. Grief broke through her. Why had she done that?
Because she did not want to walk the same path as her last life. Did not want to exhaust herself trying to win favor. Did not want to be demeaned. Did not want to spend her days in fear and unease. Did not want to end up, in the end, dying of despair and grief.
Her face was wet with tears. She looked at Xiao Huaiyan in his fury, her eyes hollow.
Why was he the one who was angry?
She was the one who should be angry.
Xiao Huaiyan — why have we ended up here again?
In the instant that Jiang Mi had reached for death, it was as though all fear left her.
She was so tired.
She was not clever enough. She could not outmaneuver so many schemes and schemes within schemes. She had been struggling all this time to change the fate of her last life, and had finally seen a glimmer of light — only for Xiao Huaiyan to crush it so easily.
What was the point of living like this? She was so tired.
Jiang Mi's eyes were heavy with grief. She opened her mouth. "Xiao Huaiyan — I do not like you. Not even a little. I never had any wish to enter the palace. If you force yourself on me — I would rather die!"
She said it, and closed her eyes.
Xiao Huaiyan's blood from the wound had fallen onto Jiang Mi's collar. He stood motionless, staring at her. He pressed his fingers to his own forehead. His head was being torn apart from the inside, his vision going blurry.
In his mind's eye he saw Jiang Mi lying there just like this, limp and still, eyes closed. The tide of fury that had been barely held in check receded entirely.
He shook his head hard and opened his eyes again.
His vision cleared. Jiang Mi lay on the bed with her eyes closed, but her lashes were still trembling faintly. He could hear her breathe.
Xiao Huaiyan looked at her with a complicated expression. He gave a short, self-mocking laugh. It was not as though she had come begging him for anything. There was no shortage of women in the world.
He, Xiao Huaiyan, was not yet reduced to forcing a fragile girl to her death.
Xiao Huaiyan's gaze slowly cleared. He pulled the quilt over Jiang Mi and left.
Jiang Mi's eyes had stayed shut the whole time, but she had been tracking his every move. She had expected her words to anger him further.
But instead she felt the quilt settle over her.
She heard his footsteps recede and leave, and then she slowly opened her eyes.
Trembling, she fastened her jacket closed, found her embroidered shoe and put it on, coaxed her hair back into order, and pulled her cape shut over the bloodstain on her collar.
She stood before the mirror, checked that nothing was amiss, and walked out.
......
Xiao Huaiyan emerged from the side hall and walked without direction. He had not expected to encounter someone on the path.
The person saw the imperial presence and came forward to pay respects.
Xue Jinglin cupped his hands. "This subject pays his respects to Your Majesty."
Xiao Huaiyan looked at him for a long moment before gesturing for him to rise.
Xiao Huaiyan asked, "What brings Young Lord Xue to the vicinity of Cining Palace?"
Xue Jinglin replied, "The banquet is nearly over, and this subject was thinking to accompany his grandmother when she departs."
Xiao Huaiyan said with a smile, "The Young Lord is indeed a dutiful grandson."
"This match that the Grand Princess has sought on the Young Lord's behalf — I will be frank with you: I find it somewhat beneath the Young Lord. What would the Young Lord say if I instead bestowed Princess An'yang upon him?"
Xue Jinglin was stunned. He had not expected the Emperor to say something like this.

chapters.title: Sixty one
chapters.content:
At some point in the black night sky, snow had begun to fall.
Xue Jinglin swept back his robe and knelt. "This subject is overwhelmed with gratitude. A princess of the imperial bloodline is too great an honor for me to presume upon. The match my grandmother has sought on my behalf is already the best I could hope for. I have long harbored feelings for the third young lady of the Jiang family, and Your Majesty has in your wisdom bestowed this good match between myself and Miss Jiang. I am sincerely grateful. I thank Your Majesty for your grace."
Xiao Huaiyan looked at Xue Jinglin. The wound on his hand throbbed with a dull, steady pain. One kneeling in the great hall, begging him to grant a wish. One kneeling here in the snow, thanking him for his grace.
How splendid. How very splendid.
That tide of fury — the feeling of having been fooled and manipulated — surged back. Xiao Huaiyan gave a smile that did not quite reach his eyes. "The Young Lord thanks me rather too soon."
Xue Jinglin's heart lurched.
Xiao Huaiyan said nothing more and walked past Xue Jinglin with a cold, set expression.
Xue Jinglin, his head still lowered, saw, in the instant Xiao Huaiyan passed, a drop of blood fall from his sleeve onto the ground.
Something passed through Xue Jinglin's eyes.
By the time he looked up, Xiao Huaiyan and his escort had already moved away. Xue Jinglin stood and examined the ground where he had passed. There were a few drops of blood, barely visible.
......
When Jiang Mi came out of the side hall, there were no palace attendants waiting outside.
The palace maid who had led her there was from Cining Palace but had clearly been acting on Xiao Huaiyan's arrangements.
It seemed her aunt had not sent anyone to look for her. She hesitated, then decided to return to the banquet hall first and avoid her aunt's questioning for now.
On the way back, she unexpectedly encountered Mama Cui.
Mama Cui had just seen the imperial physician out and was on her way back to the Cining Palace inner chambers, when she spotted Jiang Mi standing in the fine snow with her cape around her.
Mama Cui walked over. "Third Miss — have you come to see the Empress Dowager?"
Jiang Mi's expression dimmed. "Mama Cui, is my aunt feeling better?"
Mama Cui reached out and lifted the cape hood over Jiang Mi's head. "Third Miss, don't stand in the snow — you'll catch a chill. The Empress Dowager just took her medicine and has fallen asleep."
She sighed. "Miss, you really should not have done that. The Empress Dowager does not want to see anyone right now. You should go back with the Marchioness and the others."
Mama Cui turned to leave.
Jiang Mi could not help herself. "Mama — please take good care of my aunt. I... I will come to beg her forgiveness another day."
Mama Cui gave her a long, searching look, then dipped a bow. "Do not worry, Third Miss. This old servant will take her leave."
Jiang Mi walked back toward the banquet hall, her steps a little stiff, the sound of strings and music drifting toward her. She looked up at the falling snow, and held out her hand to catch a flake, watching it melt into her palm.
All of it should be over now.
She had spoken with such finality, leaving him no ground to stand on. Xiao Huaiyan was too proud and too conscious of his position to have done anything to her in that moment — so he had let her go, hadn't he?
In the instant of desperation when Xiao Huaiyan had cornered her, she had reached for the thought of death. But now that a glimmer of life had opened up again, she still wanted to live.
The seasons changing through the year — she wanted to see more of them.
If it were possible, she wanted to live a life different from her last one. Not trapped in the capital. Perhaps, one day, the chance to see the distant scenery she had only read about in books.
A night of falling snow. She hoped tomorrow would clear.
The banquet dispersed. Jiang Mi walked out with the Marchioness of Cheng'en and Jiang Yi. The ladies from various households passed by with varying expressions — those on closer terms came up to offer a word of congratulation.
The Marchioness of Cheng'en kept a smile on her face, though it was a somewhat rigid one. A marriage bestowal had been obtained — whether she liked it or not, she had to smile.
Just as they were about to board the carriage, they saw the Young Lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor escorting the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang in the same direction.
The Marchioness had no choice but to stop and pay her respects.
The Grand Princess of Zhaoyang was perfectly gracious with the Marchioness, exchanged a few words, then took her attendant's arm and made her way back to the Duke Zhen State's carriage.
Xue Jinglin looked toward Jiang Mi, who was wearing her hood, and said quietly with concern, "Miss Jiang, are you all right?"
Jiang Mi gave him a small nod.
Xue Jinglin smiled, apparently reassured. He gave a bow to Jiang Mi and the Marchioness, then returned to the Duke Zhen State's carriage.
The Marchioness exhaled with relief once he had gone. This place was busy with comings and goings — so many noble families and their households would be watching. The marriage bestowal had been granted, but the wedding had not yet taken place, and it was still not quite proper.
She hurried Jiang Mi and Jiang Yi into the carriage and back to the manor.
......
The Grand Princess of Zhaoyang watched Xue Jinglin lift the curtain and settle in beside her. She smiled. "Things have turned out as you wished, then?"
Xue Jinglin said with deep gratitude, "It was entirely owing to Grandmother's help that the bestowal went through."
The Grand Princess shook her head. She took a sip of hot tea and said slowly, "No. Not I. It was that young girl, Jiang Mi."
"I did not expect her to have such courage — defying the Empress Dowager's wishes outright, then using her act of saving the Emperor to press him into a bestowal he could not refuse. At first I was worried that such a pampered, sheltered girl might lose her nerve and back down in the middle of the banquet. But she held her ground. She is worthy of becoming the future Young Lady of this manor."
Xue Jinglin rarely heard his grandmother praise a young woman so warmly. He said, "Then Grandmother should set an early date, so we can bring her home properly."
The Grand Princess said, "The bestowal was given verbally. The imperial edict has not yet been issued. The wedding date must still be discussed with her father, and the full ceremonial rites — the three letters and six etiquettes — cannot be skipped. Even moving quickly, it will take at least half a year."
Xue Jinglin frowned. "Still half a year? The bestowal has already been granted — can it not be moved along faster?"
The Grand Princess teased, "In such a hurry, are you? The bestowal is done — if you try to shorten the wedding timeline on top of that, people will start whispering that the two of you had something going on beforehand, and that her belly couldn't wait, which is why you're rushing her through the door."
Xue Jinglin's expression turned distinctly awkward. He coughed behind his fist. "Then let us follow Grandmother's judgment. Only if it can be moved forward, please do."
The Grand Princess said with a laugh, "What is the hurry? Your future wife is not going anywhere."
Xue Jinglin said nothing, letting his grandmother tease him.
But somewhere inside, he felt uneasy.
......
In the latter half of the night, the snow fell heavier. Li Fu looked on anxiously as the Emperor sat on the stone bench beneath the plum tree in the Jinxiu Palace courtyard, drinking alone from the cup in his hand, letting the thick, soft snowflakes fall on him without restraint, not permitting any attendant to approach.
Li Fu was deeply worried. The Emperor had already finished three flasks of wine. He had drunk a good deal earlier with the court officials in the Jiaotai Hall. Continuing like this would do serious harm to his health.
Xiao Huaiyan shook the flask in his hand. Empty again.
He gave a short, contemptuous laugh and flung the flask to the ground. It shattered into pieces with a sharp, clear ring.
"Li Fu, bring wine."
Li Fu, now that he had been called, dared to come forward. He tried to reason with him. "Your Majesty, please drink no more. The snow on your person has melted — the cold water has soaked through your robes and the cold will enter your body. It will harm you, Your Majesty. Please return to the Qianqing Palace."
Xiao Huaiyan gave Li Fu a cool sideways glance. Li Fu startled. He had no choice but to have the waiting attendants bring wine, which he took and carried forward himself.
As Xiao Huaiyan reached out to take it, the sleeve that had been soaked through with blood came into view. Li Fu went white. "Your Majesty — what has happened to your hand?"

chapters.title: Sixty two
chapters.content:
Xiao Huaiyan took the wine in hand and told Li Fu to get out of his sight.
Li Fu wanted to press further, but he knew the Emperor would not listen. He had personally bestowed the marriage to another man — he was in no mood to be consoled.
Those of them who attended closely knew the Emperor's feelings toward Miss Jiang better than anyone. It was just that no one had expected Miss Jiang to be bold enough to maneuver the Emperor into granting the bestowal.
The dark fire in the Emperor, if he did not let it out, would surely become a festering problem...
Li Fu shook his head as he walked, sighing. He needed to go and fetch Chief Physician Gu.
And whatever that wound was, judging from the size of the bloodstain, it was not shallow.
In the night sky, snow fell without cease. The wind shook loose the plum blossoms — crimson petals and pure white snow twisting together.
The warm wine had long since gone cold. A mouthful of sharp spirits burned down Xiao Huaiyan's throat, and his head ached more fiercely than ever — yet the pain brought him a strange, cutting clarity.
Every time he closed his eyes, it was as though he could hear Mama Kong's voice again, urgent and frightened.
"Prince, you must not cry. Must not complain. Must not hate. You must smile. Your mother is gone. The Emperor's favor has been lost. Nurse does not know how long she can stay by your side. You cannot let those people see your hatred. You must endure, or you will not survive in this palace. My little prince — you must be harmless, gentle, accommodating, so that your enemies will give you room to breathe."
"You cannot have things you love. Even if you do, you must hide them — never let anyone know. Otherwise they will be destroyed, just like that little dog, gone without anyone noticing. And more people will use what you love to hurt you. Little prince, do you understand?"
The day after Mama Kong said these things to him, her body was found in the well.
His mother's Jinxiu Palace was sealed up after that, and he was sent to live in Consort Xian's quarters.
All these years had passed. He had returned to this palace and become its master. The Jinxiu Palace had been reopened.
That plum tree still bloomed as blazing and fierce as it had in his mother's time. But everything else had long since changed beyond recognition.
Xiao Huaiyan gave a low laugh. On his handsome face, something cold and violent had surfaced.
His forbearance had been repaid with deception and manipulation.
Xiao Huaiyan's cold laugh faded. He pressed his fingers against his aching temple. His ears were ringing.
"Long live Your Majesty."
"Thank you for Your Majesty's gift."
"Your Majesty, won't you try one?"
"Your Majesty, do you like it?"
Soft, sweet voices drifting in like wind, settling into his ears.
And then those cheerful sounds shifted.
"I beg Your Majesty to grant this."
"No — no, you cannot do this!"
"Xiao Huaiyan — I do not like you. Not even a little. I never had any wish to enter the palace."
The weeping and the pleading — like an icy wind, cutting into him.
Xiao Huaiyan hurled the wine flask to the ground. The crack of it scattering drove the voices away.
He opened his eyes abruptly. From inside his robe he drew out a gold hairpin, its tip stained with blood.
Xiao Huaiyan gripped the hairpin and stood. The motion sent a few fallen plum petals tumbling from his clothes to the ground.
Li Fu and the others, who had been waiting at a respectful distance, saw the Emperor rise and quickly had the imperial sedan brought forward.
Xiao Huaiyan rode back to the Qianqing Palace in silence. Chief Physician Gu came forward at once to bind the wound.
No one dared say a word.
Li Fu stared at the wound — deep, clearly made by something sharp — and could not fathom how any assassin could have gotten close enough to wound the Emperor. Normally an injury to the imperial person would require the Embroidered Guard to investigate thoroughly — but the Emperor had not mentioned it at all. His expression made it clear that no one was to ask.
Li Fu brought a warm draft to ward off the cold. "Your Majesty, you were out in the snow and wind so long. Please drink this to drive out the chill."
Xiao Huaiyan said flatly, "Leave it."
Li Fu set it down and withdrew.
Not long after Xiao Huaiyan fell asleep, his forehead began to burn. His head was splitting with pain, and the wine's effects rose through him. He drifted into a half-waking, half-dreaming state — and then slipped entirely into a dream.
The mist parted. Xiao Huaiyan found himself standing in a courtyard simply and quietly furnished — evidently the quarters of a consort. There were no palace servants anywhere. He stood outside the door and heard soft weeping from within.
The crying held a note of fear and grievance. "Qiuyu, I think I have angered the Emperor again."
"My lady, did you not finish embroidering an incense sachet for His Majesty not long ago? Why not give it to him the next time he visits, and say a few gentle words — perhaps he will not be angry anymore?"
Something contemptuous flickered through Xiao Huaiyan's eyes. A mere embroidered sachet, and she thought that would appease him?
"My lady, do not think too much on it. You are still unwell — please take care of yourself. The physician said to drink the medicine while it is hot."
Xiao Huaiyan frowned. Unwell again?
Through the half-open rosewood-carved door, he could see Jiang Mi dressed in a court consort's robe of deep water-blue, lying on her side on the chaise, a thin blanket over her, a look of quiet sorrow between her brows and a sickly pallor on her face.
She forced the medicine down and placed a preserved plum in her mouth to recover herself, then said in a miserable voice, "Qiuyu, I am so tired."
Xiao Huaiyan fixed his gaze on her pale lips. He heard her murmur something else: "Perhaps I should not have listened to my aunt's urging and grasped for something I was never meant to have."
As she said it, Jiang Mi covered her mouth with a cloth and coughed — short, sharp, urgent coughs.
Xiao Huaiyan tried to go in, but found he could not push the door open no matter how he tried. His legs felt nailed to the ground. He could only watch helplessly as she lowered the cloth from her mouth, and saw on it a dark stain of blood.
The mist around him began to thicken again. Xiao Huaiyan saw only a white expanse, and then everything was gone.
......
In the Qianqing Palace, the hour when the Emperor normally rose had come — but Li Fu heard no movement from within.
Worried, he lifted the bed curtain to check — and his heart nearly stopped. The Emperor's forehead was burning. He had a fierce fever.
Li Fu immediately sent someone to summon Chief Physician Gu.
"Your Majesty — Your Majesty, can you hear me?" Li Fu called into his ear.
The Emperor did not look unconscious — he looked more like a man trapped inside a nightmare, hovering between sleep and waking.
Then, suddenly, Xiao Huaiyan opened his eyes. Li Fu nearly jumped.
The Emperor's eyes were completely bloodshot — a frightening sight.
Xiao Huaiyan propped himself up on one hand, his voice rough. "What is the hour?"
Li Fu said, "The end of the third watch."
Xiao Huaiyan moved to throw off the covers and get up, but Li Fu stopped him. "Your Majesty, you have a fever. You are still unwell. Chief Physician Gu is on his way."
Xiao Huaiyan ignored Li Fu's protest and called for his attendants to come help him dress in court robes. "It is the first of the month. State affairs cannot be neglected."
Li Fu could do nothing. Once the Emperor had made up his mind, there was no reasoning with him.
He watched with deep worry. The first of the month meant ancestral rites and receiving the court's obeisance — it would be exhausting. He did not know whether the Emperor could hold up.
......
When Jiang Mi returned to the Marquess of Cheng'en's manor, her father and her eldest uncle had a fierce quarrel. Her eldest uncle had wanted to make her kneel at the ancestral hall as punishment, but her father intervened.
Her eldest aunt-by-marriage caught her eye and gestured discreetly for her to leave.
Jiang Mi knew that remaining would only stoke her uncle's anger further.
As she withdrew from the main hall, she felt a chill of dread. Her uncle's reaction told her that the Jiang family's connection to Xiao Huaiyan's mother was almost certainly not a clean one.
Why did her uncle and her aunt both believe that becoming Xiao Huaiyan's woman would prevent that disaster from befalling them? What had truly happened?
In the tenth year of Emperor Yongshun's reign, the late Emperor had made a southern tour. Her eldest uncle had been among the accompanying retinue. Her aunt had not gone.
Could she still allow herself the luxury of wishful thinking?
After Jiang Mi left, Jiang Qingde's face was a livid grey. He pointed at Jiang Qingxuan and shouted, "You went behind my back and arranged a match between Jiang Mi and the Duke Zhen State's manor! You are bent on destroying the Jiang family!"
Jiang Qingxuan said gravely, "Elder Brother — whether the Jiang family is destroyed is not something a single young woman can determine one way or the other. I don't know how deep the Jiang family's involvement was in the events of the tenth year of Yongshun's reign — you and Elder Sister have always been vague and evasive about it. But whatever comes of it, it will not be Tangtan who brings the Jiang family down. I agreed with your decision at the beginning, to send Tangtan into the palace. But look — Tangtan has been placed in mortal danger again and again. If we persist in this course, she may not live to protect the Jiang family at all."
Jiang Qingde was shaking with fury, too angry to form words. He hurled a vase to the floor. The damage was already done — the marriage bestowal was given by the Emperor's own mouth, and by now it had spread throughout the court and the capital.
Jiang Qingxuan sighed. "Elder Brother — what is destined cannot be avoided. Aiyo..."
He shook his head, clasped his hands behind his back, and left.
......
Back in Yuanzhi Courtyard, Jiang Mi finally took off her cape.
When Qiuyu helped her change her clothes, she saw the bloodstain on the collar. Her face drained of color. "Miss, you —"
Jiang Mi signaled her to be quiet.
"Do not alarm anyone. Take this dress and burn it." Jiang Mi gave the instruction quietly.
Qiuyu nodded. As she unpinned Jiang Mi's hair, she noticed one of the gold hairpins was missing.
She asked no questions. She gathered up the blood-stained dress and left the room.
Jiang Mi lay in bed for a long time, unable to sleep.
......
On the first day of the new year, titled ladies of the fourth rank and above entered the palace to pay their respects to the Empress Dowager.
The Marchioness of Cheng'en returned home exhausted, and immediately had to see to the household arrangements.
She called all the young members of the family together for the New Year's meal.
Although the two branches of the family had quarreled last night, the first day of the new year still required them to sit down together.
When the Marchioness had finished her preparations and came out, she found the Jiang sisters already gathered in the main hall.
The sisters all offered their New Year's greetings and curtsied in turn, offering their best wishes.
The Marchioness smiled and gave each of them a small silk fortune bag filled with gold melon seeds.
Jiang Yi weighed the gold seeds in her hand and said cheerfully, "Mother, you came back quite early today."
The Marchioness said, "The Empress Dowager was feeling unwell and dismissed the titled ladies after only a brief audience." She had not stayed to speak with her privately either. Then there had been something that happened at the Qianqing Palace — the Marchioness glanced at Jiang Mi and continued, "Something also occurred in the palace today. Many of the usual formalities have been suspended. Word is that the Emperor has fallen ill. He nearly faltered during the ancestral rites — Chief Physician Gu was at his side the entire time."
As the Marchioness said this, she and Jiang Wan, Jiang Yi, and Jiang Rong all stole glances at Jiang Mi. Those who had not attended the banquet had all heard about the marriage bestowal by now. Both Jiang Yi and Jiang Wan were puzzled — Tangtan had clearly been devoted to the Emperor, even shielding him from arrows, and yet after returning from the temporary palace she had arranged a match with the Young Lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor. Would she show any concern now that the Emperor was ill?
But Jiang Mi showed no reaction at all. She said with considerate concern, "Eldest Aunt, you have had a tiring journey. Is my aunt's health improving?"
The Marchioness smiled. "The Empress Dowager should still need some days of quiet rest."
She could see that Tangtan looked a little guilty, but she did not ask a single word about the Emperor.
It seemed Tangtan had truly made up her mind to cut all ties with the palace.

chapters.title: Sixty three
chapters.content:
After the New Year's meal in the main hall, Jiang Mi walked back to Yuanzhi Courtyard with Jiang Rong.
On the way, a faint drifting fragrance reached her. Jiang Rong tugged at her sleeve. "Elder Sister, the plum blossoms in the garden have opened. Shall we pick a few branches to take back?"
Jiang Mi looked at the plum boughs heavy with snow and was quiet for a moment, then gave a slow nod. "Why not."
When Jiang Mi and Jiang Rong stepped into Yuanzhi Courtyard with their plum branches, Qiuyu came to meet them. "Miss, Chief Physician Gu has come with Attendant Ye. He says he would like to take your pulse."
Jiang Mi found this puzzling. Had her eldest aunt-by-marriage not said that the Chief Physician was in attendance beside Xiao Huaiyan? How had he left the palace so quickly?
Besides, since her return from the temporary palace, once Chief Physician Gu and Attendant Ye had seen that she was nearly recovered, they had stopped coming to examine her.
Jiang Mi went inside. Chief Physician Gu rose to his feet. "Miss Jiang, best wishes for the new year."
Jiang Mi curtseyed. "Chief Physician Gu, what brings you here?"
Chief Physician Gu said with a smile, "It has been a while since I last took your pulse. As it happens, the Empress Dowager mentioned it and so I came."
Jiang Mi relaxed and held out her wrist.
Qiuyu laid a cloth. Chief Physician Gu placed his fingers, and after a moment he said, "Miss has been looking after herself well these past days. Most notably, the melancholy that was weighing on you seems to have dissipated a little."
Jiang Mi had not expected to hear that.
Chief Physician Gu said, "It is a good thing. Do not dwell too much on things, Miss, and do not keep everything shut up inside. Sometimes saying it aloud can bring a great deal of relief."
Jiang Mi thanked him sincerely.
Chief Physician Gu had heard about what happened at the New Year's banquet. He had assumed this young lady would become a consort — after all, during the Emperor's time at the temporary palace, the Emperor's regard for her had not been hidden from those close to him. He had not expected her to press the Emperor for a marriage bestowal to another man. Truly surprising.
But...
Chief Physician Gu shook his head inwardly. He wrote out a new prescription. "Miss may switch to this formula to continue her recovery." He also took two small porcelain bottles from his medicine case. "You may apply this to the wound on your back. It will prevent scarring."
After Jiang Mi thanked him again and saw him to the door, Chief Physician Gu left the manor without delay and hurried straight back to the palace.
......
In the imperial study, occasional coughs broke the quiet. Li Fu brought in the warmed medicine and found the Emperor was standing at the window, letting the bitter cold wind blow in.
Li Fu quickly set down the medicine and went to shut the window. "Your Majesty, you are still unwell. Standing in a draft will only worsen it."
Xiao Huaiyan's complexion was slightly pale, but otherwise he looked much as he always did.
He looked at Li Fu without expression. "Has Chief Physician Gu returned?"
Li Fu said, "By my reckoning he should be back before long. Your Majesty, please drink the medicine while it is hot."
Xiao Huaiyan glanced at the steaming bowl. "Leave it."
Li Fu thought: always 'leave it' — and then it goes cold and cannot be drunk. Persuading the Emperor to take medicine was its own particular ordeal.
Fortunately, Chengzhong came in before long to announce that the Chief Physician was requesting an audience.
Xiao Huaiyan turned around. "Send him in."
Chief Physician Gu entered the hall, bowed low, and said, "This subject pays his respects to Your Majesty."
Xiao Huaiyan sat and said, "Dispense with the formalities. How was it?"
Chief Physician Gu said, "Your Majesty, the third young lady of the Jiang family has no latent illness. Her constitution is weak, but with proper nourishment it can be restored."
Xiao Huaiyan frowned slightly. No latent illness? Then what was the coughing of blood in the dream?
He tapped his fingers on the desk and asked, "I recall that in the pulse reports you sent from the temporary palace, you noted excessive melancholy and brooding. What is that about?"
As he said those four characters, something like deep revulsion moved through him.
Chief Physician Gu hesitated. "Miss Jiang does tend to brood and carry a great deal of worry. Perhaps she dwells too much on her anxieties. Especially unease and fearfulness — those things compound the brooding. If they accumulate over time, the result can be illness born of suppressed emotion. For a woman, it is the kind of thing that shortens one's life."
"Shortens one's life?" Xiao Huaiyan's eyes turned cold and still.
Chief Physician Gu nodded. "It builds gradually — layer upon layer, until it finally crushes the body. But Your Majesty need not worry. When I took Miss Jiang's pulse today, the melancholy in her had eased somewhat. I believe that going forward, if she is given encouragement and her spirits remain light and content, that suppressed feeling will gradually disperse entirely."
Yet even after Chief Physician Gu said this, Xiao Huaiyan's expression did not improve.
Xiao Huaiyan raised his hand to dismiss the physician.
Li Fu, seeing the Emperor's dark mood, did not dare speak. He quietly picked up the bowl of medicine, now gone cold, and went to prepare a fresh one.
Xiao Huaiyan rested his head in his hand and closed his eyes. The images from the dream drifted through his mind — her timid compliance when she was in favor, her eager attempts to please him, the fear and the silent misery in her eyes when they were together.
She had been afraid of him all along.
A vein showed at Xiao Huaiyan's temple. Those tear-filled eyes moved across his vision, and his head throbbed in sharp, pulsing waves.
......
On the fourth day of the first month, the Duke Zhen State's carriage stopped in front of the Marquess of Cheng'en's manor.
The Young Lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor and Miss Xue arrived with New Year gifts to pay their call.
Everyone in the capital now knew that a match was being arranged between the two households. New Year visits between them were entirely natural.
Xue Ningzhu was taken to Jiang Mi's courtyard, while Xue Jinglin was shown to the front courtyard to see Jiang Qingxuan.
Xue Ningzhu linked her arm warmly through Jiang Mi's. "Sister Jiang, before long you will be one of our household! Then I can come and find you every day to play. This is so wonderful. Sister Jiang, I hope you come over soon!"
Ningzhu's bluntness made Jiang Mi's cheeks warm. Now that the most urgent danger had been resolved, she felt at a loss as to how to face the marriage ahead.
Between herself and the Young Lord — she could not begin to express her gratitude to him. The thought of going to the Duke Zhen State's manor and becoming his wife left her uncertain about how to go about it.
She did not know whether she could be a good wife to him.
Jiang Rong, sitting nearby, watched Xue Ningzhu fuss over her elder sister with a pang in her heart. She did not want her sister to marry away so soon. She wanted to keep her close a little longer.
Jiang Mi set a piece of plum blossom cake in Jiang Rong's hand and, seeing the troubled look on her face, asked quietly, "What is the matter, A'Rong?"
Jiang Rong shook her head. "Nothing, Elder Sister. I was just thinking about the Lantern Festival on the fifteenth. I wonder if Mother will allow us to go out and see the lanterns."
Xue Ningzhu tilted her head. "Lanterns? I want to see them too — it must be very lively. Why not have my brother take us all together?"
Jiang Rong looked hopefully toward Jiang Mi.
Jiang Mi was somewhat tempted herself. She had never been to a lantern festival. When her grandmother was alive she had not been permitted to go. After her grandmother's death, she had been so distant from her father and his wife that she had never thought to ask.
"I will speak to the lady of the house and my father in a couple of days. If they agree, we can have Eldest Brother Jiang Hong take us sisters out to see the lights." She felt it would be far more awkward to have the Young Lord escort them.
Xue Ningzhu said brightly, "Then my brother and I will come along too! Sister Jiang, what do you say?"
Jiang Mi hesitated, then nodded. "If the Young Lord is willing."
Xue Ningzhu declared without hesitation, "He will definitely be willing."
Jiang Mi gave a helpless smile. She rose and found a sandalwood box, opened it, and took out two handkerchiefs — one for Xue Ningzhu, one for Jiang Rong. "Here. Do you like them?"
Both Ningzhu and Jiang Rong exclaimed in delight. The peony embroidery, the fine, careful needlework — clearly done by Jiang Mi herself.
They both said at once, "We love them!"
When it was time for Xue Ningzhu to leave, Jiang Mi walked her out to the courtyard gate.
Xue Ningzhu ran to Xue Jinglin, who had come to collect her, and held up the handkerchief like a little prize. "Young Lord Brother, look — Sister Jiang embroidered this for me! Isn't it beautiful?"
Xue Jinglin took it and looked at the lifelike peonies, then nodded. "Beautiful."
He looked toward Jiang Mi. "Miss Jiang, I hope Ningzhu has not given you too much trouble."
Jiang Mi said, "You are too polite, Young Lord."
Xue Ningzhu glanced back and forth between Xue Jinglin and Jiang Mi, then said mischievously, "Sister Jiang, you embroidered a handkerchief for me — does my brother not get one? Why not embroider a sachet for Young Lord Brother?"
Xue Jinglin put on a stern expression. "Enough of that nonsense."
Xue Ningzhu was entirely unmoved and looked at Jiang Mi with a grin.
Jiang Mi was so embarrassed she did not know where to look. She felt she owed the Young Lord far too much already. She lowered her head. "It — it is no trouble. Does the Young Lord have a preferred pattern?"
Xue Jinglin looked at her — a lovely girl, soft-voiced and gentle — and paused. "Bamboo. If it is not too much bother."
Jiang Mi shook her head, and the pearl drops at her ears swayed with the motion — gentle and lively.
Xue Jinglin helped Xue Ningzhu into the carriage, and Ningzhu said, "Young Lord Brother, Sister Jiang and I agreed to go see the lanterns on the fifteenth. You have to come with us."
Xue Jinglin was half-exasperated, half-amused. "Just who would be taking whom? If you keep making a nuisance of yourself, you will not be allowed out on the fifteenth."
His tone was a little firm.
Xue Ningzhu protested and pestered him regardless, insisting she was going no matter what.
Xue Jinglin drank a cup of tea, lifted the carriage curtain and glanced outside, then said, "If you behave yourself, it is not entirely out of the question."
Xue Ningzhu was thrilled.
......
On the fifteenth of the first month, the Lantern Festival was in full, magnificent swing.
Before dark had even fallen, the streets were already packed with people. Shop fronts and street vendors everywhere were selling all manner of lanterns.
Street performers and traveling acrobats drew crowds who cheered and clapped along.
Children ran in laughing packs, chasing one another through the throngs.
As night settled over the city, Xiao Huaiyan stood at the window looking down at Xuanwu Street, watching as the lanterns were hung one by one along its length, and the ten-mile avenue blazed into brilliant light.
Pei Chi stood at attention beside him. "Your Majesty, the deployment is in place. We need only wait for the rebels to walk into the trap."
Lord Xue Jingyuan had sent word from Jiangnan: he had learned that rebels hiding in the capital planned to use the chaos of the fifteenth's lantern festival to smuggle an important item out of the city.
Xiao Huaiyan said nothing, watching the noise and life below in silence.
Then his steady, placid gaze shifted. In the crowd he caught sight of a familiar figure.
Even beneath the veiled hat, he recognized her at once. And Jiang Hong walking beside her confirmed it.
She had come out tonight to see the lanterns too.
Xiao Huaiyan said, "Pei Chi — when apprehending the rebels, keep the perimeter as contained as possible. Minimize harm to bystanders."
Pei Chi blinked. "Yes, Your Majesty."
Then Xiao Huaiyan's expression changed. The Jiang party had stopped somewhere, as though waiting for someone.
On the other side of the crowd, two figures were pushing through toward them.
When Xiao Huaiyan made out who they were, his expression darkened.

chapters.title: Sixty four
chapters.content:
In the clamor and noise of the festive street, amidst the surging crowd, Jiang Mi listened to her sisters' teasing around her and, a little shy, drew the sachet from her sleeve. She said quietly, "Young Lord Xue — I do not know if you will like it."
Xue Ningzhu eagerly cut in ahead of him: "He likes it, he likes it — how could my brother not like it?"
Xue Jinglin gave Xue Ningzhu's reaching hand a resigned swat and took the sachet from Jiang Mi himself. He smiled warmly. "Miss Jiang's artistry and skill are remarkable. I like the sachet very much. Thank you."
He then hung it at his waist.
Jiang Mi glanced down for a moment. The deep blue of the sachet suited Xue Jinglin's crow-black outer robe rather well.
Tonight the Jiang sisters had been brought out by their eldest cousin Jiang Hong to see the lanterns, and even Jiang Wan had come along.
The betrothal between Jiang Mi and the Young Lord was now public knowledge — exchanging gifts was perfectly appropriate.
The Young Lord's easy, unaffected acceptance of the sachet, and Tangtan's shy, girlish manner in offering it — from a distance they looked a matched and harmonious pair.
Xue Jinglin suggested they first head to the private room at Wangxian House up ahead. A flower-cart procession would come through the street before long, drawing large crowds, and once the floats had passed and the people dispersed, they could enjoy the lanterns properly.
Jiang Hong's face lit up. "Young Lord Xue, you reserved a room at Wangxian House in advance? That must have been extraordinarily difficult — the view from there is the best in the whole city for the float procession!"
Jiang Yi asked curiously, "What is a float procession?"
Jiang Hong started to explain, then looked around at his younger sisters and thought better of it. He said vaguely, "You will see when it comes."
Jiang Wan, who had a broader knowledge of the world than the unmarried girls, understood immediately when float processions were mentioned. It was nothing more complicated than the most famous pleasure houses in the capital putting their celebrated entertainers on decorated floats to perform in the streets, drawing admirers and attention.
Seeing that Jiang Yi was about to ask again, Jiang Wan said, "All right, Yi-jie, keep your eye on the road and don't wander off. If you get snatched by kidnappers, you will regret it."
Jiang Yi was duly frightened. Jiang Rong startled and pressed even closer to Jiang Mi.
Jiang Wan, satisfied that the two youngest were behaving, turned to Xue Jinglin. "Please lead the way, Young Lord."
Xue Jinglin nodded and moved to walk on Jiang Mi's right, creating a little distance between her and the jostling crowd.
Jiang Mi turned to glance at him, and in that instant felt, without knowing why, as though she were being watched from behind. She looked back — only the unending blaze of lanterns along the street and the constant flow of festival-goers.
Xue Jinglin asked, "Miss Jiang, is something wrong?"
Jiang Mi shook her head. Perhaps she was imagining things.
Along the way to Wangxian House, acrobats and street performers drew gasps and exclamations from the crowd. A barrel-chested man breathing fire and leaping through burning rings made the younger girls stop dead in their tracks.
Jiang Wan managed to get them moving again, though only with reluctance.
Then a commotion erupted from somewhere ahead. Voices rose: "Copper coins! Copper coins being scattered!"
Someone was throwing handfuls of coins into the crowd. People who saw them swarmed forward to snatch them, pushing and shoving, shouting over one another...
Xue Jinglin's brow furrowed at the chaos ahead. He, Jiang Hong, and their attendants formed a protective ring around the young ladies and moved against the flow, toward a more open space.
Then another wave of sound came rushing toward them from farther down the street: "The float procession! It's Tianxiang House's Miss Piaopiao!"
Another surge of people poured in from that direction.
The crowd multiplied, the situation became more and more unruly. Xue Jinglin seized Xue Ningzhu's arm and reached out for Jiang Mi — but grabbed only air.
In the blink of an eye, the space where Jiang Mi had been standing was filled by strangers pushing in from the side. Jiang Hong's group was similarly in danger of being scattered.
......
Jiang Mi was wrapped in a cloak from behind — half-lifted, half-carried — and drawn away from the crowd.
She was flooded with panic and struggled with everything she had. The roar of the cheering crowd swallowed her faint cry for help entirely.
The thought of what Jiang Wan had said about kidnappers — snatching young women away to places no one could find them — sent terror coursing through her.
The noise of the crowd faded as she was carried further away. Jiang Mi's heart sank into despair.
A door opened, and the world outside was shut away.
The iron grip around her suddenly released. Jiang Mi stumbled away from the figure, and her veiled hat fell to the ground.
She looked up and saw the person's face clearly — and stared, frozen.
Xiao Huaiyan looked at the teeth marks on his hand, then turned his cold, narrow phoenix eyes on Jiang Mi.
Her face showed wariness and fear. He took a step toward her; she took a step back.
This was the first time they had met since the marriage bestowal.
She had been at the very peak of her terror of him during the incident in the side hall. Seeing him again now, Jiang Mi was still afraid — but, she found, not quite as afraid as she had imagined she would be. At the very least, Xiao Huaiyan was not going to sell her to someone.
Jiang Mi looked at Xiao Huaiyan standing silent, his expression closed and cold. Her heart was unsettled.
She stole a glance at the door not far away. She did not know where this boldness came from — but she bolted toward it. This was not the palace. If she could get that door open, she could leave.
Xiao Huaiyan's brow lowered. In a few strides he caught her and pressed her against the door. "Has Our leave been given? Do you think you can walk out of here?"
He saw her head bow and her shoulders draw in. He suppressed his anger and softened his tone somewhat. "Jiang Mi — do you not know your place anymore?"
Xiao Huaiyan gripped her chin and forced her face up.
Those apricot eyes were hazy with apprehension. "Your — Your Majesty dragged me here without cause," she said, voice trembling badly, the words stumbling out in a shaky counterargument. "Is — is that conduct befitting a gentleman?"
Xiao Huaiyan blinked, mildly surprised. She was answering back?
He said, "You can barely get the words out — and you still want to be sharp-tongued?"
The injustice of it made Jiang Mi's chest ache with indignation. She wanted nothing more than to forget all decorum and scratch his face. But she knew that with a single move of his hand, Xiao Huaiyan could render her completely unable to act.
Jiang Mi gulped a few mouthfuls of cold air and, still shaken from the fright, began to cough. She coughed until her eyes watered.
Between coughs she managed, "Your Majesty, I beg you to let me go. My family is looking for me."
She looked, to any observer, utterly pitiable.
Xiao Huaiyan's eyes dimmed slightly. Just a short while ago she had been laughing and smiling at someone else, and now facing him she did nothing but plead and weep.
The image from the dream — Jiang Mi holding a cloth to her mouth as she coughed blood — rose in his mind. Xiao Huaiyan's grip slowly loosened.
Jiang Mi pressed a hand to her chest and caught her breath, and the coughing gradually settled. She heard a voice beside her say, "I will escort you back."
Jiang Mi was not sure she had heard correctly. She stared at him.
Xiao Huaiyan said, "What — reluctant to leave all of a sudden?"
Jiang Mi shook her head quickly. "Thank — thank you, Your Majesty." The moment she said it, she felt annoyed with herself. He was the one who had taken her away in the first place. What was she thanking him for?
But she was more frightened that Xiao Huaiyan might change his mind.
Jiang Mi watched as Xiao Huaiyan opened the door. She followed close behind him, step by careful step.
Suddenly he stopped.
She stopped too.
She saw Xiao Huaiyan turn back, pick up the fallen veiled hat, brush the dust from it, and as he passed her, set it carefully on her head.
Jiang Mi silently adjusted the hat and fell into step behind him.
They walked through a quiet alleyway, and slowly the sounds of the crowd drew close again — crashing toward them like a wave.
Xiao Huaiyan glanced back at Jiang Mi and then reached out and took hold of her wrist.
She pulled against him twice. Xiao Huaiyan said, "If you get lost again, you may not be as fortunate as you were this time."
Jiang Mi knew the streets were chaotic and she had no idea what they might encounter next. She had no choice but to stay close to him for now.
Coming out of the alley, they stepped back onto Xuanwu Street.
But the noise here was not that of lantern-watchers. It was the noise of people running in terror.
Jiang Mi saw Embroidered Guards pursuing what appeared to be jianghu men. Some black-clad figures were swinging blades at innocent bystanders, obstructing the Guards' path. On another side, imperial soldiers surged forward to encircle them.
As people fled in all directions, they dropped their lanterns without thinking, or knocked over the vendors' stalls. The burning lanterns set fire to several spots at once.
Xiao Huaiyan watched all of this with an unruffled expression. He asked the person beside him, "Are you frightened?"
Jiang Mi was naturally frightened. The scene brought back the memory of stumbling into the hunting grounds at Fengchi — that hellish, blood-soaked place.
Were these assassins, come to kill him again?
Without warning a gust of wind struck, and Jiang Mi felt the world tilt. Xiao Huaiyan pulled her into his arms, and a burning lamp stand crashed heavily into his back.
Jiang Mi heard Xiao Huaiyan let out a muffled sound. The flame caught his outer robe at once, licking at his back.
Xiao Huaiyan ripped the burning robe free and threw it aside, pulling Jiang Mi away with him.
They had not gone far when Jiang Mi felt the weight leaning against her growing heavier and heavier. She struggled to support him, and asked with alarm, "Your — Your Majesty, are you all right?"

chapters.title: Sixty five
chapters.content:
Warm breath brushed against Jiang Mi's neck. She felt a tingling numbness spreading through her.
Jiang Mi tried to push Xiao Huaiyan back a little, but found she could not move him at all. The heat of his breath grew more and more scorching, and her whole side was going soft.
She stopped walking and said between labored breaths, "Your Majesty, where are your shadow guards? This subject truly has no more strength."
Xiao Huaiyan leaned against the soft figure supporting him and looked down at her, his voice low. "I did not have them follow me."
Jiang Mi could barely believe it. Xiao Huaiyan had left the palace without his shadow guards?
"Then — then should I go and call for the Embroidered Guard?"
Xiao Huaiyan said, "Miss Jiang, I would advise you not to act rashly. If you go to call the Guard right now, the rebels still hiding in the crowd will have a new target. You will be drawn into it yourself."
Xiao Huaiyan composed himself and offered, "If Miss Jiang would rather not support me any longer, you may leave me here where I am. When the Embroidered Guard and the soldiers have dealt with the rebels, they will come and find me."
A struggle passed through Jiang Mi's eyes. If she could, she would not have wanted any further entanglement with Xiao Huaiyan.
But he had just shielded her from that lamp stand. If it had come down on her instead, she might have kept her life, but the fire would have ruined her face.
The undeniable fact was that Xiao Huaiyan had been hurt rescuing her.
And if she left him here and he came to harm from the rebels — from rebels trying to kill him — could the realm afford that upheaval? Beyond that, knowing Xiao Huaiyan's nature, if she truly abandoned him now, he would file it away and one day hold it against the Jiang family.
If she could choose, Jiang Mi did not want to be on bad terms with him.
She pressed her lips together. "I believe there is a medicine hall ahead. May this subject take Your Majesty there?"
Xiao Huaiyan made a sound of assent, low and close, as though right at Jiang Mi's ear.
Jiang Mi did her best to ignore the discomfort and walked with him toward the medicine hall.
Xiao Huaiyan studied Jiang Mi from the corner of his eye. She was clearly struggling under his weight. If he pressed down even a fraction more, those slight shoulders would give way.
The faint fragrance she carried kept reaching him, winding into his breath. He wanted to press her into his arms and breathe deep. To pull open that tightly wrapped collar...
Xiao Huaiyan pressed his hand to his own forehead. If he did that, she would cry again.
The bloodstained gold pin still rested silently in his robe, a quiet reminder.
The medicine hall was crowded. People who had been trampled, people who had been burned — everyone was calling for the physician at once.
Jiang Mi helped Xiao Huaiyan inside. They had barely gotten their footing when the person beside her lurched toward her. Jiang Mi scrambled to support him again, and Xiao Huaiyan slid almost entirely onto her side.
A round-faced woman sitting nearby waiting for her medicine stood up and said, "Young lady, your husband is badly hurt. Why don't you help him sit over here?"
Jiang Mi's face went red. She denied it quickly. "No — he is not my husband. He is — my elder brother." She knew how it must look, the two of them so close. It was an awkward situation, and she said the first thing that came to her.
The woman realized she had misspoken. So they were siblings, after all.
Though somehow they did not quite look like siblings. But plenty of young couples met in secret during the Lantern Festival. If these two would not admit it, the round-faced woman was not the type to pry. Seeing the girl struggling to hold up such a large man, she came over and helped settle him into a chair.
Jiang Mi thanked her quietly and sincerely.
When the round-faced woman got a better look at the siblings, she smiled even more warmly. "Don't mention it. You are a very pretty young lady — and your hus— ah, your elder brother is a very handsome man too. Is he spoken for?"
Jiang Mi said awkwardly, "My elder brother is not yet married. I — I am already betrothed."
The woman looked a little disappointed and was about to say more, when she felt a cold chill. She turned around and found herself facing the look in the so-called elder brother's eyes — terrifying.
She swallowed every remaining word, grabbed her medicine package, and left as fast as she could.
Jiang Mi looked at Xiao Huaiyan and met those dark, deep eyes head-on. She saw a faint smile at the corner of his lips, and he said in a mild, unhurried tone, "In such a hurry to show off your betrothal? My dear little sister?"
Jiang Mi lowered her head. "This subject spoke impulsively just now and presumed to use the name of a princess. I beg Your Majesty to forgive me. This subject will go and find the physician."
She did not dare look at Xiao Huaiyan's expression and walked briskly away.
Nearby, a middle-aged man in a blue robe and a short beard was finishing up with a patient who had twisted an ankle — repositioning the joint, applying medicine, wrapping it — before he stood and looked around.
Physician Liu caught sight of a young woman in fine clothes, beautiful in appearance, standing at the entrance. He walked over. "Is there something I can help you with?"
Jiang Mi said, "Doctor, my elder brother has taken a heavy blow to his back and his back was also burned. Could you please come and look at him?"
Physician Liu nodded and instructed his apprentice to manage the other patients, then followed Jiang Mi over.
He saw a man sitting in the bamboo chair with his eyes closed — dressed in black brocade, with a bearing that commanded the room. One look at these two and he knew they were no ordinary family.
He did not dare show any disrespect and moved forward to take the man's pulse.
Xiao Huaiyan opened one eye and said coldly, "Who are you?"
Physician Liu stopped, startled, and looked at Jiang Mi.
Jiang Mi came forward and said in a low voice, "Elder Brother, the physician has come to examine you."
Xiao Huaiyan looked at Jiang Mi for a moment, but said no more.
Physician Liu stepped forward and took his pulse, then touched his forehead.
He felt a quiet inward alarm and said to the young woman beside him, "Miss, your elder brother has a cold that has not yet cleared, and his constitution is still depleted. The current situation appears to have triggered his old complaint — he has some fever."
"As you described, a lamp stand falling from above would very easily cause internal injury. And he was burned as well? The back of his clothes need to come off to properly assess the wound. For someone this young to still be lucid and holding on at this point is remarkable."
Just as Physician Liu was about to examine the back wound, Xiao Huaiyan stopped him — without even opening his eyes. "Unnecessary. I will be well after resting."
Physician Liu looked back at Jiang Mi.
Jiang Mi was at a slight loss. She had almost forgotten — Xiao Huaiyan's wariness would never allow him to expose himself to examination by a stranger.
Since the patient refused to cooperate, the physician could hardly force it. He said to Jiang Mi, "Miss, please try to persuade your elder brother."
With other patients waiting, Physician Liu had no choice but to step away.
Jiang Mi found a clean bamboo cup and poured some warm water, then held it out to Xiao Huaiyan. "Your Majesty, I have tested this water myself. Perhaps it would help to drink a little?"
There was nothing more she could do for him. She could not make him cooperate with the physician. She could offer this much, and nothing beyond it.
Xiao Huaiyan looked at the cup. "Miss Jiang — not calling me 'Elder Brother' now?"
Jiang Mi pressed her lips together and said nothing. She was beginning to pull her hand back when the cup was taken from her.
Even in his discomfort, Xiao Huaiyan was unhurried. He drained the water calmly.
Before long, a large contingent of Embroidered Guards and imperial soldiers surrounded the medicine hall. Most of the people inside fled in panic.
The hall emptied quickly.
Li Fu came in with Chief Physician Gu and found the Emperor resting in the bamboo chair — and the third young lady of the Jiang family beside him.
Jiang Mi had heard the noise outside and started to move when the hand beside her closed around her wrist.
She looked at Xiao Huaiyan, who appeared to have fallen asleep. She pulled her wrist free.
Just then Li Fu and the others came in.
Jiang Mi let out a quiet breath of relief.
She stepped aside and watched Chief Physician Gu examine him.
"Chief Physician Gu — how are the Emperor's injuries?"
Chief Physician Gu said, "His old complaint has flared. He will need to return to the palace to have the external wounds treated."
Li Fu turned to Jiang Mi. "Since Miss Jiang is concerned for the Emperor, why not return to the palace with us?"
Jiang Mi shook her head. "Li Gonggong, with you here to look after His Majesty, he will be in the best of hands."
With Chief Physician Gu present and so many palace attendants to look after him, she was grateful to Xiao Huaiyan for the rescue — but she did not want to be further entangled with him.
Li Fu had not expected her to refuse so quickly. He had thought young ladies were the easiest to move. He had even seen her looking after the Emperor himself — and now she was putting distance between them so quickly.
At that moment the Embroidered Guard commander Pei Chi also walked in.
To Jiang Mi's surprise, there was a familiar face behind him.
"A'Rong!" Jiang Mi called out.
The small figure, still shaken with fright, saw her sister, gave a sob of startled relief, and ran toward her with tears on her face. "Elder Sister!"
Jiang Mi looked at Jiang Rong's dress, smudged with dirt and dust, her face streaked with grime. She wiped Jiang Rong's face with her handkerchief and asked, "A'Rong, what happened? Are you hurt?"
Jiang Rong hiccupped between sobs. "I got separated from Elder Sister Wan and the others. Then it got so chaotic. I was knocked to the ground and could barely get up. That official pulled me to my feet. I didn't recognize anyone — I was scared of being kidnapped, so I just followed that official and made him take me home."
Jiang Mi, listening to her, realized that the "official" she meant was Pei Chi.
She drew Jiang Rong behind her and curtseyed to Pei Chi. "Thank you, Commander Pei, for protecting my younger sister."
Pei Chi was indifferent. "A small matter." He himself found it somewhat odd — how had that little girl dared follow him.
Jiang Mi asked, "Commander, did you happen to see any of my other family members? I hope they are safe."
Pei Chi said, "I did not. But the rebels have already been dealt with — there will be news before long." He had been busy rounding up the rebels. The Jiang girl had followed him without warning, and only because she looked vaguely familiar had he bothered to do anything about it.
Jiang Mi felt a thread of worry. She did not know whether Jiang Wan and Jiang Yi had been scattered or hurt in that chaos.
From outside came a woman's voice: "Good sir, I believe my young mistress may be in this medicine hall. Might you let this old servant in to look?"
Jiang Mi recognized the voice. "Commander Pei, that is our household servant come to collect me. I would ask your permission to go." When she had gone to fetch water for Xiao Huaiyan, she had quietly asked someone to carry word to the Marquess of Cheng'en's manor to send someone to fetch her.
Pei Chi glanced at the Emperor, who appeared to be unconscious.
He looked back and said, "Since your household servant has come to take you home, there is naturally no reason to detain you. Miss Jiang, please go."
Jiang Mi turned in Xiao Huaiyan's direction and dipped a small bow. To Li Fu she said, "Li Gonggong, I will take my leave."
Li Fu said, "You are too kind, Miss. We must thank you for your care of His Majesty." Not a single question about how she and the Emperor had come to be together.
Jiang Mi said nothing more and left with Jiang Rong.
The moment Jiang Mi stepped through the door, the Xiao Huaiyan who had appeared to be asleep opened his eyes.
Those eyes were perfectly, sharply clear.
He rose to his feet, showing none of the weakness of just before.
Pei Chi and the others knelt before him. "Your Majesty."

chapters.title: Sixty six
chapters.content:
Pei Chi saw the cold, contained air about the Emperor and thought: just now when Miss Jiang wanted to leave, it was the Emperor who silently permitted it — he was the one who had let her go. Surely the Emperor would not be in a mood about that?
He steadied himself and reported, "Your Majesty — among the rebels who were captured, we found a map of the imperial city's layout, as well as a counterfeit imperial seal."
He presented both items.
Xiao Huaiyan glanced at them and gave a cold laugh. "All that fanfare, and the purpose was to smuggle out these dead objects? It is far too conspicuous. This must be a diversion. Keep investigating."
Everyone present turned the words over in their heads. Not dead objects — could it be something living?
Pei Chi received the order.
He thought of something else he could not withhold, and spoke. "Your Majesty — during the apprehension of the rebels, a small incident occurred involving Young Lord Xue Jinglin of the Duke Zhen State's manor. He was wounded while protecting Princess An'yang, and the Princess had him placed in her carriage. She then had her servants drive the carriage to find a physician and rebuked the Embroidered Guards who attempted to search it. This subject went over in person, and only then did the Princess consent to open the carriage door. Young Lord Xue was inside."
"What a remarkable coincidence." Xiao Huaiyan's smile did not reach his eyes.
"Since he protected An'yang, send an imperial physician to see to him. Young Lord Xue seems to attract misfortune at every turn — he should rest well and take proper care of himself."
Chief Physician Gu quickly acknowledged the command.
Xiao Huaiyan returned to the palace. Li Fu helped him remove his outer robe — the inner garments were soaked through with blood. When the fabric stuck to the wound was peeled away, Li Fu drew a sharp breath. It looked agonizing just to witness.
The Emperor had not made a sound.
Chief Physician Gu cleaned the burn wound and then examined the blunt injury from the lamp stand, shaking his head as he did so. "Your Majesty should not have let it go this long. These injuries would have been much better treated earlier."
Xiao Huaiyan had a certain numbness toward pain. In Yunzhou he had been to the battlefield too many times to count, and had taken wounds beyond numbering. This was nothing to him.
He said calmly, "It is fine. Apply the medicine."
Chief Physician Gu dressed and bandaged the wounds with great care.
As he left, he said quietly to Li Fu, "His Majesty's cold was not fully recovered before, and now he has both internal injury and burns on top of it — a high fever could easily follow. Even a man of iron could not withstand that. Keep a close watch tonight."
Li Fu nodded repeatedly. "Do not worry, Chief Physician."
Li Fu looked at the figure lying on the imperial bed, his mind full of puzzlement. The Emperor clearly still cared about Miss Jiang. With a single word from him, no matter how unwilling she might be, she would have had no choice but to enter the palace quietly.
Why put himself through all this...
Li Fu truly could not understand what Miss Jiang was thinking. The Emperor treated her so well — why would she refuse?
Xiao Huaiyan lay with his eyes closed. He had a feeling he was about to slip back into that dream.
At first there was only darkness. Then he heard that soft, gentle voice.
"Li Gonggong, how long has His Majesty been asleep?"
"Is His Majesty's cold any better?"
"May I stay here and keep him company for a little while?"
Each question quieter than the last — as though afraid of being turned away.
He caught the familiar fragrance. The person speaking sat down at the edge of the bed.
A breath of cool air reached him. Xiao Huaiyan's hand moved on instinct and closed around it.
A soft cry of pain.
Xiao Huaiyan opened his eyes. He saw Jiang Mi dressed in a honey-yellow short-sleeved jacket and skirt, her expression anxious, a touch of timidity in her eyes. "Your Majesty — did I disturb you?"
Xiao Huaiyan said nothing. He only looked at her.
Under his gaze, Jiang Mi's face grew warm. She looked away and said, "Li Gonggong asked me to persuade Your Majesty to drink the medicine while it is still warm. It has been sitting for a little while now — it should be just the right temperature."
Xiao Huaiyan followed her gaze to the bowl of medicine on the nearby table. He did not move.
He still had not let go of her wrist.
Jiang Mi murmured in embarrassment, "Li Gonggong always says Your Majesty refuses the medicine — surely Your Majesty is not afraid of the bitterness?"
Xiao Huaiyan said nothing, watching the unguarded nervousness that flickered across her face.
Jiang Mi seemed to catch herself. She lowered her head, looking a little vexed, and apologized. "This subject spoke out of turn. I have overstepped."
Xiao Huaiyan watched her head drop lower and lower — all restless discomfort.
He released her hand. "Bring the medicine over."
Just those few words were enough to make her anxiety dissolve.
He watched Jiang Mi bring the bowl over, watched her slender fingers around it — and changed his mind. "You feed it to me."
Jiang Mi's expression showed surprise, and then a small, rosy smile curved her lips. She stirred the medicine with the spoon and held it out.
Spoonful by spoonful, the medicine went down — just as bitter as always. Xiao Huaiyan's brow kept creasing.
But when he looked up at the person doing the feeding, he found he could bear it.
When the bowl was finished, Jiang Mi stood to leave. Xiao Huaiyan reached out — and caught only air.
The figure moved further and further away, swallowed by a thick white mist.
Xiao Huaiyan opened his eyes again.
An empty bedchamber. The whole room smelled of bitter medicine.
No one at his side.
Xiao Huaiyan stared up at the canopy, his expression slightly dazed.
......
Jiang Mi left the medicine hall with Jiang Rong and boarded the carriage. The serving woman who had come to collect her was one of Su-shi's people.
Jiang Mi told her that she and Jiang Rong had been separated in the crowd and found each other again at the medicine hall — omitting entirely the part about Xiao Huaiyan.
The woman pressed a hand to her chest in relief. "Thank heaven Third Miss and Fourth Miss are both safe and sound."
Jiang Mi asked, "And Eldest Cousin and Sister Wan — are they all right?"
The woman said, "When this old servant came out to fetch you, she had just heard that the young master's group had all made it home safely. The household had sent servants in every direction to look for Third Miss and Fourth Miss. It was fortunate that you sent word, Miss."
Only then did Jiang Mi truly exhale.
When Jiang Mi and Jiang Rong returned to the Marquess of Cheng'en's manor, the whole household from both branches was gathered in the main hall. They did not settle until they saw the two of them walk in safe and unharmed.
Su-shi was holding Jiang Rong close and questioning her. Jiang Wan drew Jiang Mi aside and said, "After you were separated, he had someone send Miss Xue back to the Duke Zhen State's manor first, then spent the whole time looking for you. When we got your message, someone was sent to let him know you were safe. This is the letter from the Duke Zhen State's manor saying everyone is all right. After all that tonight, let's not go chasing this kind of excitement anymore — who knows when rebels might come crawling out of it."
Jiang Mi nodded. Thank goodness nothing had gone badly wrong.
Back in Yuanzhi Courtyard, Jiang Mi was exhausted.
When Qiuyu took her cape off for her, she exclaimed in surprise, "Oh — there is a hole burned right through here."
Jiang Mi looked. Sure enough, there was a scorch mark.
It must have been caught by sparks when the burning lamp stand came down.
In that brief instant Xiao Huaiyan had pulled her into his arms — her cape had only taken a small hole from the fire. At the time the flames had spread so fast she had not had a moment to move.
Jiang Mi was still for a moment, then told Qiuyu to put the cape away.
She turned in bed late into the night without sleeping, and every time she closed her eyes she saw the wall of fire.
She got up and poured herself a cup of warm water. As she raised her arm she noticed two reddened fingerprints still faintly visible on her wrist.
She let out a quiet sigh.
In the second half of the night she drifted off at last.
She seemed to hear voices asking after someone.
"Greetings to Noble Consort Rou. My mistress has just taken her medicine and fallen asleep."
"How unfortunate. But I have heard that Consort Jiang is unwell, so I have come to see her. Since I am already here, I may as well look in."
Jiang Mi opened her drowsy eyes to find Noble Consort Rou, in a long moon-white gown, standing before her.
Noble Consort Rou was as soft and lovely as her title suggested. She settled herself at the bedside. "Consort Jiang, are you feeling any better?"
Jiang Mi felt weak, but managed a nod.
Noble Consort Rou said, "I heard about the business with your uncle and your cousin. His Majesty has always been generous — even when he punishes, it is not too severely. Still, your uncle and cousin became entangled in the matter of examination bribery, and that is something that cannot be overlooked regardless of whether anything actually changed hands. The chief offenders were beheaded by His Majesty. Your uncle being only demoted, your cousin only barred from the examinations — that is already exceptional leniency. Do not let it weigh on you."
Qiuyu knelt down. "Noble Consort, please say no more. My mistress does not yet know of these things. She is still unwell."
Noble Consort Rou covered her lips in surprise. "Consort Jiang, you truly did not know? It is all my fault — I was afraid you might resent His Majesty, and wanted to come and comfort you. Aiyo, it is entirely my fault. Consort Jiang, do not let it trouble you too much. The Jiang family will recover in time. You still have a young brother — there is always hope."
Jiang Mi pressed her handkerchief to her mouth and coughed hard. She looked at Qiuyu. "Is what Noble Consort Rou says true?"
Qiuyu could not hide it any longer and gave a small nod.
Noble Consort Rou continued, "Consort Jiang, you must take care of yourself. The border at Yunzhou is restless — war may break out at any moment. There is also a rebel uprising in the south. His Majesty's mind is entirely on state affairs. He has no attention to spare for anything else. As for your father's situation — His Majesty only gave him a reprimand and docked his pay. So please, do not go and plead for clemency again."
The coughing that had just settled seized Jiang Mi again. Her father — what had happened to her father now?
Noble Consort Rou looked at her with sympathy, turned her wrist to show off the new gold-and-jade jingle bracelet on it, and bent to tuck Jiang Mi's blanket in. In a lowered voice she said, "Those who rely on their looks can only hold favor for so long. Take care of yourself, Consort Jiang."
She swept her bracelet-laden wrist out the door and departed.
Jiang Mi let out a choked sob. All the burdens of her family weighed on her heart. Her aunt had asked her to enter the palace to protect the family — and yet she had not even known what had happened to her own father, had not known that the punishment for her uncle and eldest cousin had already been handed down.
The gold-and-jade jingle bracelet on Noble Consort Rou's wrist was nearly identical to the gold-and-jade ankle bells Xiao Huaiyan had put on her feet in the dream. Noble Consort Rou had come to gloat — she had done everything to please Xiao Huaiyan, had accommodated his every desire, and was still nothing.
She had given everything she had to love a man, and an emperor's heart was the most indifferent and unknowable thing in the world.
Jiang Mi came awake with tears soaking her collar, curled in on herself. Her last life had been too bitter, too painful. In this life, she would not dare.
The next morning, Jiang Mi wrote a letter to Xue Ningzhu — asking how she was faring, and tucking a separate note inside for the Young Lord, thanking him for searching for her through the night and telling him she was safe.
She had it sent to the Duke Zhen State's manor.
After midday Jiang Mi sat doing her embroidery, with Mianmian sprawled across her lap seeking attention. It was rather pleasant.
While she was absorbed in it, footsteps sounded from behind her.
Jiang Mi looked up with mild surprise but no great shock. The embroidery needle did not leave her hand — she went on stitching the cluster of begonia blossoms.
Chengzhong saw that Miss Jiang's manner was perfectly composed, and found himself a little uncertain of how to proceed. He smiled carefully. "Miss Jiang, His Majesty wishes to see you."
Jiang Mi sat without moving. "Chengzhong, you have come to the wrong place. You have come to the wrong person."
Chengzhong said, "Miss Jiang, please do not put this servant in a difficult position."
