Jiang Mi kept her eyes lowered. "Is Chengzhong not putting me in a difficult position? I have already been given a marriage bestowal by His Majesty. I am at home awaiting the wedding, and it would not be proper for me to go out. Besides, Chengzhong — even if you were to go to my eldest uncle or my father this time, it would do no good."
Chengzhong knew perfectly well it would do no good. When Miss Jiang had had no betrothal, the Marquess of Cheng'en had been only too happy for her to be summoned by the Emperor. Now that Miss Jiang had obtained the marriage bestowal and was in the process of arranging her wedding to the Young Lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor, there was no way to get her to go willingly anymore.
He could hardly go to the Marquess about it, and he could not drag Miss Jiang there by force.
He reasoned with her at length, but Miss Jiang still would not go. There was nothing for it. He would have to go back and take his punishment.
Chengzhong left with a sigh and returned to a private residence outside the palace.
He found the Emperor seated in the pavilion above the garden rockery, playing a game of chess against himself.
Chengzhong knelt. "Your Majesty, this servant has failed. This servant was unable to bring Miss Jiang."
Xiao Huaiyan set down a black piece, eyes not lifting. "She would not come."
Chengzhong did not dare answer.
Xiao Huaiyan asked nothing more and went on playing his game alone.
The game stretched until nightfall. Xiao Huaiyan placed the final piece, then rose and left.
Chengzhong glanced at the board. It was a draw.
The Emperor never played to a draw. And yet today — a draw?
......
Jiang Mi had thought her refusal would be thorough enough that she would not see Chengzhong again. But the very next day he came back.
Was he still trying to take her to meet Xiao Huaiyan at the private residence?
She was about to refuse again when Chengzhong drew a rosewood box from his sleeve.
He presented it respectfully. "Miss Jiang, my master asked this servant to give you this."
"My master also asked this servant to convey to you: a sharp blade is a dangerous thing. Whatever the circumstances, never turn it on yourself — better to use it on someone else. He returns this now to its owner, and hopes you will cherish yourself."
Chengzhong placed the rosewood box on the table, then quietly withdrew.
Jiang Mi stared at the box for a long moment before opening it.
Inside lay a gold hairpin, clean and spotless.
She picked it up. She recognized it at once — it was the one she had used in that moment of despair in the side hall when Xiao Huaiyan had cornered her.
Seeing again this pin she had turned against herself, her tears came without her willing them.
She wiped them away and set the pin back in the box.
She would not do something so foolish again.
No one was worth her life as the price.
She wanted to live — and live well.
......
In the Qianshuang Temple in the capital, sandalwood incense curled through the air. A grey-robed old monk was placing acupuncture needles into Xiao Huaiyan's meridian points.
The long, fine silver needles entered one by one.
The old monk shook his head repeatedly as he assessed the condition before him. "Why has Your Majesty left it this long before coming?"
Xiao Huaiyan said without much concern, "State affairs kept me occupied. I had no time."
The old monk said, "If Your Majesty does not care for the body, how is the head ailment to heal? Your Majesty's old complaint and latent injuries have all been aggravated at once — this will only continue to harm you."
Xiao Huaiyan said, "Master, I know my own limits."
The old monk sighed, then asked, "When did Your Majesty's jade ring disappear?"
Xiao Huaiyan closed his eyes and gave a short laugh. "It shattered."
The old monk's brow creased with deep worry. "Then can Your Majesty still keep it under control?"
Xiao Huaiyan's tone was mild. "Master, what I have always controlled is myself — not something external. It was only a jade ring."
The old monk withdrew the long needles, his expression resigned.
"Master's Buddhist insight is deep. I happen to have something I cannot make sense of." Xiao Huaiyan said suddenly.
The old monk asked, "What is it?"
Xiao Huaiyan said, "Does Master recall what I mentioned last time — that when my head ailment worsened I began to have what seemed like visions?"
The old monk said, "I recall. Are they still occurring?"
Xiao Huaiyan said, "Now I no longer think they are visions at all. They feel more like things that actually happened. Master — do you believe in past lives and the present one?"
"The Dharma speaks of the wheel of rebirth, of cause and effect in endless cycles. Naturally it exists," the old monk said, reciting a name of Buddha.
Xiao Huaiyan gave a soft laugh. "So that is how it is. Those fragmented dreams and visions do not appear without reason."
The old monk sighed. "All appearances are illusion; when one perceives that appearances are not appearances, one perceives the Tathagata. That is the heart of the Diamond Sutra. Does Your Majesty understand its meaning? Where the mind holds an attachment, a thousand fantasies arise from it. What cannot be let go in this life will still toss and turn in the next, impossible to forget."
"What the mind holds dear — all that one sees and hears — perhaps it never existed in this life, and yet perhaps through the cause and effect of a past life, it was already planted long ago."
Was his attachment from a past life so deep that in this life he could still remember it in dreams?
Xiao Huaiyan asked, "But these dreams come and go, like broken fragments. Master — how does one recover all that is lost?"
"Buddhism speaks of the workings of fate. When the karma ripens, memory returns of itself." The old monk turned the prayer beads in his hand. "If Your Majesty truly cannot let go, there will come a time when it all comes back."
Xiao Huaiyan waited for the old monk to withdraw the last of the needles, then walked out of the meditation hall and looked toward the solemn, majestic Buddha statue in the distance.
Then — did Jiang Mi share this karma too?
......
Jiang Mi heard from Su-shi that her father had already begun discussing the wedding date with the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang.
She was told to set her mind at ease and prepare herself for the wedding.
In the days that followed, Su-shi would have Jiang Mi come to her courtyard, where she would teach her things relating to household accounts and management. As for the more intimate matters of womanhood, she was not quite sure how to broach them — perhaps she would simply tuck a certain kind of book into Jiang Mi's hands closer to the wedding date.
Jiang Mi was glad to come. What Su-shi taught was knowledge she had never been exposed to before, and learning more of it could only be to her benefit.
The back and forth drew them closer together. The one most delighted by this was Jiang Rong, who tagged along and sat in on her mother's lessons beside her sister. Though she could not follow everything, she found it interesting all the same.
Jiang Mi had just returned from Su-shi's courtyard when Xia Ruo came rushing in. "Miss — Mama Cui has come. She says the Empress Dowager is seriously ill and asks you to come to the palace to attend her sickbed."
There was no refusing. Jiang Mi had no choice but to follow Mama Cui into the palace.
This was the first time Jiang Mi had seen the Empress Dowager since the marriage bestowal.
The Empress Dowager was propped against the headboard. More white had crept into her temples than before, and she looked considerably older.
Jiang Mi felt the ache of it. She knelt at the bedside. "Aunt. It is Tangtan who has disappointed your hopes again."
The Empress Dowager looked at the young woman kneeling before her and let out a long, sorrowful breath. "Get up."
Mama Cui helped Jiang Mi to her feet.
Jiang Mi stood at the bedside. She thought of how in her last life it was from about this time that her aunt's health had begun its long decline, until at the very end she extracted a promise from the Emperor to take Jiang Mi into the palace.
She had once asked Chief Physician Gu — her aunt's health should not have deteriorated so quickly. Looking at her aunt now, she felt the guilt keenly.
The Empress Dowager looked at the girl she had raised. She was supposed to have been the Jiang family's hope — the last lifeline that could save them.
Such a pity.
"Tangtan, I wish to ask you something. On the night of the Lantern Festival — was it the Emperor who saved you?"
Jiang Mi was startled. How did her aunt know?
She lowered her head and answered, "Yes."
The Empress Dowager said, "I imagine you are curious how I found out."
"Chief Physician Gu comes often on account of my illness, and in conversation let slip that the Emperor had sustained injuries. When I pressed him, he admitted they were the result of the Emperor saving you. Tangtan — by all rights and reasons, you ought to go and see him in person and thank him."
Since entering the palace as a consort was no longer possible, she could not afford to be abandoned by the Emperor either.
From this incident, the Empress Dowager had glimpsed something beyond the ordinary. Perhaps matters could still be turned around.
Jiang Mi had not expected this request.
"Aunt — His Majesty has already bestowed a marriage upon me. It would not be proper for me to go and call on him," Jiang Mi said.
The Empress Dowager pressed a hand to her chest, her voice pained. "Tangtan — setting everything else aside, the Emperor saved your life. You ought to go. This is a matter of proper conduct, and of the Jiang family's dignity. When you shielded the Emperor, he made a special allowance for you to recover at the temporary palace and rewarded you generously. Now the Emperor has saved you — are you unwilling even to pay him a visit?"
Seeing her aunt's old ailment flare, Jiang Mi helped Mama Cui ease her back against the cushions.
And so she had no choice but to agree.
When Jiang Mi arrived at the Qianqing Palace bearing the gifts the Empress Dowager had prepared and asked to be received, both Chengzhong and Li Fu thought for a moment that their eyes were deceiving them.
Li Fu hurried inside to announce her.
Xiao Huaiyan was reviewing memorials. The cold medicine from earlier still sat untouched beside him. Seeing Li Fu rush in, he looked up with displeasure.
Li Fu beamed. "Your Majesty — the third young lady of the Jiang family is outside requesting an audience."
Xiao Huaiyan's vermilion brush paused. "The third young lady of the Jiang family?"
Li Fu said brightly, "Indeed. Miss Jiang appears to have brought nourishing gifts as well. Most thoughtful."
Xiao Huaiyan rubbed his brow. "Let her in."

chapters.title: Sixty seven
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi carried the food box Mama Cui had pressed into her hands and walked with heavy steps. She had refused the Emperor's summons before, thoroughly offending him. Now she was appearing before him again — she had no idea what his mood would be.
She felt the helplessness of having no other choice.
She followed Li Gonggong into the hall. The floor heating made the room pleasantly warm, and she handed her cape to a waiting palace maid.
Li Fu stopped at the screen and gestured with his eyes for her to go in alone.
Jiang Mi came around the screen and found Xiao Huaiyan dressed in loose, comfortable everyday robes.
His hair was bound in a jade crown. A scroll lay in his hand. Without the usual trappings of authority, he looked less imposing than usual — more at ease, more casual.
Jiang Mi lowered her head and curtseyed. "Long life and health to Your Majesty."
Xiao Huaiyan set the scroll on the table. His gaze settled on her. "Rise."
His voice was mild. Jiang Mi looked up and noticed his jaw had grown leaner — he had lost some weight.
She held the food box and hesitated.
Xiao Huaiyan saw the rigid set of her spine and the nervous tension in the fingers gripping the box. He spoke first. "You have come to bring me something?"
Jiang Mi lowered her gaze and opened the box. "This subject has come at the Empress Dowager's direction to deliver restorative broth to Your Majesty. The Empress Dowager learned of Your Majesty's injury and had the small kitchen prepare this especially."
She had made clear whose idea it was. Whether he drank it was entirely his decision.
"I see — so it is the Empress Dowager's wish." Xiao Huaiyan's voice gave nothing away. He paused, then said, "Jiang Mi — and what of you?"
The two syllables of her name from Xiao Huaiyan's lips carried a quality she could not quite name — especially the second character, its trailing tone lingering, soft and unhurried.
Jiang Mi did not know what to do with herself. What was she supposed to say?
"This subject is grateful to Your Majesty for the rescue and feels guilty for having been no help."
Xiao Huaiyan gave a short laugh. "Just that? You cannot even bring yourself to ask after my injury? Jiang Mi, your thanks leave something to be desired."
Jiang Mi pressed her lips together, feeling something uncomfortable stirring in her chest. She was not the kind of person who could receive someone's help and not acknowledge it — but the someone in question was Xiao Huaiyan. She did not know what she should do or could do. She only wanted less entanglement with him, and had been trying hard to put the whole thing out of her mind.
Her lips moved, as though she were about to say something. In the end she let it go.
Xiao Huaiyan waited with perfect patience.
At that moment Chengzhong came in carrying a wooden tray, breaking the room's quiet.
He bowed. "Your Majesty, it is time to change your dressings."
Jiang Mi glanced at the assortment of bottles and jars on the tray, and felt an involuntary wave of relief. With the Emperor attending to his wounds, she could take her leave.
Xiao Huaiyan said flatly, "Leave it."
Chengzhong did not linger and withdrew quickly.
Jiang Mi blinked. Was Xiao Huaiyan not going to change his dressings?
She had no choice but to go on standing there in silence.
Xiao Huaiyan tapped his fingers on the table. "Truly nothing you want to say to me?"
He stood and walked toward her. Seeing her about to step back, he stopped.
"Gratitude should come with sincerity. Though between us, it seems we have been exchanging thanks in circles."
He looked at her. "You thanked me by shielding me from an arrow. I thanked you by granting you a wish."
Cold sweat broke out on Jiang Mi's back. Though Xiao Huaiyan's tone carried a hint of a smile, it sent a chill through her.
Seeing something like apprehension on her face, he softened his voice. "Then this time I shielded you from the lamp stand. Courtesy demands reciprocity — it would not be unreasonable for me to ask you for one small thing in return, would it?"
Jiang Mi could neither agree nor decline.
She owed him a debt, and she should repay it — but she was afraid of what Xiao Huaiyan might ask for that she could not give.
She forced the words out, her voice a little rough. "What does Your Majesty wish this subject to do?"
Xiao Huaiyan appeared to consider the question. He stepped to her side and looked at the small pearl earrings at her ear. "I will not make it difficult for you. Change my dressings once, and we are even. What do you say?"
Jiang Mi could hardly believe it. Xiao Huaiyan would let it go that easily?
She looked up, uncertain. "Does Your Majesty mean that truly?"
Xiao Huaiyan gave a short, dismissive laugh. "Of course I mean it. I do not deceive people."
The words stung. Was Xiao Huaiyan not plainly implying that she was the one who deceived?
But if changing his dressings once could buy her peace hereafter, she was willing to try.
Jiang Mi helped Xiao Huaiyan change his dressings.
The burn wound on his back was severe. She recognized these as wounds left by the burning lamp stand — wounds he had taken while shielding her from it.
As she cleaned and applied the medicine, she did not say anything. And yet when she looked at the raw, damaged skin, she felt her throat tighten.
Xiao Huaiyan noticed her movements slow. He said, his voice casual, "What are you thinking about?"
Jiang Mi returned to what she was doing. She murmured, "This subject is thinking that this wound must be very painful."
Xiao Huaiyan said, "Did you not want this subject to say 'it is nothing much'?"
Jiang Mi did not take the bait and finished the last of the dressing in silence.
She gathered the medicine and stepped back. "Finished. This subject will take her leave."
Xiao Huaiyan turned to look at her. "How does it look?"
Jiang Mi met his gaze briefly, then lowered her eyes. "Chief Physician Gu's medicine is excellent. It should heal well."
She said goodbye and went out.
When she had almost reached the hall entrance, she heard the voice behind her.
"Jiang Mi."
She stopped but did not turn around.
"This promise — whatever the circumstances, it still stands."
Jiang Mi stood still for two breaths, then left without looking back.
She walked out of the Qianqing Palace and into the corridor, and her footsteps lost their composure entirely, quickening into something close to a run.

chapters.title: Sixty eight
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi stopped in a garden, out of breath, her thoughts in complete disorder.
Why had he gone back on his word?
The marriage bestowal he had granted in the great hall — how could he take it back? There had been royal relatives, noble households, the families of officials as witnesses, and the Grand Princess of Zhaoyang herself.
What was more, her family had already exchanged betrothal papers with the Duke Zhen State's manor. The ceremonial gifts had begun.
Jiang Mi kept reassuring herself, trying to calm down.
She had thought that once the marriage bestowal was secured she could shake free of her last life's fate and break her ties to Xiao Huaiyan. But his behavior now was becoming increasingly impossible to read.
From the moment she had resolved to use his promise to secure the bestowal, she had never imagined he could be this changeable.
What was she to do? Would this drag the Young Lord into trouble too?
Just then, voices drifted from behind the garden rockery.
"Did Princess An'yang sneak out again to visit Young Lord Xue at the Duke Zhen State's manor? I'm a little worried — what do I say if the Virtuous Dowager Consort asks?"
"Just tell her the truth. Young Lord Xue protected the Princess at the lantern festival — it's only right that she should go and see how he is."
"Of course it is right, but the Princess has been going very often."
"If it weren't for the fact that Young Lord Xue is already betrothed to the girl from the Marquess of Cheng'en's household, I'd almost think the Princess wants to make him her consort..."
"Shh — stop talking. Someone might hear."
"What's the harm? I've also heard that the Jiang girl from the Marquess of Cheng'en's manor has entered the palace again. The Empress Dowager sent her to take broth to the Emperor. That Jiang girl doesn't go and visit Young Lord Xue but rushes off to the Qianqing Palace instead — I honestly cannot tell what she is playing at..."
The voices of the two small palace maids grew more distant until they faded away.
Jiang Mi leaned against the railing, her expression a little dazed.
Young Lord Xue was wounded? He had protected Princess An'yang at the lantern festival?
But in his letter to her, why had he not said a word of it? He had only told her that all was well.
Jiang Mi could not help thinking — her aunt had said it was Chief Physician Gu who accidentally let slip that Xiao Huaiyan's wounds had come from saving her.
But would someone as careful as Chief Physician Gu truly let something slip?
The wind cut bitterly cold. Jiang Mi realized only now that she was chilled. In her hurry to run from the bedchamber she had completely forgotten her cape.
She quickened her pace through the small garden and made her way back toward Cining Palace.
Qingxue was just seeing several medical attendants out of Cining Palace when she spotted Miss Jiang approaching.
"Miss, why have you come back alone? Where is Mama Cui?"
Jiang Mi looked at the retreating medical attendants and recognized one of them as Wei Yin.
She pulled herself back to the present. "I took a different path and must have missed Mama Cui. Qingxue, please send one of the maids to find her."
Qingxue nodded and dispatched a small palace maid to pass the message.
She pressed a hand warmer into Jiang Mi's hands. "Warm yourself first, Miss. Come inside quickly."
Jiang Mi had been walking fast, and was not as cold as before.
She asked as they went, "Has my aunt been calling the medical attendants again?"
Qingxue nodded. "The Empress Dowager has been troubled by headaches and difficulty sleeping these days. She does not want to take too much medicine, so she has the medical attendants come to massage her meridian points and do acupuncture — it brings some relief."
The words were said without intent, but Jiang Mi heard them with a sinking feeling. Her aunt's condition was worsening because of her.
There was a weight on her chest she could not shift. She asked quietly, "That tall medical attendant in green — the one I don't recognize — does she come often to do the acupuncture for my aunt?"
Qingxue said, "You must mean Attendant Wei. The Empress Dowager quite likes her technique. She has been called several times recently."
Jiang Mi suddenly stopped walking, her brow creasing. She asked with a careful uncertainty, "Did she suggest using incense to help my aunt sleep?"
Qingxue looked surprised. "How did you know, Miss? Attendant Wei did indeed suggest an incense blend to the Empress Dowager. The few herbs and aromatics she named were all examined by the imperial physicians and confirmed to have a sleep-inducing effect."
Jiang Mi felt cold inside. "How long has my aunt been using it?"
Qingxue answered, "About two months now. Miss, what is the matter?"
Jiang Mi's heart sank. That would have started right around the time she went to the Fengchi hunt.
In her last life she had been at the Marquess of Cheng'en's manor the entire time and had never known that Attendant Wei had also treated her aunt.
Her aunt had died suddenly in the seventh month of the second year of Yuanxi.
That was only a little more than half a year from now.
At that time, Consort Duan and Noble Consort Rou had entered the palace in succession, and her aunt's anxiety and agitation had caused her illness to worsen sharply.
Her aunt had summoned her to attend her sickbed, and on her deathbed had extracted a promise from Xiao Huaiyan to take Jiang Mi into the palace.
At the time she had wept with grief, and felt her heart sink with humiliation at her aunt's final wish. If Xiao Huaiyan had wanted her, he could have summoned her to the palace long before — he had not needed her aunt to make it a dying wish. Her aunt was forcing him into a corner where he could not refuse.
Her aunt's health had never been good, but for it to have collapsed so utterly within only half a year — could that truly have been a coincidence?
Jiang Mi stepped into the inner chamber. The room was heavy with the scent of medicine, thick enough to induce drowsiness.
She saw her aunt resting against the chaise.
Her footsteps must have disturbed her. The Empress Dowager slowly opened her eyes.
She looked at Jiang Mi for a moment, rubbed her eyes as though coming into focus, and smiled. "It is Tangtan. You have come back."
Jiang Mi's nose pricked with feeling. She went forward and curtseyed. "Aunt, it is me."
The Empress Dowager took her hand and asked, "How was it? Is the Emperor's injury better? Did he drink the broth?"
Jiang Mi looked at her aunt's well-kept hands. From childhood she had loved those hands — the way they touched her forehead, pinched her cheeks, wiped her tears. Those hands held so many of her most cherished early memories. She held them in return and said, "Aunt — His Majesty admitted me out of regard for you. Whether he drank the broth, Tangtan does not know."
Jiang Mi rested her head in her aunt's lap. "Aunt — I beg you, please do not send me to the Emperor with things anymore. I have been betrothed to the Duke Zhen State's manor. Going before the Emperor again would be improper — it would invite gossip and bring shame to the Marquess of Cheng'en's household."
The Empress Dowager looked at her with a complicated expression and reached out to touch her face. "What a foolish child. Tangtan — the truth is, entering the palace would have been good for both you and the Jiang family. It is a pity you chose such a poor way of going about it and made an already tangled situation worse."
Jiang Mi could not stop herself. "Aunt — why have you always been so insistent on sending me into the palace? Father says the Jiang family may face a disaster. What is it, Aunt? Can it truly not be avoided?"
The Empress Dowager's expression darkened. How dare Jiang Qingxuan raise this with her!
She yanked her hand away from Jiang Mi's and said sharply, "Enough. Do not ask any more of this."
Jiang Mi felt helpless. This again — her aunt evading, as she always did.
What had her aunt done?
In her last life she had only known that people everywhere said her aunt had been involved in the events of the tenth year of Emperor Yongshun's reign, that her aunt had harmed Consort Jin, that the Jiang family was implicated. At the time she had not believed it at all — her aunt had already been Empress, and had no quarrel with Consort Jin. Why would she have harmed her? Besides, had the guilty parties not been the late Emperor's Noble Consort Gui and Consort De? Had their crimes not already been made public?
Seeing that her aunt was unwilling to speak further, Jiang Mi stood and went to the incense burner at the side of the room. She extinguished it, then opened the window. Cold air poured in, scattering the thick medicinal fragrance from the room.
The Empress Dowager saw what she was doing and looked displeased. "What are you doing?"
Jiang Mi sank to her knees. "Aunt, please do not be angry. Tangtan has something to tell you."
The Empress Dowager rarely saw Jiang Mi like this. She narrowed her eyes. "Get up. I will not punish you."
Jiang Mi shook her head. "Aunt, please hear me out. I heard from Qingxue that you have been relying on this incense blend to sleep these past weeks — and it was Attendant Wei who suggested it?"
The Empress Dowager nodded. "That is so. Is there a problem? The imperial physicians all checked the ingredients — there was nothing incompatible among them."
Jiang Mi said, "The ingredients themselves are not the problem. But I noticed that among them are costus root and rose dew."
"I realize this may be hard to believe. When Su-shi arranged a medical attendant from Jiangnan for me, that attendant noticed I had also been using a blend of costus root and rose dew to help me sleep, and she cautioned me: these two aromatics should not be used together over a long period. They create dependence, and they deepen whatever sadness and anxiety is already in the heart — they can even cause hallucinations."
"She also said that many people do not know these two cannot be combined. There have been cases of someone using the blend for a prolonged time, and eventually taking their own life. I have been so worried about your health that I took it upon myself to extinguish the incense. Aunt — please use it no more."
The Empress Dowager's expression grew more and more severe. When Jiang Mi had finished, she slapped the armrest. "Outrageous — that there could be such a viper hiding within these palace walls. Qingxue, go and have the Office of Discipline bring Attendant Wei here at once."
Qingxue was about to go and carry out the order.
Jiang Mi stopped her. "Aunt — wait."
"Aunt — very few people know these two aromatics cannot be combined. The herbs and aromatics were all checked by the imperial physicians and nothing was found. If you have Attendant Wei brought here, and she claims she did not know? Even under torture, could you be certain she would confess? Was this careless ignorance or deliberate intent? A mere medical attendant — where would she find the nerve to do this on her own? Might she have been directed by someone behind her? It would be better, Aunt, not to alert the snake before you have found its burrow. Have Attendant Wei's background quietly investigated first."
If she had not encountered Wei Yin at Cining Palace — if she had not known that her aunt's incense also contained costus root and rose dew — she might have thought the suggestion Wei Yin had once made to her was innocent, or perhaps the work of some jealous consort. But Wei Yin had already used the same method on her aunt, long before that. Could it still be unintentional?
Xiao Huaiyan's inner court was still empty — there could be no rivalry between consorts.
Had her aunt's early death been connected to this incense? And had Wei Yin then tried the same method on her? Was Wei Yin targeting the Jiang family?
The Empress Dowager rose and helped Jiang Mi to her feet. A look of sharp recognition moved through her eyes. "Tangtan, you are right. This must be looked into properly."
A viper lay coiled in the shadows — that was indeed enough to make one sleep uneasily.
Jiang Mi felt a little calmer seeing that her aunt believed her and had heeded the advice.
She asked, "Aunt — Qingxue says you have been relying on the incense for some time now. Have you developed a dependence on it? If you stop suddenly, will it be very hard on you?" She wanted to go back and ask the medical attendant whether the incense had any other hidden dangers. Her aunt had been using a blend with a few more ingredients than her own — she was not sure whether the others might also cause problems.
The Empress Dowager said, "I will live as many days as heaven grants me. Without the incense, let the physicians brew medicine instead — one way or another I will manage."
Jiang Mi knew that medicine carried its own toll. Too much of it would not benefit her aunt's illness.
"Aunt, what about trying a calming incense? It also aids sleep, and it has been used for many years without ill effect..."
Before she had even finished, the Empress Dowager recoiled as though from a snake, pushing her away. "No, no, no. I will not use calming incense. Take it away. Do not let me see that thing!"
Jiang Mi stumbled from the push and nearly fell. Mama Cui caught her from behind. "Miss, be careful."
Once Jiang Mi had steadied herself, Mama Cui went to the Empress Dowager and gently soothed her. "It was Miss who spoke carelessly, Empress Dowager. There will be no calming incense, I promise. Please put your mind at rest."
Mama Cui took a porcelain bottle and fed the Empress Dowager a small pill, then had Qingxue bring warm water to help her swallow it.
Jiang Mi stood helplessly to the side, watching her aunt lean against Mama Cui with closed eyes, breathing in shallow pulls.
After the space of one cup of tea, the Empress Dowager opened her tired eyes. Seeing the worry on Jiang Mi's face, she beckoned her over. "Do not worry. Your aunt is only uncomfortable — some rest and I will be fine. Go back to the Marquess of Cheng'en's manor now."
Mama Cui, seeing Jiang Mi had not moved, said gently, "Third Miss — the Empress Dowager needs to sleep."
Jiang Mi slowly let go of her aunt's hand.
Mama Cui walked her to the door of the inner chamber. Jiang Mi asked, "Mama Cui — what just happened to my aunt? Has a physician seen her?" This was different from when her aunt's old heart complaint flared.
Mama Cui could not say. She answered helplessly, "There are things you need not ask about, Miss. Do not mention calming incense before the Empress Dowager in future. This old servant will take good care of her. Please go home and do not worry."
How was Jiang Mi to not worry?
Why could calming incense not be mentioned? It was the most ordinary fragrance imaginable.
How many secrets was her aunt carrying?
Jiang Mi left the palace and arrived back at the Marquess of Cheng'en's manor near dusk. She had barely stepped into Yuanzhi Courtyard when she sent Xia Ruo to find the medical attendant.
She held Mianmian in her arms to settle the unease in her heart.
About the time it took to burn a stick of incense, she saw the medical attendant following Xia Ruo in.
Jiang Mi rose. "Calling you here in such a hurry — I am sorry for the trouble. Please forgive me."
The attendant curtseyed. "Miss need not stand on ceremony. I am in the employ of Su-shi's household. You may call on me for anything."
Jiang Mi dispensed with further pleasantries. She wrote down all the aromatic herbs and ingredients that Qingxue had mentioned, then passed the list to the attendant. "Among these — besides the costus root and rose dew you warned me about before, are any of the others incompatible with each other? Or harmful to the mind?"
The attendant studied the list carefully. "At first glance there is nothing obviously wrong. But if you are concerned, Miss, I could have my younger apprentice-brother take a look — he has a particular gift with aromatic remedies."
Jiang Mi nodded. "I would be grateful."
The attendant added, "My apprentice-brother is in the Jiangnan region, though he travels often and may not receive a letter promptly. You may need to wait a little while. But I have heard that Su-shi will soon be taking Fourth Miss and the young master back to her family home in Jiangnan to celebrate her grandmother's birthday. If you sent someone to help find my apprentice-brother through Su-shi's people there, it might be faster."
Jiang Mi was surprised. "Su-shi and the others are going to Jiangnan?"
The attendant said, "Yes. I imagine Su-shi simply has not had a chance to tell you yet, as you were in the palace today. In fact, if you were to go yourself and find my apprentice-brother in person, he could answer your questions directly on the spot — that would be far better than exchanging letters."

chapters.title: Sixty nine
chapters.content:
Jiangnan — a place she had longed for a very long time.
When the medical attendant mentioned that Su-shi was taking Jiang Rong and the others back to her family home to celebrate a birthday, a thought began to take root in Jiang Mi's heart.
She wanted to go with them.
For one thing, she did not know what Xiao Huaiyan might do next. If he were to use her aunt's authority to summon her to the palace again, she truly did not know how to manage it. If she was in Jiangnan, she could avoid the situation for a while.
By the time Elder Sister Duan Fei and Noble Consort Rou entered the palace, his anger would have had time to cool. With poised, graceful beauties and gentle, pliant ones keeping him company, he would have no attention to spare for her. Jiang Mi felt that his present stubbornness and refusal to let her go must stem from his anger at being deceived. Given time, it would fade.
For another, she had too many misgivings about the incense matter and wanted to find the medical attendant's apprentice-brother to get to the bottom of it. Her aunt had been badly affected by the incense — she hoped he might have some remedy that could truly help her sleep while also nourishing her health. Perhaps in this life her aunt would not leave the world so early.
These thoughts circled in her mind all night. The next morning Jiang Mi went to Su-shi's courtyard.
She explained her purpose.
Su-shi was both surprised and delighted. "If you are willing to come along, Tangtan, that would be wonderful. Only — you are in the middle of arranging your wedding, and the journey to Jiangnan takes at least three months at the least. I do not know whether the Duke Zhen State's manor will have any objection."
Jiang Mi said, "Do not worry, Su-shi. The wedding date Father and the Grand Princess have settled on will not be so soon. We will be back in time. I will pay a visit to the Young Lord and discuss it with him."
Su-shi nodded. This was not something she should involve herself in — the wedding arrangements had always been the master's domain, and she had only to prepare Jiang Mi's trousseau. Jiang Mi's mother had left her a dowry, and with Su-shi's own additions, she was determined to send her off in proper splendor.
As for Jiang Mi coming to Jiangnan with them, she was thoroughly pleased. Her family was in trade, and though they had wealth, even wealthy merchants were looked down upon by the capital's nobility. Jiang Mi was the Empress Dowager's beloved niece and soon to be wed to the Young Lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor — her presence in Su-shi's ancestral home would be a great honor.
......
Though custom held that a couple should not meet before marriage, customs were fixed things and people were living ones.
Once Jiang Mi learned of the Young Lord's injury, she wanted to go and call on him.
She had been about to send Qiuyu ahead with a visiting card for Ningzhu — but Ningzhu's card arrived first, inviting her to come and admire the plum blossoms.
Jiang Mi laughed at the coincidence. She had Qiuyu prepare a selection of nourishing medicines and supplements, and set off for the Duke Zhen State's manor.
She first paid her respects to the Grand Princess in Rong'an Hall. The Grand Princess teased her warmly for a while, and then Xue Ningzhu dragged her off toward the Young Lord's courtyard.
Xue Ningzhu linked her arm through Jiang Mi's. "Sister Jiang, it wasn't that I didn't tell you my brother was hurt. It was my brother who wouldn't let me say anything — he said you would worry."
"If that annoying woman weren't always coming around, I wouldn't have called you over either. Sister Jiang, I only want you to be my sister-in-law. I don't recognize anyone else." Xue Ningzhu said, her lower lip pushed out.
Jiang Mi listened quietly without speaking.
In truth, the relationship between herself and the Young Lord was closer to that of allies. She had worried from the start of the bestowal plan that it might ruin his chances at a good match — but the Young Lord had said he had feelings for her, and wanted to help. She wanted to give something back for that. And yet if the Young Lord's feelings were to change, if some other bond were to form — she would not stand in his way.
As the two of them reached the doorway, they heard a coy, laughing voice from inside.
"Young Lord Xue, have you forgotten that you left something with me?"
Jiang Mi recognized Princess An'yang's voice. She stopped and pulled Xue Ningzhu's arm to signal her to be quiet.
"What thing?" Xue Jinglin's voice sounded somewhat indifferent.
An'yang drew a sachet from her sleeve and dangled it in front of him with evident satisfaction. "Recognize it now?"
Xue Jinglin reached out to take it. An'yang stepped back a few paces. "Don't rush. Promise to convalesce at the villa in the capital's outskirts, and I will give it back. Otherwise — consider it a gift to me."
Leaning against the headboard, Xue Jinglin's expression was cold. He pushed back the quilt and moved to stand — but the wound on his chest pulled, setting off a coughing fit.
An'yang hurried over to support him. "What are you rushing for? Your wound is already seeping blood again. It is only a sachet — I was only teasing you. Is it worth all this?"
Xue Jinglin pushed An'yang's hand away with displeasure. "Your Highness, return the sachet."
"This sachet was given to me by someone I hold dear. There is no use in Your Highness keeping it."
An'yang's expression changed. "Someone you hold dear? Who?"
She paused, her eyes narrowing. A look of displeasure crossed her face. "Could it be Jiang Mi?"
An'yang gripped the sachet tight. It felt unbearably sharp against her palm. She had thought it was something Xue Jinglin carried out of habit and had wanted to keep it. But Xue Jinglin was saying Jiang Mi had given it to him?
On the night of the lantern festival, she had been in her carriage watching the chaos below when she saw Xue Jinglin protecting a group of children who had become separated from their families. She had invited them into the carriage to shelter them. Then rebels had attacked the carriage, and Xue Jinglin had taken a blade for her that would otherwise have struck her.
She had thought that was a sign — their fate together.
When he had been the Prince of Qi's companion, she had found ways to 'run into' him back then. But she had been too young at the time — everyone only ever treated her like a little sister.
She remembered a small palace maid once telling her she had overheard that the Emperor had intended to betroth her to Xue Jinglin — and that it was only Jiang Mi's interference that had led the Emperor to grant the marriage bestowal instead.
In the imperial household, once you were of different surnames, there was no question of seniority by relation. What she wanted, she would find a way to get.
Xue Jinglin said, "Your Highness would do well not to make me resort to force."
An'yang smiled, clutched the sachet tight in both hands, and tore it with vicious force. The brocade ripped apart.
She threw the shreds to the floor. "This sort of sachet has dirtied this Princess's hands. Here — take it back. Next time Young Lord Xue accepts a sachet, do take a good look at where it comes from."
An'yang softened her voice. "I also know how to embroider sachets. One day..."
The words died in her throat. Xue Jinglin's cold gaze made her stumble back several steps.
For just a moment, An'yang felt as though a hand had closed around her throat.
Xue Jinglin said in a freezing voice, "Your Highness may leave. I deeply regret my impulsive meddling that day."
An'yang was so furious she was speechless. She felt utterly wronged and could do nothing but bite her lip and run out of the room.
As she came through the door, she came face to face with Jiang Mi and Xue Ningzhu.
An'yang stared at Jiang Mi with red-rimmed eyes and spat, "Don't be so pleased with yourself! We will see about this!"
With her chin up, she swept away with her attendants.
Xue Jinglin pulled on his blue robe and picked up the ruined sachet from the floor. He was brushing off the dust when someone appeared in the doorway.
Against the light, he tilted his head slightly and saw the jewel-bright young woman walking toward him through the glow, like a celestial maiden descending from heaven.
Another figure came rushing forward and took his arm. "Young Lord Brother, why have you gotten out of bed? You are still unwell!"
Xue Jinglin rose slowly and slipped the sachet into his sleeve. He soothed Xue Ningzhu. "Ningzhu, I am fine. I need a few words with your Sister Jiang — go and play outside for a little while."
Xue Ningzhu nodded obediently, helped him sit back on the bed, and went out.
Xue Jinglin looked at the young woman before him — she seemed a little ill at ease. "Miss Jiang, please sit down."
Jiang Mi stood at the bedside. "I did not know you were injured. I have not come to see you this whole time. Are you feeling better?"
Xue Jinglin said with a smile, "Only a small wound — not worth mentioning. That is why I did not let Miss Jiang know. Please do not hold it against me."
Jiang Mi shook her head.
Xue Jinglin spoke first. "I expect you had a bit of a spectacle just now. Do not worry — our marriage will not change. My feelings will not change either."
Jiang Mi did not know what to say. She knew the Young Lord was a good man — gentle and perceptive, kind to her. She wanted to try to care for him in return, to answer his feelings.
"Young Lord Xue — I..." Jiang Mi pressed her lips together. "I am worried this marriage will implicate you and the Duke Zhen State's manor. I..."
Xue Jinglin cut her off. "Grandmother has told me — the wedding date has been set for the fifth month. Miss Jiang, do not let your thoughts run away with you."
Jiang Mi was quiet for a moment. "Young Lord Xue — Su-shi will be going to Jiangnan soon to celebrate her family's elder's birthday. I am thinking of going with her. Things here in the capital are unsettled — perhaps a spell away in Jiangnan would help. And it would give the Young Lord some time to think things through as well."
Something moved in Xue Jinglin's eyes. "Jiangnan? It is a long way. Though Su-shi will bring attendants, I am not easy in my mind about it. What if I go with you and bring some of my men along as escort? I could use the opportunity to get away from certain unwanted parties myself. The wedding is in the fifth month — there and back, we have enough time."
This was more than Jiang Mi had expected. She had only meant to tell the Young Lord of her plans — she had not anticipated that he would want to come along.
"Is your wound truly all right for travel, Young Lord?"
Xue Jinglin smiled. "It will be fine. From the capital to Jiangnan we can take the water route by boat — a gentle journey, just right for convalescence."
......
In the imperial study, Xiao Huaiyan was reviewing a series of confidential letters.
Pei Chi stood below and reported, "Your Majesty — Lord Xue Jingyuan's last letter was sent from Yangzhou. Since then there has been no word from him at all. The contact who went to their meeting point found no trace. Only that he was known to have gone out once — and then he vanished."
Xiao Huaiyan was reading Xue Jingyuan's final letter. In it, he had written that he had found evidence of a private army being concealed in the Jiangnan region. It was no simple militia — the troops were disciplined and trained, resembling a standing military force.
And after that letter, Xue Jingyuan had disappeared.
If what Xue Jingyuan had described was true, then elements within the court had colluded with remnants of the rebel faction and were secretly maintaining a private army — plotting insurrection.
The regular army itself might even have been infiltrated.
Pei Chi said, "This subject will send more men to find Lord Xue."
Gu Chang stepped forward and volunteered. "This subject requests to go to Jiangnan to find Lord Xue and investigate the rebel conspiracy."
Xiao Huaiyan pressed his hand on the letter. His gaze drifted to a point beyond the room, and a fragment of memory surfaced — one that should not have been there.
The fifth year of Yuanxi: the rebels hiding in Jiangnan raised their banner, claiming to be the rightful line, and launched a full insurrection. At the same time, the border at Yunzhou was under assault from foreign forces. He had led the army himself.
Before leaving, he had gone to see Jiang Mi, who was ill. He told her to wait for his return.
The memory came sudden and chaotic — a battlefield of carnage, traitors selling out the realm, all of them deserving death.
Xiao Huaiyan pressed his fingers to his temple, trying to push further into the memory. But however hard he tried, he could not recover what had happened after.
He wanted to know: had Jiang Mi still been there when he returned?

chapters.title: Seventy
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi took Qiuyu's hand and stepped aboard the Su family's merchant vessel. She stood on the deck looking at the light playing on the water, the cool wind on her face, and felt no cold at all — only a lightness in her heart, all the stale and heavy air swept clean from her chest.
She was going to Jiangnan.
Jiangnan — that place of misty rain and hazy waters, of scenery said to be the finest in the realm. She was going to see it with her own eyes at last.
"The bow is windy. Mind you do not catch cold."
A clear, pleasant voice came from behind her.
Jiang Mi turned and lifted her veiled hat, smiling at the newcomer. "Young Lord Xue, you are here."
Xue Jinglin nodded. "I had my guards help bring all the baggage aboard, and then asked them to assist Su-shi's party with some of their things as well."
Jiang Mi smiled. "Su-shi has been married in the capital so many years — it is rare for her to go back to Jiangnan. She has brought a great deal to take home with her, and your eldest aunt-by-marriage added several trunks of gifts from the other branch as well."
She thanked him. "That was too much trouble for the Young Lord."
Xue Jinglin said with a laugh, "I am the one using your family's boat. It is I who have gotten the better of the arrangement — there is nothing to thank me for."
While they were talking, Su-shi came out with Jiang Rong and Jiang Xuan.
Jiang Xuan shook free of Jiang Rong's hand and ran to Jiang Mi. "Third Sister, Third Sister — I brought a new chess set onto the boat. Will you play with me? I have learned a new opening. This time I definitely will not lose so quickly."
Jiang Rong tried to catch hold of him. "Xuan-er, behave yourself — who runs straight to chess the moment they're on a boat? Haven't you played enough at home?"
Jiang Xuan dodged away and hid behind Jiang Mi, sticking his tongue out at Jiang Rong. "I don't want to play with you. I want to play with Third Sister."
Jiang Rong's face went red with indignation.
Su-shi gave the nursemaid a look, signaling her to take Xuan-er back inside.
Jiang Mi patted Jiang Xuan's head. "All right — Elder Sister will play chess with you. But we have to ask Fourth Sister to join us too. And after chess, Xuan-er must recite his lessons for Elder Sister. Agreed?"
Jiang Xuan hesitated, then nodded.
Jiang Mi took Jiang Xuan's small hand and smiled at Su-shi and Xue Jinglin. "Su-shi, Young Lord — I will take Xuan-er inside first."
Both nodded.
Jiang Mi called over her shoulder, "A'Rong, come on."
Jiang Rong beamed and fell into step beside her, chatting quietly.
After Jiang Mi and the children had gone, Su-shi exchanged a few polite words with Xue Jinglin and suggested he go and rest.
At that moment a detachment of soldiers came aboard to carry out an inspection. Su-shi hurried off to see to things.
......
The great vessel began to move, gliding slowly southward.
Jiang Mi spent the afternoon with Jiang Rong and Jiang Xuan. Near dusk she finally returned to her own cabin.
She raised her aching arm and accepted the cup of hot tea Qiuyu handed her.
She heard Xia Ruo muttering quietly to herself as she sorted through the luggage. "That's strange — there's an extra trunk here. I was sure there were exactly so many. Where did this empty one come from? Did the serving women mix them up?"
Jiang Mi looked over. "What is it?"
Xia Ruo said, "Miss, there's an extra trunk. But I clearly remember the count, and I organized everything by category — it cannot be a mistake."
Qiuyu said, "Perhaps you miscounted."
Xia Ruo opened the extra trunk. "Every item Miss wanted to bring has been sorted into its category. The count was exact. There is no way I could be wrong."
Jiang Mi went over. The inside of the trunk's base had a few smears of mud on it. She frowned and said to Xia Ruo, "Ask a serving woman to come and take this trunk away."
Xia Ruo said, "Yes, Miss."
Jiang Mi asked Qiuyu, "After I went to Xuan-er's cabin, was there anything unusual on the boat?"
Qiuyu said, "Soldiers came aboard for a routine inspection. But when they heard this was a Jiang family boat and learned that you and the Young Lord were aboard, they only had a brief look and let it through."
The small incident drew no further thought from anyone.
The great vessel moved on the water for three days.
At first Jiang Mi was content, but as time went on she developed a mild seasickness and spent most of her time resting in her cabin.
When Jiang Rong saw she had been lying down too long, she would come and take her up to the deck for a short walk.
Jiang Mi stood in the breeze, and the heaviness in her chest eased a little.
She looked out at the scenery along the riverbanks and listened to Jiang Rong tell stories about her maternal grandparents' home. Jiang Rong had only been there once as a small child, and much of the memory was blurry — but she remembered that in Jinling there was a beautiful river called the Qinhuai.
Jiang Rong said, "Elder Sister, I remember Second Cousin taking me out on a pleasure boat on the Qinhuai. All these flower boats came drifting over, and some pretty ladies threw flowers and handkerchiefs at us — but they all landed on Second Cousin. For some reason when we got back, Aunt gave First Cousin a beating."
Jiang Mi covered her mouth, laughing. That cousin of hers deserved the beating. What sort of scoundrel took a small child onto a flower boat?
She was about to say something when she heard children squabbling nearby.
She had thought it was Jiang Xuan. She looked toward the sound and saw, on the deck below, three or four young children chasing and roughhousing. Among them, a girl of three or four pushed one of her playmates down and then stood on the fallen child's hand, making them wail.
A serving woman quickly came and took the little girl away. In the instant before the girl disappeared into the cabin, Jiang Mi got a clear look at her face. She felt a flicker of recognition — as though she had seen this child somewhere before — but could not place it.
"What is it, Elder Sister?" Jiang Rong noticed Jiang Mi staring.
Jiang Mi said, puzzled, "How are there so many small children on the boat?"
Jiang Rong said, "Some are the children of the Jiangnan serving women who came along — they wanted to bring them back to see the old family home. And some are probably children of the boat crew."
Back in her cabin, Jiang Mi rested for a while and fell into a dream.
She was eleven or twelve. She was accompanying her aunt through the imperial garden when they happened upon the Prince of Qi and Princess Anhui, a pair of siblings. She greeted them with a bow, and as she raised her eyes she saw the Prince of Qi's face.
He was smiling at her.
The surroundings shifted and blurred, and in an instant the Prince of Qi became a small boy standing at the edge of a lake, also smiling at her.
The little boy's face and the little girl's face from the deck began to slowly overlap.
Jiang Mi jolted awake and sat straight up.
Qiuyu was startled and hurried over. "Miss, what is it? Did you have a nightmare?"
Jiang Mi rubbed her temple. "I am fine. Bring me a cup of tea."
Qiuyu nodded and went to pour it.
Jiang Mi took the hot cup and blew on it, drinking slowly. The chill that had come over her settled down.
She was probably just overtired. Seeing things.
The following day, Xue Jinglin heard that Jiang Mi's seasickness seemed to have worsened. He came to see how she was faring.
On the boat, among people they knew, there was less need to be so strict about the usual proprieties between men and women.
Xue Jinglin said, "My cook knows how to make dishes with sour, sweet, and spicy flavors. Let me have him make a few for you to try — see which sits best. You cannot go on eating nothing with seasickness; you will not be able to hold up."
Jiang Mi said, "There is really no need to go to all that trouble. I will just have some plain congee. I truly cannot stomach anything else."
Xue Jinglin said, "It is no trouble. I brought all these people onto the boat — of course they should be put to proper use."
But in the end she could not eat the dishes. Qiuyu reported that after the cook had carried them away, a few of the children on the boat had claimed several of the plates.
Jiang Mi asked Xue Jinglin, "Did any of your serving women bring small children aboard?"
Xue Jinglin looked at her steadily, puzzled. "What children?"
Jiang Mi shook her head. "Never mind."
She was probably overthinking it.
......
Deep in the night, Jiang Mi — a light sleeper at the best of times — was suddenly jolted by the feeling of something colliding with the boat. She nearly fell from her bed.
The collisions continued in rapid succession, rocking the vessel badly.
Screams cut through the night air outside.
Qiuyu came running in, panicked. "Miss, this is terrible — we have been hit by river pirates! They are ramming the hull. The Young Lord is leading the guards to hold them off. He says you and Su-shi should take the small boat and leave first — if the pirates get aboard, it will be too late."
Jiang Mi dressed quickly and ran out with Qiuyu. She came upon Xue Jinglin, sword in hand, coming toward her with an urgent expression. "Miss Jiang — I will see you to the boat. I have left men on it and they will protect you. The pirates are after the cargo of the merchant vessel. I have also made arrangements for Su-shi's party — they only need to take the small boat and someone will meet them. The fifth month wedding is well within reach from there and back."
Xue Jinglin helped Jiang Mi onto the small boat. His guards climbed in after her. Just as the boat was about to be pushed off, Jiang Mi looked back in urgent distress. "Young Lord, are you not coming with us?"
Xue Jinglin said, "I have a way out arranged. Miss Jiang, do not worry. These pirates only want money — they would not dare face the court openly. Once you and Su-shi's party are together, someone will escort you to Jinling. I will come and find you there."
Hearing the approaching footsteps behind him growing louder, Xue Jinglin pushed the small boat away and signaled his men to row Jiang Mi clear.
Xue Jinglin gripped his bloodied sword and turned back to the main vessel. His remaining guards were still fighting the pirates.
He frowned and was about to call out when his most trusted guard came up close. "Young Lord — trouble. There is another group appearing."
......
The small boat drifted on the river, moving further and further from the merchant vessel. Jiang Mi stood and watched the larger ship burning, her heart frantic.
How had river pirates appeared out of nowhere?
She did not know whether Su-shi, Jiang Rong, and Jiang Xuan had managed to get away.
One of Xue Jinglin's guards said, "Miss, please do not worry. We will escort you to meet with the others."
In the dark, deep water, a pair of hands emerged and gripped the side of the small boat. A black-clad figure pulled himself up without a sound and struck the guards unconscious one by one.
Jiang Mi sensed something wrong. She was turning around when she felt a sharp pain at her neck and lost consciousness.
When awareness returned, she found herself lying in a bed.
The moment she moved, she heard footsteps approaching from a distance.
Jiang Mi quietly closed her hand around the gold hairpin. She felt the person draw closer, then stop at the bedside.
She held her spine rigid and did not breathe.
As the figure bent down toward her, Jiang Mi drove the hairpin toward them with every last ounce of her strength.
The figure sidestepped neatly. A startled voice rang out, "You strike so hard? Who taught you that, Miss Jiang?"
Jiang Mi's wrist was caught — and she froze.
That voice. She knew that voice. It was a woman's voice.
She looked up and found Gu Xuan smiling brilliantly down at her.
Gu Xuan greeted her cheerfully. "Miss Jiang — it has been a while!"
Jiang Mi felt as though she might be dreaming. How was Gu Xuan here?
Gu Xuan plucked the hairpin from her hand and reassured her. "Miss Jiang, do not be afraid. I am not one of the bad people. I just happened to be passing by and rescued you. Do not believe me? Ask His Majesty."
Jiang Mi, belatedly, looked past Gu Xuan to the figure standing not far behind her. A man stood there — and by his bearing, he was someone who had no business being in this place at all.

chapters.title: Seventy one
chapters.content:
Whatever Jiang Mi's suspicions might be, Xiao Huaiyan's rank demanded she rise and greet him.
He saw her move and said, "We are not in the palace. There is no need for ceremony."
Jiang Mi paused, glanced at Gu Xuan, and still pushed through the pain in her neck to stand and curtsey.
Xiao Huaiyan's brow creased.
Gu Xuan quickly moved to support her. "Miss Jiang — His Majesty said there is no need to stand on ceremony. Are you feeling any discomfort?"
Jiang Mi shook her head. "I am all right. Thank you for saving me, Sister Xuan."
Gu Xuan could not very well accept the credit — she was acting on orders, after all. She gave an awkward little smile. "It was only because His Majesty happened to be passing through that we were able to reach you in time."
Jiang Mi looked toward Xiao Huaiyan. Had she received his rescue again?
Her worry for her family had been suppressing every other feeling, and she could not afford to dwell on ceremony. She asked urgently, "Sister Xuan — when you rescued me, did you see anyone else nearby? Our merchant vessel was attacked by river pirates, and after everyone scattered I have no idea what became of them. And Young Lord Xue of the Duke Zhen State's manor was still on the merchant ship holding off the pirates — I do not know whether he is safe."
Gu Xuan's right eye gave an involuntary twitch. She thought: Miss Jiang, you have asked entirely the wrong person.
Before Gu Xuan could answer, Xiao Huaiyan spoke. "Your family has taken another vessel and is on its way to Jiangnan. As for Xue Jinglin — he drove off most of the pirates and has also left by boat. You, however, will have to travel with us for the time being."
Seeing the anxiety still written across Jiang Mi's face, Xiao Huaiyan restrained the impulse to go to her and said curtly, "Gu Xuan, look after her."
He turned and walked away.
His departure noticeably relaxed the air in the room.
Jiang Mi asked Gu Xuan, "Sister Xuan — what exactly happened?"
Gu Xuan said, "When we arrived, the pirates were trying to take you away. We got to you first. While you were unconscious, official vessels were already dispatched to intercept the Jiang party — everyone is safe. Young Lord Xue's side lost some of his guards, but he himself is all right. Some of the older servants and children on the merchant ship were not so fortunate — there were deaths, injuries, and some who are still missing — but the local authorities have taken charge of that. You need not worry."
Jiang Mi finally let out a full breath. "Sister Xuan — is there any way to send me to rejoin my family? They will be searching for me and worrying."
Gu Xuan looked troubled. "Miss Jiang — you understand, I am sure, that His Majesty's movements outside the palace must remain secret. Not even the smallest slip can be afforded. So you will have to bear with us for now. I have already sent word to Su-shi explaining that you have been rescued and are safe, and that you will be brought to Jinling to reunite with her party in due course."
Jiang Mi was surprised. "His Majesty is also going to Jiangnan?"
Gu Xuan nodded. "When we are closer to Jiangnan, you can ask His Majesty to arrange an escort to take you to Jinling."
She added, "Miss Jiang, do not overthink things. Lie down and rest first. What will be will be. Let me go and bring you something to eat."
Jiang Mi had no appetite and was about to decline, but Gu Xuan had already gone.
Jiang Mi sat quietly at the edge of the bed, still not quite caught up to herself.
Of all the ways her Jiangnan journey could have gone, how had it come to this within only a few days?
She had meant to avoid Xiao Huaiyan — meant to travel south to clear her head and find a physician. She had never imagined she would be taken by pirates and rescued by Sister Xuan. And Sister Xuan was apparently travelling with Xiao Huaiyan toward Jiangnan.
In her last life, Sister Xuan had been on the verge of entering the palace as Consort Duan by now. And she had never heard any news of Xiao Huaiyan leaving the capital.
Why was this life not matching her memories?
Gu Xuan returned before long, a maidservant behind her carrying a food box.
The maid set out a bowl of chicken broth, a steamed shad fish, a plate of pork in hibiscus batter, a dish of stir-fried wild mushrooms, braised bamboo shoots, and radish cake. Last came a bowl of soft, yielding congee.
Gu Xuan said apologetically, "We prepared things rather quickly for you, Miss Jiang — there was not much to choose from. I hope it suits your taste."
Jiang Mi looked at the spread from her seat. "Sister Xuan, this is far too much. I could never finish all of this."
"Never mind — eat whatever you can manage. You've had a frightening ordeal. You need to replenish yourself."
Unable to refuse such warmth, Jiang Mi ladled some congee and slowly began to eat.
Gu Xuan propped her chin on her hand and watched with amused interest.
"Miss Jiang — just call me A'Xuan. All this 'Sister Xuan, Sister Xuan' sounds too formal. A'Xuan is friendlier."
Jiang Mi swallowed a small piece of the hibiscus pork. She nodded. "My pet name is Tangtan. If A'Xuan-jie does not mind, you can call me that."
Gu Xuan grinned. "Tangtan! Even the name is sweet."
Sweet as a person, too. Soft and warm. You could not help liking her just looking at her.
No wonder His Majesty had gone to such deliberate lengths to maneuver the girl onto the boat. She herself had only managed to hold the girl for a moment after pulling her from the water, and the Emperor had already swept her away. She had watched it happen.
Jiang Mi felt a little pink and put her head down to concentrate on her congee.
Gu Xuan watched her eat in small, careful bites and grew worried she was not getting enough.
She ladled some broth. "Tangtan, don't just stick to the congee — have some soup, a bit of the dishes. And this radish cake is quite good."
Jiang Mi shook her head. "A'Xuan-jie, I can serve myself. I cannot eat this much — won't you eat with me?"
She offered a piece of radish cake to Gu Xuan. Gu Xuan was not the sort to stand on ceremony and sat down to share the meal.
When she was satisfied that Jiang Mi had truly eaten enough, Gu Xuan had the maid clear the table.
"Tangtan, you eat so little." Gu Xuan shook her head. "No wonder you are so slender."
Jiang Mi smiled softly.
Gu Xuan said, "Rest for a while. I will have hot water prepared — a proper soak will do you good. Do not stand on ceremony. If you need anything, just say so. I will take good care of you."
Jiang Mi felt a warmth she could not quite express. A'Xuan-jie was exactly the same as she remembered from her last life — always looking after her.
"Thank you, A'Xuan-jie. You have been here too long on my account — you should go back to His Majesty." She did not know what Xiao Huaiyan had come to Jiangnan for, but in her last life he had always relied on Sister Xuan. She should not take up too much of her time.
Gu Xuan thought about it and admitted she did have something to report to the Emperor. Though how had Tangtan sensed that? Was she being that obvious?
She was still turning it over in her mind as she came out onto the deck and found a man standing in the night.
Gu Xuan approached carefully and gave a bow to the silhouetted figure. "Your Majesty — Tangtan ate only a few bites of the hibiscus pork, half a small bowl of congee, and a few mouthfuls of the braised bamboo. She did not touch the stir-fried mushrooms. The radish cake — I ate that."
Xiao Huaiyan's expression was hidden in the darkness.
"What did you call her?"
Gu Xuan blinked. "Miss Jiang asked me to use her pet name — Tangtan."
Xiao Huaiyan fell quiet again. Her habits were identical to those in the dream — still hated anything in the mushroom family, still partial to the hibiscus pork.
After a moment of silence, Gu Xuan ventured, "Your Majesty, if there is nothing further, this subject will take her leave. Miss Jiang needs a change of clothes sent to her while she bathes."
Xiao Huaiyan turned, his voice carrying a cooler note. "Gu Xuan — have you located the pirates' base? Have you made contact with Gu Chang? Is there truly nothing else for you to do?"
Gu Xuan straightened. "This subject will go and make contact at once."
She withdrew, and sent a maid to deliver the clothes instead.
......
Jiang Mi lay in the warm bath and felt the comfort of it seep into her. The ache in her neck was still there, but she thought pressing on it herself might ease it — she did not want to trouble anyone else.
She splashed water on her face to chase away the drowsiness threatening to pull her under. She could not fall asleep in the bath. She was not well to begin with, and if she caught a chill on top of it, she would be in an even worse state.
She heard footsteps come into the room. Through the screen and the steam she could not make out the shape clearly.
"A'Xuan-jie, is that you?"
Silence from behind the screen.
Jiang Mi shifted in the tub, uneasy. She looked toward the screen and asked more sharply, "Who is there?"
"It — it is only me, miss. Xiang Yun. I have been sent by Miss Gu to bring your clothes."
The voice was the same maid as earlier. Jiang Mi let out a breath.
"Come in, then." She had been soaking long enough — she should not stay in any longer.
The maid cast a terrified glance sideways at the man beside her. He gave a slight nod, and she lowered her head and carried the clothes around the screen.
Xiao Huaiyan listened to the sounds of water, watching the silhouette projected through the screen as it rose from the tub.
Every ripple of water followed the movement of her form.
The moment she stepped out of the tub and was wrapped in the bathing cloth, Xiao Huaiyan's gaze deepened.
The soft rustle of clothing. Her quiet murmurs to the maid. Something in the air turned faintly, wordlessly suggestive.
The maid, having tied Jiang Mi's sash, watched her walk toward the edge of the screen with quiet dread.
Jiang Mi found no one on the other side. She turned back to the maid. "Did you come with A'Xuan-jie?"
The maid steadied herself. "Yes, miss. Miss Gu must have had something to attend to and left already."
Jiang Mi nodded and asked no further questions.
The maid gathered the things and withdrew.
Jiang Mi lay back down on the bed. She turned from side to side, unable to sleep.
This water journey to Jiangnan would take over a month. Was she really going to spend all that time on this ship in Xiao Huaiyan's company?
She should stay in her room as much as possible.
Though she was a little worried — would the seasickness get worse? She had been going up to the deck before for air, which had helped. Well. She would just have to endure it.

chapters.title: Seventy two
chapters.content:
The next morning Jiang Mi woke and moved, and found that the pain in her neck had spread to her shoulder.
She kneaded it for a while before slowly getting out of bed.
The light outside was already bright. She had no idea what time it was.
Xiang Yun, who had heard her stir, came in to help her wash and dress.
"What is your name?" Jiang Mi asked.
"Xiang Yun, miss," the maid replied.
Xiang Yun brought breakfast and set it out. "Would you like to go out for some air after you eat, miss?"
Jiang Mi shook her head. "I am a little tired. I will rest in the room."
Xiang Yun said nothing more.
Jiang Mi pressed at her shoulder again. The ache had not eased.
Just then, music drifted in from outside — a guqin, clear and pure as meltwater from a snowbound mountain, a sound that made one forget one's troubles.
Was Sister Xuan playing outside?
Jiang Mi went to the window and opened it. A soft breeze touched her face. She looked out.
Xiao Huaiyan, in a white brocade robe, was playing the guqin with effortless ease. The wind stirred his robes around him — he had the unhurried bearing of an exiled immortal descended to the mortal world.
Xiao Huaiyan raised his eyes abruptly and looked directly at Jiang Mi.
Their gazes met.
Jiang Mi's heart jolted. She slapped the window shut.
......
Outside, the music paused — then resumed.
The quick motion of closing the window had pulled at Jiang Mi's shoulder, and her face showed a flash of pain.
The ache had not been helped by her kneading. The clear strains of the qin drifting in from outside seemed to be making her thoughts more, not less, disordered.
It was a tranquil piece of music. It should have brought calm.
Jiang Mi stood at the window in a tangle of feeling as the melody gradually opened and spread, like warm spring wind carrying a trace of heat.
She came back to herself and thought to ask Xiang Yun where Sister Xuan was — but when she turned, she found the breakfast things had been cleared and Xiang Yun had slipped out.
Jiang Mi leaned back on the bed and, with the music in her ears, drifted off without realizing it.
When she woke again, the music outside had stopped.
Had Xiao Huaiyan gone?
Xiang Yun came in and curtseyed. "Miss — it is time for the midday meal. They are serving in the main hall."
Jiang Mi found it odd that the food was not being brought to her room in a box — but she was a guest on someone else's ship, and she ought to follow their customs.
She followed Xiang Yun out.
Only now, emerging from her room, did she see the ship clearly. It looked much like any merchant vessel from the outside, but the guards patrolling and standing watch had a sharpness and discipline that gave it away.
She looked toward the deck. It was empty.
Jiang Mi followed Xiang Yun to a door. Xiang Yun opened it and said respectfully, "Miss, please come in."
Jiang Mi went in and found Xiao Huaiyan — not unexpectedly.
He was still in the white brocade robe, jade crown in his hair, his bearing refined and remote.
The old guqin had been set on a stand beside him.
Xiao Huaiyan was looking over a letter. He glanced up at Jiang Mi, set the letter aside, and said to Chengzhong, "Bring the meal in."
Chengzhong bowed and withdrew, giving Jiang Mi a small bow as he passed.
Only Jiang Mi and Xiao Huaiyan remained in the room.
Jiang Mi curtseyed. "Long life and health to Your Majesty."
Xiao Huaiyan said, "I have already said there is no need for ceremony. Out here, I am traveling under the name Liu Xingze — of the Liu family. You may call me Cousin, or simply Elder Brother."
Jiang Mi knew that Xiao Huaiyan's mother's family name was Liu. He had a real cousin in Noble Consort Rou. Calling him Cousin would be presuming on a connection she did not have. In her last life she had done exactly that — shameless and thick-skinned, she had called him Cousin and been quietly mocked behind her back for it. On what grounds was she anyone's cousin?
And calling him Elder Brother was entirely out of the question.
Jiang Mi pressed her lips together and said quietly, "Sir."
Well — it was not technically wrong.
Xiao Huaiyan had not heard what he wanted to hear. He felt a small pang, but was not in any hurry.
Let her be.
Chengzhong led the attendants in and set out the midday dishes one by one, hot and steaming.
Xiao Huaiyan rose and moved to the table, then looked at Jiang Mi still standing. "You need not serve. Sit and eat with me."
Jiang Mi hesitated and glanced toward the door.
"What is it?" Xiao Huaiyan asked.
"Why is Sister Xuan not here for the meal?"
He seemed not to have expected that question. His brow creased slightly. "Why would I summon her to eat with me?"
Jiang Mi thought: if Sister Xuan is not coming, then I have even less reason to be here.
Since the day she had changed his dressings in the palace, she had only grown less certain about how to be in Xiao Huaiyan's company. And now after this accidental encounter, receiving his rescue again, the entanglement between them was becoming more and more impossible to unravel.
Seeing his wordless press of fingers to his brow, she had no choice but to pick a seat as far from him as the round table allowed.
Which, on a round table, was not very far at all.
Xiao Huaiyan said nothing and picked up his chopsticks.
Jiang Mi looked at the dishes and found that a good half of them were things she ate regularly.
She helped herself only from the dishes nearest to her. She had little appetite and was satisfied at about seven parts full.
The meal passed in quiet.
Xiao Huaiyan noticed how little she had eaten. "Not to your taste?"
Jiang Mi shook her head. "This subject is full."
He thought of the few dumplings she had managed at the winter solstice. Her stomach had always been weak — she never ate much. He did not press her.
But suddenly the food seemed to lose its flavor for him too. He set down his chopsticks.
Jiang Mi seized the moment and made to rise and take her leave.
Xiao Huaiyan's gaze rested on her face for a moment. He said nothing and let her go.
Jiang Mi quietly exhaled. She was nearly at the door when a low voice came from behind her.
"Wait."
Xiao Huaiyan looked at her back and asked, "What is wrong with your hand?"
From the moment the meal began, he had sensed something off. He had put it down at first to tension making her movements stiff, but when she turned just now he had caught the way her left hand seemed unable to bear any weight.
Something flickered across Jiang Mi's eyes. She had been enduring the pain in her left shoulder the whole time. She had been using her right hand to eat, but every time her shoulder was engaged the dull ache still pulled.
"This subject is fine," she said, keeping her eyes lowered.
Xiao Huaiyan saw the faint trembling at the tips of her left-hand fingers and the way she seemed desperate to leave.
He stood and crossed the room in a few strides, stepping into her path.
"Jiang Mi. I want an honest answer."
She could hear the edge of anger in his voice. The injury was hers — why was he the one who was angry?
She had no choice. "This subject's shoulder is a little uncomfortable. It is nothing serious."
The moment she said it, Xiao Huaiyan raised his hand and placed it on her left shoulder.
Jiang Mi sucked in a sharp breath of pain.
He let go. "And this is what you call nothing serious?"
Xiao Huaiyan took her right wrist. "Come with me."
Jiang Mi stood her ground.
He looked at the alarm on her face and softened his tone slightly. "I am only going to look at the injury."
"Jiang Mi. There is no physician on this ship. If you have damaged bone and leave it too long, it will only get worse. Do you want your shoulder to be ruined?"
Jiang Mi had assumed the pain would pass with rest. She had not wanted to bother anyone and had stayed quiet about it. She had not expected it to keep spreading.
Was it a bone injury causing the pain, as he said?
She looked up. "Your Majesty — could Sister Xuan examine it for me instead?" She knew Sister Xuan had trained in martial arts and would know how to assess an injury. Her neck and shoulder were involved, which meant she would need to open her clothes. She could not have Xiao Huaiyan tend to it.
Xiao Huaiyan refused outright. "Gu Xuan is not here."
In the next cabin along, Gu Xuan, who was eating her meal in perfect peace, suddenly sneezed. She looked up, puzzled. Who was speaking ill of her?
Jiang Mi bit her lower lip. "Can you not simply apply medicine? Is there nothing to ease the pain?" She could manage a while longer and wait until Sister Xuan returned.
Xiao Huaiyan looked at her steadily. "Your injury was likely caused when the pirates knocked you unconscious. In most cases that kind of blow heals on its own. But since the pain has spread to your shoulder, medicine alone will not fix it. I need to determine whether there is bone displacement. Jiang Mi — I understand your hesitation. I will not look. Setting bone does not require looking."

chapters.title: Seventy three
chapters.content:
A black wooden box was pressed into Jiang Mi's hands.
Xiao Huaiyan reached up and drew the ribbon from her hair. Her dark, gleaming hair cascaded down at once.
Before Jiang Mi's alarm could fully form, Xiao Huaiyan took the red ribbon and tied it over his own eyes.
The red against his handsome face gave him an unsettling, almost otherworldly beauty.
Jiang Mi stared. Of all the things she had expected, she had not expected him to blindfold himself.
His hand settled on her shoulder and his fingers moved toward her neck. Feeling her flinch and try to pull away, he said, "Hold still."
His fingertips met the skin of her neck. They moved up and down, and he pressed lightly — and heard her sharp intake of breath.
"Here?" Xiao Huaiyan was, for the first time, uncertain about the right amount of pressure to use.
In the darkness, with all other senses amplified, the skin beneath his hands felt impossibly fine and fragile.
