He eased aside her collar and touched her shoulder. His fingers explored the injury, and his brow drew together. There was a slight misalignment of the joint.
He said, "The longer you leave it the worse it will hurt. Bear with me."
A few careful breaths later, he set the joint. His hands moved, and he felt her trembling.
The pain broke through in a clammy wash of cold sweat. Jiang Mi suppressed herself as long as she could, but in the peak of the agony several small, smothered gasps and shallow breaths escaped.
The soft sounds reached his ears. Xiao Huaiyan's brow pulled tighter. He released his hands and reached out toward her.
"Pass me the white bottle from the box."
Jiang Mi had barely caught her breath. She looked down at the box, opened it, and found several porcelain vials inside.
She passed him the white one.
Xiao Huaiyan reached out to take it, and their fingertips met. Her faintly cool fingers withdrew immediately.
He kept his expression unchanged and tipped the medicine from the bottle, applying it to her injury.
Jiang Mi caught the sharp, pungent smell. Where the medicine touched her skin it stung and burned — and then, remarkably, the pain seemed to lift. Even the reflexive pull from her right hand when she handed him the bottle had not triggered it.
"Have your maid apply this once a day. Rest properly for a while and it will heal."
Xiao Huaiyan drew her collar back into place and passed the bottle to her.
Jiang Mi looked down at the vial now back in her hand and felt strangely disoriented.
This Xiao Huaiyan felt unfamiliar to her.
She quietly put her clothes in order. With each movement she could feel how much lighter her shoulder already was.
She rose. "Thank you, Your Majesty," she said softly.
Xiao Huaiyan gave a short laugh. "If you want to thank me, then untie this ribbon for me."
Jiang Mi looked at his hands, which still had medicine on them, and after a moment's hesitation rose on her toes, lifted her right hand, and slid the red ribbon free.
The trailing ribbon swept lightly across Xiao Huaiyan's face as she pulled it away. His phoenix eyes opened and fixed on her without blinking.
Jiang Mi's expression stiffened. She looked away and closed her hand around the red ribbon, then curtseyed. "This subject takes her leave."
......
After Jiang Mi left, Chengzhong brought a basin of clean water.
Xiao Huaiyan washed his hands unhurriedly, rinsing away the medicine.
In recent days, his dream fragments had been gradually connecting — threads and pieces weaving together into something more coherent, and among them memories he had never expected to see.
These dreams held some answers. Others only left him more puzzled.
All the changes in Jiang Mi — every shift in her — traced back to one moment: the thirteenth day of the ninth month, in the first year of Yuanxi. A bowl of sobering broth.
In the dream, Jiang Mi had also come to bring him that sobering broth. She had crossed a line he guarded jealously — leaning over him, bringing the medicine to his lips, loosening his robes, pulling back the blanket and pressing close — and the Virtuous Dowager Consort had walked in and ruined her reputation.
In this life, Jiang Mi had fed that bowl of broth to a pot of orchids instead.
From that bowl of broth, she had begun to put distance between them.
She carried a secret buried deep inside her, and she had used it to walk away from him step by step.
However desperately he tried to see what came after his march to war in the dream, he could never get there.
But this time the rebel threat had been found early. There would be no parting as there was in the dream.
......
Jiang Mi sat at the edge of the bed looking at the red ribbon in her hand. After she was rescued, the clothing and ornaments she wore had all been provided by Sister Xuan. The only thing on her that was her own was this ribbon.
She felt the complexity of her own expression without being able to name it. Every time she closed her eyes she saw the image of pulling the ribbon from Xiao Huaiyan's face.
Jiang Mi shook her head and lay down, telling herself firmly: sleep. Sleep and when I wake I will be calm again.
The next morning Jiang Mi woke to the sound of the qin again.
She went to the window, but did not push it open. She knew it was Xiao Huaiyan playing.
She stood there quietly listening, and still did not open it.
She did not know how much time passed. She fell asleep leaning in the chair. When she woke, the music had stopped.
A blanket had appeared over her at some point.
For several days in a row, Jiang Mi would wake each morning to the sound of the qin. She would drift in a half-state below the window — staring at nothing, reading, sleeping.
She stayed in her cabin and tended her injury. She did not go out.
In truth, her shoulder was healing quickly. Xiao Huaiyan's realignment and the medicine had taken care of most of the pain.
She simply did not feel like going out.
Then one morning she woke and found the steady music had stopped. The boat had also stopped moving. She heard footsteps outside.
Xiang Yun came in and curtseyed. "Miss — we have reached Linzhou. Would you like to come out and have a look?"
"Linzhou?"
Xiang Yun nodded. "The vessel is stopping here to take on supplies for a few hours. Miss, it is quite lively — there are local people in little boats selling trinkets and things. Would you like to come and see?"
Jiang Mi had been shut in this room for days, growing thoroughly bored, with not even Sister Xuan to talk to.
At Xiang Yun's words, a flicker of interest stirred.
She came out onto the deck. A wind hit her and she felt immediately more awake.
The wind was warmer now than it had been at the start of the journey.
Still, with her constitution, she kept her cape on.
She looked out to find a fleet of small vessels clustering around them. Some were selling river fish and shellfish; others had seasonal vegetables and fresh fruits. One young woman sculled her little boat with a whole cargo of cut flowers, bright and bursting with life.
The flower-selling girl spotted Jiang Mi on the deck and waved with enthusiasm. "Miss! Would you like to buy some flowers? I have winter jasmine, camellia, orchid, and apricot blossom — anything catch your fancy? The road to Jiangnan is long — fresh flowers on a boat make the spirit lighter! Please, miss, buy some flowers!"
Jiang Mi looked at the boat full of blossoms and the girl's cheerful face, and felt genuinely tempted. The journey south was long and the ship was dull — flowers really would be a welcome sight.
But she had no money. She had none of her own things with her.
She gave the flower girl an apologetic shake of her head.
The girl looked a little disappointed, but smiled anyway. "Miss, you are prettier than any flower — since we've met, it must be fate. Here — take this blue blossom as a gift."
Just then Xiao Huaiyan came out onto the deck in a deep navy robe, surrounded by his attendants.
He saw Jiang Mi — and heard the flower girl's words.
He glanced at Chengzhong.
Chengzhong understood at once. He sent a man over, and the entire boatload of flowers was purchased on the spot.
From where she stood, Jiang Mi watched the girl's expression shift from shocked to delighted. The girl took her coins and waved and waved.
Her happiness was contagious. Jiang Mi could not help smiling.
And when Jiang Mi smiled — it was like ice breaking, brilliant and alive.
She turned, and found Xiao Huaiyan walking toward her.
Jiang Mi composed her expression and curtseyed. After a moment's hesitation, she said, "Sir."
Xiao Huaiyan nodded. "I am going into Linzhou town. Would you like to come ashore and have a look?"
If Jiang Mi said she did not want to, she would be lying.
Xiao Huaiyan saw that she had not answered, but he could read the longing in her eyes.
He said, "Go and put on your veiled hat."
Xiang Yun, who was standing nearby, fetched the hat. Jiang Mi followed Xiao Huaiyan off the ship.

chapters.title: Seventy four
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi had assumed Xiao Huaiyan must have important business to attend to on shore, but he simply wandered as the mood took him.
Linzhou sat at the junction of several canal routes — a bustling, prosperous place where merchants came and went. The city was full of noise and life, shops of every kind lining both sides of its streets.
Jiang Mi walked a few steps behind Xiao Huaiyan. She noticed more than a few young women letting their gazes linger on him.
Along the way, two women dropped their handkerchiefs — and came over, faces flushed, to retrieve them and try to strike up a conversation. One tossed a perfume sachet toward him: Xiao Huaiyan simply stopped walking without picking it up, yet the girl still came over blushing to thank him. Another woman contrived to stumble toward him and was quietly deflected by a shadow guard before she got close.
Jiang Mi felt she was seeing something new. The social customs of Linzhou were considerably more forward than other places, and its women considerably bolder. Watching the entertainment playing out around Xiao Huaiyan was actually rather diverting.
Under the veil of her hat, Jiang Mi pressed her lips together to hide a smile.
Xiao Huaiyan stopped and turned around.
Jiang Mi wiped the smile from her face immediately. Thank goodness for the veiled hat.
He walked back toward her. His handsome face carried a smile as he bent slightly, his voice warm. "Why are you walking so slowly? Are you tired?"
The moment he said it, Jiang Mi felt several pairs of eyes swivel toward her.
With a smile and two words he had managed to redirect all the fire her way.
Behind the veiled hat, Jiang Mi's eyes went wide with indignation.
Xiao Huaiyan flicked his fan. "Fanggé is just ahead. Come."
He waited for Jiang Mi to fall into step, and they walked on together, side by side.
The dropped handkerchiefs and tossed sachets grew noticeably fewer after that.
But Jiang Mi was now on the receiving end of stares, and it made her deeply uncomfortable. He attracted every eye in the street — why did he have to drag her into it?
Fortunately, Fanggé was not far, and they arrived quickly.
From the outside it looked unremarkable. Inside it was laid out like a tiered tower — four floors, each selling different things.
At a glance, Jiang Mi could see calligraphy and paintings, antiques, jewellery, silks and satins, powders and perfumes.
The shop drew many women, and a number of men accompanying their female relatives.
The first and second floors were busy with customers; the third was much quieter; the fourth appeared nearly sealed off, with only a few open windows.
The moment they entered, they were met by attendants and shop maids.
Before long, a middle-aged couple who appeared to manage the establishment came forward with great deference and escorted them upstairs.
On the third floor, Xiao Huaiyan stopped and turned to Jiang Mi. "Go and see if there are any clothes or ornaments that interest you. The journey still has some way to go and it is not easy to buy things on the road — look here while we can. I will come for you in a while."
Jiang Mi saw that Xiao Huaiyan meant to go up to the fourth floor for a meeting and gave a small nod.
The woman of the managing couple said, "Sir, please do not worry. Ru-niang will look after the miss well."
Jiang Mi followed the woman called Ru-niang to the third-floor sitting area.
Ru-niang appeared to be in her early thirties, with a shapely figure and graceful bearing.
Knowing that Jiang Mi was a person of importance, she dared show no carelessness, and personally served her tea before having fresh arrivals in jewellery and ready-made gowns brought for her to look over.
Jiang Mi was not particularly interested in any of this — she had simply wanted to come ashore for a walk and see a different place. Still, she found Fanggé's layout and the way it was run rather interesting.
When Ru-niang had four small maids line up in a row, each presenting a rosewood box of jewellery, Jiang Mi's eyes widened. Seven-winged phoenix hairpins, a delicate camellia-bead comb, freshwater pearl ear drops — each piece elegant and original in design, the gems and pearls of exceptional quality.
Seeing her guest show a flicker of interest, Ru-niang began presenting set after set, talking as she went — offering to try a piece on if Jiang Mi's gaze lingered, swapping out immediately for different styles if it did not.
Item after item appeared and disappeared, each chosen by reading her guest's response.
It was the first time Jiang Mi had encountered anyone so adept at both talking and reading a customer, and she found herself slightly overwhelmed by Ru-niang's enthusiasm.
......
Xiao Huaiyan sat by the window. Opening it gave him a clear view down to the third floor — Jiang Mi's seat directly in his line of sight.
He tapped the table with his fan and said flatly, "Speak."
Gu Chang, who had been waiting inside, stepped forward. "Your Majesty — the river pirates who appeared in Cangzhou have been traced to the local county magistrate. Someone had leverage over him and was using it to let the pirates operate freely in his jurisdiction. Their base has been destroyed, but their leader appears to have died on the night of the attack on the Su family vessel. In the days since, there have also been people in the Cangzhou area asking after Miss Jiang's whereabouts."
"The garrison commander at Cangzhou could not have been unaware of pirates operating under his nose. The county magistrate was being coerced — what about the garrison commander?"
Gu Chang said, "You are correct, Your Majesty. General Zhao of the Cangzhou garrison took bribes and deliberately turned a blind eye. This subject has had him placed under arrest to await Your Majesty's judgment."
Xiao Huaiyan looked out the window, his voice indifferent. "Execute him. Exterminate his clan. Let it serve as a warning to the military."
Among the memories that had surfaced — in this same place, the same pirates had colluded with the Cangzhou garrison commander when the rebels rose in open revolt, burning and killing and pillaging their way through the city as they swept north to join the insurrection.
Investigated now, and sure enough it had begun in the second year of Yuanxi.
"Stay here to deal with this matter, then move your troops to Yangzhou to converge."
Gu Chang clasped his fists. "By your command."
Xiao Huaiyan added, "Where has Pei Chi reached?"
A young man standing to one side replied, "Your Majesty — Commander Pei is aboard the official vessel escorting the Jiang party. They have reached Tengzhou."
Xiao Huaiyan said, "Have him divert to Xuzhou first. I will arrive in due course."
The young man said, "Yes, Your Majesty."
......
Jiang Mi had drunk an entire cup of tea. She had exhausted herself looking at jewellery and was now being shown gowns.
"All of these fabrics were brought up from Suzhou, miss. Feel how smooth it is — how comfortable? Do you like the color? I think this hibiscus shade would suit your complexion beautifully." Ru-niang had not caught a glimpse of her face, but she could see that this young lady's hands were like soft jade, her skin flawless — such a person was made to wear bright, vivid colors.
"If you don't care for it, there are other styles and colors — " and she had more maids bringing further gowns before she had finished the sentence.
Ru-niang was still in full flow when a sharp female voice came from the staircase: "Ru-niang! I hear you have been moving all the new arrivals to the third floor. Just who is more important than my lady?"
Ru-niang's brow tightened. Jiang Mi looked toward the voice.
Three people appeared at the stairhead. The one in the lead was dressed in expensive finery and had a pretty face. Her maid continued: "So this is the special guest Ru-niang is personally attending to?"
Ru-niang rose and smiled pleasantly. "Why, it is Miss Chen, the Prefect's daughter! I have a guest at present, I am afraid I cannot step away."
Miss Chen had not yet spoken, but her maid pressed on: "Ru-niang, since our young mistress has come all the way up here, you should be showing her the new arrivals."
Ru-niang knew the maid was only saying this because she had been put up to it. But there was a true guest sitting here, and she was not going to let some Prefect's daughter override that. She beckoned her own maid over and said evenly, "Miss Chen, I do apologize. These new arrivals have already been spoken for. Please do come back next time."
Miss Chen had never been turned away like this before. A flush of anger began to rise.
"Ru-niang, our young mistress honors you with her patronage," the maid said, her voice unpleasant, her gaze sliding over Jiang Mi. "They say fine clothes are there to set off a beauty. I wonder — what kind of person keeps her veiled hat on even indoors? How dreadfully unpresentable."
A low, smooth laugh broke the air.
Everyone on the third floor looked up. Coming down from the upper floor was a young man — striking and handsome, his bearing effortlessly composed.
He held a folding fan. His phoenix eyes swept the room.
Miss Chen's face went crimson on the spot.
Xiao Huaiyan curved his lips into a smile. "Set off a beauty? Is there a beauty here to speak of?"
He walked past Miss Chen and came to Jiang Mi's side. "All done choosing? If so, let us go back. No need to tire your eyes any further."
Whether Jiang Mi had finished choosing or not, she was nodding and preparing to leave.
Miss Chen stood watching, her face cycling through white and mottled red, as the young man and the veiled woman walked right past her.
As the woman descended the staircase, a gust of wind lifted the veil for just a moment — just enough for anyone watching to catch a glimpse of a face so beautiful it seemed almost unearthly.
Ru-niang's eyes brightened. The three of the other party — mistress and maids — all wore expressions progressively more wretched than the last.
......
They left Fanggé near dusk.
Passersby were moving quickly along the streets — people heading home, people just setting out their stalls.
The road back to the ship was a different street from the one they had come by.
Along this one, vendors were selling local food. The smell of it carried a long way.
Diners ate standing up, large flatbreads in hand; others sat on low stools, hunched happily over bowls of noodles.
At a simple wonton stall, a young couple stood looking at the steaming pot. The wife swallowed at the sight of the dumplings. Her husband tried to order a bowl for her; she stopped him. He went ahead anyway, calling over to the old stallkeeper, and sat his wife down while he fetched two small bowls. He divided the one bowl of wontons between them, placing the larger share in front of her. They went back and forth pressing the other to take more, and in the end the husband insisted. The wife dipped her spoon and ate, and the expression on her face was one of simple, complete contentment.
Jiang Mi was not hungry. She only felt that there was something living and immediate about this kind of scene.
Xiao Huaiyan noticed her stop walking and followed her gaze. "In Fanggé just now, nothing there made you look twice. Yet here you have stopped for a bowl of wontons. Why?"
Jiang Mi breathed in the aroma, something she could not quite name settling in her chest. She said softly, "Perhaps because it smells so good."
Xiao Huaiyan caught the wistfulness in her voice. He watched the young couple finish their meal, carefully count out a few copper coins, and leave together.
Xiao Huaiyan took Jiang Mi's hand. "You need not envy anyone else eating wontons. You can have some too."
Before Jiang Mi could react to the surprise of it, Xiao Huaiyan had pulled her over to the stall and said to the vendor, "Two bowls of wontons, if you please."
The old stallkeeper had never seen gentry-folk in such fine clothes sitting at his stall, but he set to work without missing a beat.
Two steaming bowls appeared on the table.
Jiang Mi saw Xiao Huaiyan pick up his spoon and eat without any hesitation. She said, "Sir — you..."
Didn't he require food to be tasted first?
Xiao Huaiyan said, "It is fine. Eat. These wontons are very fresh."
Jiang Mi looked down and stirred her bowl, then scooped one up and put it in her mouth. Just as expected — and exactly as good as the smell had promised.
Neither of them spoke. Xiao Huaiyan finished first and watched Jiang Mi eat her way through the bowl with careful, absorbed attention. He found himself smiling without meaning to.
Jiang Mi finished, looked up, and met his gaze. He said with a smile, "It is rare to see you finish a bowl down to the last drop."
He set a silver ingot on the table, then turned to Jiang Mi. "Come. Time to get back to the ship."

chapters.title: Seventy five
chapters.content:
When Jiang Mi returned to her cabin, there was a vase of fresh flowers on the table — and several new trunks, with Xiang Yun busy sorting through their contents.
Xiang Yun hurried over to curtsey. "Miss — which of these gowns and ornaments would you like to wear in the coming days? I will take those out for you."
Jiang Mi looked over. Every gown, every piece of jewellery in the trunks was something she had looked at in Fanggé — and it appeared that almost anything she had lingered over for more than a moment had been packed up and sent along.
Seeing Xiang Yun still waiting for an answer, she said, "Just pick out a few, whatever you think."
Jiang Mi felt a little tired. She settled down to sleep early.
She turned from side to side, unable to drop off.
Every time she closed her eyes she was back at the wonton stall, the fragrance still drifting through the air.
In the darkness, Jiang Mi let out a quiet sigh.
......
The following evening, Jiang Mi was trimming the flower stems in the vase when she heard a laugh behind her. "Tangtan — are you feeling better?"
Jiang Mi turned around, face lighting up. "A'Xuan-jie! You're back!"
Gu Xuan, looking at that bright smile, felt thoroughly pleased with herself.
"I am indeed — finished some business and can finally take a few days to breathe. I hear His Majesty took you into Linzhou yesterday? How was it — any fun?"
Jiang Mi shook her head, a look of mild guilt on her face.
Gu Xuan could not quite read it. His Majesty had seemed in reasonable spirits when she checked, but Tangtan was looking like this? Was the girl harder to please than she thought? Or was His Majesty the one who was lacking?
Gu Xuan set the wine she had brought down on the table. "Never mind — Linzhou does not have much to offer anyway. Let me pour you a drink and we can talk."
Jiang Mi was genuinely glad of the company.
They had the evening meal served in the cabin.
Gu Xuan took a sip from her cup and sighed expressively. "Still not enough punch to it. The real thing is the strong spirits they make in Yunzhou. This is just barely drinkable. Here — try some, Tangtan."
Jiang Mi took a small sip and was immediately hit by the burn of it. This was nothing like the fruit wines she usually drank.
But after a moment, there was a trace of sweetness on the finish.
"Not bad," she said.
Gu Xuan laughed. "I thought you would not be able to manage it. This stuff has some kick after a few cups — take your time with it."
Jiang Mi nodded and took another small sip.
Gu Xuan, a few cups in now, grew more expansive. She began telling stories from Yunzhou.
Jiang Mi listened, absorbed.
The scene reminded her, without warning, of her last life. Consort Duan had been exactly like this with her — warm and easy, wanting her to stay for dinner and wine.
Afterward the two of them had talked on and on until they were so at ease with each other that Jiang Mi simply stayed the night at Yikun Palace.
Gu Xuan propped her chin on her hand and looked at Jiang Mi, whose face had gone prettily pink after less than half a cup.
There was a saying that beauty seen by lamplight only grows more beautiful — but there was something especially alluring about a beauty flushed with drink.
No wonder someone had decided to keep her.
Gu Xuan leaned forward with a conspiratorial smile and lowered her voice. "Let me tell you a secret."
Jiang Mi nodded earnestly. "A'Xuan-jie, I promise I will not tell a soul."
Gu Xuan said, "My brother Gu Chang and I grew up following our father in the military camps. In those days when His Majesty was drilling troops he showed no mercy — but he also had that face, and the air of a refined gentleman about him. All the daughters of the civil and military officials in Yunzhou had that little flicker of feeling for him. Even if they could not be a proper wife, some would have been perfectly willing to be a concubine. Those girls tried all manner of methods to attract His Majesty's attention. He turned them all down."
Jiang Mi had not expected Sister Xuan to start talking about Xiao Huaiyan's past.
Gu Xuan continued, "At first we thought — perhaps those girls just were not beautiful enough. Then the late Emperor sent beauties, and the Prince of Jin and the Prince of Qi sent beauties too. All of them — not a single one stayed. Those poor girls would sit there prettily for an entire evening and get nothing for it. We whispered to each other that His Majesty must have some kind of ailment. But then I saw you at the Fengchi temporary palace, and I thought perhaps we had all been wrong."
"Tangtan — I had always assumed you would enter the palace." Gu Xuan had not forgotten the Emperor's expression at the New Year banquet — the worst she had ever seen on his face.
Jiang Mi wrapped both hands around her cup and took a sip. She shook her head gently. "I will not. A'Xuan-jie — I will not enter the palace."
She also knew, better than anyone, whether Xiao Huaiyan had any ailment to speak of. He had certainly not accepted those beauties because of his suspicious and guarded nature — why would he let anyone with the opportunity and the means near him while the realm was not yet fully in his hands?
"Why are you so afraid of His Majesty?" In Gu Xuan's memory, Jiang Mi seemed always to be keeping her distance.
Why indeed.
Where would she even begin? She had drunk two or three cups now without realizing it, and her head was beginning to swim — but the wine had also loosened something in her, some need to let it out.
"I made a mistake once," Jiang Mi said quietly. "I do not want to make it again. I only want a quiet, simple life. The palace is not for me." She thought of the endless dread of her last life, of Xiao Huaiyan's unpredictable turns, of the weight of the Jiang family's fate pressing down on her. How could she bear to step back in, even if this life's Xiao Huaiyan was in some ways different from the one she remembered?
He was still himself, in the end.
The first part of what she had said did not land with Gu Xuan, but the second part she could agree with. "That is true enough. The palace is not so wonderful either. Out here is freer. For my part, I have done enough meritorious service that His Majesty owes me favors. When the time comes, even if I set my sights on some young lord from a noble family, His Majesty could arrange a match."
Jiang Mi was a little muddled now. "But A'Xuan-jie — are you not going to enter the palace as a consort?"
By everything she remembered from her last life, Sister Xuan should have been made a consort already.
Gu Xuan said, "Who said anything about that? I would sooner beat drums and set off fireworks than do that. Once I finish this business in Jiangnan I might even be able to head back to Yunzhou early."
She thought privately: there had been a time when His Majesty intended to have her enter the palace to clear certain obstacles and investigate certain things. But that conversation had simply never happened again.
And how had Tangtan known about it anyway?
Jiang Mi was now thoroughly confused. What business? How had the role of first and highest consort become some kind of assignment?
Sister Xuan must have had considerably more to drink than she had.
Jiang Mi rubbed her temple. Her head was spinning. She slumped forward onto the table.
Gu Xuan looked at her. "Tangtan — shall I help you over to the bed?"
Jiang Mi could hear her, and shook her head. She felt her head growing heavier and heavier. She just needed to rest here for a moment.
She heard Sister Xuan's voice, but it seemed to come from very far away.
Outside the door, Xiao Huaiyan had been standing there, and now his expression had darkened.
He pushed the door open. Gu Xuan was just leaning over to help Jiang Mi up.
Gu Xuan was somewhat flushed herself. She heard the door and saw who had come in, and sobered up by half.
"Your — Your Majesty..." Gu Xuan wilted immediately. Had his Majesty heard the things she had been saying? Please, let him not have heard.
Xiao Huaiyan's temple was pulsing. "Get out," he said, his voice cold.
Gu Xuan looked at Jiang Mi and thought to herself: the wish not to enter the palace — sadly, from the moment she stepped onto this ship, it had become impossible.
After Gu Xuan left, Xiao Huaiyan looked at Jiang Mi, who was slumped over the table in a haze.
Jiang Mi lifted her head and blinked at the newcomer in bleary confusion. "A'Xuan-jie?"
A pair of hands, strong as iron, took hold of her waist and lifted her sideways into the air.
Jiang Mi turned her head and stared. How was she seeing Xiao Huaiyan?
A feeling came over her — as though she had been sent back to her last life. To being carried bodily out of Consort Duan's bed by Xiao Huaiyan.
She had spent the whole night absorbing his fury. The next morning she had not been able to get out of bed. She had not dared set foot in Yikun Palace for days afterward.
Jiang Mi struggled. She wanted to get out of these arms.
A sharp smack landed on her hip. "Stop squirming."
Jiang Mi's head spun harder. She was no longer sure what was dream and what was real.
Her chin was lifted. Xiao Huaiyan looked down at her and said, "Do you see clearly who I am?"
Jiang Mi gave a small nod. Her eyes had gone red. "Your Majesty — I... I will not go to see Sister Duan Fei again. Please let me go. I am afraid of the pain..."
Xiao Huaiyan's expression became unreadable. He knew that when she was sick with fever she tended to go soft and childlike. He had not expected that drunk, she would confuse the present with her dreams.
He looked down at her and said, "What if I do not let you go?"
Jiang Mi seemed to have sunk further into that other place — her gaze hazy and full of fear.
Different from the wary avoidance of her ordinary days.
Xiao Huaiyan pressed his thumb lightly to her lips. "Jiang Mi. Do not be afraid of me."
He bent and kissed her — gentle, unhurried, a slow and careful pressure.
That slender white neck. He wanted to bite down into it — and only let his fingertips drift back and forth across it instead.
Jiang Mi felt a wave of tingling warmth. The hand at her waist pressed and kneaded until she went limp.
He watched her responses, and saw her relax.
He knew every inch of her. Knew every reaction.
He could feel her trembling.
His voice dropped to something rough and low. "You will come to feel it again."
"No... no, I will not..." She would not let herself fall for Xiao Huaiyan again. She did not dare.
Jiang Mi pushed against him with both hands and began to cry in muffled, helpless sobs.
Xiao Huaiyan's eyes were dark as ink. "Jiang Mi — there is no use running. Whether last life or this one, you carry the mark I left on you."
......
The next morning Jiang Mi woke feeling that something was wrong.
Her waist in particular ached.
But she had been drunk — she should only have a headache.
Her memory seemed to stop at Sister Xuan saying she wanted to go back to Yunzhou.
And then she thought she had seen Xiao Huaiyan.
She could not quite determine whether it had been a dream or whether he had actually come.
Jiang Mi got up. Xiang Yun came in to help her wash and dress.
"When did I go to sleep last night?" Jiang Mi asked.
Xiang Yun said, "You had too much to drink, miss. Miss Gu helped you to bed. This servant is not sure of the exact hour."
Jiang Mi walked out of her cabin and came face to face with Gu Xuan.
"Tangtan — did you sleep well last night?" Gu Xuan asked, with rather less certainty than usual.

chapters.title: Seventy six
chapters.content:
Gu Xuan was deeply vexed with herself. What had she been thinking, drinking last night?
She had not even gotten properly drunk — and yet she had managed to get Jiang Mi drunk in her place, and then left her alone with the Emperor.
Who knew what the Emperor might have done.
Gu Xuan was a little uneasy.
Jiang Mi noticed Gu Xuan's concern. Her waist was still somewhat uncomfortable, but the rest of her felt all right.
She smiled. "Well enough. A'Xuan-jie — when did you leave last night?"
Gu Xuan relaxed immediately. She laughed it off. "I was too drunk to remember clearly. I think Xiang Yun helped me back to my room."
"A'Xuan-jie, that was good wine — but the kick it carries afterward is too much. You should drink less of it in the future."
"Of course, of course." Gu Xuan privately resolved never to drink with Jiang Mi again. The sight of that soft little creature drunk was far too dangerous for everyone involved.
The two of them made their way out onto the deck, talking as they went. A spring breeze drifted over the water — quite pleasant.
Jiang Mi noticed the ship's security seemed to have tightened, and there were more unfamiliar faces coming and going near Xiao Huaiyan's quarters.
She sneezed, and felt a little embarrassed.
Gu Xuan smiled. "Tangtan, don't go catching a chill. It may be getting warmer, but this is exactly the season when colds come on. You had too much to drink last night — why don't you go back and rest for a while."
Jiang Mi had been enduring the discomfort in her waist, and she did feel genuinely tired. She said her goodbyes and went back to her room.
She sat on the edge of the bed for a while, thinking.
Then she went to the dressing table and undressed.
Xiang Yun had said Sister Xuan helped her to bed. But Sister Xuan had said it was Xiang Yun who helped her back to her own room.
The confusion she had been carrying was becoming harder to deny.
And the familiar feeling in her own body was the most impossible thing to lie to herself about.
Jiang Mi looked in the mirror and saw the clear imprint of fingers at the small of her back, and several marks of red and purple across her skin.
Her hands trembled as she drew her clothes back together.
It had not been a dream.
These marks shattered the last of her wishful thinking.
What she had seen of Xiao Huaiyan had not been a dream.
In this life she had not crept into his bed while he slept. She had not danced for him or played coy to win his favor. She had not done a single thing to entice him.
So why had it come to this anyway.
Why were his desires identical to those of her last life.
Jiang Mi pressed her palm to her forehead and leaned back against the bed, forcing herself to remember. Fragments from the drunken night flickered through — a kiss that left no room to breathe, arms that held like shackles, every part of her trembling at his touch beyond her own control.
In her last life Xiao Huaiyan had been like that too — always wanting to hold her waist, knowing precisely that pressure there made her go soft and powerless. In bed he liked to drag his lips and teeth across her skin, and she had always been terrified he would bite down.
Jiang Mi wrapped her arms tightly around herself, lost and at a loss.
Xiang Yun had noticed the door shut for some time. She knocked. "Miss — it is time for the midday meal."
She waited. Silence. She was about to push the door open when she heard Jiang Mi's voice. "I have no appetite. I want to sleep for a while."
Xiang Yun hesitated, then went to find Chengzhong.
Chengzhong looked in at the Emperor, who was still occupied and showed no sign of summoning Miss Jiang for the meal. He said, "Then stay with the young miss and see to her meal when she wakes."
Xiang Yun nodded repeatedly.
Two hours passed. Xiang Yun went in to check.
She drew back the bed curtain. Miss Jiang was still lying there. Xiang Yun called softly — "Miss Jiang..."
She leaned closer and her expression changed. Miss Jiang's face was flushed with an unnatural colour. She reached out and touched her forehead. It was hot.
Xiang Yun did not delay. She went out at once to find help.
Chengzhong was standing by the door. When he heard that Miss Jiang had fallen ill, he was also alarmed. He peered inside — the Emperor was still reviewing memorials. The head eunuch had stayed behind in the palace for this trip; there was no one else but him to go in.
Xiao Huaiyan was writing with his vermilion brush. He heard the movement and did not look up. "What is it?"
Chengzhong bowed. "Your Majesty — Miss Jiang appears to have developed a fever."
The brush stopped mid-stroke. She had been fine last night. How had she fallen ill so suddenly?
......
Chief Physician Gu had not come on this journey. In his place was a young imperial physician named Song.
Physician Song, one of the Emperor's trusted men, grabbed his medicine box the moment he received the summons and hurried over.
When Chengzhong led him into the cabin, something immediately felt wrong. The Emperor was sitting calmly to one side, and behind the lowered bed curtains he could just make out the shape of someone lying within.
The only thing visible was half a wrist — already covered with a cloth. Thoroughly well-covered.
Physician Song caught sight of the Emperor's expression and hastened forward to take the pulse.
After he withdrew his hand, Xiao Huaiyan asked, "Well?"
Physician Song said, "Your Majesty — this young miss has a somewhat weak constitution. With the change of season and the shift in region, a moment's inattention is all it takes to catch a chill. I will prescribe something to bring down the fever. As for the cold itself, it will need rest and care to heal. There is also some emotional congestion in this miss — if she could be in better spirits, her recovery will come faster."
After the physician left, Xiao Huaiyan drew back the bed curtain and tucked Jiang Mi's hand back under the covers. He stood in silence looking at her sleeping face.
She seemed to be sleeping uneasily — tossing in small distress, murmuring in a barely audible voice.
Xiao Huaiyan leaned closer. The sound was plaintive, almost a sob. "What must I do... to stop being wrong..."
He reached out and wiped the warm trace of tears from her face.
He waited until Jiang Mi had taken her medicine and sunk into a deep sleep, then walked out of the cabin.
He looked out at the boundless dark of the river and said quietly, "Tomorrow we reach Xuzhou?"
Chengzhong replied, "Yes, Your Majesty."
......
The next morning, Jiang Mi surfaced half-awake from her fever and felt herself being held. She opened her eyes and found herself looking at someone's throat. She raised her gaze — and met a pair of phoenix eyes.
She closed her eyes again. She could tell from the sounds that they were disembarking. Where was Xiao Huaiyan taking her?
She avoided facing him and let herself drift back into sleep.
When she woke again, she found herself lying in a bed of pear-wood, with Xiang Yun dozing in a chair beside her.
She had been sleeping so long that it took a moment to sit up.
Xiang Yun heard the rustling and looked up with a pleased expression. "Miss — you are awake!"
She poured a cup of warm water and handed it over. "Are you hungry, miss? I will go and fetch something to eat."
Jiang Mi shook her head. "Where is this?"
They were no longer on the ship.
"We have reached Xuzhou, miss. The young master saw that you were unwell and brought you here to recover."
Jiang Mi had thought she could be sick in peace, hiding in her cabin until they reached Jiangnan. She had not anticipated that he would stop in Xuzhou for her.

chapters.title: Seventy seven
chapters.content:
After taking her medicine Jiang Mi broke a sweat. She asked Xiang Yun for hot water, bathed, ate a little congee, and then lay back down — still feeling weak and heavy.
When she woke again she was thirsty. She called out for Xiang Yun.
Getting no answer, she thought to get up and pour the water herself.
She had just started to push the covers aside when she saw Xiao Huaiyan sitting by the latticed window, turning the pages of a book.
He set the book down and looked up.
Jiang Mi pulled the covers back over herself.
Xiao Huaiyan rose and walked toward her. "No need to get up. You are ill — lie still."
"Thank you, Your Majesty." Being improperly dressed made getting up inconvenient in any case; she was glad of the excuse to stay where she was.
"Are you feeling better?"
Jiang Mi covered her mouth and coughed a few times, then said quietly, "This subject has been indisposed and begs Your Majesty's pardon. My illness may take some days yet to heal. I would not wish to delay Your Majesty's affairs — would it be possible to leave me here to recuperate and have someone escort me to Jinling once I am recovered?"
Xiao Huaiyan was quiet for a moment, then smiled. "You are quite considerate of me."
Jiang Mi's expression stiffened. She did not want to travel with him any further.
He noticed her lips looked dry from the repeated chewing she had been doing, and poured a cup of warm water, which he held out to her.
Her throat was parched and the cough had left it raw — that cup of water was as welcome as rain.
Jiang Mi took it, thanked him, and drank.
The moment it touched her tongue she detected a floral sweetness — the faint, gentle taste of flower nectar dissolved in the water.
She felt a small start of surprise. It was exactly as she was used to at home — a little honey blended into the water.
The warmth soothed her throat immediately and she felt her whole body ease.
Xiao Huaiyan took the empty cup from her hands and said, unhurried, "I have some business to attend to in Xuzhou as well. There is no inconvenience in waiting for you."
Her chest tightened at that. She could not help another small fit of coughing.
Xiao Huaiyan set down the cup and stood with his back to her. "Jiang Mi — your body belongs to you. When you fall ill you do damage to your own foundation. Whatever your reasons may be, it is not worth doing this to yourself."
Jiang Mi froze. What did he mean? Did he know her illness was deliberate?
He turned around. "I would guess — you remembered what happened when you were drunk?"
Jiang Mi's face went slightly pale. He had found her out.
Xiao Huaiyan's frown deepened. He had guessed correctly after all.
"So you would rather catch a cold than be near me. You would rather make yourself ill than face me." His tone was level, unreadable.
Jiang Mi tightened her hands quietly under the covers. "If Your Majesty already knows full well — then why did you do it?"
If he knew, why had he carried her off the ship to recover here?
Xiao Huaiyan looked at the small figure in front of him who seemed about to cry. What he had been about to say died on his tongue. Evidently she did not even fully understand what had happened.
He looked at her for another moment. "Do not do this to yourself again. Rest well. I will come and see you when I have time."
After Xiao Huaiyan left, Jiang Mi stared unseeing at the canopy overhead.
That night, after taking her medicine, she fell into a heavy sleep.
She dreamed. It seemed to be that drunken night — the fragments that had flickered through her mind began to resolve into something clearer.
Jiang Mi struggled to wake herself. She watched herself drunk, her memories of this life and the last bleeding into one another, and in one of those moments she seemed to become Consort Jiang again — she watched herself reach out and wind her arms around Xiao Huaiyan's neck.
Jiang Mi's eyes snapped open. She lay there breathing, trying to calm herself.
She raised her hand and pressed it over her eyes.
Why was this happening.
......
After several days of rest, Jiang Mi's illness had much improved.
Xiang Yun proposed cheerfully, "Miss — the weather is lovely today. Would you like to take a walk in the garden? The peach blossoms are out — the whole grove is beautiful."
Jiang Mi glanced out the window. Spring sunlight, birdsong, and the faint thread of a floral scent.
She had not stepped outside since arriving here to recuperate.
She still put on her cape, and followed Xiang Yun out.
The garden, Xiang Yun explained, was called Sùyuán. Its paths wound in graceful curves past swaying banana palms and drifted with a faint sweetness. Through a moon gate, Jiang Mi suddenly found herself looking at a sweep of vivid rose-pink.
A breeze moved through it. Pink petals fell like rain, settling on her hair and shoulders.
Walking among the peach trees, Jiang Mi felt her spirits lift considerably.
As lovely as it was, her strength did not allow her to linger. She would have to come back tomorrow.
She had just come out from among the trees when a voice came from behind her. "Is it Miss Jiang?"
She turned. It was Pei Chi.
Pei Chi had come to report something important to the Emperor and was about to leave when he spotted Jiang Mi — and remembered a particular errand he had been given.
He walked over. Seeing her puzzled expression, he said, "Your younger sister asked me to pass something on to you — a message and a small token. She asked me to tell you she will be waiting in Jinling at the Su family home. And this is what she wanted you to have."
He held out a woven good-luck knot. He had only met the girl a handful of times and still was not sure how she had been so certain he would run into Miss Jiang. She had pressed a second knot on him to keep as well.
Jiang Mi recognized Jiang Rong's handiwork at once. She took it. "Thank you, Commander Pei. Are my family all well?"
"Miss Jiang need not worry. His Majesty has arranged for people to escort them. Their vessel should arrive in Jinling within the next few days."
She thanked him again.
Pei Chi clasped his hands in a bow. "The person you should be thanking is His Majesty. My errand is done — I will take my leave."
He turned and walked away.
Jiang Mi looked down at the knot in her hand. She wasn't sure how Xue Jinglin's arrangements had become Xiao Huaiyan's arrangements, but the result was yet another debt to Xiao Huaiyan.
She also felt a small puzzlement — why had Jiang Rong asked Pei Chi to deliver it? When had those two had any dealings with each other?
She shook her head. But knowing everyone was safe settled something in her chest.
In the days that followed, Xiao Huaiyan would occasionally come to see how she was recovering — sometimes staying for a meal before leaving. They did not talk much.
Then for several days he did not appear.
Jiang Mi felt relief, but underneath the relief something nagged quietly at her.
Xuzhou. This place carried a particular weight.
At dusk she was watching the colors at the edge of the sky when Chengzhong came in quickly and bowed. "Miss — His Majesty requests your presence."
It was the first summons since they had arrived at Sùyuán.
Jiang Mi went out and found a sedan chair waiting.
"Eunuch Cheng — where are we going?"
"You will see when you arrive, miss."
He would not say more, and there was no point pressing. Jiang Mi settled into the chair and felt it carry her out through the garden gate.
She heard crowds outside — strange, at this hour, for the market to be so full of noise.
She lifted the curtain for a look. Was there some celebration in Xuzhou?
She saw men and women in fine clothes holding lotus river lanterns.
When the chair stopped and she stepped out, she found herself at the river's edge.
The sky had gone fully dark. The river was almost covered in floating lanterns — flames rocking on the black water like clusters of red lotus blossoms.
She spotted a lone figure at the bank, dressed entirely in white.
He was releasing a lantern into the water.
Xiao Huaiyan turned. "Come here."
Jiang Mi's feet felt strangely weighted. She knew this moment was coming.
She moved to stand silently beside him and watched as he lifted a jug of wine and poured it into the river.
The wine carried the fragrance of plum blossom. Consort Jin had loved plum blossom — had loved to brew wine with it.
He was making offerings to his birth mother.
Xuzhou — the place where it had happened.
She had only ever heard in passing that the late Emperor, on his return from a southern tour in the tenth year of Yongshun's reign, had stopped in Xuzhou — and that Consort Jin had met her end here.
Jiang Mi looked at the wine jugs arranged on the ground. She picked one up and poured it into the river too.
Her feelings were too tangled to name. Her aunt's manner, her Uncle Qingde's manner — both made it impossible to pretend the Jiang family had nothing to do with this.
The Jiang family bore guilt. She bore guilt.
She knew Xiao Huaiyan was watching her, but she did not dare look up.
All the plum wine had been poured into the river now. Its fragrance was everywhere.
Xiao Huaiyan looked out over the lantern-covered water. A sardonic curve came to his lips.
When his mother died she had been wrapped in a rush mat and dragged away. They had not even given her a decent grave.
Now here in Xuzhou, on the Festival of Flowers, the whole city released lanterns in her memory.
The smile faded slowly. Xiao Huaiyan stood a long time in the dark.
Jiang Mi stood quietly beside him. What had truly happened in the tenth year of Yongshun's reign?
Why had he brought her here? The people who had harmed Consort Jin were dead. In a few years, when the Jiang family's involvement was exposed, Xiao Huaiyan would surely regret having brought her to this place.
Xiao Huaiyan drew his thoughts back. "Let us go."
The whole journey back neither of them spoke. When Jiang Mi was stepping down from the carriage, taking Xiang Yun's hand —
Xiao Huaiyan's voice came from behind her. "Jiang Mi — do you know how to make yangchun noodles?"
Jiang Mi stilled. She hesitated, then nodded. "Yes."
......
Jiang Mi was not unfamiliar with yangchun noodles. She had made them for Xiao Huaiyan in her last life too.
His palate was exacting. It had taken her many attempts to work out exactly what he liked.
Yangchun noodles are not difficult to make, but they are difficult to make well. The broth must be clear; the flavour must be fresh; the noodles must be tender yet have spring.
Jiang Mi scattered a pinch of scallion over the top. The bowl was ready.
She carried it to Xiao Huaiyan's table.
He looked at the bowl and said, "Chengzhong — bring another set of chopsticks and a bowl."
Jiang Mi did not understand.
Chengzhong fetched them quickly.
Jiang Mi watched as Xiao Huaiyan divided the noodles into two bowls and said, "Sit down. Taste your own cooking."
She stood still.
In her last life she had made noodles for him several times. She had always watched him eat — anxious about whether the taste was right — and had never once eaten with him.
Xiao Huaiyan looked up at her motionless figure.
Her heart gave a startled jolt, and she sat down beside him. She kept her eyes lowered, stirring the noodles with her chopsticks.
Xiao Huaiyan lifted his chopsticks and tasted a mouthful. The same flavour as in the dream.
She was still willing to make a bowl of noodles for him.
"Good," he said. "Very like what my mother used to make."
Jiang Mi chewed a mouthful of noodle and could not taste a thing. Her chest felt blocked and heavy.
When Xiao Huaiyan finished, he said, "In two days I will have someone escort you to the Su family home in Jinling. When my business here is concluded, I will come for you."

chapters.title: Seventy eight
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi had not expected him to relent so quickly on what he had refused before.
But his last sentence gave her pause.
Xiao Huaiyan took something from his sleeve and held it out. "Take this."
It was a wrist-mounted crossbow.
He saw that she had not moved and stood up. "Come here. I will show you how to use it."
Jiang Mi's mind gave a sudden lurch. Memory from her last life surged up: Xiao Huaiyan, exactly as he looked now, saying — "You want to win that badly? Come here. I will teach you how to shoot."
Among the consorts and concubines there was no shortage of generals' daughters, and at the temporary palace they all vied for ways to show themselves before the Emperor — competing at pitch-pot and archery. She herself was accomplished in music, chess, calligraphy, and painting, but hopeless at anything like this.
She had lost several rounds and was sulking. Xiao Huaiyan had taught her himself — and set a forfeit: for every arrow short of a set number within a certain time, she would have to pay a penalty of his choosing.
He had been very well satisfied in those days. And the fear of forfeits had driven her improvement at a remarkable pace.
She came back to herself to find she had followed Xiao Huaiyan out into the courtyard without realizing it.
He was watching her from a short distance away. Her slight figure stood in the dappled shadow of a tree with a strange, solitary quality to it.
He watched her quietly. Fragments surfaced — the archery ground in that other life, scattered clothing on the ground, skin white as snow, the sound of soft sighs and the clear bright ringing of ankle bells...
Xiao Huaiyan's gaze darkened. His indulgences, his recklessness — they had only ever been for her.
He walked over and took her wrist, drawing her out from the shadow of the tree.
He saw that her expression held a trace of bewilderment. "What are you daydreaming about?"
He held the wrist-crossbow out before her. "It fits along the inner forearm. To fire, flick this butterfly plate here — are you following?"
Her long lashes swept down. She nodded.
"This is more useful than that hairpin of yours," Xiao Huaiyan said. "Though I would rather you never have cause to use it. Wait for me in Jinling."
......
Two days later, her illness fully recovered, Jiang Mi was escorted toward Jinling as Xiao Huaiyan had promised.
On the new vessel she found the wife and daughter of the Cangzhou prefect — courteous and well-mannered. They were travelling under official military escort to visit family in Jinling and were going the same way.
This matched what Gu Xuan had told her earlier. The official explanation was that the Cangzhou prefect's wife had rescued her and was accompanying her to Jinling.
Xiang Yun continued in attendance.
The rest of the journey was quiet. Jiang Mi spent long stretches on the deck, turning things over in her mind.
The words Gu Xuan had said while drunk. Xiao Huaiyan's behaviour.
It all made the past life feel as though a veil had been drawn over it — what she had lived through, and what she had believed she knew, seemed no longer entirely the same.
In her last life she had drunk with Sister Duan Fei too, and she too had spoken of longing for the world outside the palace, of wanting to finish her assignment and be free. At the time Jiang Mi had thought she was joking and had not taken it seriously.
Sister Duan Fei had always been in robust health. How had she died so suddenly of an acute illness? Thinking now of those words she had spoken — Jiang Mi began to wonder whether Consort Duan had truly died of illness in her last life at all.
But why? What had Xiao Huaiyan's purpose been?
If the clear-eyed, principled Consort Duan of her last life had been a performance, and the gentle, cultured Emperor had been a mask — then what had been real?
......
When they arrived in Jinling, the Su family's servants were already waiting at the harbour.
Su-shi and Jiang Rong had both come. Only Xuan-ge'er, too young, had been left at home.
Jiang Rong kept craning her neck. "Mother — the letter said this was about the right time. Where is Elder Sister's boat? I still can't see it."
Su-shi was equally puzzled, but she soothed Jiang Rong. "It should be soon. Don't be impatient."
Before long, an official vessel appeared ahead. The boats moored nearby cleared a path at once.
When it docked, two rows of soldiers filed off in formation. Jiang Rong stood on her toes and caught a glimpse of her sister.
"Elder Sister!" she called out at the top of her voice.
Heads turned all around her. Her face went red.
But she still could not help waving toward her sister.
Jiang Mi heard the voice and looked. She saw Su-shi and A'Rong.
She said her farewells to the prefect's wife and walked toward them.
Su-shi came forward and wrapped her arms around Jiang Mi the moment she stepped off the ship. "Good child — you have had a hard journey."
Jiang Mi smiled. "Please don't worry, Madam. I travelled with the prefect's wife from Cangzhou. Everything was fine."
Su-shi was relieved. She noticed an unfamiliar maidservant following Jiang Mi. "And who is this?"
"This is Xiang Yun — she was given to me by Madam Liu."
Su-shi nodded and said no more.
She ushered Jiang Mi to the carriage and they set off for the Su family estate.
In the carriage Jiang Rong pressed close to Jiang Mi's side.
Jiang Mi asked about the night of the pirate attack.
Su-shi said, "I would never have imagined river pirates in Cangzhou waters. The Su family vessel had made the run so many times — and it had to happen this one. Not long after the ship was rammed, guards from Young Lord Xue's side came and told us to take the small boats away. I had A'Rong and Xuan-ge'er with me but I could not find you. When I asked, the guard said the Young Lord had gone to fetch you himself and that you would leave on a separate boat. By then the pirates were already on deck, knives out, killing everywhere... We got away in the small boats, but the pirates chased us the whole way. Thank heavens an official vessel came in time and the soldiers reached us. Tangtan — you were rescued by the soldiers too, I expect?"
Jiang Mi paused briefly, then nodded. She asked, "Madam — were there many casualties among our party?"
Su-shi sighed. "We lost some twelve or thirteen guards and a few of the servants. Compensation has already been sent to their families. I am told the court has since executed those pirates — good riddance. They were no loss to anyone."
Jiang Mi said, "And the children — were any of them hurt?"
Su-shi looked confused. "What children?"
Jiang Rong chimed in, "Mother — she means the little grandson that Old Nurse Zhang was watching, surely."
Su-shi remembered. "He was fine. Zhang kept him right at her side the whole time, and brought him along when they took the small boats. Just one child — the pirates had bigger concerns. I don't recall anyone else mentioning it."
Jiang Mi's brow creased. Just one child? She had seen several. Had they all been taken off separately?
She asked quietly, "How is Young Lord Xue?"
Su-shi's expression brightened considerably. "After we scattered, he was not on our vessel — he seemed to have other business and did not come with us. But he acquitted himself very bravely. They say he killed the pirates' leader himself on that ship — the court has already commended him. They are apparently going to give him a formal commendation when he returns to the capital."
"He should already be in Jinling, actually — though he has not shown his face. He did send gifts to the Su estate. Once he learns you are here I am sure he will come to see you."
Jiang Mi looked down at the handkerchief in her lap.
Su-shi took it for shyness and did not tease her further.

chapters.title: Seventy nine
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi's arrival caused quite a stir throughout the Su estate.
The old matriarch's courtyard was filled with the ladies and young misses from every branch of the family. The Su matriarch had two sons and one daughter — Su-shi was her younger daughter.
The family had been curious about Jiang Mi for some time. The eldest daughter of the second household's original wife — deeply favoured by the Empress Dowager, and now formally betrothed to the Young Lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor. A young woman of that standing coming to stay at the Su estate felt to them like a great honour.
When Jiang Mi walked in with Su-shi, every eye in the room brightened.
Jiangnan had no shortage of beauties, but a face like this was rare even here — every expression carrying a natural, artless charm that made you want to cherish her.
The old matriarch was delighted. Laughter filled the room.
Jiang Mi worked at learning all the faces: the ladies, the young misses, the young wives of the first and second households.
Before long, several of the young men came through as well — among them the second cousin Jiang Rong had mentioned.
The eldest son was already married, and had a settled, composed air. The second had a pair of bright, expressive eyes and the gift of making the old matriarch laugh with a single sentence. The third was fifteen, visibly shy.
Jiang Mi and Jiang Rong greeted them, and all three young men returned the courtesy properly before taking their leave before too long.
The Su matriarch kept Jiang Mi for the midday meal, after which Su-shi and Jiang Rong walked her to the courtyard that had been arranged for her.
It was tranquil and tastefully furnished, with a faint resemblance to her own Yuanzhi Courtyard back home. Someone had clearly put thought into it.
Qiuyu, Xia Ruo, and her other maids came out to meet her.
Su-shi wanted to take Jiang Rong away, but for once the usually biddable girl insisted on staying to keep her sister company. Su-shi had no choice but to go on ahead.
Jiang Mi spent a little time catching up with Qiuyu and the others, then noticed Xiang Yun hanging back behind them. She asked Qiuyu to take her and get her settled.
Feeling a little tired, Jiang Mi lay down with Jiang Rong for an afternoon nap. Eyes closed, she asked, "A'Rong — what do you think of Commander Pei?"
Jiang Rong, already half-asleep, answered without thinking, "He is a good person! He helped Elder Sister and he saved me, so he must be a good person."
Jiang Mi listened, and thought: if you knew that after the Jiang family's fall in the last life, Pei Chi had taken you as a concubine by force — would you still think him a good person?
Jiang Rong rubbed her eyes. "Elder Sister — I asked Commander Pei to bring you the good-luck knot. I wonder if you met up with him?"
Jiang Mi was quiet for a moment. "Not yet," she said.
Jiang Rong was a little disappointed. "Never mind, Elder Sister — I will make you another one."
Jiang Mi smiled. "That would be lovely. Now sleep."
......
A few days passed. Young Lord Xue had not appeared in person, but a letter arrived by messenger.
Jiang Mi opened it to find his opening lines asking after her wellbeing, followed by an explanation that certain matters had detained him in Suzhou — he might not be back in time for the old matriarch's birthday banquet, but he had arranged for gifts to be sent. He would come to see her as soon as he reached Jinling.
Jiang Mi folded the letter. The child's face kept rising in her mind. She could not quite read Xue Jinglin, and she did not know whether any of it was coincidence.
She sat down at the writing table, picked up her brush, and composed a reply. She asked after several things, and at the end added one line: Young Lord — when do you expect to return to the capital?
The Duke Zhen State's manor, the Grand Princess, and Ningzhu were all in the capital. Xue Jinglin had ties that bound him there.
She finished writing and had Qiuyu give it to the messenger to carry back.
......
In what seemed no time at all, the day of the Su matriarch's birthday arrived. The estate was hung with lanterns and bright with celebration.
A renowned opera troupe had been engaged well in advance.
At the banquet, the family members presented their gifts to the matriarch — some costly, some made by hand, each expressing its own variety of affection.
The pair of ten-thousand-longevity jade ruyi sceptres sent by Xue Jinglin surprised and delighted the old matriarch.
The Su family all understood: the Young Lord of the Duke Zhen State's manor had sent gifts out of regard for Jiang Mi.
Jiang Mi presented her own prepared gift as well.
The gifts were nearly all done when the estate's head steward came hurrying in. "Old Madam — there are people outside who have come to present a gift. They say they were sent by Miss Jiang's cousin-brother."
Jiang Mi looked up. Her cousin-brother?
The old matriarch and Su-shi were both surprised. The steward was told to bring them in at once.
A young manservant she did not recognise came forward, carrying a rosewood box in both hands. He opened it and said, "May the Old Madam's fortune be as vast as the Eastern Sea and her years as long as the Southern Mountains. My young master presents this jade Guanyin."
Everyone's gaze went to the figure. The Guanyin seemed to emit its own inner luminescence — the jade was of exceptional quality, its value self-evident at a glance.
Even the Su family, wealthy merchants of the region, had never seen jade of this calibre.
The old matriarch rose from her seat and walked over, supported by her maid's hand, to look at it more closely. She could not quite bring herself to touch it. She murmured reverently, "This — this is far too precious."
She looked across at Jiang Mi, her expression full of feeling. "Miss Jiang — your cousin-brother's gift is too generous."
Jiang Mi's gaze settled on the jade Guanyin. Her cousins were far away in Qingzhou. They could not possibly know of the old matriarch's birthday, let alone send something like this.
This style — the sheer flair of it — bore every mark of a certain someone she knew.
But by what right did he call himself her cousin-brother.
She could see how much the old matriarch adored the piece, and said simply, "A gift is only a token of sentiment, Old Madam. Please do accept it."
The third son of the Su family, Su Jingchen, chimed in agreeably, "Grandmother — since Miss Jiang says so, just accept it happily. And I still have a gift of my own to present to you!"
The old matriarch stopped demurring and had the jade Guanyin carefully carried to her Buddha hall.
She turned to Su Jingchen. "And what has this rascal gone and prepared?"
Su Jingchen smiled. "With such a magnificent piece already before us, my hard-won painting of the Immortal Magu Offering Birthday Wishes — I only hope you will not think it too poor by comparison, Grandmother."
He unrolled the scroll. The room turned to look.
Almost at once, someone spotted the seal and exclaimed with delight, "Second Brother — is this a painting by Master Yunqing?"
"A genuine work by Master Yunqing himself!"
"A painting like that is beyond price!"
The old matriarch had Su Jingchen bring the scroll closer and studied the procession of birthday-offering immortal women with care. It was unmistakably Master Yunqing's style — the figures graceful and otherworldly, full of ethereal elegance.
Jiang Mi loved painting. She had long admired Master Yunqing's work — she had even once copied his famous last piece, the Peony Beauty.
When she saw Su Jingchen produce this scroll of the Magu she went very still.
Because she had seen this painting before — in Xiao Huaiyan's possession, in her last life. He had pointed out a mark on the lower left corner, left there when Yunqing's youngest son had smeared it while playing. He had told her: that mark is how you tell the real from a copy.
The scroll in Su Jingchen's hands had a clean lower left corner. Even the wisps of cloud beneath the immortal figures' feet differed in subtle ways from the one in the palace. And the brushwork on the Magu figures themselves — while convincingly in Yunqing's style, anyone who had spent time copying from an actual Yunqing original would catch the difference.
She could see that the old matriarch was genuinely happy, and that Su Jingchen — by all accounts a bit of a maverick, but deeply devoted to his grandmother — could not possibly have knowingly bought a fake to fool her.
After the banquet, Jiang Mi quietly sought out Su-shi. "Madam — do you happen to know where Second Young Master Su acquired that painting?"
Su-shi smiled. "Tangtan, are you thinking of buying a painting yourself? I heard that Jingchen bought it at Yǒuyuán. He let slip at the banquet that he paid over ten thousand taels for that genuine masterwork — emptied his whole private treasury. Poor boy."
"'Yǒuyuán'?"
"The yǒu of 'having everything one could want,' also readable as 'all wishes granted.' Yǒuyuán. It has been enormously popular in Jinling these past few years — patronised mostly by people with money, and even by quite a few officials' families. The garden itself is said to be very beautiful. They deal in unexpected rarities: precious objects, rare plants and flowers. Apparently one can only enter by the introduction of someone already known to them. Inside there is something for every taste. Jingchen has spent a fortune there — thousands here, ten thousand there. At this rate there will be nothing left when he wants to find a wife. The Su family may be wealthy, but money does not last forever at that rate. This time he spent all that for a painting to please Grandmother — that is the only reason his wife is holding her tongue."
Jiang Mi said, "Has Second Young Master Su bought calligraphy and antiques there as well? Could you ask him to let me have a look at what he has purchased?"
Su-shi knew Jiang Mi's character well enough. She also knew that with Jiang Mi's background, nothing she could have seen there would have been new to her. Something had prompted these questions.
"Tangtan — is something wrong?"
"If Second Young Master Su has bought calligraphy and antiques there, Madam — could you ask him to bring them for me to look at?"
Su-shi did not delay. She sent for Su Jingchen at once.
Su Jingchen listened quietly to everything Jiang Mi said, then struggled to believe he had bought four antiques and only one was genuine — and not even worth what he had paid for it. Even harder to accept was that the Yunqing masterwork he had gifted his grandmother was a forgery.
And yet this young woman, pointing to each piece in turn and naming the specific flaws, only made more and more sense the longer he listened.
Besides — the Empress Dowager's niece had no reason to deceive him.
Su Jingchen was silent for a long time. At last he said, "Miss Jiang — I believe you. But Yǒuyuán has dealings with many important families. That is part of why I trusted it. What I cannot tell is whether they singled me out to sell fakes, or whether most of what they sell to everyone is fake — preying on merchants like me who want to seem cultured and get a foot in the door with official circles. And especially that 'all wishes granted' service — I have heard that no request goes unanswered. They can apparently even arrange an official post, for the right price."
He lowered his voice. "Miss Jiang — if you are willing, would you come with me to Yǒuyuán? My sisters have been saying the hundred-flower garden is in full bloom and want to go. It would be a natural occasion."

chapters.title: Eighty
chapters.content:
Jiang Mi spent a whole night turning it over in her mind — and agreed to go with Su Jingchen.
She wanted to see for herself whether the calligraphy and antiques were being sold as a mix of real and fake, or were all fraudulent from the start.
A single counterfeit could command tens of thousands of taels on Yǒuyuán's reputation alone. If this was happening across the board, the wealth they had accumulated was staggering.
And whoever stood behind Yǒuyuán was brazen enough to offer to sell official positions. Who was backing an operation this bold?
If any of it was true, she needed to write to her aunt and have the court send people to investigate.
Su Jingchen made his arrangements, and three days later they set out — him escorting his sisters to view the hundred flowers.
Before they left, Jiang Mi found Su-shi and asked her to help locate the physician's apprentice-brother that the medical attendant had mentioned. Su-shi agreed without hesitation — she would send word the moment she heard anything.
Jiang Mi and Jiang Rong shared a carriage. Jiang Rong was in high spirits about the outing. "Third Cousin-Sister says the hundred-flower garden has flowers from foreign lands that we have never seen!"
Jiang Mi set down her book and smiled. "Is that so? Then we shall have to feast our eyes." She remembered that A'Rong had always loved flowers.
Jiang Rong flushed slightly. "Honestly I am just a little curious. The best part is getting to go out with Elder Sister."
Jiang Mi had not expected Yǒuyuán to be built on a hillside outside the city. They set out in the morning and it took well over two hours to arrive.
She stepped down from the carriage wearing her veiled hat and found maids and attendants already waiting to receive them — their manners impeccable.
The place carries itself like an aristocratic household, Jiang Mi thought. No wonder it impresses people.
Su Jingchen came with the third and fourth Su daughters. He turned to Jiang Rong. "A'Rong-biaomei — why don't you go ahead with the girls to the hundred-flower garden. I will bring Miss Jiang along in a little while."
Jiang Rong glanced at Jiang Mi. Seeing her nod, she went off with the Su sisters.
Su Jingchen said to Jiang Mi, "Miss Jiang — I must ask you to be my distant cousin for the occasion."
"Second Young Master is too formal," Jiang Mi said. "A'Rong is your cousin. Then so am I."
Su Jingchen was quite touched. He had not expected this young woman to be so easy to talk to.
He took Jiang Mi first to the Hall of Rarities. When they entered, there were already a number of other visitors inside.
She saw room after room displaying different wares: calligraphy and paintings, antiques and curios, precious jewels, guqin, silks and satins, aromatics and incense.
There was also a section selling live creatures. She had just glimpsed someone carrying away a pure white fox in a cage.
"That section deals in exotic animals," Su Jingchen told her. "Shall we have a look?"
"Let us look at the calligraphy and paintings."
Su Jingchen led her into the painting room and rapped on the counter. "Shopkeeper — any fine goods come in lately?"
A young man in a blue robe looked up, recognised the visitor, and broke into a wide smile. "Why, it is Second Young Master Su! Please, sit, sit!"
He cast a glance at Jiang Mi — the hat concealed her face, but her bearing alone was unmistakably exceptional. His smile widened in her direction too. "Miss, please do sit down."
He snapped his fingers at an attendant to bring tea.
He said, "Second Young Master — that Magu scroll by Master Yunqing which I had in my collection — you already bought it. There is nothing of that calibre to speak of at the moment."
Watching Su Jingchen's expression fall, he hesitated, then added, "That said — Second Young Master is one of our most valued patrons, so I will be candid. We have no more Yunqing pieces, but a short while ago we did acquire a calligraphy work by Master Qinyuan. Would you care to take a look?"
Su Jingchen nodded. "Bring it out."
The man smiled. "A moment — I will fetch it personally."
Once he had gone, Su Jingchen noticed Jiang Mi examining the pieces hung on display. He asked quietly, "Well?"
Jiang Mi gave only a small shake of her head and said nothing.
Su Jingchen realised — too eager, and walls have ears.
He picked up his cup and made a show of looking at the paintings too.
Before long the young man returned, carefully unrolling a scroll across the counter. "Second Young Master, please look! Master Qinyuan's calligraphy — a single powerful stroke from beginning to end. A treasure for any collector!"
Su Jingchen leaned in. The calligraphy was so densely stylised he could barely read it. This was called good?
"This is indeed fine calligraphy," Jiang Mi said.
Su Jingchen gave her a startled look. Miss Jiang actually agreed?
He asked the shopkeeper, "How much?"
"Very reasonable — a mere thousand taels."
Su Jingchen nearly choked. A thousand taels for something he could barely decipher?
He caught Jiang Mi looking away and said, "Is there nothing but calligraphy? I still prefer paintings."
The shopkeeper's expression flickered. He thought for a moment and said, "We do have some — none with Yunqing's reputation, but Second Young Master is welcome to see."
"Bring them all out."
Two more scrolls were produced: a Butterfly-Catching scene and a Night Banquet.
Su Jingchen found these considerably easier to appreciate — paintings he could actually look at.
After Jiang Mi had examined them he asked about the prices, one by one. At the end he shook his head with a rueful air. "Shopkeeper He — it is not that I am unwilling to do business. I simply find these pieces a little lacking."
Shopkeeper He saw that the sale was slipping away. Urgency flashed briefly across his eyes. "Second Young Master — is there a particular master whose work you are seeking? Or a specific piece you have in mind?"
Su Jingchen smiled. "My cousin came to Yǒuyuán having heard so much about it — I brought her today to broaden her horizons. But perhaps these paintings are not quite what she hoped for."
The shopkeeper looked toward the veiled woman. "And what sort of painting does the miss favour? No harm in saying — even if we do not have it now, there is always the chance fate brings it our way."
Jiang Mi said, "I saw the Magu Birthday scroll by Master Yunqing that my cousin purchased and admired it greatly. It made me think of Yunqing's very last work — the Peony Beauty. I wonder whether there is any hope of seeing it someday."
Shopkeeper He said, "Ah — so it is the Peony Beauty the miss has in her heart. That painting is something many people have sought. Yǒuyuán has been searching for it for years — and recently we have picked up a few promising leads. If we truly locate it, I will be sure to send word to Second Young Master first."
Su Jingchen placed a banknote on the counter. "Much obliged."
Shopkeeper He bowed deeply. "Of course, of course."
He saw them to the door.
Su Jingchen took Jiang Mi through the antique room and the jewellery room, spending a little money in each.
When they came out of the Hall of Rarities and reached an open space, Su Jingchen asked, "So?"
Jiang Mi said quietly, "The calligraphy is genuine. The paintings are forgeries. Though even the calligraphy has something odd about it."
That Qinyuan scroll — she had seen it as a child in the palace. Her aunt had once had it brought out for her to study. She had lost track of it afterward — whether her aunt had put it away or given it to someone in the palace, she did not know. In any case, it had no business appearing in Jinling.
So when had it left the palace? Had a eunuch or serving girl smuggled it out? Or had it come out in the chaos of a past coup with someone who had fled?
And the shopkeeper had truly had the nerve to say they were following leads on the Peony Beauty — that painting had long since passed into Xiao Huaiyan's hands. There were no leads for them to follow.
Su Jingchen pressed, "What exactly is odd about it? And what about the other antiques?"
"I am not deeply knowledgeable about antiques. Some of what I saw looked genuine. A few showed one or two small inconsistencies. But the craftsmanship is sophisticated — genuinely difficult to distinguish. The real and the false are mixed together. They may be targeting specific buyers differently depending on the customer."
This Yǒuyuán was no simple operation. Even if a buyer later discovered the truth, Yǒuyuán's entrenched connections with prominent families would make it impossible to touch.
Su Jingchen's face had gone iron-grey with fury. They had looked at him — a merchant's son who could not tell real from fake — and played him perfectly.
