The two of them were walking when they came upon a particular door. Several people were waiting outside. Suddenly a young man came bursting out, face flushed with elation, gripping a slip of paper, muttering excitedly to himself — "The road to the heavens, the road to the heavens..."
Jiang Mi watched him rush off with an uncomprehending look. "What is 'the road to the heavens'?"
"That door is the 'All Wishes Granted' room," Su Jingchen said. "There is a Master Zhu inside. If the price is right, he will provide a solution for anything. Quite a few people have come out very satisfied."
He lowered his voice. "What that young man was muttering — 'road to the heavens' — that means the imperial examinations."
Jiang Mi's expression sharpened. "The examinations? Impossible. They would never dare leak the questions openly like this!"
"It is not as severe as leaking questions — they would not be so brazen as that. What a friend of mine bought was a note on a particular examiner's preferences and temperament. He went to the capital and used it accordingly — knew exactly how to make an impression, had a far better chance of being noticed than others. As for rising from there — that still depends on one's own abilities."
Jiang Mi went very still. Was this not serious enough? She found it deeply alarming.
Who could compile such detailed intelligence on officials of the imperial court?
"Miss Jiang — would you like to go in and see?"
She shook her head. There was no need. She would go back and write to her aunt immediately. She also wanted to ask Xiang Yun whether it was possible to reach Sister Xuan, and get a letter to her.
"Then let me take you to the hundred-flower garden to spend some time with A'Rong and the others. Miss Jiang — just think of today as a flower-viewing excursion."
She knew there were things that could not be said here, and that two visitors leaving so quickly after arriving would arouse suspicion. She went with Su Jingchen to the garden.
At the entrance to the hundred-flower garden, Su Jingchen ran into an acquaintance who immediately began pressing him to come for a drink.
Jiang Mi could see he was torn and told him to go ahead, asking one of Yǒuyuán's maids to lead the way.
She had barely entered the garden when the layout gave her an odd sense of familiarity. Gardens often resembled one another — it was nothing unusual.
She had not gone far when she spotted the Su sisters and A'Rong playing under the peach trees in the distance. She paused, then said to the maid, "Take me to change."
The maid bowed and led her off toward a side courtyard.
Jiang Mi came out again quickly. The maid was waiting ahead. She was just about to walk over when she heard a burst of small urgent chirps — thin, quick, anxious.
She looked around. At the foot of a tree near one of the garden's decorative rock formations, a small dark bundle was hopping frantically, flapping its wings but unable to fly.
A fledgling bird.
She spotted a nest in the branches above — it must have fallen from there.
Jiang Mi walked over, cupped the fluttering little creature gently in her hands, and stood on her toes to lift it back into the nest.
The moment she let go, an urgent voice came from the other side of the rock formation.
"Young Master, young master — please stop running this way. If the master finds out, this servant will be punished."
Jiang Mi turned to look.
A maidservant had placed herself in the path of a small child, who had just lifted his face.
That face. Jiang Mi could not forget it no matter how many times she tried.
She had seen it at the Duke Zhen State's manor. She had seen it on the ship. She had seen it in nightmares.
Why was this child here?
A low male voice cut through the air. "How did you let him run out here? If the wrong eyes see him — a stranger catching a glimpse that should not be seen — you will not keep your life."
The maid dropped to her knees, head knocking the ground in supplication.
A slightly older voice said, "The Young Lord shows mercy in sparing your life. Now take the young master away quickly."
The maid scrambled up and hurried the child away.
In the instant that voice had sounded, Jiang Mi had pressed herself into the rock formation.
She could not collect herself. The man who was supposed to be in Suzhou — how was he here in Jinling, in this Yǒuyuán?
Through a gap in the rocks, Jiang Mi saw Xue Jinglin in a deep sapphire robe. Standing beside him was an elderly man in grey.
The old man said, "Young Lord — Xu Shiming is very displeased that you killed Yang Da in Cangzhou and destroyed everything they had built there. Particularly with the court's deliberate commendation of you — that faction now suspects you of divided loyalties. Though this old one actually thinks it is fortunate that your cover was not truly blown."
Xue Jinglin's mouth curved in a cold smile. "That was merely a test. I let them come to collect the child — and two factions within their own camp both tried to take him. Xu Shiming sent Yang Da to seize him, thinking that one child was all it would take to make everything fall into place."
The old man said, "The Young Lord is meticulous. By disguising the young master as a girl and scattering several children to flee separately, you exposed their intentions completely."
Xue Jinglin said, "Master Zhu — I have been feeling uneasy of late. The others need reining in. If any of them start entertaining ideas again, kill them outright. We will lie low for now. I have a few things to arrange, and then I will return to the capital."
The old man stroked his beard. "The Young Lord need not worry. Xu Shiming cannot make much of a wave. The lesson you gave him will keep him quiet."
Xue Jinglin said nothing.
The old man continued, "This old one did happen to meet with Official Gao a few days ago. The official seems somewhat displeased about the Young Lord's betrothal. He had apparently had a mind to seek an alliance with you."
Xue Jinglin gave only a faint smile and looked at the plants growing between the stones.
The old man said, "There is no hurry on this matter. Once things are done, it can be discussed at leisure. The Young Lord's betrothal to the Cheng'en Marquessate has both advantages and disadvantages — all in all..."
Xue Jinglin snapped his head up and looked directly ahead. "Who is there?"
Jiang Mi held her breath, utterly motionless.
She heard footsteps approaching. Her face went white, her heart hammering.
"My greetings to the Young Master. This servant came to find someone — it seems the young lady is not here. I will take my leave."
Jiang Mi recognised the voice of the Yǒuyuán maid who had been showing her the way.
Xue Jinglin waved a hand, dismissing her.
The old man said, "Young Lord — the garden has many visitors today. This is not a good place to talk. Shall we go to the Cāngfēng Courtyard?"
Xue Jinglin did not refuse. He turned and walked away with the old man.
Only when their footsteps had faded completely did Jiang Mi dare to breathe.
Her hands and feet had gone to water.
She could not believe what she had heard.
That Xue Jinglin was a stranger to her. Utterly unlike the man she thought she knew.
She had not even begun to piece together what it all meant when a hand shot from the shadows and seized her by the throat.
Jiang Mi struggled with everything she had.
The hand at her throat — which should simply have snapped her neck — paused. For some reason it reached up and lifted her veiled hat instead.
Jiang Mi looked at the face before her. Tears spilled without her permission.
Xue Jinglin released her at once. Jiang Mi sagged against the rock formation, coughing, unable to speak through the pain in her throat.
Xue Jinglin slowly closed his hand. He looked down at her in silence. "How did you come to be here?"
That was precisely what Jiang Mi wanted to ask him.
The old man stepped to Xue Jinglin's side. He looked at the woman's face and could not conceal his surprise. "Young Lord — you know her?"

books.title: The house Is On fire
books.author: Fang Chen Yi
books.synopsis:
Slow-burn romance · Marry first, fall in love later · Both leads untouched · Family drama in an ancient noble household

The moment Qin Ruyin opened her eyes, she found herself transmigrated into the body of a legitimate daughter of a prominent official family in the Great Ying Dynasty. Before she could even begin to enjoy the privileges of wealth and status, she was blindsided by her cousin Qin Ruyue's scheming switcheroo marriage plot.

Qin Ruyue had been reborn. In her previous life, she had married the widowed and supremely powerful Lord Jiang — a man at the very pinnacle of imperial authority. She had assumed a life of endless luxury awaited her, only to find him cold and indifferent, utterly unmoved no matter how desperately she tried to win his favor.

Refusing to accept her fate, she turned the inner household upside down with her tantrums — only for the Jiang family to fall from grace, leaving her divorced and sent back in disgrace, the laughingstock of the entire capital. Upon her rebirth, she discovered that Qin Ruyin — the cousin she had always looked down on in her past life — was about to receive a marriage proposal from the Wang family. The Wang family, who would one day command power over the entire court! There was absolutely no way she was letting Qin Ruyin have that.

So she went weeping to her grandmother, begging for a marriage swap, and successfully offloaded her "cold and heartless ex-husband" onto her cousin.

Qin Ruyin: Wait, there's a deal this good? She quietly looked into it:

Lord Jiang has no interest in women? Perfect — that's exactly what she wanted.
The inner household is clean, with no concubines or serving girls? Ideal!
Her mother-in-law won't make her life difficult? This marriage might as well have been custom-made for her.

All she had ever wanted was to live out her days in peace. Since her cousin didn't want this marriage, she was more than happy to take it off her hands. All she had to do was walk in with a generous dowry, keep her head down, make no demands, and coast through life in comfortable mediocrity. Easy.

Or so she thought.

As it turned out, Lord Jiang wasn't cold-blooded at all. Nor did his tastes lie elsewhere. No — he was more like a house that had been empty for years: the moment a single spark caught, the whole thing went up in flames, and there was simply no stopping it.

Later, she would often wonder: she had signed up for a quiet, effortless life — so how exactly did she end up with a husband completely and utterly obsessed with spoiling her?

chapters.title: The marriage swap
chapters.content:
The moment Qin Ruyin opened her eyes and took in the antique furnishings of the room around her, she understood at once; she had transmigrated.

The original owner of this body had attended a flower-viewing banquet, where she was accidentally shoved into a lake by the third daughter of the Guozijian Jijiu's family. By the time the servants fished her out, she was already gone.

The soul that woke up in her place was a wandering spirit from another world.

The current dynasty was the Great Ying Dynasty.

The Guozijian Jijiu — the Imperial Academy Chancellor — was a senior fourth-rank capital official, a position traditionally held by men of distinguished scholarly families with exceptional literary accomplishments.

The current Chancellor was Lord Wang, whose family was indeed a well-established household of scholarly renown.

They were known for their propriety and their principled conduct.

The very day that the third Wang daughter had accidentally pushed the original Qin Ruyin into the lake, Lady Wang personally called on the Qin family, accompanied by gifts and an official matchmaker.

The visit served three purposes: to offer an apology, to inquire after the girl's health, and — out of a sense of responsibility — to request the original Qin Ruyin's hand in marriage for their second son.

The current head of the Qin family was Qin Ruyin's second uncle, who also held the highest official rank in the family — a senior third-rank post in the Ministry of Works.

However, Qin Ruyin's father and his second brother were not from the same mother. Her father held only a junior fifth-rank position in the Ministry of Rites. By that measure, a marriage into the distinguished Wang family was a considerable step up for Qin Ruyin.

As for the Wang family's second son, Wang Huaimin, he was an exceptional scholar — a prodigy, no less. Though barely past the age of twenty, he had already claimed the title of jie yuan, the top scorer in the regional examinations for the capital district.

Barring any mishap, he was almost certain to pass the spring imperial examinations the following year, and with a bit of fortune, he might even place in the top three.

In all fairness: had the clumsy third Wang daughter not knocked Qin Ruyin into the lake, the Wang family would never have come seeking her hand for their second son.

Qin Ruyin's family — the first branch of the Qin household — was overjoyed at the news. Her parents in particular were ready to accept on the spot.

But the decision was not theirs to make.

...

Dowager Lady Qin had sent Lady Wang away with polite pleasantries. She said that her youngest granddaughter from the first branch was the youngest legitimate granddaughter of Qin Ruyin's generation, and that she was too fond of the girl to see her married off before the age of eighteen. In other words: she needed time to consider, and would send word in a few days.

Lady Wang took this as the usual feminine coyness expected from the bride's side of negotiations and thought nothing of it. After all, in matters of marriage, it was the groom's family that bowed low and the bride's family that held their head high — a little pride from the girl's side was only natural.

Never mind whether she was truly satisfied with the match.

What mattered most was that the Wang family's reputation must not suffer.

Besides, it was their daughter who had pushed the sixth Qin girl into the lake, with witnesses from many of the capital's most prominent families.

A young lady falling into a lake and being pulled out soaking wet — even if it was only the servants who dragged her out — had been seen by far too many eyes.

If the Wang family failed to take responsibility, what would that say about them?

The only eligible Wang son of marrying age was their second boy.

And truth be told, Lady Wang was genuinely pleased with the eldest branch's youngest legitimate daughter. Both her looks and her temperament were charming. That strikingly lovely face of hers was the sort that even another woman couldn't help but like.

She had heard things about Qin Ruyin's character before — sensible, well-mannered, easy to get along with. Her scholarly accomplishments weren't particularly remarkable, but for a family looking for a daughter-in-law, that was hardly the deciding factor.

They say: marry a wife for her virtue; take a concubine for her beauty. But a wife who was both virtuous and beautiful? That was better still.

Besides, her second son was talented enough for the both of them — who knew, perhaps he'd one day become a grand minister. The inner household would inevitably fill up with women, and having a wife of exceptional beauty would be enough to keep those little vixens in check.

More importantly, by being so principled and taking responsibility for their daughter's recklessness, marrying Qin Ruyin into the Wang family would only raise their standing — both among the upper circles of the capital and in the hearts of the scholarly community.

That very evening, Qin Ruyin and her parents were summoned to Dowager Lady Qin's Shouhe Hall.

The Dowager was silver-haired, her low bun pinned in place without a strand out of order, secured with a single long jade-green hairpin. She wore a half-new Hangzhou silk coat embroidered with auspicious characters, and on her wrist sat a jade bracelet as luminously green and translucent as the hairpin.

Round-faced, round-eyed — she looked altogether kind and approachable.

When the three members of the first branch arrived, she fussed over her granddaughter Qin Ruyin at length, asking after her health with every appearance of concern.

Only when she was assured the girl had recovered — that she could eat and drink normally — did she seem satisfied.

She had seats arranged and tea served, and then her expression turned serious.

She looked at Qin Ruyin with warm affection, her voice tender.

"Sixth girl, you've always been such an obedient child. Falling into the lake at the flower banquet was an undeserved misfortune — but once this disaster has passed, good fortune will follow."

The old woman looked mild and benevolent on the surface, but behind those aged eyes lurked a sharp and cold glint.

Qin Ruyin had a gentle temperament and had never harbored grand ambitions in her life. Perhaps it was her experience from her previous life — she had an unusually keen sense for the undercurrents of human relationships.

The old woman's words made her feel that nothing good was coming.

Sure enough, Dowager Lady Qin pivoted.

"Your grandmother knows that the Wang family's second son is a fine young man, and I want nothing more than to see you well-matched. But what you don't know, child, is that Wang Huaimin is actually in love with your Fifth Sister."

The "Fifth Sister" the Dowager referred to was the youngest legitimate daughter of the second branch — Qin Ruyue, born one year before Qin Ruyin. In the seniority ranking of Qin granddaughters, she was fifth; Qin Ruyin was sixth, and was therefore called "Sixth Miss" throughout the household.

The moment these words left the Dowager's mouth, Qin Ruyin's parents — Lord Qin and Lady Li — both went pale with shock. Lady Li stared at the Dowager in disbelief.

"How is that possible?" Even Lord Qin, who was relatively composed, couldn't help but rise from his seat to question her.

The Dowager glanced at him, her tone weary.

"Eldest son, this is the absolute truth. Otherwise, why would I have put Lady Wang off today instead of accepting at once?"

Lady Li's eyes began to redden.

After so many years in the Qin household, she knew all too well what this meant.

She stood and dipped in a bow, barely keeping her anger in check. "But — at the beginning of the month, wasn't it Old Madame Liu who accompanied Madame Jiang to our house to look over Fifth Young Miss..."

"That is precisely what I was about to tell you." The Dowager cut her off.

Lady Li pressed her lips together, eyes full of resentment, and forced herself to listen. If she didn't, she'd find herself buried under the enormous weight of filial impropriety — and even if she could bear that herself, her children would suffer for it.

Qin Ruyin frowned slightly.

She knew that in this feudal ancient society, a junior like her had no standing to speak up in matters like this. Look at it — even her own parents had no authority over her marriage; it was firmly in this step-grandmother's hands.

Of course, had her father held the highest rank in the Qin family, even without being the family head, they might have had a say in their children's marriages.

But he didn't.

The situation now was exactly the one where she was the fish and someone else held the cleaver.

"Eldest son, daughter-in-law — earlier this month, Madame Jiang did indeed come to look over Fifth Miss on behalf of Lord Jiang." The old woman cleared her throat and continued.

"At the time, I didn't agree outright... but Lord Jiang is, after all, a senior second-rank court official. Most of the Jiang family's men have done well in their careers, and they are at the height of their power right now."

Lady Li immediately interjected: "Dowager, isn't it wonderful then that Fifth Miss has secured such a fine match?"

The Dowager frowned, fixing Lady Li with a sharp look. "Let me finish!"

Lady Li had no choice but to comply.

"Our Qin family, as it stands, has only your second brother holding a respectable post. Whether it's your generation or the younger ones below — none of them have made particularly impressive careers. And so, the Jiang family is one we absolutely cannot afford to offend."

Even Lord Qin's expression cooled at that.

The old woman was deliberately taking a dig at him.

Even Qin Ruyin caught it, and glanced at her father with quiet concern.

He was her father now, after all.

Lord Qin drew a slow breath, clasped his hands, and addressed the Dowager formally. "Is it Dowager's intention, then, to have our Sixth Miss accept the Jiang family's proposal, while Fifth Miss marries into the Wang family?"

chapters.title: An Old Widower
chapters.content:
Dowager Lady Qin fixed her stepson with a steady gaze. The warmth in her aged eyes had completely vanished, replaced by cold severity.

"Eldest son, for the good of the family, this is the only way."

"Besides — with your current rank, you are in no position to find Sixth Miss a better match than Lord Jiang, are you?"

Lord Qin's fingers clenched tight inside his wide sleeves.

For his daughter's sake, he swallowed the humiliation and pressed on. "Dowager, from time immemorial, a daughter's marriage has always been decided by a matchmaker's word and a parent's command. If Fifth Miss has privately... conducted herself in such a manner with the Wang family's second son — that is simply improper!"

"And besides, Lord Jiang is considerably older than our Sixth Miss."

The Dowager raised her teacup and took a small, deliberate sip, watching him with cold eyes.

"Fifth Miss and the Wang boy have done nothing more than admire each other from afar. They are perfectly innocent — not a single impropriety has occurred."

Then her voice softened.

She looked at Lord Qin and said slowly, "Lord Jiang may be a full twelve years older than our Sixth Miss, but he is only twenty-eight."

"You ought to know, Eldest Son — at such a young age, to already hold a senior second-rank post in the imperial court... If the Jiang family hadn't set their eyes on Fifth Miss first, Sixth Miss would never have had a chance at the Jiang family in a thousand years. Wouldn't you agree?"

The fist inside Lord Qin's sleeve tightened further.

If the Jiang match were truly as wonderful as the old woman claimed, why hadn't she kept it for her own granddaughter? Why did it need to be handed off to his daughter?

Lord Qin pressed on, with lowered eyes.

"Second Brother has not yet returned home, and Mother should also consider his opinion. After all, a marriage swap is a major matter for both girls, and a family alliance is an even greater matter for the household..."

The Dowager seemed to have anticipated exactly this. She waved him off.

"The swap was my idea. But your second brother has always wanted what's best for this family and has devoted himself to that end entirely."

She paused, then raised one eyelid, her voice going stern again.

"Eldest Son — as the elder brother, you ought to show more consideration for your second brother's labors. He exhausts himself in the court for the Qin family's sake. The least you can do is not drag him down."

"Especially you, as the eldest legitimate son of the Qin family! The less impressive your own career, the less trouble you should be causing him."

Such unvarnished, shameless admonishment...

Lord Qin's eyes grew colder by the second. He managed only a flat, toneless reply: "With how capable Second Brother is, he'll surely rise two full ranks this year — straight from senior third-rank to senior second-rank."

The Dowager looked at him and suddenly curled her lips into a smile. "Then I'll take that as a blessing from you, Eldest Son."

Qin Ruyin sighed inwardly.

This old woman was truly formidable. Even in plotting something as significant as the marriage swap between herself and Qin Ruyue, she had managed to keep Second Uncle entirely out of it — and still claimed the moral high ground while putting her father down.

Using her, the step-granddaughter, as a paving stone for her biological granddaughter's path. One stone, killing many birds.

No wonder, after so many years, her parents — as the eldest legitimate son and daughter-in-law of the Qin family — had been held down so completely.

It seemed the Jiang family match was one she would have no choice but to accept.

Even knowing that any match rejected by Qin Ruyue must have something wrong with it.

Then again — Lord Jiang was a senior second-rank court official at such a young age. Back in her previous life, she wouldn't have dared to dream of that, not even in her sleep.

Qin Ruyin's clear eyes shifted slightly. If the old Dowager wanted to scheme her way through her marriage, she wasn't going to get away without paying a price.

...

Qin Ruyin followed her parents back to the first branch's courtyard.

The second branch, being the most powerful, naturally occupied the main compound — the East Courtyard. The other branches — fourth, fifth, sixth — also lived in better quarters thanks to their closeness to the second branch, since all four of those brothers shared the same mother.

The first branch was left with the West Courtyard, the worst of the lot — scorching hot in summer from the afternoon sun. Even the third branch, born of a concubine, had a better arrangement in the North Courtyard.

At the time, the old Dowager had claimed the West Courtyard was the largest and had the most rooms — perfectly suited to the first branch, which had the most children. Naturally, she failed to mention that the second branch had even more children overall, even if fewer of them were legitimate.

Now, Lady Li pulled Qin Ruyin into her inner chamber.

The moment they stepped over the threshold, Lady Li said bitterly, "Ruyin, you know Lord Jiang is so young and already holds such a high position — do you know why the Dowager still looks down on him? It's because he's a widower. And there's more..."

"His first wife passed away?" Qin Ruyin blinked, momentarily startled, then recovered.

Lady Li nodded, her eyes red.

Qin Ruyin considered. If that was all, it wasn't really a problem.

The age gap, though — that was a bit much. A full twelve years was not nothing.

Lady Li continued, "It's not just that he's a widower. He and his first wife were married for four years — four years — and she never gave him a single child."

Qin Ruyin thought: a childless widower with no heirs, still young, of high rank — in her original world, that sort of man would be fought over tooth and nail on the marriage market!

Lady Li could see her youngest daughter still wearing that innocent expression, and after a struggle with herself, she pressed on. "Word is his inner household is completely clean — not even a concubine or a serving girl to his name. It's all just male attendants and older manservants looking after him..."

Qin Ruyin blinked, genuinely puzzled.

A man of high rank with such a spotless household — what exactly was the problem? And this was considered undesirable?

But Lady Li was livid — furious on her daughter's behalf.

Qin Ruyin, fearing for her mother's health, did her best to soothe her. "Mother, don't work yourself up. If you feel this match is unsuitable, let's think of something — see if there's any way we can refuse."

Lady Li snatched up her handkerchief and flung it down.

"You foolish girl! It's not that your mother thinks this match is bad — it's — it's — oh!"

"How is your mother supposed to explain this to you?"

Qin Ruyin had a feeling something far more significant was lurking beneath the surface, something that had made her mother so violently opposed to this match.

After a moment's thought, she said gently, "Mother, just tell me plainly. I can handle it."

In her last life, she had always pursued the idea of a carefree single life — marriage was a complication she'd never wanted. And as for the political machinations of noble houses in the ancient world, a perfectly ordinary person like her from a previous life couldn't begin to fathom them.

Lady Li waved her hand miserably. "Oh — your father and I are both unhappy about it, and we've tried to fight for you, but we both know in our hearts there's no escaping this Jiang match..."

"The truth is, if the Wang family hadn't come to propose, that match would certainly have been Fifth Miss's..."

Qin Ruyin noticed her mother had trailed off before explaining what was actually wrong with Lord Jiang, and was circling back to complaints again. She gave herself a small mental shake.

Her mother's train of thought was a little... non-linear.

She redirected. "Mother, did Wang Huaimin really fall for Fifth Sister that early?"

"Oh!" Lady Li looked at her perfectly innocent — and perhaps slightly oblivious — youngest daughter and sighed heavily.

"Who knows? However they put it, they've found a way to take what was supposed to be your match. That's what matters." The words came out grinding through Lady Li's teeth.

"If you measure by family name and future prospects, the Jiang match is clearly the better one, isn't it?" Qin Ruyin brought the conversation back around.

"And Lord Jiang didn't have any children with his first wife — so it's practically the same as a first marriage, isn't it?"

Lady Li pressed a hand to her forehead. Her youngest daughter was still young, and had always been too sweet and unworldly to understand the dangers lurking in all this.

She took Qin Ruyin's hands in both of hers. "Ruyin, my darling — a grown man, married for four years with no children, five years after his wife's death without a single woman in his household... That is a very serious problem."

"Your mother was trying to spare your ears, young as you are — but you're clearly not going to understand unless I say it plainly, so I suppose I have no choice."

"Lord Jiang doesn't have some terrible ailment... it's that he simply has no interest in women. Otherwise, how could a man of his age still have no children?"

That was when it finally hit Qin Ruyin.

And her eyes lit up like stars.

There was a deal this good?

She came from a modern world and, while she didn't endorse or oppose people of that persuasion, she certainly respected them.

They'd have nothing to do with each other — and she'd be spared the business of bearing children. When the time came, she could arrange to adopt a few orphaned children from the Jiang clan...

If she truly had no choice but to marry — then the Jiang match was, in fact, beyond anything she could have dreamed up herself.

Lady Li watched her daughter — who, instead of looking worried, was positively beaming — and felt fresh tears threatening.

"You foolish girl — do you think you're the first to figure this out? That's exactly why your grandmother didn't immediately agree to this match for Fifth Miss. She, your second uncle, his wife, and even Fifth Miss herself — they all had the same concern."

"What woman would want to marry a man like that? No matter how fine his family, no matter how bright his future — what good is any of it? It would be a life of living widowhood."

chapters.title: Setting The Wedding Date Sooner
chapters.content:
Lady Li reached out and pressed a hand to her daughter's forehead.

"What family that truly loves their daughter could bear to hand her over to a man like that?"

Qin Ruyin finally nodded, slowly, as if just now catching up. "I suppose you're right — if this match were truly good, Grandmother wouldn't want to give it to me either."

Lady Li's heart ached even more at those words.

"Oh, don't be frightened. Your mother will go visit your maternal grandmother tomorrow — see if she and your aunts have any ideas."

Qin Ruyin quickly stopped her. "Mother, please don't act rashly just yet. Let's wait and watch."

"This is your entire future, Ruyin! We cannot wait—" Lady Li dabbed at her eyes with her handkerchief, her expression grave.

Qin Ruyin set aside her usual carefree manner and looked at her mother steadily.

"Mother, let's send someone to quietly look into the Jiang household first. The ones who should be panicking right now are not us — they're the Dowager and the second branch."

Lady Li paused.

Then she understood exactly what Qin Ruyin meant.

She bit her lip and let out a cold, quiet laugh.

"Ruyin is right! They want the swap — whether it's the Jiang family or the Wang family giving them trouble, it's their problem to sort out."

"Exactly. Let's just watch." Qin Ruyin poured her mother a cup of tea and pressed it into her hands. "Besides, Fifth Sister is older than me by a year — the Dowager and the second branch are the ones who should be in a hurry."

"And no matter whether they manage to pull off this swap or not — either way, we'll be watching a very entertaining show from the sidelines." Frankly, she thought the Jiang match was by far the better deal.

Lady Li took a slow breath, studying the expression on her youngest daughter's exquisitely lovely face — that guileless look with a hint of unexpected cunning — and stared for a moment.

Then she smiled helplessly.

"You little schemer — this is your marriage we're talking about! And you have the nerve to sit there and enjoy the spectacle?"

Qin Ruyin said soothingly, "Mother, don't overthink it. Tell Father not to overthink it either."

Then she gave her mother a bright smile. "If I truly have no choice but to marry into the Jiang family, the Dowager and Second Uncle and Second Aunt-in-law will never be stingy with my dowry."

"For one thing, I'm going to the Jiang family in Fifth Sister's place. For another — a marriage to a senior second-rank official like Lord Jiang demands a proper dowry. A paltry one would simply look bad."

"You can't turn an alliance into a feud, can you?"

Something stirred in Lady Li's chest.

She was right!

However terrible Lord Jiang's reputation might be, he was still a fully legitimate senior second-rank official of the Great Ying Dynasty!

The Left Deputy Minister of the Ministry of Personnel!

Oh — her usually scatterbrained youngest daughter had gone and had a genuinely sharp moment!

Right. The second branch had made up their minds about this swap. Worrying about it now was useless. What she needed to do was think clearly about how to set her sixth girl up as well as possible!

Three days later, Dowager Lady Qin had Lady Li and Qin Ruyin summoned back to Shouhe Hall.

This time, Qin Ruyin met her step-grandmother Tong and her cousin Qin Ruyue for the first time.

Second Aunt Tong was the daughter of the Tong family — a distinguished household from Hexi province — raised with the careful polish that great families put into their daughters. Her schemes and methods went without saying.

Qin Ruyin studied her face: not unpleasant to look at, but something about the wide jaw and long, slightly crooked nose spoke of a certain... hidden artfulness. And that was just the face. From what the servants had whispered, her mother was no match for this woman in intrigue. Even without the Dowager's backing, Second Aunt Tong could have managed this swap on her own.

With Second Uncle behind her as well, the second branch's advantages were almost unfair.

After the customary exchange of greetings, the ladies took their seats.

Qin Ruyue had been specifically called to the Dowager's side. She settled in prettily, leaning against the old woman with practiced ease.

Qin Ruyin glanced at her cousin, and in that single glance, she was struck speechless.

A true, rare beauty — the kind that stopped your heart.

Before she could finish marveling, a procession of maids entered bearing trays.

Dowager Lady Qin swept her aged eyes slowly across the room, then let them settle on Qin Ruyin, who was just reaching out to take a teacup from a young maidservant.

"Sixth girl — yesterday, I took your birthday characters and your Fifth Sister's to have them cross-referenced by the retired Grand Astronomer Lu..."

"When yours were matched against Lord Jiang's, they aligned perfectly."

She paused before continuing unhurriedly, "And your Fifth Sister's, of course, aligned just as perfectly with the Wang family's second son."

Second Aunt Tong added with a gentle smile, looking toward Qin Ruyin, "Sixth girl — doesn't it seem like a double blessing has arrived at once? What do you think?"

Qin Ruyin blinked her pretty eyes and did not answer Tong's question. She looked only at the Dowager. "Whatever Grandmother says must be right."

Qin Ruyue, overhearing this, glanced at her cousin with a flicker of contempt.

Look at this fool. At a moment like this, still trying to flatter Grandmother. Beyond help.

"Sixth girl," Tong said, her expression warm and maternal, "what happened with your Fifth Sister — none of us anticipated it, and it wasn't done on purpose. But it has put you in an uncomfortable position, and your second uncle and I have already disciplined her."

She looked at Qin Ruyin with fond eyes, not sparing a single real thought for this girl the first branch had raised as pure and naive as a blank page.

"But as it turns out, you were the one Lord Jiang was truly fated for — and that puts your Second Aunt's heart at ease."

Lady Li, sitting nearby, was grinding her teeth.

She burst out: "Sister-in-law, you can't say it that way — just yesterday I heard Mother say that Fifth Miss privately—"

Qin Ruyin reached out and lightly touched her mother's arm.

She turned to look at Tong and Qin Ruyue.

Tong was formidable — knowing full well Lady Li was about to say something cutting, her expression didn't flicker.

Qin Ruyue was younger and couldn't hold herself as steadily. A thin flush of anger rose to her beautiful face, and she looked at Lady Li with wounded eyes.

"First Aunt, how could you say such things? Are you blaming me for this?"

Qin Ruyin studied the great beauty who had stolen her original match. No shame, not a trace of guilt — not even the faintest acknowledgment that she had engineered this swap. She was something else.

And so Qin Ruyin smiled lightly. "Fifth Sister has misunderstood. My mother was actually trying to congratulate you on finding such a wonderful match. I also offer my congratulations — may you and Wang Huaimin have a long and happy marriage."

Qin Ruyue gripped her handkerchief tight and took a slow breath, refusing to stoop to the level of this fool.

But before she could compose herself, Qin Ruyin was already looking at her again with that perfectly innocent expression, asking brightly: "Fifth Sister, I'm curious — when did you and Wang Huaimin first meet?"

A sharp light flashed through Qin Ruyue's eyes.

The Dowager tightened her grip on Qin Ruyue's hand and cut Qin Ruyin off with an amused reproof.

"Sixth girl, really! Young people falling for each other is perfectly natural. Your Fifth Sister and the Wang boy are completely innocent — don't go embarrassing her."

Having said that, the old woman's sharp eyes swept the room.

"As it happens, our Great Ying Dynasty is far more lenient than past dynasties on the subject of propriety between men and women. A young man and woman meeting privately, so long as there are witnesses present, is quite permissible."

"Only — it's not the sort of thing to be spoken of outside. Those in the family may know. That is all." The Dowager's voice grew gradually more pointed, a clear warning to all present. "Is that understood?"

Tong, knowing perfectly well the old woman was siding with her daughter, quickly agreed with a smile.

Lady Li was unwilling, but had no choice but to murmur her compliance.

After all, if Qin Ruyue's shameless behavior became a public scandal, every unmarried Qin girl — and even those already married — would suffer for it. She might not care about the others, but she had three daughters of her own, and three precious little granddaughters.

Qin Ruyin accepted with a smile, as if she hadn't understood the warning was meant for her at all.

Satisfied that her juniors were all behaving, the Dowager's expression eased back into graciousness.

In truth, she had never been worried the swap would fall apart.

The first branch couple had been thoroughly domesticated by her over the years — they had no real way to make waves.

As for this youngest daughter of theirs — pretty face, meek as a rabbit. Easy to manage.

Dowager Lady Qin turned to Lady Li with a look of pleasant finality. "Daughter-in-law, you needn't worry anymore. The matter of Sixth Miss's marriage has already been settled — your second brother-in-law personally went to the Jiang family to finalize everything after he left his post."

Lady Li's face went white.

So there was absolutely no room left to maneuver.

Even though she and her husband had steeled themselves for this, hearing the old woman say it so plainly still ignited a fresh wave of fury in her chest.

She pressed her fingers together tight, simmering with silent rage.

Watching Lady Li's face, the Dowager's smile deepened slightly.

Then she looked at Qin Ruyin.

"Sixth girl — Madame Jiang has indicated that the substitution is acceptable, and they would like the wedding to proceed sooner rather than later. Your second uncle has already agreed with the Jiang family on a date: the twenty-sixth of the third month."

"It is already the end of the second month. Your wedding is less than a month away — spend these days at home working diligently on your bridal garments."

She turned to Lady Li and instructed: "Daughter-in-law, keep a close eye on Sixth Miss. Starting today, decline any poetry gatherings, flower banquets, or outings on her behalf."

"Dowager, why so sudden?" Lady Li could not contain herself any longer. She dropped all pleasantries, her eyes blazing.

chapters.title: The Dowry Cannot Be Skimped On!
chapters.content:
Dowager Lady Qin turned a gaze on Lady Li that could cut glass.

"Daughter-in-law, you are well past forty — when are you going to stop being so impulsive? What sort of example does this set for the children?"

Lady Li's cheeks went red with rage.

Second Aunt Tong lowered her eyes, a smile on her lips that didn't reach them, and offered Lady Li a smooth explanation.

"Sister-in-law, Lord Jiang isn't young anymore, and Madame Jiang has always fretted over his marriage. Now that Lord Jiang has finally agreed to remarry, Madame Jiang is eager to welcome a daughter-in-law before he changes his mind."

Remarry. Remarry.

And Tong was throwing that word in her face — telling her, over and over, that her Ruyin was going to someone as a second wife.

Lady Li's fury boiled over. Even her breathing became ragged. The love she bore her daughter drove the words out of her: "So what you're saying is Lord Jiang didn't want to remarry in the first place? Then what kind of life is my Ruyin going to have when she gets there?"

Tong maintained her gentle, placid smile. "Sister-in-law, how can you say such a thing? Think of it as Sixth Miss's good fortune — even with the substitution, Lord Jiang was willing."

The implication being that Lord Jiang had had his eye on Qin Ruyue all along, and had only agreed to take Qin Ruyin as a favor to Second Uncle.

Lady Li understood perfectly. She nearly choked.

She was on the verge of making a scene — dignity be damned.

Qin Ruyin quickly rose to her feet and went to her mother's side, resting her hands lightly on Lady Li's shoulders.

She didn't mind playing along. But watching her mother be treated this way was another matter entirely.

She looked up at Tong with a wide, guileless expression.

Her voice was soft and sweet.

"Second Aunt — I don't quite understand... but from what you're saying, it sounds as though Lord Jiang still prefers Fifth Sister?"

Tong wasn't sure what this girl was playing at.

At occasions like this, the girl always shrank into a corner and said nothing. She never spoke up. Not once.

Qin Ruyin continued, "And since this match is so fine... and I know I'm not really worthy of the Jiang family's standing... perhaps it would be better to return it to Fifth Sister after all."

Tong stiffened.

Qin Ruyue's lovely face lost its color entirely.

Only the Dowager noticed, her sharp eyes cutting over to Qin Ruyin.

This girl — something was different about her today. These words were dangerously clever.

Far more pointed than anything Lady Li had managed in her outburst earlier.

Qin Ruyin saw that her mother had gone quiet, and turned to the Dowager, who was frowning at her with studied assessment.

"Grandmother — perhaps there's no need for the swap at all. After all, the Wang family originally came to propose to me..."

"Nonsense!" The Dowager cut her off before she could finish.

"Marriage has always been decided by one's elders. You have always been such a well-behaved and filial child — what has gotten into you today?"

Qin Ruyin's peony-bright little face went pale.

"Grandmother... I wouldn't dare. But isn't that exactly what Second Aunt's words implied?"

Tong quickly stepped in: "You silly girl, what are you thinking? The Jiang match is wonderful — and since it has fallen to you, your Second Aunt is delighted for you!"

"Oh, is that so?" Qin Ruyin brightened up immediately. "Then I must thank Fifth Sister for so generously giving me such a wonderful match."

"But Fifth Sister — are you sure you won't regret it?" She tilted her head and looked at the great beauty with an expression of wide-eyed innocence.

Would she regret it?

You fool.

Does Sixth Miss actually think the Jiang match is good? Does she actually think it was freely given?

Hopeless, brainless girl.

Let her go off and marry into the Jiang family and spend her days in humiliation, becoming the laughingstock of the entire city!

Lord Jiang Jiuxiao is no decent man. And besides, the Jiang family in the future...

Either way — she had been so devoted to him in her last life. Even a stone should have warmed by now. But that cold-hearted man's heart was harder and colder than stone.

It served him right that he and the whole Jiang family came to ruin in the end.

Qin Ruyue collected herself, and a faint, contemptuous smile surfaced in her beautiful eyes.

She smoothed her emotions, dropped her earlier air of superiority, and looked at Qin Ruyin with a gracious smile.

"Sixth Sister, what are you saying? As the elder, it is only right that I let the better things go to you. I have no regrets at all."

How could she have regrets?

After next spring's examinations, Wang Huaimin would be this year's most celebrated third-place scholar — the Tanhua.

And ten years from now, Wang Huaimin would become the youngest Grand Tutor this dynasty had ever seen — a first-rank official, truly standing second only to the Emperor.

She would be the youngest Grand Tutor's wife, the most sought-after, the most fawned-over woman in all the capital's aristocratic circles!

What did Sixth Miss know about any of this?

Still — now that she had been reborn, now that she had taken this fool's match, she no longer had any interest in making her life difficult.

Only... something about this fool seemed off today.

She had better not do anything foolish and get herself tangled up in Qin Ruyue's affairs.

...

"Mother, look — Grandmother and Second Uncle and Second Aunt have worked so hard for my marriage. I'm truly moved." Qin Ruyin gave her mother a quick, meaningful glance.

"Otherwise, someone like me could never have hoped to marry into a family like the Jiang's. Please don't worry."

Lady Li felt something crack open in her chest.

But seeing the odd brightness in her daughter's eyes, she forced herself to calm down.

What was done was done. The swap between her sixth girl and Qin Ruyue was not going to be undone.

Then it was time to do exactly what the family had discussed: fight for what they could.

Lady Li steadied herself, dabbed at her eyes with her handkerchief.

She looked at the Dowager. "Please forgive me, Mother — I was being hasty. I've been worried that Lord Jiang is so much older than Sixth Miss, but when I think about it clearly, the Jiang family truly is a fine match."

The Dowager smiled thinly. "It is good that you have come around, daughter-in-law."

"Only," Lady Li continued, "the wedding date is very rushed indeed. Surely you can allow us a bit more time to prepare Sixth Miss's dowry?"

Tong hadn't expected Lady Li to regain her composure so quickly, let alone pivot to the subject of the dowry. Something shifted behind her eyes.

The Dowager's fingers, working her prayer beads, paused.

Tong was anxious, but managed a thin smile. "Sixth Miss's dowry — hasn't Sister-in-law been preparing that for some time already?"

Lady Li's expression was measured and serious.

"I had no idea my Ruyin would be marrying into a family like the Jiang's!"

"When the Wang family came to propose, I was already thinking of adding thirty to forty percent more to what I had prepared for Ruyin..."

"And now with this swap — marrying into the Jiang family, a household of such distinguished nobility — I may be unworldly, but even I know the Jiang family and the Wang family are not the same."

The Dowager and Tong's composure began to crack at the edges.

If the Wang match required a thirty or forty percent increase to the dowry, and the Jiang family was self-evidently above the Wang family in rank and prestige...

This meant the Jiang match demanded an even larger increase?

Lady Li, who normally seemed more foolish than clever, had apparently found her sharp side today.

But both women were accomplished performers. They quickly smoothed away any trace of displeasure.

Tong tested the waters gently. "Sister-in-law, the Jiang family's standing cannot be treated carelessly — you and your husband should think of how to add more to the dowry."

Lady Li nodded. "That is exactly my thinking."

Then she furrowed her brow — a crease that made Tong's heart jump.

The brow furrowed deeper still.

"But Sister-in-law — if the Qin family's dowry for Sixth Miss is too modest, the Jiang family will certainly feel we don't take them seriously."

"My husband holds a low position, and frankly, there's little hope of advancement. Once he becomes Lord Jiang's father-in-law, Lord Jiang will surely understand our first branch's humble circumstances and forgive us if we cannot put together a lavish dowry."

"As for our Ruyin — she's not the sort to fight and claw for things. If I quietly set aside a bit more in private savings for her, she should manage well enough in the Jiang household."

Then Lady Li pivoted sharply. "But, Sister-in-law — if the Jiang family is displeased with our Qin family, wouldn't that be harmful to Second Brother's position in the court?"

"Everyone in our social circle in the capital knows it is Second Brother and you who run this household."

"You have insisted on this swap. We in the first branch have accepted it. But the dowry for Ruyin cannot be stinted!"

The moment those words landed, both Tong and the Dowager lost the last of their composed, commanding air.

"Well — I truly hadn't expected the wedding date to be set so abruptly. Mother, Ruyin and I will take our leave."

Lady Li rose. Qin Ruyin was on her feet even faster, moving to her mother's side and taking her arm.

But Lady Li hadn't really meant to leave — her steps stayed deliberately slow.

chapters.title: A loss
chapters.content:
Inwardly, Qin Ruyin gave her mother a silent salute.

Noble family alliances were built on goodwill and mutual face — not hostility.

The second branch had originally prepared a generous dowry for Qin Ruyue, their own daughter, who had been the one the Jiang family came for first. Now that the Qin family had gone to the Jiang family to arrange the substitution — and the Jiang family had agreed — it meant that whichever Qin girl was going over, her dowry had to be comparable to what they'd originally prepared for Ruyue. Anything less would be an insult to the Jiang family's face.

Had the Dowager and Tong truly never considered this?

Of course they had.

They had simply counted on the first branch being too cowed to say a word... and had not anticipated being wrong this time.

"Daughter-in-law. Wait."

The Dowager spoke.

Lady Li stopped. Qin Ruyin stopped with her.

"Daughter-in-law — even if you hadn't brought it up, I was going to say something about Sixth Miss's dowry."

"Sixth Miss marrying into the Jiang family is a great occasion. The dowry must be respectable."

"Besides, I have always been especially fond of how obedient Sixth Miss is. Since she is to be married, I have already set aside a generous personal contribution for her dowry."

The Dowager made the call without hesitation. No matter how reluctant she was — this was for her own son's and grandson's futures.

"As for the family's public contribution — the standard allotment is the same for all legitimate daughters of this household. But Sixth Miss is marrying considerably above her station, so it would not be appropriate to add too much from the public funds."

The Dowager looked at Tong. "Let her second uncle and second aunt-in-law contribute a generous personal portion as well."

Tong pressed her lips together behind downcast eyes.

When she looked up again, she was smiling.

"Mother is absolutely right. Of course — since Sixth Miss is being married off, her second uncle and I will naturally contribute a generous dowry on her behalf."

She paused to suppress her irritation, then looked at Lady Li.

Lady Li laughed coldly to herself. Fine words indeed! Hadn't she just been telling her to dig into her own pocket?

And didn't that face change fast!

It was thanks to Ruyin's quick thinking earlier, coaching her on what to say, that it hadn't come to a public showdown. If they'd had to force Tong and the Dowager into it through a scene, the first branch would have technically won the dowry — but lost face.

This way, without a single skirmish, those two had been maneuvered into volunteering the increase themselves.

Having secured more dowry for her daughter, Lady Li's chest felt somewhat lighter, and she felt like smiling.

Her tongue loosened.

"Oh, my deepest thanks to Mother and Sister-in-law! After all, my Ruyin is going to the Jiang family in Fifth Miss's place, isn't she?"

"We in the first branch have always been the poor branch — I could never have imagined a match like the Jiang family falling to us. It's a good thing Mother and Sister-in-law have it all planned out, or I'd be completely at a loss."

"There's no helping it — if Ruyin doesn't go in Fifth Miss's place, there simply isn't another legitimate girl of the right age left in our Qin family."

This time, even Tong's face couldn't hold. The barely-suppressed fury was visible to the naked eye.

For the first time, she had not won the verbal sparring with Lady Li.

She had lost.

Even the Dowager felt herself choke, and was inwardly cursing Lady Li for being shameless — taking the upper hand and then gloating about it.

If she didn't want to let go of the chance to attach themselves to the Jiang family, she had no choice. As Lady Li had so helpfully pointed out: the only eligible legitimate girl in the Qin family left to send was Qin Ruyin.

There were other Qin girls of age — those born of concubines.

But the Jiang family's standing was too high, and Lord Jiang too young and too capable, already outranking their own Second Uncle by two full grades. Even for a second marriage, they would never accept a girl of lower birth.

And truth be told, even royal princes' illegitimate daughters had floated the idea — and Lord Jiang had politely declined them all.

But Tong could not stomach losing ground to Lady Li, a woman she had always looked down on.

The Dowager, seeing her daughter-in-law open her mouth, gave her the subtlest shake of her head.

Tong swallowed her words and, with great effort, bowed her head in silence.

...

For the first time, Lady Li walked out of Shouhe Hall with her head held high, her step light, her whole bearing radiant.

Qin Ruyin settled sideways onto the wide daybed by her mother's window, leaning against the cushions, nibbling on a piece of pastry.

Lady Li looked at her daughter in that posed, contented position and blinked.

This girl...

Since when had she developed such an air?

Catching her mother's startled expression, Qin Ruyin gave a small internal start.

She quickly sat upright, reached over to the plate, and took another piece of pastry — which she promptly pressed to her mother's lips.

"Mother, have some pastry."

She smiled prettily, looking soft and sweet and delightfully silly about it. Lady Li's expression softened.

She opened her mouth and accepted the pastry, and stopped thinking about it.

"Mother, the Dowager said she'd increase the dowry, but I could see she wasn't happy about it — and neither was Second Aunt."

Privately, Qin Ruyin refused to call that old woman "Grandmother."

"Well, of course! Even you noticed — you think your mother didn't?" Lady Li was still chewing, her voice muffled.

Qin Ruyin hugged her mother's arm with both hands. "You don't think they'd try to cheat us when it comes to adding the dowry, do you?"

Lady Li nearly choked on the pastry. Qin Ruyin quickly let go and thrust a cup of tea into her hands, fussing over her.

Lady Li took a sip and managed to swallow. Then she laughed a little despite herself.

"So you've finally started worrying about your dowry? I was watching you just now, looking for all the world like you hadn't a care!"

"Of course I care! The dowry is what determines whether my life will be comfortable. Of course I have to pay attention."

In any era, money was more dependable than a husband.

She could do without the husband. She could not do without the silver.

Lady Li set her teacup down, and a look of deep contempt settled in her eyes.

"Don't worry! Your grandmother has always been the kind who wants everything and gives up as little as possible."

"But since she's already made up her mind to use your marriage to pave the way for your second uncle and his sons..."

"And since I made sure they understood — even if they want to shortchange us, they won't dare go too far. Because the man you're marrying is the Left Deputy Minister of the Ministry of Personnel of the Great Ying Dynasty. The second branch may not give the first branch a second thought — but they would never dare disrespect Lord Jiang!"

Lady Li was right.

After she and Qin Ruyin left, Tong was clutching her handkerchief, furious beyond words.

"Mother," she said, barely keeping her voice low, "Sister-in-law has never been particularly clever. How did she manage to scheme so well about the dowry today?"

She pushed her anger down and looked at the Dowager.

The old woman raised her teacup, took a small, deliberate sip, and set it down.

"Whether it's scheming or resentment — the Jiang match is ultimately a great advantage to the Qin family."

The greatest advantage, of course, would go to the second branch.

The Dowager's sharp gaze slid to Tong. "Daughter-in-law, you and your husband didn't want Fifth Miss to be anyone's second wife. And Wang Huaimin was the one Fifth Miss had her heart set on, and he apparently put it out that he intended to marry her... it was a coincidence I hadn't anticipated."

"Since we took the risk and made this swap, there will naturally be a price to pay. Bleeding a little over Sixth Miss's dowry in exchange for securing Fifth Miss a fine marriage is a very good deal."

"Don't let it sour your mood. From where I stand, that Wang boy is promising."

"The Wang family has been a family of scholars and honor for generations — their reputation in scholarly circles is exceptional. Lord Wang's rank may not be high, but his former students and colleagues are everywhere."

"As long as Wang Huaimin continues to rise through the examinations, his future is assured."

The Dowager smiled to herself as she spoke, her tone carrying no small amount of self-satisfaction. "Say what you will — our Fifth Miss has an excellent eye. This was a trade well worth making!"

chapters.title: The Wang Family's Calculations
chapters.content:
While Dowager Lady Qin was feeling pleased with herself, Lady Wang in the Wang household was seething.

That day, Lord Wang had taken leave from work to discuss with his wife the news of the Qin family's sudden request for a marriage substitution.

"My lord, I don't mean to gossip, but the Qin family is supposedly a distinguished household — how could they act so indecently?"

Lord Wang stroked his slightly graying beard and replied calmly, "The Qin family wants to send the second branch's Fifth Miss in place of the first branch's Sixth Miss... Truthfully, the swap is the better deal."

Lady Wang wiped the corner of her mouth with her handkerchief. "I understand the logic."

"But on the day of the accident, it was our Third Daughter who pushed the Qin Sixth Miss into the lake. We went to their house immediately afterward to propose — and it earned us a very fine reputation..."

"If the Qin family now sends a different girl, won't people talk about us?"

Lord Wang raised his teacup and took a composed sip.

"There's no need to worry, my lady. The Qin family has already given us an explanation, haven't they? According to the story, our Second Son had set his eyes on the Fifth Miss from the start, and it was Sixth Miss who stepped back of her own accord rather than ruin her sister's happiness."

Lady Wang was worldly and shrewd, but she had no respect for this sort of maneuvering.

Claiming their son had been the one who first liked the Fifth Miss — hmph! Who knew what actually happened behind closed doors? The Qin second branch schemed this entire substitution and managed to pin it all on their son, while the Fifth Miss got to slip away without a blemish.

And besides — she had genuinely preferred the Sixth Miss.

That girl's looks were dazzling, her temperament gentle and biddable. She was Lady Wang's ideal daughter-in-law.

She had encountered the Fifth Miss a few times as well. The girl was undeniably more striking — a beauty that could stop you in your tracks.

But beauty that aggressive made her uneasy. And there was something beneath that gracious, poised surface — a sharpness, a relentless ambition to always be first.

Every woman had once been a calculating young girl in her own way — Lady Wang had lived through it herself. She could see it plainly now that she was on the other side.

In their circles, who would actually want a daughter-in-law like the Fifth Miss?

"My lord, you may not know this, but the Fifth Miss, while certainly striking, comes from..."

No matter how Lady Wang turned it, she simply could not warm to this new future daughter-in-law.

The resistance ran deep.

Lord Wang looked at her.

"The Fifth Miss's maternal family is the Tong family, one of the great households of Hexi province. A daughter raised by the Tong family will have better breeding and refinement than the Qin Sixth Miss, regardless."

Lady Wang sighed.

Lady Li's family, admittedly, couldn't compare to the distinguished Tong household.

But as for who actually raised her children better — that was an entirely different question.

"But my lord... Second Son has a limitless future ahead of him. If he has the right connections and backing, he could one day be ennobled, or even become a Grand Minister." Lord Wang glanced at his wife.

"The Qin Sixth Miss has a soft, yielding temperament — she won't be able to manage Second Son's inner household."

"And her father, at his age, is still only a useless fifth-rank sinecure official."

"Her two older brothers have nothing to show for themselves in the military either. There is a younger brother who seems academically gifted — but he's only thirteen and hasn't even sat his first examination yet. Who knows how he'll turn out?"

Lady Wang thought it over.

She sighed again, with greater resignation. "Then let the swap happen."

Lord Wang nodded as she agreed, then his expression grew gradually more serious.

"Besides — refusing isn't really an option anymore. The Qin Right Deputy Minister didn't come forward himself, but his superior, Minister of Works Supervisor Wei, has already paid me a visit."

Lady Wang's expression shifted. She asked quickly: "What? The Qin second son actually went through Minister Wei to broker this substitution?"

"He had Minister Wei personally act as the guarantor for this marriage." A gleam of calculation entered Lord Wang's eyes.

For the Qin family to have Minister Wei personally stand as guarantor — that spoke to a very deep relationship between the two men.

And Minister Wei's influence extended far beyond the Ministry of Works.

The Consort Wei in the palace was none other than Minister Wei's own younger sister. And the Consort's son, Prince Song, was currently the Emperor's favorite.

Meanwhile, the Crown Prince had fallen from his horse during last autumn's hunt and injured his leg — and though he had recovered, the injury had left him with a permanent limp.

The Emperor had not deposed the Crown Prince, but the court had grown increasingly vocal about the matter of an heir with a disability.

Lady Wang needed only a moment to see all the threads connecting beneath the surface.

She looked at her husband, a flicker of shock in her eyes.

"My lord — are you saying the Qin family has chosen to back Prince Song? And us — where does that leave us?"

Lord Wang gave a subtle shake of his head.

"We watch for now. Our Third Daughter carelessly pushed the Qin Sixth Miss into the lake that day — we are already tied to the Qin family and there is no getting untangled. Since the connection is unavoidable, it is better to find the alliance that is most beneficial for Second Son."

"It was our daughter's accidental mistake, and we responded with both accountability and integrity. Whatever may come later, we have a clean record to stand on."

Lady Wang's expression cooled inwardly. Her father-in-law and the Wang family's ancestors had always been men of principled, non-partisan scholarship — that legacy should not be broken.

But she also knew very well that her husband had grown increasingly distant from her over the years.

She said too much at her own peril.

"I understand, my lord. I will defer to you in all things."

...

What surprised Qin Ruyin was how quickly the Wang family exchanged betrothal letters with the Qin family.

Of course, it was Wang Huaimin and Qin Ruyue's.

She had assumed the Wang family would be reluctant — they were principled, dignified people, Lord Wang a prestigious and upright official. A last-minute substitution like this would not be easy to accept.

But now, seeing how smoothly it had all gone, she supposed she had been too naive.

Lady Li was furious to the point of being unwell.

Qin Ruyin's eldest and second brothers had both requested leave from their military posts to return home; her eldest and second sisters had come back to their parents' house. Even her youngest brother Jinge had shown up.

Her second brother, young and hot-blooded, and her youngest brother, even more impulsive, had put their heads together and decided they wanted to beat Wang Huaimin within an inch of his life.

Fortunately, the steady and composed eldest brother discovered their plan before they could do anything reckless, and gave them both a thorough dressing-down.

When their father found out, he added his own punishment — another solid thrashing.

He said bluntly: "Ruyin's marriage swap is something not even I could do anything about. What do you two think you can accomplish? And besides, what does it have to do with Wang's son? I've already investigated privately, and that Wang boy genuinely did tell his close friends that he intended to marry the Fifth Miss — and the two of them were exchanging letters in secret."

Everyone in the first branch went cold with shock.

Only Qin Ruyin, a beat behind everyone else, let out a quiet "Oh?"

So the Fifth Miss and Wang Huaimin actually had been corresponding in secret.

These ancient people really could be quite bold.

Lord Qin was a man of few words and a heavy temperament, but he was a person of genuine depth — and fiercely protective of his own.

In front of his wife and children, he looked at Qin Ruyin and said:

"Ruyin — it's not that your father doesn't love you. The Wang family moved so fast because your second uncle used his connections. There is no way to turn this around now."

"But don't be sad. In your father's view — the Wang family may not be as good a match as it looks, and the Jiang family may not be as bad."

He held a quiet, unremarkable post — but his personal network, built over years, was nothing to dismiss. It simply never brought him into contact with men of Lord Jiang's rank.

Knowing the swap could not be undone, he had called in some favors and made discreet inquiries.

What he'd heard was that Lord Jiang had suffered a significant humiliation in his youth — and that this was what had left him indifferent to women.

But one thing had led to another during his investigation, and what he had uncovered about Second Brother had left him genuinely shaken.

It seemed his brother, who thought himself so clever, had taken a very wrong turn this time.

The first branch needed to find a way to quietly distance themselves from the direction the second branch was steering the Qin family.

chapters.title: Showered with Love
chapters.content:
"Father, Ruyin isn't sad." Qin Ruyin reassured her father.

A father who could speak to his children so openly was a good father.

A mother who had fought so hard against her step-mother-in-law for her daughter's sake was a good mother.

Brothers and sisters who had all rushed home the moment she was wronged — she felt deeply fortunate. Genuinely so.

And for the sake of these people — the people the original Qin Ruyin had treasured most, and who were now her own to treasure — she told herself she could not truly afford to coast along without a care in the world.

She needed to think of something. She needed a plan.

"Father, I honestly don't think the Jiang match is so bad. Please don't feel wronged on my behalf!"

She thought for a moment, then added, "And since the Jiang family agreed to the substitution without hesitation — and we couldn't have refused them anyway — this is simply how it has to be."

"You child." Lord Qin shook his head with tender exasperation.

"You're too understanding, and far too undemanding. A temperament like yours — how can your father and mother rest easy once you're in someone else's household?"

They say the youngest child is the heart's most tender attachment. As the youngest daughter of Lord Qin and Lady Li, Qin Ruyin naturally received an extra measure of their love.

Lady Li nodded in worried agreement.

Her eldest and second brothers, her eldest and second sisters, and her youngest brother Jinge all looked at her with expressions full of concern.

Even her eldest and second sisters-in-law, who were careful not to speak up too readily in front of their elders, wore faces full of worry.

Qin Ruyin's heart went warm.

"Father, Mother, Elder Brother and Sister-in-law, Second Brother and Second Sister-in-law, Elder Sister and Second Sister, Little Brother — please don't worry."

"The Jiang household is not a dragon's den. And even if it were — I still have all of you."

"If I'm truly not happy there, none of you would stand by and do nothing. Right?"

"Of course not." Eldest Brother Qin Rongcheng spoke first, his expression earnest and resolute. "Your elder brother and second brother will work hard in the military — we'll earn our merits and our rank, and then we'll be able to protect both our elder sisters and our little sister."

Qin Rongcheng was tall, with deep-set eyes and a striking, martial bearing.

Second Brother Qin Ronglie nodded vigorously in agreement. He looked more like Lady Li — refined and handsome — but no less physically imposing than his elder brother.

Little Brother also made his own solemn pledge: he would study hard, pass the imperial examinations, become a top scholar, and serve as a great official — so that along with his two military brothers, their family would have both civil and martial strength, and no one would dare mistreat any of their sisters.

Their youngest brother's earnest, youthful declaration made the whole family burst out laughing, and swept away the heavy cloud that had been hanging over them.

Elder Sister Qin Rujin gently patted Qin Ruyin's forehead as she sat beside her.

"Your elder brother-in-law also said just yesterday — he expects to earn a top performance review this year, and should move up another rank. His mentor is the Left Chief Censor of the Censorate, and with the Censorate watching over things, the Jiang family won't dare do anything unreasonable."

Qin Rujin carried herself with quiet grace, and was strikingly beautiful.

Years ago, the Dowager had nearly pushed her into the Eastern Palace as a concubine — all to pave the way for the Second Uncle she doted on.

That the plan had failed was due to a fortunate accident.

Her elder brother-in-law, Han Zheng, had been the second-place scholar of his examination year.

On the day the results were posted, Qin Rujin had gone with her maternal cousin Wang Lanzhi to watch the boards being put up in support of her cousin's brother Wang Gui. Han Zheng was one of the candidates that year and was also there to see the results.

When the crowds surged and a stampede broke out, Qin Rujin was separated from her cousin and her cousin's brother — and it was Han Zheng, standing nearby, who shielded her from the chaos.

After becoming the second-place scholar, Han Zheng had been received in audience by the Emperor, who was deeply impressed by his quickness of mind and his breadth of vision, and had lingered in conversation — hinting, in his way, that a marriage to a princess was being considered.

Han Zheng had instead made a request.

He told the Emperor plainly and fully what had happened at the posting boards — how he had shielded Qin Rujin when the crowd turned to a stampede. Given that there had been physical contact in such a chaotic moment, he asked that he be permitted to handle the matter of his own marriage.

The Emperor admired Han Zheng's directness and his willingness to take responsibility, and granted him the right to choose his own bride.

Han Zheng wasted no time in sending an official matchmaker to the Qin family.

The Dowager, unaware at that point of what had happened at the palace, had given a clean and immediate refusal.

It was the Second Uncle, arriving in haste, who urged the Dowager to reconsider.

After all — while it was not an imperial decree of marriage, the Emperor had given his tacit approval before the entire court when Han Zheng made his request. How many heads did the Qin family have to spare, to refuse?

Qin Rujin married Han Zheng, the son of a humble family, without further resistance.

Han Zheng's career had continued on an upward trajectory.

The couple was deeply devoted to each other, and Han Zheng kept a house with only Qin Rujin in it.

These past few years, her elder sister's life had been genuinely good.

The only shadow was that they had not yet had any children.

...

Qin Ruyin leaned her head on her elder sister's shoulder. "Thank you, Elder Sister, and thank you, Brother-in-law."

"Silly girl, what is there to thank?" Qin Rujin smiled warmly.

Second Sister Qin Ruxiu thought of something her husband had said, and felt simultaneously annoyed and amused.

"Your second brother-in-law is just so reckless — a blunt military man, no subtlety at all. He said... he said..."

"Second Sister, what did Brother-in-law say?" Qin Ruyin was dying to know.

With the whole family's eyes on her, Qin Ruxiu gritted her teeth and just came out with it.

"He said — if you ever can't bear it at the Jiang household, just get divorced and come home. Between our first branch, your two sisters' families, you think between all of us we can't keep one little sister fed and housed?"

Everyone was briefly speechless.

Then Lord Qin was the first to recover, and he laughed out loud.

"Hah — Duan Ze said that well! Very well indeed!"

Duan Ze was Second Brother-in-law Dou Junwei's courtesy name.

Second Sister Qin Ruxiu stamped her foot. "Father!"

"He's a complete dolt, and you're taking his side! Who goes around telling their little sister-in-law to get divorced?"

"What do you mean? You have such a fine husband and you're still complaining? I can tell you, Ruxiu — Duan Ze has courage and integrity, and he'll go far."

"Yes, yes, Second Sister — you really should be nicer to your husband!" Qin Ruyin thought of her second sister and brother-in-law, an endlessly bickering pair who adored each other, and covered her mouth to hide her laugh.

Truth be told, what her brother-in-law had said had hit exactly where she lived.

Second Sister yanked her younger sister by the arm and tapped her on the forehead. "Look at you — this is your marriage we're talking about, and you're over here making fun of me?"

Qin Ruyin made an exaggerated show of "ow, ow, ow!"

Eldest Sister-in-law Lin Xi drew Qin Ruyin into her arms, smiling. "Second Sister-in-law, Ruyin is right — Second Brother-in-law treats you wonderfully. Stop criticizing him so much."

Since Eldest Sister-in-law had spoken, Qin Ruxiu had no choice but to agree, red-faced.

She was the spiciest of the sisters — the most hot-tempered, not like either parent in disposition. And yet she was the most soft-hearted of them all.

So — she was being showered with love, wasn't she?

Qin Ruyin felt the warmth spread through her chest. She loved this family — this family that loved each other.

As long as they stood together, all facing the same direction and pulling toward the same goal, this family would one day emerge from the shadow of being suppressed. The day they soared would come.

Before she transmigrated, Lord Qin had been quietly teaching his branch to endure, to keep a low profile, to wait.

Now that she was here — she had said she wanted to coast along, but you needed the footing to coast. She needed to build the family up to a stable standing first, so that she could comfortably coast later.

She was already making arrangements toward that goal.

After dinner, her closest handmaid Zisu came to her with a grave expression, leaning close to whisper her report.

"Miss, the person we sent out has returned. Lord Jiang will be off duty in two days and will be receiving guests at the Xilai Restaurant."

chapters.title: Father's Dowry Gift
chapters.content:
Qin Ruyin straightened.

Seeing her mistress grow serious, Zisu instinctively stood up taller as well.

Qin Ruyin beckoned her closer and said quietly, "Find a way. I need to leave the compound in two days."

Zisu was startled. "Miss — you're not going to go and see Lord Jiang in person, are you?"

That was absolutely out of the question!

If the Master and the Mistress found out, every one of the personal attendants around their Young Miss would be beaten half to death!

Qin Ruyin glanced at her.

Zisu had originally been a second-tier maid in Lady Li's room. The year the original Qin Ruyin came of age, Lady Li had given the girl to her daughter as her head maid.

She was a trustworthy person.

Which was precisely why she had been entrusted with a task this important.

As for the other head maid, Zicao — she had been assigned by the Dowager. Naturally, no trust could be placed there.

It happened that, a few days earlier, Zicao's mother had fallen ill. Qin Ruyin had sent her home to tend to her mother, using the greatest virtue the era prized — filial piety — as the reason Zicao could not refuse, no matter how reluctant she was.

A reasonable pretext to temporarily remove the Dowager's spy from her side.
